## ============================================================================
## DEFERRED (2026-06-14): ring-3 fork()+wait4() reap E2E self-test
## ============================================================================
## WHAT: an end-to-end self-test where a real Linux-ABI process fork()s a child,
##   the child exits with a sentinel, and the parent wait4()s it and exits with
##   the decoded WEXITSTATUS — the core toolchain primitive (make→gcc→cc1/as/ld
##   all rely on fork+exec+wait). Today fork/vfork/clone and wait4 have only
##   *dispatch-validation* coverage (syscall/linux.rs self_test_clone_vfork_parent,
##   self_test_wait4_dispatch) — no test actually creates a child, runs it, and
##   reaps it through the real ring-3 path.
## WHY DEFERRED (not a capability gap — a test-safety call): the cooperative
##   spawn-based self-test harness (proc::spawn::self_test_linux_*) yields a fixed
##   number of times and then tears the process down. A fork+wait test is
##   inherently scheduler/timing-dependent (child must be scheduled to exit before
##   the parent's wait can reap it) and creates a SECOND process the driver doesn't
##   track. This codebase has a documented history of intermittent timing-dependent
##   self-test races at boot (known-issues.md W1 watchlist; F1/F2/F3 were all
##   self-test/ISR races). Introducing another timing-dependent multi-process boot
##   self-test AUTONOMOUSLY, while the operator is away, risks an intermittent boot
##   hang that passes my boot-test but fails later — exactly the trust-eroding
##   failure mode to avoid unsupervised.
## PROPER DESIGN (when built): make the parent NON-BLOCKING to remove all hang
##   risk — wait4 with WNOHANG in a bounded sched_yield() retry loop (fixed max
##   iterations), exiting a distinct "timeout" sentinel if the child never reaps
##   (so a real fork/wait bug surfaces as a clean assertion failure, never a hang).
##   The launcher needs one forward branch after fork (jz child) + the bounded
##   retry loop; assemble with a two-pass label/disp patch (rel8 jumps suffice for
##   the small body). Decode WEXITSTATUS as byte[status+1] (status = exit<<8).
##   Add a matching execve-in-child variant (child execve's build_linux_exit_elf)
##   to cover the full fork→exec→wait cycle gcc/make exercise.
## TRIGGER: do it on a tick the operator is reachable, or once a non-timing
##   harness exists to run multi-process tests deterministically. WHERE:
##   new elf::build_linux_fork_wait_test_elf + proc::spawn::self_test_linux_fork_wait,
##   wired into main.rs alongside the other post-VFS Linux self-tests.

## ============================================================================

## ============================================================================
## DELIVERED: file identity (FileMeta.ino) + mm::page_cache store (refcount §37) +
##   FileBacked fault-path integration (shared RO frames + CoW on private write) +
##   VFS coherence invalidation + memory-pressure shrinker. Boot-verified (two
##   clean BOOT_OK; shrinker freed 54 idle frames under real critical pressure, no
##   fault). Commits: f6003260c (shrinker), 0c6b8caaa, b6e336af0. The writable
##   MAP_SHARED path stays declined (ENOSYS) per §22/§23 — by design.
## REMAINING (operator-gated, NOT blocking): de-double-cache the page cache against
##   the block buffer cache (fs/cache.rs) so a file's data lives in exactly one
##   place. This is an FS-data-path architectural fork -> queued as open-questions.md

##   §36 is otherwise treated as delivered.

## ============================================================================
## DEFERRED (2026-06-14): ptrace(2) behind the Process+DEBUG capability (Q6 / design-decisions §24)
## ============================================================================
## WHAT: full ptrace — breakpoints, single-step, register get/set, signal-delivery
##   interception. Currently returns EPERM/ENOSYS.
## DONE THIS TURN: the cross-address-space process_vm_readv/writev path is now
##   implemented and gated on a Process capability carrying Rights::DEBUG over the
##   target (EPERM without it). See §24.
## TRIGGER: when a debugger/consumer needs live control (gdb-style) over a target.
## GATE: ptrace must use the SAME Process+DEBUG capability gate as process_vm_*;
##   never an ambient same-uid/PID check (CLAUDE.md: no ambient authority).
## WHERE: kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs (ptrace dispatch), kernel/src/proc/ (thread
##   stop/continue, register save areas), kernel/src/cap/rights.rs (Rights::DEBUG,
##   already added).
##
## DEFERRED-WITH-DESIGN (2026-06-14): ring-3 END-TO-END test of the cross-AS
##   process_vm_readv path (the gated read of ANOTHER process's memory from ring 3).
## WHY DEFERRED (not done now): the kernel-context self-test
##   (self_test_process_vm_cross_as) already covers the two hard parts — the
##   capability-gate PREDICATE exhaustively (false w/ no cap, false w/ READ-only
##   cap, true after DEBUG granted, false for wrong pid) and the cross-AS TRANSFER
##   mechanism (copy_from_user_as/copy_to_user_as read+write a remote AS via HHDM).
##   The only glue it can't drive is sys_process_vm_readv reading caller_pid() from
##   a REAL ring-3 caller (kernel context forces caller=None -> same_addr_space).
##   A true ring-3 E2E needs a LIVE target process (see design below) and so risks
##   HANGING the cooperative-scheduled boot self-test if the target spins; the
##   marginal assurance is low for a feature with NO consumer yet (ptrace above).
## TRIGGER: build it when the real debugger consumer lands (ptrace) — a live
##   debuggee gives a live target for free, making the orchestration natural.
## TURNKEY DESIGN (worked out 2026-06-14, so future impl is mechanical):
##   - owner_process(pid_arg) treats arg0 as a TID -> owning PCB; the gate checks a
##     Process cap over that OWNER pid with Rights::DEBUG; get_pml4(owner) supplies
##     the remote page table. So the target needs (a) a LIVE thread registered in
##     THREAD_OWNERS (zombies are removed from THREAD_OWNERS -> owner_process None
##     -> ESRCH, so a zombie target does NOT work even though its pml4 is retained
##     until destroy()), and (b) its stamped page resident.
##   - Make the target LIVE-BUT-BLOCKED, never spinning: target ELF maps an anon
##     page at a fixed addr, stamps a known 8-byte pattern, then issues an
##     indefinitely-BLOCKING syscall (futex-wait on a never-woken addr, or
##     pause/blocking pipe read) so the thread stays in THREAD_OWNERS without
##     burning CPU (no boot-test hang). Verify first that the chosen block syscall
##     truly blocks and that pcb::destroy() cleanly tears down a blocked thread.
##   - Orchestration: spawn target; yield; POLL the target's page via HHDM
##     (translate target pml4 + addr) with a bounded retry until the stamp appears
##     (readiness gate, same trick as the existing kernel-context test); then build
##     the debugger ELF baking target_TID + target_addr(imm64) + expected(imm64),
##     spawn it with capabilities=[(Process, target_PID, Rights::DEBUG)], yield,
##     assert Zombie + exit_code==0x42 (match) ; then destroy debugger AND target.
##   - Debugger ELF (new elf::build_linux_pvm_readv_test_elf): sub rsp,0x40 for
##     scratch; build local_iov{base=rsp,len=8} at [rsp+0x10] and
##     remote_iov{base=target_addr,len=8} at [rsp+0x20]; rdi=tid, rsi=&local_iov,
##     rdx=1, r10=&remote_iov, r8=1, r9=0, eax=310 (SYS_process_vm_readv), syscall;
##     cmp [rsp] to expected (imm64); cmove exit code 0x42/0x43; eax=60 exit.
##   - ADD a NEGATIVE ring-3 case too: spawn a second debugger WITHOUT the DEBUG
##     cap; it should see rax==-EPERM and exit a distinct code (e.g. 0x44), proving
##     the gate denies an unauthorized ring-3 caller end-to-end.

## DONE (2026-06-13): mechanical rename OuRoS -> Slate OS, EXECUTED.
## ============================================================================
## The full rename plan (survey facts, failure-mode analysis, execution recipe,
## checklist) is preserved in git at commit ecb01d284 ("docs(todo): record careful
## OuRoS -> Slate OS mechanical rename plan"). It is NOT re-pasted here because the
## mechanical pass would have re-mangled its own ouros->slateos survey text.
## Execution summary:
##   - Case-sensitive per-form substring replace (NOT word-boundary, which was proven
##     to miss OUROS0NV001 / *_XATTR_DIR / CC_x86_64_ouros): ouros->slateos,
##     OurOS->SlateOS, OUROS->SLATEOS, OuRoS->SlateOS across 3178 tracked text files.
##   - Verification caught one casing the survey missed: `OurOs` (EntryKind::OurOs boot
##     variant, parallel to MacOs) -> SlateOs in bootcfg.rs / kshell.rs / roadmap.md.
##   - git mv toolchain/x86_64-ouros.json -> x86_64-slateos.json; JSON `"os":"linux"`
##     preserved untouched (cfg(target_os) semantics unchanged); .cargo/config.toml
##     target table + build/check/test aliases now x86_64-slateos; ouros-stubs crate
##     -> slateos-stubs (and libouros_stubs.a -> libslateos_stubs.a in build-sysroot.ps1).
##   - CLAUDE.md / claude.md.bak: operator-owned, left for the operator to update the
##     `CC_x86_64_ouros` references (RESOLVED: operator gave permission; applied in
##     commit f90f336f7).
##   - DISPLAY FORM (operator decided): user-visible text reads "Slate OS" (with a space);
##     code identifiers/consts/env-vars/filenames stay "SlateOS"/"slateos"/"SLATEOS".
##     Applied via scripts/display_rename.py, which rewrites a standalone "SlateOS" ->
##     "Slate OS" (not adjacent to . / _ - :, so "SlateOS.installer", PRODID "//SlateOS//",
##     "SlateOS::Foo", "SlateOSConfig", "SlateOSUser", "SlateOS_1.0" are left intact).
##     Two modes: default = string literals; --comments = line/block comments + module-docs.
##     COMMENTS/MODULE-DOCS now ALSO converted (operator: "change comments/module docs for
##     consistency, it'll look better to developers") — commit 1c5f2ce3a.
##     SCANNER-BUG FIX (1c5f2ce3a): the original line-by-line scanner treated a lone quote
##     inside a comment (e.g. `// 2: "`) or a char literal ('"') as a string start, desyncing
##     string detection and SKIPPING real display strings (~13 files: qrcode, backup, terminal,
##     bc, wc, uname, ...) while risking wrong conversion of code identifiers. Rewrote the tool
##     as a single region tokenizer (code / string / char-literal / line+block comment,
##     Rust-nested) so embedded quotes can't desync scanning; both modes idempotent.
##     Regenerated BOTH passes from the pre-display base (a7ef65778).
##     Verified: 0 standalone "SlateOS" remain in ANY region; "Slate OS" appears only inside
##     strings/comments (never in code); kernel boot-test PASSED; readelf 48/48 host tests pass.
##     String pass touched 2546 .rs, comment pass 2782 .rs (2781 source committed; build/ .rs
##     excluded). Inserting a space in a literal/comment can't break Rust syntax, and paired
##     test assertions convert in lockstep.
##     NOT changed (intentional): lowercase "slateos" (paths/domains/identifiers), all-caps
##     "SLATEOS" (consts/banners), all code identifiers. If a future need arises for "Slate OS"
##     in non-.rs user-facing assets (yaml configs, generated /etc/os-release, docs prose),
##     extend the same rule.

## IN PROGRESS (2026-06-13): ALSA/PulseAudio compatibility shim — roadmap 5.1 / 5095
## Goal: Linux audio apps (and PulseAudio/PipeWire/WINE) play sound by speaking the ALSA kernel PCM
## interface: open("/dev/snd/pcmC0D0p"), a sequence of ioctl(SNDRV_PCM_IOCTL_*), then stream frames.
## ALSA is the foundation everything else sits on, so build it first. Backed by the existing
## kernel/src/audio_mixer.rs (open_stream/write_pcm/close_stream/set_volume; fixed 48kHz/S16_LE/stereo).
## Decomposed into small, individually-tested commits (same pattern as the ELF-loader work):
##   [DONE 924db82d3 commit 1] kernel/src/audio_alsa.rs — pure ABI layer: SNDRV_PCM_* stream/access/
##       format/state constants, the _IOC request-number encoder, and the no-struct/plain-int PCM ioctls
##       (PVERSION/PREPARE/RESET/START/DROP/DRAIN/PAUSE/RESUME/XRUN/HWSYNC/LINK/UNLINK/TTSTAMP), asserted
##       against known Linux hex. format->bytes-per-sample + mixer_accepts_directly/mixer_frames_to_bytes
##       translation. Exhaustive boot self-test. No unsafe/devnodes/state yet.
##   [DONE 4bfdb5efe commit 2] Byte-exact #[repr(C)] mirrors: snd_mask(32B)/snd_interval(12B)/
##       snd_pcm_hw_params(608B)/snd_pcm_sw_params(136B)/snd_xferi(24B)/snd_pcm_info(288B), sizes asserted
##       in self_test, and the struct-carrying ioctl numbers derived from size_of and asserted vs known
##       Linux hex (HW_REFINE 0xC2604110, HW_PARAMS 0xC2604111, SW_PARAMS 0xC0884113, WRITEI 0x40184150,
##       READI 0x80184151, INFO 0x81204101). STILL DEFERRED: snd_pcm_status (STATUS=_IOR('A',0x20)) and
##       snd_pcm_sync_ptr (SYNC_PTR=_IOWR('A',0x23)) embed struct timespec and have time32/time64 (STATUS64)
##       variants — handle them in the wiring commit that consumes them, using the explicit non-timespec
##       layout ALSA-lib actually sends (SNDRV_PCM_IOCTL_STATUS64 / mmap status with __snd_timespec).
##   [DONE commit 3] /dev/snd PCM device node + HandleKind::AlsaPcm fd family. New module
##       kernel/src/ipc/alsa_pcm.rs: refcounted per-open PCM substream instance (AlsaPcmHandle +
##       ALSA_PCM_TABLE BTreeMap, create/dup/close/exists, STATE_* + format/rate/channels/mixer_stream
##       fields, accessors, boot self-test) — mirrors ipc/timerfd.rs. close() defers the mixer
##       close_stream() until after the table lock is dropped (no mixer lock nested under ALSA_PCM_TABLE).
##       Wiring: cap::ResourceType::AlsaPcm=22; HandleKind::AlsaPcm + FdEntry::alsa_pcm() in linux_fd.rs;
##       fork dup_one/close_one; ipc::cleanup_handles; ~14 exhaustive HandleKind match sites in
##       syscall/linux.rs (close/read/write/lseek/fsync/ftruncate/readahead/cachestat/flock/fstat/statx/
##       poll/epoll_ctl/kcmp/pidfd_getfd dup+release+rt-map + the self-test rt-map); procfs fd_link_target
##       resolves to /dev/snd/pcmC0D0{p,c}. openat path recognition via try_open_alsa_pcm() consulted by
##       BOTH open_common (absolute) and open_kernel_path_install (resolved-relative); openat2 routes
##       through sys_openat so it's covered too. read/write/poll currently EINVAL/not-ready — the actual
##       ioctl/mixer routing + POLLOUT readiness lands in commit 4. Built clean, clippy 17462, BOOT_OK
##       (both [alsa] ABI and [alsa_pcm] instance self-tests PASS).
##   [DONE commit 4] Per-open PCM state machine + ioctl/write routing to the mixer.
##       ipc/alsa_pcm.rs gained the transition API (all leaf-lock-safe — mixer touched only after the
##       table lock is dropped): hw_params (validate native via audio_alsa::mixer_accepts_directly,
##       open a mixer slot for playback, OPEN->SETUP), hw_free (release slot -> OPEN), prepare (->PREPARED,
##       clear ring), start (PREPARED->RUNNING), drop_stream/drain (->SETUP), pause (RUNNING<->PAUSED),
##       reset, write_frames (auto-start PREPARED->RUNNING, push via audio_mixer::write_pcm, advance
##       appl_ptr, EAGAIN/WouldBlock when the ring is full), writable/readable for poll. audio_alsa.rs
##       gained refine_to_native() (collapses a snd_pcm_hw_params config space onto RW-interleaved /
##       S16_LE / STD / 48k / stereo, fills cmask/info/msbits/exact-rate; period/buffer left free) +
##       SNDRV_PCM_IOCTL_HW_FREE + SUBFORMAT_STD + a refine self-test. audio_mixer.rs gained
##       space_available/writable/clear. syscall/linux.rs: sys_ioctl routes the 'A'-magic family to a new
##       alsa_pcm_ioctl() (PVERSION, INFO, HW_REFINE, HW_PARAMS, SW_PARAMS echo, HW_FREE, PREPARE/START/
##       DROP/DRAIN/RESET, PAUSE via int arg, WRITEI_FRAMES via snd_xferi; HWSYNC/RESUME/XRUN/UNLINK/
##       TTSTAMP accepted as no-ops); write(2)->dispatch_alsa_pcm_write (frame-aligned, ring-capped),
##       read(2)->dispatch_alsa_pcm_read (capture=silence, playback=EINVAL), POLLOUT/POLLIN readiness from
##       alsa_pcm::writable/readable. Helpers read_user_struct/write_user_struct (byte-copy, no user-ptr
##       alignment assumption) + write_cstr_field. Built clean, clippy 17462, BOOT_OK; the alsa_pcm
##       boot self-test now drives the full state machine (hw_params/prepare/write/pause/drain/start/drop/
##       hw_free + capture) and PASSES.
##   [DONE commit 4b] SYNC_PTR + READI_FRAMES position reporting. PcmStream gained boundary + avail_min
##       (stored by set_sw_params, called from the SW_PARAMS ioctl); sync_position(handle) returns a
##       PcmPosition { state, hw_ptr, appl_ptr, avail_min } with appl_ptr = frames submitted and
##       hw_ptr = appl_ptr - mixer-buffered frames, both reduced modulo boundary (leaf-lock-safe: the
##       mixer buffered() call is made after the table lock is dropped). set_appl_ptr/set_avail_min adopt
##       the values a client pushes via SYNC_PTR's !APPL/!AVAIL_MIN flags. read_frames() zero-fills a
##       capture buffer. audio_alsa.rs gained byte-exact snd_pcm_mmap_status (56) / snd_pcm_mmap_control
##       (16) / snd_pcm_sync_ptr (136, status+control each in a 64-byte union so the size is timestamp-ABI
##       independent) + the SYNC_PTR ioctl number (0xC0884123) + SYNC_PTR_HWSYNC/APPL/AVAIL_MIN flags, all
##       size-asserted in the ABI self-test. syscall/linux.rs: alsa_pcm_ioctl routes SYNC_PTR ->
##       alsa_pcm_ioctl_sync_ptr (push/pull appl_ptr+avail_min per flags, fill state+hw_ptr) and
##       READI_FRAMES -> alsa_pcm_ioctl_readi (validate capture, copy out silence); SW_PARAMS now stores the
##       boundary/avail_min instead of only echoing. Built clean, clippy 17462, BOOT_OK; the alsa_pcm
##       self-test now also checks the SYNC_PTR snapshot (appl=2/hw=0), appl_ptr/avail_min push-adopt, and
##       capture silence read.
##       [DEFERRED to commit 4c] STATUS / STATUS_EXT still return ENOTTY. Unlike SYNC_PTR, snd_pcm_status
##       embeds bare struct timespecs directly (not in a padded union), so its sizeof — and the ioctl number
##       — depends on the time64 vs legacy-timespec ABI. It is a convenience overlay (ALSA-lib gets the
##       pointers from SYNC_PTR), so deferring it does not block the playback hot path. See known-issues TD10.
##       Also note: only RW_INTERLEAVED access is advertised (mmap-based clients unsupported); non-native
##       formats/rates are rejected rather than converted (resampling/format conversion is future work).
##   [DONE commit 5a] control device pure ABI layer (audio_alsa_ctl.rs, no device nodes / no state / no
##       unsafe — mirrors PCM commits 1+2). 'U'-magic ioctl encoding + SNDRV_CTL_VERSION (2,0,9 = 0x20009);
##       byte-exact repr(C) structs snd_ctl_card_info (376) / snd_ctl_elem_id (64) / snd_ctl_elem_list (80) /
##       snd_ctl_elem_info (272) / snd_ctl_elem_value (1224) — the elem_info/value unions modelled by their
##       integer arm (leading i64 forces the 8-byte alignment + post-scalar pad, so the layout is exact);
##       ioctl numbers PVERSION 0x80045500 / CARD_INFO 0x81785501 / ELEM_LIST 0xC0505510 / ELEM_INFO
##       0xC1105511 / ELEM_READ 0xC4C85512 / ELEM_WRITE 0xC4C85513 (size-derived, asserted vs known Linux
##       hex); IFACE/TYPE/ACCESS constants; the card's element model (NUMID_MASTER_VOLUME=1 INTEGER 0..100,
##       NUMID_MASTER_SWITCH=2 BOOLEAN, ELEM_COUNT=2). Exhaustive self-test (sizes, alignments, ioctl encs,
##       version, constants) wired into boot after the PCM self-tests. Built clean, clippy 17462, BOOT_OK.
##   [DONE commit 5b] /dev/snd/controlC0 open interception + HandleKind::AlsaControl fd family. Added a
##       STATELESS AlsaControl fd kind (raw_handle = card 0, needs_kernel_close=false, no instance table, no
##       capability resource — a control fd is a pure window onto the global mixer, mirroring Console/PidFd).
##       try_open_alsa_control() intercepts the path before the VFS open in both open entry points (matching
##       try_open_alsa_pcm — the PCM nodes are also pure path-interception, not real devfs nodes). Threaded
##       the new variant through all ~25 match sites in linux.rs + procfs fd-link + the kcmp ord table:
##       read/write→EINVAL, lseek→ESPIPE, poll→never ready, fstat→S_IFCHR|0o660, fork-dup/close→no-op,
##       fcntl-lock→EBADF, cachestat→EOPNOTSUPP, /proc/self/fd→/dev/snd/controlC0. ioctl routed to a new
##       alsa_control_ioctl() implementing PVERSION + CARD_INFO (SlateOS card identity); ELEM_* return ENOTTY
##       for now. Built clean, clippy 17462, BOOT_OK (alsactl self-test PASSED).
##   [DONE commit 5c] control ioctl dispatch: ELEM_LIST/INFO/READ/WRITE for the two master elements.
##       Added pure card-model helpers to audio_alsa_ctl.rs (resolve_numid by numid OR iface+name,
##       elem_id_for, fill_elem_info) with self-test coverage; dispatch in linux.rs:
##         ELEM_LIST  -> writes up to `space` ids from `offset` into the user pids[] array, sets used/count;
##         ELEM_INFO  -> resolves id, fills type/access/count/range (vol INTEGER 0..100, switch BOOLEAN 0..1);
##         ELEM_READ  -> master_volume() / !is_master_muted() into value_integer[0];
##         ELEM_WRITE -> set_master_volume(clamp 0..100) / set_master_mute(value==0).
##       "Switch" is active-high unmute (1=on). Unknown elements -> ENOENT. Built clean, clippy 17462,
##       BOOT_OK (alsactl self-test PASSED). The ALSA control device is now fully functional for
##       card enumeration + master volume/mute, which is what alsamixer/amixer/PulseAudio drive.
##   DESIGN NOTE (clearly-correct default, not blocking): ALSA-direct kernel interface chosen over a
##   userspace PulseAudio-protocol daemon because PA/PipeWire/JACK/SDL/WINE all layer on ALSA — providing
##   ALSA gives the broadest compat with one interface. If the operator prefers a userspace PA-protocol
##   shim instead, this is reversible (the kernel ALSA nodes can coexist or be dropped).

## DONE (2026-06-13): PIE (ET_DYN) main-executable load basing — continues the Linux ABI loader
## Modern Linux executables are PIE (ET_DYN, segments often at vaddr 0). spawn currently loads the main
## executable at bias 0 and enters at the raw e_entry — for a PIE that maps the null page and gives wrong
## AT_PHDR/AT_ENTRY. Fix: load PIE executables at a fixed base and report runtime addresses. Test ELFs are
## ET_EXEC (is_pie()==false → bias 0), so they are unaffected; this is forward-looking infra like ld.so.
##   [DONE 69907f218 commit A] linux_stack: base_auxv()/install_linux_stack() gained exec_load_bias;
##       AT_ENTRY = e_entry+bias, AT_PHDR = phdr_vaddr+bias (saturating_add — sum already validated
##       <= USER_SPACE_END at segment-load). spawn build_linux_initial_stack forwards it; spawn+exec pass 0
##       for now (ET_EXEC bias is genuinely 0 → no behavior change). Self-test Test 9 verifies the deltas.
##   [DONE 0acccc20c commit B] spawn_process + exec: defined LINUX_PIE_BASE = 0x0000_5555_5555_4000 (well
##       below LINUX_INTERP_BASE=0x7000_0000_0000 and the stack; classic Linux no-ASLR PIE base, 16 KiB-
##       aligned). Both paths compute exec_load_bias = if elf.is_pie() { LINUX_PIE_BASE } else { 0 }, load
##       the executable via load_segments_with_bias(elf, pml4, exec_load_bias), bias the entry point with
##       checked_add, and pass exec_load_bias to build_linux_initial_stack so AT_ENTRY/AT_PHDR report
##       runtime addresses. ET_EXEC images unaffected (bias 0). Built clean, clippy 17462, BOOT_OK.
##   [REMAINING] End-to-end proof of PIE entry basing still wants a PIE variant of an argc-exit test ELF
##       (position-independent entry stub). Deferred as low-value vs. cost: commit-A Test 9 already proves
##       the auxv deltas equal the bias, and load_segments_with_bias is exercised by the interpreter loader
##       at LINUX_INTERP_BASE (same bias mechanism). A real glibc/musl PIE binary run will be the true test
##       once a userspace Linux binary is available to exec.
##
## DONE (2026-06-12): Linux dynamic-linker (ld.so) load path — roadmap 5.1 remaining item
## The Linux ABI loader currently handles STATIC executables only: spawn_process maps the
## executable's PT_LOAD segments and enters at its e_entry. A dynamically-linked Linux binary
## instead names a program interpreter (ld.so) in a PT_INTERP segment; the kernel must load THAT
## interpreter at a chosen base and enter ld.so's entry, passing the real program entry via AT_ENTRY
## and the interpreter base via AT_BASE. ld.so then maps shared libs and jumps to the program.
## Decomposed into small, individually-tested commits:
##   [DONE 0e706334] elf::ElfFile::interp_path() — extract+NUL-trim the PT_INTERP path (raw bytes,
##       never str). Returns None for static/malformed/empty. Boot self-tests: dynamic/static/empty.
##   [DONE 3b039e40] elf::load_segments_with_bias(elf,pml4,bias) — map every PT_LOAD at bias+p_vaddr,
##       re-validating against USER_SPACE_END. load_segments() now delegates with bias=0 (pure refactor;
##       init-process load path unchanged, boot still BOOT_OK). bias!=0 path lands with spawn wiring.
##   [DONE 7189a2c0] Plumb AT_BASE into the auxv: base_auxv()/install_linux_stack() gained Option<u64>
##       interp_base; build_linux_initial_stack() forwards it; when Some, push AuxEntry(AT_BASE, base).
##       AT_ENTRY stays the EXECUTABLE's e_entry. spawn+exec pass None for now. Self-test verifies AT_BASE
##       round-trips into the stack image when supplied and is absent otherwise. Boot still BOOT_OK.
##   [DONE commit 4] Wire spawn_process (+ exec): load_interpreter() reads elf.interp_path(), if Some
##       converts the bytes to &str (VFS read API is str-based; non-UTF-8 → fallback), reads the file via
##       Vfs::read_file, parses it, loads its PT_LOAD segments at LINUX_INTERP_BASE (0x0000_7000_0000_0000,
##       fixed — no ASLR yet) via load_segments_with_bias, and returns LoadedInterp{base, entry_rip=
##       base+interp.e_entry}. spawn_process/exec gate it on is_linux_abi/new_abi_mode==Linux: on Some they
##       enter the interpreter (UserEntryInfo.entry_rip / ExecResult.entry_rip = interp entry) and pass
##       Some(base) to build_linux_initial_stack (→ AT_BASE); AT_ENTRY stays the executable's e_entry.
##       DEFENSIVE FALLBACK: non-UTF-8 path / missing / unreadable / unparseable / unloadable interpreter
##       all log + return Ok(None) → enter the executable directly (today's behavior), so static and native
##       binaries are wholly unaffected and a missing loader degrades gracefully. Boot self-test
##       test_load_interpreter_fallbacks() (spawn.rs Test 21) covers the static-ELF and absent-interpreter
##       Ok(None) paths against a real freshly-created PML4; stamps "[spawn] load_interpreter fallbacks
##       (static + absent): OK". elf::build_dynamic_interp_test_elf() added as the test ELF builder.
##   [DONE 2026-06-13] END-TO-END interpreter EXECUTION now verified WITHOUT needing a real glibc/musl
##       ld.so: hand-crafted minimal interpreter ELF removes the blocker. elf::build_linux_interp_exit_elf()
##       builds an ET_DYN (PIE, p_vaddr=0/e_entry=0) "ld.so" stand-in that exit(42)s; elf::
##       build_linux_dynamic_exe_elf() builds an ET_EXEC with PT_INTERP + its own PT_LOAD that would
##       exit(7). proc::spawn::self_test_linux_dynamic_interp() (called post-VFS-init from main.rs, since it
##       needs a writable VFS) writes the interpreter to /slateos-test-ld.so, spawns the dynamic exe, and
##       asserts the child exits 42 (interpreter ran) not 7 (executable) — proving the kernel loaded the
##       interpreter at LINUX_INTERP_BASE and transferred control to ITS entry, and that the interpreter's
##       Linux exit() routed through dispatch_linux. Boot-verified: "loaded interpreter '/slateos-test-ld.so'
##       at base=0x700000000000, entry=0x700000000000" + "entered ld.so -> exit(42): OK".
##       [STILL REMAINING] Running a REAL glibc/musl ld.so (actual relocation processing, shared-lib
##       loading, TLS setup) still needs a real interpreter on the FS — that is a toolchain-port concern,
##       not a kernel-launch-path gap. The kernel side (base selection, biased segment mapping,
##       AT_BASE/AT_ENTRY auxv, ring-3 entry) is now verified end-to-end.
## DESIGN NOTES (Claude autonomous, clearly-correct defaults; revisit if operator disagrees):
##   - Interpreter base: fixed address (no ASLR yet). ASLR is a security enhancement, not required for
##     correctness; documenting rather than blocking.
##   - Interpreter path read as bytes via VFS (CLAUDE.md path rule), substring-matched for Linux loaders
##     only via detect_linux_abi already.

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 552 — getcpu: attempt both writes, accumulate EFAULT (match v6.6)
## Linux v6.6 kernel/sys.c SYSCALL_DEFINE3(getcpu) — verified verbatim against
## raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6:
##   int err = 0; int cpu = raw_smp_processor_id();
##   if (cpup)  err |= put_user(cpu, cpup);
##   if (nodep) err |= put_user(cpu_to_node(cpu), nodep);
##   return err ? -EFAULT : 0;
## DIVERGENCE FIXED: both put_user writes are ATTEMPTED independently and errors accumulated; a fault
## on cpup does NOT skip the nodep write. Pre-batch we returned EFAULT immediately on a failed cpup
## write, so getcpu(faulting_cpup, valid_nodep, NULL) left *nodep unwritten where Linux writes it (and
## still returns EFAULT). New code computes cpu_id + node_id(=0 on UMA), attempts each non-NULL write,
## sets a `faulted` flag on any validate/copy failure, and returns EFAULT at the end iff faulted (since
## put_user can only fail with EFAULT, any write error maps to EFAULT). Translator-only; no native API
## or smp changes.
## SELF-TEST: getcpu(&cpu,&node) writes both sentinels (cpu==current_cpu_index, node==0); getcpu(NULL,
## &node) writes node only. The partial-fault accumulation path is NOT boot-testable — a genuinely-
## faulting user pointer would be dereferenced under the kernel-context validate bypass and could fault
## the kernel — so only valid-pointer success paths are exercised. Stamp "getcpu both-writes
## accumulate-EFAULT (batch 552): OK".
## VERIFIED: build clean; clippy 17462 (<= baseline 17463); boot BOOT_OK, EXIT=0, stamp present.

## DEFERRED (2026-06-12): membarrier PRIVATE_EXPEDITED issue without prior REGISTER -> -EPERM (known-issues TD8)
## Linux v6.6 kernel/sched/membarrier.c membarrier_private_expedited(): when the issuing mm has not
## set the matching MEMBARRIER_STATE_*_READY bit (i.e. never called the corresponding ..._REGISTER_*
## command), it returns -EPERM, and that check runs BEFORE the single-CPU `return 0` shortcut. So even
## on our uniprocessor an unregistered PRIVATE_EXPEDITED issue should be -EPERM, not 0. We return 0.
## WHY DEFERRED: the READY bits live in mm->membarrier_state (per-mm, shared across the process's
## threads). A per-task map would be WORSE — it would reject a cross-thread issue (thread A registers,
## thread B issues) that Linux accepts. The correct home is a per-mm membarrier_state on the Process
## struct (kernel/src/proc/pcb.rs). But the boot self-test (our only harness) runs in kernel context
## where owner_process(current_task) == None, so the per-mm path cannot be exercised at boot. Chose to
## document rather than ship untested process-model code.
## PROPER FIX (when a userspace harness or boot owner-process shim exists): add per-mm
## membarrier_state: AtomicU32 to Process; REGISTER arms OR-in the READY/PREPARE bits; issue arms test
## the READY bit and return -EPERM when unset; GET_REGISTRATIONS reports the registered bitmask.
## The sys_membarrier REGISTER arm carries a matching "KNOWN DIVERGENCE (known-issues TD8)" comment.

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 551 — set_mempolicy_home_node: restore v6.6 gate order (home_node before len/end)
## Linux v6.6 mm/mempolicy.c SYSCALL_DEFINE4(set_mempolicy_home_node) — verified verbatim against
## raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6. Gate order:
##   if (start & ~PAGE_MASK) return -EINVAL;
##   if (flags != 0) return -EINVAL;
##   /* Check home_node is online to avoid accessing uninitialized NODE_DATA. */
##   if (home_node >= MAX_NUMNODES || !node_online(home_node)) return -EINVAL;   <-- BEFORE len/end
##   len = PAGE_ALIGN(len); end = start + len;
##   if (end < start) return -EINVAL;
##   if (end == start) return 0;
## REGRESSION FIXED: batch 240 mis-read the source and moved the home_node check AFTER the
## end==start short-circuit, so `set_mempolicy_home_node(start, len=0, home_node=99, 0)` returned 0
## instead of EINVAL. Batch 551 moves the home_node check back to position 3 (between flags and
## PAGE_ALIGN(len)), matching the literal upstream order. The batch-240 self-test that asserted the
## wrong behavior (len=0 + bad node -> 0) is corrected to expect EINVAL.
## KNOWN DIVERGENCE (NOT fixed, documented): on a valid non-empty range Linux walks the VMAs and
## returns -ENOENT (no explicit MPOL_BIND/MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY policy) or -EOPNOTSUPP (wrong mode);
## we return 0 because mbind is a UMA no-op with no per-VMA policy storage. We pick 0 (the common
## post-mbind success path) over -ENOENT. Logged as known-issues TD7 and open-questions Q1
## (per-VMA mempolicy storage is the only fully-faithful fix).
## VERIFIED: build clean; clippy 17462 (<= baseline 17463); boot self-test stamp
## "set_mempolicy_home_node home_node check before len/end gates (batch 551): OK".

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 550 — get_mempolicy: faithful do_get_mempolicy / copy_nodes_to_user rewrite
## Linux v6.6 mm/mempolicy.c kernel_get_mempolicy + do_get_mempolicy + copy_nodes_to_user
## (verified verbatim against raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6). Divergences fixed:
##   D1. kernel_get_mempolicy FIRST check: `if (nmask != NULL && maxnode < nr_node_ids) return -EINVAL;`
##       nr_node_ids == 1 on UMA, so a non-NULL nmask with maxnode == 0 -> EINVAL (we returned 0).
##       Runs BEFORE do_get_mempolicy's flags validation.
##   D2. do_get_mempolicy `else if (addr) return -EINVAL;` — an address passed WITHOUT MPOL_F_ADDR
##       is rejected (we ignored addr in that case).
##   D3. MPOL_F_NODE WITHOUT MPOL_F_ADDR -> -EINVAL for a default-policy task (the `else { err =
##       -EINVAL; }` arm; only INTERLEAVE current->mempolicy or the MPOL_F_ADDR/lookup_node path
##       succeed). We previously returned node 0 / ret 0 — wrong. With MPOL_F_ADDR it stays node 0.
##   D4. MPOL_F_MEMS_ALLOWED also writes *policy = 0 (kernel_get_mempolicy still runs
##       put_user(pval=0, policy) after do_get_mempolicy returns). We had skipped the mode write.
##   D5. nodemask copy: copy_nodes_to_user uses `copy = ALIGN(maxnode-1,64)/8`, caps at
##       `copy > PAGE_SIZE` (== maxnode-1 > 32768, NOT the old 1<<23 cap), zero-fills bytes above
##       the 8-byte kernel mask, and is called AFTER *policy is written (so an over-cap maxnode
##       still updates *policy before returning EINVAL). Our single zeroed buffer of `copy` bytes
##       (byte0 = node-0 presence, rest 0) reproduces clear_user(upper)+copy_to_user(low8) exactly.
## VERIFIED: build clean; clippy 17462 (<= baseline 17463 — the rewrite dropped two old closure
## warnings); boot self-test stamp "get_mempolicy faithful do_get_mempolicy/copy_nodes_to_user
## (batch 550): OK" with 11 cases (incl. new F_NODE-alone EINVAL, F_NODE|F_ADDR node 0, addr-without-
## F_ADDR EINVAL, nmask+maxnode==0 EINVAL, over-cap EINVAL). NOTE: new asserts use
## i64::from(errno::X).wrapping_neg() — `-i64::from(..)` trips DENY-adjacent arithmetic_side_effects.

## DONE (2026-06-12): hrtimer self-test Test 2 — close interrupt-window race vs APIC ISR (known-issues F11)
## Test 2 scheduled a 0-delay timer then called process_expired() manually with interrupts ENABLED.
## The periodic APIC timer ISR also calls process_expired(); if it fired in the window between
## schedule_ns and the manual drain, the ISR drained the 0-delay timer first -> manual call returned
## n==0 -> assert!(n >= 1) panicked, intermittently blocking the boot gate for ANY batch.
## Fix: wrap schedule_ns(0,..) + process_expired() in crate::cpu::without_interrupts(|| {..}) so the
## manual drain is deterministic. Test-only fix; hrtimer subsystem was already correct.

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 549 — move_pages: per-page bad-node status = -ENODEV (was -EINVAL), matching do_pages_move (closes TD6)
## Linux v6.6 mm/migrate.c do_pages_move per-page node validation (verified verbatim against raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6):
##   err = -ENODEV;
##   if (node < 0 || node >= MAX_NUMNODES) goto out_flush;     // out of range -> -ENODEV
##   if (!node_state(node, N_MEMORY))      goto out_flush;     // node has no memory -> -ENODEV
##   err = -EACCES;
##   if (!node_isset(node, task_nodes))    goto out_flush;     // valid+online but disallowed -> -EACCES
##   ... add_page_for_migration(...); store_status(status, i, err ? : current_node, 1);
## On our single-node UMA box: task_nodes = mems_allowed = {0}; node 0 is the ONLY node with N_MEMORY and is always
##   in task_nodes.  So target 0 -> status 0 (page already there); EVERY other target (negative, >= MAX_NUMNODES, or
##   in-range-but-no-memory like node 1) fails the range/N_MEMORY check and stores -ENODEV.  The -EACCES branch is
##   UNREACHABLE on a single-node box: it requires a node that HAS memory yet is not in task_nodes, impossible when
##   only node 0 has memory and node 0 is always allowed.
## DIVERGENCE (pre-batch, batch-105 stub): sys_move_pages stored status[i] = -EINVAL for any non-zero target node.
##   -EINVAL is not a value do_pages_move ever stores for a bad node; the correct UMA answer is -ENODEV.
## FIX (translator-only): per-entry decision now `want == 0 ? 0 : -ENODEV` (errno::ENODEV.wrapping_neg()).  Updated
##   the function doc bullet and batch-105 self-test Case 4: status [0,-EINVAL,0] -> [0,-ENODEV,0].
## SELF-TEST (boot, dispatch_linux) stamp:
##   "move_pages per-page bad-node status=-ENODEV (batch 549): OK"
## VERIFIED: build clean; clippy 17463 (== batch 548; the two `-errno::EINVAL` arithmetic warnings became
##   `.wrapping_neg()`); boot-test BOOT_OK + stamp in serial-test.txt + EXIT=0, no SELF-TEST FAILURE.
## NOTE: used errno::ENODEV.wrapping_neg() (NOT i32::from(...).wrapping_neg()) — errno consts are already i32, so
##   i32::from() trips clippy::useless_conversion, which is DENY-level (an error, not a warning).  Remember this for
##   future i32-status asserts: only i64 comparisons (r.value) need i64::from(errno::X).wrapping_neg().
## TD6 (known-issues.md) is now RESOLVED by this batch.

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 548 — move_pages: add missing pid->ESRCH lookup (find_mm_struct); remove invented E2BIG count cap
## Linux v6.6 mm/migrate.c (verified verbatim against raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6):
##   kernel_move_pages(pid, nr_pages, pages, nodes, status, flags):
##       if (flags & ~(MPOL_MF_MOVE|MPOL_MF_MOVE_ALL)) return -EINVAL;
##       if ((flags & MPOL_MF_MOVE_ALL) && !capable(CAP_SYS_NICE)) return -EPERM;
##       mm = find_mm_struct(pid, &task_nodes); if (IS_ERR(mm)) return PTR_ERR(mm);
##       if (nodes) err = do_pages_move(...); else err = do_pages_stat(...); mmput(mm); return err;
##   find_mm_struct(pid, *mem_nodes):
##       if (!pid) { mmget(current->mm); *mem_nodes = cpuset_mems_allowed(current); return current->mm; }
##       task = find_task_by_vpid(pid); if (!task) return ERR_PTR(-ESRCH);
##       if (!ptrace_may_access(task, PTRACE_MODE_READ_REALCREDS)) mm = ERR_PTR(-EPERM); goto out;
##       mm = ERR_PTR(security_task_movememory(task)); if (IS_ERR(mm)) goto out;
##       *mem_nodes = cpuset_mems_allowed(task); mm = get_task_mm(task);
##       out: put_task_struct(task); if (!mm) mm = ERR_PTR(-EINVAL); return mm;
##   There is NO nr_pages limit ANYWHERE in kernel_move_pages / do_pages_move / do_pages_stat (both do_pages_*
##   chunk through `while (nr_pages)` 16 at a time and return 0 for nr_pages==0 without touching pages/status).
## DIVERGENCES (pre-batch sys_move_pages), both now fixed:
##   (a) NO pid lookup at all.  find_mm_struct's ESRCH (unknown/negative pid -> find_task_by_vpid NULL) was missing;
##       a foreign pid fell through to the pages/status validation and returned 0 / EFAULT instead of ESRCH.  The
##       lookup belongs AFTER the MOVE_ALL EPERM gate and BEFORE the count==0 shortcut + pages handling, so even
##       count==0 with an unknown pid is ESRCH (same class of bug fixed for migrate_pages in batch 547).
##   (b) INVENTED `count > (1<<20) -> E2BIG` cap.  No such limit exists in Linux; removed.  A large count now flows
##       to the pages/status validation (NULL -> EFAULT; unmapped -> EFAULT) exactly as Linux's chunked do_pages_*.
## FIX (translator-only): inserted the pid!=0 && pid!=current -> ESRCH gate (pid==0 = self, no permission check),
##   removed the E2BIG check, kept the count==0 -> 0 shortcut (now AFTER the pid lookup), kept the MOVE_ALL EPERM gate
##   ahead of everything.  ptrace -EPERM / !mm -EINVAL are documented as the slots for a future real task model.
## SELF-TEST (boot, dispatch_linux) stamp:
##   "move_pages MOVE_ALL EPERM / pid-ESRCH (no E2BIG cap) (batch 548): OK"
##   Updated batch-105 cases: Case 5 (large count, NULL pages) E2BIG->EFAULT; Case 8 now (large count, FOREIGN pid,
##   MOVE_ALL) -> EPERM (proves step-2 gate beats step-3 ESRCH and step-4 EFAULT); added Case 10 (foreign pid,
##   count=0) -> ESRCH and Case 11 (negative pid) -> ESRCH.  NUMA-block duplicates: huge-count test E2BIG->ESRCH
##   (foreign pid drives it); count==0 test switched to self pid (pid==0) so it still returns 0.  Cases 1-4/6/7/9
##   (all self pid) unchanged.
## FOLLOW-UP (tracked, NOT done — needs its own verification):

##     -EACCES (!node_isset in task_nodes), NOT the -EINVAL we currently store (sys_move_pages Case 4, status

##     non-zero target node, mirroring do_pages_move's `err = -ENODEV; if (!node_state(node, N_MEMORY)) store;`
##     (the -EACCES branch is unreachable on a single-node box since only node 0 has N_MEMORY). See

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 547 — migrate_pages: convert to get_nodes_uma; faithful EPERM/EINVAL/ESRCH split (kernel_migrate_pages order)
## Linux v6.6 mm/mempolicy.c kernel_migrate_pages (verified verbatim against raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6):
##   err = get_nodes(old, old_nodes, maxnode); if (err) goto out;     // OLD mask first
##   err = get_nodes(new, new_nodes, maxnode); if (err) goto out;     // NEW mask second (same maxnode)
##   task = pid ? find_task_by_vpid(pid) : current;
##   if (!task) { err = -ESRCH; goto out; }                           // unknown/neg pid => ESRCH
##   err = -EINVAL;                                                   // default once task found
##   if (!ptrace_may_access(task, PTRACE_MODE_READ_REALCREDS)) { err = -EPERM; goto out_put; }
##   task_nodes = cpuset_mems_allowed(task);                          // = {0} on UMA
##   if (!nodes_subset(*new, task_nodes) && !capable(CAP_SYS_NICE)) { err = -EPERM; goto out_put; }
##   nodes_and(*new, *new, cpuset_mems_allowed(current));             // ∩ {0}
##   if (nodes_empty(*new)) goto out_put;                             // err still -EINVAL
##   ... security_task_movememory, get_task_mm, do_migrate_pages -> 0
## get_nodes (shared with mbind/set_mempolicy via get_nodes_uma): --maxnode; nodes_clear; if(maxnode==0||!nmask) return 0
##   (NULL mask => empty + SUCCESS, NOT -EFAULT); if(maxnode>32768) -EINVAL; copy/EFAULT.
## DIVERGENCES (pre-batch sys_migrate_pages had its own inline logic), all now fixed:
##   (a) cap was maxnode > (1<<23)=8388608; Linux caps (after --maxnode) at 32768.  Now via get_nodes_uma.
##   (b) `maxnode == 0 -> EINVAL` was UNCONDITIONAL; Linux only EINVALs maxnode==0 with a NON-NULL mask (the --maxnode
##       underflow over-caps); with a NULL mask, maxnode==0 is empty SUCCESS and the pid lookup runs (=> ESRCH for
##       unknown pid).  Now faithful.
##   (c) `arg2==0 || arg3==0 -> EFAULT` rejected NULL masks; Linux treats a NULL mask as an empty set + success.
##   (d) new-mask handling `(new[0]&1)==0 -> EINVAL` CONFLATED Linux's two distinct gates:
##       new names a node other than 0 (not subset of {0}) + no CAP_SYS_NICE -> -EPERM (checked FIRST);
##       new intersects {0} to empty (i.e. new was empty) -> -EINVAL (checked AFTER).
## FIX (translator-only): sys_migrate_pages now calls get_nodes_uma(arg2, maxnode) then get_nodes_uma(arg3, maxnode)
##   (old then new), keeps the pid!=0 && pid!=current -> ESRCH lookup, then `if new_extra -> EPERM; if new_empty -> EINVAL`.
##   Old-mask bits are irrelevant on UMA (do_migrate_pages source->dest map is a single-node no-op): `_old_*` unused.
##   The inline maxnode cap / NULL-EFAULT / double copy_from_user / mask[0] indexing block was removed.
## SELF-TEST (boot, dispatch_linux) stamp:
##   "migrate_pages get_nodes(old,new)/pid-ESRCH/new-subset EPERM (batch 547): OK"
##   Updated cases in the batch-104 block: Case 3 ({0},{1}) EINVAL->EPERM (new not subset of {0}, no CAP_SYS_NICE);
##   Case 5 (NULL old,{0}) EFAULT->0 (NULL = empty success); Case 9 (neg pid, NULL old) EFAULT->ESRCH (get_nodes ok,
##   pid lookup drives result).  Cases 1/2/4/6/7/8 unchanged.  The duplicate maxnode=0 tests (NUMA block) were given a
##   NON-NULL mask so they still exercise the --maxnode underflow -> EINVAL path (with NULL masks they'd now be ESRCH).
## NOTE (capability model): EPERM for new_extra assumes no CAP_SYS_NICE in the dispatch context (the unprivileged ABI
##   surface; caller_pid() is None at boot).  When a real privilege model lands, a CAP_SYS_NICE holder should instead
##   reach the nodes_and/nodes_empty gate (new_extra then maps to EINVAL, not EPERM) — slot the capable() check there.
## VERIFIED: build clean; clippy 17466 (DOWN 6 from batch-546's 17472 — removed the inline mask[0] indexing + two
##   double-copy validate/copy blocks, and the changed asserts use i64::from(errno).wrapping_neg()); boot-test BOOT_OK
##   + stamp in serial-test.txt + EXIT=0, no SELF-TEST FAILURE.

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 546 — mbind/set_mempolicy: get_nodes + mpol_new fidelity (NULL-mask=empty, 32768-bit cap, per-mode/flag emptiness rules, do_mbind ordering)
## Linux v6.6 mm/mempolicy.c (verified verbatim against raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6):
##   get_nodes(nodemask_t*, const unsigned long __user *nmask, unsigned long maxnode):
##       --maxnode;
##       nodes_clear(*nodes);
##       if (maxnode == 0 || !nmask) return 0;               // NULL mask => empty + SUCCESS (not EFAULT)
##       if (maxnode > PAGE_SIZE*BITS_PER_BYTE) return -EINVAL;  // x86_64 ABI: 4096*8 = 32768
##       ... get_bitmap (masks final long to maxnode bits); EFAULT if unreadable; bits above MAX_NUMNODES nonzero => EINVAL
##   mpol_new(mode, flags, nodes):
##       DEFAULT:        !nodes_empty => EINVAL
##       PREFERRED:      empty => (STATIC|RELATIVE ? EINVAL : becomes LOCAL); non-empty ok
##       LOCAL:          !empty || STATIC || RELATIVE => EINVAL
##       PREFERRED_MANY/BIND/INTERLEAVE: empty => EINVAL  (the trailing `else if (nodes_empty) return -EINVAL`)
##   kernel_mbind: sanitize_mpol_flags -> get_nodes -> do_mbind{flags&~VALID->EINVAL; MOVE_ALL&&!CAP_SYS_NICE->EPERM;
##       start&~PAGE_MASK->EINVAL; end<start->EINVAL; end==start->return 0; mpol_new(...)}
##   kernel_set_mempolicy: sanitize_mpol_flags -> get_nodes -> do_set_mempolicy -> mpol_new
## DIVERGENCES (pre-batch), all now fixed:
##   (a) NULL nmask with maxnode>0 returned -EFAULT in both syscalls; Linux returns the per-mode result of an
##       EMPTY mask (DEFAULT/LOCAL/PREFERRED -> 0; BIND/INTERLEAVE/PREFERRED_MANY -> EINVAL).
##       => mbind self-test Case 9 corrected EFAULT->EINVAL; new set_mempolicy(DEFAULT,NULL,64)->0 and (BIND,NULL,64)->EINVAL.
##   (b) nodemask bit cap was maxnode>(1<<23)=8388608; Linux caps (after --maxnode) at 32768.  Over-cap maxnode
##       now -EINVAL BEFORE any user read (avoids a multi-KiB over-read of a tiny user buffer).
##   (c) MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY with an empty mask was ACCEPTED; Linux requires non-empty -> EINVAL.
##   (d) MPOL_PREFERRED empty + (STATIC|RELATIVE) and MPOL_LOCAL + (STATIC|RELATIVE) were ACCEPTED; Linux -> EINVAL.
##   (e) mbind ordering: get_nodes (cap/EFAULT) now runs BEFORE the MOVE_ALL->EPERM and addr-align checks,
##       matching kernel_mbind; previously addr/EPERM could preempt the get_nodes EINVAL/EFAULT.
## FIX (translator-only): added two shared verbatim helpers next to sanitize_mpol_flags:
##   - get_nodes_uma(nmask, maxnode) -> Result<(mask_empty, has_extra_bits), errno>  (the --maxnode/cap/NULL/EFAULT logic)
##   - mpol_new_check(mode, flags, mask_empty) -> Result<(), errno>                  (the per-mode/flag emptiness rules)
##   Both sys_mbind and sys_set_mempolicy were restructured to the verbatim kernel_* order using these helpers.
##   The inline duplicated nodemask-reading blocks (with the wrong cap and the mask[0] indexing) were removed.
## SELF-TEST (boot, dispatch_linux) stamp:
##   "get_nodes NULL-mask=empty + 32768-bit cap + mpol_new PREFERRED_MANY/STATIC/RELATIVE rules (batch 546): OK"
##   Covers: set_mempolicy PREFERRED_MANY empty->EINVAL / {0}->0; DEFAULT,NULL,64->0; BIND,NULL,64->EINVAL;
##   PREFERRED|STATIC empty->EINVAL; LOCAL|STATIC->EINVAL; BIND,{0},40000->EINVAL(cap); mbind PREFERRED_MANY empty->EINVAL;
##   plus the corrected mbind Case 9 (NULL,64 -> EINVAL).
## VERIFIED: build clean; clippy 17472 (DOWN 3 from baseline 17475 — removed two inline mask[0] indexings, helper uses
##   .first()/.last_mut(); no new warnings); boot-test BOOT_OK + stamp in serial-test.txt + EXIT=0, no SELF-TEST FAILURE.
## FOLLOW-UPS (tracked, not done this batch):
##   * [DONE in batch 547] sys_migrate_pages converted to get_nodes_uma (see batch-547 entry above).
##   * APPROXIMATION (see known-issues.md): get_nodes_uma collapses "any node other than 0" to EINVAL, so a mask of
##     {0, N} (node 0 PLUS a non-existent node) is rejected, whereas Linux intersects with mems_allowed={0} and would
##     ACCEPT it for PREFERRED/BIND/INTERLEAVE (effective mask = {0}).  Real programs don't pass such masks on a
##     single-node box; documented rather than implemented (would need full per-mode intersection semantics).

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 545 — mbind/set_mempolicy: sanitize_mpol_flags fidelity (NUMA_BALANCING flag bit, static/relative mutual exclusion, numa-balancing-requires-BIND)
## Linux v6.6 mm/mempolicy.c (verified verbatim against raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6):
##   #define MPOL_F_STATIC_NODES   (1 << 15)
##   #define MPOL_F_RELATIVE_NODES (1 << 14)
##   #define MPOL_F_NUMA_BALANCING (1 << 13)
##   #define MPOL_MODE_FLAGS (MPOL_F_STATIC_NODES | MPOL_F_RELATIVE_NODES | MPOL_F_NUMA_BALANCING)
##   MPOL_MAX = 6  (DEFAULT 0, PREFERRED 1, BIND 2, INTERLEAVE 3, LOCAL 4, PREFERRED_MANY 5)
##   static inline int sanitize_mpol_flags(int *mode, unsigned short *flags) {
##       *flags = *mode & MPOL_MODE_FLAGS;
##       *mode &= ~MPOL_MODE_FLAGS;
##       if ((unsigned int)(*mode) >= MPOL_MAX)                      return -EINVAL;
##       if ((*flags & MPOL_F_STATIC_NODES) && (*flags & MPOL_F_RELATIVE_NODES)) return -EINVAL;
##       if (*flags & MPOL_F_NUMA_BALANCING) {
##           if (*mode != MPOL_BIND) return -EINVAL;
##           *flags |= (MPOL_F_MOF | MPOL_F_MORON);
##       }
##       return 0;
##   }
##   Both kernel_mbind() and kernel_set_mempolicy() call sanitize_mpol_flags() before get_nodes()/nodemask handling.
## DIVERGENCE (pre-batch): our MPOL_MODE_FLAGS was (1<<14)|(1<<15) — it OMITTED MPOL_F_NUMA_BALANCING
##   (bit 13).  The two syscalls used a single combined gate:
##       if ((mode & 0x7) > 5 || (mode & ~(0x7 | MPOL_MODE_FLAGS)) != 0) return -EINVAL;
##   Consequences:
##     (a) MPOL_BIND | MPOL_F_NUMA_BALANCING (0x2002) — Linux ACCEPTS (mode==BIND); we returned EINVAL
##         because bit 13 read as a stray high bit.
##     (b) MPOL_BIND | MPOL_F_STATIC_NODES | MPOL_F_RELATIVE_NODES (0xC002) — Linux returns EINVAL
##         (mutual exclusion); we ACCEPTED it (both flag bits were in the allowed mask, no exclusion check).
##     (c) MPOL_INTERLEAVE | MPOL_F_NUMA_BALANCING — Linux returns EINVAL (NUMA_BALANCING demands BIND);
##         after fixing (a) this would have been wrongly accepted without the dedicated rule.
## FIX (translator-only): added a shared `sanitize_mpol_flags(mode_raw) -> Result<(mode, flags), errno>`
##   helper (verbatim port) just above sys_mbind, and replaced the combined ad-hoc gate in BOTH
##   sys_mbind (arg2) and sys_set_mempolicy (arg0) with a call to it.  MPOL_MODE_FLAGS now
##   (1<<13)|(1<<14)|(1<<15); the residual-mode >= MPOL_MAX, static/relative mutual-exclusion, and
##   numa-balancing-requires-BIND rules are enforced in Linux's exact order.  mode returned by the
##   helper is `mode_raw & ~MPOL_MODE_FLAGS`, which sanitize guarantees is in 0..=5, so the downstream
##   per-mode emptiness match arms are unaffected.  (MPOL_F_MOF|MPOL_F_MORON augmentation is observable
##   only via policy enforcement, which we do not implement; the accept/reject decision now matches Linux.)
## SELF-TEST (boot, dispatch_linux): set_mempolicy(BIND|NUMA_BALANCING,{0})->0; (INTERLEAVE|NUMA_BALANCING)->EINVAL;
##   (BIND|STATIC|RELATIVE)->EINVAL; and the same three for mbind over a 4 KiB range.  Stamp:
##   "sanitize_mpol_flags NUMA_BALANCING bit + static/relative mutual-exclusion + numa-balancing-requires-BIND (batch 545): OK"
## VERIFIED: build clean; clippy 17475 (baseline, new asserts use i64::from(errno::EINVAL).wrapping_neg());
##   boot-test BOOT_OK + stamp in serial-test.txt + EXIT=0, no SELF-TEST FAILURE.
## RESOLVED (2026-06-13, verified already-fixed): get_nodes() maxnode cap divergence. The fix lives in
##   `get_nodes_uma` (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~25602): it does `let maxnode = maxnode_arg.wrapping_sub(1)`
##   (the C `--maxnode`, with unsigned wraparound so maxnode==0 -> u64::MAX fails the cap), then
##   `if maxnode > NODE_BITS_CAP` where `NODE_BITS_CAP = 4096*8 = 32768` = PAGE_SIZE*BITS_PER_BYTE using the
##   Linux ABI's 4 KiB page (NOT our 16 KiB frame), matching mm/mempolicy.c get_nodes() bit-for-bit. The
##   PAGE_SIZE "murkiness" is resolved the same way sys_mbind resolves addr alignment: honour the ABI value.
##   sys_get_mempolicy carries an identical NODE_BITS_CAP=32768 with `(maxnode-1) > cap`. No standalone batch
##   was needed — the cap was already correct when this note was revisited.

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 544 — semop/semtimedop: E2BIG/EINVAL structural gates precede copy_from_user(sops); nsops unsigned-int truncation
## Linux v6.6 ipc/sem.c (verified verbatim against raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6):
##   SYSCALL_DEFINE3(semop)  = do_semtimedop(semid, tsops, nsops, NULL)
##   SYSCALL_DEFINE4(semtimedop) -> ksys_semtimedop:
##       if(timeout){ if(get_timespec64(&ts,timeout)) return -EFAULT; return do_semtimedop(...,&ts);} ...
##       (get_timespec64 only COPIES 16 bytes; it does NOT range-check tv_sec/tv_nsec.)
##   do_semtimedop():
##       if (nsops > ns->sc_semopm) return -E2BIG;     // E2BIG FIRST
##       if (nsops < 1)            return -EINVAL;      // then nsops<1
##       if (nsops > SEMOPM_FAST) sops = kvmalloc_array(...);
##       if (copy_from_user(sops, tsops, nsops*sizeof(*tsops))) { ret=-EFAULT; goto out_free; }  // AFTER the gates
##       ret = __do_semtimedop(semid, sops, nsops, timeout, ns);
##   __do_semtimedop(): if(timeout){ tv_sec<0||tv_nsec<0||tv_nsec>=1e9 -> -EINVAL }  // after sops copy
##                      sma = sem_obtain_object_check(ns, semid); IS_ERR -> -EINVAL  // semid validated HERE
##   nsops is C `unsigned int` -> x86_64 ABI delivers only the low 32 bits.
## DIVERGENCE: batch 204's model was BACKWARDS. It (a) ran the sops-pointer EFAULT check BEFORE the
##   E2BIG / nsops<1 EINVAL gates, so (sops=NULL, nsops=501/600) returned EFAULT where Linux returns
##   E2BIG; (b) compared nsops at full 64-bit width, so (nsops=0x1_0000_0000) returned E2BIG where
##   Linux's truncated nsops=0 returns EINVAL; (c) checked semid<0 up front, though Linux defers semid
##   validation to __do_semtimedop AFTER the sops copy.  (The batch-204 doc comment literally claimed
##   "copy_from_user runs BEFORE the structural gates" — the verbatim source shows the opposite.)
## FIX (translator-only, kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
##   - sys_semop (~20145): reordered to nsops>SEMOPM E2BIG -> nsops==0 EINVAL -> sops(arg1==0)EFAULT +
##     validate_user_read -> terminal EINVAL; nsops now `args.arg2 as u32`; dropped up-front semid<0.
##   - sys_semtimedop (~20277): timeout-readability EFAULT -> E2BIG -> nsops==0 EINVAL -> sops EFAULT ->
##     timeout content EINVAL (validate_user_timespec64) -> terminal EINVAL; nsops u32; dropped semid<0.
##   - Rewrote both doc comments to the verbatim 4-step / 6-step v6.6 orders.
##   Self-tests (kernel ~59947+): flipped semop(sops=NULL,nsops=501) EFAULT->E2BIG; added nsops
##   truncation probes (0x1_0000_0000 -> EINVAL for both semop and semtimedop) and a semtimedop
##   (sops=NULL,nsops=600)->E2BIG E2BIG-before-copy probe; stamp "sem(timed)op E2BIG-before-sops-copy
##   + nsops unsigned-int truncation (batch 544): OK".  Existing semid=-1 / timespec64-content tests
##   still pass under the corrected order.
## SEMOPM kept at 500 (ns->sc_semopm default; unchanged).
## VERIFIED: cargo build clean; clippy 17475 (unchanged baseline; new asserts use
##   i64::from(errno::X).wrapping_neg()); boot-test BOOT_OK EXIT=0, batch-544 stamp present in
##   build/serial-test.txt, no SELF-TEST FAILURE.

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 543 — mq_timedsend/mq_timedreceive: msg buffer untouched before fdget (EBADF preempts EFAULT)
## Linux v6.6 ipc/mqueue.c (verified verbatim against raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6):
##   SYSCALL_DEFINE5(mq_timedsend): if(u_abs_timeout){prepare_timeout(...)}  // EFAULT then EINVAL
##   prepare_timeout(): if(get_timespec64()) -EFAULT; if(!timespec64_valid()) -EINVAL;
##   do_mq_timedsend() gate order:
##     1. if(msg_prio >= MQ_PRIO_MAX) -EINVAL;
##     2. (timeout already converted)
##     3. f = fdget(mqdes); if(!f.file) -EBADF;
##     4. if(f.file->f_op != &mqueue_file_operations) -EBADF;   // fd valid but not a queue
##     5. if(!(f.file->f_mode & FMODE_WRITE)) -EBADF;
##     6. msg_ptr = load_msg(u_msg_ptr, msg_len); if(IS_ERR) ...  // FIRST msg-buffer touch -> EFAULT
##   do_mq_timedreceive(): timeout -> fdget EBADF -> f_op EBADF -> FMODE_READ EBADF ->
##                         msg_len<mq_msgsize EMSGSIZE -> store_msg EFAULT -> prio write-back EFAULT.
## DIVERGENCE: our translator validated the msg pointer (EFAULT) BEFORE the fd lookup, so a
##   bad fd + unreadable/unwritable buffer returned EFAULT where Linux returns EBADF.  The
##   userspace buffer is only touched by load_msg/store_msg at the LAST step, after fdget plus
##   the f_op / FMODE gates (all EBADF for any non-mqueue fd).  We never model a real message
##   queue, so every fd that survives validate_linux_fd is by definition not a queue and fails
##   Linux's f_op check with EBADF before the buffer copy ever runs.
## FIX (translator-only, kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
##   - sys_mq_timedsend (~20691): removed the premature `arg1==0 -> EFAULT` + validate_user_read
##     msg block; kept timeout -> msg_prio -> validate_linux_fd -> terminal EBADF.
##   - sys_mq_timedreceive (~20770): removed the premature validate_user_write (msg buffer) and
##     validate_user_write (prio pointer) blocks; kept timeout -> validate_linux_fd -> EBADF.
##   - Updated both doc comments to the full 6-step / 5-step v6.6 gate orders.
##   Self-tests updated (kernel ~60429+): mq_timedsend(NULL)/(prio=32767,NULL) and the three
##   unsigned-int truncation cases (a,b,d) now expect EBADF not EFAULT; (c) prio>=MAX stays
##   EINVAL (prio gate precedes fdget); mq_timedreceive(NULL) now EBADF.  Added a cross-check
##   block: NULL buffer + VALID timespec -> EBADF for both send and receive (proves the buffer
##   is never inspected ahead of fdget) with stamp "mq_timed{send,receive} msg buffer untouched
##   before fdget (batch 543): OK".
## VERIFIED: cargo build clean; clippy 17475 (unchanged baseline; new asserts use
##   i64::from(errno::EBADF).wrapping_neg() to avoid arithmetic_side_effects); boot-test BOOT_OK
##   EXIT=0, batch-543 stamp present in build/serial-test.txt, no SELF-TEST FAILURE.

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 542 — io_pgetevents timeout/sigset gate order + NULL-mask sigsetsize short-circuit
## Linux v6.6 fs/aio.c SYSCALL_DEFINE6(io_pgetevents) + kernel/signal.c set_user_sigmask (verified
## verbatim against raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6):
##   io_pgetevents: if(timeout && get_timespec64(&ts,timeout)) return -EFAULT;
##                  if(usig && copy_from_user(&ksig,usig,16)) return -EFAULT;
##                  ret=set_user_sigmask(ksig.sigmask, ksig.sigsetsize); if(ret) return ret;
##                  ret=do_io_getevents(ctx_id, min_nr, nr, events, timeout?&ts:NULL);
##   set_user_sigmask: if(!umask) return 0;  // NULL mask: NO further checks
##                     if(sigsetsize != sizeof(sigset_t)) return -EINVAL;  // 8 on x86_64
##                     if(copy_from_user(&kmask,umask,8)) return -EFAULT;
##   do_io_getevents -> lookup_ioctx -> -EINVAL (no AIO ctx ever exists here = terminal); the min_nr/nr
##     ordering check and the events write live in read_events, reached only AFTER a valid ctx.
## Pre-batch sys_io_pgetevents had TWO real divergences: (a) it ran the min_nr/nr -> EINVAL check and
## the events write validation FIRST, before even reading timeout — so a probe with (min_nr>nr,
## timeout=BADPTR) saw EINVAL where Linux returns EFAULT (get_timespec64 runs first); (b) it checked
## sigsetsize != 8 -> EINVAL even when sigmask was NULL, but set_user_sigmask early-returns 0 on a NULL
## mask BEFORE the size check, so a NULL mask with a bogus sigsetsize must fall through, not EINVAL.
## Fix (translator-only, kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_io_pgetevents ~32757): reorder to
##   timeout EFAULT -> usig copy EFAULT -> [sigmask!=0 ? (sigsetsize!=8 EINVAL -> sigmask EFAULT) : skip]
##   -> terminal EINVAL. Removed the leading min_nr/nr and events gates (unreachable; collapse to terminal).
## VERIFIED: at boot all paths surface EINVAL (the size-gate value equals the terminal) and EFAULT is
## bypassed in kernel context, so the dispatch tests prove which path is taken (premise) rather than
## distinguishing by errno. Updated the self-tests: the NULL-mask sigsetsize cases now document the
## short-circuit-to-terminal semantics, and a non-NULL-mask case exercises the real sigsetsize gate.
## Stamp "io_pgetevents timeout/sigset order + NULL-mask short-circuit (batch 542): OK". cargo build
## clean; clippy 17475 (DOWN 2 from 17477 — removing the old min_nr/nr cast+arithmetic code dropped two
## warnings; new baseline 17475); boot-test BOOT_OK 27s EXIT=0.

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 541 — vmsplice fdget/vmsplice_type EBADF before import_iovec (gate reorder)
## Linux v6.6 fs/splice.c SYSCALL_DEFINE4(vmsplice) (verified verbatim against
## raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6) + vmsplice_type() + import_iovec/iovec_from_user:
##   1. if (flags & ~SPLICE_F_ALL) return -EINVAL;
##   2. f = fdget(fd); vmsplice_type(f,&type):
##        if (!f.file) return -EBADF;
##        else if (f_mode & FMODE_WRITE) type=ITER_SOURCE;
##        else if (f_mode & FMODE_READ)  type=ITER_DEST;
##        else return -EBADF;   // O_PATH: neither read nor write
##   3. import_iovec -> iovec_from_user:
##        if (nr_segs == 0) -> 0-count iter -> success 0;
##        if (nr_segs > UIO_MAXIOV) return -EINVAL;
##        copy_iovec_from_user -> -EFAULT;
##   4. transfer (vmsplice_to_pipe/_to_user) — unimplemented -> EINVAL.
## Pre-batch sys_vmsplice ran the nr_segs gates (>MAXIOV EINVAL, ==0 success) and the iov EFAULT
## BEFORE the fd EBADF check (and its comment documented the WRONG order). USER-VISIBLE BUG: a bad fd
## with nr_segs==0 returned success (0) where Linux returns EBADF (fdget+vmsplice_type precede
## import_iovec). Fix (translator-only, kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_vmsplice ~16396): reorder to
## flags -> validate_linux_fd(fd) EBADF -> nr_segs==0 success -> nr_segs>1024 EINVAL -> iov NULL/bad EFAULT
## -> terminal EINVAL. Also reordered nr_segs==0 before the >MAXIOV check to match iovec_from_user.
## NOTE: vmsplice_type's "neither FMODE_READ nor FMODE_WRITE -> EBADF" corner only applies to O_PATH
## handles, whose f_mode our O_ACCMODE-derived access helpers cannot represent (every modelled fd is
## readable or writable), so nothing extra to gate; transfer direction does not affect the errno.
## VERIFIED: the reorder is bypassed at boot (kernel-ctx caller_pid()=None skips the fd gate), so it is
## not directly dispatch-observable; the regression guard re-affirms the import_iovec gates still fire
## identically once the fd gate passes (nr_segs==0 -> 0, >1024 -> EINVAL, NULL iov -> EFAULT). Stamp
## "vmsplice fdget/vmsplice_type EBADF before import_iovec (batch 541): OK". cargo build clean; clippy
## 17477 (unchanged — used i64::from(errno).wrapping_neg() to avoid arithmetic_side_effects in the new
## test block); boot-test BOOT_OK 28s EXIT=0.

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 540 — tee do_tee() FMODE_READ(in)/FMODE_WRITE(out) -> EBADF gate

##   SYSCALL_DEFINE4(tee): if(flags&~SPLICE_F_ALL) -EINVAL; if(!len) return 0;
##     fdget(fdin)->EBADF; fdget(fdout)->EBADF; do_tee(...).
##   do_tee(): ipipe=get_pipe_info(in); opipe=get_pipe_info(out); int ret=-EINVAL;

##     return ret;  // default -EINVAL when not two distinct pipes

## terminal EINVAL directly after the two fd validations.
