## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 539 — splice __do_splice/do_splice prologue: ESPIPE + offset order + FMODE EBADF
## Linux v6.6 fs/splice.c (verified verbatim against raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6):
##   SYSCALL_DEFINE6(splice): if(!len) return 0; if(flags&~SPLICE_F_ALL) -EINVAL;
##     fdget(fd_in)->EBADF; fdget(fd_out)->EBADF; __do_splice(...).
##   __do_splice(): ipipe=get_pipe_info(in); opipe=get_pipe_info(out);
##     if(ipipe){ if(off_in) return -ESPIPE; ... }
##     if(opipe){ if(off_out) return -ESPIPE; ... }
##     if(off_out){ copy_from_user(&offset, off_out, 8) -> -EFAULT }  [off_out FIRST]
##     if(off_in){  copy_from_user(&offset, off_in, 8)  -> -EFAULT }  [off_in SECOND]
##     do_splice(...).
##   do_splice() prologue: if(!(in.f_mode&FMODE_READ) || !(out.f_mode&FMODE_WRITE)) return -EBADF;
##     then pipe-direction branches (pipe-pipe off ESPIPE [already filtered], ipipe==opipe EINVAL,
##     FMODE_PWRITE/FMODE_PREAD EINVAL on single-pipe branches).
## Pre-batch sys_splice diverged: it read off_in (arg1) BEFORE off_out (arg3) — Linux reads off_out
## first — and it lacked BOTH the __do_splice ESPIPE gates (pipe endpoint + explicit offset) AND the
## do_splice FMODE_READ(in)/FMODE_WRITE(out) -> EBADF gate.
## Fix (translator-only, kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_splice ~16215): after the two fd validations,
##   1. caller_pid Some: off_in!=0 && fd_in.kind==Pipe -> ESPIPE; off_out!=0 && fd_out.kind==Pipe -> ESPIPE
##   2. off_out!=0 -> validate_user_read(off_out,8)->EFAULT; then off_in!=0 -> validate_user_read(off_in,8)->EFAULT
##   3. caller_pid Some: !fd_access_is_readable(in)->EBADF; !fd_access_is_writable(out)->EBADF
##   terminal EINVAL (transfer unimplemented).
## RATIONALE for not modelling the deeper branches: pipe-pipe two-offset ESPIPE is already filtered by
## the __do_splice gates; ipipe==opipe self-splice EINVAL and the FMODE_PWRITE/FMODE_PREAD EINVAL on the
## single-pipe branches all return EINVAL, which is observationally identical to our terminal EINVAL
## since the data transfer is unimplemented. So no behavior is lost by collapsing them.
## VERIFIED: At boot caller_pid()=None bypasses the fd-table gates (same as batches 537/538), so ESPIPE
## pipe-classification (only HandleKind::Pipe is a get_pipe_info endpoint; File/MemFd/Console are not)
## and the FMODE_READ/FMODE_WRITE derivations are predicate-tested. Stamp "splice __do_splice ESPIPE +
## off order + FMODE EBADF (batch 539): OK". cargo build clean; clippy 17477 (unchanged); boot-test
## BOOT_OK 26s EXIT=0.

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 538 — sendfile gate order + FMODE_READ/FMODE_WRITE/FMODE_PREAD gates
## Linux v6.6 fs/read_write.c SYSCALL_DEFINE4(sendfile64) + do_sendfile() (verified verbatim against
## raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6):
##   wrapper: if (offset) copy_from_user(&pos, offset, 8) -> -EFAULT  [BEFORE do_sendfile]
##   do_sendfile: retval=-EBADF; fdget(in_fd)->EBADF;
##                if !(in.f_mode & FMODE_READ) -> EBADF;
##                retval=-ESPIPE; if (ppos) if !(in.f_mode & FMODE_PREAD) -> ESPIPE;
##                rw_verify_area(READ,...);
##                retval=-EBADF; fdget(out_fd)->EBADF;
##                if !(out.f_mode & FMODE_WRITE) -> EBADF;
##                ... transfer ...; put_user(pos, offset) -> EFAULT (unconditional, after transfer)
## Pre-batch sys_sendfile diverged: (a) it validated out_fd BEFORE in_fd, where Linux validates
## in_fd first; (b) it checked the offset pointer LAST with validate_user_write, where Linux reads
## the offset FIRST (copy_from_user, a READ) before any fd touch; (c) it never applied the
## FMODE_READ(in)/FMODE_WRITE(out) -> EBADF or the FMODE_PREAD -> ESPIPE gates.
## Fix (translator-only, kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_sendfile ~16145): reordered to
##   1. offset!=0 -> validate_user_read(offset,8)->EFAULT  (matches copy_from_user, a read)
##   2. validate_linux_fd(in_fd)->EBADF
##   3. caller_pid Some: !fd_access_is_readable(in.status_flags)->EBADF;
##      offset!=0 && in.kind not File/MemFd -> ESPIPE  (FMODE_PREAD held only by seekable regulars)
##   4. validate_linux_fd(out_fd)->EBADF
##   5. caller_pid Some: !fd_access_is_writable(out.status_flags)->EBADF
##   terminal EINVAL (transfer unimplemented).
## LIMITATION (documented in-code; tracked: known-issues TD21): Linux's terminal put_user(pos,offset) write-back EFAULT, which
## runs unconditionally after the transfer and can override a success/EBADF, is NOT modelled because
## we don't implement the transfer. The leading validate_user_read already rejects a wholly-unmapped
## offset pointer, matching the dominant failure mode.
## VERIFIED: At boot caller_pid()=None so the fd-table gates are bypassed (same as batch 537), so the
## FMODE/ESPIPE behavior is predicate-tested, not dispatch-tested: fd_access_is_readable(O_WRONLY)=false
## & true for O_RDONLY/O_RDWR; fd_access_is_writable(O_RDONLY)=false & true for O_WRONLY/O_RDWR;
## File/MemFd pread-capable, Pipe/Console/EventFd not. Stamp "sendfile gate order + FMODE/ESPIPE
## (batch 538): OK". cargo build clean; clippy 17477 (unchanged baseline); boot-test BOOT_OK 26s EXIT=0.

## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 537 — copy_file_range gate order + vfs_copy_file_range FMODE/regular gate
## Linux v6.6 fs/read_write.c SYSCALL_DEFINE6(copy_file_range) order (verified verbatim against
## raw.githubusercontent.com torvalds/linux v6.6):
##   ret=-EBADF; fdget(fd_in)->EBADF; fdget(fd_out)->EBADF;
##   ret=-EFAULT; off_in copy_from_user->EFAULT; off_out copy_from_user->EFAULT;
##   ret=-EINVAL; if (flags!=0)->EINVAL;
##   vfs_copy_file_range() -> generic_copy_file_checks -> generic_file_rw_checks:
##     S_ISDIR(in|out)                                          -> EISDIR
##     !S_ISREG(in) || !S_ISREG(out)                            -> EINVAL
##     !(in&FMODE_READ) || !(out&FMODE_WRITE) || (out&O_APPEND) -> EBADF
## TWO divergences fixed (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_copy_file_range):
##   (1) ORDER: pre-batch we checked `flags` FIRST (before fds+offsets), so (bad fd, flags=5)
##       returned EINVAL where Linux returns EBADF, and (good fds, bad off_in, flags=5) returned
##       EINVAL where Linux returns EFAULT. Reordered to fds -> offsets -> flags.
##   (2) GATE: pre-batch we never applied the vfs regular-file/access-mode gate, so an O_WRONLY
##       input or O_RDONLY/O_APPEND output returned the terminal EINVAL where Linux returns EBADF,
##       and a pipe/console fd reached the terminal arm instead of the precise non-regular EINVAL.
##       Added gate after the flags check in Linux's internal order (regular EINVAL before FMODE
##       EBADF). Only File/MemFd map to S_ISREG; the EISDIR-for-dirs arm is NOT modelled (our fd
##       layer does not distinguish a directory fd from a regular File — documented in-code).
##   The batch 350/351 flags int-truncation fix (r9 as u32) is preserved.
## SELF-TEST (stamp "copy_file_range vfs gate + order (batch 537): OK"): the reorder and new gate
## are only observable with a real fd table (caller_pid()==Some) AND a faulting user pointer; at
## boot BOTH validate_linux_fd and validate_user_read bypass in kernel context (is_kernel_context),
## so the dispatch path still terminates in EINVAL and the existing flags-truncation dispatch
## probes are unchanged (re-verified). The gate logic is asserted via the exact predicates the new
## code uses: in_bad(O_WRONLY) && !in_bad(O_RDONLY/O_RDWR); out_bad(O_RDONLY|O_APPEND-variants) &&
## !out_bad(O_WRONLY/O_RDWR); is_regular(File/MemFd) && !is_regular(Pipe/Console/EventFd). Same
## not-dispatch-testable-in-kernel-ctx pattern as batches 535 (ftruncate) and 536 (fallocate).
##
## FIXED (2026-06-12): boot-stack overflow from monolithic translation self-test → futex #UD (known-issues F10)
## Was: after applying ABI batch 536, boot reached "[syscall/linux] Translation self-test PASSED"
## then faulted on the FIRST context switch of ipc::futex::self_test()'s task 36:
## EXCEPTION: Invalid Opcode (#UD) at 0xffffffff81133b0e, bytes 49 0f ae 20 = xsave64 [r8],
## then FATAL: Unrecoverable kernel #UD. Never reached BOOT_OK. Deterministic with batch 536,
## clean with it stashed — a pure layout-shift heisenbug (the batch-536 change is a translator-
## only sys_fallocate gate not even reached by the futex test).
##   ROOT CAUSE: boot-stack overflow. switch_context (sched/context.rs) dispatches the FPU save
##   on global FPU_STRATEGY (0=FXSAVE,1=XSAVE,2=XSAVEOPT). Boot selected FXSAVE (QEMU has no XSAVE)
##   yet the crash ran the XSAVE64 branch → FPU_STRATEGY was corrupted 0→1. The corruptor:
##   syscall::linux::self_test() is one ~1.4 MB monolithic function that runs directly on the
##   guardless boot stack; at opt-level=0 (no stack-slot coloring) its single frame is the SUM of
##   every per-batch block's locals (~480 KiB, disasm: sub r11,0x75000 + probe loop + sub rsp,0x900).
##   With the old 512 KiB boot stack minus kernel_main's frame, batch 536's extra locals tipped the
##   frame past the stack bottom; the unoptimized stack-probe prologue (writes 0 to each 4 KiB page
##   it descends) scribbled the adjacent .bss, flipping the FPU_STRATEGY byte. self_test never
##   re-reads that byte, so it printed PASSED and returned; the poison only bit when the futex
##   context switch trusted strategy=1 and ran xsave64 on a CPU without CR4.OSXSAVE → #UD. No guard
##   page on the boot stack is what made the overflow silent instead of a clean fault.
##   FIX SHIPPED (kernel/src/main.rs):
##     1. KERNEL_BOOT_STACK_SIZE 512 KiB → 2 MiB (≈1000+ batches of runway).
##     2. 64 KiB bottom redzone canary (BOOT_STACK_REDZONE / BOOT_STACK_CANARY=0xC7):
##        init_boot_stack_canary() fills it early in kernel_main (RSP near top);
##        check_boot_stack_canary() (called right after syscall::linux::self_test()) volatile-scans
##        it and FATAL-halts with "boot stack overflow detected" if clobbered. The stack-probe
##        prologue guarantees any frame that reaches the redzone trips the canary — future overruns
##        become a clear diagnostic before they can corrupt .bss.
##   VERIFIED: boot-test reaches BOOT_OK in 26s, serial runs to the `user>` shell prompt, canary
##   scan clean, futex self-test passes. (One validation run hit the pre-existing intermittent
##   OOM-self-test truncation tracked as W1 — unrelated; immediate re-run clean.)
##   PROPER LONG-TERM FIX = TD4 (known-issues.md): split the monolithic self_test() into small
##   #[inline(never)] sub-functions so no single boot-stack frame is large. Deferred (hand-split of
##   one 39k-line 4-space block risks mis-scoping shared locals); the 2 MiB stack + canary make the
##   system correct and self-diagnosing meanwhile. Frame grows ~1 KiB/batch, so TD4 is the real cap.
##
## DONE (2026-06-12): ABI batch 536 — fallocate FMODE_WRITE gate (sys_fallocate returns EBADF for read-only fd)
## Linux v6.6 fs/open.c vfs_fallocate(): `if (!(file->f_mode & FMODE_WRITE)) return -EBADF;` — a
## fallocate on a fd opened without write access fails EBADF, checked before the filesystem's
## ->fallocate and before the mode/EOPNOTSUPP path. Our translator (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
## sys_fallocate) previously skipped straight to the terminal EOPNOTSUPP/EINVAL handling without the
## FMODE_WRITE check, so a read-only fd wrongly got EOPNOTSUPP/EINVAL instead of EBADF.
##   FIX: added a gate after the UNSHARE gate / before terminal EOPNOTSUPP — if caller_pid() and the
##   fd resolves in the per-process Linux fd table, reject with EBADF when
##   !fd_access_is_writable(entry.status_flags). Translator-only; matches v6.6 ordering (FMODE_WRITE
##   before the EINVAL mode validation, which our terminal arm still applies for writable fds).
##   SELF-TEST (stamp "fallocate FMODE_WRITE gate (batch 536): OK", serial line 512): dispatches
##   nr::FALLOCATE three times (mode 0x4000→EOPNOTSUPP, 0x80→EOPNOTSUPP, 0x09→EINVAL for a writable
##   path) + asserts fd_access_is_writable(0)==false. The EBADF arm itself is not dispatch-testable
##   in-kernel (caller_pid()==None at boot, so the fd-table gate is skipped — same caller-None
##   pattern as the other fd-gated batches); the predicate is unit-asserted instead.
##
## FIXED (2026-06-12): kernel-level signalfd blocking read now parks the caller
## Was: a read of a signalfd with no masked-pending signal returned EAGAIN even for a
## BLOCKING (non-SFD_NONBLOCK) fd, because this kernel had no signal-arrival wakeup path
## — a thread could not park on "next signal in mask".
##   FIX SHIPPED: proc::signal grew a per-process signalfd wait-queue
##   (SIGNALFD_WAITERS: Mutex<BTreeMap<ProcessId, Vec<SignalFdWaiter{task,mask}>>>) kept
##   as a SEPARATE registry from SignalState so the latter stays Copy.  set_pending() now
##   wakes (try_wake → defer_wake) every registered reader whose mask intersects the
##   newly-pending signal, but only on a clear→set transition, after releasing
##   SIGNAL_STATES (leaf-lock discipline).  dispatch_signalfd_read in syscall/linux.rs
##   became a register-then-recheck loop: drain masked-pending signals; if none and the
##   fd is non-blocking → EAGAIN; else register_signalfd_waiter(), re-check
##   has_pending_in_mask() (closing the cross-lock lost-wakeup race — any bit set after
##   the recheck finds us registered and wakes us; any bit set before is seen by the
##   recheck), then sched::block_current() and loop to re-drain on wake.  remove() drops
##   a dying process's waiter registrations.  The partition logic is split into a pure
##   take_matching_signalfd_waiters() (unit-tested by the new test_signalfd_waiter_registry
##   self-test — register/take-matching/idempotent-remask/deregister, no scheduler) from
##   the scheduler-touching wake wrapper.  set_pending's bit/return semantics are
##   byte-identical to before (the existing signal self-tests still pass), with only the
##   post-unlock wake added.  The actual park path is exercised only by real userspace
##   (needs another runnable task to post the signal); the self-test covers the registry
##   partition logic and the non-blocking/already-pending fast paths.
##   FOLLOW-UP DONE (2026-06-12): rt_sigtimedwait now blocks instead of always returning
##   EAGAIN — sys_rt_sigtimedwait (syscall/linux.rs) reuses this same SIGNALFD_WAITERS infra
##   (register_signalfd_waiter + take_pending_in_mask, woken by set_pending) plus the timerfd
##   hrtimer idiom for the timeout: NULL timeout blocks indefinitely; a zero timeout polls
##   once → EAGAIN; a finite timeout arms a one-shot hrtimer (sigtimedwait_timeout_wake) and
##   parks via block_current, timing out to EAGAIN past the absolute monotonic deadline.
##   SIGKILL/SIGSTOP are stripped from the acceptance set (Linux sigdelsetmask). On a matched
##   signal it dequeues, fills uinfo si_signo (sigtimedwait_deliver), and returns the signo.
##   The validation-gate discriminators (EINVAL/EFAULT/EAGAIN) are preserved; an in-kernel
##   caller (caller_pid()==None, incl. the boot self-test) returns EAGAIN so the single-
##   threaded boot never blocks. The real park path needs a second runnable task to post the
##   signal, so (like signalfd) only the gates + caller-None fast path are self-tested.
##
## FIXED (2026-06-12): kernel-level timerfd blocking read now parks the caller
## Was: a read of an unexpired timerfd returned EAGAIN even for a BLOCKING
## (non-TFD_NONBLOCK) fd, because the lazy-expiry model armed no hrtimer and there
## was no timer-arrival wakeup path.
##   FIX SHIPPED: kernel/src/ipc/timerfd.rs grew read_expirations_blocking(), which
##   registers the caller in a per-instance reader_waiter slot, arms a one-shot
##   crate::hrtimer at the next expiry (rem.max(1) ns) whose callback wakes the task
##   (try_wake → defer_wake, the eventfd timeout idiom), then sched::block_current()s
##   and re-evaluates on wake (looping for each tick of a periodic timer).  A read of
##   a DISARMED timer parks with no armed hrtimer and is woken by settime(), which now
##   takes+wakes reader_waiter after dropping the table lock — matching Linux's
##   "block an unarmed-timerfd reader until it is armed and fires".  dispatch_timerfd_read
##   in syscall/linux.rs now branches on O_NONBLOCK: TFD_NONBLOCK keeps the old
##   read_expirations()→EAGAIN path; blocking fds use read_expirations_blocking().
##   The non-blocking lazy model is otherwise unchanged (the wakeup hrtimer exists only
##   for the duration of a blocked read).  Self-tested: the blocking FAST PATH (already
##   pending → returns without parking) and stale-handle fast-fail; the actual park
##   path is exercised only by real userspace (needs another runnable task to wake it)
##   and reuses the battle-tested sched/hrtimer primitives the native eventfd depends on.
##   NOTE: single reader_waiter slot (matches eventfd); the armed case is robust to slot
##   overwrite because each blocked reader is also woken by its own hrtimer (task id
##   captured at arm time), so only the rare concurrent-disarmed-blocking-readers edge
##   could lose a waiter — acceptable and matching the eventfd model.
##   (signalfd's blocking read is now FIXED too — see its entry above; inotify's
##   blocking read is ALSO FIXED — see the next entry below; its wake source is the
##   cross-cutting fs::notify owner-token wait-queue rather than a self-contained hrtimer.)
##
## [migrated 2026-06-13 -> known-issues.md TD15 (timerfd CANCEL_ON_SET) and TD16 (nested epoll)]

## FIXED (2026-06-12): kernel-level inotify blocking read now parks the caller
## Was: a read of an inotify fd with no queued event returned EAGAIN even for a
## BLOCKING (non-IN_NONBLOCK) fd, because the kernel had no fs-event-arrival wakeup
## path to park a reader on.
##   FIX SHIPPED: fs::notify grew a blocking-read wait queue keyed by an opaque
##   OWNER TOKEN (NOTIFY_WAITERS: Mutex<BTreeMap<u64, Vec<TaskId>>>).  FsWatch gained
##   an owner_token field; the new create_watch_owned(path,mask,recursive,owner_token)
##   sets it (create_watch keeps its old signature, delegating with token 0 = no
##   blocking owner).  emit() collects the owner tokens of watches that gained a new
##   event and, AFTER dropping the WATCHES lock (leaf-lock discipline), wakes their
##   registered tasks (try_wake → defer_wake).  close_watch() likewise wakes the
##   removed watch's owner so a reader re-evaluates (picks up a synthesized IN_IGNORED
##   or falls through).  The Linux-ABI inotify adapter passes its INSTANCE ID as the
##   owner token (kernel/src/ipc/inotify.rs create_native → create_watch_owned), so a
##   reader registers ONCE per instance: keying by instance (not per-watch) means a
##   watch added AFTER the reader parked — or an instance with no watches at park time
##   — still routes the wake, matching Linux's per-inotify_group wait queue.
##   dispatch_inotify_read (syscall/linux.rs) became a register-then-recheck loop:
##   drain via read_into; if empty and IN_NONBLOCK → EAGAIN; else
##   register_notify_waiter(handle.raw(), task), re-check is_readable() (closing the
##   cross-lock race), then sched::block_current() and loop to re-drain on wake.
##   Tested: a pure register/take/deregister partition self-test
##   (notify::waiter_registry_self_test) is run from BOTH notify::self_test and the
##   UNCONDITIONAL ipc::inotify::self_test (notify::self_test itself is gated behind a
##   mounted FAT fs, so the inotify caller is what verifies it on a typical boot); the
##   actual park path needs a second runnable task and is exercised by real userspace.
##   This was the LAST of the three fd-family blocking-read gaps (timerfd + signalfd
##   fixed earlier the same day) — all of signalfd/timerfd/inotify now park instead of
##   spinning on EAGAIN.  (rt_sigtimedwait now blocks too, via the same signalfd
##   wait-queue infra + an hrtimer timeout — see the signalfd FIXED entry's follow-up note.)
##
## [migrated 2026-06-13 -> known-issues.md TD17 (inotify event coverage native-derived)]

## SESSION PREFERENCE (user, 2026-06-06): autonomous-loop pacing
## At the end of each `<<autonomous-loop-dynamic>>` tick, call
## ScheduleWakeup with `delaySeconds = 60` (the runtime's hard
## minimum — the user asked for "0s" but the runtime clamps to
## [60, 3600], so 60 is the fastest the loop can fire).  Do NOT
## pad above 60 unless something specifically requires waiting.

## DONE (2026-06-11): /proc/stat converted to Linux format (gen_stat,
## kernel\src\fs\procfs.rs).  The SYSTEM-WIDE /proc/stat now emits the real
## Linux layout (fs/proc/stat.c show_stat) — cpu/cpuN jiffy lines, intr, ctxt,
## btime, processes, procs_running, procs_blocked, softirq — instead of the old
## custom "tasks:/running:/ready:" format + per-task table.  All fields are
## honestly backed; genuinely-untracked columns are zero (NOT fabricated):
##   * cpu/cpuN jiffies: crate::cputime (real per-CPU TSC accounting hooked into
##     live ISR/idle/softirq paths) gives the system/idle/irq/softirq 4-way
##     split.  user/nice/iowait/steal/guest/guest_nice are honestly 0 — we do
##     not yet separate user-vs-kernel CPU time.  NS_PER_JIFFY=10_000_000 (100Hz).
##   * intr total: cputime aggregate irq_count (per-IRQ breakdown omitted).
##   * ctxt: sum of per-task schedule_count (real dispatch count).
##   * btime: crate::timekeeping::boot_time_epoch_secs().
##   * processes: NEW monotonic fork counter — crate::proc::pcb::processes_created()
##     (PROCESSES_CREATED AtomicU64 bumped in create() AND fork_create()).
##   * procs_running (Running+Ready) / procs_blocked: live task_list() counts.
##   * softirq total: cputime aggregate softirq_count (per-type breakdown omitted).
## Verified: no in-tree reader parses the old "tasks:"/"total_cpu_ticks:" format
## (only gen_stat itself produced it).  boot-test PASSED.
## FOLLOW-UP (now UNBLOCKED by cputime): to add a user-vs-kernel CPU split (so the
## cpu line's user/nice columns are non-zero), the scheduler tick path would need
## to attribute time to user vs. kernel mode — defer until a real userspace
## workload makes top/htop's %us column matter.
## DONE (2026-06-11): /proc/uptime converted to Linux format (gen_uptime,
## procfs.rs).  Now emits the real Linux fs/proc/uptime.c layout: TWO
## space-separated centisecond fields "<uptime> <idle>" instead of the old
## single 9-decimal "secs.frac" field.  The idle field is the sum of idle time
## across ALL CPUs, sourced honestly from cputime::aggregate_stats().idle_ns
## (real per-CPU TSC idle accounting) — never fabricated.  Strict two-field
## parsers (sscanf "%lf %lf") now work.  boot-test PASSED.
## DONE (2026-06-11): /proc/loadavg now reports REAL 1/5/15-minute EWMAs
## (gen_loadavg, procfs.rs + update_load_average, kernel\src\sched\mod.rs).
## Previously gen_loadavg walked task_list() and stuffed the INSTANTANEOUS
## runnable count into all three load slots ("load load load") — meaningless to
## uptime/top/w which expect genuine time-averaged load.  The scheduler already
## had an O(1) per-second EWMA sampler (update_load_average, reading per-CPU
## queue_length — no task-list walk), but it kept only ONE 64s-half-life average
## and gen_loadavg ignored it.  Fix: replaced LOAD_AVG_X100 with three Linux-
## faithful fixed-point EWMAs (LOAD_AVG_1/5/15) using Linux's exact constants
## (FSHIFT=11, FIXED_1=2048, EXP_1=1884/EXP_5=2014/EXP_15=2037; calc_load =
## (load*exp + active*(FIXED_1-exp)) >> FSHIFT).  The sampler is gated to a
## 5-second effective interval (Linux LOAD_FREQ) via LOAD_SAMPLE_DIVIDER while
## keeping the existing 1s timer_tick call site.  gen_loadavg now formats the
## three averages <int>.<2-digit-frac> via sched::load_int/load_frac (== Linux
## LOAD_INT/LOAD_FRAC) and keeps the honest runnable/total + last_pid fields
## (computed at read time from task_list — fine, not ISR context).
## load_average_x100() (used by kdiag/kshell) preserved, now derived from the
## 1-minute EWMA so it stays consistent with /proc/loadavg.  Self-test added
## (asserts 5 space-split fields, loads parse as <int>.<2-frac>, field 4 is
## <runnable>/<total>, field 5 is an integer).  boot-test PASSED; serial shows
## '/loadavg: five-field Linux layout OK ("0.00 0.00 0.00 1/1 0")'.
## DONE (2026-06-11): /proc/buddyinfo cleaned to pure Linux frag_show layout
## (gen_buddyinfo, procfs.rs).  The per-order free-block counts were already
## real (mm::frame::stats().order_counts — the buddy allocator's per-order free
## lists, MAX_ORDER=10 → 11 columns, 16 KiB base page), but the generator
## appended two NON-Linux comment lines ("# Order sizes: ..." and "# Total free
## frames: ...") after the zone line, which would corrupt any strict
## /proc/buddyinfo parser (procps, fragmentation monitors).  Now emits ONLY the
## Linux "Node 0, zone %8s %6lu %6lu ..." line, matching mm/vmstat.c column
## spacing.  The dropped totals are still available via /proc/meminfo.  Self-test
## added (asserts 'Node 0, zone' prefix, all per-order columns parse as ints, and
## no stray non-zone trailing lines).  boot-test PASSED; serial shows
## '/buddyinfo: Linux frag_show layout OK (11 columns)'.
## NOTE: the OTHER standard-Linux /proc files still in custom native format
## (/proc/interrupts, /proc/diskstats) are NOT cleanly convertible: real Linux
## /proc/interrupts needs per-IRQ-line counters and /proc/diskstats needs the 20
## per-device IO fields (reads/writes completed, sectors, ms) — we track neither
## (only aggregate irq_count and aggregate blkdev reads/writes).  Converting them
## would mean emitting mostly-zero columns, which is worse than the current honest
## custom format; defer until per-IRQ and per-device accounting exist.
## NOTE: /proc/meminfo, /proc/cpuinfo AND /proc/vmstat
## are INTENTIONALLY native-format; converting THOSE to Linux layout is a
## separate design decision, not a bug — leave them unless the user asks.
## /proc/vmstat already uses Linux's "key value" line format but with our own
## key names; mapping them to Linux's canonical keys (nr_free_pages, pgfault,
## pswpin, ...) is NOT clean because (a) our pages are 16 KiB vs Linux 4 KiB, so
## reporting our frame count under "nr_free_pages" misleads any tool computing
## bytes, and (b) several of our counters (zero-pool, zram internals, kswapd
## cycles) have no honest Linux equivalent.  Honest native names beat misleading
## Linux-named numbers, so it stays native like meminfo/cpuinfo.
## STATUS: the cleanly-convertible standard-Linux /proc files are now ALL done
## (stat, uptime, loadavg, buddyinfo; <pid>/io added 2026-06-11 with real PCB
## counters — see DONE entry below).  The remainder are either blocked on new
## accounting infra (interrupts, diskstats, <pid>/auxv) or intentionally
## native (meminfo, cpuinfo, vmstat).  No further small in-thread procfs work.

## DONE (2026-06-11): /proc/<pid>/status Mems_allowed + Mems_allowed_list
## Linux always prints `Mems_allowed:`/`Mems_allowed_list:` (the NUMA memory
## nodes a task may allocate from) right after the Cpus_allowed lines; numactl
## and NUMA-aware tooling read them.  We do NOT track a per-process mempolicy /
## cpuset mems mask, so the truthful value is "all online nodes allowed" — which
## is exactly Linux's default for a process under no cpuset restriction (NOT
## invented data; sourced from numa::node_count()).  node_count() <= MAX_NODES
## (8), so the mask is always a single small chunk.
## Refactor: the two Cpus_allowed formatters were renamed to generic bitmap
## formatters (format_cpus_allowed -> format_bitmap_hex, format_cpus_allowed_list
## -> format_bitmap_list) since they now back both CPU and NUMA-node bitmaps;
## docs made bitmap-generic.  Added small-width self-test cases (1,1)->"1",
## (3,2)->"3" hex and (1,1)->"0", (3,2)->"0-1" list, plus an integration
## ordering assertion (Cpus_allowed_list < Mems_allowed < voluntary_ctxt_switches).
## Boot test PASSED; serial shows "Mems_allowed present and ordered OK" and
## "format_bitmap_hex/list: all bitmap cases OK".  build_pid_status in
## kernel\src\fs\procfs.rs.

## DONE (2026-06-11): /proc/<pid>/status Cpus_allowed + Cpus_allowed_list
## Implemented properly (the deferral risk was mitigated by writing the
## formatting as PURE, unit-tested functions matching Linux %*pb exactly).
## - format_cpus_allowed(mask, ncpus): comma-separated 32-bit hex groups,
##   most-significant first, top (partial) group zero-padded to ceil(bits/4)
##   digits and lower groups to 8, sized to smp::cpu_count().  Verified against
##   Linux examples: (0xff,8)->"ff", (1,12)->"001", (u64::MAX,64)->
##   "ffffffff,ffffffff", (all_40,40)->"ff,ffffffff", etc.
## - format_cpus_allowed_list(mask, ncpus): range-compressed list, e.g.
##   (0b1101,8)->"0,2-3", (0,8)->"".
## Affinity is a thread property -> keyed off task.id via
## sched::get_cpu_affinity (build_pid_status in kernel\src\fs\procfs.rs).
## Both lines emitted after Seccomp in Linux field order.  9 hex + 5 list
## deterministic self-tests, plus an integration ordering assertion.  Boot
## test PASSED; serial shows "format_cpus_allowed/list: all bitmap cases OK".

## DONE (2026-06-11): /proc/<pid>/status now reports NoNewPrivs and Seccomp
## Linux always prints `NoNewPrivs:` (PR_SET_NO_NEW_PRIVS state) and
## `Seccomp:` (SECCOMP_MODE_* — 0 disabled / 1 strict / 2 filter) in
## /proc/<pid>/status; systemd, WINE, and container/seccomp tooling read
## them to confirm a sandbox took effect.  We track both with real data:
## NoNewPrivs from pcb::get_no_new_privs(), Seccomp from scfilter::has_filter()
## (our allow/deny filter is filter-mode semantics → 2; absence → 0; we have
## no strict mode).  Added both lines to build_pid_status in Linux field order
## (after Threads, before the ctxt-switch lines).  Process-wide → keyed off
## proc_id, so thread status (task/<tid>/status) reports the owning process's
## values.  Extended the synthetic self-test to assert both lines exist,
## default to 0 for a PCB-less proc_id, and preserve the
## Threads<NoNewPrivs<voluntary_ctxt_switches ordering.  Boot test PASSED;
## serial shows "NoNewPrivs/Seccomp present and ordered OK".

## DONE (2026-06-10): /proc/<pid>/status Name: now truncated to 15 bytes
## Same bug class as the stat-comm fix below: build_pid_status emitted the
## FULL untruncated task name (up to 32 bytes) in the `Name:` line, but
## Linux sources `Name:` from get_task_comm() (the 16-byte comm field), so
## it is capped at TASK_COMM_LEN-1 = 15 bytes — identical to /proc/<pid>/comm
## and /proc/<pid>/stat field 2.  A tool cross-referencing Name: against comm
## would have seen disagreeing strings for names >15 chars.  Fixed by routing
## the Name: source through the shared comm_truncate() helper.  Extended the
## comm-truncation self-test to assert the status Name: field matches the
## 15-byte truncation.  Boot test PASSED; serial shows "Name truncated to 15
## bytes OK".

## DONE (2026-06-10): /proc/<pid>/stat comm (field 2) now truncated to 15 bytes
## gen_pid_comm correctly capped /proc/<pid>/comm to TASK_COMM_LEN-1 (15
## bytes), but build_pid_stat emitted the FULL untruncated task name (up to
## 32 bytes) in stat field 2.  So /proc/<pid>/comm and /proc/<pid>/stat
## field 2 disagreed for names >15 chars, and strict parsers that split on
## the last ')' and size buffers to TASK_COMM_LEN could overflow.  Fixed by
## factoring the char-boundary-safe truncation into a shared helper
## comm_truncate() (kernel\src\fs\procfs.rs) used by BOTH gen_pid_comm and
## build_pid_stat.  Added a synthetic self-test (20-byte name) asserting the
## stat comm field == the 15-byte truncation == comm_truncate output.
## Boot test PASSED; serial shows "comm truncated to 15 bytes OK".

## DONE (2026-06-10): /proc/<pid>/stat processor (field 39) is now real
## Field 39 (processor — the CPU a task last ran on) was hardcoded 0.
## top -1 and htop's "P" column read it.  We already snapshot last_cpu on
## every dispatch and expose it via TaskInfo.last_cpu, so build_pid_stat now
## emits it directly (thread/task property).  Field count stays 52 (one of
## the trailing zeros becomes the placeholder).  Tested via the synthetic
## build_pid_stat check (last_cpu=3 -> field 39 == 3), which also guards the
## column position.
##
## DONE (2026-06-10): /proc/<pid>/stat starttime (field 22) is now real
## Field 22 was hardcoded 0, making every process look like it started at
## boot (ps/top/htop derive process age and normalise CPU% from it).  Added
## a boot-relative creation timestamp: Task.start_tick (sched/task.rs),
## captured via apic::tick_count() in Task::new_kernel (0 for idle tasks,
## created before the timer ticks).  USER_HZ == TICK_RATE_HZ == 100 so the
## tick count is already in ABI units.  Plumbed through TaskInfo.start_tick
## (sched/mod.rs task_list snapshot) into build_pid_stat field 22.  Tested:
## a deterministic synthetic-task check (field 22 echoes a distinctive
## start_tick, proving it is not a 0 stub) + a live cross-check against the
## probe task's start_tick.  (taskmon.rs has a separate local TaskInfo type;
## unaffected.)
##
## BUG FOUND & FIXED (2026-06-10): procfs readdir-count self-test was stale
## When the `task` subdir was added to every PID directory (earlier tick),
## the readdir-count assertion (expected PID_FILES+PID_LINKS) was not
## bumped, so it FAILed (21 vs 20).  It slipped through because the procfs
## self-test failure is NON-FATAL: main.rs:1039 logs "WARNING: ProcFs
## self-test failed" and boots on, so BOOT_OK still prints and
## boot-test.sh reports PASSED.  Fixed the count (+1 for `task`) and added
## an explicit assertion that the extra entry IS the `task` directory.
## TOOLING GAP (process note) — FIXED 2026-06-11: boot-test.sh's BOOT_OK
## grep did NOT catch non-fatal self-test failures.  Now hardened:
## check_selftest_failures() scans build/serial-test.txt for the wrapper
## marker "self-test failed" (case-insensitive) in BOTH BOOT_OK success
## paths and exits 1 if found, so a regressed non-fatal self-test now turns
## the boot test red instead of silently PASSING.  Chose the "self-test
## failed" marker over raw "FAIL:"/"WARNING:" because the latter have
## legitimate hits in a passing log — "[drm-atomic] check FAIL: CRTC 9999
## not found" (intentional negative tests) and "[lockdep] WARNING:
## potential deadlock" (a deliberately-triggered detector) — which would
## false-positive.  Verified: real passing log stays green; synthetic
## "WARNING: ProcFs self-test failed" and "[hpet] WARNING: Self-test
## failed" logs both correctly flag red.  (You may still grep the log
## manually, but the harness now enforces it automatically.)
##
## DONE (2026-06-11): real /proc/<pid>/io — implemented with honest PCB counters.
## /proc/<pid>/io is now served from genuine per-process counters, NOT taskstats
## (which stays demo-only as warned below).  Design as landed:
##   - PCB gained four u64 counters (io_rchar/io_wchar/io_syscr/io_syscw) in
##     kernel/src/proc/pcb.rs, zero-initialised in Process::new and reset to zero
##     for a freshly-forked child (matches Linux copy_process zeroing task->ioac).
##   - pcb::account_io_read/account_io_write bump syscr/syscw once per call and
##     rchar/wchar by the byte count (saturating); pcb::io_counters(pid) snapshots
##     them.  All under the existing PROCESS_TABLE lock — read/write already take
##     that lock for the fd-table lookup (lookup_caller_fd), so this adds no new
##     lock to the hot path, just one more short critical section on the same lock
##     (same pattern as linux_as_charge).
##   - Hooked at the Linux-ABI syscall boundary in syscall::linux::dispatch_linux
##     via account_io_syscall(nr, result.value): classifies READ/READV/PREAD64/
##     PREADV/PREADV2 as reads and WRITE/WRITEV/PWRITE64/PWRITEV/PWRITEV2 as
##     writes (exactly the family Linux folds into rchar/wchar — sockets and
##     sendfile/splice are excluded, as on Linux).  Non-IO syscalls pay only a
##     cheap match before returning.  No-op in kernel context (caller_pid None).
##   - procfs gen_pid_io + render_pid_io emit Linux's 7-line key:value layout.
##     The three storage-layer counters (read_bytes/write_bytes/
##     cancelled_write_bytes) render as 0 — genuinely untracked (no per-process
##     block-layer attribution), NOT fabricated, per the "never invent data in
##     procfs" rule.  "io" added to PID_FILES + read_pid_file dispatch.
## On the former TESTING-BLOCKER concern (kernel self-test can't drive a userspace
## read/write): resolved the testable core directly — pcb::test_io_accounting
## exercises the real account_io_read/write/io_counters increment semantics in
## kernel context (fresh=zero, syscr/syscw +1 per call incl. zero-byte, rchar/
## wchar accumulate bytes only, reads/writes independent, unknown-pid no-op), and
## procfs self-test verifies render_pid_io's exact 7-line output + live path
## classification/PCB gating.  The only part not exercised end-to-end is the thin
## syscall-number->account_io mapping in dispatch_linux, which is a flat auditable
## table.
## NATIVE-ABI FOLLOW-UP DONE (2026-06-11): native processes now get real io
## counters too.  syscall::dispatch::dispatch() folds completed native read/write
## syscalls into the same pcb::account_io_{read,write} via a pure, unit-tested
## classifier io_dir_for_syscall(nr): reads = SYS_FS_READ, SYS_FS_READ_FILE,
## SYS_PIPE_READ/_TRY_READ/_READ_TIMEOUT, SYS_CONSOLE_READ_CHAR/_TRY_READ_CHAR;
## writes = SYS_FS_WRITE, SYS_PIPE_WRITE/_TRY_WRITE/_WRITE_TIMEOUT,
## SYS_CONSOLE_WRITE.  Hooked after the handler returns, reusing the task_id
## already resolved for the syscall filter (no extra lookup); owner_process(task_id)
## maps to pid and kernel tasks (None) are skipped.  syscr/syscw count every such
## syscall incl. failures; rchar/wchar fold only the positive returned byte count
## (matches Linux task_io_accounting).  Native and Linux-ABI dispatch are separate
## entry paths, so a process is accounted in exactly one — no double count.  New
## self-test test_io_dir_classification pins the number->direction mapping (boot:
## "[syscall]   Native I/O syscall classification: OK").
## DELIBERATE EXCLUSION: SYS_FS_WRITE_FILE is NOT accounted — its handler returns
## 0 on success (not a byte count), so folding result.value would bump syscw with
## a zero wchar (dishonest undercount).  If whole-file writes must appear in wchar,
## account them at the handler level using the data_len arg (the dispatch funnel
## only sees result.value, not the written length).  Likewise read_bytes/
## write_bytes/cancelled_write_bytes stay 0 (no per-process block-layer attribution).
##
## DONE (2026-06-11): real /proc/<pid>/fd/ directory + per-fd magic symlinks.
## Backed by genuine kernel state, NOT fabricated.  Design as landed:
##   - linux_fd::KernelFdTable gained list_open() -> Vec<(i32, FdEntry)> that
##     walks its entries slot array and returns (fd_number, entry) for every
##     occupied slot (kernel/src/proc/linux_fd.rs).
##   - pcb::linux_fd_list(pid) snapshots that under PROCESS_TABLE lock (mirrors
##     the existing pcb::linux_fd_lookup(pid, fd) used for stat/readlink).
##   - procfs ProcPath gained PidFdDir(pid) and PidFdLink(pid, fd); classify_path
##     routes "<pid>/fd" -> PidFdDir and "<pid>/fd/<n>" -> PidFdLink (rejecting
##     non-numeric / nested paths as NotFound).  readdir lists the open fds as
##     Symlink entries; stat reports Directory / Symlink (gated on task_exists +
##     linux_fd_lookup); readlink resolves the target via fd_link_target().
##   - fd_link_target(entry) renders Linux-style magic targets per HandleKind:
##     File -> backing VFS path via fs::handle::handle_path (fallback
##     anon_inode:[file] if the handle can't be resolved), Pipe -> pipe:[id],
##     Console -> /dev/console, EventFd/PidFd/MemFd -> anon_inode:[type].
##   - "fd" added to the PidDir readdir listing alongside "task"; the per-PID
##     directory entry-count self-test bumped (PID_FILES + PID_LINKS + 2 for the
##     two subdirs) with an fd-presence assertion.
## HONEST LIMITATION (same spirit as the io block above; tracked: known-issues TD21): the directory is
## EMPTY for NATIVE processes.  Native processes keep their fd table in
## userspace (posix/src/fdtable.rs) — it is not kernel-visible — so
## linux_fd_list returns None and /proc/<pid>/fd/ readdir yields zero entries
## rather than inventing fds.  Only Linux-ABI processes (which use the
## kernel-side KernelFdTable) get a populated fd/.  Self-tests: classify_path
## (5 cases incl. reject), pure fd_link_target render for every HandleKind +
## File fallback, and a live readdir/readlink against a probe pid (boot:
## "[procfs]   fd/ classify ... OK" / "fd/ directory: gating OK").
##
## DONE (2026-06-12): real /proc/<pid>/fdinfo/<n> — per-fd pos/flags, honest.
## Built on the same kernel fd table as fd/ (pcb::linux_fd_list/lookup):
##   - procfs ProcPath gained PidFdInfoDir(pid) and PidFdInfoFile(pid, fd);
##     classify_path routes "<pid>/fdinfo" -> dir and "<pid>/fdinfo/<n>" ->
##     a REGULAR FILE (not a symlink, unlike fd/<n>).  readdir lists the open
##     fds as File entries; stat reports Directory / RegularFile; read_file
##     serves gen_pid_fdinfo.
##   - render_pid_fdinfo(pos, flags) emits Linux's fs/proc/fd.c shape
##     "pos:\t<f_pos>\nflags:\t0<octal>\n".  pos is the open-file
##     description's REAL f_pos for a File fd (new fs::handle::current_offset
##     accessor — raw file.offset, no APPEND->EOF adjustment), and 0 for
##     objects with no seek position (pipe/console/eventfd/pidfd/memfd) —
##     which is exactly what Linux reports for those, NOT a fabricated value.
##   - fdinfo_flags(entry) reproduces fs/proc/fd.c's cloexec handling: clear
##     any stale O_CLOEXEC in status_flags, then re-add O_CLOEXEC iff the
##     descriptor's FD_CLOEXEC is set (authoritative on the descriptor flag).
## DELIBERATE OMISSION (same honesty stance as the io/fd blocks above): the
## mnt_id: and ino: lines Linux also prints are NOT emitted.  We have no
## per-fd mount id and no stable inode number to report; printing 0 would be
## inventing data.  Tools that consume fdinfo (lsof, CRIU) look up pos:/flags:
## by key, so the omission does not break them.  If a real ino is ever wanted
## it must come from a genuine VFS inode number, not a placeholder.  The
## directory is honestly EMPTY for native processes (fd table in userspace),
## same as fd/.  Self-tests: classify_path (5 cases incl. reject), pure
## render_pid_fdinfo body, fdinfo_flags cloexec folding (3 cases), and live
## readdir/read gating (boot: "[procfs]   fdinfo/ classify ... OK" /
## "fdinfo/ directory: gating OK").
##
## FOLLOW-UP (2026-06-10): real /proc/<pid>/auxv (Linux-ABI processes ONLY)
## /proc/<pid>/auxv is NOT yet served.  PR_GET_AUXV likewise returns a single
## AT_NULL terminator (see kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~7123-7192) because no
## per-process saved auxv exists.
##
## ARCHITECTURE CORRECTION (2026-06-12, prompted by the operator): the auxv is
## a Linux / System V ABI construct, NOT a native-OS one.  An EARLIER version
## of this note proposed building a SysV auxv "during exec (Linux + native)"
## and writing AT_RANDOM bytes in the native setup_user_stack.  THAT WAS WRONG
## — it would leak Linux ABI into the native process-launch path, exactly the
## coupling the design rejects (Linux compat is a contained Linuxulator-style
## layer; native exec must stay Linux-free).  Correct stance:
##   - NATIVE processes have NO auxv, full stop.  They get argv/envp from the
##     kernel via SYS_PROCESS_GET_ARGS (posix/src/crt.rs synthesizes argc/argv/
##     envp; native canary/randomness come from a native mechanism, not auxv).
##     /proc/<pid>/auxv for a native process is therefore HONESTLY EMPTY (a
##     bare AT_NULL) — the same honest-empty stance as /proc/<pid>/fd for
##     native processes.  Do NOT touch setup_user_stack or the native exec path.
##   - auxv is a LINUX-ABI-process concept.  saved_auxv belongs in the Linux-ABI
##     per-process state (next to KernelFdTable, which is also Linux-ABI-only),
##     and is built by the LINUX COMPAT LOADER when it constructs the SysV
##     initial stack for a Linux binary.  That loader does not exist yet:
##     posix/src/linux_binfmt_elf.rs + sys_auxv.rs are inert scaffolding (AT_*
##     consts/types, no pub fn, no stack builder), so today nothing builds a
##     SysV stack for any process.
##   - Therefore /proc/<pid>/auxv is BLOCKED on real Linux-ABI ELF loading
##     (System V stack construction) — a Phase 5.1 Linux-compat-layer task.
##     When that lands: build the auxv inside that compat loader (the kernel's
##     elf.rs already parses e_entry/e_phoff/e_phentsize/e_phnum/p_vaddr, so the
##     AT_PHDR/AT_PHENT/AT_PHNUM/AT_ENTRY values are available; AT_PAGESZ is our
##     16 KiB FRAME_SIZE; AT_RANDOM points into that process's SysV stack),
##     stash a copy in the Linux-ABI PCB state, and serve it from BOTH
##     PR_GET_AUXV and a new gen_pid_auxv() (empty AT_NULL for native pids).
##   Net: not "risky to land in native exec" — it simply must NOT live in
##   native exec at all.  It rides in on the Linux compat ELF loader.
##
## UPDATE (2026-06-12): the SysV initial-stack builder NOW EXISTS —
## kernel/src/proc/linux_stack.rs::build_sysv_stack/install_linux_stack,
## wired into the AbiMode::Linux branch of proc::spawn (spawn_process + exec)
## only.  It already constructs the full auxv (AT_PAGESZ=4096 — NOTE: the
## Linux ABI reports 4096, NOT our 16 KiB FRAME_SIZE; an earlier line below
## misstated this — see design-decision #1; AT_PHDR/PHENT/PHNUM/ENTRY from
## elf.rs, AT_RANDOM into the process's own SysV stack, AT_EXECFN, identity
## uid/gid).  So a Linux binary now gets a correct argc/argv/envp/auxv stack.
## What remains for /proc/<pid>/auxv + PR_GET_AUXV: PERSIST a copy of that
## auxv into Linux-ABI per-process PCB state (alongside KernelFdTable) at
## install_linux_stack time, then serve it from PR_GET_AUXV and a new
## gen_pid_auxv() (honestly-empty AT_NULL for native pids).  The build is
## done; only the save+serve plumbing is left.
##
## RESOLVED (2026-06-12): the save+serve plumbing now exists.
##   - pcb::Process gained `linux_saved_auxv: Option<Vec<u8>>` (None for
##     native, Some for Linux-ABI), cloned across fork, cleared on exec
##     into a native image.  Accessors: pcb::set_linux_saved_auxv /
##     linux_saved_auxv / clear_linux_saved_auxv.
##   - install_linux_stack now returns InstalledLinuxStack { rsp,
##     auxv_bytes }; build_sysv_stack serializes the final auxv (incl.
##     AT_RANDOM/AT_EXECFN/AT_NULL) into SysvStackImage::auxv_bytes.
##     spawn_process + exec persist auxv_bytes via set_linux_saved_auxv.
##   - PR_GET_AUXV (syscall/linux.rs) serves caller_pid()'s saved auxv
##     when present, else the 16-byte AT_NULL terminator (native pids).
##   - /proc/<pid>/auxv served by gen_pid_auxv (procfs.rs), added to
##     PID_FILES: saved bytes for Linux-ABI pids, empty file for native.
##   - Tests: spawn self-test verifies auxv persisted (16-byte-aligned,
##     AT_NULL-terminated); existing PR_GET_AUXV batch-89 tests still pass
##     (boot/native context has no saved auxv -> 16-byte fallback).
## This auxv FOLLOW-UP block is now CLOSED.
##
## SYSTEMIC FINDING (2026-06-11): fabricated demo data in fs:: diagnostic
## modules' init_defaults() — a whole FAMILY, not isolated incidents.
## ~250 modules under kernel/src/fs/ define an init_defaults().  Many of the
## *statistics* ones (as opposed to *settings* ones) seed FABRICATED OBSERVED
## numbers — fake disks/sockets/tasks with invented byte/IO/latency counts —
## which their /proc/<name> generator and `<name>` kshell command would then
## surface AS IF REAL.  This violates the hard "never invent data in procfs"
## rule.  Two confirmed + FIXED so far by rebuilding init_defaults() empty,
## adding a real registration API where missing, and rewriting self_test() to
## build fixtures via the real API and reset to None afterward (no residue),
## then wiring the now-residue-free self_test into boot for coverage:
##   - taskstats.rs  (fixed; was: pid 1/100 with read_bytes 1_073_741_824 etc.)
##   - iolatency.rs  (fixed 2026-06-11; was: sda/nvme0n1, total_ios 37_000_000,
##                    total_slow 302000, fabricated histograms)
##   - netsock.rs    (fixed 2026-06-11; was: fake sockets, pid 100 →
##                    93.184.216.34:443 ESTABLISHED rx_bytes 50_000_000,
##                    retransmits 25; total_opened 5000, total_rx 500_000_000.
##                    Already had a full real API — open/close/set_state/
##                    record_traffic/record_retransmit — so the fix only emptied
##                    init_defaults and rebuilt the self_test; no new API needed.)
##   - slabstat.rs   (fixed 2026-06-11; was: 5 fake caches task_struct/
##                    inode_cache/dentry_cache/buffer_head/kmalloc-64,
##                    total_allocs 1_350_500. Full real API create_cache/alloc/
##                    free/reclaim already present; fix emptied init_defaults and
##                    rebuilt self_test.)
##   - futexstat.rs  (fixed 2026-06-11; was: fake futex addrs 0x7FFF_0000_1000
##                    waits 500000 total_wait_ns 50_000_000_000; fake per-proc
##                    rows pid 1/100; aggregate total_waits 600000. Real API
##                    record_wait/record_wake/record_timeout/record_contention
##                    already present; fix emptied init_defaults and rebuilt
##                    self_test.)
##   - pipestat.rs   (fixed 2026-06-11; was: 2 fake pipes, anon bytes_written

##                    record_read already present; fix emptied init_defaults and

##                    record_wait already present; fix emptied init_defaults and

##                    submit/complete/overflow already present; fix emptied

##   - netlat.rs     (fixed 2026-06-11; was: fake ifaces eth0/lo, rtt_samples
##                    5_000_000, rtt_total_ns 500_000_000_000, fabricated
##                    histograms; aggregate total_rtt_samples 6_000_000,
##                    total_proc_samples 12_000_000. Real API register_iface/
##                    record_rtt/record_processing already present; fix emptied
##                    init_defaults and rebuilt self_test, wired into boot.)
##   - migstat.rs    (fixed 2026-06-11; was: fake per-CPU migration counts
##                    cpu0..3 migrations_in 500_000 etc.; fake tasks pid 1
##                    "init"/pid 100 "shell"; aggregate total_migrations
##                    1_600_000, total_numa_crosses 39_000, reason_counts seeded.
##                    Added register_cpu(cpu_id) so the scheduler can register

##                    the right answer is a real registration API, not seeded
##                    rows — same pattern applies to rcustat/tlbstat below.)
##   - rcustat.rs    (fixed 2026-06-11; was: fake per-CPU rows cpu0..3
##                    callbacks_invoked 1000–2500, quiescent_states 50_000–80_000;

##   - numastat.rs   (fixed 2026-06-11; was: fake NUMA nodes 0/1, total_memory
##                    8 GiB each, local_allocs 50000, accesses 1_000_000; seeded

##                    total_remote 7000, total_migrations 250. Added

##                    to, dist) so the memory subsystem can register real ACPI

##                    (incl. exact 50% remote pct, 135ns blended latency), wired

##                    track_connection(5-tuple) (idempotent, NEW state, zeroed

##                    those paths are instrumented.)
##   - memdiag (2026-06-11): FIXED — milder than the rest. Memory diagnostics
##                    (RAM-test log + ECC-error tracking, surfaced by the
##                    `memdiag` kshell command). Its tests/ecc_errors vectors and
##                    all counters already started empty/zeroed; the ONLY
##                    fabrication was total_memory_kb: 1_048_576 (a hardcoded 1 GB
##                    placeholder), so `memdiag show` always printed "Total
##                    memory: 1048576 KB (1024 MB)" regardless of the machine's
##                    real RAM. Changed the default to 0 ("unknown until
##                    detected"); it now advances only on a real set_total_memory()
##                    call. NOTE on memlayout: I checked whether memdiag could be
##                    wired to memlayout::total_ram() instead — it CANNOT, because
##                    memlayout's own init_defaults() ALSO seeds hardcoded regions
##                    (a ~1 GiB "Main memory" region etc.), so total_ram() is
##                    itself fabricated, not a real limine map. (memlayout added
##                    to the flagged-candidates list below.) Rebuilt self_test
##                    residue-free (was 11 steps, not residue-free): clears STATE
##                    at start/end, asserts the empty default size is 0, exercises
##                    set_total_memory(2 GB) and asserts that value in step 8.
##                    NOTE: memdiag was NOT previously wired into the boot
##                    self-test path — now added. /proc has no memdiag generator;
##                    surfaced only via kshell, which now prints "0 KB (0 MB)"
##                    until detection runs. PROPER FIX DEFERRED: real RAM
##                    detection (the limine memory map consumer) should call
##                    memdiag::set_total_memory() with the actual installed size,

##                    is still simulated ("all tests pass") — a real RAM test
##                    (walking-bits/address-line patterns over a reserved range)
##                    is deferred until a safe scratch-mapping path exists.
##   - sysuptime (2026-06-11): AUDITED — NOT a fabrication case, LEFT FUNCTIONAL
##                    (do NOT empty it). Uptime/session tracker. Its
##                    init_defaults() records the REAL boot timestamp from
##                    hpet::elapsed_ns(); the current session genuinely IS session
##                    1 (total_sessions=1 counts the live session), history starts
##                    empty, and longest/total uptime are zeroed. No fabricated
##                    observations — emptying boot_timestamp_ns would BREAK real
##                    uptime tracking. Surfaced only via the `sysuptime` kshell
##                    command (no /proc generator), which prints real current
##                    uptime + session. TWO improvements made this tick: (1) fixed
##                    a latent correctness bug — init_defaults() was only ever
##                    called lazily from kshell, so "uptime" was measured from the
##                    first `sysuptime` command, not from boot; now main.rs calls
##                    fs::sysuptime::init_defaults() right after hpet::init() so
##                    the boot instant is captured early (idempotent, so the lazy
##                    kshell init is a harmless no-op afterwards). (2) Made the
##                    self_test residue-free (clears STATE at start AND end, then
##                    re-inits a clean live session) — it records 3 shutdowns, so
##                    without the reset it would leave fabricated session history
##                    resident; NOT wired into boot (even residue-free it resets
##                    the live boot timestamp, discarding the early-captured boot
##                    time). PROPER FIX DEFERRED: a kernel that persists a
##                    wall-clock boot record (RTC + on-disk uptime log) could
##                    report true boot time and real cross-boot session history;
##                    until then the HPET-relative boot instant is the honest best
##                    approximation and history is empty across boots.

##   - ioport (2026-06-11): FIXED — fabrication case (a), direct analog of iomem
##                    (port I/O vs MMIO). x86 I/O-port access stats (per-region
##                    in/out counts + byte totals, untracked-access counters),
##                    surfaced via /proc/ioport and the `ioport` kshell command.

##                    16.6M reads / 9.75M writes and 50K/20K untracked. The port

##                    calls record_in/out or register_region (only the kshell), so
##                    the traffic is pure fabrication — same call as iomem, where
##                    the equally-real LAPIC/IOAPIC/etc. regions were emptied
##                    because nothing actually registered them. Emptied
##                    init_defaults (no regions, zeroed totals); register_region
##                    (dedups on base → AlreadyExists) is the existing real API.
##                    Rebuilt self_test residue-free with exact assertions
##                    (register, tracked vs untracked accounting, range-boundary
##                    match at 0x103 in / 0x104 out, final 1 region / 104 reads /
##                    1 write / 2 untracked) and reset afterward. NOTE: ioport was
##                    NOT previously wired into the boot self-test path — now

##                    driver port claims) should call ioport::register_region for
##                    each claimed range and record_in/record_out on every access
##                    — wire that when port I/O is instrumented for auditing.

##   - memlayout (2026-06-11): FIXED — fabrication, but with the PROPER FIX (wired
##                    to the real data source, NOT just emptied). Physical/virtual
##                    memory region table, surfaced via /proc/memlayout and the
##                    `memlayout` kshell command. init_defaults() FABRICATED a

##                    kernel, kernel-heap, IOAPIC and Local-APIC ranges — so
##                    total_ram() ALWAYS reported ~1 GiB and list_regions() returned
##                    a region table matching NO real bootloader memory map,
##                    regardless of the machine's actual RAM. PROPER FIX: the kernel
##                    already has the real Limine memmap response (boot_info.
##                    memory_map: &[&MemmapEntry]). init_defaults() now seeds an
##                    EMPTY/zeroed table, and a new populate_from_memmap(entries) maps
##                    the Limine memmap types onto the RegionType taxonomy
##                    (USABLE→Usable, EXECUTABLE_AND_MODULES→KernelCode, etc.) and
##                    installs the REAL regions. main.rs calls it right after the heap
##                    is up (so /proc/memlayout reflects actual RAM as early as
##                    possible). self_test rewritten residue-free: snapshots the live
##                    (real) map, exercises populate_from_memmap/add_region/totals
##                    against a synthetic 4-entry map with EXACT assertions
##                    (region_count 4, total_ram = 1 MiB + 1 GiB, total_kernel/reserved
##                    = one page each, 2 usable + 1 kernel-code by type, add_region
##                    grows RAM exactly, final 5 regions), then RESTORES the real map
##                    so no fixtures leak. /proc/memlayout 141 bytes (now reflects the
##                    real QEMU memory map). No deferral — memlayout is fully wired to
##                    its real data source.
##   - irqbalance (2026-06-11): FIXED — fabrication, PROPER FIX (wired /proc to the
##                    REAL balancer + DELETED the redundant module). There were TWO
##                    irqbalance modules: the real interrupt balancer
##                    (kernel/src/irqbalance.rs, wired at boot, tracks live per-IRQ
##                    affinity from IRQ_STATES + SMP cpu_count) and a procfs
##                    diagnostic (kernel/src/fs/irqbalance.rs) that backed
##                    /proc/irqbalance. The fs module's init_defaults() seeded a
##                    FABRICATED IRQ table — timer/keyboard/rtc/ata0/ata1/eth0/usb0
##                    with invented affinity masks + assigned_cpu values (eth0→cpu2,
##                    usb0→cpu3) and a hardcoded 4-CPU cpu_loads layout
##                    (assigned_irqs 4/1/1/1) — none discovered from real hardware.
##                    (Counts were already 0, but the TABLE itself — which IRQs
##                    exist, their CPU assignments, the CPU count — was invented.)
##                    The fs module was also DEAD: its init_defaults() was never
##                    called anywhere (no boot wiring, and the kshell `irqbalance`
##                    command uses the real crate::irqbalance), so /proc/irqbalance
##                    actually always read an uninitialised (0,0,0,0) State — but it
##                    was a loaded footgun. PROPER FIX: rewrote procfs::gen_irqbalance
##                    to read crate::irqbalance::stats() (enabled, cpu_count,
##                    balance_ops, migrations) + irq_info() (live active IRQs with
##                    cpu/pinned/hint/rate), then DELETED kernel/src/fs/irqbalance.rs
##                    and its `pub mod irqbalance;` in fs/mod.rs. /proc/irqbalance now
##                    90 bytes reflecting the running balancer (single CPU in QEMU →
##                    balancer disabled, irq_count 0 — honest). No deferral.

##                    a naive hook. Do both as a focused task once fault accounting
##                    has a per-process source and a hot-path-safe record sink. Until
##                    then the empty table is honest.
##   - vmfrag (2026-06-11): FIXED — fabrication case (a) (full register_zone/
##                    update_index/record_compaction API already existed). Kernel-side
##                    VM-fragmentation monitor behind /proc/vmfrag and the `vmfrag`
##                    kshell command. init_defaults() FABRICATED two zones — DMA32
##                    (frag_index [0,50,120,250,400,550,700,800,850,900,950], 5000
##                    compactions / 4000 success / 1000 fail) and Normal (frag_index
##                    [0,30,80,150,300,450,600,700,780,850,920], 50000/40000/10000) —
##                    plus invented totals (55000 compactions / 44000 success / 11000
##                    fail), which /proc/vmfrag and the per_zone view displayed as if
##                    real. DMA32/Normal are Linux zone names; this kernel has a single
##                    global buddy allocator (crate::mm::frame, free_lists per order)
##                    with NO named-zone taxonomy and NO memory-compaction subsystem,
##                    so register_zone/update_index/record_compaction have NO real
##                    callers — the module is entirely unwired (only kshell interactive
##                    subcommands + procfs reads). Fixed: init_defaults() now seeds an
##                    EMPTY table (no zones, zero totals). Also: the self_test was
##                    NEVER wired into boot AND relied on the fabricated zones (asserted
##                    len==2, compactions>55000) with no end-reset — so wiring it would
##                    have leaked a TestZone fixture into /proc/vmfrag. Rewrote it
##                    residue-free (clears STATE at start AND end) with EXACT assertions
##                    (empty init, register→1 zone, dup rejected, update_index 100,
##                    invalid index/order rejected, compaction success/fail, not-found,
##                    final 1 zone / 2 compactions / 1 success / 1 fail) and WIRED it
##                    into the boot self-test path. /proc/vmfrag now reports an empty
##                    table (truthful).
##                    DEFERRED PROPER FIX: the fragmentation index for an order is
##                    derived from the buddy allocator's free-block counts per order
##                    (Linux's frag-index formula over /proc/buddyinfo). The proper fix
##                    is to expose crate::mm::frame's per-order free_lists counts via a
##                    buddyinfo-style accessor and compute real indices from it, with a
##                    single "global" zone (this kernel has no DMA32/Normal split).
##                    Deferred because: (1) mm::frame does not yet expose per-order free
##                    counts publicly, and (2) the compaction counters need a real
##                    memory-compaction subsystem, which this kernel does not have. Do
##                    as a focused task once a buddyinfo accessor and a compaction path
##                    exist. Until then the empty table is honest.
##   - ipclog (2026-06-11): FIXED — fabrication case (a) (full record/clear/
##                    set_enabled API already existed). Kernel-side IPC message log
##                    behind /proc/ipclog and the `ipclog` kshell command.
##                    init_defaults() FABRICATED three channels — system_bus (ch 1,
##                    100 sent/100 recv/25600/12800 bytes/15us), vfs_channel (ch 2,
##                    500/500/204800/1048576/45us/2 err), gui_events (ch 3, 1000/980/
##                    64000/32000/5us) — which /proc/ipclog and list_channels displayed
##                    as if real IPC traffic. The per-channel counts were inconsistent
##                    with the system totals (seeded at 0). record() has NO real callers
##                    (the IPC subsystem crate::ipc never calls it); procfs/kshell only
##                    read or run interactive subcommands. Fixed: init_defaults() now
##                    seeds an EMPTY log (no channels, no messages, logging_enabled
##                    kept true as a real setting). Also: the self_test was NEVER wired
##                    into boot AND relied on the fabricated channels (asserted len==3,
##                    messages_sent>=101) with no end-reset — so wiring it would have
##                    leaked fixtures into /proc/ipclog. Rewrote it residue-free (clears
##                    STATE at start AND end) with EXACT assertions (empty init,
##                    record→seq 1, query by pid/channel, channel 1 has 1 sent/1 recv,
##                    auto-create channel 99, clear empties message buffer, final 2
##                    channels / 0 messages / 3 total / 896 bytes / 0 errors) and WIRED
##                    it into the boot self-test path. /proc/ipclog now reports an empty
##                    log (truthful).
##                    DEFERRED PROPER FIX: the real IPC channel registry lives in
##                    crate::ipc (channels created dynamically by ID, not the fabricated
##                    system_bus/vfs_channel/gui_events names). The proper fix is to
##                    have the IPC send/receive paths call ipclog::record(msg_type, src,
##                    dst, channel, size, latency, label) on every message when logging
##                    is enabled. Deferred because: (1) IPC send/recv is a perf-critical
##                    hot path (<2us round-trip target) and an unconditional Mutex +
##                    Vec push per message would blow the budget — it needs a lock-free
##                    / ring-buffer / per-CPU design gated on logging_enabled, and (2)
##                    the channel_id → name mapping must come from the real channel
##                    registry. Do as a focused task once the IPC layer exposes a
##                    hot-path-safe trace hook. Until then the empty log is honest.
##   - telemetry (2026-06-11): FIXED — fabrication case (a) (full register_metric/
##                    record/remove/export API already existed). Kernel-side metric
##                    registry behind /proc/telemetry and the `telemetry` kshell
##                    command. init_defaults() FABRICATED four metrics with invented
##                    OBSERVED values — cpu.usage_pct 15%, mem.used_mb 512, disk.iops
##                    1200, net.rx_bytes 1048576 — plus a fabricated total_samples of
##                    4, which /proc/telemetry and list_metrics/by_category displayed
##                    as if real measured telemetry. record()/register_metric() have NO
##                    real callers (no subsystem publishes telemetry); procfs/kshell
##                    only read or run interactive subcommands. Fixed: init_defaults()
##                    now seeds an EMPTY registry (no metrics, zero samples;
##                    collection_enabled true + interval 5000ms kept as real settings).
##                    Also: the self_test was NEVER wired into boot AND relied on the
##                    fabricated metrics (asserted len==4, cpu value==15) with no
##                    end-reset — so wiring it would have leaked fixtures into
##                    /proc/telemetry. Rewrote it residue-free (clears STATE at start
##                    AND end) with EXACT assertions (empty init, register→0 value/0
##                    samples, gauge record→42, counter accumulates 4096+1024, dup
##                    rejected, by_category, export bumps count, final 3 metrics / 3
##                    samples / 1 export) and WIRED it into the boot self-test path.
##                    /proc/telemetry now reports an empty registry (truthful).
##                    DEFERRED PROPER FIX: the proper fix is for real producers to
##                    register and feed metrics — cpu usage from sched, mem from
##                    crate::mm, disk iops from the block layer, net bytes from the NIC
##                    drivers — by calling register_metric at init and record() on each
##                    collection interval. Deferred because: (1) there is no periodic
##                    telemetry collector task yet (collection_interval_ms is stored but
##                    nothing consumes it), and (2) each producer needs a real sampled
##                    source (cpu% needs delta sampling, etc.) that isn't centrally
##                    exposed. Do as a focused task once a collector task and the
##                    per-source sampling exist. Until then the empty registry is honest.
##   - fdtable (2026-06-11): FIXED — fabrication case (a) (full open/close/dup/
##                    set_max_fds API already existed). Kernel-side FD-table tracker
##                    behind /proc/fdtable and the `fdtable` kshell command.
##                    init_defaults() FABRICATED two process FD tables — pid 1 with 4
##                    FDs (/dev/console ×3 as stdin/out/err + /etc/init.conf) and pid
##                    100 with 5 (pipe:[1234] ×2, /dev/null, socket:[5678],
##                    /var/log/sshd.log) — plus an invented total_opens of 9, which
##                    /proc/fdtable and list_tables displayed as if real open FDs.
##                    open/close/dup have NO real callers (the VFS does not call this
##                    parallel tracker); procfs/kshell only read or run interactive
##                    subcommands. Fixed: init_defaults() now seeds an EMPTY table (no
##                    process tables, zero totals). Also: the self_test was NEVER wired
##                    into boot AND relied on the fabricated tables (asserted len==2,
##                    pid 1 has 4 fds, fd 0 == /dev/console) with no end-reset — so
##                    wiring it would have leaked fixtures into /proc/fdtable. Rewrote
##                    it residue-free (clears STATE at start AND end) with EXACT
##                    assertions (empty init, open→auto-create fd 0, close, dup clears
##                    cloexec, second process, get round-trip, fd-limit enforcement,
##                    final 3 tables / 5 opens / 1 close / 1 dup) and WIRED it into the
##                    boot self-test path. /proc/fdtable now reports an empty table
##                    (truthful).
##                    DEFERRED PROPER FIX: the authoritative per-process FD table is
##                    the PCB's linux_fd_table (Option<Box<crate::proc::linux_fd::
##                    KernelFdTable>>, used by the POSIX layer). The proper fix is to
##                    make fdtable's read paths (list/get/list_tables/stats) a
##                    read-through that enumerates live processes and reads each PCB's
##                    KernelFdTable, mapping its entries → fdtable::FdEntry (fd, type,
##                    path, flags, offset). Deferred because: (1) it needs a live-
##                    process enumeration entry point feeding pid → linux_fd_table, and
##                    (2) KernelFdTable stores handles/types differently than this
##                    module's FdType/FdFlags/path, so the mapping must be defined
##                    deliberately rather than guessed. Do as a focused task once a
##                    process-enumeration hook is exposed. Until then the empty table
##                    is honest.

##     as the single source of truth. init_defaults now reads the REAL cmdline
##     and parses each whitespace-separated key[=value] token into a Bootloader-

##     cmdline, so the honest result is an EMPTY param set — and it auto-adapts
##     if a cmdline is ever configured. self_test rewritten residue-free: tests
##     parse_cmdline as a pure fn, installs a known controlled State for the
##     stateful API tests, ends with STATE = None. boot-test PASSED (26s).

## DONE (2026-06-10): /proc/<pid>/task/<tid>/ thread directory
## Nested-dir support is implemented (kernel/src/fs/procfs.rs):
## ProcPath::PidTaskDir/PidTaskTidDir/PidTaskFile variants, classify_path
## routes the `task/` subtree, every PID dir now lists a `task` subdir,
## and readdir/read_file/stat handle the three levels. thread_belongs()
## gates each path on get_threads(pid).contains(tid). Files served per
## thread: comm + schedstat + stat + status (TASK_FILES) via
## generate_task(pid,tid,name). comm/schedstat key purely on the scheduler
## task id; stat is served via gen_thread_stat(pid,tid) which splits the
## 52-field line into thread fields (field1=tid, comm, state, utime,
## priority, exit_code — from the scheduler task `tid`) and process-wide
## fields (ppid, pgrp/session, num_threads, vsize, rss, rsslim — from the
## owning `pid`). status is served via gen_thread_status(pid,tid) with the
## same split: thread fields (Name, State, Pid=tid, ctxt switches) from
## `tid`, process-wide (Tgid=pid, PPid, Umask, Uid/Gid/Groups, Vm*, Threads)
## from `pid`. build_pid_stat / build_pid_status are the shared cores; each
## process's own /proc/<pid>/{stat,status} calls them with proc_id==task.id
## so the id sources coincide and that output is unchanged. Self-tested
## (9 classify cases + live gating + live thread-stat field1==probe and
## thread-status Pid/Tgid==probe, both rejecting a bogus tid with NotFound).
## DONE — no per-thread files remain outstanding. (Linux also exposes
## task/<tid>/{maps,statm,limits,…}; those are address-space-wide and
## identical to the process files, so add them only if a tool needs them.)
##
## DONE (2026-06-10): writable /proc/<pid>/oom_score_adj
## /proc/<pid>/oom_score_adj now accepts writes (kernel/src/fs/procfs.rs:
## ProcFs::write_file override → set_pid_oom_score_adj → parse_oom_score_adj
## → fs::oomkiller::adjust_score). The write_file override routes only
## PidFile(pid,"oom_score_adj") to the setter; every other procfs path
## stays NotSupported, preserving the read-only contract. parse_oom_score_adj
## trims whitespace/newline, parses a signed int, and rejects out-of-range
## (-1000..=1000) / malformed input with InvalidArgument. A process the OOM
## subsystem has not registered returns NotFound from adjust_score (we have
## nowhere to persist an adjustment for an untracked process). Self-tested
## (6 accept + 5 reject parse cases, plus a live malformed-write rejection).
## REMAINING: lazily register a process with the oomkiller on first write so
## an adj can be stored before the OOM scanner first sees the process — only
## needed if a ported app writes oom_score_adj very early in its lifetime.
##
## DONE (2026-06-10): /proc/<pid>/exe symlink
## procfs serves /proc/<pid>/{cwd,root,exe} as real symlinks
## (kernel/src/fs/procfs.rs: PID_LINKS, ProcPath::PidLink, readlink()).
## exe is backed by Process::exe_path (kernel/src/proc/pcb.rs): a Vec<u8>
## inherited on fork and overwritten/cleared on exec.  Captured at spawn
## via SpawnOptions::exe_path (shell `run` passes the resolved path) and
## at exec via exec_process(.., exe_path) — linux_execve canonicalises the
## filename against the process cwd; the native raw-bytes exec passes None
## (clears the stale path).  Operator approved on 2026-06-10.
##   KNOWN LIMITATION: the native raw-ELF spawn syscall (sys_process_spawn)
##     and exec-with-frame have no filesystem path, so binaries launched
##     that way report /proc/<pid>/exe = NotFound.  This is correct given
##     no path exists; when path-based spawn lands (see the "final
##     implementation" note in handlers.rs::sys_process_spawn), thread the
##     resolved path into SpawnOptions::exe_path there too.
