##          There is no path where a restart of sigsuspend would be correct
##          (restarting would re-suspend, which is wrong), so the only achievable
##          outcome IS -EINTR.  Implementing ERESTARTNOHAND would also need the
##          no-handler checkpoint to restore saved_sigmask before re-installing
##          the temp mask (mask-corruption hazard) — pure cost, zero benefit.
##          Decision: leave at -EINTR; do not convert.
##        - signalfd blocking read DONE 2026-06-18: was the same hang-bug
##          class — dispatch_signalfd_read (linux.rs) registered its
##          signal-waiter with ONLY the fd's acceptance mask, so a deliverable
##          signal NOT in that mask (e.g. SIGUSR1 with a handler installed)
##          could never wake the parked read.  The handler runs only at the
##          syscall-return checkpoint, which a parked read never reaches, so the
##          thread blocked forever.  Fix:
##            * Park with wait_mask = acceptance-mask | deliverable (deliverable
##              = !blocked(caller)), so set_pending wakes the park for EITHER a
##              watched signal (to drain into records) OR any other deliverable
##              signal (to be interrupted).
##            * After the drain produces 0 records, before re-parking, check
##              interrupt_mask = deliverable & !acceptance-mask; if a signal is
##              pending there, return restart::restart_result(ERESTARTSYS).  The
##              syscall-return checkpoint then runs the handler + restarts
##              (SA_RESTART) or surfaces -EINTR (and for a no-handler default-
##              terminate signal, terminates).  signalfd read returns its
##              SyscallResult raw through dispatch_read/sys_read (no
##              linux_from_native wrap), so the -512 sentinel reaches the return
##              path untouched (no CrossDevice collision).  readv path passes a
##              first-iov sentinel through and truncates on partial data —
##              matching Linux.
##            * Ring-3 test (elf::build_linux_signalfd_interrupt_test_elf +
##              spawn::self_test_linux_signalfd_interrupt, wired into main.rs):
##              child installs a NON-SA_RESTART SIGUSR1 handler, creates a
##              signalfd watching only SIGUSR2, blocks in read(); kernel posts
##              the out-of-mask SIGUSR1; a correct kernel wakes it and the read
##              returns -EINTR → exit(0x3D).  A park-uninterruptible regression
##              leaves the child parked forever (non-zombie → FAIL); draining the
##              signal as data → exit 0xEE → FAIL.  This DISTINGUISHES the fix
##              from the bug (a restart-based two-signal design would not, since
##              the handler would still run at the post-read checkpoint either
##              way).  Build clean (32.10s) + boot-test PASSED (229s); serial
##              shows "exit == 61: OK".
##        - eventfd blocking read/write DONE 2026-06-18: same single-slot-waiter
##          hang-bug class as pipe/stream_socket/signalfd, Linux-reachable via
##          eventfd2 (syscall 290).  All 4 block sites in ipc/eventfd.rs (read,
##          write, read_timeout, write_timeout) parked with a bare
##          block_current() and a single reader_waiter/writer_waiter slot that
##          only the opposite side woke — a deliverable signal could never
##          interrupt them.  Fix (mirror of pipe.rs):
##            * 3 helpers after EVENTFD_TABLE: current_user_pid(),
##              deliverable_signal_pending(pid), park_for_eventfd(pid, task)
##              (register-then-recheck against the signal-waiter set; pid 0 =
##              kernel task → plain block_current, unchanged).
##            * Each indefinite block site: check deliverable_signal_pending
##              before parking — clear our own waiter slot if set, return
##              Err(Interrupted); replace block_current with park_for_eventfd.
##            * Each timed block site: same, plus cancel the hrtimer before
##              returning Interrupted.
##            * linux.rs dispatch_eventfd_read / dispatch_eventfd_write map
##              Err(Interrupted) → restart::restart_result(ERESTARTSYS) (eventfd
##              is a slow object → restartable under SA_RESTART; the -512
##              sentinel reaches the return path untouched, no CrossDevice
##              collision since these arms don't pass through linux_from_native).
##              The dispatch arms only call the indefinite read/write, so only
##              the ERESTARTSYS mapping is reachable here; the timed variants
##              return EINTR if/when separately wired.
##            * Ring-3 test (elf::build_linux_eventfd_interrupt_test_elf +
##              spawn::self_test_linux_eventfd_interrupt, wired into main.rs):
##              child installs a NON-SA_RESTART SIGUSR1 handler, creates
##              eventfd2(0,0) (counter 0), blocks in read(); kernel posts
##              SIGUSR1; a correct kernel wakes it and read returns -EINTR →
##              exit(0x2C=44).  A park-uninterruptible regression leaves the
##              child parked forever (non-zombie → FAIL); draining as data →
##              exit 0xEE → FAIL.  Build clean (36.77s) + boot-test PASSED;
##              serial shows "exit == 44: OK".
##        - timerfd blocking read DONE 2026-06-18: same single-slot-waiter
##          hang-bug class as pipe/eventfd/signalfd, Linux-reachable via
##          timerfd_create (283) + read.  read_expirations_blocking (ipc/
##          timerfd.rs) parked with a bare block_current() and a single
##          reader_waiter slot woken only by settime / the expiry hrtimer — a
##          deliverable signal could never interrupt it (a disarmed-timerfd read
##          blocks indefinitely, so this hung forever).  Fix (mirror of pipe.rs):
##            * 3 helpers (current_user_pid, deliverable_signal_pending,
##              park_for_timerfd) after timerfd_wake.  park_for_timerfd registers
##              a signal-waiter via the register-then-recheck idiom (the expiry
##              hrtimer remains a separate wake source); pid 0 → plain
##              block_current.
##            * The block loop checks deliverable_signal_pending before parking
##              (clearing its own reader_waiter slot, returning Err(Interrupted))
##              and parks via park_for_timerfd.
##            * dispatch_timerfd_read (linux.rs) maps Err(Interrupted) →
##              restart::restart_result(ERESTARTSYS) (timerfd is a slow object →
##              restartable under SA_RESTART; -512 sentinel bypasses
##              linux_from_native so no CrossDevice collision).
##            * Ring-3 test (elf::build_linux_timerfd_interrupt_test_elf +
##              spawn::self_test_linux_timerfd_interrupt, wired into main.rs):
##              child installs a NON-SA_RESTART SIGUSR1 handler, creates a
##              timerfd_create(CLOCK_MONOTONIC,0) it never arms, blocks in
##              read(); kernel posts SIGUSR1; correct kernel wakes it and read
##              returns -EINTR → exit(0x1B=27).  Park-uninterruptible regression
##              → parked forever (non-zombie → FAIL); count-as-data → 0xEE →
##              FAIL.  Build clean (31.43s) + boot-test PASSED; serial shows
##              "exit == 27: OK".
##        - inotify blocking read DONE 2026-06-18: same hang-bug class, Linux-
##          reachable via inotify_init1 (294) + read.  dispatch_inotify_read
##          (linux.rs) parked with a bare block_current() and registered ONLY a
##          notify-waiter (fs::notify::register_notify_waiter) — a deliverable
##          signal could never interrupt it (a read of an inotify fd with no
##          queued events blocks indefinitely, so it hung forever).  Fix is
##          inline in dispatch_inotify_read (not a pipe.rs mirror — this is a
##          syscall-layer loop, not an ipc module):
##            * Before parking, check has_pending_in_mask(caller, deliverable)
##              (deliverable = !blocked(caller)); if pending, return
##              restart::restart_result(ERESTARTSYS).
##            * Register a signal-waiter (register_signalfd_waiter) ALONGSIDE the
##              notify-waiter, with the same register-then-recheck idiom (recheck
##              both is_readable AND has_pending_in_mask before parking);
##              deregister both on every exit path.  A signal wake re-drains the
##              loop and the top-of-branch deliverable check returns ERESTARTSYS.
##            * inotify read returns its SyscallResult raw through
##              dispatch_read/sys_read (no linux_from_native wrap), so the -512
##              sentinel reaches the return path untouched (no CrossDevice
##              collision).
##            * Ring-3 test (elf::build_linux_inotify_interrupt_test_elf +
##              spawn::self_test_linux_inotify_interrupt, wired into main.rs):
##              child installs a NON-SA_RESTART SIGUSR1 handler, creates
##              inotify_init1(0) with no watches, blocks in read(); kernel posts
##              SIGUSR1; correct kernel wakes it and read returns -EINTR →
##              exit(0x66=102).  Park-uninterruptible regression → parked forever
##              (non-zombie → FAIL); data-instead-of-EINTR → 0xEE → FAIL.  Build
##              clean (31.62s) + boot-test PASSED; serial shows "exit == 102: OK".
##          NOTE: DrmCard read is non-blocking (EAGAIN when no event, never
##          parks) — no bug.  AlsaPcm capture returns silence (never parks).
##          MemFd is memory-backed (never parks).  The native Slate IPC
##          semaphore / channel / completion primitives are NOT on the Linux-ABI
##          read path (separate native syscalls), so they are outside this
##          SA_RESTART taxonomy.  This completes the Linux-reachable slow-object
##          blocking-read sweep: pipe / stream_socket / signalfd / eventfd /
##          timerfd / inotify (+ futex / wait4 done earlier).
##        - poll / select / epoll_wait DONE 2026-06-18: the "always -EINTR,
##          NEVER restarted (even under SA_RESTART)" branch of the taxonomy.
##          These three wait primitives (poll_core / select_core /
##          epoll_wait_core in syscall/linux.rs) busy-polled fd readiness in
##          ≤10 ms sched::sleep_ms slices and NEVER checked for a pending
##          signal, so a process blocked in poll/select/epoll_wait could not be
##          interrupted at all — it slept forever while the signal stayed
##          pending (same hang-bug class as the slow-object reads, but the fix
##          surfaces -EINTR, not a restart sentinel: per POSIX/Linux these are
##          on the always-fail-with-EINTR list and are never transparently
##          restarted).  Fix (syscall/linux.rs):
##            * caller_has_deliverable_signal(pid: Option<u64>) =
##              has_pending_in_mask(p, !blocked(p)); None (in-kernel caller) →
##              false.
##            * interruptible_wait_slice(pid, slice_ms): registers a
##              signal-waiter (register_signalfd_waiter, mask = !blocked) around
##              the sleep so set_pending WAKES THE WAIT PROMPTLY via try_wake
##              instead of after the full 10 ms slice (Linux waitqueue
##              semantics).  Register-then-recheck closes the post-before-park
##              race; deregister on every exit path; returns true iff a
##              deliverable signal is pending (→ caller returns -EINTR).  The
##              10 ms slice is retained purely as the fd-readiness re-poll
##              mechanism (another task can change a polled fd's state without
##              waking us).  pid None → plain sleep_ms (kernel task, no signal
##              queue).
##            * interruptible_sleep_ms (the nfds==0 pure-timed-wait quick path
##              of poll/select) now loops over interruptible_wait_slice, so
##              poll(NULL,0,timeout) / select with no fds is also interruptible.
##            * All three cores' wait loops: replaced the bare
##              `sleep_ms(slice)` (and the now-redundant leading
##              caller_has_deliverable_signal check) with
##              `if interruptible_wait_slice(pid, slice) { return EINTR }`.
##            * These return linux_err(EINTR) (-4), NOT a restart sentinel, so
##              there is no -512/CrossDevice collision concern and SA_RESTART
##              cannot resurrect the call — correct per the taxonomy.
##            * Ring-3 test (elf::build_linux_poll_interrupt_test_elf +
##              spawn::self_test_linux_poll_interrupt, wired into main.rs):
##              child installs a SIGUSR1 handler, eventfd2(0,0) (never
##              POLLIN-ready), poll(&pollfd,1,-1) blocks; kernel posts SIGUSR1;
##              correct kernel wakes the registered signal-waiter and poll
##              returns -EINTR → exit(0x4B=75).  Parked-forever regression →
##              non-zombie FAIL; data-instead-of-EINTR (poll returned >=0) →
##              0xEE FAIL.  Build clean (33.87s) + boot-test PASSED; serial
##              shows "exit == 75: OK".
##          NOTE: select_core/epoll_wait_core share interruptible_wait_slice and
##          got the identical loop edit, so they are covered by the same fix
##          (the poll ring-3 test exercises the shared helper end-to-end).
##        - poll(NULL,0,-1) / select empty-set infinite-wait DONE 2026-06-18:
##          the nfds==0 (and select nfds==0||len==0) quick paths only slept for
##          a POSITIVE timeout and returned ok(0) for a negative (infinite)
##          one, so poll(NULL,0,-1) returned 0 immediately instead of blocking
##          until a signal (Linux treats an empty-set infinite poll like
##          pause()).  Fix: new empty_set_wait(timeout) helper shared by
##          poll_core/select_core — timeout==0 → ok(0); timeout>0 →
##          interruptible_sleep_ms (unchanged); timeout<0 → loop on
##          interruptible_wait_slice until a deliverable signal → -EINTR.  An
##          in-kernel caller (pid None) returns ok(0) for the infinite case
##          rather than hanging unbreakably (no signal queue to wake it).  Ring-3
##          test (elf::build_linux_poll_empty_infinite_test_elf +
##          spawn::self_test_linux_poll_empty_infinite, wired into main.rs):
##          child installs a SIGUSR1 handler, poll(NULL,0,-1) blocks; kernel
##          posts SIGUSR1; poll returns -EINTR → exit(0x5C=92).  Bug regression
##          → child exits 0xEE (poll returned 0) before the signal is posted.
##          Build clean (34.77s) + boot-test PASSED; serial shows "exit==92:OK".
##        - futex(FUTEX_WAIT / FUTEX_WAIT_BITSET) DONE 2026-06-18: was the
##          same confirmed bug class as wait4 — the native futex_wait_bitset /
##          futex_wait_bitset_timeout paths registered ONLY in the futex bucket
##          and never as a signal-waiter, and had no signal check, so a thread
##          blocked in FUTEX_WAIT (e.g. glibc pthread_cond_wait, pthread_join's
##          lll_wait_tid) could not be interrupted by a signal AT ALL — it slept
##          forever while the signal stayed pending.  Fix (ipc/futex.rs +
##          handlers.rs + linux.rs + error.rs):
##            * New KernelError::Interrupted (-8 → EINTR via linux_errno_for /
##              kernel_error_from_code).
##            * remove_self_waiter(addr, addr_space, task) helper: dequeues this
##              task's lingering futex waiter (original bucket then full scan for
##              a CMP_REQUEUE move); the timeout path's old inline dequeue was
##              refactored onto it.
##            * Indefinite futex_wait_bitset and timed futex_wait_bitset_timeout
##              now register as signal-waiters (register-then-recheck) for USER
##              processes only (pid 0 kernel tasks / boot self-tests keep the
##              unchanged plain block_current), and on a signal wake dequeue
##              self + return Err(Interrupted).  Spurious wakes (still queued,
##              no deliverable signal) return Ok(true) so glibc re-checks.
##            * Handlers: indefinite sys_futex_wait / sys_futex_wait_bitset map
##              Err(Interrupted) → ERESTARTSYS sentinel (FUTEX_WAIT is
##              restartable; a restart re-checks *uaddr==val).  The TIMED
##              handlers pass Err(Interrupted) through → -EINTR (a relative
##              timeout must NOT be restarted from scratch — that would reset the
##              clock; Linux uses ERESTART_RESTARTBLOCK with the absolute
##              deadline, here simplified to EINTR, which glibc tolerates by
##              recomputing the timeout on retry).
##            * linux_from_futex_wait now passes any restart sentinel through
##              untouched — critical, because the negated sentinel -512
##              (ERESTARTSYS) collides with KernelError::CrossDevice == -512 and
##              would otherwise be mis-mapped to EXDEV by linux_from_native.
##            * No-livelock: same argument as wait4 — classify_post only keeps a
##              signal deliverable-and-pending when it has a handler, so the
##              ERESTARTSYS always resolves to handler-run + restart/EINTR.
##            * PI futexes (FUTEX_LOCK_PI / FUTEX_WAIT_REQUEUE_PI) intentionally
##              NOT touched (priority-inheritance restart semantics differ).
##            * self_test_restart_action extended: Interrupted→EINTR mapping,
##              code -8 round-trip, sentinel pass-through, timed→EINTR.
##            Build + clippy (no new warns) + boot-test PASSED (237s; glibc
##            pthread/futex unaffected).  Ring-3 end-to-end test (step c)
##            now DONE 2026-06-18 — see (c) below.
##        - pipe read/write DONE 2026-06-18: same confirmed bug class — the
##          blocking pipe primitives (ipc/pipe.rs read/write/wait_readable and
##          the timed read_timeout/write_timeout) all parked with a bare
##          sched::block_current() and a single-slot reader_waiter/writer_waiter,
##          with NO signal-waiter registration and NO signal check, so a process
##          blocked on an empty/full pipe (e.g. a shell pipeline stage) could
##          never be interrupted by a signal — it slept forever while the signal
##          stayed pending.  Fix (ipc/pipe.rs + syscall/linux.rs):
##            * Three helpers after `static PIPES`: current_user_pid() (owner pid
##              or 0 for a kernel task), deliverable_signal_pending(pid) (pending
##              ∩ !blocked, always false for pid 0), and park_for_pipe(pid,task)
##              — registers a signal-waiter with the register-then-recheck idiom,
##              blocks, then deregisters; pid 0 parks uninterruptibly as before.
##            * All 5 block sites: before parking, if deliverable_signal_pending
##              they clear any stale waiter slot (reader/writer_waiter == self)
##              and return Err(Interrupted); the indefinite sites then park via
##              park_for_pipe.  The 2 TIMED sites additionally cancel the hrtimer
##              before returning Interrupted.
##            * Slot-clear on signal wake is the key subtlety: the waker .take()s
##              the slot before waking, so a signal wake (not a peer wake) leaves
##              the slot set — the interrupted task must clear its own slot.
##            * New linux_from_slow_io() in linux.rs: maps native Interrupted (-8)
##              → ERESTARTSYS sentinel (slow object, SA_RESTART-restartable);
##              everything else (success, EOF=0, real errors) passes through
##              linux_from_native.  Wired into dispatch_read/dispatch_write Pipe
##              arms (so read/write AND readv/writev, which loop over those, are
##              covered).  The timed pipe paths return Interrupted but are reached
##              only by non-read/write callers and map to plain EINTR — correct,
##              a relative timeout must not restart from scratch.
##            * NOTE/edge: splice/sendfile pipe sources (splice_read/splice_write)
##              still map Interrupted → EINTR via linux_from_native (not the
##              ERESTARTSYS sentinel).  splice IS restartable on Linux, but the
##              blocking window is tiny (allow_block only on the first read) and
##              EINTR is safe; left for a later pass if it ever bites.
##            * self_test_restart_action extended: linux_from_slow_io(Interrupted)
##              →ERESTARTSYS, (0)→0 passthrough, (ChannelClosed)→EPIPE.
##            Build clean + boot-test PASSED (229s).  Ring-3 end-to-end test
##            (step c) now DONE 2026-06-18 — see (c) below.
##        - Unix stream socket (socketpair) send/recv DONE 2026-06-18: the
##          blocking ipc/stream_socket.rs primitives (send/recv and the timed
##          send_timeout/recv_timeout) had the identical hang bug as pipes —
##          bare block_current() with a single-slot reader/writer_waiter, no
##          signal-waiter registration, no signal check.  Applied the same fix:
##          current_user_pid / deliverable_signal_pending / park_for_socket
##          helpers (mirroring ipc::pipe), and all 4 block sites now clear a
##          stale waiter slot + return Err(Interrupted) on a deliverable signal
##          (timed sites also cancel the hrtimer) and park via park_for_socket.
##          NOTE — currently LATENT: the Linux ABI socketpair(AF_UNIX) still
##          returns ENOSYS (no Linux-side backend wired), so these primitives
##          are reachable only via the native SYS_SOCKETPAIR_* syscalls, which
##          return Err(Interrupted) (-8) directly — there is no Linux signal
##          frame / ERESTARTSYS translation on the native path, so no
##          linux_from_slow_io mapping was added.  When socketpair is wired to
##          the Linux fd table (read/write + recv/send via dispatch_*), route
##          its Pipe-like arms through linux_from_slow_io for SA_RESTART, exactly
##          as the pipe arms do.  Fix is safe/inert until then (identical
##          behaviour when no signal is pending; boot self-tests run as pid 0 →
##          uninterruptible park, unchanged).  Build clean + boot-test PASSED
##          (232s).
##        - wait4 DONE 2026-06-18: was a confirmed bug — the blocking loop
##          had NO signal-interruption check and did not register as a
##          signal-waiter, so a process blocked in wait4 could not be
##          interrupted by a signal at all (set_pending only wakes
##          signal-waiters; wait4 only registered on the child wait-task
##          list).  Both the specific-pid and any-child loops now: (1) check
##          for a deliverable pending signal after the child scan and return
##          ERESTARTSYS (Linux wait4 is restartable), and (2) register as a
##          signal-waiter around block_current (register-then-recheck) so a
##          delivered signal wakes the park.  No-livelock argument: only
##          handler-backed signals can be pending-and-deliverable at a park
##          point (classify_post drops no-handler default-ignore signals and
##          terminates on fatal ones), so the sentinel always resolves to a
##          handler run + restart/EINTR.  Ring-3 end-to-end test (step c) now
##          DONE 2026-06-18 — see (c) below (single-process self-contained
##          pipe design, no two-process fd sharing needed).
##      (b) DONE 2026-06-18: restart_block storage + restart_syscall replay.
##        - New restart_block module (linux.rs, sibling of `restart`): a
##          per-TaskId BTreeMap<NanosleepBlock{deadline_ns, rem_ptr}> with
##          save_nanosleep/take(single-shot)/clear.
##        - interruptible_sleep_until(pid,task,deadline_ns)->bool helper:
##          arms hrtimer + registers signal-waiter (register-then-recheck),
##          cancels the timer after each park; returns true if the deadline
##          was reached, false if a deliverable signal interrupted.
##        - nanosleep_core(deadline_ns, rem_ptr): boot-self-test path (no
##          caller_pid) falls back to plain sleep_ns; otherwise interruptible
##          sleep.  On completion -> restart_block::clear + ok(0).  On
##          interruption -> writes remaining timespec to rem_ptr (if non-NULL),
##          save_nanosleep, returns ERESTART_RESTARTBLOCK.
##        - sys_nanosleep / sys_clock_nanosleep rewritten to compute an
##          absolute deadline_ns and call nanosleep_core.  ABSTIME path passes
##          rem_ptr=0 (Linux writes no remaining for absolute sleeps).
##        - sys_restart_syscall(NR=219) now take()s the saved block and resumes
##          nanosleep_core(block.deadline_ns, block.rem_ptr); no block -> EINTR.
##        - build_linux_rt_frame Eintr/RestartBlock handler arm clears any saved
##          block (a handler interrupting the sleep invalidates a later stray
##          restart_syscall so it can't replay a stale deadline).
##        - self_test_restart_action extended: restart_block save/take(single-
##          shot)/cross-task-isolation/clear-drops coverage.
##        Build clean + boot-test PASSED (222s).
##      (c) DONE 2026-06-18: ring-3 end-to-end SA_RESTART transparent-restart
##      test.  elf::build_linux_sa_restart_test_elf(sentinel) — a self-
##      contained hand-assembled Linux-ABI payload that needs no kernel<->child
##      fd sharing: _start does pipe(rsp) (read end fd 3, write end fd 4 since
##      stdio occupies 0/1/2), builds a 32-byte struct kernel_sigaction on the
##      stack (sa_handler=embedded handler addr, sa_flags=SA_RESTART|SA_RESTORER
##      =0x14000000, sa_restorer=embedded rt_sigreturn trampoline, sa_mask=0),
##      rt_sigaction(SIGUSR1=10,&act,NULL,8), then read(3,buf,1) which blocks on
##      the empty pipe.  The handler does write(4,&sentinel,1) then ret into the
##      restorer (mov eax,15; syscall = rt_sigreturn).  spawn::
##      self_test_linux_sa_restart() spawns it, yields until it parks in read,
##      asserts it is NOT already a zombie (the read actually blocked), posts
##      SIGUSR1 via signal::set_pending, yields, and asserts Zombie +
##      exit_code==sentinel(0x7E).  A correct kernel: interrupts the parked read
##      (-> ERESTARTSYS), runs the handler (writes one byte into the pipe), then
##      because SA_RESTART is set transparently restarts the read, which now
##      returns that byte -> exit(sentinel).  A park-uninterruptible regression
##      leaves the child Running (never wakes); a missing/wrong restart-sentinel
##      mapping surfaces EINTR with buf untouched -> wrong exit code.  Wired into
##      main.rs after self_test_linux_brk.  Build clean (36.19s) + boot-test
##      PASSED (229s); serial log shows "exit == 126: OK".  This is the capstone
##      validation for the whole slow-object interruptibility sweep (wait4,
##      nanosleep, futex, pipe, stream_socket) — previously only unit-tested at
##      the restart-sentinel mapping level.
##   6. MPROTECT — DONE.
##      sys_mprotect now walks the caller's PML4 and applies
##      WRITABLE / NO_EXECUTE / USER_ACCESSIBLE flag changes per 16 KiB
##      frame, with a first-pass mapping check that returns -ENOMEM if
##      the range contains a hole (matches Linux semantics).  PROT_NONE
##      is now REAL (see NEXT STEPS #3, DONE 2026-06-17): mprotect clears
##      USER_ACCESSIBLE on the PTEs and marks the VMA, so a subsequent
##      access raises an SEH access violation instead of demand-paging a
##      zero frame.  The old "approximated as read-only" limitation is
##      gone.
##      TLB invalidation is now CROSS-CPU: a single batched shootdown
##      at the end of the syscall (flush_range for <=64 4 KiB pages,
##      flush_all otherwise) sends an IPI to every other online CPU,
##      so multi-threaded Linux processes see consistent permissions
##      across cores.  CoW pages requesting PROT_WRITE are NOT
##      immediately made writable; the existing CoW fault handler
##      upgrades them on first write.
##   7. BRK — RESOLVED 2026-06-14.  Was a no-op stub (echoed the
##      input address).  Now a real per-process demand-paged
##      VmaKind::Brk heap with grow/shrink, RLIMIT_AS/RLIMIT_DATA
##      enforcement, image-dependent ceiling, and Linux's
##      return-unchanged-break-on-failure semantics.  See
##      known-issues.md TD25.  Remaining minor gap: no
##      arch_randomize_brk gap between data segment and heap floor
##      (see "Linux arch_randomize_brk" item below).
##   8. GETEUID == GETUID, GETEGID == GETGID.  Our credential model
##      doesn't track effective IDs separately.  Documented; will need
##      a follow-up if/when we add setuid binary support.
##   9. KILL accepts every signal in 1..=NSIG (=64).  sys_kill
##      delegates to handlers::sys_signal_send, which calls
##      signal::classify_post(target, sig) — that function knows the
##      POSIX default-action table (Terminate / Drop / Stop / Continue /
##      Deliver-to-handler) and routes accordingly.  sig=0 and
##      sig>NSIG return -EINVAL.  Limitation: job-control signals
##      (SIGSTOP / SIGCONT / SIGTSTP / SIGTTIN / SIGTTOU) are silently
##      dropped — we don't have job control / process stopping, so
##      classify_post resolves them as PostDecision::Drop.  Real Linux
##      programs that block on SIGSTOP/SIGCONT for foreground/
##      background terminal control won't behave correctly, but most
##      non-shell programs are unaffected.
##      *** IN PROGRESS 2026-06-12: real SIGSTOP/SIGCONT job control. ***
##      The scheduler already has suspend()/resume() with a Suspended
##      TaskState, so stopping a process's threads without killing them
##      is feasible.  PLAN (single-process / pid>0 delivery only;
##      process-GROUP routing stays deferred, consistent with the
##      waitpid pid<=0 decision in design-decisions.md):
##        [DONE] (1) PCB state model: JobControlEvent{Stopped(sig),
##          Continued} + Process.stopped + Process.jc_report, with
##          record_jc_stopped/record_jc_continued (return parent waiters
##          to wake) + is_stopped/peek_jc_report/take_jc_report.  Inert,
##          unit-tested (pcb::test_job_control_state).  Commit 268ded94.
##        [DONE] (2) Signal-delivery wiring (commit pending): classify_post
##          now returns PostDecision::Stop(sig)/Continue.  SIGSTOP always
##          Stop (uncatchable, even with trampoline); SIGCONT always
##          Continue (+ set_pending if a handler is registered so it also
##          runs); catchable stops (SIGTSTP/TTIN/TTOU) -> Deliver if a
##          handler exists, else Stop now (or kept pending->Drop if blocked,
##          stopping later at the checkpoint).  Mutual cancellation via
##          signal::clear_pending: SIGCONT discards pending stops, a stop
##          discards pending SIGCONT.  handlers::stop_process_for_signal
##          suspends all target threads (current thread LAST on a self-stop,
##          since suspend(current) yields), record_jc_stopped + wakes parent
##          waiters; continue_process resumes + record_jc_continued + wakes.
##          Wired into sys_signal_send (Stop/Continue arms) and into
##          deliver_pending_signal's no-trampoline loop (DefaultAction::Stop
##          self-stop at the syscall-return checkpoint).  classify_post
##          self-test extended (stop/continue/mutual-cancel/blocked cases);
##          12 signal tests + pcb job-control test pass; boot OK.
##        [DONE] (3) wait4 reporting (commit pending): sys_wait4 now honours
##          WUNTRACED (report a stopped child, status (sig<<8)|0x7f, WITHOUT
##          reaping) and WCONTINUED (status 0xffff).  Both reaping loops
##          (specific-pid and any-child) check pcb::jc_report_for_child /
##          jc_report_any_child after the zombie try_reap; each loop now
##          breaks with a pre-encoded wstatus (encode_linux_wstatus for an
##          exit, encode_jc_wstatus for a stop/continue).  A stop/continue
##          wakes a blocked wait4 via the parent waiters that
##          stop_process_for_signal/continue_process already signal.  The
##          jc_report is consumed on report (wait4 has no WNOWAIT, so the
##          helpers are called with consume=true).  pcb job-control test
##          extended (jc_report_for_child/any_child: match by option class,
##          non-consuming peek, parent-mismatch PermissionDenied, ECHILD on
##          no children); encode_jc_wstatus unit-tested in linux::self_test.
##          Build + clippy clean; boots OK.
##      DONE — real sys_waitid (no longer a reaping stub).  Factored a
##          waitid_scan(parent, target, want_exited/stopped/continued, nowait)
##          core in syscall/linux.rs that does the full child selection:
##          target=Some(pid) for P_PID/P_PIDFD (P_PIDFD resolves the target
##          via entry.raw_handle), target=None for P_ALL/P_PGID (process
##          groups unmodelled → any-child, same simplification wait4(pid<=0)
##          makes).  WEXITED reaps zombies via new pcb::peek_exit /
##          peek_exit_any (non-destructive, also return the child uid) + the
##          existing try_reap; WNOWAIT peeks without reaping; WSTOPPED/
##          WCONTINUED reuse pcb::jc_report_for_child / jc_report_any_child
##          (consume = !WNOWAIT), exactly the helpers wait4 uses.  siginfo_t
##          is a #[repr(C)] WaitidSiginfo (128 bytes, si_pid@16/si_uid@20/
##          si_status@24) written via write_unaligned: si_signo=SIGCHLD(17),
##          si_code = CLD_EXITED/KILLED/DUMPED/STOPPED/CONTINUED, si_status =
##          exit code / signal.  WNOHANG with eligible-but-unchanged children
##          returns 0 with a zeroed siginfo (si_pid==0); no eligible child →
##          ECHILD.  The blocking loop is lost-wakeup-safe (set_wait_task /
##          set_wait_any_task, re-scan, block_current) and wakes on the same
##          parent waiters stop/continue/exit already signal.  Tested:
##          test_waitid_scan() drives the core against synthetic PCBs
##          (zombie reap + CLD_EXITED/status, WNOWAIT peek-no-reap,
##          NoSuchProcess/NoChildProcess→ECHILD, CLD_STOPPED/CONTINUED
##          consume-once, killed-by-signal encoder); the dispatch gate tests
##          were made hang-proof (specific absent PID → deterministic ECHILD,
##          never block_current).  Build + clippy clean; "waitid scan/siginfo:
##          OK" prints at boot.  Also fixed 3 pre-existing partialeq_to_none
##          clippy errors in pcb::test_job_control_state (!= None → is_some()).
##      DEFERRED (needs process groups): waitid(P_PGID, pgid) currently waits
##          on ANY child rather than the named group (no process-group model
##          yet) — same gap as kill(-pgid).  Revisit with process groups.
##          UPDATE 2026-06-20: process-group model now EXISTS (pcb pgid/sid,
##          setpgid/getpgid/getpgrp/getsid/setsid, fork inheritance,
##          kill(-pgid) via pcb::pids_in_group).  kill(-pgid, SIG) RESOLVED.
##          UPDATE 2026-06-20 (Increment B): RESOLVED.  wait4(pid<=0) and
##          waitid(P_PGID) now match by process group instead of waiting on
##          any child.  pcb gained filter-aware cores
##          (reap_any_matching/jc_report_matching/peek_exit_matching, each
##          taking Option<pgid>) exposed as try_reap_group / jc_report_group /
##          peek_exit_group wrappers; sys_wait4 computes a pgid_filter
##          (None for -1, caller's pgid for 0, |pid| for <-1) and routes the
##          wait-any loop through the group variants; sys_waitid resolves a
##          pgid_filter for P_PGID (caller's group when upid==0, else upid)
##          and threads it through waitid_scan.  ECHILD fires when the caller
##          has no child in the named group at all.  Tested: test_waitid_scan
##          P_PGID case (two forked children moved into a shared group;
##          empty-group scan → NoChildProcess, group scan finds the member).
##          Build + clippy clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK).
##      Increment C (console job control, see known-issues TD30):
##          UPDATE 2026-06-20 (Increment C): PARTIALLY RESOLVED — Ctrl-C/Ctrl-\
##          (SIGINT/SIGQUIT) now delivered to the console foreground process
##          group.  The tty gained a FOREGROUND_PGID atomic with
##          foreground_pgid()/set_foreground_pgid() accessors and the
##          TIOCGPGRP(0x540F)/TIOCSPGRP(0x5410) ioctls (tcgetpgrp/tcsetpgrp);
##          console_read() now returns a ConsoleRead{Data(n)|Signal(sig)} enum,
##          and on a ^C/^\ in canonical mode (feed → LineStep::Signal) the
##          syscall layer's deliver_console_signal() resolves the foreground
##          pgrp via pcb::pids_in_group and posts SIGINT/SIGQUIT (SI_KERNEL
##          siginfo) to every member, then returns ERESTARTSYS so the blocked
##          reader's signal checkpoint runs (transparent restart when not in
##          the fg group / SA_RESTART, else EINTR / default action).  No
##          foreground group installed (pgid==0) ⇒ no signal, read restarts.
##          Tested: tty self-test foreground pgrp set/get round-trip; build +
##          clippy clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK).
##          UPDATE 2026-06-20 (Increment D): Ctrl-Z (VSUSP) → SIGTSTP now
##          RESOLVED too.  feed() recognises VSUSP under ISIG (default ^Z=26)
##          and returns LineStep::Signal(20); deliver_console_signal routes
##          SIGTSTP to the fg pgrp, whose DefaultAction::Stop (already in
##          proc::signal) suspends the job (SIGCONT resumes).  Tested:
##          tty self-test feed VQUIT/VSUSP cases + ISIG-off literal ^C; boot
##          test PASSED.  NOFLSH not yet honoured.
##          UPDATE 2026-06-20 (Increment E): VTIME now RESOLVED.  raw_read()
##          honours all four (VMIN,VTIME) combos per POSIX via a new
##          keyboard::read_char_timeout(deadline_ns) (HLT-yield loop bounded by
##          hrtimer::now_ns()): VMIN=0/VTIME>0 = bounded read timeout,
##          VMIN>0/VTIME>0 = inter-byte timer (first byte blocking), the two
##          VTIME=0 cases unchanged.  Build + clippy clean; boot PASSED.
##          UPDATE 2026-06-20 (Increment F): raw-mode ISIG now RESOLVED.
##          raw_read() classifies each byte against VINTR/VQUIT/VSUSP when ISIG
##          is set (all four VMIN/VTIME arms) and returns ConsoleRead::Signal,
##          discarding bytes collected so far (input flush; NOFLSH TODO).  Apps
##          that clear ISIG still get the chars as data.  Boot PASSED.
##      Increment G (orphaned-process-group SIGHUP/SIGCONT — TD30 final gap):
##          DONE.  On any process exit that zombifies the process, POSIX requires
##          newly-orphaned process groups containing a STOPPED member to receive
##          SIGHUP then SIGCONT.  Implemented WITHOUT a controlling-terminal model:
##          - pcb::guarded_child_pgrps(pid): distinct pgids of pid's children that
##            are in a different group but the same session (the groups pid guards).
##          - pcb::pgrp_orphaned_with_stopped(pgid): true iff no live member has a
##            guardian (live parent in a different group, same session; zombies are
##            neither member nor guardian) AND some member is stopped.
##          - handlers::deliver_kernel_signal(pid, sig): authority-free post +
##            default action (Terminate/Stop/Continue), skips zombie/unknown.
##          - handlers::kill_orphaned_pgrp(pgid): if orphaned-with-stopped, SIGHUP
##            then SIGCONT to every pids_in_group member.
##          - thread::on_thread_exit captures guarded_child_pgrps(pid) BEFORE
##            remove_thread reparents the children, then after the zombie branch
##            calls kill_orphaned_pgrp for each captured group.
##          - signal::SIGHUP (=1) const added.
##          Boot self-test pcb::test_orphaned_pgrp (guarded-not-orphaned,
##          orphaned-with-stopped, and orphaned-without-stopped negative case)
##          PASSED; full boot PASSED (321s).  TD30 now fully RESOLVED.
##      Increment H (NOFLSH — TD30 closeout):
##          DONE.  feed() now honours the NOFLSH (0x80) lflag in canonical mode:
##          a signal char (^C/^\/^Z) flushes the in-progress line by default, but
##          with NOFLSH set the buffered input is preserved and only the signal is
##          generated (line completes normally on the next newline).  Raw mode
##          keeps no input queue across read() calls, so NOFLSH has nothing to
##          preserve there (documented on raw_read).  tty boot self-test +
##          #[cfg(test)] unit test added; boot PASSED.  TD30 now FULLY resolved
##          with no remaining gaps.
##
##      Increment I (mount/umount native ABI — TD18 mount/umount sub-item):
##          DONE.  Added two native syscalls: SYS_FS_MOUNT=652 and
##          SYS_FS_UMOUNT=653 (kernel/src/syscall/number.rs, doc-commented;
##          registered in dispatch.rs; handlers in handlers.rs).  SYS_FS_MOUNT
##          takes three ptr+len string pairs (source/target/fstype, consuming
##          all six arg slots -> mount flags deferred to a future versioned
##          extension) and dispatches on the fstype string to the existing
##          kernel fs backends: ext4 / tmpfs|memfs|ramfs / iso9660 / devfs /
##          proc|procfs / sysfs / vfat|fat|fat32|fat16|msdos.  SYS_FS_UMOUNT
##          takes target ptr+len and calls Vfs::unmount (which refuses "/" and
##          refuses busy mounts with sub-mounts).  Both root-gated via a new
##          require_mount_authority() helper (mirrors require_clock_authority,
##          keyed on creds.is_root(); kernel/PID-0 tasks bypass).  A shared
##          read_user_str() helper validates+copies+UTF-8-decodes each user
##          string (clamped to 256/64 bytes).  Boot self-test:
##          fs::vfs::mount_self_test() runs unconditionally (called from
##          main.rs in the virtual-fs self-test section, NOT inside the
##          fat_ok-gated block) — mounts a scratch tmpfs at /_mount_selftest,
##          does a write/read roundtrip, confirms unmount("/") is refused,
##          unmounts, verifies it's gone; boot PASSED (serial shows all OK).
##          Userspace: userspace/mount now issues the real syscalls via a
##          syscall6 inline-asm helper instead of returning ENOSYS;
##          canonical_fstype() maps user fstype names -> kernel fstypes (auto
##          -> None, since the kernel has no auto-detect), bind/remount are
##          rejected (ABI carries no flags), other options warn "not yet
##          honoured".  kernel_errstr() maps negative kernel codes to messages.
##          Old ENOSYS tests replaced with helper unit tests; cargo test -p
##          mount --target x86_64-pc-windows-gnu -> 6 pass.  RESIDUAL: the
##          separate userspace/mount-cli demo tool STILL prints fabricated
##          mount listings and fake-succeeds without any syscall — should be
##          rewired to the real ABI or deleted (it duplicates mount); logged in
##          known-issues TD18 mount/umount sub-item.
##
##      Increment J (mkfs/format native ABI — TD18 mkfs/diskutil sub-item):
##          DONE.  Added SYS_FS_FORMAT=654 (kernel/src/syscall/number.rs,
##          doc-commented; registered in dispatch.rs import+table; handler in
##          handlers.rs).  ABI: arg0/arg1 = device-name ptr+len (the BLOCK-DEVICE
##          REGISTRY name e.g. "vda"/"sda"/"nvme0n1", NOT a /dev/ path),
##          arg2/arg3 = fstype ptr+len, arg4/arg5 = optional label ptr+len
##          (0/0 = none).  sys_fs_format dispatches on the fstype string to the
##          existing kernel fs::fat::mkfs_fat(device, label) for the FAT family
##          (vfat|fat|fat32|fat16|msdos); all other fstypes -> NotSupported
##          (ext4 mkfs not yet ported; tmpfs has no device to format).  Root-gated
##          via a new require_format_authority() helper (mirrors
##          require_mount_authority/require_clock_authority; creds.is_root();
##          kernel/PID-0 bypass).  Reuses read_user_str() (its needless explicit
##          lifetime was also elided per clippy).  New test infra in blkdev.rs:
##          blkdev::unregister(name) + a RamBlockDevice (Vec-backed, impls
##          BlockDevice; bounds via try_from/checked_mul/checked_add).  Boot
##          self-test: fs::fat::format_self_test() runs unconditionally (called
##          from main.rs right after mount_self_test()) — registers a 4 MiB
##          RamBlockDevice "fmttest0" (-> mkfs auto-selects FAT16), runs
##          mkfs_fat, mounts the formatted volume via FatFs::mount + Vfs::mount at
##          /_fmt_selftest, write/read roundtrips HELLO.TXT, tears everything down
##          (remove/unmount/invalidate/unregister) regardless of outcome; boot
##          PASSED (serial: "[fat] mkfs/format self-test PASSED").  Userspace:
##          both userspace/mkfs and userspace/diskutil `format` now issue the real
##          syscall via a syscall6 inline-asm helper (FAT family only; unsupported
##          fstypes -> honest "kernel cannot format X yet" error, not ENOSYS).
##          mkfs warns that -F/-s/-S are advisory (kernel auto-selects FAT type +
##          cluster geometry from device size).  syscall_error_msg in both tools
##          updated to map native KernelError codes (-2/-3/-400/-500/-509/-601/
##          -602) plus legacy errno fallbacks.  cargo test -p mkfs --target
##          x86_64-pc-windows-gnu -> 35 pass; both build for x86_64-slateos and
##          pass clippy.  RESIDUAL (still TD18): fsck/diskutil verify+repair+trim
##          and statfs have no syscall yet; fs::fat::fsck_fat exists in-kernel but
##          unexposed — a future SYS_FS_CHECK would wire them.
##
##      Increment K (fsck/verify+repair native ABI — TD18 fsck/diskutil sub-item):
##          DONE.  Added SYS_FS_CHECK=655 (kernel/src/syscall/number.rs,
##          doc-commented; registered in dispatch.rs import+table; handler
##          sys_fs_check in handlers.rs).  ABI: arg0/arg1 = device-name ptr+len
##          (the BLOCK-DEVICE REGISTRY name e.g. "vda"/"sda", NOT a /dev/ path),
##          arg2 = flags (bit0 = repair).  Returns the count of OUTSTANDING errors
##          (report.errors after repair, i.e. errors.saturating_sub(repaired) when
##          repair requested, else report.errors) or a negative KernelError.  FAT
##          only — delegates to the existing fs::fat::fsck_fat(device, repair).
##          Root-gated via require_fsck_authority() — which, along with
##          require_format/mount/clock_authority, now delegates to a single new
##          private require_root_authority() helper (collapsed 4 duplicated bodies
##          per the "no band-aid accumulation" rule; named wrappers kept for
##          doc/future per-capability split).  Boot self-test:
##          fs::fat::fsck_self_test() runs unconditionally (called from main.rs
##          after format_self_test()) — registers a 4 MiB RamBlockDevice
##          "fscktest0", mkfs_fat(dev, Some("FSCKTEST")), fsck_fat(dev, false)
##          asserts errors==0, fsck_fat(dev, true) asserts
##          errors.saturating_sub(repaired)==0, teardown via invalidate+unregister;
##          boot PASSED (serial: "[fat] fsck self-test PASSED").  Userspace:
##          - userspace/fsck REWIRED from the COLLIDING SYS_FS_VERIFY=652 /
##            SYS_FS_REPAIR=653 (those numbers had just been reassigned to
##            SYS_FS_MOUNT/SYS_FS_UMOUNT in Increment-J-adjacent mount work, so fsck
##            was invoking mount/umount with garbage args — a real bug I introduced).
##            Now uses fs_check(dev_name, repair) inline-asm helper +
##            FS_CHECK_REPAIR=1<<0; removed syscall3/build_flags/FSCK_FLAG_*; added
##            wants_repair(opts) and device_registry_name() (strips /dev/) helpers;
##            errno_msg -> native codes.  cargo test -p fsck -> 39 pass.
##          - userspace/diskutil `verify` = fs_check(false), `repair` =
##            fs_check(true) (FS_CHECK_REPAIR); trim/statfs still honest ENOSYS.
##          Both build for x86_64-slateos and pass clippy.  RESIDUAL (still TD18):
##          diskutil trim (needs discard ABI) + statfs (needs free/used-block
##          query) have no syscall yet.
##
##      Increment L (diskutil usage -> real statvfs; remove the two fabricating
##          mkfs-cli/fsck-cli shims — TD18 cleanup):
##          DONE.  Two parts.
##          (1) Removed userspace/mkfs-cli and userspace/fsck-cli: pure-fabrication
##          multi-personality demo shims (mkfs.ext4/xfs/btrfs/fat/mkswap;
##          fsck/e2fsck/xfs_repair) that printed fake UUIDs, "done", "clean,
##          NNN/NNN files" WITHOUT issuing any syscall — telling the user a
##          format/check succeeded when nothing happened (same no-fabrication
##          violation as the already-removed mount-cli).  Every personality is
##          already covered by the real syscall-backed userspace/mkfs (argv0
##          mkfs.<type> -> SYS_FS_FORMAT=654) and userspace/fsck (argv0
##          fsck.<type> -> SYS_FS_CHECK=655).  Logged under Judgment Calls.
##          (2) diskutil `usage`: was an ENOSYS stub falling back to a sysfs
##          SIZE estimate, but a real native SYS_FS_STATVFS=608 already existed
##          (sys_fs_statvfs, backed by the fully-implemented Vfs::statvfs(path) ->
##          FsInfo across FAT/ext4/memfs/devfs/iso9660/procfs/sysfs).  cmd_usage
##          now calls it via fs_statvfs(path): arg0/arg1 = path ptr+len, arg2 =
##          64-byte out buffer (off 0 block_size, 8 total_blocks, 16 free_blocks,
##          24 total_inodes, 32 free_inodes, 40 max_name_len, 48 read_only u8),
##          returns 0 / negative KernelError.  Prints exact Total/Used/Free/
##          Available/inodes; falls back to the sysfs estimate ONLY on real
##          syscall failure.  Kernel exposes one free count (no separate
##          available-to-unprivileged), so diskutil reports available == free.
##          Added diskutil host tests (first ones): read_u64_le LE-parse +
##          out-of-range-is-zero, syscall_error_msg kernel-code mapping,
##          format_size sanity — cargo test -p diskutil -> 5 pass.  Build +
##          clippy clean for x86_64-slateos.  RESIDUAL (still TD18): diskutil
##          trim (needs a discard syscall) is the only remaining fs-admin stub.
##
##      Increment M (Linux-ABI statfs/fstatfs returned fake data — kernel bug):
##          DONE.  sys_statfs/sys_fstatfs (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs) NEVER
##          resolved the path/fd — they always fill_statfs_default()'d a
##          hardcoded block (TMPFS_MAGIC, 16 GiB total / 8 GiB free, 64K inodes)
##          regardless of the real filesystem, so Linux df/statfs callers got
##          bogus capacity.  Fixed: sys_statfs canonicalises the path vs the
##          caller cwd (mirroring stat/access) and routes through Vfs::statvfs;
##          sys_fstatfs resolves the fd's VFS handle to a path via
##          fs::handle::handle_path then queries Vfs::statvfs.  New
##          fill_statfs_from_info maps FsInfo -> the 15-u64 struct statfs layout
##          with a real f_type via statfs_magic_for (ext4 0xEF53, FAT 0x4d44,
##          iso9660 0x9660, procfs 0x9fa0, sysfs 0x62656572, else TMPFS_MAGIC);
##          NotFound -> ENOENT; non-VFS fds (pipes/eventfd/...) and virtual FS
##          keep neutral defaults (honest — no on-disk capacity).  Field packing
##          refactored from manual i*8 indexing to chunks_exact_mut (kills the
##          indexing/arithmetic lints) via a shared write_statfs_fields helper.
##          The pre-mount linux::self_test() keeps only the NULL->EFAULT checks
##          (it runs before any FS is mounted, so a positive statfs("/") would
##          correctly ENOENT there); a NEW post-mount pub fn
##          self_test_statfs_root() — called from main.rs after the root mount,
##          alongside self_test_rename_noreplace — asserts statfs("/") returns 0
##          with non-zero f_type + f_namelen (proves it routes through statvfs,
##          not zero-fill).  Kernel build + clippy clean; boot PASSED.
##
##      Increment N (diskutil trim -> real fstrim — last fs-admin stub): DONE.
##          Built the full fstrim stack bottom-up:
##          (1) blkdev discard primitive: BlockDevice::supports_discard()/
##              discard(start_lba,count) (default NotSupported); RamBlockDevice
##              zeroes the range (bounds/overflow-checked); registry helpers
##              blkdev::supports_discard()/discard(); boot self_test_discard().
##          (2) FileSystem::trim() (default Ok(0)) + Vfs::trim(path); FatFs::trim
##              walks the FAT, coalesces free-cluster runs, issues blkdev::discard
##              per run after cache::invalidate_range (so stale free-space data
##              can't resurface).  NON-DESTRUCTIVE: only free clusters touched.
##          (3) FileSystem::device_name() (FatFs -> registry name) +
##              Vfs::trim_device(dev) for device->mount resolution.
##          (4) SYS_FS_TRIM (656, root-only via require_trim_authority) wired to
##              Vfs::trim_device; returns bytes discarded (i64, clamped).
##          (5) diskutil cmd_trim issues SYS_FS_TRIM and prints bytes discarded;
##              dropped the now-unused ENOSYS const.
##          Tests: 3 boot self-tests (blkdev discard; FAT fstrim end-to-end via
##          Vfs::trim_device incl. poison-a-free-sector + verify-zeroed; unknown-
##          device rejection) + 5 diskutil host tests.  Kernel + diskutil build/
##          clippy clean; boot PASSED (fstrim discarded 4,160,512 bytes).
##          RESIDUAL (TD18 follow-ups, NOT blocking):
##            - virtio-blk does not negotiate VIRTIO_BLK_F_DISCARD, so on real/
##              virtio devices supports_discard()=false and fstrim is a successful
##              no-op (0 bytes); discard only actually fires on RamBlockDevice.
##              Proper fix: negotiate the discard feature, parse the discard
##              config (max_discard_sectors/alignment), implement the
##              VIRTIO_BLK_T_DISCARD (type 11) command in kernel/src/virtio/blk.rs,
##              and override supports_discard()/discard() in the blkdev impl.
##              Deferred because it can't be boot-tested without a QEMU virtio-blk
##              discard=on config and a wrong discard range could corrupt a real
##              disk — wanted the testable RAM path landed first.
##              NOTE (scoping, found 2026-06-20): our virtio-blk driver is
##              LEGACY (PCI device id 0x1001, I/O-port transport,
##              set_guest_features(0)).  VIRTIO_BLK_F_DISCARD (feature bit 13)
##              is only offered by MODERN virtio-blk (device id 0x1042, MMIO/
##              capability transport).  So virtio discard is GATED on modern
##              virtio-blk support — a separate, larger driver initiative, not a
##              quick add.  Until then fstrim on virtio devices is a correct
##              no-op (0 bytes).
##            - ext4 still uses the default trim() no-op (no free-block-bitmap
##              enumeration).  Proper fix: walk the ext4 block bitmaps (see
##              fs/ext4/balloc.rs) for free runs and issue blkdev::discard,
##              mirroring FatFs::trim_free_clusters.
##
## TESTING:
##   - linux::self_test() at boot exercises: errno mapping (16 KernelError
##     variants); linux_from_native round-trip; unknown nr -> -ENOSYS;
##     sched_yield; write to bad fd -> -EBADF; writev bad iovcnt ->
##     -EINVAL; clock_gettime bad clk -> -EINVAL; arch_prctl bad code
##     -> -EINVAL; LinuxTimespec to_nanos / from_nanos roundtrip;
##     LinuxTimespec rejects negative tv_sec and tv_nsec >= 1e9;
##     kernel_error_from_code inverse of code() for all mapped variants;
##     direct dispatch_linux invocation for getpid, gettid, getuid,
##     getgid.  Fd-table-backed syscalls (read/close/dup/fcntl/lseek)
##     correctly return -EBADF when the caller has no fd table; dup3
##     with oldfd==newfd returns -EINVAL; openat with a non-AT_FDCWD
##     dirfd returns -ENOSYS; translate_open_flags maps O_RDONLY,
##     O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC, and O_RDWR|O_APPEND correctly.
##   - linux_fd::self_test() at boot exercises: empty / with_stdio
##     construction; install_lowest after stdio gives 3,4,5;
##     install_lowest_from(10, ...) honours the min_fd; lookup OOB
##     returns None; is_handle_referenced semantics; dup_lowest clones
##     entry and clears FD_CLOEXEC; dup2 closes prior occupant and
##     returns it; dup2(fd,fd) is a no-op; take removes entry;
##     set_fd_flags / set_status_flags preserve O_ACCMODE;
##     take_cloexec_entries returns exactly the FD_CLOEXEC'd entries
##     and leaves non-cloexec fds intact; ensure_stdio refills missing
##     stdio slots with Console entries but does NOT overwrite a
##     non-Console fd sitting at 0/1/2.
##   - dispatch_linux_with_frame routing: non-frame syscalls return
##     None; EXECVE from a contextless self-test task returns
##     Some(-ESRCH) (no owning process to exec); FORK / VFORK from a
##     contextless self-test task return a negative errno (proves
##     routing reaches fork_process); CLONE with thread-creation
##     bits but child_stack=0 returns Some(-ENOSYS); CLONE with bad
##     stack and no CLONE_VM/CLONE_THREAD returns Some(-ENOSYS);
##     full pthread-style CLONE (CLONE_VM | CLONE_THREAD | ... with
##     non-zero child_stack) reaches thread_clone::clone_thread which
##     returns Some(-ESRCH) when there's no owning process — proves
##     the new thread-creation route is wired (NOT -ENOSYS).
##   - read_user_cstr(NULL, _) returns Err(EFAULT) without touching
##     userspace; read_user_ptr_array(NULL, _) returns Ok(empty) (how
##     glibc passes an argv-less execve).
##   - thread_clone::self_test() at boot exercises: build_register_image
##     slot layout (RIP/RFLAGS/RSP/RDI/R9/R15/FS_BASE positions);
##     RFLAGS substitution (zero -> 0x202); CLEAR_CHILD_TID register
##     and clear (register-to-0 removes the entry per Linux
##     set_tid_address semantics); on_thread_exit_hook is a no-op for
##     unregistered tasks; clone_thread rejects child_stack==0 with
##     KernelError::InvalidArgument BEFORE looking up the pid.
##   - rt_sigreturn via dispatch_linux_with_frame with a misaligned
##     user_rsp returns -EFAULT (both candidate ucontext addresses
##     fail the alignment gate before any unsafe deref).  user_rip is
##     left untouched on the failure path so userspace can recover.
##   - Linux-sigaction table round-trip: a fresh pid reads back
##     SIG_DFL/0/0/0; storing a non-default entry round-trips; per-
##     signal independence (writing signal 11 leaves signal 10
##     unaffected); on_exec preserves SIG_IGN handlers but zeros their
##     flags/restorer/mask while clearing caught (non-DFL/IGN) entries
##     back to defaults; on_fork copies the parent table to a fresh
##     child pid; on_exit removes every entry so a re-read returns
##     defaults again.
##   - dispatch_linux rt_sigaction validation: sig=0, sig=65, and
##     sigsetsize=7 each return -EINVAL without mutating any state.
##   - mprotect argument validation via dispatch_linux: zero length
##     succeeds as no-op; unknown prot bits, misaligned addr, and
##     kernel-space addr each return their respective errnos (EINVAL
##     / EINVAL / EFAULT).
##   - mprotect_flush_range routing: degenerate (end<=start) is a
##     no-op; ranges <= 64 4 KiB pages bump tlb::stats().range_flushes;
##     ranges > 64 pages bump tlb::stats().full_flushes; tested with
##     kernel-VA dummy addresses since the function only does TLB
##     work, never page-table walks.
##   - clone(SIGCHLD | CLONE_VFORK) via dispatch_linux_with_frame
##     reaches fork::fork_process (returns ESRCH in the contextless
##     test task) — proves CLONE_VFORK is no longer rejected at the
##     flag check.  clone(SIGCHLD | CLONE_PARENT/CLONE_NEWNS/
##     CLONE_PTRACE) each still return -ENOSYS.
##   - kill signal-number gate via dispatch_linux: sig=0 (existence
##     probe) MUST NOT return -EINVAL (expects ESRCH for a probe pid
##     that doesn't exist); sig=65 and sig=u64::MAX each return
##     -EINVAL; sig in {9, 15, 17, 64} each progress past the gate
##     (i.e. don't return -EINVAL, proving the linux ABI accepts the
##     full 1..=NSIG range and not just SIGKILL/SIGTERM).
##   - madvise: len=0 succeeds even with bogus addr+advice; known
##     hints 0/1/2/3/MADV_DONTNEED(4)/MADV_FREE(8)/MADV_COLLAPSE(25)
##     return 0; MADV_HWPOISON(100) and MADV_SOFT_OFFLINE(101) return
##     -EPERM (privileged on real Linux, never exposed here);
##     advice=26 (between documented max and HWPOISON) returns
##     -EINVAL; misaligned addr with nonzero len returns -EINVAL;
##     kernel-space addr with nonzero len returns -ENOMEM.
##   - encode_linux_wstatus pure-function: normal exit code 42 ->
##     WIFEXITED true (low 7 bits == 0) and WEXITSTATUS == 42; kernel
##     exit code 128+15 -> WTERMSIG == 15 and WIFSIGNALED true; any
##     ExitInfo.crash.is_some() -> wstatus low 7 bits == 11 (SIGSEGV).
##   - rlimit_default pure-function: RLIMIT_STACK (3) is (8 MiB,
##     u64::MAX), RLIMIT_NOFILE (7) is (1024, 4096), RLIMIT_CORE
##     (4) is (0, 0), RLIMIT_CPU (0) is (u64::MAX, u64::MAX).
##   - prlimit64 dispatch: resource == 16 and u64::MAX both return
##     -EINVAL; cross-pid query (pid=1 with caller_pid=0/None)
##     returns -EPERM; pid=0 with both NULL pointers is a 0-return
##     no-op success.
##   - wait4 dispatch via dispatch_linux: unknown option bits return
##     -EINVAL before any reap attempt; wait-any WNOHANG from
##     contextless test task does NOT return -EINVAL (proves the
##     options validator didn't reject the legal WNOHANG=1 bit);
##     wait-specific WNOHANG for fake pid 0xDEAD_BEEF returns a
##     negative errno other than -EINVAL (proves we reached
##     try_reap's "not a child" path -> ECHILD).  Bad wstatus / rusage
##     pointer EFAULT is NOT tested at boot because
##     validate_user_write has a documented kernel-context bypass
##     that makes it impossible to observe — the shared validation
##     infrastructure is exercised by every other syscall and will
##     EFAULT on bad pointers from real userspace.
##   - Cross-ABI dispatch routing in entry.rs is *structurally* tested
##     by the AbiMode default-Native path that every existing native
##     test hits.  The Linux path will get end-to-end coverage when an
##     actual Linux binary can be loaded.
##
## NEXT STEPS (in priority order):
##   1. GETRANDOM CSPRNG — DONE.  sys_getrandom now calls
##      crate::rng::fill (ChaCha20-based CSPRNG seeded at boot from
##      RDSEED/RDRAND/HPET/TSC).  The previous xorshift64-from-TSC
##      stream is gone.  Flags (GRND_NONBLOCK / GRND_RANDOM /
##      GRND_INSECURE) are accepted but ignored — we never block and
##      we don't distinguish urandom/random sources.
##   2. Cross-CPU TLB shootdown for mprotect — DONE.  sys_mprotect
##      now batches all PTE updates and ends with a single
##      mprotect_flush_range() call that delegates to
##      crate::tlb::flush_range / flush_all.  Both are SMP-aware (send
##      an IPI to every other online CPU and spin until they ack),
##      so a sibling pthread running on another core cannot observe
##      stale permissions past the mprotect return.  Strategy:
##        - len <= 64 4 KiB pages -> flush_range (one IPI, N invlpgs)
##        - len  > 64 4 KiB pages -> flush_all (one IPI, CR3 reload)
##      Threshold 64 ≈ Linux's tlb_single_page_flush_ceiling (33),
##      bumped up to 64 because our hardware page is the same 4 KiB
##      but our frames are 16 KiB so the average mprotect covers a
##      smaller page-count for a given byte size.  Boot self-test
##      observes crate::tlb::stats() delta to prove small ranges take
##      the range path and large ranges promote to full-flush.
##   3. Real PROT_NONE — DONE 2026-06-17.  With full VMA tracking in
##      place, PROT_NONE is now represented as the ABSENCE of the
##      USER_ACCESSIBLE page-table flag (the literal x86-64 "no ring-3
##      access" semantic), NOT a new VMA field — see design-decisions.md
##      §32 for why we overload the flag rather than add a field.
##      Enforced at two layers: (a) present pages get U/S cleared on the
##      PTE (mmap/mprotect build flags via linux_prot_to_page_flags /
##      handlers::sys_mmap honouring MAP_READ); (b) lazy/demand pages are
##      gated in pcb::try_resolve_fault, which now returns false (-> SEH
##      access violation) when the resolving VMA lacks USER_ACCESSIBLE,
##      instead of demand-paging a zero frame.  CoW is safe: cow.rs ORs
##      WRITABLE but never adds USER_ACCESSIBLE, so a CoW copy of a
##      PROT_NONE page stays inaccessible.  mprotect round-trips
##      losslessly (want_access = prot != 0 recomputes the bit).
##      procfs render_maps now prints '-' for the read column on
##      PROT_NONE VMAs ("---p").  Self-tests: proc test_prot_none (read
##      faults, mprotect RW then back to PROT_NONE) + procfs maps render
##      "---p" case; both pass under boot-test.
##   4. Honour brk properly — RESOLVED 2026-06-14.  brk is now a real
##      demand-paged heap (VmaKind::Brk), not a no-op.  See
##      known-issues.md TD25.
##      arch_randomize_brk — DONE 2026-06-14.  spawn::choose_brk_start
##      shifts the heap floor up by a random gap (13 bits of entropy,
##      128 MiB max at 16 KiB pages, mirroring Linux x86_64 per
##      design-decisions #20/#21), wired into both set_brk_region call
##      sites; always-on when seeded, no-gap fallback before seeding.
##      Covered by spawn::test_brk_aslr_gap + the ring-3
##      self_test_linux_brk.
##   4b. sigaction sa_mask / sa_flags / SA_RESTART — STORAGE DONE.
##      kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs now keeps a per-process per-signal
##      table (linux_sigaction_table) of the full Linux struct sigaction
##      { sa_handler, sa_flags, sa_restorer, sa_mask }.  rt_sigaction
##      writes the requested entry (rejecting unknown sa_flags bits via
##      sa_flags::MASK, sig=0 / sig>NSIG / sigsetsize != 0 && != 8 with
##      EINVAL, and SIGKILL/SIGSTOP as SET targets with EINVAL) and
##      copies the previous entry out to oldact before overwriting.
##      Fork copies the parent table to the child; exec preserves
##      SIG_IGN entries (but clears flags/restorer/mask) and resets
##      every caught entry to SIG_DFL per POSIX; process exit clears
##      the table.  Boot self-tests cover round-trip, per-signal
##      independence, on_fork / on_exec / on_exit, plus the EINVAL
##      validation paths.
##      Limitation: stored sa_flags / sa_mask are NOT yet consumed at
##      delivery time.  deliver_pending_signal still uses the legacy
##      proc::signal trampoline (which rt_sigaction continues to sync
##      for catchable handlers, so basic SIGINT/SIGTERM keep working),
##      so SA_NODEFER / SA_RESETHAND / SA_RESTART / sa_mask blocking
##      and SA_SIGINFO siginfo_t* delivery are all no-ops until the
##      Linux-shaped rt_sigframe delivery work (item 6 below) lands.
##      sys_rt_sigaction's oldact return value IS truthful now even
##      for stored-but-unused fields, so glibc that round-trips via
##      sigaction-old==sigaction-new sees consistent state.
##   5. RT_SIGRETURN — DONE (via native restore path).  Linux's
##      rt_sigreturn(2) is now routed through
##      dispatch_linux_with_frame and reuses sys_signal_return_with_
##      frame to restore the saved SignalContext.  Both calling
##      conventions are supported: direct sigreturn from the handler
##      (ctx at user_rsp + 8) and via SA_RESTORER (ctx at user_rsp).
##      Defensive alignment check on the candidate ucontext pointer
##      keeps us out of Rust's debug-mode misaligned-deref panic when
##      validate_user_read short-circuits in kernel context.
##      Limitation: signal delivery (proc::signal::deliver_pending_
##      signal) writes our *native* SignalContext on the user stack,
##      not Linux's ucontext_t.  Real Linux programs using a glibc
##      that walks the ucontext at known field offsets won't work
##      until delivery emits a Linux-shaped rt_sigframe — tracked as
##      a follow-up below.
##   6. Linux-shaped signal delivery frame.  Today
##      deliver_pending_signal writes a native SignalContext for both
##      ABIs.  For full Linux compat the delivery path needs to know
##      the calling ABI and emit either:
##        - native SignalContext (current behavior, for our ABI), or
##        - Linux rt_sigframe { ucontext_t uc; siginfo_t info; ... }
##          with mcontext_t.gregs[*] populated per AMD64 SysV.
##      rt_sigreturn would then need to detect which layout is on the
##      stack (probably via a magic / sentinel field) and restore
##      accordingly.  Acceptable to defer until real Linux binaries
##      are running.
##   7. CLONE_VFORK — DONE-with-approximation.  linux_clone now
##      accepts clone(CLONE_VFORK | SIGCHLD, ...) and degenerates it
##      to a plain fork.  Linux's vfork() guarantees (a) parent blocks
##      until child execve's or _exit's, and (b) child shares parent's
##      address space until then.  (a) is a performance hint per POSIX
##      ("vfork == fork is permitted"), not a correctness requirement
##      — every conformant caller of vfork must work against a plain
##      fork.  (b) we don't honour, but CoW gives the child a logically
##      identical address space.  Same approximation as the dedicated
##      VFORK syscall (which has worked this way since the initial
##      Linux ABI port).  Real parent-blocking vfork semantics are
##      tracked as a future enhancement but are low-value since
##      conformant callers work either way.
##      CLONE_PARENT / CLONE_NEWNS / CLONE_PTRACE continue to return
##      -ENOSYS — they need infrastructure (PID reparenting, mount
##      namespaces, ptrace lineage) we don't have yet.
##      Boot self-test exercises both: clone(SIGCHLD | CLONE_VFORK)
##      no longer returns -ENOSYS (it reaches fork::fork_process and
##      bails with ESRCH in the contextless test task); clone with
##      any of CLONE_PARENT / CLONE_NEWNS / CLONE_PTRACE still
##      returns -ENOSYS.
##   8. MADVISE — DONE-as-advisory-no-op.  sys_madvise validates
##      addr alignment, frame-rounds len, range-checks against
##      USER_SPACE_END, then accepts all documented Linux MADV_*
##      hints in 0..=25 (NORMAL/RANDOM/SEQUENTIAL/WILLNEED/DONTNEED/
##      FREE/REMOVE/DONTFORK/DOFORK/MERGEABLE/UNMERGEABLE/HUGEPAGE/
##      NOHUGEPAGE/DONTDUMP/DODUMP/WIPEONFORK/KEEPONFORK/COLD/
##      PAGEOUT/POPULATE_READ/POPULATE_WRITE/DONTNEED_LOCKED/
##      COLLAPSE) returning 0.  HWPOISON (100) and SOFT_OFFLINE (101)
##      return -EPERM (privileged memory-failure injection that we
##      don't expose).  Unknown advice returns -EINVAL.
##      Rationale: glibc/jemalloc/tcmalloc/mimalloc call
##      MADV_DONTNEED on every free() to release pages back to the
##      kernel.  Returning -ENOSYS made every free() syscall-and-fail
##      while RSS grew unbounded.  Treating advisory hints as a no-op
##      is always semantically valid (Linux is allowed to ignore any
##      MADV_* hint) and lets allocators stop spamming the syscall.
##      UPDATE 2026-06-14 — RECLAIM NOW IMPLEMENTED.  The old
##      limitation ("we don't actually free anonymous frames on
##      MADV_DONTNEED yet — needs VMA tracking") is resolved: the VMA
##      layer from the brk/mmap work is now in place, so MADV_DONTNEED /
##      MADV_FREE / MADV_DONTNEED_LOCKED free the resident frames backing
##      anonymous-class pages (Anonymous/Stack/Brk VMAs) in the range via
##      madvise_reclaim() (reuses unmap_user_range: refcount-aware free +
##      per-page TLB invalidation; VMA + RLIMIT_AS reservation kept, so the
##      mapping stays valid and a later access zero-fills via the fault
##      resolver — exactly Linux's anonymous DONTNEED contract).  Only
##      whole 16 KiB frames fully inside the (4 KiB-ABI) request are
##      dropped; partial edge frames are preserved.  File-backed/fixed
##      mappings keep the lenient no-op (acting on those needs the page
##      cache declined in design-decisions.md §22).  Tested by
##      self_test_madvise_dontneed (boot, main.rs): faults 2 anon frames,
##      stamps a sentinel, reclaims, asserts PTEs gone + VMA persists +
##      re-fault reads zero.  MADV_FREE is realised as an immediate reclaim
##      (Linux may reclaim MADV_FREE pages at any time; post-reclaim reads
##      see zero — a valid eager realisation).  Minor remaining fidelity
##      gap: MADV_FREE on a file mapping returns 0 here where Linux returns
##      EINVAL (we keep the lenient success rather than walk the VMA type
##      for an errno-only distinction) — negligible.
##   9. WAIT4 — DONE.  sys_wait4 is wired through dispatch_linux,
##      validates the options bitmap (WNOHANG=1, WUNTRACED=2,
##      WCONTINUED=8; unknown bits -> EINVAL), pre-validates wstatus
##      and rusage user pointers, then routes:
##        - pid > 0  -> pcb::try_reap(parent, target)
##        - pid <= 0 -> pcb::try_reap_any(parent)
##      and threads through the same wait-task registration / re-check
##      / block_current loop as sys_process_wait when WNOHANG is
##      clear.  WNOHANG with no ready child returns 0 (Linux
##      convention).  PermissionDenied / NoSuchProcess from try_reap
##      collapse to -ECHILD (Linux: "not your child" and "no such
##      child" are both ECHILD).
##      encode_linux_wstatus translates ExitInfo to Linux <sys/wait.h>
##      encoding: normal exit code C -> (C&0xff)<<8 (WIFEXITED+
##      WEXITSTATUS); native exit_code in 128..=255 (our kill
##      convention) -> sig&0x7f (WIFSIGNALED+WTERMSIG); crash -> 11
##      (SIGSEGV).  WUNTRACED/WCONTINUED are accepted-but-ignored
##      because we have no process-stop mechanism — there are no
##      stopped or continued children to report.
##      Rusage is zeroed when the user pointer is non-null (we don't
##      track per-process resource usage yet; programs that consume
##      ru_utime/ru_stime see zero CPU time but don't crash).
##      Limitation: crash -> SIGSEGV is a heuristic.  Most crashes
##      ARE page faults so SIGSEGV is correct most of the time, but
##      a DivideError should map to SIGFPE, InvalidOpcode to SIGILL,
##      etc.  Future enhancement: read CrashInfo.exception_code and
##      map to the right Linux signal number.  Also: process groups
##      aren't implemented (pid==0 and pid<-1 both degrade to
##      "any child"), which is conformant but loses fidelity for
##      shells that wait on a process group.
##  10. PRLIMIT64 — DONE with static defaults.  sys_prlimit64
##      validates resource in 0..=15 (otherwise EINVAL), permits
##      target pid 0 or pid == caller (otherwise EPERM — Linux
##      requires CAP_SYS_RESOURCE for cross-process limits), pre-
##      validates new_limit / old_limit user pointers (each 16
##      bytes: rlim_cur, rlim_max as u64), accepts the new limit
##      as a no-op, and writes a compiled-in default to old_limit
##      via rlimit_default(resource).
##      Defaults that matter for glibc / typical programs:
##        - RLIMIT_STACK = (8 MiB, INFINITY)   sizes glibc's main
##          thread stack correctly
##        - RLIMIT_NOFILE = (1024, 4096)       fits FD_SETSIZE for
##          select(); matches typical Linux distro defaults
##        - RLIMIT_CORE = (0, 0)               we never produce
##          core dumps
##        - RLIMIT_NICE / RLIMIT_RTPRIO = (0, 0)  no nice / RT
##        - everything else = (INFINITY, INFINITY)
##      Limitation: limits are global and read-only.  Per-process
##      storage would let setrlimit honour user changes; today they
##      silently no-op.  This is fine for read-mostly users (glibc
##      sizing its main thread stack from RLIMIT_STACK) but
##      programs that lower a limit then verify it failed (e.g. a
##      sandbox dropping privileges) won't see the lowered value
##      reflected.  Tracked as a future enhancement when a real
##      per-process rlimit store is added.
##  11. RT_SIGPENDING — DONE.  sys_rt_sigpending validates sigsetsize
##      == sizeof(sigset_t) == 8 first (EINVAL otherwise) so a sane
##      libc bug-checks its own assumptions before we touch user
##      memory, validates the user-write range, then copies the
##      caller process's pending-signal bitmap from
##      crate::proc::signal::pending(pid) into the user sigset_t.
##      For tasks with no owning process (kernel self-tests) the
##      pending mask reads back as 0, which matches "no signals
##      pending" — observationally correct.
##  12. TKILL / TGKILL — DONE-with-approximation.  sys_tkill resolves
##      owner_process(tid) and, on ESRCH for non-thread tids,
##      degenerates to sys_kill(pid, sig) with the same sig==0
##      existence-probe semantics.  sys_tgkill additionally verifies
##      that owner_process(tid) == tgid (Linux's race-free tid-reuse
##      check); a mismatch yields ESRCH.  Both wire through
##      dispatch_linux at the new nr::TKILL=200 / nr::TGKILL=234
##      slots.
##      Limitation: we don't have multi-threaded per-thread signal
##      routing yet.  Every signal — whether sent via kill(pid),
##      tkill(tid), or tgkill(tgid,tid) — gets posted to the owning
##      process's single pending mask and delivered to whichever
##      thread of that process happens to be ready.  For single-
##      threaded programs (the common case for early Linux binaries)
##      this is observationally identical to Linux.  For glibc's
##      pthread_kill targeting a specific worker thread of a multi-
##      threaded process, the signal will be delivered, just not
##      necessarily to the requested thread.  Real per-thread
##      delivery is tracked alongside the Linux-shaped rt_sigframe
##      delivery work (item 6) — they touch the same pending-set
##      machinery in proc::signal.
##  13. UMASK — DONE as static-stub.  sys_umask always returns 0o022
##      (the de-facto Linux distro default) and silently discards the
##      requested mask.  Rationale: the PCB doesn't carry per-process
##      umask storage, the VFS doesn't apply umask at create time,
##      and no other kernel code observes the value — so a no-op
##      returning the conventional default is the most useful stub
##      for programs that read-then-restore (`old = umask(0);
##      umask(old);`) or check whether umask is "sane" at startup.
##      Limitation: a program that does `umask(0o077); creat(...);`
##      and then stats the new file will see the kernel-default
##      permissions, not mask-respecting ones.  Tracked as needing
##      per-process umask storage on the PCB plus VFS plumbing to
##      consume it at create time.
##  14. SIGALTSTACK — DONE as disabled-stub.  sys_sigaltstack
##      validates ss/old_ss user pointers (24 bytes: stack_t {ss_sp,
##      ss_flags, padding, ss_size}) when non-NULL, silently
##      discards any input ss, writes ss_flags == SS_DISABLE (= 2)
##      to old_ss to communicate "no alternate stack in effect",
##      and returns 0.
##      Limitation: programs that catch SIGSEGV with SA_ONSTACK to
##      print a backtrace after blowing the main stack will deliver
##      to the already-blown stack and double-fault.  Tracked
##      alongside item 6 (Linux-shaped rt_sigframe delivery) — the
##      signal-delivery path is the same refactor.
##  15. IOCTL — DONE as ENOTTY-everywhere.  sys_ioctl returns
