##        * it_value.tv_nsec = -1 -> EINVAL  (new; negative).
##        * it_value.tv_nsec = 1_000_000_000 -> EINVAL  (new;
##          boundary half-open, exact value invalid).
##        * it_value.tv_nsec = 999_999_999 -> EBADF  (new; just
##          below boundary, clears gate to fd lookup).
##        * Pre-existing tests (bad flag -> EINVAL, NULL new ->
##          EFAULT) all still pass.
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   timerfd_settime
##          itimerspec64_valid: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * In kernel context the terminal EBADF is reached for
##          any valid-shaped itimerspec because caller_pid()
##          returns None.  Real user-context fd lookup will exhibit
##          the proper EBADF vs EINVAL distinction once timerfd
##          fds are implemented.
##        * No timerfd implementation yet; the terminal at the
##          bottom of the function (EINVAL for "fd is real but
##          not a timerfd") is unreachable from kernel-context
##          tests.  Will become exercisable once timerfd_create
##          actually returns a real fd.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 22s).
##      Serial confirms "[syscall/linux]   timerfd_settime
##      itimerspec64_valid: OK" in build/serial-test.txt.

## 204. Batch 175: sys_timer_settime NULL/itimerspec/flags-last gating
##      -----------------------------------------------------------------
##      Reordered sys_timer_settime to mirror Linux's
##      SYSCALL_DEFINE4(timer_settime) in kernel/time/posix-timers.c:
##        1. if (!new_setting) return -EINVAL;
##        2. if (get_itimerspec64(&new_spec, new_setting)) -EFAULT;
##        3. do_timer_settime: timespec64_valid on both halves -> -EINVAL
##        4. lock_timer -> -EINVAL when not found
##        5. common_timer_set: (flags & ~TIMER_ABSTIME) -> -EINVAL,
##           checked AFTER timer lookup.
##      Pre-batch code returned EFAULT for NULL new_setting and rejected
##      bad flags BEFORE the user-pointer copy.  Two visible divergences:
##        * (new=NULL) returned EFAULT where Linux returns EINVAL.
##        * (flags=bad, new=bad_ptr) returned EINVAL where Linux returns
##          EFAULT (copy_from_user fails before flags are looked at).
##
##      Implementation:
##        * NULL new -> EINVAL gate first.
##        * validate_user_read(32) + copy_from_user(32) -> EFAULT.
##        * Parse 4 i64s and gate on timespec64_valid (tv_sec >= 0,
##          tv_nsec in [0, NSEC_PER_SEC)) for both it_interval and
##          it_value -> EINVAL.
##        * Optional old_setting write-validate.
##        * Flags consumed but not gated on; the terminal EINVAL (no
##          timer found) subsumes the flags-EINVAL that would fire
##          inside common_timer_set after lookup.
##
##      Self-tests updated/added:
##        * timer_settime(new=NULL) -> EINVAL (was EFAULT pre-batch).
##        * timer_settime(flags=0xff, new=valid_itspec) -> EINVAL.
##        * timer_settime(flags=0xff, new=NULL) -> EINVAL (NULL first).
##        * timer_settime(new=valid) -> EINVAL terminal (no timers).
##        * timer_settime(new=bad_itspec it_value.tv_nsec=1e9) -> EINVAL
##          via timespec64_valid.
##        * timer_settime(new=neg_itspec it_interval.tv_sec=-1) -> EINVAL
##          via timespec64_valid.
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   timer_settime
##          NULL/itimerspec/flags-last gating: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * The (flags=bad, new=unmapped_ptr) -> EFAULT case is not
##          testable in kernel context: validate_user_read is bypassed
##          and the subsequent copy_from_user page-faults on the
##          ad-hoc pointer.  The flags-last ordering is correct but
##          not observable from kernel-context self-tests; will be
##          exercisable from real user contexts once timers exist.
##        * No POSIX timer implementation; the EINVAL terminal "no
##          timer" path is reached for every valid itimerspec.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).
##      Serial confirms "[syscall/linux]   timer_settime
##      NULL/itimerspec/flags-last gating: OK" in
##      build/serial-test.txt.

## 205. Batch 176: sys_timer_gettime lookup-first gating
##      ----------------------------------------------------
##      Reordered sys_timer_gettime to match Linux's
##      SYSCALL_DEFINE2(timer_gettime) in
##      kernel/time/posix-timers.c:
##        int ret = do_timer_gettime(timer_id, &cur_setting);
##        if (!ret) { if (put_itimerspec64(...)) ret = -EFAULT; }
##        return ret;
##      do_timer_gettime looks up the timer via posix_timer_by_id;
##      with no timer it returns -EINVAL before put_itimerspec64
##      touches curr_value.  Our pre-batch gate returned EFAULT
##      for NULL/bad curr_value, masking the EINVAL terminal.
##
##      Implementation: dropped the upfront NULL/validate_user_write
##      checks.  With no POSIX timer subsystem, every timer id is
##      unknown and the terminal EINVAL fires for all callers.
##
##      Self-tests updated/added:
##        * timer_gettime(timerid=0, curr=NULL) -> EINVAL (was EFAULT).
##        * timer_gettime(timerid=0, curr=valid) -> EINVAL (unchanged).
##        * timer_gettime(timerid=0, curr=0x1 bogus) -> EINVAL (new;
##          proves the lookup-first ordering — the user ptr is never
##          touched).
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   timer_gettime lookup-first
##          gating: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * The EFAULT path (valid timer id, bad user ptr) is not
##          reachable until a POSIX timer subsystem exists.  Once
##          timer_create returns real timer ids, this stub needs
##          the put_itimerspec64-style write validation re-added
##          AFTER the timer lookup succeeds.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 35s).
##      Serial confirms "[syscall/linux]   timer_gettime
##      lookup-first gating: OK" in build/serial-test.txt.

## 206. Batch 177: sys_clock_nanosleep clockid/EOPNOTSUPP/validity/flags
##      ------------------------------------------------------------------
##      Tightened sys_clock_nanosleep to mirror Linux's
##      SYSCALL_DEFINE4(clock_nanosleep) in kernel/time/posix-timers.c.
##      Pre-batch the stub jumped straight into read_timespec and
##      treated every clockid except CLOCK_REALTIME(0) as "use
##      hrtimer" — invalid clockids silently slept, bad flags were
##      ignored, malformed timespecs were converted via saturation.
##      Glibc-style probes saw success where Linux rejects.
##
##      Implementation (Linux gate order):
##        1. clockid in [0..=11] AND != 10 (SGI_CYCLE reserved)
##           -> else EINVAL.
##        2. clockid in {3,4,5,6} (THREAD_CPUTIME_ID, MONOTONIC_RAW,
##           REALTIME_COARSE, MONOTONIC_COARSE; no .nsleep in Linux)
##           -> EOPNOTSUPP.
##        3. read_timespec(req_ptr) -> EFAULT on NULL/bad ptr.
##        4. timespec64_valid: tv_sec >= 0 AND
##           tv_nsec in [0, NSEC_PER_SEC) -> else EINVAL.
##        5. flags & ~TIMER_ABSTIME -> EINVAL.
##        6. Sleep (existing logic preserved).
##
##      Self-tests added (9 cases):
##        * clockid=99 + NULL req -> EINVAL (was EFAULT pre-batch
##          because read_timespec ran first).
##        * clockid=10 (SGI_CYCLE) + valid req -> EINVAL.
##        * clockid=3 (THREAD_CPUTIME_ID) -> EOPNOTSUPP.
##        * clockid=5 (REALTIME_COARSE) -> EOPNOTSUPP.
##        * clockid=1 (MONOTONIC) + NULL req -> EFAULT.
##        * clockid=1 + tv_nsec=1e9 -> EINVAL.
##        * clockid=1 + tv_sec=-1 -> EINVAL.
##        * clockid=1 + flags=0x2 + valid req -> EINVAL.
##        * clockid=99 + flags=0x2 + valid req -> EINVAL (clockid first).
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   clock_nanosleep
##          clockid/EOPNOTSUPP/validity/flags gating: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * Linux's posix_clock_nanosleep further forbids TIMER_ABSTIME
##          with CLOCK_PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID (returns EINVAL) — we accept
##          it for now since we don't model per-process cputime
##          accounting.  Documented under judgment calls.
##        * Linux's nsleep returns ERESTART_RESTARTBLOCK on signal
##          interrupt and writes remaining time to rem_ptr if non-NULL;
##          we never deliver signals to the running thread mid-sleep
##          (no signal infrastructure on this kernel) so rem_ptr is
##          never used.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).
##      Serial confirms marker in build/serial-test.txt.

## 207. Batch 178: sys_clock_settime clockid/EOPNOTSUPP gating
##      ------------------------------------------------------
##      Tightened sys_clock_settime to match Linux's
##      SYSCALL_DEFINE2(clock_settime) gate order
##      (kernel/time/posix-timers.c):
##        1. clockid_to_kclock NULL -> EINVAL
##        2. !kc->clock_set -> EOPNOTSUPP
##        3. get_timespec64 -> EFAULT
##        4. kc->clock_set -> per-impl result (EPERM in our case)
##      Pre-batch we skipped (1) and (2) — any clockid (even garbage
##      99 or SGI_CYCLE) returned EFAULT/EPERM where Linux returns
##      EINVAL/EOPNOTSUPP.
##
##      Implementation:
##        * clockid in [0..=11] AND != 10 -> else EINVAL.
##        * clockid in {1,3,4,5,6,7,8,9} -> EOPNOTSUPP (clocks whose
##          k_clock table entry has .clock_set == NULL in Linux:
##          MONOTONIC, THREAD_CPUTIME_ID, MONOTONIC_RAW,
##          REALTIME_COARSE, MONOTONIC_COARSE, BOOTTIME,
##          REALTIME_ALARM, BOOTTIME_ALARM).
##        * NULL tp -> EFAULT, validate_user_read(16) -> EFAULT.
##        * Terminal EPERM (no CAP_SYS_TIME).
##
##      Self-tests added (5 new + 2 updated):
##        * clock_settime(CLOCK_REALTIME=0, NULL) -> EFAULT (unchanged).
##        * clock_settime(CLOCK_REALTIME=0, 0x1000) -> EPERM (unchanged).
##        * clock_settime(99, NULL) -> EINVAL (was EFAULT pre-batch).
##        * clock_settime(SGI_CYCLE=10, valid) -> EINVAL.
##        * clock_settime(MONOTONIC=1, valid) -> EOPNOTSUPP.
##        * clock_settime(BOOTTIME=7, NULL) -> EOPNOTSUPP
##          (proves EOPNOTSUPP beats NULL ptr EFAULT).
##        * clock_settime(TAI=11, valid) -> EPERM
##          (TAI has .clock_set, falls through to EPERM).
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   clock_settime
##          clockid/EOPNOTSUPP gating: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID=2 has .clock_set in Linux but always
##          returns EINVAL for non-current PID; we return EPERM
##          instead (no per-process cputime accounting yet).
##          Acceptable as a stronger refusal than Linux's EINVAL
##          since CAP_SYS_TIME would be checked first anyway.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).
##      Serial confirms marker in build/serial-test.txt.

## 208. Batch 179: sys_clock_getres clockid gating
##      ----------------------------------------------
##      Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE2(clock_getres) calls
##      clockid_to_kclock and returns -EINVAL for unknown clockids
##      BEFORE touching the user tp pointer.  Our stub returned
##      1ns success for every clockid (including SGI_CYCLE=10 and
##      garbage like 99) — a probe walking clockids would think
##      our kernel supports the full set.
##
##      Implementation: add clockid in [0..=11] AND != 10 gate
##      ahead of the existing NULL-permitted res-ptr write.
##      Negative clockids (Linux's dynamic-CPU/posix-fd encoding)
##      rejected as EINVAL — we don't implement any of them.
##
##      Self-tests added (5):
##        * clock_getres(REALTIME, valid ptr) -> 0.
##        * clock_getres(MONOTONIC, NULL) -> 0 (NULL permitted).
##        * clock_getres(99, valid ptr) -> EINVAL (was 0 pre-batch).
##        * clock_getres(SGI_CYCLE=10, valid ptr) -> EINVAL.
##        * clock_getres(u64::MAX -> -1 i32, NULL) -> EINVAL
##          (clockid check beats NULL-ok).
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   clock_getres clockid gating: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * Always returns 1ns resolution; Linux varies per clock
##          (REALTIME_COARSE = jiffy resolution, etc.).  Acceptable
##          since our hrtimer provides ns granularity uniformly.
##        * Negative clockids that would be valid dynamic-CPU clocks
##          in Linux (kthread_create_cpu encoding) are rejected
##          here — no CPU clock subsystem to back them.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 22s).
##      Serial confirms marker in build/serial-test.txt.

## 209. Batch 180: sys_setitimer NULL-cancel/timeval/which-last gating
##      -----------------------------------------------------------------
##      Tightened sys_setitimer to mirror Linux's
##      SYSCALL_DEFINE3(setitimer) in kernel/time/itimer.c:
##        if (value) { copy_from_user -> EFAULT }
##        else memset(&set_buffer, 0, sizeof(...));
##        error = do_setitimer(which, &set_buffer, ...);
##      do_setitimer: timeval_valid on both halves -> EINVAL;
##      switch (which) default -> EINVAL.
##      Pre-batch ordering was: which > 2 -> EINVAL FIRST; then
##      NULL value -> EFAULT; then read.  Two visible divergences:
##        * (which=0, value=NULL) returned EFAULT where Linux
##          treats NULL as "clear the timer" and returns 0.
##        * (which=99, value=bad_ptr) returned EINVAL where Linux
##          returns EFAULT (copy_from_user fails before which).
##      Also missing: timeval_valid gate on both itimerval halves.
##
##      Implementation:
##        * NULL new -> zeroed set_buffer (cancel semantics).
##        * Non-NULL new -> validate_user_read + copy_from_user
##          (EFAULT propagated).
##        * Parse 4 i64s and gate timeval_valid:
##          tv_sec >= 0 AND tv_usec in [0, USEC_PER_SEC) on both
##          it_interval and it_value -> else EINVAL.
##        * which > 2 -> EINVAL (now AFTER copy + validity).
##        * is_cancel terminal: zero buf -> 0 with old_value write;
##          non-zero -> ENOSYS (no timer arming infrastructure).
##
##      Self-tests updated/added (5 cases, 2 updated):
##        * setitimer(99, valid_zero_itv, _) -> EINVAL (was using
##          0x1000 ptr that worked accidentally via early which gate).
##        * setitimer(0, NULL, _) -> 0 (was EFAULT pre-batch).
##        * setitimer(0, tv_usec=2e6, _) -> EINVAL (new validity gate).
##        * setitimer(0, neg tv_sec, _) -> EINVAL.
##        * setitimer(99, NULL, _) -> EINVAL (NULL->zero, which-default).
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   setitimer
##          NULL-cancel/timeval/which-last gating: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * Non-zero (arming) itimerval still returns ENOSYS rather
##          than 0; we have no signal infrastructure to deliver
##          SIGALRM/SIGVTALRM/SIGPROF on expiry.
##        * The (which=99, bad_ptr) -> EFAULT case is not testable in
##          kernel context because validate_user_read is bypassed and
##          copy_from_user page-faults on ad-hoc pointers.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 22s).
##      Serial confirms marker in build/serial-test.txt.

## 210. Batch 181: sys_nanosleep timespec64_valid gating
##      -------------------------------------------------
##      Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE2(nanosleep) (kernel/time/hrtimer.c)
##      gates: get_timespec64 -> EFAULT; !timespec64_valid -> EINVAL;
##      then hrtimer_nanosleep.  Our pre-batch stub copied the
##      timespec then called to_nanos() which silently clamps
##      malformed values (tv_sec < 0 or tv_nsec outside
##      [0, NSEC_PER_SEC)) to 0 — a probe with negative tv_sec saw
##      success (yield + return 0) where Linux returns -EINVAL.
##
##      Implementation: insert timespec64_valid gate
##      (tv_sec >= 0 AND tv_nsec in [0, NSEC_PER_SEC)) between
##      read_timespec and to_nanos.
##
##      Self-tests added (6):
##        * nanosleep(NULL) -> EFAULT (existing read_timespec gate).
##        * tv_nsec = 1_000_000_000 -> EINVAL (boundary, exact).
##        * tv_nsec = -1 -> EINVAL.
##        * tv_sec = -1 -> EINVAL.
##        * tv_nsec = 999_999_999 (just below boundary) -> 0.
##        * All-zero req -> 0 (yields).
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   nanosleep timespec64_valid gating: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * Linux's nanosleep writes the remaining time to rem on
##          signal interruption; ours doesn't model interruption
##          (no signal infrastructure), so rem is always untouched.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).
##      Serial confirms marker in build/serial-test.txt.

## 211. Batch 182: epoll_wait/pwait/pwait2 EP_MAX_EVENTS gating
##      -----------------------------------------------------------
##      Linux's do_epoll_wait (fs/eventpoll.c) gates:
##        if (maxevents <= 0 || maxevents > EP_MAX_EVENTS)
##            return -EINVAL;
##      where EP_MAX_EVENTS = INT_MAX / sizeof(struct epoll_event)
##      = INT_MAX / 12 = 178956970.  Our stubs gated maxevents <= 0
##      but not the upper bound; a probe with INT_MAX maxevents
##      passed the lower check, saturating_mul to ~25 GiB of length,
##      validate_user_write was a no-op in kernel context, then hit
##      the terminal EBADF — where Linux returns EINVAL.
##
##      Implementation: define module-level
##      `const EP_MAX_EVENTS: i32 = i32::MAX / 12;` and gate
##      `maxevents > EP_MAX_EVENTS` alongside the existing
##      `maxevents <= 0` check in sys_epoll_wait, sys_epoll_pwait,
##      and sys_epoll_pwait2.  Single source of truth for the cap.
##
##      Self-tests added (5):
##        * epoll_wait(_, _, INT_MAX) -> EINVAL (was EBADF).
##        * epoll_pwait(_, _, INT_MAX, _, _, sigsetsize=8) -> EINVAL.
##        * epoll_pwait2(_, _, INT_MAX, _, _, sigsetsize=8) -> EINVAL.
##        * epoll_wait(_, _, EP_MAX_EVENTS) -> EBADF (boundary, valid).
##        * epoll_wait(_, _, EP_MAX_EVENTS+1) -> EINVAL (boundary+1).
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   epoll_wait/pwait/pwait2
##          EP_MAX_EVENTS gating: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * No real epoll implementation; the terminal EBADF stands
##          for "epfd is not a valid fd".  Once epoll_create returns
##          real fds, this stub should resolve the fd, verify it's
##          an epoll fd (EINVAL on non-epoll), and actually wait.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).
##      Serial confirms marker in build/serial-test.txt.

## 212. Linux ABI: epoll_pwait/pwait2 sigsetsize-iff-umask gating
##      (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##
##      Linux's set_user_sigmask (kernel/signal.c) implements:
##          if (!umask) return 0;
##          if (sigsetsize != sizeof(sigset_t)) return -EINVAL;
##          if (copy_from_user(&kmask, umask, ...)) return -EFAULT;
##
##      That is: sigsetsize is only validated when umask is non-NULL.
##      Our sys_epoll_pwait and sys_epoll_pwait2 stubs unconditionally
##      rejected any sigsetsize that wasn't 0 or 8, diverging in two
##      cases that feature-detection probes can hit:
##        * (umask = NULL, sigsetsize = 99) — Linux: 0 (continues into
##          the wait), us: EINVAL.
##        * (umask != NULL, sigsetsize = 0)  — Linux: EINVAL, us:
##          accepted.
##
##      Fix: reorder so the umask presence gate runs first.  When
##      umask is NULL, skip the sigsetsize check entirely.  When
##      umask is non-NULL, require sigsetsize == 8 (EINVAL on any
##      other value) before validating the umask pointer (EFAULT on
##      bad pointer).  Maxevents/events checks still run after, in
##      the same order as before.
##
##      epoll_pwait2 keeps its timespec gate ahead of the umask
##      check, matching Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE6(epoll_pwait2) which
##      copies the timespec before delegating to do_epoll_pwait
##      (which is what runs set_user_sigmask).
##
##      Self-tests updated/added:
##        * epoll_pwait(_, _, 1, _, umask=0x1000, sigsetsize=4) ->
##          EINVAL  (was EINVAL via the old unconditional gate, now
##          via the correct non-NULL-umask path).
##        * epoll_pwait(_, _, 1, _, umask=NULL,   sigsetsize=99) ->
##          EBADF   (NEW: pre-batch returned EINVAL).
##        * epoll_pwait(_, _, 1, _, umask=0x1000, sigsetsize=0)  ->
##          EINVAL  (NEW: pre-batch returned EBADF).
##        * epoll_pwait2(_, _, 1, _, umask=NULL,   sigsetsize=99) ->
##          EBADF.
##        * epoll_pwait2(_, _, 1, _, umask=0x1000, sigsetsize=0)  ->
##          EINVAL.
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   epoll_pwait/pwait2
##          sigsetsize-iff-umask gating: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * In kernel context (the only context the boot self-tests
##          run in) validate_user_read is a no-op, so we cannot
##          distinguish "sigsetsize == 8 but bad pointer" (which
##          Linux returns EFAULT for) from a successful read.  A
##          dummy non-NULL pointer like 0x1000 lets us exercise the
##          sigsetsize check; the EFAULT path needs a real userspace
##          caller with an unmapped page to test.
##        * Still no real epoll implementation behind the gates; the
##          terminal EBADF stands in for "epfd is not a valid fd".
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 213. Linux ABI: timer_create sigevent/clockid/EOPNOTSUPP gating
##      (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##
##      Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE3(timer_create) + do_timer_create
##      (kernel/time/posix-timers.c) order:
##        1. if (timer_event_spec)
##               if (copy_from_user(&event, ...)) return -EFAULT;
##        2. kc = clockid_to_kclock(which_clock);
##           if (!kc) return -EINVAL;
##        3. if (!kc->timer_create) return -EOPNOTSUPP;
##        4. ... create timer ...
##        5. if (copy_to_user(created_timer_id, ...)) return -EFAULT;
##
##      Pre-batch sys_timer_create diverged in three places:
##        a) clockid range was checked BEFORE the sigevent copy, so
##           (sigevent=bad_ptr, clockid=99) returned EINVAL where
##           Linux returns EFAULT.
##        b) SGI_CYCLE (clockid=10) was accepted by our `(0..=11)`
##           gate, but Linux has no k_clock entry for it
##           (posix_clocks[CLOCK_SGI_CYCLE] is unset) → EINVAL.
##        c) MONOTONIC_RAW(4), REALTIME_COARSE(5),
##           MONOTONIC_COARSE(6) — reader-only clocks with no
##           .timer_create — returned ENOSYS where Linux returns
##           EOPNOTSUPP.
##
##      Fix: reorder so sigevent validation runs first, exclude
##      SGI_CYCLE from the accepted set, add an EOPNOTSUPP gate for
##      {4,5,6}, and keep the timerid_t output-ptr EFAULT check after
##      the clockid gates (mirroring Linux's copy_to_user position).
##
##      Self-tests added (5 new):
##        * TIMER_CREATE(clockid=10) -> EINVAL  (SGI_CYCLE rejection)
##        * TIMER_CREATE(clockid=4)  -> EOPNOTSUPP  (MONOTONIC_RAW)
##        * TIMER_CREATE(clockid=5)  -> EOPNOTSUPP  (REALTIME_COARSE)
##        * TIMER_CREATE(clockid=6)  -> EOPNOTSUPP  (MONOTONIC_COARSE)
##        * TIMER_CREATE(clockid=11, timerid=NULL) -> EFAULT  (TAI
##          reaches the output-ptr gate)
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   timer_create
##          sigevent/clockid/EOPNOTSUPP gating: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * Valid (clockid, timer_id) still returns ENOSYS because we
##          have no POSIX timer subsystem.  Linux would return 0 and
##          deposit a kernel_timer_t.  Self-tests can't distinguish
##          (sigevent=bad_ptr, clockid=valid) → EFAULT in kernel
##          context because validate_user_read is bypassed; the
##          reorder is observable from userspace.
##        * Capability checks for REALTIME_ALARM(8) /
##          BOOTTIME_ALARM(9) (CAP_WAKE_ALARM in Linux) are not
##          modelled — we accept them through to ENOSYS.  Real
##          timer support will add the cap gate.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 214. Linux ABI: clock_adjtime copy-first/EOPNOTSUPP gating
##      (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##
##      Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE2(clock_adjtime) + do_clock_adjtime
##      (kernel/time/posix-timers.c) gate order:
##        1. if (copy_from_user(&ktx, utx, sizeof(ktx))) return -EFAULT;
##        2. kc = clockid_to_kclock(id);  if (!kc) return -EINVAL;
##        3. if (!kc->clock_adj) return -EOPNOTSUPP;
##        4. kc->clock_adj(&ktx): CAP_SYS_TIME for write modes -> EPERM.
##        5. if (copy_to_user(utx, &ktx, sizeof(ktx))) return -EFAULT.
##
##      Pre-batch sys_clock_adjtime rejected every clockid except
##      REALTIME(0) / TAI(11) up front with EINVAL, which mismatched
##      Linux in two ways:
##        * (clockid=99, tx=NULL) returned EINVAL; Linux returns
##          EFAULT because copy_from_user runs before clockid lookup.
##        * (clockid=MONOTONIC=1 / coarse / monotonic_raw, tx=valid)
##          returned EINVAL; Linux returns EOPNOTSUPP — these clocks
##          have a k_clock entry but no .clock_adj.
##      SGI_CYCLE(10) was already covered by our EINVAL gate but only
##      incidentally (it was outside {0,11}); the new code rejects it
##      via the same explicit "no k_clock entry" path Linux uses.
##
##      Fix: reorder to do tx pointer/copy first, then clockid range
##      gate (EINVAL for out-of-range or SGI_CYCLE), then
##      clock_adj-presence gate (EOPNOTSUPP for clockids in {1..9}\{10}),
##      then modes-driven EPERM, then validate_user_write +
##      adjtimex_fill_read.
##
##      Self-tests added/updated (6):
##        * CLOCK_ADJTIME(clockid=99, tx_valid) -> EINVAL (was already
##          EINVAL via old gate, now via the explicit range gate).
##        * CLOCK_ADJTIME(clockid=10 SGI_CYCLE, tx_valid) -> EINVAL.
##        * CLOCK_ADJTIME(clockid=1 MONOTONIC, tx_valid) -> EOPNOTSUPP
##          (was EINVAL).
##        * CLOCK_ADJTIME(clockid=5 REALTIME_COARSE, tx_valid)
##          -> EOPNOTSUPP (was EINVAL).
##        * CLOCK_ADJTIME(clockid=99, tx=NULL) -> EFAULT (was EINVAL).
##        * CLOCK_ADJTIME(clockid=REALTIME, tx=NULL) -> EFAULT
##          (unchanged).
##        * Pre-existing CLOCK_REALTIME/CLOCK_TAI modes=0 -> TIME_ERROR
##          tests and modes=1 -> EPERM test still hold.
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   clock_adjtime
##          copy-first/EOPNOTSUPP gating: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * sys_adjtimex (no clockid) is the CLOCK_REALTIME entry and
##          already had the right order — left alone.
##        * The full copy_from_user only reads 4 bytes (modes field).
##          Linux's reads the whole 208-byte struct, so a torn page
##          past offset 4 wouldn't EFAULT in our stub.  Acceptable
##          because validate_user_read covers the whole struct
##          immediately above.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 215. Linux ABI: clock_gettime ALARM/TAI accept + SGI_CYCLE reject
##      (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##
##      Linux's clockid_to_kclock + .clock_get_timespec covers every
##      clockid 0..=11 except SGI_CYCLE(10), which has no posix_clocks[]
##      entry.  Pre-batch sys_clock_gettime only matched clockids
##      0..=7 and returned EINVAL for everything else, diverging from
##      Linux on CLOCK_REALTIME_ALARM(8), CLOCK_BOOTTIME_ALARM(9),
##      and CLOCK_TAI(11).  Real userspace (systemd-timesyncd, glibc
##      clock-feature probes) hits this when checking whether the
##      kernel supports CLOCK_TAI.
##
##      Fix:
##        * CLOCK_REALTIME_ALARM(8), CLOCK_TAI(11) join the wall-clock
##          family (return clock_realtime()).  Our TAI offset is 0 so
##          TAI == REALTIME, matching Linux's tai_get_timespec when
##          tai_offset is unset.
##        * CLOCK_BOOTTIME_ALARM(9) joins the monotonic family
##          (alarm_clock_get_timespec returns the BOOTTIME equivalent).
##        * SGI_CYCLE(10) and anything outside 0..=11 still return
##          EINVAL — matches Linux's clockid_to_kclock NULL branch.
##
##      Self-tests added:
##        * For clockid in {8, 9, 11}: CLOCK_GETTIME(clockid, NULL_tp)
##          -> EFAULT (was EINVAL).  Confirms the clockid is no longer
##          rejected up front; write_timespec fires instead.
##        * CLOCK_GETTIME(10, NULL_tp) -> EINVAL (unchanged but now
##          gated explicitly).
##        * Pre-existing CLOCK_GETTIME(999) -> EINVAL still holds.
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   clock_gettime ALARM/TAI accept +
##          SGI_CYCLE reject: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * CLOCK_PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID(2) / CLOCK_THREAD_CPUTIME_ID(3)
##          still return monotonic ns instead of the actual
##          process/thread CPU usage.  Tracking CPU time per task is
##          a real scheduler change, out of scope for this batch.
##        * CLOCK_REALTIME_ALARM(8) and CLOCK_BOOTTIME_ALARM(9) in
##          Linux differ from their non-alarm twins only when timers
##          armed on them fire while the system is in suspend; for
##          a bare clock_gettime they're identical, which is what we
##          implement.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 216. Linux ABI: inotify_add_watch mask/EBADF gating
##      (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##
##      Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE3(inotify_add_watch)
##      (fs/notify/inotify/inotify_user.c) gate order:
##        1. if (mask & ~ALL_INOTIFY_BITS) return -EINVAL;
##        2. if ((mask & (IN_MASK_ADD|IN_MASK_CREATE)) ==
##               (IN_MASK_ADD|IN_MASK_CREATE)) return -EINVAL;
##        3. fdget(fd) -> EBADF;
##        4. f.file->f_op != inotify_fops -> EINVAL;
##        5. user_path_at(pathname) -> EFAULT, ENOENT, etc.
##
##      Pre-batch sys_inotify_add_watch did path NULL/validate
##      first, then mask==0 -> EINVAL, then fd lookup.  Divergences:
##        * (fd=0, path=NULL, mask=IN_ACCESS) returned EFAULT;
##          Linux returns EBADF (the f_op check / EBADF fdget result
##          fires before user_path_at).
##        * (fd=any, path=valid, mask=0) returned EINVAL;
##          Linux accepts mask=0 (no events filter, watch still
##          added).  The mask==0 rejection was a guess, not Linux.
##        * Probes with unknown high bits walked past us to EBADF;
##          Linux gates them with the ~ALL_INOTIFY_BITS check
##          before fdget.
##
##      ALL_INOTIFY_BITS resolves to 0xF007_EFFF on Linux:
##        events: 0x0FFF (IN_ACCESS .. IN_MOVE_SELF)
##        meta:   0xE000 (IN_UNMOUNT, Q_OVERFLOW, IGNORED)
##        flags:  0x0700_0000 (ONLYDIR, DONT_FOLLOW, EXCL_UNLINK)
##        meta2:  0xF000_0000 (MASK_CREATE, MASK_ADD, ISDIR, ONESHOT)
##
##      Fix: reorder so mask bit-validity runs first, then the
##      IN_MASK_ADD+IN_MASK_CREATE mutual-exclusion check, then fd
##      lookup, then path validation, then terminal EINVAL (would-be
##      "not an inotify fd").  Drop the spurious mask==0 EINVAL.
##
##      Self-tests added/updated (5):
##        * INOTIFY_ADD_WATCH(fd=0, path=NULL, mask=1) -> EBADF
##          (was EFAULT).
##        * INOTIFY_ADD_WATCH(fd=0, path=0x1000, mask=0) -> EBADF
##          (was EINVAL).
##        * INOTIFY_ADD_WATCH(_, path, mask=0x1000) -> EINVAL
##          (NEW: reserved bit between IGNORED and ONLYDIR).
##        * INOTIFY_ADD_WATCH(_, path, mask=MASK_ADD|MASK_CREATE)
##          -> EINVAL  (NEW).
##        * Emits "[syscall/linux]   inotify_add_watch mask/EBADF
##          gating: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * In kernel context (caller_pid=None) every fd lookup is
##          EBADF, so the (valid fd, not inotify_fops) -> EINVAL
##          path is unreachable from the boot self-test.  Once
##          inotify support lands the test will need a real
##          non-inotify fd to exercise that gate.
##        * Still no real inotify implementation behind the gates;
##          inotify_init / init1 return ENOSYS.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

## 217. Linux ABI: pidfd_open PIDFD_THREAD acceptance
##      (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##
##      Linux 6.2 added PIDFD_THREAD (= O_EXCL = 0o200) as a valid
##      pidfd_open flag (include/uapi/linux/pidfd.h):
##          #define PIDFD_NONBLOCK O_NONBLOCK  // 0o4000
##          #define PIDFD_THREAD   O_EXCL       // 0o200
##      Pre-batch sys_pidfd_open only accepted PIDFD_NONBLOCK, so a
##      probe (libpidfd / glibc 2.36+ / systemd-pidfd) requesting a
##      per-thread pidfd saw EINVAL where Linux 6.2+ creates one.
##
##      Fix: extend VALID_FLAGS to PIDFD_NONBLOCK | PIDFD_THREAD.
##      Our pcb backing the FdEntry::pidfd doesn't model threads
##      separately from processes, so PIDFD_THREAD is accepted as a
##      hint and the resulting fd refers to the target's process —
##      Linux semantics aside from "wait4 on a thread pidfd only
##      reaps that thread" which we'd model only once threads are
##      first-class.
##
##      Self-tests added (2):
##        * PIDFD_OPEN(pid=0x7FFF_FFFE, PIDFD_THREAD) -> ESRCH
##          (was EINVAL).  Confirms PIDFD_THREAD passes the flag
##          gate and reaches the pid lookup.
##        * PIDFD_OPEN(pid=0x7FFF_FFFE, NONBLOCK|THREAD) -> ESRCH.
##        * Pre-existing pidfd_open(0) -> EINVAL and
##          pidfd_open(_, flag=0x1) -> EINVAL still hold (0x1 is
##          outside the new mask).
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * No per-thread pidfd semantics.  Once threads are
##          first-class in pcb, PIDFD_THREAD should switch the entry
##          kind to a thread pidfd and route waitid / pidfd_send_signal
##          to the thread, not the whole process.
##        * No new marker emitted (the change is a single flag
##          addition; the existing pidfd test block is the validator).
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 218. Linux ABI: eventfd legacy arg1 ignored
##      (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##
##      Linux's legacy eventfd syscall is declared as
##          SYSCALL_DEFINE1(eventfd, unsigned int, count)
##      in fs/eventfd.c and its body is literally
##          return do_eventfd(count, 0);
##      The kernel never reads the second register at all — only
##      eventfd2 takes a flags argument.
##
##      Pre-batch sys_eventfd enforced "arg1 == 0" "for forward
##      compat", rejecting any caller that invoked
##      syscall(SYS_eventfd, count) while rsi held leftover data
##      (very common with libc shims that zero only the arguments
##      they were told about).  Linux happily creates the eventfd in
##      that case, so a probe sniffing for SYS_eventfd saw EINVAL on
##      us where Linux returns a valid fd.
##
##      Fix: drop the arg1!=0 EINVAL gate; explicitly bind
##      `let _ = args.arg1;` and call eventfd_create_with_flags
##      with flags=0 unconditionally, matching SYSCALL_DEFINE1.
##
##      Self-test updated (1):
##        * EVENTFD(initval=0, arg1=1) used to expect EINVAL;
##          it now expects EBADF (the kernel-context fd-install
##          failure mode).  Comment updated to explain that arg1 is
##          intentionally ignored and that the EBADF surfaces from
##          caller_pid()==None rather than from any flag gate.
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * In kernel context (caller_pid=None) the fd cannot be
##          installed, so the test cannot observe the successful
##          creation path — only that the gate is gone and the
##          syscall reaches fd install.
##        * sys_eventfd2 (which does take real flags) is unchanged
##          and continues to honour EFD_CLOEXEC | EFD_NONBLOCK |
##          EFD_SEMAPHORE.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 219. Linux ABI: pidfd_send_signal flags/fd/sig gate order
##      (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##
##      Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE4(pidfd_send_signal) in
##      kernel/signal.c gates in this order:
##          1. flags & ~PIDFD_SIGNAL_* -> EINVAL
##          2. fdget(pidfd)            -> EBADF (no such fd)
##          3. pidfd_to_pid(f.file)    -> EBADF (fd isn't a pidfd)
##          4. access_pidfd_pidns      -> EINVAL
##          5. switch on flags
##          6. if (info) copy_siginfo_from_user -> EFAULT
##          7. kill_pid_info_type / valid_signal(sig) -> EINVAL
##
##      Pre-batch sys_pidfd_send_signal checked sig range and the
##      siginfo pointer BEFORE looking up the fd, so callers probing
##      pidfd_send_signal with (fd=anything, sig=999) saw EINVAL
##      where Linux returns EBADF (fdget fires first).  Likewise a
##      bogus siginfo pointer with a junk fd surfaced EFAULT
##      pre-batch where Linux returns EBADF.
##
##      Fix: reorder so flags -> fd validate -> fd lookup ->
##      pidfd-kind -> siginfo readability -> sig range.  Keep
##      flags as the first gate (Linux does this before fdget).
##
##      Self-tests updated/added (4):
##        * PIDFD_SEND_SIGNAL(fd=0, sig=999, flags=0) now expects
##          EBADF (was EINVAL).  Confirms fd lookup precedes sig
##          validation.
##        * PIDFD_SEND_SIGNAL(fd=u64::MAX, sig=9, flags=1) -> EINVAL.
##          Confirms flags-check is still first.
##        * PIDFD_SEND_SIGNAL(fd=0, sig=999, info=0x1, flags=0) ->
##          EBADF (new).  Confirms fd lookup shadows both the EFAULT
##          and EINVAL paths for the siginfo + sig combination.
##        * PIDFD_SEND_SIGNAL(fd=3, sig=9, flags=0) -> EBADF
##          (unchanged) — kernel-context lookup_caller_fd.
##      Marker emitted: "pidfd_send_signal flags/fd/sig gate order: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * In kernel context (caller_pid=None) every fd lookup is
##          EBADF, so the (valid pidfd, bad sig) -> EINVAL terminal
##          path is unreachable from the boot self-test.
##        * PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD / PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD_GROUP /
##          PIDFD_SIGNAL_PROCESS_GROUP (Linux 6.9+) are still
##          rejected as EINVAL by the flags gate; once thread-pidfd
##          semantics land they should be accepted.
##        * pidns access check (#4) is not modelled — we have no
##          pid namespaces yet.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 220. Linux ABI: memfd_create flags-first / EXEC+NOEXEC_SEAL
##      (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##
##      Linux's mm/memfd.c SYSCALL_DEFINE2(memfd_create) order:
##          1. flags & ~MFD_ALL_FLAGS (or, with MFD_HUGETLB,
##             & ~(MFD_ALL_FLAGS | MFD_HUGE_MASK))   -> EINVAL
##          2. MFD_EXEC & MFD_NOEXEC_SEAL collision  -> EINVAL
##             (since Linux 6.3)
##          3. check_sysctl_memfd_noexec policy      -> EACCES
##          4. strnlen_user(uname, NAME_MAX+1)       -> EFAULT
##                                                       or EINVAL
##          5. allocate + fd install
##
##      Pre-batch sys_memfd_create checked the user pointer
##      (arg0==0 -> EFAULT, then validate_user_read) BEFORE the
##      flags gate, so a probe calling memfd_create(NULL, junk_flag)
##      saw EFAULT where Linux returns EINVAL.  We also accepted
##      MFD_EXEC | MFD_NOEXEC_SEAL together (no mutex check), so
##      glibc 2.37+ which probes that combination as a feature
##      detector saw a kernel-context EBADF rather than the EINVAL
##      Linux returns.  And the HUGE_SIZE_MASK bits were accepted
##      unconditionally rather than only when MFD_HUGETLB is set.
##
##      Fix:
##        * Hoist the flag gate above any user-pointer touch.
##        * Make the wider HUGE_SIZE_MASK acceptance conditional
##          on MFD_HUGETLB.
##        * Add the MFD_EXEC | MFD_NOEXEC_SEAL mutual exclusion.
##        * MFD_ALL_FLAGS spelled out as
##          CLOEXEC(1) | ALLOW_SEALING(2) | HUGETLB(4) |
##          NOEXEC_SEAL(8) | EXEC(0x10) = 0x1F.
##
##      Self-tests added / updated (3 new, 1 unchanged in shape):
##        * MEMFD_CREATE(name=NULL, flags=bogus reserved bit)
##          -> EINVAL (new).  Confirms flags fire before NULL.
##        * MEMFD_CREATE(_, MFD_EXEC | MFD_NOEXEC_SEAL) -> EINVAL
##          (new).  Pre-batch fell through to EBADF.
##        * MEMFD_CREATE(_, MFD_HUGETLB | huge-size bits)
##          -> EBADF (new).  Confirms the HUGETLB-conditional
##          wider mask actually accepts hugepage bits when the
##          gate flag is set.
##        * MEMFD_CREATE(_, hugesize without MFD_HUGETLB) -> EINVAL
##          (replaces the old unconditional-hugesize-EBADF test,
##          which was wrong for Linux semantics).
##        * MEMFD_CREATE(NULL, 0) -> EFAULT (unchanged in shape;
##          now arrives via the post-flags NULL gate).
##        * MEMFD_CREATE(name, junk flag) -> EINVAL (unchanged).
##        * MEMFD_CREATE(name, 0) -> EBADF and
##          MEMFD_CREATE(name, CLOEXEC|ALLOW_SEALING) -> EBADF
##          (unchanged kernel-context terminal paths).
##      Marker emitted: "memfd_create flags-first / EXEC+NOEXEC_SEAL
##      / HUGETLB: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * MFD_NOEXEC_SEAL behaviour past the gate (the actual
##          sealing of the underlying memfd against PROT_EXEC
##          mappings) is not implemented — we accept the flag and
##          return EBADF / a fresh memfd; once memfd_seal_executable
##          lands the flag should drive it.
##        * check_sysctl_memfd_noexec (the global memfd_noexec
##          sysctl) is not modelled — we never emit EACCES from
##          policy, only EINVAL from the gate.
##        * In kernel context the install path is unreachable so
##          the successful-create case still surfaces EBADF.
##
##      Build clean; first background boot run timed out (apparent
##      QEMU flake — no panic line in the output), re-run PASSED
##      (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 221. Linux ABI: bpf size-first / NULL-iff-size>0 / cmd-range
##      (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##
##      Linux's kernel/bpf/syscall.c::__sys_bpf gate order:
##        1. bpf_check_uarg_tail_zero(uattr, sizeof(bpf_attr), size)
##           - size > PAGE_SIZE -> E2BIG
##           - size <= sizeof(attr) -> 0 (no buffer touch)
##           - else: scan trailing bytes for zero
##        2. copy_from_bpfptr(&attr, uattr, size) -> EFAULT.
##           Crucially, copy with size=0 is a no-op success, so
##           NULL is fine when size==0.
##        3. switch (cmd) ... default -> EINVAL
##
##      Pre-batch sys_bpf had three divergences from Linux:
##        a. cmd>64 was rejected with EINVAL BEFORE the size check,
##           so a probe with (cmd=garbage, size=huge) saw EINVAL
##           where Linux returns E2BIG.
##        b. size==0 was rejected with EINVAL unconditionally; Linux
##           treats it as a valid no-op copy (and the per-cmd handler
##           decides whether the all-zero bpf_attr is acceptable).
##        c. arg1==NULL was rejected with EFAULT even when size==0,
##           so a probe doing bpf(cmd, NULL, 0) saw EFAULT where
##           Linux's copy_from_bpfptr copies zero bytes successfully.
##
##      Fix: reorder so size -> NULL-iff-size>0 -> validate_user_read
##      -> cmd-range -> ENOSYS.  Drop the unconditional size==0
##      gate.
##
##      Self-tests updated / added (3 updated, 2 new):
##        * BPF(0, NULL, 0) now expects ENOSYS (was EINVAL).
##          Confirms size==0 + cmd-in-range + NULL is no longer
##          short-circuited.
##        * BPF(0, NULL, 8) -> EFAULT (unchanged).
##        * BPF(0, attr, 8) -> ENOSYS (unchanged).
##        * BPF(100, attr, 8) -> EINVAL (unchanged; cmd>64).
##        * BPF(0, attr, 8192) -> E2BIG (unchanged).
##        * BPF(100, NULL, 8192) -> E2BIG (new).  Confirms size check
##          shadows the cmd-range gate.  Pre-batch this was EINVAL.
##        * BPF(100, NULL, 0) -> EINVAL (new).  Confirms cmd-range
##          fires after size + NULL gates when size==0.
##      Marker emitted: "bpf size-first / NULL-iff-size>0 /
##      cmd-range: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * No real BPF dispatcher; valid cmds still terminate at
##          ENOSYS.  Once the dispatcher lands, replace the cmd<=64
##          forward-compat gate with an exact __MAX_BPF_CMD compare
##          and surface per-cmd errnos (e.g. EINVAL for malformed
##          attr, EPERM for missing CAP_BPF, etc.).
##        * The "tail bytes must be zero" check Linux runs for
##          size > sizeof(bpf_attr) is not modelled.  Once the real
##          attr struct lands we should call the equivalent helper.
##        * security_bpf LSM hook is not modelled (we have no LSM).
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 222. Linux ABI: ptrace TRACEME-first / pid-lookup / no-request-gate
##      (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##
##      Linux's kernel/ptrace.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4(ptrace) gates:
##        1. if (request == PTRACE_TRACEME) { ptrace_traceme();
##           goto out; }                    -- ignores pid
##        2. child = find_get_task_by_vpid(pid);
##           if (!child) return -ESRCH;
##        3. attach / check / arch_ptrace; unknown request inside
##           arch_ptrace -> -EIO
##
##      Pre-batch sys_ptrace:
##        * Rejected request > 0x10_000 with EINVAL upfront — Linux
##          has no such gate; unknown requests surface EIO from the
##          per-arch switch default, AFTER the pid lookup.
##        * Returned ESRCH for (request=0=TRACEME, pid=negative).
##          Linux ignores pid for TRACEME entirely; the negative pid
##          shouldn't enter the picture at all.
##        * Returned EPERM for (any request, pid=positive but
##          non-existent).  Linux returns ESRCH first because
##          find_get_task_by_vpid fails.
##
##      Fix: reorder so PTRACE_TRACEME is checked first (returns
##      EPERM — we deny self-tracing); then pid<=0 -> ESRCH; then
##      pcb::name(pid).is_none() -> ESRCH; then terminal EPERM.
##      Drop the synthetic request-range gate.
##
##      Self-tests updated/added (4):
##        * PTRACE(TRACEME=0, pid=neg) -> EPERM (was ESRCH).
##          Confirms TRACEME ignores pid.
##        * PTRACE(ATTACH=16, pid=neg) -> ESRCH (new).  Same as old
##          "negative pid" coverage but with a non-TRACEME request.
##        * PTRACE(ATTACH=16, pid=0x7FFF_FFFE non-existent) -> ESRCH
##          (new).  Confirms pcb::name existence check fires.
##        * PTRACE(req=0x20_000 bogus, pid=neg) -> ESRCH (was
##          EINVAL).  Confirms pid lookup shadows the synthetic
##          request-range gate.
##      Marker emitted: "ptrace TRACEME-first / pid-lookup /
##      no-request-gate: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * Real PTRACE_TRACEME should succeed once on a fresh
##          caller (and only return EPERM on a re-call from an
##          already-traced task).  We return EPERM unconditionally
##          because no tracer can attach in this kernel.
##        * Request-switch dispatch isn't implemented at all; once a
##          ptrace subsystem lands, the EIO-for-unknown-request path
##          should fire after the pid resolves (e.g. for
##          (req=0x20_000, pid=valid) -> EIO).
##        * No PR_SET_PTRACER / Yama policy enforcement.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

##  223. Linux ABI: setns nstype mask check (batch 194).
##      Linux's kernel/nsproxy.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(setns) routes on the
##      fd kind:
##        * proc_ns_file path: nstype=0 OK; non-zero must equal the
##          file's ns->ops->type bit, else EINVAL.
##        * pidfd path: check_setns_flags requires nstype != 0 AND
##          nstype is a subset of CLONE_NEWNS_FLAGS, else EINVAL.
##      Both paths uniformly reject any bit outside the
##      CLONE_NEWNS_FLAGS mask before the ns_capable check (EPERM).
##
##      Pre-batch sys_setns validated the fd then went straight to
##      EPERM, skipping nstype entirely.  A probe walking nstype
##      values (e.g. nspawn, util-linux's `nsenter --setns`) saw
##      EPERM for any input — including 0xDEAD_BEEF and other
##      clearly-bogus bit patterns Linux rejects with EINVAL.
##
##      Fix: between the fd check and the terminal EPERM, reject any
##      bit outside CLONE_NEWNS_FLAGS (union of NEWNS/NEWCGROUP/
##      NEWUTS/NEWIPC/NEWUSER/NEWPID/NEWNET/NEWTIME).  nstype=0
##      flows through to EPERM because we can't distinguish
##      proc_ns_file (accepts 0) from pidfd (rejects 0) in our
##      kernel-context model; EPERM is the truthful answer on
##      either path for an unprivileged caller.
##
##      Self-tests updated/added (5):
##        * setns(fd=0, nstype=0) -> EPERM (existing, unchanged).
##        * setns(fd=0, nstype=CLONE_NEWNS=0x20000) -> EPERM (new,
##          confirms single valid bit passes).
##        * setns(fd=0, nstype=CLONE_NEWNS|CLONE_NEWUTS) -> EPERM
##          (new, confirms multi-bit subset is accepted at this
##          gate — Linux's proc_ns_file path would later reject
##          for not matching the file's ns type, but we have no
##          proc_ns_files to dispatch on).
##        * setns(fd=0, nstype=0xDEAD_BEEF) -> EINVAL (new, was
##          EPERM; bogus high bits outside the mask).
##        * setns(fd=0, nstype=1) -> EINVAL (new, bit 0 is not any
##          CLONE_NEW* — confirms the gate catches single-bit
##          garbage too, not just multi-bit nonsense).
##      Marker emitted: "setns nstype mask: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * We don't model the per-path validation difference
##          (proc_ns_file requires exact ns-type match; pidfd
##          requires nstype != 0).  When real ns files / pidfds
##          land, sys_setns must check the fd kind and apply the
##          stricter per-path gate.
##        * No CAP_SYS_ADMIN path to grant privilege; EPERM is the
##          only terminal an unprivileged caller can reach.
##        * No namespace subsystem at all yet, so even a valid
##          (fd, nstype) pair has nothing to attach to.
##
##      Build clean; boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

##  224. Linux ABI: rt_sigqueueinfo / rt_tgsigqueueinfo
##      info-before-sig (batch 195).
##
##      Linux's kernel/signal.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(rt_sigqueueinfo) calls
##      __copy_siginfo_from_user(sig, &info, uinfo) first, which does
##      copy_from_user(&info, uinfo, sizeof(struct siginfo)) and returns
##      -EFAULT on a bad uinfo pointer before any signal-number
##      validation.  The sig argument is only consulted later by
##      post_copy_siginfo_from_user (using info->si_signo for layout
##      dispatch) and ultimately kill_proc_info -> valid_signal which
##      gates with EINVAL.  Same pattern for rt_tgsigqueueinfo.
##
##      Pre-batch we validated sig first (0..=64 -> EINVAL) and the
##      info pointer second (NULL -> EFAULT, validate_user_read ->
##      EFAULT).  A probe passing (sig=99, info=NULL) saw EINVAL
##      where Linux returns EFAULT, breaking the gate-order shape
##      that strace's --decode-fds and CRIU's signal replay depend
##      on to distinguish "kernel doesn't have this signal" from
##      "userspace passed a junk buffer."
##
##      Fix: swap the validation order in both sys_rt_sigqueueinfo
##      and sys_rt_tgsigqueueinfo so the siginfo-pointer check
##      (NULL + validate_user_read) runs before the sig range check.
##      The forward to sys_kill / sys_tgkill is unchanged and still
##      enforces sig range via its own valid_signal-equivalent.
##
##      Self-tests updated/added (5 each call, was 2 each):
##        * rt_sigqueueinfo(sig=99, info=valid) -> EINVAL
##          (unchanged; sig check fires after copy succeeds).
##        * rt_sigqueueinfo(sig=9, info=NULL) -> EFAULT
##          (unchanged; happens to match either order).
##        * rt_sigqueueinfo(sig=99, info=NULL) -> EFAULT (new,
##          was EINVAL; this is the gate-order discriminator).
##        * Same three for rt_tgsigqueueinfo with corresponding
##          arg shifts (sig is arg2, info is arg3).
##      Marker emitted: "rt_sig*queueinfo info-before-sig: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * Siginfo payload is still dropped at the kill forward —
##          we don't carry si_code/si_pid/si_uid through to delivery.
##        * No CAP_KILL check on cross-process queueinfo; our
##          kill_proc_info equivalent allows any send (matches
##          our existing posix_signal model).
##        * Linux's post_copy_siginfo_from_user does additional
##          per-signo layout checks (siginfo size discrimination
##          for SIGCHLD vs SIGSEGV etc.) that we don't replicate;
##          a probe with mismatched layout sees ENOSYS-equivalent
##          from us where Linux returns EINVAL.
##
##      First boot run timed out at 120s (apparent QEMU flake,
##      matches batch 191 pattern); re-run with --timeout=300
##      PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).  Build clean.

## 225. Linux ABI: msync flags=0 / 4K-aligned / overflow=ENOMEM (batch 196).
##      Linux's mm/msync.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(msync) gate order:
##        1. flags & ~(MS_ASYNC|MS_INVALIDATE|MS_SYNC) -> -EINVAL.
##        2. offset_in_page(start)                     -> -EINVAL.
##        3. (flags & MS_ASYNC) && (flags & MS_SYNC)   -> -EINVAL.
##        4. len = round_up_page(len); end = start + len;
##           overflow (end < start, or rounding overflow) -> -ENOMEM
##           (error is set to -ENOMEM before the if (end < start)
##           goto out, so any out-path uses -ENOMEM).
##        5. end == start                              ->  0.
##        6. Walk VMAs.
##
##      Our previous implementation diverged in three places that
##      a Linux feature-detection probe would observe:
##        (a) We required flags & (MS_SYNC|MS_ASYNC) != 0, returning
##            -EINVAL for flags=0 and for flags=MS_INVALIDATE alone.
##            Linux accepts both: the sync-vs-async distinction only
##            matters per-VMA inside the walker, not at the gate.
##        (b) We aligned addr against FRAME_SIZE (16 KiB, our
##            internal page size).  Linux's PAGE_SIZE on x86_64 is
##            4 KiB and userspace probes via sysconf(_SC_PAGESIZE)
##            see 4 KiB; a probe passing a 4 KiB-aligned (but not
##            16 KiB-aligned) addr was spuriously rejected with
##            -EINVAL.  Switched to ABI_PAGE_SIZE = 4096.
##        (c) Overflow on start + len returned -EINVAL.  Linux's
##            error variable is pre-set to -ENOMEM before the
##            overflow check; we now return -ENOMEM and also use
##            -ENOMEM for the rounding overflow path (len near
##            u64::MAX).
##
##      The new gate order matches Linux exactly:
##        flag mask -> 4K alignment -> SYNC&ASYNC exclusion ->
##        len rounding (overflow=ENOMEM) -> end overflow (ENOMEM) ->
##        end==start short-circuit -> user-range validation.
##
##      Self-tests updated/added in dispatch_linux self-test:
##        * msync(addr=0,len=0,flags=0)              -> 0
##          (was EINVAL pre-batch; this is the SYNC/ASYNC
##           discriminator).
##        * msync(addr=0,len=0,flags=MS_INVALIDATE)  -> 0
##          (proves MS_INVALIDATE alone is accepted).
##        * msync(addr=0,len=0,flags=MS_SYNC|MS_ASYNC) -> EINVAL
##          (mutual exclusion preserved).
##        * msync(addr=0,len=0,flags=0x10)           -> EINVAL
##          (unknown flag bit).
##        * msync(addr=0x1234,len=4096,flags=MS_ASYNC) -> EINVAL
##          (sub-4K-aligned addr).
##        * msync(addr=0x1000,len=0,flags=MS_ASYNC)  -> 0
##          (4K-aligned addr accepted despite not being 16K-aligned;
##           this is the ABI_PAGE_SIZE discriminator).
##        * msync(addr=0x4000,len=u64::MAX,flags=MS_ASYNC) -> ENOMEM
##          (rounding overflow; this is the ENOMEM-vs-EINVAL
##           discriminator).
##      Marker emitted: "msync flags=0 / 4K-aligned / ENOMEM: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * We still don't actually walk VMAs or flush page cache
##          on success — there is no page cache yet.  msync returns
##          0 for any in-range request once the gates pass, which
##          is correct for anonymous mappings but loses the
##          "file-backed writes hit disk" guarantee for file
##          mappings.  When file-backed mmap lands, msync needs to
##          forward to the per-VMA fsync_range equivalent.
##        * validate_user_read in the kernel-context self-test
##          path is a no-op, so the test for ENOMEM-on-bad-range
##          (Linux's VM_BAD_AREA path) is not exercised by the
##          boot self-test — only the gate-level errno is.
##
##      First boot run timed out at 120s (apparent QEMU flake,
##      matches batches 191 and 195); re-run with --timeout=300
##      PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).  Build clean.

## 226. Linux ABI: rseq sig mismatch = EPERM, re-register
##      ptr/len = EINVAL (batch 197).
##      Linux's kernel/rseq.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4(rseq) discriminates
##      three failure modes that we collapsed into one:
##
##      On the UNREGISTER path:
##        * ptr != current->rseq        -> -EINVAL
##        * rseq_len != current->rseq_len -> -EINVAL
##        * sig != current->rseq_sig    -> -EPERM   (was EINVAL pre-batch)
##
##      On the register path when a registration already exists:
##        * ptr != current->rseq        -> -EINVAL  (was EBUSY pre-batch)
##        * rseq_len != current->rseq_len -> -EINVAL (was EBUSY)
##        * sig != current->rseq_sig    -> -EPERM   (was EBUSY)
##        * all three match             -> -EBUSY
##
##      The EPERM-vs-EINVAL discrimination is the kernel's way of
##      saying "your syscall is well-formed, but you don't own this
##      rseq slot" — a probe that registered with sig=A and tries
##      to unregister with sig=B is doing something semantically
##      different from one that passed garbage in the args.
##
##      Also restructured the function so that alignment / access_ok
##      validation runs only on the first-time-register path.  Linux
##      does NOT validate alignment or access_ok on UNREGISTER (the
##      pointer is implicitly known to be valid because it had to
##      pass validation at register time).  Pre-batch we ran those
##      checks unconditionally, so an unregister with an unmapped
##      (but previously-valid) pointer would see EFAULT where Linux
##      returns EINVAL.
##
##      New gate order (matches Linux):
##        1. flags & UNREGISTER set ->
##           a. Other flag bits      -> EINVAL
##           b. No prior reg         -> EINVAL
##           c. Stored ptr != rseq   -> EINVAL
##           d. Stored len != len    -> EINVAL
##           e. Stored sig != sig    -> EPERM
##           f. Clear and return 0
##        2. UNREGISTER bit clear:
##           a. Any flag bit          -> EINVAL
##           b. Already registered:
##              - ptr/len mismatch    -> EINVAL
##              - sig mismatch        -> EPERM
##              - else                -> EBUSY
##           c. Validate len/ptr/align/access_ok
##           d. Store + init runtime fields
##
##      Self-tests updated/added in dispatch_linux self-test:
##        * Re-register sig mismatch  -> EPERM (was EBUSY pre-batch).
##        * Re-register ptr mismatch  -> EINVAL (was EBUSY).
##        * Unregister sig mismatch   -> EPERM (was EINVAL).
##        * Unregister ptr mismatch   -> EINVAL.
##      Marker emitted:
##        "rseq sig-mismatch=EPERM / re-reg ptr=EINVAL: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * Linux's rseq_len validation is a range
##          (ORIG_RSEQ_SIZE=20 <= len <= PAGE_SIZE=4096), permitting
##          older callers that use the deprecated 20-byte layout.
##          We still require exactly RSEQ_STRUCT_SIZE=32.  A musl or
##          legacy-glibc caller passing rseq_len=20 sees EINVAL where
##          Linux would accept and only zero the 20-byte subset.
##          Documented for a follow-up if such a caller appears.
##        * The cpu_id / cpu_id_start / mm_cid context-switch hook
##          is still deferred — see todo entries from batch 121.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 26s on first try).

## 227. Linux ABI: mq_timed{send,receive} timespec64_valid
##      gate ahead of fd lookup (batch 198).
##      Linux's ipc/mqueue.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE5(mq_timedsend) and
##      SYSCALL_DEFINE5(mq_timedreceive) call prepare_timeout on
##      the abs_timeout pointer as the very first step:
##
##        int res = prepare_timeout(u_abs_timeout, &ts);
##        if (res) return res;
##
##      prepare_timeout copies in the timespec and runs
##      timespec64_valid:
##        tv_sec  >= 0
##        tv_nsec in [0, NSEC_PER_SEC=1_000_000_000)
##      EFAULT on copy fail, EINVAL on bad value.  Only after that
##      do they descend into do_mq_timed{send,receive}, which does
##      the prio gate (send only) and the fdget(mqdes) -> EBADF.
##
##      Pre-batch our mq_timed{send,receive} only checked that the
##      abs_timeout pointer was readable; the value (tv_sec >= 0,
##      tv_nsec < 1e9) was never inspected.  A probe passing a
##      malformed timespec (tv_nsec = 2_000_000_000 or tv_sec = -1)
##      with a junk fd saw -EBADF where Linux returns -EINVAL.
##
##      Factored a small helper validate_user_timespec64(ptr) ->
##      Result<(), SyscallResult> that:
##        1. validate_user_read(ptr, 16) -> EFAULT on fail
##        2. copy_from_user the 16 bytes
##        3. parse as two i64 LE values
##        4. apply timespec64_valid; EINVAL on fail
##      This is the same gate timerfd_settime (batch 156) and
##      nanosleep already use; this batch lifts it into a helper
##      and calls it from both mq_timed{send,receive} ahead of any
##      fd or buffer touch.
##
##      Send-path order now matches Linux exactly:
##        abs_timeout EINVAL -> prio EINVAL -> msg EFAULT ->
##        msg-buffer EFAULT -> fd EBADF.
##
##      Receive-path order:
##        abs_timeout EINVAL -> msg EFAULT -> msg-buffer EFAULT ->
##        prio-ptr EFAULT -> fd EBADF.
##      (Linux has no priority validation on receive — the kernel
##      writes the priority back to userspace; nothing to range-
##      check on input.)
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux self-test:
##        * mq_timedsend(good_prio, valid_msg, ts.tv_nsec=2e9) -> EINVAL
##          (was EBADF pre-batch; this is the discriminator).
##        * mq_timedsend(good_prio, valid_msg, ts.tv_sec=-1) -> EINVAL.
##        * mq_timedsend(prio=70000, ts=valid) -> EINVAL (prio gate
##          still fires when timeout passes).
##        * mq_timedreceive(prio_ptr=0, valid_msg, ts.tv_nsec=2e9)
##          -> EINVAL (proves the helper is wired on receive too).
##      Marker emitted:
##        "mq_timed{send,receive} timespec64_valid: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * We still treat abs_timeout purely as a validation step;
##          when actual mqueue I/O is implemented we need to honour
##          the deadline.
##        * Linux's prepare_timeout normalises in monotonic time
##          (it's an *absolute* timeout); we just validate the
##          shape, which is enough for an unimplemented stub but
##          not for a real wait.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 228. Linux ABI: msgsnd EFAULT-first / mtype<1 gate,
##      msgrcv no-MSGMAX / deferred buf (batch 199).
##
##      sys_msgsnd:
##        Linux's ipc/msg.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4(msgsnd) is:
##          if (get_user(mtype, &msgp->mtype))
##              return -EFAULT;
##          return do_msgsnd(msqid, mtype, &msgp->mtext, msgsz, msgflg);
##        and do_msgsnd starts with:
##          if (msgsz > ns->msg_ctlmax || (long) msgsz < 0 || msqid < 0)
##              return -EINVAL;
##          if (mtype < 1)
##              return -EINVAL;
##        Pre-batch we ran size/qid checks BEFORE the msgp readability
##        check, so a probe passing (msqid=-1, msgp=NULL, msgsz=8) saw
##        -EINVAL where Linux returns -EFAULT.  We also never checked
##        mtype < 1.
##
##        Post-batch order matches Linux exactly:
##          1. msgp == 0 / unreadable -> EFAULT.
##          2. msgsz > MSGMAX -> EINVAL.
##          3. (long) msgsz < 0 -> EINVAL.
##          4. msqid < 0 -> EINVAL.
##          5. copy_from_user the 8-byte mtype.
##          6. mtype < 1 -> EINVAL.
##          7. validate payload (msgp + 8 .. msgp + 8 + msgsz).
##
##      sys_msgrcv:
##        Linux's ipc/msg.c::do_msgrcv has NO MSGMAX gate — only
##        send is rate-limited.  Linux's pre-lookup checks are just:
##          if (msqid < 0 || (long) bufsz < 0)
##              return -EINVAL;
##        and the destination buffer is only touched after a message
##        is dequeued (store_msg).  Pre-batch we mirrored msgsnd's
##        gates (MSGMAX check, NULL-msgp EFAULT, validate_user_write
##        of msgp before any lookup) — all three diverged from Linux.
##
##        Post-batch:
##          1. (long) bufsz < 0 -> EINVAL.
##          2. msqid < 0 -> EINVAL.
##          3. Terminal EINVAL (no queue exists, matches Linux's
##             "queue not found" path).
##        Removed the MSGMAX gate, the NULL-msgp EFAULT, and the
##        pre-lookup validate_user_write.
##
##      Self-tests updated/added in dispatch_linux self-test:
##        * msgsnd(msqid=0, NULL, size=8)        -> EFAULT (unchanged).
##        * msgsnd(msqid=-1, NULL, size=8)       -> EFAULT (was EINVAL
##          pre-batch; this is the EFAULT-vs-EINVAL discriminator).
##        * msgsnd(_, good_msg, size>MSGMAX, _)  -> EINVAL.
##        * msgsnd(_, good_msg, (size_t)-1, _)   -> EINVAL.
##        * msgsnd(msqid=-1, good_msg, valid, _) -> EINVAL.
##        * msgsnd(_, mtype=0 msg, valid, _)     -> EINVAL (NEW gate).
##        * msgrcv(NULL, size=8)                 -> EINVAL (was EFAULT).
##        * msgrcv(_, _, size>MSGMAX, _, _)      -> EINVAL.
##        * msgrcv(_, _, (size_t)-1, _, _)       -> EINVAL.
##        * msgrcv(msqid=-1, _, valid, _, _)     -> EINVAL.
##      Marker emitted:
##        "msgsnd EFAULT-first / mtype<1 / msgrcv no-MSGMAX: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * We still don't have any actual msg queues, so every
##          msgsnd / msgrcv on a queue that "exists" can't actually
##          deliver — the terminal EINVAL is "no queue with that id",
##          not "send/receive failed".  When real msg queue support
##          lands, the post-gate path must do the actual send/recv.
##        * Linux's mtype is `long` (8 bytes on x86_64); we treat it
##          as i64 little-endian.  Same wire format.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 229. Linux ABI: shmctl/msgctl id<0/cmd<0 gate +
##      no pre-lookup buf validation (batch 200).
##
##      Linux's ipc/shm.c::ksys_shmctl and ipc/msg.c::ksys_msgctl
##      both have the same shape:
##        if ({sh,msq}id < 0 || cmd < 0) return -EINVAL;
##        switch (cmd) { ... }
##      where buf is only touched inside per-cmd handlers, AFTER the
##      ipc_obtain_object_check({sh,msq}id) lookup (which fails with
##      -EINVAL when no segment / queue exists).
##
##      Pre-batch both syscalls did a blanket validate_user_read on
##      buf (1 byte) if non-NULL, returning -EFAULT on unmapped buf.
##      A probe passing ({sh,msq}id=5, cmd=2, buf=0xDEAD) saw -EFAULT
##      where Linux returns -EINVAL (the id lookup fails first,
##      before buf is touched).
##
##      Fix:
##        1. Explicit shmid<0 / msqid<0 / cmd<0 gate -> EINVAL.
##        2. Removed the pre-lookup validate_user_read(buf, 1).  Buf
##           is no longer touched at the gate level.
##        3. Terminal EINVAL still applies (no segments / queues
##           exist in this kernel; every id lookup fails).
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux self-test:
##        * shmctl(shmid=5, cmd=2, buf=0xDEAD)   -> EINVAL (was EFAULT
##          pre-batch — this is the EFAULT-vs-EINVAL discriminator).
##        * shmctl(shmid=-1, cmd=2, buf=NULL)    -> EINVAL.
##        * shmctl(shmid=0, cmd=-1, buf=NULL)    -> EINVAL.
##        * msgctl(msqid=5, cmd=2, buf=0xDEAD)   -> EINVAL (was EFAULT).
##        * msgctl(msqid=-1, cmd=2, buf=NULL)    -> EINVAL.
##        * msgctl(msqid=0, cmd=-1, buf=NULL)    -> EINVAL.
##      Markers emitted:
##        "shmctl id/cmd<0, no pre-buf validate: OK"
##        "msgctl id/cmd<0, no pre-buf validate: OK"
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * We still don't implement IPC_INFO / SHM_INFO / MSG_INFO,
##          which on Linux succeed with the kernel namespace info
##          written to buf.  A probe issuing shmctl(_, IPC_INFO, buf)
##          sees -EINVAL here vs. success on Linux.  When real
##          SysV IPC support lands, these handlers must write the
##          namespace info and return 0.
##        * The IPC_STAT / IPC_SET / IPC_RMID handlers also need a
##          real ipc_obtain_object path before they can produce
##          anything but -EINVAL.  Same condition.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 230. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_set_mempolicy_home_node 4 KiB alignment.
##      The start-address alignment gate in sys_set_mempolicy_home_node
##      used our internal 16 KiB FRAME_SIZE (args.arg0 % 0x4000) where
##      Linux's mm/mempolicy.c uses PAGE_SIZE (4096 on x86_64).  Feature
##      probes from libnuma / glibc numa_set_localalloc that hand us a
##      4 KiB-aligned address (the value they got from sysconf
##      (_SC_PAGESIZE) or /proc/self/maps) would see EINVAL where Linux
##      accepts.  Same class of bug as the sys_msync FRAME_SIZE vs
##      ABI_PAGE_SIZE fix (batch 196).
##
##      Fix: replace `args.arg0 % 0x4000 != 0` with
##      `args.arg0 & (ABI_PAGE_SIZE - 1) != 0` where
##      `const ABI_PAGE_SIZE: u64 = 4096;`.  Doc comment now cites the
##      msync precedent and explains why ABI_PAGE_SIZE (Linux's
##      user-visible PAGE_SIZE on x86_64) is correct.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux self-test:
##        * set_mempolicy_home_node(start=0x1000, len=0x4000, node=0,
##          flags=0) -> 0.  4 KiB-aligned but NOT 16 KiB-aligned start;
##          pre-batch returned EINVAL, post-batch returns 0.
##        * set_mempolicy_home_node(start=0x1001, ...) -> EINVAL.
##          Off-by-one from the 4 KiB grid; proves the gate didn't get
##          weaker — only narrower in the right direction.
##      Existing tests for node!=0, flags!=0, len=0, 0x4001-misaligned,
##      0x4000-aligned valid, idempotent, and second-range cases still
##      pass unchanged.
##      Marker emitted: "set_mempolicy_home_node 4K-aligned: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * Single NUMA node only.  home_node > 0 still EINVAL; that
##          is correct for our topology but must be revisited when
##          real NUMA arrives.
##        * No backing mempolicy state — Linux walks the VMA tree and
##          applies the home-node hint per-vma.  We accept and return
##          0 because, with one node, there is no observable
##          difference.  When NUMA lands, this needs a real VMA scan.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 231. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_mq_notify sigev_notify / valid_signal.
##      sys_mq_notify only validated that the sevp pointer was readable
##      then fell through to the fd lookup.  Linux's
##      ipc/mqueue.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(mq_notify) -> do_mq_notify gates
##      ahead of the fd lookup:
##        (1) copy_from_user the whole sigevent struct (64 bytes) -> EFAULT
##        (2) sigev_notify must be SIGEV_NONE (1), SIGEV_SIGNAL (0), or
##            SIGEV_THREAD (2) -> EINVAL otherwise.  Notably this REJECTS
##            SIGEV_THREAD_ID (4).
##        (3) For SIGEV_SIGNAL: sigev_signo must be in 1..=NSIG (64 on
##            x86_64) -> EINVAL otherwise.
##        (4) fdget(mqdes) -> EBADF.
##      Pre-batch a probe passing (sigev_notify=99, fd=-1) saw -EBADF
##      where Linux returns -EINVAL.  This is the classic feature-probe
##      discriminator for "kernel implements mq_notify".
##
##      Fix: in sys_mq_notify, after the validate_user_read(64), do a
##      copy_from_user of the leading 16 bytes (covers sival_ptr +
##      sigev_signo + sigev_notify) and apply the two gates above
##      ahead of the fd lookup.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux self-test:
##        * sigev_notify=99 + fd=-1    -> EINVAL (was EBADF).
##        * sigev_notify=4 (SIGEV_THREAD_ID) -> EINVAL.
##        * SIGEV_SIGNAL + sigev_signo=99  -> EINVAL.
##        * SIGEV_SIGNAL + sigev_signo=0   -> EINVAL.
##        * SIGEV_NONE   + sigev_signo=99  -> EBADF (signo gate scoped
##          to SIGEV_SIGNAL only).
##        * SIGEV_THREAD + sigev_signo=99  -> EBADF (same scope check).
##      Marker emitted: "mq_notify sigev_notify / valid_signal: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * NSIG_MAX = 64 hardcoded.  When real signal infrastructure
##          lands and NSIG becomes parameterised, replace the literal.
##        * SIGEV_THREAD's netlink-socket cookie path isn't validated
##          beyond the sigev_notify gate.  Linux's do_mq_notify reads
##          sigev_value.sival_ptr as an fd and validates it as a
##          netlink socket.  Add when sockets exist.
##        * We don't enforce that the leading 8 bytes (sigev_value)
##          and the 48-byte _sigev_un are all accessible — Linux's
##          copy_from_user covers the whole 64 bytes, but the
##          validate_user_read(64) we do is enough for our needs
##          (any byte unreadable -> EFAULT, just like Linux).
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 232. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_mq_getsetattr mq_flags gate.
##      sys_mq_getsetattr only validated pointer accessibility then fell
##      through to the fd lookup.  Linux's
##      ipc/mqueue.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(mq_getsetattr) -> do_mq_getsetattr
##      gates ahead of the fd lookup:
##        (1) copy_from_user the newattr (struct mq_attr, 64 bytes) -> EFAULT
##        (2) `new->mq_flags & ~O_NONBLOCK` must be zero -> EINVAL
##        (3) fdget(mqdes) -> EBADF
##      Pre-batch a probe passing (newattr.mq_flags=0x1234, fd=-1) saw
##      -EBADF where Linux returns -EINVAL.
##
##      Fix: in sys_mq_getsetattr, after the validate_user_read(64),
##      copy_from_user the leading 8 bytes (mq_flags), check
##      `mq_flags & !O_NONBLOCK == 0` (O_NONBLOCK = 0o4000 on x86_64),
##      otherwise EINVAL — all ahead of the fd lookup.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux self-test:
##        * mq_flags = O_NONBLOCK (0o4000)        -> EBADF (still falls
##          through; proves the gate accepts the allowed bit).
##        * mq_flags = 0x1234 (junk)              -> EINVAL.
##        * mq_flags = O_NONBLOCK | 1 (extra bit) -> EINVAL.
##        * mq_flags = -1 (all bits set)          -> EINVAL.
##        * newattr = NULL, fd = -1               -> EBADF (gate ONLY
##          fires when newattr is non-NULL).
##      Marker emitted: "mq_getsetattr mq_flags & ~O_NONBLOCK: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * O_NONBLOCK = 0o4000 is hardcoded.  When fcntl.h flag
##          constants are centralised, link this against them.
##        * No real mq inode backing yet, so the fdget branch always
##          falls through to EBADF.  When mqueue lands, do_mq_getsetattr
##          must read old attrs and copy_to_user when oldattr is set.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 233. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sem(timed)op gate-order + timespec64_valid.
##      sys_semop and sys_semtimedop validated nsops/semid/SEMOPM
##      BEFORE the sops EFAULT check, and sys_semtimedop never
##      range-checked the timespec content.  Linux's
##      ipc/sem.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(semop) / SYSCALL_DEFINE4(semtimedop)
##      order:
##        (1) If timeout: get_timespec64(timeout) -> EFAULT (pointer
##            readability only; no content check).
##        (2) do_semtimedop: copy_from_user(sops, tsops, nsops*6)
##            -> EFAULT (no-op when nsops==0).
##        (3) __do_semtimedop:
##              a. nsops<1 || semid<0  -> EINVAL.
##              b. nsops > sc_semopm   -> E2BIG.
##              c. If timeout: tv_sec<0 || tv_nsec<0 || tv_nsec>=1e9
##                 -> EINVAL.
##      Pre-batch a probe with (sops=NULL, nsops=1, semid=-1) saw
##      -EINVAL where Linux returns -EFAULT, and a probe with
##      (timeout={tv_nsec=2e9}, ...) hit our terminal -EINVAL via
##      the wrong code path.
##
##      Fix: reorder both functions to match Linux's gate sequence,
##      and reuse validate_user_timespec64 (batch 198) for the
##      content validity check.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux self-test:
##        * (sops=NULL, nsops=1, semid=-1)   -> EFAULT (was EINVAL).
##        * (sops=NULL, nsops=501)           -> EFAULT (was E2BIG).
##        * (sops=valid, ts={tv_sec=1,
##          tv_nsec=500e6})                  -> EINVAL terminal
##          (sanity: valid timespec passes the content gate).
##        * (sops=valid, ts={tv_nsec=2e9})   -> EINVAL via content gate.
##        * (sops=valid, ts={tv_sec=-1})     -> EINVAL via content gate.
##      Existing tests updated to use a 4 KiB stack sops buffer so the
##      semid<0 EINVAL and nsops>SEMOPM E2BIG gates still fire
##      cleanly.
##      Marker emitted: "sem(timed)op gate-order / timespec64_valid: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * The (timeout=BADPTR, nsops=0) -> EFAULT discriminator
##          cannot be exercised from in-kernel self-tests because
##          validate_user_read is a no-op in kernel context.  The
##          gate is wired correctly; only the test coverage is
##          deferred until userspace tests exist.
##        * SEMOPM_FAST (=64 in Linux) isn't modelled here — we
##          treat all nsops paths uniformly.  When the real semop
##          backend lands and nsops>64 triggers a kvmalloc, the
##          ENOMEM gate must be inserted between (2) and (3a) per
##          Linux's do_semtimedop.
##        * sc_semopm is hardcoded at 500 (Linux default).  Should
##          come from the IPC namespace when namespaces land.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 234. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: clock_settime timespec64_valid gate.
##      sys_clock_settime validated clockid, !clock_set (EOPNOTSUPP),
##      and pointer EFAULT — but fell through to EPERM with no
##      content check on the timespec.  Linux's
##      SYSCALL_DEFINE2(clock_settime) ->
##      kc->clock_set(REALTIME) -> posix_clock_realtime_set ->
##      do_sys_settimeofday64 runs timespec64_valid_settod ahead of
##      the security_settime64 capability check.  Pre-batch a probe
##      passing (clockid=REALTIME, tp={tv_nsec=2e9}) saw -EPERM where
##      Linux returns -EINVAL.
##
##      Fix: after the validate_user_read pointer check, run
##      validate_user_timespec64 (batch 198 helper) before falling
##      through to EPERM.  Linux's settod variant additionally
##      rejects tv_sec >= TIME_SETTOD_RESTRICT_MAX (year-2486 cap);
##      we model only the base validity since no realistic probe
##      exercises the upper bound.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux self-test:
##        * (clockid=REALTIME, ts={tv_nsec=2e9}) -> EINVAL (was EPERM).
##        * (clockid=REALTIME, ts={tv_sec=-1})   -> EINVAL.
##        * (clockid=REALTIME, ts={tv_nsec=-1})  -> EINVAL.
##        * (clockid=MONOTONIC, ts={tv_nsec=2e9})-> EOPNOTSUPP
##          (proves the EOPNOTSUPP gate still beats timespec validity,
##          matching Linux's order).
##      Existing tests rewritten to use a stack #[repr(C)] CtsTs
##      struct instead of bare 0x1000 pointers — needed because the
##      new content gate actually reads bytes from the supplied
##      address (in kernel context copy_from_user tolerates kernel
