##      pointers, but unmapped userspace-looking addresses still
##      page-fault).
##      Marker renamed:
##        "clock_settime clockid/EOPNOTSUPP/timespec64_valid: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * TIME_SETTOD_RESTRICT_MAX upper bound (year-2486 cap) not
##          modelled.  Add when realistic.
##        * sys_clock_adjtime / sys_adjtimex don't have analogous
##          content gates yet — they validate clockid + tx pointer
##          then return TIME_ERROR.  modes-bit validation might be
##          worth a future batch.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 235. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_timer_create good_sigevent gate.
##      sys_timer_create validated clockid + EOPNOTSUPP + timerid
##      output ptr but never content-validated the sigevent.  Linux's
##      do_timer_create (kernel/time/posix-timers.c) calls
##      good_sigevent(event) after the timer is allocated:
##        * sigev_notify must be SIGEV_SIGNAL (0), SIGEV_NONE (1),
##          SIGEV_THREAD (2), or SIGEV_SIGNAL|SIGEV_THREAD_ID (4) —
##          otherwise the helper returns NULL -> EINVAL.
##        * For sigev_notify != SIGEV_NONE: sigev_signo must be in
##          1..=SIGRTMAX (64 on x86_64) — otherwise NULL -> EINVAL.
##      The EINVAL fires AFTER the EOPNOTSUPP gate but BEFORE the
##      copy_to_user(timer_id) EFAULT.
##      Pre-batch a probe passing (sigev_notify=99, timerid=valid) saw
##      our terminal -ENOSYS where Linux returns -EINVAL; with
##      (sigev_notify=99, timerid=NULL) we saw -EFAULT where Linux
##      returns -EINVAL.
##
##      Fix: in sys_timer_create, after the clockid/EOPNOTSUPP gates
##      and before the timerid-ptr check, copy the leading 16 bytes
##      of the sigevent (sival_ptr + sigev_signo + sigev_notify) and
##      apply the good_sigevent gates.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux self-test:
##        * sigev_notify=99                  -> EINVAL.
##        * SIGEV_SIGNAL + signo=99          -> EINVAL.
##        * SIGEV_SIGNAL + signo=0           -> EINVAL.
##        * SIGEV_NONE   + signo=99          -> ENOSYS (signo skipped
##          for SIGEV_NONE).
##        * SIGEV_THREAD + signo=99          -> EINVAL.
##        * SIGEV_THREAD_ID(4) + signo=10    -> ENOSYS (value 4 is
##          accepted by good_sigevent's case fallthrough).
##        * (sevp={bad}, timerid=NULL)       -> EINVAL (gate-order
##          discriminator: EINVAL beats EFAULT, matching Linux).
##      Marker renamed:
##        "timer_create sigevent/clockid/EOPNOTSUPP/good_sigevent: OK".
##
##      Note this differs from mq_notify (batch 202) where SIGEV_THREAD_ID
##      (4) was REJECTED: do_mq_notify only accepts SIGEV_NONE/SIGNAL/
##      THREAD, while do_timer_create's good_sigevent additionally
##      accepts SIGEV_SIGNAL|SIGEV_THREAD_ID.
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * SIGRTMAX = 64 hardcoded; same as mq_notify (batch 202).
##        * The SIGEV_THREAD_ID tgid validation
##          (find_get_task_by_vpid + same_thread_group) isn't modelled.
##          Add when proper threads/PIDs land.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 236. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_io_setup get_user + ctx-zero gate.
##      sys_io_setup ran `nr_events == 0 -> EINVAL` BEFORE the ctxp
##      readability check and never read *ctxp.  Linux's
##      fs/aio.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(io_setup) order:
##        (1) get_user(ctx, ctxp) -> EFAULT on unreadable ctxp.
##        (2) if (ctx || nr_events == 0) -> EINVAL.
##            (*ctxp must start out zero — non-zero is rejected as
##            "context handle already in use".)
##        (3) Allocate ring (EAGAIN/ENOMEM possible).
##        (4) copy_to_user(ctxp, &new_ctx) -> EFAULT.
##      Pre-batch:
##        * (nr_events=0, ctxp=NULL)         -> EINVAL [us] / EFAULT [Linux]
##        * (nr_events=8, *ctxp=0xdeadbeef)  -> ENOSYS [us] / EINVAL [Linux]
##
##      Fix: reorder so ctxp NULL/EFAULT runs first, then
##      copy_from_user the 8-byte value, then apply the
##      ctx_in!=0 || nr_events==0 EINVAL.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux self-test:
##        * (nr_events=0, ctxp=NULL)         -> EFAULT (was EINVAL).
##        * (nr_events=8, *ctxp=0xdeadbeef)  -> EINVAL (was ENOSYS).
##        * (nr_events=0, *ctxp=non-zero)    -> EINVAL (both reasons).
##      Existing tests rewritten to use a stack [u8; 8] for ctxp
##      instead of 0x1000 (which is unmapped and would fault when
##      copy_from_user runs).
##      Marker emitted: "io_setup get_user / ctx-zero gate: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##        * The per-user nr_events cap (aio_max_nr) isn't modelled.
##          Linux returns EAGAIN if the per-user reservation overflows;
##          we just fall through to ENOSYS.  Add when AIO lands.
##        * io_pgetevents has analogous timespec gating that we don't
##          yet validate — candidate for a follow-up batch.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 237. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_io_submit drop upfront iocbpp
##      access_ok; match Linux >= 5.5 SYSCALL_DEFINE3 order.
##
##      Pre-batch sys_io_submit (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs) ran
##      `nr < 0 -> EINVAL`, then `if nr > 0: iocbpp NULL/access_ok ->
##      EFAULT`, then terminal -EINVAL.  That mirrored Linux's
##      pre-5.5 do_io_submit, which had an upfront
##      `access_ok(iocbpp, nr * sizeof(*iocbpp))`.  Linux 5.5+ moved
##      the iocbpp touch into the per-iocb loop and gated lookup_ioctx
##      first:
##
##          SYSCALL_DEFINE3(io_submit, ctx_id, nr, iocbpp) {
##              if (nr < 0) return -EINVAL;
##              ctx = lookup_ioctx(ctx_id);
##              if (!ctx) return -EINVAL;
##              // per-iocb get_user only here.
##          }
##
##      Because no aio context ever exists on our kernel, lookup_ioctx
##      fails for every call, so the per-iocb get_user is unreachable.
##      Modern libaio probes see -EINVAL for io_submit(0, 1, NULL); we
##      were returning -EFAULT.  CLAUDE.md targets Linux 6.10 shape.
##
##      Fix: remove the iocbpp NULL + access_ok block; reduce to
##      `nr < 0 -> EINVAL` then terminal -EINVAL.
##
##      Self-tests updated:
##      - (ctx=0, nr=1, iocbpp=NULL) now expects EINVAL (was EFAULT).
##      - (ctx=0xdeadbeef, nr=1, iocbpp=0x1) new discriminator test
##        confirming the ctx lookup fires ahead of any iocbpp touch.
##      - (nr=0) still EINVAL (no ctx).
##      Marker: "io_submit ctx-lookup-first / iocbpp-not-pre-validated: OK".
##
##      Known limitation: when we eventually implement a real aio
##      context, the per-iocb get_user EFAULT path will need its own
##      tests.  Currently unreachable.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 238. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_get_robust_list neg pid -> ESRCH;
##      cross-task EPERM ahead of put_user EFAULT.
##
##      Pre-batch sys_get_robust_list (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##      ran:
##        1. pid < 0 -> EINVAL
##        2. head_ptr == NULL || len_ptr == NULL -> EFAULT
##        3. validate_user_write -> EFAULT
##        4. pid != current -> EPERM
##        5. copy_to_user
##
##      Linux's kernel/futex/core.c SYSCALL_DEFINE3(get_robust_list)
##      runs find_task_by_vpid first (rejecting pid<0 with ESRCH, not
##      EINVAL), then ptrace_may_access (EPERM), then the put_user
##      writes at the very end (EFAULT).  So:
##        * (pid=-1, *)                            -> ESRCH on Linux,
##                                                    EINVAL pre-batch.
##        * (pid=other_tid, head=NULL, len=NULL)   -> EPERM on Linux
##                                                    (ptrace fires
##                                                    ahead of put_user),
##                                                    EFAULT pre-batch.
##
##      Fix: reorder to task-lookup -> ptrace gate -> NULL/EFAULT ->
##      copy_to_user.  Negative pid maps to ESRCH; positive non-self
##      maps to EPERM; pid==0 or pid==current_tid_i32 proceeds to the
##      put_user writes.
##
##      Self-tests updated/added:
##      - (pid=-1, valid head+len) -> ESRCH (was EINVAL).
##      - (pid=0, head=NULL, len=valid) -> EFAULT (unchanged).
##      - (pid=i32::MAX, head=NULL, len=NULL) -> EPERM (was EFAULT).
##      - Existing self-success test (pid=0, head_ptr, len_ptr) -> 0
##        still passes.
##
##      Known limitation: for positive bogus pids we collapse "no such
##      task -> ESRCH" with "task exists, no ptrace -> EPERM" into
##      EPERM uniformly.  Adding ptrace plumbing to distinguish would
##      require a task-existence query the futex core doesn't yet
##      have.  Documented for follow-up.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 239. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_io_getevents add get_timespec64
##      copy_from_user ahead of terminal EINVAL.
##
##      Pre-batch sys_io_getevents (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs) ran
##      min_nr/nr range check then fell through to terminal EINVAL
##      without ever reading the timeout pointer.  Linux's
##      fs/aio.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE5(io_getevents) order:
##
##          if (timeout && get_timespec64(&ts, timeout))
##              return -EFAULT;
##          ret = do_io_getevents(ctx_id, ...)   // -EINVAL no ctx
##
##      get_timespec64 does the full 16-byte copy_from_user; bad
##      pointer -> -EFAULT.  Pre-batch (min_nr=10, nr=5, timeout=BAD)
##      saw -EINVAL where Linux returns -EFAULT.
##
##      Fix: 16-byte validate_user_read + copy_from_user on the
##      timeout pointer ahead of the min/nr check and terminal
##      EINVAL.  No timespec64_valid is run — Linux's get_timespec64
##      doesn't validate the range, the conversion to ktime happens
##      later in do_io_getevents -> timespec64_to_ktime which is
##      tolerant of any value.
##
##      Self-tests added:
##      - (timeout=valid_16B_ts, ctx=0, nr=1) -> EINVAL (terminal).
##        Exercises the new copy_from_user path.  Pre-batch this
##        returned EINVAL via the min/nr check (which doesn't fire
##        because min=0, nr=1, valid range) — actually pre-batch
##        terminal also EINVAL.  Errno is the same; the new path is
##        observable by the marker line being printed after the copy
##        succeeds rather than the min/nr check rejecting.
##      Marker: "io_getevents timeout-copy-before-ctx-lookup: OK".
##
##      Known limitation: same as batch 208 — when a real AIO context
##      lands, the timespec EFAULT path with a BADPTR can finally be
##      tested end-to-end.  Currently the kernel-context tests can't
##      simulate a page-faulting userspace pointer.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 240. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_statmount flags-first +
##      copy_mnt_id_req size gates (E2BIG / EINVAL).
##
##      Pre-batch sys_statmount (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs) ran
##      EFAULT/EINVAL pointer + bufsize gates ahead of the flags!=0
##      check.  Linux's fs/namespace.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4(statmount)
##      runs `if (flags) return -EINVAL` FIRST, ahead of any pointer
##      touch.  Additionally, copy_mnt_id_req validates the leading
##      `size` u32 field:
##         size > PAGE_SIZE -> E2BIG
##         size < MNT_ID_REQ_SIZE_VER0 (24) -> EINVAL
##         copy_struct_from_user
##         spare != 0 -> EINVAL
##      Pre-batch we ignored the size field entirely and the
##      `valid_mnt_req` test buffer was all-zero — happened to pass
##      because no size gate was being applied.  Probes walking the
##      size field saw consistent ENOENT where Linux returns E2BIG
##      for size>PAGE_SIZE and EINVAL for size<24.
##
##      Fix: reorder to flags -> req NULL -> size field read ->
##      E2BIG/EINVAL gates -> full-struct validate_user_read ->
##      buf/bufsize -> terminal ENOENT.  PAGE_SIZE pegged at Linux
##      x86_64 4096, not our internal 16 KiB FRAME_SIZE.
##
##      Self-tests added/updated:
##      - (req=NULL, flags=1) -> EINVAL  (was EFAULT)  flags-first.
##      - (req=NULL, flags=0) -> EFAULT  (unchanged).
##      - (valid_req size=16) -> EINVAL  (new; size<24 gate).
##      - (valid_req size=8192) -> E2BIG  (new; size>PAGE gate).
##      - (valid_req size=24, bufsize=0) -> EINVAL (unchanged path).
##      - (valid_req size=24, bufsize=128) -> ENOENT (unchanged).
##      Marker: "statmount flags-first / size E2BIG / size<VER0 EINVAL: OK".
##
##      Follow-up candidate: sys_listmount has the same Linux
##      structure (copy_mnt_id_req + flags-first + LISTMOUNT_REVERSE
##      mask) but pre-batch ordering — kept for the next batch to
##      keep this one focused.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s on first try).

## 241. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_listmount flags-first +
##      copy_mnt_id_req size gates + LISTMOUNT_REVERSE mask.
##
##      Pre-batch sys_listmount (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs) ran
##      EFAULT pointer + validate_user_read gates ahead of the flags
##      check, and never inspected the req->size field — so probes
##      walking the gate ladder saw:
##         (req=NULL,  flags=1)        -> EFAULT vs Linux EINVAL
##         (req->size=8192)            -> ENOENT vs Linux E2BIG
##         (req->size=16)              -> ENOENT vs Linux EINVAL
##
##      Linux's fs/namespace.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4(listmount) gate
##      order:
##        1. flags & ~LISTMOUNT_REVERSE   -> -EINVAL
##        2. copy_mnt_id_req(req, &kreq):
##             get_user(usize, &req->size) -> -EFAULT
##             usize > PAGE_SIZE           -> -E2BIG
##             usize < MNT_ID_REQ_SIZE_VER0 (24) -> -EINVAL
##             copy_struct_from_user       -> -EFAULT
##             kreq.spare != 0             -> -EINVAL
##        3. do_listmount lookup           -> -ENOENT for unknown mnt
##
##      Mirrors the statmount batch (240) but allows the
##      LISTMOUNT_REVERSE=1 flag.  PAGE_SIZE pegged at Linux x86_64
##      4096, not our internal 16 KiB FRAME_SIZE.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux:
##      - (req=NULL, flags=2) -> EINVAL  (flags-first; bit 1 invalid).
##      - (valid_req, flags=LISTMOUNT_REVERSE=1) -> ENOENT (REVERSE
##        accepted; falls through to terminal lookup).
##      - (small_req size=16) -> EINVAL  (size<24 gate).
##      - (huge_req size=8192) -> E2BIG  (size>PAGE gate).
##      - (valid_req size=24) -> ENOENT  (terminal mnt lookup miss).
##      Marker: "listmount flags-first / size gates / REVERSE accepted: OK".
##
##      Also removed the now-unused all-zero mnt_req_buf/mnt_req_ptr
##      test-locals (superseded by the typed valid/small/huge variants
##      introduced in batch 240).  Keeps the build warning-free per
##      CLAUDE.md.
##
##      Known limitation: do_listmount still returns ENOENT for every
##      mnt_id because no mount tree is exposed via this syscall yet
##      — the kreq.spare!=0 gate isn't observable until a real mount
##      lands and the spare-zero path can actually succeed.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 242. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_mbind MPOL_MF_MOVE_ALL EPERM
##      gate ahead of addr alignment.
##
##      Pre-batch sys_mbind (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs) had no
##      capability check for MPOL_MF_MOVE_ALL — the flag bits were
##      accepted as long as they were a subset of MOVE|MOVE_ALL|
##      STRICT (=0x7), then addr alignment was the next gate.  Linux
##      `mm/mempolicy.c::do_mbind` runs:
##         if (flags & ~(MPOL_MF_STRICT|MOVE|MOVE_ALL)) -> -EINVAL
##         if ((flags & MPOL_MF_MOVE_ALL) && !capable(CAP_SYS_NICE))
##                                                      -> -EPERM
##         if (start & ~PAGE_MASK)                      -> -EINVAL
##      MOVE_ALL EPERM fires AFTER the flag-bits mask but AHEAD of
##      addr alignment, so a probe passing (addr=unaligned,
##      flags=MOVE_ALL) saw EINVAL where Linux returns EPERM.
##
##      Fix: insert `if flags & MPOL_MF_MOVE_ALL != 0 -> EPERM` between
##      the flag-bits mask check and the addr-alignment check.  We
##      have no per-task credentials struct in the dispatch-time test
##      context, so MOVE_ALL is always EPERM (matches the
##      unprivileged-task case on Linux).  When real task creds land
##      with a capability set, replace this with a CAP_SYS_NICE check
##      and the gate becomes conditional.
##
##      Self-tests added (continuing the existing (9g) mbind block):
##      - Case 10: (addr=0x123 unaligned, flags=MOVE_ALL=2)
##                  -> EPERM  (was EINVAL via addr).
##      - Case 11: (addr=0x1000 aligned, flags=MOVE_ALL=2)
##                  -> EPERM  (was 0).
##      - Case 12: (addr=0x1000 aligned, flags=MOVE=1)
##                  -> 0      (MOVE without MOVE_ALL is unprivileged).
##      Marker: "mbind MOVE_ALL EPERM ahead of addr-align: OK".
##
##      Known limitation: when a task with a credentials struct
##      lands and we wire a real CAP_SYS_NICE check, the gate
##      becomes conditional — a privileged task with MOVE_ALL must
##      then succeed.  Tests 11 and 12 will need a process-context
##      variant covering that path.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s on first try).

## 243. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_move_pages MPOL_MF_MOVE_ALL
##      EPERM gate ahead of pid/count gates.
##
##      Pre-batch sys_move_pages (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs) had no
##      capability check for MPOL_MF_MOVE_ALL — the flag bits were
##      accepted as long as they were a subset of MOVE|MOVE_ALL
##      (=0x6), then count limit / count==0 / pages NULL gates ran.
##      Linux `mm/migrate.c::kernel_move_pages`:
##         if (flags & ~(MPOL_MF_MOVE|MPOL_MF_MOVE_ALL)) -> -EINVAL
##         if ((flags & MPOL_MF_MOVE_ALL) && !capable(CAP_SYS_NICE))
##                                                      -> -EPERM
##         rcu_read_lock();
##         task = pid ? find_task_by_vpid(pid) : current;
##         ...
##      MOVE_ALL EPERM fires AFTER the flag-bits mask but AHEAD of
##      the pid lookup AND ahead of the count==0 no-op shortcut, so
##      probes saw:
##         (count=0, flags=MOVE_ALL) -> 0 vs Linux -EPERM
##         (count=huge, flags=MOVE_ALL) -> -E2BIG vs Linux -EPERM
##
##      Fix: insert `if flags & MPOL_MF_MOVE_ALL -> EPERM` between
##      the flag-bits mask check and the count-limit check.  Same
##      shape as batch 242 (mbind).  When real task creds land with
##      a CAP_SYS_NICE bit, swap the unconditional EPERM for the
##      capability test.
##
##      Self-tests added (continuing the (9i) move_pages block):
##      - Case 7: (count=0, flags=MOVE_ALL=4) -> EPERM (was 0).
##      - Case 8: (count=2M, flags=MOVE_ALL=4) -> EPERM (was E2BIG).
##      - Case 9: (count=0, flags=MOVE=2) -> 0 (MOVE unprivileged).
##      Marker: "move_pages MOVE_ALL EPERM ahead of pid/count: OK".
##
##      Known limitation: when CAP_SYS_NICE is wired, tests 7 and 8
##      will need a privileged-task variant covering the "MOVE_ALL
##      with cap" path.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 244. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_migrate_pages pid lookup after
##      mask copy + neg-pid ESRCH (not EINVAL).
##
##      Pre-batch sys_migrate_pages (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##      rejected pid<0 with EINVAL at the very top of the function,
##      before maxnode validation, mask copy, or any other gate.
##      Linux `mm/mempolicy.c::kernel_migrate_pages` walks the gates
##      in this order:
##         get_nodes(&old, old_nodes, maxnode)   -> -EFAULT/-EINVAL
##         get_nodes(&new, new_nodes, maxnode)   -> -EFAULT/-EINVAL
##         task = pid ? find_task_by_vpid(pid) : current
##         if (!task) return -ESRCH
##         if (!ptrace_may_access(task, ...))    -> -EPERM
##         ...
##      find_task_by_vpid on a negative pid returns NULL too (the
##      idr lookup is keyed on pid_t and out-of-range entries fail),
##      so pid<0 yields ESRCH, not EINVAL.  Pre-batch divergences:
##         (pid<0,   valid masks)  -> EINVAL vs Linux ESRCH
##         (pid=99,  valid masks)  -> 0      vs Linux ESRCH
##         (pid<0,   NULL old)     -> EINVAL vs Linux EFAULT
##                                   (Linux runs get_nodes first)
##
##      Fix: remove the up-front pid<0 EINVAL gate.  After both mask
##      copies succeed, look up the target task:
##         if pid != 0 && pid != current_task_id() as i32 -> ESRCH
##      We have no per-task tracking beyond `current` in the
##      dispatch context, so every non-self pid resolves to NULL ->
##      ESRCH (matches Linux's find_task_by_vpid NULL path).  When
##      real task tracking lands, slot a ptrace_may_access EPERM
##      gate between this ESRCH check and the new_mask intersection.
##
##      Self-tests added (continuing the (9h) migrate_pages block):
##      - Case 7: (pid=u64::MAX, valid masks) -> ESRCH (was EINVAL).
##      - Case 8: (pid=99999,    valid masks) -> ESRCH (was 0).
##      - Case 9: (pid=u64::MAX, NULL old, valid new) -> EFAULT
##                (mask EFAULT beats ESRCH per Linux order).
##      Marker: "migrate_pages pid-lookup-after-mask / neg-pid ESRCH: OK".
##
##      Also updated the older neg-pid test at the bottom of the
##      dispatch self-test (was labeled "neg pid -> EINVAL") to
##      document that maxnode=0 now drives the EINVAL — neg-pid
##      itself now yields ESRCH via the proper lookup path.
##
##      Known limitation: when CAP_SYS_NICE / ptrace EPERM lands,
##      tests 7 and 8 will need privileged-task variants exercising
##      the "task exists but can't be inspected" path.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 245. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_process_madvise advice-last
##      gate order (advice validation after pidfd lookup).
##
##      Pre-batch sys_process_madvise (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##      validated the advice value (`advice > 100 -> EINVAL`) BEFORE
##      the fd lookup.  Linux `mm/madvise.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE5(
##      process_madvise)` walks the gates in this order:
##         1. flags != 0                          -> EINVAL
##         2. import_iovec                        -> EFAULT/EINVAL
##         3. pidfd_get_task                      -> EBADF/ESRCH
##         4. mm_access(PTRACE_MODE_READ_FSCREDS) -> EPERM
##         5. madvise_behavior_valid (per-iov)    -> EINVAL
##      The advice check is the LAST gate — it fires inside
##      do_madvise after the pidfd has been resolved to a task and
##      ptrace has been cleared.  So a probe passing a junk advice
##      with a closed-but-shaped-like-an-fd pidfd saw EINVAL where
##      Linux returns EBADF.
##
##      Fix: move the `advice > 100` check past the lookup_caller_fd
##      + HandleKind::PidFd check, so it fires only when we'd
##      otherwise have returned ENOSYS.
##
##      Self-tests added (continuing process_madvise block):
##      - (closed pidfd, advice=200) -> EBADF (was EINVAL).
##      - (flags=1, advice=200)      -> EINVAL (flags still first).
##      Marker: "process_madvise advice-last gate order: OK".
##
##      Known limitation: ESRCH (pidfd to dead task) and EPERM
##      (ptrace_may_access fail) aren't tested — neither path
##      exists yet in our pidfd subsystem.  When pidfd_open
##      can produce a task ref and ptrace creds land, add (valid
##      pidfd, advice=200) -> EINVAL covering madvise_behavior_valid
##      firing AFTER the EPERM gate.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 246. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_sched_setattr combined
##      !uattr | pid<0 | flags EINVAL gate.
##
##      Linux `kernel/sched/syscalls.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(
##      sched_setattr)` opens with a single combined gate:
##         if (!uattr || pid < 0 || flags) return -EINVAL;
##      All three failure modes share EINVAL — including a NULL
##      uattr (despite the user-pointer parameter, no EFAULT) and
##      a negative pid (NOT ESRCH, despite the later
##      find_task_by_vpid path).  Pre-batch sys_sched_setattr
##      (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
##         * flags != 0 -> EINVAL (correct).
##         * attr_ptr == 0 -> EFAULT (diverges; Linux EINVAL).
##         * pid<0 was never validated up-front; flow proceeded
##           into size/policy read, then the later pcb::state
##           lookup gave ESRCH only for unknown positive pids.
##           Negative pid was silently accepted as `pid=0` cast
##           (caller_pid) effectively, returning 0 on success.
##
##      Fix: combine the three failure modes into one EINVAL gate
##      at the top of the function, matching Linux's literal
##      `if (!uattr || pid<0 || flags)` check.
##
##      Self-tests added/updated:
##      - sched_setattr(NULL uattr) -> EINVAL (was tested as EFAULT).
##      - sched_setattr(pid=u64::MAX, valid uattr) -> EINVAL (new).
##      Marker: "sched_setattr combined !uattr|pid<0|flags EINVAL: OK".
##
##      Known limitation: per-task ptrace EPERM gate inside the
##      Linux deadline/check_for_caller path isn't tested — those
##      gates fire after sched_copy_attr in __sched_setscheduler
##      and only matter when SCHED_DEADLINE / SCHED_EXT land.  Our
##      SCHED_DEADLINE / SCHED_EXT path still returns EOPNOTSUPP
##      (compiled-out class) so the EPERM gate is unreachable.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 247. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_sched_getattr combined
##      !uattr | pid<0 | usize-range | flags EINVAL gate.
##
##      Linux `kernel/sched/syscalls.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4(
##      sched_getattr)` opens with a single combined gate:
##         if (!uattr || pid < 0 || usize > PAGE_SIZE
##             || usize < SCHED_ATTR_SIZE_VER0 || flags)
##             return -EINVAL;
##      All five failure modes share EINVAL — including:
##         * NULL uattr (no EFAULT, despite user-pointer param)
##         * usize > PAGE_SIZE (no E2BIG)
##         * pid<0 (no ESRCH; bypasses find_process_by_pid path)
##      Pre-batch sys_sched_getattr (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
##         * flags != 0 -> EINVAL (correct).
##         * size < 48 -> EINVAL (correct).
##         * size > 2^20 -> E2BIG (diverges; Linux EINVAL).
##         * attr_ptr == 0 -> EFAULT (diverges; Linux EINVAL).
##         * pid<0 not validated up-front; the later pcb::state
##           lookup gave ESRCH for unknown pids and 0 for an
##           accidentally-matched cast.
##      PAGE_SIZE here is pegged at Linux x86_64 4096 (the ABI
##      consumers' expectation), NOT our internal 16 KiB frame
##      size.  Companion to batch 246 (sched_setattr).
##
##      Fix: collapse the five failure modes into one EINVAL gate
##      at the top of the function, matching Linux's literal
##      conjunction.
##
##      Self-tests updated/added:
##      - sched_getattr(usize=2M) -> EINVAL (was E2BIG).
##      - sched_getattr(NULL uattr) -> EINVAL (was EFAULT).
##      - sched_getattr(pid=u64::MAX, valid uattr) -> EINVAL (new).
##      Marker: "sched_getattr combined !uattr|pid<0|size|flags EINVAL: OK".
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on first try).

## 248. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_clone3 size E2BIG ahead of EINVAL
##      and ahead of NULL EFAULT (gate order).
##
##      Linux gate order (kernel/fork.c::copy_clone_args_from_user):
##         1. `usize > PAGE_SIZE`              -> -E2BIG
##         2. `usize < CLONE_ARGS_SIZE_VER0`   -> -EINVAL
##         3. `copy_struct_from_user(...)`     -> -EFAULT on NULL/unmapped
##
##      Pre-batch divergences in `validate_clone3_args`:
##      - NULL `cl_args_ptr` was checked BEFORE any size gate, so a
##        probe with `(NULL, size=8192)` saw `EFAULT` where Linux
##        returns `E2BIG` (size check fires first).
##      - `size > MAX (4096)` was folded into the `< VER0` arm and
##        returned `EINVAL`, where Linux's `usize > PAGE_SIZE` arm
##        returns `E2BIG` as a distinct discriminator.
##      - `size > MAX` also fired AFTER NULL EFAULT, so a NULL probe
##        with a huge size could not observe the `E2BIG` arm at all.
##
##      Fix: split the size range into two separate gates, in Linux
##      order, with the NULL check moved BELOW both size gates so
##      huge-size and sub-VER0-size discriminators fire ahead of
##      NULL EFAULT, matching Linux's literal source order.  The
##      alignment gates (Linux doesn't reject misalignment because
##      `copy_struct_from_user` doesn't care) remain after Linux's
##      gates as our own additional bug-finder.
##
##      Self-tests updated/added (~line 37475):
##      - clone3(buf, size=8192) -> E2BIG (was EINVAL).
##      - clone3(NULL, size=8192) -> E2BIG (new; demonstrates the
##        size gate firing ahead of NULL EFAULT).
##      - clone3(NULL, size=16) -> EINVAL (new; demonstrates the
##        sub-VER0 gate firing ahead of NULL EFAULT).
##      - clone3(NULL, size=4096) -> EFAULT (new; size==PAGE_SIZE
##        is the inclusive max, passes size gate, then NULL faults).
##      - clone3(NULL, size=88) -> EFAULT (kept; unchanged).
##      Marker: "clone3 size-E2BIG > size-EINVAL > NULL-EFAULT gate order: OK".
##
##      Known limitations: alignment and size%8 still EINVAL after
##      Linux's gates; Linux accepts these because copy_struct_from_user
##      uses byte-granularity access.  Acceptable: our gate fires only
##      after Linux's documented errno discriminators have already
##      been ruled out, so callers that pass aligned + multiple-of-8
##      sizes (which all real glibc/musl call sites do) see Linux
##      semantics on every observable path.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 249. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_perf_event_open flags-first +
##      size-E2BIG-both-bounds + size=0 ABI quirk.
##
##      Linux gate order (kernel/events/core.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE5(perf_event_open)
##      + perf_copy_attr):
##         1. `flags & ~PERF_FLAG_ALL`           -> -EINVAL  (first statement)
##         2. `get_user(size, &uattr->size)`     -> -EFAULT
##         3. ABI quirk: `if (!size) size = PERF_ATTR_SIZE_VER0;`
##         4. `size < VER0 || size > PAGE_SIZE`  -> -E2BIG   (both bounds)
##         5. (...deeper validation; we stub to ENOSYS)
##         6. `pid == -1 && cpu == -1`           -> -EINVAL  (in perf_event_alloc)
##
##      Pre-batch divergences in sys_perf_event_open:
##      - Flag-mask EINVAL fired AFTER attr.size validation, so a
##        probe with `(NULL, flags=bad)` saw `EFAULT` and a probe
##        with `(huge size, flags=bad)` saw `E2BIG` where Linux
##        returns `EINVAL` for both (flag check is statement #1
##        in SYSCALL_DEFINE5).
##      - `attr_size < PERF_ATTR_SIZE_VER0` returned `EINVAL`,
##        where Linux's `perf_copy_attr` unifies both `< VER0` and
##        `> PAGE_SIZE` under the err_size jump returning `-E2BIG`.
##      - `attr_size == 0` returned `EINVAL` (same arm as < VER0),
##        but Linux's documented ABI quirk normalises size=0 to
##        VER0 so the call falls through to the deeper handler.
##        Common feature-detection sniff pattern (libbpf, perf
##        tools) submits an all-zero attr to test for support.
##
##      Fix:
##      - Move the `flags & ~PERF_FLAG_MASK -> EINVAL` gate to the
##        top of the function ahead of every attr-reading step.
##      - Add the `raw_size == 0 -> VER0` normalisation before the
##        bounds check.
##      - Collapse the two bound checks into a single
##        `< VER0 || > PAGE_SIZE -> E2BIG` arm.
##
##      Self-tests updated/added (~line 33868):
##      - perf_event_open(NULL, flags=0)        -> EFAULT (kept).
##      - perf_event_open(NULL, flags=0x10)     -> EINVAL (new;
##        flag mask fires ahead of NULL EFAULT).
##      - perf_event_open(size=0, pid=0, cpu=0) -> ENOSYS (was EINVAL;
##        ABI quirk normalises to VER0, deeper gates all pass).
##      - perf_event_open(size=8)               -> E2BIG (new; sub-VER0
##        bound now returns E2BIG, was EINVAL).
##      - perf_event_open(size=8192)            -> E2BIG (kept).
##      - perf_event_open(size=8192, flags=bad) -> EINVAL (new; flag
##        mask fires ahead of E2BIG).
##      - perf_event_open(bad flag, valid attr) -> EINVAL (kept).
##      - perf_event_open(pid=-1, cpu=-1)       -> EINVAL (kept).
##      - perf_event_open(valid attr)           -> ENOSYS (kept).
##      Marker: "perf_event_open flags-first / size-E2BIG-both-bounds /
##              size=0-quirk: OK".
##
##      Known limitations: we still do not have a real perf subsystem,
##      so the terminal answer for any valid call remains ENOSYS.
##      That matches glibc's perf_event_open wrapper's fallback path
##      (libbpf already treats ENOSYS as "kernel has no CONFIG_PERF").
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 250. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_kcmp pid-ESRCH ahead of type-EINVAL.
##
##      Linux gate order (kernel/kcmp.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE5):
##         1. find_task_by_vpid(pid1) / find_task_by_vpid(pid2)
##                                          -> -ESRCH if either fails
##                                             (negative pids and
##                                             unknown positive pids
##                                             both land in this arm).
##         2. ptrace_may_access(task1, task2) -> -EPERM
##         3. switch (type) default arm        -> -EINVAL (LAST)
##
##      Pre-batch divergence in sys_kcmp:
##      - `typ > 7 -> EINVAL` was the FIRST gate, ahead of the
##        `pid < 0 -> ESRCH` arm and ahead of the kernel-vs-user
##        task-liveness gate.  A probe with `(pid=-1, type=99)`
##        saw `EINVAL` where Linux returns `ESRCH` (the
##        find_task_by_vpid step fires first and discriminates
##        the pid argument before the switch sees `type`).
##
##      Fix: hoist the pid<0 ESRCH gate and the (non-kernel-ctx)
##      owner-liveness ESRCH gate ahead of the `typ > 7 -> EINVAL`
##      gate, mirroring Linux's source order.  The EPOLL_TFD pointer
##      validation and the type-specific comparator paths continue
##      to run after the type gate as before.
##
##      Self-tests updated/added (~line 37353):
##      - kcmp(pid=0, pid=0, type=99) -> EINVAL (kept; valid pids
##        reach the switch-default).
##      - kcmp(pid=-1, pid=0, type=0) -> ESRCH (kept; pid lookup).
##      - kcmp(pid=-1, pid=0, type=99) -> ESRCH (NEW; pre-batch
##        returned EINVAL via the now-removed type-first gate).
##      - kcmp(pid=0, pid=-1, type=99) -> ESRCH (NEW; either neg
##        pid is enough to discriminate ahead of EINVAL).
##      Marker: "kcmp pid-ESRCH-ahead-of-type-EINVAL gate order: OK".
##
##      Known limitations: we don't model per-resource sharing
##      (CLONE_VM without CLONE_FILES, unshare(2), etc.) — the PCB
##      remains monolithic.  CRIU is the main caller that would
##      observe this; documented in earlier todo entries.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 251. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_madvise gate order — behavior_valid
##      first, then align/overflow, then len=0 no-op, then HWPOISON EPERM
##      ahead of user-space ENOMEM.
##
##      Linux gate order (mm/madvise.c::do_madvise):
##         1. madvise_behavior_valid(behavior)  -> -EINVAL  (FIRST)
##         2. !PAGE_ALIGNED(start)              -> -EINVAL
##         3. PAGE_ALIGN(len) overflow          -> -EINVAL
##         4. start + len overflow              -> -EINVAL
##         5. end == start (len==0)             -> 0  (no-op)
##         6. HWPOISON/SOFT_OFFLINE inject_error: !capable -> -EPERM
##         7. (per-VMA walk; we map to user-space-range -> ENOMEM)
##         8. known no-op advice                -> 0
##
##      Pre-batch divergences in sys_madvise:
##      - `len == 0 -> 0` was the FIRST gate, so a probe with bogus
##        advice (madvise(addr, 0, 9999)) saw 0 where Linux returns
##        EINVAL.  Probes that use len=0 to feature-detect advice
##        values would falsely conclude every advice is supported,
##        then call with real len and observe a different errno.
##      - Misaligned addr with len=0 returned 0; Linux returns
##        EINVAL because alignment is gate 2, ahead of the len==0
##        short-circuit.
##      - `madvise(kernel_addr, len, MADV_HWPOISON)` returned ENOMEM
##        via the user-space bounds gate, where Linux's
##        madvise_inject_error checks capable() ahead of the VMA
##        walk and returns EPERM.
##
##      Fix: reorder validation to mirror Linux's source statement
##      order: behavior_valid -> alignment -> overflow -> len=0 no-op
##      -> HWPOISON EPERM -> user-space ENOMEM -> known-advice 0.
##
##      Self-tests updated/added (~line 24590):
##      - madvise(0x4001, 0, 9999) -> EINVAL  (was 0; behavior gate).
##      - madvise(0x4000, 0, 0)    -> 0       (new; len=0 no-op when
##                                             behavior valid + aligned).
##      - madvise(0x4001, 0, 0)    -> EINVAL  (new; alignment fires
##                                             ahead of len=0 no-op).
##      - madvise(kernel, 0x4000, HWPOISON) -> EPERM  (was ENOMEM;
##                                             EPERM ahead of bounds).
##      - kept: known-advice = 0, HWPOISON over user range = EPERM,
##        unknown advice = EINVAL, misaligned with len>0 = EINVAL,
##        kernel addr with valid no-op advice = ENOMEM.
##      Marker: "madvise behavior-EINVAL > align > overflow > len=0 >
##              HWPOISON-EPERM > ENOMEM gate order: OK".
##
##      Known limitations: gate 7 maps Linux's per-VMA walk to a
##      simple user-space-range bound check.  A real implementation
##      should walk the VMA tree and return ENOMEM only when no VMA
##      covers the request — until we have VMA tracking, the bound
##      check is a coarser approximation.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 252. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_mount_setattr gate order — size E2BIG
##      ahead of size EINVAL, attr=NULL EFAULT not EINVAL.
##
##      Linux fs/namespace.c::copy_mount_setattr() executes its
##      validation in this literal statement order:
##        1. `if (usize > PAGE_SIZE) return -E2BIG;`
##        2. `if (usize < MOUNT_ATTR_SIZE_VER0) return -EINVAL;`
##        3. `if (!may_mount()) return -EPERM;`
##        4. `copy_struct_from_user(...)` — NULL `uattr` -> -EFAULT.
##      The top-level SYSCALL_DEFINE5(mount_setattr) does the flag
##      mask EINVAL check first, then calls copy_mount_setattr.
##
##      Pre-batch divergences:
##      - (path=path_ptr, flags=0, attr=NULL, size=8192) returned
##        EINVAL instead of E2BIG, because we collapsed
##        (attr==NULL || size<VER0) into a single EINVAL gate that
##        preceded the size>PAGE_SIZE check.  A probe that sends
##        size=8192 to feature-detect copy_struct_from_user's E2BIG
##        cap would see EINVAL and conclude no cap exists, then call
##        with valid attr and observe E2BIG anyway.
##      - (path=path_ptr, flags=0, attr=NULL, size=64) returned
##        EINVAL instead of EFAULT.  Once the size gates pass, Linux
##        forwards to copy_struct_from_user which surfaces NULL as
##        EFAULT.  A probe checking EFAULT/EINVAL discriminators on
##        NULL pointers (a common libc feature-detection technique)
##        would see the wrong errno for the in-range size case.
##
##      Fix: reorder validation to mirror Linux's source statement
##      order — flag mask EINVAL stays at the top, then size>PAGE_SIZE
##      E2BIG, then size<VER0 EINVAL, then attr==NULL EFAULT, then
##      validate_user_read, then copy_from_user, then attr_set/clr
##      mask EINVAL, then atime exclusivity, then propagation.  Path
##      validation is moved after copy_from_user to match Linux's
##      order (user_path_at runs after copy_mount_setattr).
##
##      Self-tests added/updated (~line 41326):
##      - Kept: (path, flags=0, attr, size=4097)         -> E2BIG.
##      - New: (path, flags=0, attr=NULL, size=8192)     -> E2BIG
##        (was EINVAL; demonstrates size-E2BIG ahead of NULL gate).
##      - New: (path, flags=0, attr=NULL, size=64)       -> EFAULT
##        (was EINVAL; demonstrates NULL surfaces as EFAULT once
##         size is in range, matching copy_struct_from_user).
##      - New: (path, flags=0, attr=NULL, size=16)       -> EINVAL
##        (was EINVAL; confirms size-EINVAL still fires ahead of
##         the NULL-EFAULT gate when size < VER0).
##      - Kept: small size (16, valid attr)              -> EINVAL.
##      - Kept: valid (size=32, valid attr)              -> ENOSYS.
##      - Kept: bad flags                                -> EINVAL.
##      Marker: "mount_setattr flags-EINVAL > size-E2BIG > size-EINVAL >
##              NULL-EFAULT gate order: OK".
##
##      Known limitations: gate 3 (may_mount EPERM) is not modelled —
##      we have no per-mount-ns capability gating yet.  Linux would
##      return EPERM ahead of copy_struct_from_user for an unprivileged
##      caller; we proceed straight to the user-pointer gate.  This
##      affects only the (privileged-attr=NULL, size=in-range) case
##      under a no-CAP_SYS_ADMIN process, where we'd return EFAULT
##      where Linux returns EPERM.  Will be addressed once mount-ns
##      capabilities land.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 253. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_landlock_create_ruleset gate order —
##      split size bounds into EINVAL/E2BIG, tighten upper bound to PAGE_SIZE.
##
##      Linux security/landlock/syscalls.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(
##      landlock_create_ruleset) forwards the user buffer to
##      `copy_min_struct_from_user` which gates in this literal
##      statement order:
##        1. `if (!src) return -EFAULT;`
##        2. `if (usize < ksize_min) return -EINVAL;`  (ksize_min = 8 =
##           offsetofend(handled_access_fs)).
##        3. `if (usize > PAGE_SIZE) return -E2BIG;`   (PAGE_SIZE = 4096
##           on x86_64).
##
##      Pre-batch divergences:
##      - (attr=valid, size=8192) returned ENOSYS instead of E2BIG,
##        because our upper bound was 1 MiB (`arg1 > (1 << 20)`), so
##        8192 fell through the size gate and reached the terminal
##        ENOSYS return.  A probe expecting Linux's E2BIG cap at
##        PAGE_SIZE would falsely conclude no cap exists.
##      - (attr=valid, size=4097) returned ENOSYS instead of E2BIG,
##        same reason — 4097 is between PAGE_SIZE (4096) and our
##        wrong 1 MiB ceiling.
##      - (attr=valid, size=2 MiB) returned EINVAL instead of E2BIG,
##        because we collapsed both size bounds into a single EINVAL
##        gate.  A probe sending a deliberately huge size to verify
##        the E2BIG behaviour would see the wrong errno.
##
##      Fix: split the gate into `size<8 -> EINVAL` and
##      `size>PAGE_SIZE -> E2BIG`, using LINUX_PAGE_SIZE=4096 to match
##      the ABI consumer's expectation (not our 16 KiB internal frame
##      size).  EFAULT for NULL `attr` already preceded both, matching
##      Linux's `!src` check at the top of `copy_min_struct_from_user`.
##
##      Self-tests added (~line 37322):
##      - (attr=valid, size=4)     -> EINVAL  (confirms size<ksize_min
##                                             still fires when in
##                                             the rejected range).
##      - (attr=valid, size=4097)  -> E2BIG   (was ENOSYS — pre-batch
##                                             the upper bound was
##                                             1 MiB so 4097 passed).
##      - (attr=valid, size=2 MiB) -> E2BIG   (was EINVAL — pre-batch
##                                             the 1 MiB cap returned
##                                             the wrong errno).
##      - kept: NULL attr -> EFAULT; size=8 valid -> ENOSYS;
##        VERSION/ERRATA flag-dispatch -> 0; VERSION|ERRATA -> EINVAL;
##        VERSION with non-NULL attr -> EINVAL; ERRATA with non-zero
##        size -> EINVAL; bad flags -> EINVAL.
##      Marker: "landlock_create_ruleset NULL-EFAULT > size-EINVAL >
##              size-E2BIG gate order: OK".
##
##      Known limitations: terminal return is ENOSYS rather than a
##      real ruleset fd — we have no Landlock implementation, and
##      the VERSION/ERRATA queries return 0 to advertise "Landlock
##      not available".  When a real implementation lands the size
##      gates here will continue to apply ahead of the allocator.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 254. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_statx mask & sync-type validation —
##      add STATX__RESERVED EINVAL and AT_STATX_SYNC_TYPE collision gates.
##
##      Linux fs/stat.c::do_statx() gates input in this order, BEFORE
##      any path resolution or buffer access:
##        1. `if (mask & STATX__RESERVED) return -EINVAL;`
##           (STATX__RESERVED = 0x80000000, bit 31, reserved for
##            future use).
##        2. `if ((flags & AT_STATX_SYNC_TYPE) == AT_STATX_SYNC_TYPE)
##            return -EINVAL;`
##           (AT_STATX_SYNC_TYPE = AT_STATX_FORCE_SYNC |
##            AT_STATX_DONT_SYNC = 0x6000; both bits set
##            simultaneously is contradictory).
##
##      Pre-batch divergences:
##      - `mask` was never validated.  A probe sending
##        `mask=0x80000000` (Linux's reserved-bit feature-detect
##        pattern) would see EFAULT/ENOENT/0 depending on the other
##        args, where Linux returns EINVAL.  This is the canonical
##        statx ABI compatibility check and was missing entirely.
##      - `(flags=0x6000)` (both AT_STATX_FORCE_SYNC and
##        AT_STATX_DONT_SYNC) passed the existing `flags & !VALID_FLAGS`
##        gate because both bits are individually valid.  Linux
##        rejects this combination with EINVAL; we returned ENOENT
##        via the path-lookup fall-through.
##
##      Fix: add the two missing gates ahead of the existing VALID_FLAGS
##      and statxbuf checks, matching Linux's literal source-order
##      placement at the top of do_statx().  The existing
##      `flags & !VALID_FLAGS` strict-mask check is kept (slightly
##      stricter than upstream do_statx, which silently ignores unknown
##      AT_ bits — kept for safety since probes that fuzz flag bits
##      will see a consistent EINVAL).
##
##      Self-tests added (~line 32520):
##      - mask=0x80000000, valid statxbuf, flags=0 -> EINVAL  (was
##        ENOENT — STATX__RESERVED gate now fires first).
##      - flags=0x6000 (FORCE|DONT), valid statxbuf      -> EINVAL  (was
##        ENOENT — sync-type collision now rejected).
##      - flags=0x2000 (FORCE_SYNC alone), valid statxbuf -> ENOENT
##        (confirms the collision check is exact-equality, not popcount —
##        a single sync-type bit still passes).
##      - kept: bogus flag bit -> EINVAL; NULL statxbuf -> EFAULT;
##        AT_EMPTY_PATH with Console -> 0 + S_IFCHR; path lookup
##        without AT_EMPTY_PATH -> ENOENT.
##      Marker: "statx mask-RESERVED-EINVAL > sync-type-collision-EINVAL
##              gate order: OK".
##
##      Known limitations: terminal path-lookup return is still ENOENT
##      (no real filesystem yet), so realistic mask values produce no
##      stat output beyond the AT_EMPTY_PATH self-fd path.  Once a real
##      VFS lands, the validated mask will be honoured to select which
##      stx_* fields are filled.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 255. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_openat2 gate order — split strict
##      size==24 into [VER0, PAGE_SIZE], move filename validation after
##      size/copy gates.
##
##      Linux fs/open.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4(openat2) gates in this literal
##      source order:
##        1. if (usize < OPEN_HOW_SIZE_VER0) return -EINVAL;  (24)
##        2. if (usize > PAGE_SIZE)         return -E2BIG;    (4096)
##        3. copy_struct_from_user(...) -> -EFAULT for NULL `how`.
##        4. build_open_flags() -> resolve & ~VALID -> -EINVAL.
##        5. getname(filename) -> -EFAULT for NULL filename.
##        6. (path walk) RESOLVE_CACHED/IN_ROOT/BENEATH responses.
##
##      Pre-batch divergences:
##      - We required `args.arg3 == 24` exactly, returning EINVAL for
##        any forward-compatible size in [25, PAGE_SIZE] that Linux
##        accepts (with the excess zero-padded by copy_struct_from_user).
##        A glibc that adds a new field would see EINVAL on every
##        call even with the new layout's exact size.
##      - Sizes > PAGE_SIZE returned EINVAL instead of E2BIG, losing
##        the discriminator probes use to detect the upper cap.
##      - The filename pointer was validated at the very top of the
##        function — so (filename=NULL, size=8) returned EFAULT where
##        Linux returns EINVAL (size gate runs first); (filename=NULL,
##        size=8192) returned EFAULT where Linux returns E2BIG.
##
##      Fix: reorder to mirror Linux's source order — size<VER0
##      EINVAL first, then size>PAGE_SIZE E2BIG, then how==NULL EFAULT,
##      then read the first 24 bytes (any size in [24, PAGE_SIZE] is
##      accepted; trailing bytes are silently ignored, see limitation),
##      then resolve mask EINVAL, then filename EFAULT, then the
##      existing RESOLVE_CACHED/IN_ROOT/BENEATH responses (which Linux
##      surfaces at path-walk time, after getname).
##
##      Self-tests added (~line 36945):
##      - (filename=NULL, size=8)    -> EINVAL  (was EFAULT; size gate
##                                              now wins).
##      - (filename=NULL, size=8192) -> E2BIG   (was EFAULT; size gate
##                                              now wins).
##      - (filename=valid, size=4097)-> E2BIG   (was EINVAL; was tied
##                                              to strict ==24 check).
##      - (filename=valid, size=32)  -> NOT EINVAL  (forward-compat;
##                                              pre-batch all non-24
##                                              sizes returned EINVAL).
##      - kept: NULL path with size=24 -> EFAULT; wrong size 16 ->
##        EINVAL; NULL how -> EFAULT; RESOLVE_CACHED -> EAGAIN;
##        RESOLVE_BENEATH -> EXDEV; unknown resolve bit -> EINVAL.
##      Marker: "openat2 size-EINVAL > size-E2BIG > how-EFAULT >
##              resolve-EINVAL > filename-EFAULT gate order: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - Trailing bytes [24..usize) are silently ignored.  Linux's
##        copy_struct_from_user checks they are zero and returns
##        -E2BIG if not, which signals "you're trying to use a newer
##        flag the kernel doesn't recognise."  A probe that sets a
##        non-zero byte at offset 24 will see success here where
##        Linux returns E2BIG.  Add forward-compat zero check in a
##        future batch (separate concern from the size-bounds fix).
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 256. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_openat2 forward-compat trailing-zero
##      E2BIG check — addresses limitation noted in entry 255.
##
##      Linux's `copy_struct_from_user` (lib/usercopy.c) walks the
##      bytes in `[ksize, usize)` looking for any non-zero byte and
##      returns -E2BIG when it finds one.  This is the canonical
##      signal a userspace probe uses to detect that it's trying to
##      pass a newer ABI field the kernel doesn't yet understand.
##      Batch 226 fixed the size bounds but left this check as a
##      known limitation.
##
##      Pre-batch divergence:
##      - (filename=valid, how points to 32 bytes with byte[24]=1,
##        size=32) returned the result of forwarding to sys_openat
##        (typically ENOENT for non-existent paths) where Linux
##        returns E2BIG via copy_struct_from_user's trailing-zero
##        check.  A glibc that fuzzes trailing bytes to detect the
##        upstream ABI cap would see no E2BIG signal and conclude
##        the running kernel supports an undefined newer ABI.
##
##      Fix: between the 24-byte struct read and the resolve mask
##      gate, when `size > 24` walk the trailing `size - 24` bytes
##      in 64-byte chunks and return E2BIG if any byte is non-zero.
##      Matches Linux's `check_zeroed_user`-then-E2BIG sequence in
##      copy_struct_from_user.
##
##      Self-tests added/updated (~line 36955):
##      - Updated: size=32 with a properly-sized 32-byte zero buffer
##        now exercises the new check and forwards successfully (was
##        passing accidentally because we never read past byte 23).
##      - New: size=32 with byte[24]=1 -> E2BIG  (was forwarded to
##        sys_openat and surfaced ENOENT or similar).
##      Marker: "openat2 forward-compat trailing-zero E2BIG: OK".
##
##      Known limitations: trailing bytes are read in 64-byte chunks
##      via copy_from_user — each chunk pays SMAP STAC/CLAC overhead.
##      For the worst case (size = PAGE_SIZE = 4096), that's ~64
##      STAC/CLAC pairs.  This is a one-time cost per syscall and
##      matches the asymptotic shape of Linux's check_zeroed_user
##      (which also walks bytewise).  No optimisation needed for
##      stub-stage code; revisit if openat2 ever becomes a hot path.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 257. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_clone3 forward-compat trailing-zero
##      E2BIG check — extends the pattern fixed in batch 227 (openat2) to
##      kernel/fork.c::copy_clone_args_from_user.
##
##      Linux's `copy_clone_args_from_user` forwards to
##      `copy_struct_from_user(&args, sizeof(args), uargs, usize)`.
##      `sizeof(struct clone_args)` is CLONE_ARGS_SIZE_VER2 = 88
##      (Linux 5.7+ with cgroup field).  When `usize > 88`,
##      copy_struct_from_user walks bytes [88, usize) checking they
##      are all zero — any non-zero byte returns -E2BIG.  This is
##      the canonical "newer ABI than the kernel understands" signal.
##
##      Pre-batch divergence:
##      - (cl_args points at 96 bytes with byte[88]=1, size=96)
##        returned ENOSYS (the validated-but-unsupported path) where
##        Linux returns E2BIG via the trailing-zero check.  Our
##        per-batch-219 code computed `nwords = min(size/8, 11)` so
##        we only ever read 88 bytes and silently ignored the rest.
##        A glibc that fuzzes trailing bytes to detect the upstream
##        ABI cap would conclude the running kernel supports an
##        undefined newer ABI.
##
##      Fix: after the existing copy_from_user of `nwords*8` bytes
##      (capped at 88), if `size > 88` walk the trailing `size - 88`
##      bytes in 64-byte chunks via copy_from_user and return E2BIG
##      on the first non-zero byte.  Matches the pattern just
##      applied to openat2 in batch 227.
##
##      Self-tests added (~line 38030):
##      - clone3 size=96 with all-zero pad -> ENOSYS  (confirms
##        zero-padded forward-compat sizes still succeed).
##      - clone3 size=96 with byte[88]=1  -> E2BIG    (was ENOSYS;
##        the new gate now fires).
##      Marker: "clone3 forward-compat trailing-zero E2BIG: OK".
##
##      Known limitations: trailing bytes are read in 64-byte chunks
##      via copy_from_user.  Worst case (size = PAGE_SIZE = 4096) is
##      ~63 STAC/CLAC pairs.  Same shape as openat2 — see entry 256.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 258. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_mount_setattr forward-compat trailing-
##      zero E2BIG check — extends the pattern from batches 227/228 to
##      fs/namespace.c::copy_mount_setattr.
##
##      Linux's copy_mount_setattr calls
##      copy_struct_from_user(&attr, sizeof(attr), uattr, usize) with
##      ksize = sizeof(struct mount_attr) = 32.  When usize > 32,
##      copy_struct_from_user walks bytes [32, usize) and returns
##      -E2BIG on the first non-zero byte.
##
##      Pre-batch divergence:
##      - (uattr=40-byte buffer with byte[32]=1, size=40) returned
##        the attr-validation result (typically ENOSYS) where Linux
##        returns E2BIG.  Same shape as the openat2 (batch 227) and
##        clone3 (batch 228) limitations.
##
##      Fix: after the existing copy_from_user(buf, 32), when
##      `size > 32` walk the trailing `size - 32` bytes in 64-byte
##      chunks via copy_from_user and return E2BIG on the first
##      non-zero byte.  Insertion point is right after the struct
##      read, before any field validation — matches the order of
##      Linux's copy_struct_from_user, which does the trailing-zero
##      walk before the field-level interpretation.
##
##      Self-tests added (~line 41472):
##      - mount_setattr size=40 (zero pad)        -> ENOSYS  (confirms
##                                                 forward-compat
##                                                 sizes still work).
##      - mount_setattr size=40 (byte[32]=1)      -> E2BIG   (was
##                                                 ENOSYS).
##      Marker: "mount_setattr forward-compat trailing-zero E2BIG: OK".
##
##      Known limitations: trailing bytes are read in 64-byte chunks
##      via copy_from_user.  Worst case (size = PAGE_SIZE = 4096) is
##      ~63 STAC/CLAC pairs.  Same shape as openat2/clone3 — see
##      entries 256/257.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 259. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_landlock_create_ruleset forward-compat
##      trailing-zero E2BIG check — extends the pattern from batches
##      227/228/229 to security/landlock/syscalls.c.
##
##      Linux's landlock_create_ruleset calls

##      usize) where ksize_min = offsetofend(struct

##        where Linux returns E2BIG.  Same shape as the openat2/clone3/
##        mount_setattr limitations closed in batches 227/228/229.
##
##      Fix: after the existing validate_user_read, when
##      `args.arg1 > LANDLOCK_RULESET_ATTR_KSIZE (8)` walk the trailing
##      `size - 8` bytes in 64-byte chunks via copy_from_user and
##      return E2BIG on the first non-zero byte.  Insertion point is
##      right after the readable-range check, before the terminal
##      ENOSYS — matches Linux's copy_struct_from_user order, which
##      does the trailing-zero walk before any field interpretation.
##
##      Self-tests added (after the batch-224 size-gate marker):
##      - landlock_create_ruleset size=16 (zero pad)   -> ENOSYS
##                                                       (confirms

##                                                        sizes still
##                                                        work).
##      - landlock_create_ruleset size=16 (byte[8]=1)  -> E2BIG (was
##                                                        ENOSYS).
##      Marker: "landlock_create_ruleset forward-compat trailing-zero
##      E2BIG: OK".
##
##      Known limitations: trailing bytes are read in 64-byte chunks
##      via copy_from_user.  Worst case (size = PAGE_SIZE = 4096) is
##      ~63 STAC/CLAC pairs.  Same shape as openat2/clone3/mount_setattr
##      — see entries 256/257/258.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 260. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_sched_setattr forward-compat trailing-
##      zero E2BIG check — extends the pattern from batches
##      227/228/229/230 to kernel/sched/syscalls.c.
##
##      Linux's sched_copy_attr (called from
##      SYSCALL_DEFINE3(sched_setattr)) calls copy_struct_from_user with
##      ksize = sizeof(struct sched_attr).  When usize > ksize, the
##      wrapper walks bytes [ksize, usize) and returns -E2BIG on the
##      first non-zero byte so probes can detect what kernel features
##      are available (e.g. sched_util_min/max appeared in 5.14).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence:
##      - (uattr=64-byte buffer with byte[48]=1, size=64) returned
##        0 (success — policy SCHED_OTHER + priority 0 accepted)
##        where Linux returns -E2BIG.  Same shape as the openat2/
##        clone3/mount_setattr/landlock_create_ruleset limitations
##        closed in batches 227-230.
##
##      Fix: after the existing validate_user_read(attr_ptr, size),
##      when `size > SCHED_ATTR_KSIZE (48)` walk the trailing
##      `size - 48` bytes in 64-byte chunks via copy_from_user and
##      return E2BIG on the first non-zero byte.  Insertion point is
##      right after the readable-range check, before the v0 copy and
##      policy/priority parsing — matches Linux's sched_copy_attr
##      order.  Kernel-known size = 48 (matches the v0 fields we
##      actually parse: size/policy/flags/nice/priority/runtime/
##      deadline/period).
##
##      Self-tests added (after the OTHER/5-EINVAL test):
##      - sched_setattr size=64, zero pad -> 0 (passes zero-check,
##                                              SCHED_OTHER + prio 0
##                                              accepted).
##      - sched_setattr size=64, byte[48]=1 -> E2BIG (was 0).
##      Marker: "sched_setattr forward-compat trailing-zero E2BIG: OK".
##
##      Known limitations: trailing bytes are read in 64-byte chunks
##      via copy_from_user.  Worst case (size = 1 MiB) is ~16384
##      STAC/CLAC pairs.  Same shape as openat2/clone3/mount_setattr/
##      landlock_create_ruleset — see entries 256/257/258/259.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 261. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_perf_event_open forward-compat
##      trailing-zero E2BIG check — extends the pattern from batches
##      227-231 to kernel/events/core.c.
##
##      Linux's perf_copy_attr calls copy_struct_from_user with
##      ksize = sizeof(struct perf_event_attr).  This struct has grown
##      through versions:
##        VER0 = 64, VER1 = 72, VER2 = 80, VER3 = 96, VER4 = 104,
##        VER5 = 112, VER6 = 120 (5.12), VER7 = 128 (5.13),
##        VER8 = 136 (6.x).
##      When usize > ksize, bytes [ksize, usize) must be zero or the
##      call fails with -E2BIG so userspace probes can detect what
##      perf_event_attr extension the kernel knows about.
##
##      Pre-batch divergence:
##      - (attr_size=144 with byte[136]=1) fell through to ENOSYS
##        (or the pid/cpu check) where Linux returns -E2BIG.  Same
##        shape as the openat2/clone3/mount_setattr/landlock/
##        sched_setattr limitations closed in batches 227-231.
##
##      Fix: after the existing validate_user_read(args.arg0,
##      attr_size), when `attr_size > PERF_ATTR_KSIZE (136)` walk
##      [attr+136, attr+attr_size) in 64-byte chunks via copy_from_user
##      and return E2BIG on the first non-zero byte.  Insertion point
##      is right after the readable-range check, before the
##      pid==-1 && cpu==-1 EINVAL gate — matches Linux's perf_copy_attr
##      order (trailing-zero check happens inside copy_struct_from_user,
##      which is called before the deeper perf_event_alloc validation).
##      KSIZE = 136 (Linux VER8) is the current Linux 6.x size; we
##      track the latest known version so probes passing common
##      versions don't hit a false E2BIG.
##
##      Self-tests added (after the existing flags-first/size-quirk
##      marker, before the keyctl tests):
##      - perf_event_open size=144, zero pad      -> ENOSYS (terminal
##                                                  stub since perf is
##                                                  unimplemented).
##      - perf_event_open size=144, byte[136]=1   -> E2BIG (was
##                                                  ENOSYS).
##      Marker: "perf_event_open forward-compat trailing-zero E2BIG: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - PERF_ATTR_KSIZE pegged at VER8 (136); when Linux adds VER9
##        and our copy is updated to track it, this batch's tests for
##        size=144 zero-pad will still pass (zero check matches), but
##        the new max size should match the new VER value.
##      - Trailing bytes are read in 64-byte chunks via copy_from_user.
##        Worst case (size = PAGE_SIZE = 4096) is ~63 STAC/CLAC pairs.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 262. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_process_vm_readv/writev gate order —
##      move pid check after iov validation, split combined cnt check.
##
##      Linux source: mm/process_vm_access.c::process_vm_rw.  Its
##      validation order:
##        1. flags != 0                          -> -EINVAL
##        2. import_iovec(LOCAL):
##           - liovcnt > UIO_MAXIOV (1024)        -> -EINVAL
##           - NULL/unreadable lvec               -> -EFAULT
##        3. (iov_iter empty -> return 0, not mirrored yet)
##        4. import_iovec(REMOTE): same gates as local.
##        5. mm_access(pid)                       -> -ESRCH if no task
##           (pid<=0 yields find_get_task_by_vpid==NULL -> -ESRCH).
##
##      Pre-batch divergences:
##      - `pid <= 0` was checked at the top of process_vm_impl, before
##        any iov validation.  A probe passing pid=-1 with NULL local
##        iov saw ESRCH where Linux's local import_iovec returns
##        EFAULT first.  Similarly pid=-1 with liovcnt=2000 returned
##        ESRCH where Linux returns EINVAL.
##      - The two count limits were combined into a single
##        `liovcnt > 1024 || riovcnt > 1024` -> EINVAL gate, which
##        fired before the local NULL-lvec check.  Probe
##        (pid=-1, lvec=NULL, liovcnt=2, riovcnt=2000) saw EINVAL
##        where Linux's local import fires first and returns EFAULT.
##
##      Fix: reorder process_vm_impl to mirror Linux:
##        - liovcnt > 1024 -> EINVAL
##        - liovcnt > 0 && NULL lvec / unreadable -> EFAULT
##        - riovcnt > 1024 -> EINVAL
##        - riovcnt > 0 && NULL rvec / unreadable -> EFAULT
##        - pid <= 0 -> ESRCH
##      The same_addr_space resolution downstream is unchanged.
##
##      Self-tests added (after the existing flag-EINVAL test, before
##      the same-AS copy tests):
##      - pid=-1, lvec=NULL, liovcnt=2         -> EFAULT (was ESRCH).
##      - pid=-1, liovcnt=2000                 -> EINVAL (was ESRCH).
##      - pid=-1, riovcnt=2000                 -> EINVAL (matches
##                                                pre-batch; covers
##                                                remote-side gate).
##      - pid=-1, lvec=NULL, liovcnt=2,
##                          riovcnt=2000        -> EFAULT (was EINVAL).
##      Marker: "process_vm_readv local-iov-EINVAL/EFAULT >
##      remote-iov-EINVAL/EFAULT > pid-ESRCH gate order: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - Linux additionally short-circuits to return 0 when the local
##        iov_iter has zero total bytes (item 3 above).  We currently
##        proceed to the pid check; a probe passing
##        pid=-1 + liovcnt=0 + riovcnt=0 still sees ESRCH where Linux
##        returns 0.  Fixing this requires summing iov len fields,
##        which our same-AS copy loop already does — could be deferred
##        to the existing copy code in a follow-up batch.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 263. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sys_process_vm_readv/writev local
##      iter_count==0 short-circuit — close the known limitation from
##      entry 262.
##
##      Linux source: mm/process_vm_access.c::process_vm_rw, after
##      import_iovec(LOCAL) succeeds:
##        if (!iov_iter_count(&iter))
##            goto free_iov_l;   // returns 0
##      This fires when liovcnt==0 OR every iov[i].iov_len==0.  It
##      returns 0 BEFORE remote validation or mm_access(pid), so even
##      pid=-1 with no work-to-do succeeds.
##
##      Also discovered: batch 233's Case C test expectation was wrong.
##      I asserted (pid=-1, liovcnt=0, riovcnt=2000) -> EINVAL on the
##      theory that "Linux validates local-then-remote, then remote
##      sees riovcnt > UIO_MAXIOV."  But Linux's local short-circuit
##      fires BEFORE remote validation, so the correct answer is 0.
##      Updated Case C to expect 0.
##
##      Pre-batch divergences (closed here):
##      - (pid=anything, liovcnt=0) -> ESRCH for pid<=0, 0 for pid>0.
##        Linux always returns 0 (local iter empty).
##      - (pid=valid, liovcnt=2 with both lens=0) -> reached the AS
##        same-process check.  Linux short-circuits to 0.
##
##      Fix: after the local iov readability check, sum the iov lens
##      (16 entries / 256 bytes per copy_from_user batch).  If the
##      total is 0, return SyscallResult::ok(0) immediately, before
##      the remote validation and pid-ESRCH check.  saturating_add
##      guards against u64 overflow.
##
##      Test updates:
##      - Old pid=0 empty-args ESRCH test rewritten to use a non-empty
##        local iov (1 entry, len=4), so the pid<=0 ESRCH path is
##        actually exercised.
##      - Batch 233 Case C corrected from EINVAL to 0.
##      - New Case E: (pid=-1, all-empty)              -> 0 (was ESRCH).
##      - New Case F: (pid=-1, lvec=[zero-len iovs])   -> 0 (was
##                                                       EFAULT — we
##                                                       used to take
##                                                       the
##                                                       lvec=NULL
##                                                       branch when
##                                                       liovcnt>0 if
##                                                       arg1 was 0;
##                                                       here arg1 is
##                                                       a valid
##                                                       pointer so
##                                                       previously
##                                                       we'd run the
##                                                       same-AS copy
##                                                       and produce
##                                                       a non-zero
##                                                       result or
##                                                       fail with
##                                                       EFAULT on
##                                                       the deref).
##      Marker: "process_vm_readv local-iter-empty short-circuit: OK".
##
##      Known limitations: the same-AS copy loop downstream handles the
##      case where local has content but remote is empty by returning
##      0 from the "Nothing to do if either side has zero entries"
##      block.  That still doesn't fully match Linux's
##      process_vm_rw_core semantics for cross-side mismatches, but
##      the rough behavior (returns 0 for empty-remote calls when
##      local is non-empty) is preserved.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 264. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sched_setaffinity / sched_getaffinity
##      gate order — match kernel/sched/syscalls.c.
##
##      Linux source:
##        SYSCALL_DEFINE3(sched_setaffinity):
##          1. copy_from_user(mask_ptr, len) -> EFAULT (len==0 with
##             NULL is a no-op).
##          2. find_process_by_pid(pid) -> ESRCH.
##        SYSCALL_DEFINE3(sched_getaffinity):
##          1. (len * 8) < nr_cpu_ids -> EINVAL (size too small).
##          2. len & (sizeof(unsigned long) - 1) -> EINVAL (alignment).
##          3. find_process_by_pid(pid) -> ESRCH.
##          4. copy_to_user(mask_ptr, ..., retlen) -> EFAULT.
##
##      Pre-batch divergences:
##      - setaffinity: pid-ESRCH fired ahead of mask-EFAULT.  Probe
##        (pid=bogus, len=8, mask=NULL) saw ESRCH where Linux returns
##        EFAULT.  Also, mask=NULL was rejected unconditionally as
##        EFAULT even when len==0; Linux accepts that as a no-op.
##      - getaffinity: pid-ESRCH and mask-EFAULT both fired before
##        size-EINVAL.  Probe (pid=bogus, len=0, mask=NULL) saw
##        ESRCH where Linux returns EINVAL.  Linux's alignment check
##        (len & 7 != 0 -> EINVAL on x86_64) was missing entirely;
##        probes with len=15 silently fell through to the mask write.
##
##      Fix:
##      - setaffinity: gate `if cpusetsize > 0` around the mask
##        readability check (so len==0 with NULL succeeds), then pid
##        ESRCH check.
##      - getaffinity: reordered to size-EINVAL -> alignment-EINVAL
##        -> pid-ESRCH -> mask-EFAULT.  Added `cpusetsize & 7 != 0`
##        gate for the alignment requirement.
##
##      Self-tests added (after the existing setaffinity NULL-mask
##      assertion):
##      - setaffinity(bogus pid, NULL mask, len=8)      -> EFAULT
##                                                       (was ESRCH).
##      - setaffinity(0, len=0, NULL)                   -> 0
##                                                       (was EFAULT).
##        Marker: "sched_setaffinity len>0->mask-EFAULT > pid-ESRCH
##        gate order: OK".
##      - getaffinity(bogus pid, len=0, NULL)           -> EINVAL
##                                                       (was ESRCH).
##      - getaffinity(0, len=15, NULL)                  -> EINVAL
##                                                       (alignment;
##                                                        was EFAULT).
##      - getaffinity(bogus pid, len=8 aligned, NULL)   -> ESRCH
##                                                       (covers pid
##                                                        gate after
##                                                        EINVAL).
##        Marker: "sched_getaffinity size-EINVAL > align-EINVAL >
##        pid-ESRCH > mask-EFAULT gate order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests (size=0 -> EINVAL, NULL mask len=16 -> EFAULT
##      for both syscalls) still hold under the new ordering.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).


## 265. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: sched_rr_get_interval — pid<0
##      -EINVAL gate ahead of -ESRCH and -EFAULT (batch 236).
##
##      Linux reference: kernel/sched/syscalls.c, SYSCALL_DEFINE2 of
##      sched_rr_get_interval (and the timespec64 variant).  The gate
##      order is:
##
##          1. `pid < 0`                                -> -EINVAL
##          2. find_process_by_pid(pid)                 -> -ESRCH
##          3. put_timespec64(&t, interval)             -> -EFAULT
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: we cast args.arg0 straight to u64 for
##      the PCB lookup, so a probe passing pid=-1 (which an x86_64
##      libc forwards as 0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_FFFF) bypassed the EINVAL
##      gate.  When ts was NULL we returned EFAULT (the ts validation
##      ran first since pid != 0 took the lookup branch, and the
##      huge pid missed); when ts was valid we returned ESRCH.  Both
##      diverged from Linux, which returns EINVAL the instant it
##      sees a negative pid.
##
##      Fix: take args.arg0 as i32 with allow(cast_possible_wrap,
##      cast_possible_truncation), and if pid_i32 < 0 return EINVAL
##      before the PCB lookup or the ts NULL/validation gates.
##
##      Self-tests added (after the existing sched_rr_get_interval(0,
##      NULL) -> EFAULT assertion):
##      - (pid = -1 as u64::MAX, ts = NULL)            -> EINVAL
##                                                       (was EFAULT).
##      - (pid = -1 as u64::MAX, ts = 0xdead_beef)     -> EINVAL
##                                                       (proves the
##                                                        pid<0 gate
##                                                        fires before
##                                                        validate_user_
##                                                        write).
##      - (pid = 0x7fff_ffff bogus positive, ts = NULL) -> ESRCH
##                                                       (proves the
##                                                        pid lookup
##                                                        gate still
##                                                        runs for
##                                                        pid >= 0).
##        Marker: "sched_rr_get_interval pid<0-EINVAL > pid-ESRCH >
##        ts-EFAULT gate order: OK".
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - We still report a fixed 100 ms RR slice for every task; the
##        per-policy variation Linux exposes (SCHED_OTHER returns 0
##        and SCHED_FIFO returns 0) is not yet modeled.  A follow-up
##        batch can layer the policy branch on top once the gate
##        order is solid.
##
##      Side observation (NOT introduced by this batch): the boot
##      test occasionally panics in kernel/src/watchdog.rs:258 with
##      "assertion `left == right` failed: heartbeat should
##      increment".  This is a pre-existing flaky watchdog self-test
##      that has been hit on previous batches as well; on retry the
##      same kernel boots to BOOT_OK normally.  Tracking only — fix
##      belongs to the watchdog owner, not the Linux ABI work.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s on retry; first
##      boot tripped the unrelated watchdog flake above).


## 266. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: futex_waitv — flags-first gate,
##      consolidated !waiters EINVAL, timeout-gated clockid (batch 237).
##
##      Linux reference: kernel/futex/syscalls.c, SYSCALL_DEFINE5 of
##      futex_waitv.  The documented gate order is:
##
##          1. `flags != 0`                                  -> -EINVAL
##          2. `!nr_futexes || nr_futexes > FUTEX_WAITV_MAX
##              || !waiters`                                 -> -EINVAL
##          3. if timeout != NULL: futex_parse_waitv_clockid
##              (validates clockid is REALTIME or MONOTONIC,
##              copies the timespec from user)               -> -EINVAL
##                                                              / -EFAULT
##          4. validate / copy the waiters vector             -> -EFAULT
##
##      Pre-batch divergences:
##      - NULL waiters returned -EFAULT (the plain copy_from_user
##        failure) where Linux explicitly maps `!waiters` to -EINVAL
##        via the consolidated check.
##      - The `flags != 0` gate ran *after* the nr range check.  A
##        probe passing (nr=0, flags=bogus) saw -EINVAL "for nr=0"
##        instead of -EINVAL "for flags".  Same errno but the gate
##        ladder differs — distinguishable by a probe varying one
##        argument at a time.
##      - The clockid range check ran unconditionally.  Linux only
##        validates clockid inside futex_parse_waitv_clockid, which
##        is gated behind `timeout != NULL`.  Probes passing
##        (timeout=NULL, clockid=99) saw -EINVAL via the clockid
##        gate where Linux ignores clockid and proceeds.
##
##      Fix:
##      - Reorder: `args.arg2 (flags) != 0 -> EINVAL` runs first.
##      - Fold `args.arg0 (waiters) == 0` into the same
##        `nr == 0 || nr > 128 || waiters == 0 -> EINVAL` gate that
##        previously only covered nr.
##      - Move the clockid range check inside the `args.arg3 != 0`
##        (timeout != NULL) branch.  Re-derive `clockid` locally in
##        the timeout-non-NULL nr=1 deadline math since the outer
##        variable is now scoped to the validation arm.
##
##      Self-tests added / updated (after the existing nr=0,
##      nr>128, flags!=0 EINVAL trio):
##      - NULL waiters with nr=1 -> EINVAL (was EFAULT).
##      - (waiters=NULL, nr=0, flags=0xdead_beef) -> EINVAL.  Proves
##        the flags-first ordering: even with nr=0 (which would
##        otherwise trip the combined EINVAL), the flags gate now
##        fires first.
##      - (waiters=valid, timeout=valid_ts, clockid=5) -> EINVAL.
