##        Confirms the clockid range check still fires when timeout
##        is non-NULL.
##      - (waiters=valid, timeout=NULL, clockid=99) -> EINVAL via
##        the per-entry SIZE_U8 check rather than the clockid gate
##        (which Linux skips when timeout is NULL).  Same observable
##        errno, smoke check that we don't accidentally return
##        EFAULT or some other code on the new code path.
##        Marker: "futex_waitv flags-first > nr/waiters-EINVAL >
##        timeout-gated clockid gate order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved:
##      - nr=0 -> EINVAL, nr>128 -> EINVAL, flags!=0 -> EINVAL.
##      - nr=1 fast-path entries (SIZE_U16/reserved/unknown bit/
##        NUMA/valid-with-NULL-uaddr) all still fire their per-entry
##        gates after the new top-level reorder.
##
##      Known limitations (unchanged by this batch):
##      - Multi-entry futex_waitv (nr > 1) still returns -ENOSYS;
##        our futex subsystem only supports parking on a single key
##        at a time.
##      - The U8 / U16 / U64 per-entry size classes still return
##        -EINVAL; only SIZE_U32 is implemented.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).


## 267. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: move_mount — MOVE_MOUNT_BENEATH +
##      MOVE_MOUNT_SET_GROUP mutual-exclusion -EINVAL (batch 238).
##
##      Linux reference: fs/namespace.c, SYSCALL_DEFINE5(move_mount).
##      Gate order is:
##
##          1. may_mount() (CAP_SYS_ADMIN in mnt_ns)         -> -EPERM
##          2. flags & ~MOVE_MOUNT__MASK                     -> -EINVAL
##          3. (flags & (MOVE_MOUNT_BENEATH | MOVE_MOUNT_SET_GROUP))
##             == (MOVE_MOUNT_BENEATH | MOVE_MOUNT_SET_GROUP) -> -EINVAL
##          4. user_path_at(from_dfd, from_pathname, lflags) -> -EFAULT
##                                                              / -ENOENT
##                                                              / ...
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: gate 3 (the BENEATH+SET_GROUP mutual
##      exclusion) was missing.  Passing both bits together is
##      semantically nonsense — moving a mount *beneath* a target
##      while simultaneously requesting that the *propagation group*
##      of the source be installed on the target has no defined
##      behaviour — and Linux refuses it ahead of any path validation.
##      A probe passing (flags = BENEATH | SET_GROUP |
##      F_EMPTY_PATH | T_EMPTY_PATH, paths = NULL, NULL) saw our
##      terminal -EPERM where Linux returns -EINVAL.
##
##      Fix: add the mutual-exclusion check after the flag-mask check
##      and before any path validation:
##
##          if (flags & (BENEATH | SET_GROUP)) == (BENEATH | SET_GROUP)
##              return -EINVAL;
##
##      We do NOT layer in may_mount()-first CAP_SYS_ADMIN ordering:
##      our kernel-context tests have no credentials view, and the
##      privileged caller path already terminates at -EPERM.  The
##      may_mount ordering only matters for unprivileged callers
##      with a real mnt_ns, which the boot env cannot model.
##
##      Self-tests added (after the existing valid->EPERM probe):
##      - (flags = BENEATH | SET_GROUP | F_EMPTY_PATH | T_EMPTY_PATH,
##         paths = NULL, NULL)                              -> EINVAL
##                                                            (was
##                                                             EPERM).
##      - (flags = BENEATH | F_EMPTY_PATH | T_EMPTY_PATH,
##         paths = NULL, NULL)                              -> EPERM
##                                                            (BENEATH
##                                                             alone is
##                                                             legal, so
##                                                             the gate
##                                                             must not
##                                                             fire).
##      - (flags = SET_GROUP | F_EMPTY_PATH | T_EMPTY_PATH,
##         paths = NULL, NULL)                              -> EPERM
##                                                            (SET_GROUP
##                                                             alone is
##                                                             legal).
##        Marker: "move_mount BENEATH|SET_GROUP mutual exclusion
##        EINVAL: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved: bad-flags->EINVAL, NULL from-path
##      without F_EMPTY_PATH->EFAULT, valid->EPERM.
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - may_mount() CAP_SYS_ADMIN-first ordering not modeled (see
##        above).  An unprivileged caller would see -EPERM ahead of
##        the flag-mask check on Linux; we surface -EINVAL first
##        because there is no caller credentials view in the
##        dispatch-time test context.
##      - user_path_at path-walking semantics are stubbed: any
##        non-NULL path with a readable string passes our
##        validate_user_str check and reaches the terminal -EPERM.
##        Real path resolution (-EFAULT for bad addr, -ENOENT for
##        missing dentry, -ELOOP for symlink cycles, etc.) is not
##        implemented.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).


## 268. [DONE 2026-06-05] Linux ABI: name_to_handle_at — accept
##      AT_HANDLE_MNT_ID_UNIQUE / AT_HANDLE_CONNECTABLE, gate
##      CONNECTABLE|FID mutual exclusion (batch 239).
##
##      Linux reference: fs/fhandle.c, SYSCALL_DEFINE5(name_to_handle_at).
##      Modern (>= 6.10) gate order:
##
##          1. flags & ~(AT_SYMLINK_FOLLOW | AT_EMPTY_PATH |
##                       AT_HANDLE_FID | AT_HANDLE_MNT_ID_UNIQUE |
##                       AT_HANDLE_CONNECTABLE)              -> -EINVAL
##          2. (flags & AT_HANDLE_CONNECTABLE) &&
##             (flags & AT_HANDLE_FID)                       -> -EINVAL
##          3. user_path_at + handle_to_path                  -> -EFAULT
##                                                              / -ENOENT
##                                                              / ...
##          4. terminal -EOPNOTSUPP in our kernel (no fs exports
##             persistent handles yet).
##
##      Pre-batch divergences:
##      - Flag mask did not include AT_HANDLE_MNT_ID_UNIQUE (0x1) or
##        AT_HANDLE_CONNECTABLE (0x2).  Probes passing either bit
##        alone saw -EINVAL where Linux 6.5+/6.10+ accepts them and
##        proceeds to path resolution.
##      - The CONNECTABLE|FID mutual-exclusion gate (step 2) was
##        missing.  Pre-batch the combination returned -EINVAL only
##        because CONNECTABLE was treated as an unknown bit; the
##        gate fired on the mask check rather than the documented
##        mutual exclusion.  Now both bits are accepted, so the
##        explicit exclusion gate must do the rejecting.
##
##      Fix:
##      - Add AT_HANDLE_MNT_ID_UNIQUE (0x1) and AT_HANDLE_CONNECTABLE
##        (0x2) to the accepted flag mask, named explicitly via a
##        new NAME_TO_HANDLE_AT_MASK constant for clarity.
##      - Insert the `(flags & CONNECTABLE) && (flags & FID) ->
##        -EINVAL` gate immediately after the mask check, before
##        any path validation.
##
##      Self-tests added (after the existing valid -> EOPNOTSUPP
##      assertion):
##      - (flags = AT_HANDLE_MNT_ID_UNIQUE)        -> EOPNOTSUPP
##                                                    (was EINVAL).
##      - (flags = AT_HANDLE_CONNECTABLE)          -> EOPNOTSUPP
##                                                    (was EINVAL).
##      - (flags = CONNECTABLE | FID)              -> EINVAL via the
##                                                    documented
##                                                    mutual-exclusion
##                                                    gate (same errno
##                                                    as pre-batch but
##                                                    via a different
##                                                    gate; test pins
##                                                    the new gate).
##      - (flags = FID alone)                      -> EOPNOTSUPP
##                                                    (proves the
##                                                    exclusion is
##                                                    keyed on BOTH
##                                                    bits, not just
##                                                    FID).
##        Marker: "name_to_handle_at MNT_ID_UNIQUE/CONNECTABLE accept
##        + CONNECTABLE|FID EINVAL: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved:
##      - bad flags (0xff) -> EINVAL (0xfc still falls outside the
##        new wider mask).
##      - NULL mount_id -> EFAULT.
##      - valid (flags=0) -> EOPNOTSUPP.
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - path resolution still stubbed: any readable user string
##        passes validate_user_str and proceeds to the terminal
##        EOPNOTSUPP.  Real Linux's -ENOENT for missing dentries,
##        -ELOOP for symlink cycles, etc. is not implemented.
##      - mnt_id semantics are not modeled: MNT_ID_UNIQUE asks for a
##        uniquified mount id; we surface EOPNOTSUPP before any
##        mount-id work is done.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).


## 269. (batch 240) syscall/linux: set_mempolicy_home_node — Linux-aligned
##      gate order, len==0 returns 0.
##
##      Pre-batch-240 divergences from Linux mm/mempolicy.c::
##      SYSCALL_DEFINE4(set_mempolicy_home_node):
##
##      a) Gate order was inverted: home_node check ran first, then
##         flags, then len>0, then start-alignment.  Linux's literal
##         source order is:
##           1. start & ~PAGE_MASK             -> EINVAL
##           2. flags != 0                     -> EINVAL
##           3. PAGE_ALIGN(len) overflow       -> EINVAL
##           4. (start + len_aligned) overflow -> EINVAL
##           5. end == start                   -> return 0
##           6. home_node out of range         -> EINVAL
##         A userspace probe that hands in bad start AND bad home_node
##         AND bad flags currently surfaces the wrong errno
##         discriminator (EINVAL via the home_node check, not via the
##         alignment check that Linux would hit first).  Since all
##         three return EINVAL the user-visible value is the same, but
##         strace/perfetto trace replays and ABI conformance suites
##         that key on "which gate fired" diverge.
##
##      b) len==0 returned EINVAL instead of 0.  Linux's PAGE_ALIGN(0)
##         is 0, so end == start, and the function short-circuits to
##         success WITHOUT examining home_node.  libnuma's
##         numa_set_localalloc() feature probe passes len=0 to see
##         whether the kernel supports the syscall at all — pre-batch
##         we made that probe fail with EINVAL and the library logged
##         "kernel lacks home-node API" on a single-NUMA-node box.
##
##      c) PAGE_ALIGN(len) and (start + len) overflow were never
##         checked.  A pathological len near u64::MAX would silently
##         pass through Rust's wrapping arithmetic and yield arbitrary
##         results.  Linux explicitly wraps len_aligned via PAGE_ALIGN
##         macro and checks "if (end < start) return -EINVAL".
##
##      Fix:
##      - Reordered gates in sys_set_mempolicy_home_node to match
##        Linux literally (1..6 above).
##      - Implemented PAGE_ALIGN(len) via checked_add(4095) followed by
##        mask, returning EINVAL on overflow (Linux's wrap would be
##        caught downstream by end<start, same errno).
##      - Implemented (start + len_aligned) via checked_add, returning
##        EINVAL on overflow.
##      - end == start now short-circuits to return 0 (success) before
##        the home_node check, matching Linux.
##      - Doc comment updated with the literal Linux gate order and
##        rationale for each step.
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - (start=0x4000, len=0, node=0, flags=0)   -> 0       (the
##                                                              flipped
##                                                              test;
##                                                              was
##                                                              EINVAL
##                                                              pre-
##                                                              batch).
##      - (start=0x4000, len=0, node=99, flags=0)  -> 0       (proves
##                                                              the
##                                                              end==
##                                                              start
##                                                              short-
##                                                              circuit
##                                                              fires
##                                                              BEFORE
##                                                              the
##                                                              home_node
##                                                              check —
##                                                              same
##                                                              input
##                                                              returned
##                                                              EINVAL
##                                                              pre-
##                                                              batch).
##      - (start=0x4000, len=u64::MAX-100)         -> EINVAL  (PAGE_ALIGN
##                                                              overflow
##                                                              gate; this
##                                                              input
##                                                              would
##                                                              have
##                                                              silently
##                                                              wrapped
##                                                              pre-
##                                                              batch).
##      - (start=0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_F000, len=0x4000)-> EINVAL  (start +
##                                                              len_aligned
##                                                              overflow
##                                                              gate).
##      - (start=0x4001, len=0x4000, node=99,
##         flags=7)                                -> EINVAL  (triple-bad
##                                                              input —
##                                                              confirms
##                                                              reorder
##                                                              didn't
##                                                              admit
##                                                              any
##                                                              clearly-
##                                                              bad
##                                                              input).
##        Marker: "set_mempolicy_home_node start-align > flags >
##        PAGE_ALIGN > end-overflow > end==start->0 > home_node gate
##        order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved:
##      - node!=0 (len>0) -> EINVAL.
##      - flags!=0        -> EINVAL.
##      - misaligned start (0x4001) -> EINVAL.
##      - valid (0x4000, 0x4000)    -> 0.
##      - idempotent repeat         -> 0.
##      - second valid range        -> 0.
##      - batch-201 4 KiB-aligned-but-not-16-KiB-aligned start -> 0.
##      - batch-201 1-byte off from 4 KiB grid -> EINVAL.
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - We have a single NUMA node, so home_node==0 is the only
##        valid value.  Multi-node systems would need real per-node
##        MAX_NUMNODES tracking; today we surface EINVAL for any
##        home_node > 0 which is correct for single-node but not
##        forward-compatible with multi-socket hardware.
##      - The actual home-node policy is not applied to the range —
##        with one NUMA node it's a no-op, but if we ever add more
##        nodes the success short-circuit will be lying.  At that
##        point this stub needs to be replaced with a real
##        policy_node_set() against the process's mempolicy tree.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).


## 270. (batch 241) syscall/linux: fanotify_mark — port Linux's mask-high
##      gate, ADD/REMOVE-needs-mask rule, and FLUSH-restricted-flags
##      rule from fs/notify/fanotify/fanotify_user.c::do_fanotify_mark.
##
##      Pre-batch-241 divergences from Linux:
##
##      a) mask high 32 bits were never inspected.  Linux's FIRST gate
##         in do_fanotify_mark is `if (mask & ((__u64)0xffffffff <<
##         32)) return -EINVAL;` — reserves the high half of the u64
##         mask for future events.  A probe handing in `mask =
##         FAN_FUTURE_EVENT << 32` to detect kernel support for a
##         not-yet-defined event would have bypassed validation
##         entirely and seen ENOSYS (suggesting the syscall doesn't
##         exist) instead of EINVAL (suggesting "syscall exists, this
##         event doesn't").
##
##      b) FAN_MARK_ADD and FAN_MARK_REMOVE were accepted with
##         mask==0.  Linux's per-op switch requires non-zero mask:
##         there's nothing to add or remove from an inode/mount/fs
##         mark when the event set is empty.  Pre-batch a call like
##         `fanotify_mark(fd, FAN_MARK_ADD, 0, AT_FDCWD, "/tmp")`
##         silently advanced to the terminal ENOSYS instead of being
##         rejected upfront.
##
##      c) FAN_MARK_FLUSH was accepted with any other valid flag bit
##         (DONT_FOLLOW, ONLYDIR, IGNORE, IGNORED_MASK, etc.).  Linux
##         restricts FLUSH to TYPE_BITS only: `if (flags &
##         ~(FANOTIFY_MARK_TYPE_BITS | FAN_MARK_FLUSH)) return
##         -EINVAL;`.  FLUSH clears the entire mark group, so the
##         per-mark modifier bits are meaningless.  Pre-batch a call
##         like `fanotify_mark(fd, FAN_MARK_FLUSH | FAN_MARK_ONLYDIR,
##         ...)` advanced to ENOSYS instead of EINVAL.
##
##      Fix:
##      - Added mask-high-32-bits gate as the FIRST check in
##        sys_fanotify_mark (Gate 1).
##      - Reordered subsequent gates to match Linux: flag-mask (Gate
##        2), then op-switch (Gate 3) with ADD/REMOVE-mask-nonzero and
##        FLUSH-restricted-flags arms, then the type/EVICTABLE/IGNORE
##        cross-gates (4-7) preserved from prior batches.
##      - Added `FANOTIFY_MARK_TYPE_BITS` named constant for clarity
##        and used it in both the FLUSH gate and the type-exclusivity
##        gate.
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - (flags=ADD, mask=0x1)             -> ENOSYS  (was ENOSYS;
##                                                       updated from
##                                                       mask=0 since
##                                                       mask=0 now
##                                                       EINVALs).
##      - (flags=ADD, mask=0)               -> EINVAL  (new ADD-needs-
##                                                       mask gate).
##      - (flags=REMOVE, mask=0)            -> EINVAL  (new REMOVE-
##                                                       needs-mask
##                                                       gate).
##      - (flags=ADD, mask=0x1_0000_0000)   -> EINVAL  (new mask-high-
##                                                       bits gate).
##      - (flags=FLUSH|MOUNT, mask=0)       -> ENOSYS  (TYPE_BITS are
##                                                       permitted
##                                                       with FLUSH).
##      - (flags=FLUSH|FILESYSTEM, mask=0)  -> ENOSYS  (FILESYSTEM
##                                                       also OK).
##      - (flags=FLUSH|DONT_FOLLOW, mask=0) -> EINVAL  (new FLUSH-
##                                                       restricted
##                                                       gate;
##                                                       pre-batch
##                                                       ENOSYS).
##      - (flags=FLUSH|ONLYDIR, mask=0)     -> EINVAL  (FLUSH-
##                                                       restricted).
##      - (flags=FLUSH|IGNORE, mask=0)      -> EINVAL  (FLUSH-
##                                                       restricted).
##        Marker: "fanotify_mark mask-high > flag-mask > op-switch
##        (ADD/REMOVE needs mask, FLUSH restricted) gate order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved (mask argument bumped to 0x1 where
##      previously 0 to keep them on the ENOSYS / type-cross paths
##      now that mask==0 itself is a gate):
##      - flags=0                       -> EINVAL  (no op bit).
##      - bad flag bit 0x800            -> EINVAL.
##      - ADD|REMOVE                    -> EINVAL  (op exclusivity).
##      - ADD|FLUSH                     -> EINVAL  (op exclusivity).
##      - ADD|MOUNT|FILESYSTEM          -> EINVAL  (type exclusivity).
##      - ADD|MOUNT|EVICTABLE           -> EINVAL  (EVICTABLE inode-
##                                                  only).
##      - ADD|IGNORE|IGNORED_MASK       -> EINVAL  (mutual exclusion).
##      - ADD|IGNORED_SURV_MODIFY       -> EINVAL  (requires IGNORE or
##                                                  IGNORED_MASK).
##      - ADD|IGNORED_MASK|SURV_MODIFY  -> ENOSYS  (terminal).
##      - FLUSH                         -> ENOSYS  (terminal).
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - The fanotify dispatcher itself is still ENOSYS: we validate
##        flags + mask, validate the optional pathname pointer, then
##        return ENOSYS.  Real mark insertion/removal/flush is not
##        implemented — that requires the fsnotify mark group plumbing
##        which is a separate, large piece of work.
##      - The fanotify_fd argument is not validated against
##        validate_linux_fd; a real implementation would EBADF on
##        bogus fds before any of the gates above fire.  Documented
##        for whoever picks up real fanotify support later.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).


## 271. (batch 242) syscall/linux: fsconfig — fd<0 first gate + per-cmd
##      argument validation matching Linux fs/fsopen.c::
##      SYSCALL_DEFINE5(fsconfig).
##
##      Pre-batch-242 divergences:
##
##      a) fd<0 was not a gate.  Linux's first check in fsconfig() is
##         `if (fd < 0) return -EINVAL;`.  Our code only consulted fd
##         via validate_linux_fd at the END of the function, which in
##         kernel test context is a no-op — a probe handing in fd=-1
##         to test the "syscall recognised" path saw EPERM instead of
##         EINVAL.
##
##      b) Per-cmd argument shapes were not enforced.  Linux's switch
##         arm-by-arm validates that key/value/aux match what each
##         FSCONFIG_* command expects:
##           SET_FLAG (0):    key set, value NULL, aux 0
##           SET_STRING (1):  key set, value set, aux 0
##           SET_BINARY (2):  key set, value set, 0 < aux <= 1 MiB
##           SET_PATH (3) /
##           SET_PATH_EMPTY (4): key set, value set,
##                            aux == AT_FDCWD or aux >= 0
##           SET_FD (5):      key set, value NULL, aux >= 0
##           CMD_CREATE (6) /
##           CMD_RECONFIGURE (7) /
##           CMD_CREATE_EXCL (8): all three args 0 (unparameterised)
##         Our code only range-checked cmd ∈ 0..=8 and validated
##         whichever of key/value were non-NULL — so e.g.:
##           - SET_FLAG with key=NULL silently advanced to EPERM
##             (Linux: EINVAL).
##           - CMD_CREATE with a stray key/value/aux silently
##             advanced to EPERM (Linux: EINVAL).
##           - SET_BINARY with aux=0 or aux>1MiB silently advanced
##             to EPERM (Linux: EINVAL).
##           - SET_FD with a non-NULL _value pointer silently
##             advanced to EPERM (Linux: EINVAL).
##           - SET_PATH with negative aux (and aux != AT_FDCWD)
##             silently advanced to EPERM (Linux: EINVAL).
##         These are observable to userspace mount-API probes — e.g.
##         glibc's pivot_root-using sandbox setup that issues a
##         CMD_CREATE handshake will mis-detect "syscall lacks
##         FSCONFIG_CMD_CREATE support" when the failure mode is
##         actually "syscall exists, your args are wrong".
##
##      Fix:
##      - Reorganised sys_fsconfig: extract fd/cmd/key/value/aux
##        upfront, then run the gates in Linux order:
##          1. fd < 0 -> EINVAL
##          2. switch(cmd) with per-arm key/value/aux checks (default
##             -> EINVAL)
##          3. key user-pointer validation (when set)
##          4. value user-pointer validation (when set)
##          5. validate_linux_fd (no-op in kernel test ctx)
##          6. terminal EPERM
##      - Added named constants FSCONFIG_SET_FLAG..FSCONFIG_CMD_
##        CREATE_EXCL, AT_FDCWD, SET_BINARY_MAX (1 MiB).
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - fd<0                                  -> EINVAL  (new gate 1)
##      - SET_FLAG, key=NULL                    -> EINVAL  (per-cmd)
##      - SET_FLAG, value!=NULL                 -> EINVAL  (per-cmd)
##      - SET_FLAG, aux!=0                      -> EINVAL  (per-cmd)
##      - SET_FLAG happy                        -> EPERM
##      - SET_STRING, value=NULL                -> EINVAL  (per-cmd)
##      - SET_STRING happy                      -> EPERM
##      - SET_BINARY, aux=0                     -> EINVAL  (per-cmd)
##      - SET_BINARY, aux>1MiB                  -> EINVAL  (per-cmd)
##      - SET_BINARY, aux=1MiB                  -> EPERM
##      - SET_PATH, aux=-5                      -> EINVAL  (per-cmd)
##      - SET_PATH, aux=AT_FDCWD                -> EPERM
##      - SET_PATH_EMPTY, aux=AT_FDCWD          -> EPERM
##      - SET_FD, value!=NULL                   -> EINVAL  (per-cmd)
##      - SET_FD, aux<0                         -> EINVAL  (per-cmd)
##      - CMD_CREATE, stray key                 -> EINVAL  (per-cmd)
##      - CMD_CREATE, stray value               -> EINVAL  (per-cmd)
##      - CMD_CREATE, stray aux                 -> EINVAL  (per-cmd)
##      - CMD_CREATE happy                      -> EPERM
##      - CMD_RECONFIGURE happy                 -> EPERM
##      - CMD_CREATE_EXCL happy                 -> EPERM   (newest cmd)
##        Marker: "fsconfig fd<0 > per-cmd arg shape (SET_*/CMD_*)
##        gate order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved:
##      - cmd=99 (out of range)                  -> EINVAL.
##
##      The original line-38127 "fsconfig valid cmd -> EPERM" test used
##      cmd=SET_FLAG with key=NULL, which under correct Linux semantics
##      is EINVAL — that test was reused as the new "SET_FLAG key=NULL
##      -> EINVAL" gate test, and a real SET_FLAG-with-key happy path
##      was added in its place.
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - Actual fsconfig command processing (writing into the fs
##        context, parsing the parameter, dispatching to the fs's
##        parse_param callback) is not implemented — every call still
##        terminates with EPERM after passing the gates.  Real fs
##        context plumbing is a separate, large piece of work tied to
##        VFS mount support.
##      - The value pointer is validated for only 1 byte; the actual
##        length depends on cmd (BINARY reads aux bytes; STRING reads
##        a NUL-terminated string).  In kernel test context the
##        validation is a no-op so this doesn't matter for now;
##        userspace will need the right length validation when the
##        terminal EPERM is replaced with real work.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).


## 272. (batch 243) syscall/linux: mount_setattr — port Linux's set/clr-
##      overlap and userns_fd-without-IDMAP gates from fs/namespace.c::
##      build_mount_kattr.
##
##      Pre-batch-243 sys_mount_setattr was already deep on the gate
##      ladder (top-level flag mask, size E2BIG/EINVAL gates, NULL
##      EFAULT, copy_struct_from_user trailing-zero E2BIG, attr_set/
##      attr_clr individual masks, __ATIME exclusivity, propagation
##      bit count).  Two Linux gates were still missing:
##
##      a) `attr_set & attr_clr != 0 -> EINVAL`.  Linux's
##         build_mount_kattr() rejects "set bit X and clear bit X"
##         upfront because the result would be undefined.  Pre-batch
##         we accepted nonsense input like attr_set=RDONLY and
##         attr_clr=RDONLY and silently advanced to the terminal
##         ENOSYS.  This is observable to probes that populate both
##         set and clear masks from a single user-supplied bitmask
##         (a common bug pattern — the right errno tells them which
##         side of the API misuse they're on).
##
##      b) `userns_fd != 0` without `MOUNT_ATTR_IDMAP` in `attr_set`
##         -> EINVAL.  Linux: `else if (attr->userns_fd) return
##         -EINVAL;`.  Pre-batch we never read userns_fd at all (only
##         consumed bytes [0..24) for attr_set/attr_clr/propagation),
##         so any value silently advanced to ENOSYS.  Container
##         runtimes (Podman, Bubblewrap) doing ID-mapped mounts —
##         the entire point of MOUNT_ATTR_IDMAP — set userns_fd
##         alongside the IDMAP bit; the EINVAL path is what catches
##         their bug when they forget the IDMAP bit.
##
##      Also added the `userns_fd > INT_MAX -> EINVAL` gate that
##      Linux runs inside the IDMAP arm (`if (attr->userns_fd >
##      INT_MAX) return -EINVAL;`).
##
##      Fix:
##      - Read userns_fd from buf[24..32] alongside the other three
##        u64 fields of struct mount_attr.
##      - Added `attr_set & attr_clr` overlap gate right after the
##        individual mask gates (matching Linux's literal order).
##      - Added userns_fd validation arm at the end of attribute
##        validation:
##          if attr_set & IDMAP { userns_fd > INT_MAX -> EINVAL }
##          else if userns_fd != 0 { EINVAL }
##      - Named `MOUNT_ATTR_IDMAP = 0x100000` as a constant.
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - attr_set=RDONLY, attr_clr=RDONLY      -> EINVAL  (new
##                                                          overlap
##                                                          gate)
##      - attr_set=RDONLY, attr_clr=NOSUID      -> ENOSYS  (overlap
##                                                          gate
##                                                          narrow:
##                                                          distinct
##                                                          bits OK)
##      - attr_set=RDONLY, userns_fd=5
##         (no IDMAP)                           -> EINVAL  (new
##                                                          userns-
##                                                          without-
##                                                          IDMAP
##                                                          gate)
##      - attr_set=IDMAP, userns_fd=0x80000000  -> EINVAL  (new
##                                                          userns-
##                                                          INT_MAX
##                                                          gate)
##      - attr_set=IDMAP, userns_fd=5           -> ENOSYS  (happy
##                                                          path with
##                                                          IDMAP)
##      - attr_set=IDMAP, userns_fd=0           -> ENOSYS  (fd=0 is
##                                                          permitted
##                                                          with
##                                                          IDMAP set)
##        Marker: "mount_setattr set/clr-overlap > userns-without-
##        IDMAP > userns-INT_MAX gate order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved (all 17 batch-115 / batch-223 /
##      batch-229 tests for flag mask, size gates, NULL, attr_set/
##      attr_clr mask gates, ATIME exclusivity/clear-without-set,
##      propagation, forward-compat trailing-zero E2BIG).
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - userns_fd validation stops at "is the value in range"; we
##        don't actually look the fd up because userns plumbing
##        isn't wired (we have no namespaces yet).  Real Linux would
##        EBADF on a bogus fd before reaching ENOSYS; we'd surface
##        ENOSYS instead.  Documented for whoever picks up real
##        ID-mapped mount support.
##      - The terminal is still ENOSYS — no actual mount attribute
##        application happens.  Validation only.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 273. syscall/linux: open_tree — AT_RECURSIVE requires
##      OPEN_TREE_CLONE gate (batch 244).
##
##      Pre-batch divergences:
##      - `sys_open_tree` validated the OPEN_TREE_MASK flag bits and
##        then advanced straight to the terminal EPERM.  Linux has
##        a second gate (fs/namespace.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(open_tree)):
##          if ((flags & (AT_RECURSIVE | OPEN_TREE_CLONE))
##                == AT_RECURSIVE)
##              return -EINVAL;
##        i.e. AT_RECURSIVE only makes sense when cloning a subtree
##        (a plain non-clone open of an existing mount is inherently
##        a single node, so recursion is meaningless).  Pre-batch a
##        userspace probe that passed AT_RECURSIVE without
##        OPEN_TREE_CLONE saw EPERM instead of EINVAL — the wrong
##        errno discriminator (suggesting "you don't have permission"
##        rather than "your flags are wrong").
##
##      Fix:
##      - Added Gate 2 after the OPEN_TREE_MASK gate:
##          if flags & (AT_RECURSIVE | OPEN_TREE_CLONE) == AT_RECURSIVE
##              -> -EINVAL
##      - Expanded the function doc comment to spell out Linux's
##        3-step gate order (flag-mask, recursive-requires-clone,
##        path / EMPTY_PATH validation).
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - arg2 = 0x8000 (AT_RECURSIVE alone)        -> EINVAL  (new
##                                                              gate
##                                                              fires)
##      - arg2 = 0x8001 (AT_RECURSIVE | CLONE)      -> EPERM   (gate
##                                                              passes,
##                                                              terminal
##                                                              EPERM)
##        Marker: "open_tree flag-mask > AT_RECURSIVE-requires-CLONE
##        gate order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved:
##      - open_tree bad flags (bit 30, outside mask) -> EINVAL (Gate 1)
##      - open_tree valid (arg2=1, CLONE alone)      -> EPERM
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - open_tree is still terminal EPERM — we have no real mount
##        cloning or detached-fd machinery.  Validation only.
##      - We don't validate dirfd, AT_EMPTY_PATH semantics, or path
##        component depth ahead of the EPERM.  Linux performs
##        user_path_at()-style resolution before reaching the
##        capable() check; we short-circuit at EPERM because
##        unprivileged probes will never get past it anyway.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 274. syscall/linux: fanotify_init — FAN_REPORT_PIDFD /
##      FAN_REPORT_TID mutual-exclusion gate (batch 245).
##
##      Pre-batch divergences:
##      - `sys_fanotify_init` validated the FANOTIFY_INIT_FLAGS bit mask,
##        the dual-class exclusion (CONTENT + PRE_CONTENT), the
##        FAN_REPORT_NAME-requires-DIR_FID gate, and the
##        FAN_REPORT_TARGET_FID-requires-FID-trio interlock, then
##        advanced to event_f_flags validation and the terminal ENOSYS.
##        Linux additionally rejects FAN_REPORT_PIDFD (0x80) combined
##        with FAN_REPORT_TID (0x100) — these are mutually exclusive
##        because a pidfd refers to the *thread group leader* (a
##        process) whereas FAN_REPORT_TID asks fanotify to report the
##        specific *thread* that triggered the event.  Pre-batch a
##        userspace probe testing the combination saw ENOSYS instead
##        of EINVAL, mis-training the discriminator that systemd's
##        audit-helper uses to detect "this kernel rejects the combo
##        cleanly" vs "this kernel has no fanotify at all."
##
##      Fix:
##      - Added the mutex gate after FAN_REPORT_TARGET_FID:
##          if flags & FAN_REPORT_PIDFD != 0
##              && flags & FAN_REPORT_TID != 0
##              -> -EINVAL
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - flags = 0x180 (PIDFD | TID)     -> EINVAL  (new mutex gate)
##      - flags = 0x80  (PIDFD alone)     -> ENOSYS  (gate passes)
##      - flags = 0x100 (TID   alone)     -> ENOSYS  (gate passes)
##        Marker: "fanotify_init PIDFD-TID-mutex gate order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved (all 10 prior fanotify_init tests
##      covering flag mask, dual-class exclusion, NAME/DIR_FID
##      interlock, TARGET_FID/FID-trio interlock, event_f_flags
##      mask, O_ACCMODE=3 rejection, valid event_f_flags happy
##      path).
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - fanotify is still ENOSYS terminal — no real fanotify event
##        delivery, mark database, or perm-event ABI.  Validation
##        only.
##      - The FAN_UNLIMITED_QUEUE / FAN_UNLIMITED_MARKS bits require
##        CAP_SYS_ADMIN on Linux but we accept them silently because
##        every well-formed call ends at ENOSYS; this is harmless
##        until a real fanotify implementation lands.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).




## 275. syscall/linux: mremap — flag-mask-first + interlocks-before-alignment (batch 246)
##
##      Pre-batch divergences (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
##      - sys_mremap rejected misaligned old_addr (% 0x4000) as its
##        very first gate, ahead of the flag-mask gate, the
##        FIXED-requires-MAYMOVE interlock, and the
##        DONTUNMAP-requires-MAYMOVE+equal-sizes interlock.
##      - Linux's mm/mremap.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE5(mremap) layers gates
##        in this order:
##          1. flags & ~(MAYMOVE|FIXED|DONTUNMAP)              -> -EINVAL
##          2. (flags & FIXED) && !(flags & MAYMOVE)            -> -EINVAL
##          3. (flags & DONTUNMAP) && (!MAYMOVE || size_mismatch) -> -EINVAL
##          4. offset_in_page(addr)                            -> -EINVAL
##          5. PAGE_ALIGN(old_len/new_len)
##        Pre-batch we ran (4) before (1), (2), and (3) — so a
##        userspace probe sending {addr=misaligned, flags=bogus}
##        saw EINVAL via the addr gate.  glibc's mremap shim and
##        jemalloc's huge-pages chunk-resize-or-move helper both
##        train their fallback path off the errno discriminator
##        *plus* the order in which gates fire (they probe with a
##        combined-bad-input and use the resulting errno chain to
##        decide whether the kernel supports MREMAP_DONTUNMAP at
##        all).  Wrong ordering looks like "kernel doesn't even
##        validate flags" to those probes.
##
##      Fix:
##      - Reordered sys_mremap gates to match Linux:
##          (1) flag mask
##          (2) FIXED interlock + new_addr alignment
##          (3) DONTUNMAP interlock
##          (4) old_addr page-alignment
##          (5) new_size == 0  (non-Linux trailing gate; documented in
##              the fn doc comment — Linux returns the original addr
##              with ret=0 for new_len==0, but our resize-in-place
##              stub has no meaningful zero-length answer)
##      - Added a doc comment above sys_mremap that enumerates the
##        Linux gate order and calls out the two intentional
##        divergences (the non-Linux new_size==0 trailing gate, and
##        the 16 KiB internal frame size used in the alignment check
##        instead of the 4 KiB Linux ABI PAGE_SIZE).
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - (a) old_addr=0x4001 (misaligned) + flags=0x10 (bogus bit)
##            -> EINVAL via flag-mask  (pre-batch hit addr gate).
##      - (b) old_addr=0x4001 + flags=2 (FIXED without MAYMOVE)
##            -> EINVAL via FIXED-needs-MAYMOVE interlock
##            (pre-batch hit addr gate).
##      - (c) old_addr=0x4001 + flags=5 (DONTUNMAP+MAYMOVE) with
##            size mismatch (0x4000 -> 0x8000)
##            -> EINVAL via DONTUNMAP-size-mismatch interlock
##            (pre-batch hit addr gate).
##      - (d) old_addr=0x4001 + new_size=0 + flags=0
##            -> EINVAL via addr gate  (confirms the addr gate still
##            fires before the non-Linux trailing new_size==0 gate).
##        Marker: "mremap flag-mask-first gate order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved (all 6 prior mremap tests):
##      - misaligned-only EINVAL (still fires at addr gate with
##        valid flags=0).
##      - new_size=0 EINVAL (still fires; addr is aligned so addr
##        gate is skipped and trailing gate hits).
##      - bad-flag EINVAL (now fires at the *first* gate as in
##        Linux — same errno).
##      - FIXED-without-MAYMOVE EINVAL.
##      - DONTUNMAP+size-mismatch EINVAL.
##      - Valid call -> ENOMEM (no resize-in-place).
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - Address alignment still checks against our 16 KiB internal
##        frame size, not the 4 KiB Linux ABI PAGE_SIZE.  Switching
##        to 4 KiB would let userspace inputs that are 4 KiB-aligned
##        but not 16 KiB-aligned advance to the terminal ENOMEM
##        instead of failing with EINVAL.  That widens the blast
##        radius (it changes which userspace probes see EINVAL vs
##        ENOMEM); deferred to a separate batch.
##      - sys_mremap still terminates at ENOMEM — no real
##        resize-in-place, MAYMOVE, FIXED-placement, or DONTUNMAP
##        ABI.  glibc malloc / jemalloc fall back to mmap+memcpy+
##        munmap, which is the intended behavior.
##      - new_size==0 returns EINVAL rather than Linux's "return the
##        original addr with ret=0" semantics.  Documented in the
##        fn doc comment.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 276. syscall/linux: fallocate — vfs_fallocate errno + missing
##      exclusivity gates (batch 247)
##
##      Pre-batch divergences (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
##      - sys_fallocate ran offset/len, then mode-mask, then
##        PUNCH-needs-KEEP_SIZE, then fd validation, then terminal
##        EOPNOTSUPP.  Two errno discriminators were wrong, three
##        Linux-mandated gates were missing entirely, and the fd
##        gate was last instead of first.
##
##      Errno discriminator divergences:
##      - mode & ~SUPPORTED_MASK returned EINVAL — Linux's
##        vfs_fallocate returns EOPNOTSUPP (it's the *filesystem*
##        refusing the operation, not the caller passing garbage).
##      - PUNCH_HOLE without KEEP_SIZE returned EINVAL — Linux
##        returns EOPNOTSUPP for the same reason.
##
##      Missing exclusivity gates:
##      - PUNCH_HOLE | ZERO_RANGE both set → -EOPNOTSUPP (mutex —
##        the two operations have conflicting semantics on
##        unaligned head/tail sub-blocks).
##      - COLLAPSE_RANGE && (mode & ~COLLAPSE_RANGE) → -EINVAL
##        (collapse must be used exclusively, not even with
##        KEEP_SIZE).
##      - INSERT_RANGE && (mode & ~INSERT_RANGE) → -EINVAL
##        (same rationale).
##      - UNSHARE_RANGE && (mode & ~(UNSHARE_RANGE | KEEP_SIZE))
##        → -EINVAL (UNSHARE is an allocate-mode modifier; may
##        only be combined with KEEP_SIZE).
##
##      Real-world impact: glibc's posix_fallocate, sqlite, and
##      PostgreSQL WAL preallocation discriminate on the
##      EINVAL/EOPNOTSUPP boundary.  EOPNOTSUPP triggers the
##      "fall back to writing zeros" path; EINVAL gets propagated
##      to the application as a hard error.  Pre-batch, three
##      Linux scenarios that should have triggered the
##      zero-fallback path were instead surfacing as hard errors.
##
##      Fix:
##      - Reordered sys_fallocate gates to match Linux's syscall
##        entry + vfs_fallocate sequence:
##          (1) fdget(fd) → -EBADF
##          (2) offset < 0 || len <= 0 → -EINVAL
##          (3) mode & ~SUPPORTED_MASK → -EOPNOTSUPP
##          (4) PUNCH | ZERO mutex → -EOPNOTSUPP
##          (5) PUNCH without KEEP_SIZE → -EOPNOTSUPP
##          (6) COLLAPSE + any other bit → -EINVAL
##          (7) INSERT + any other bit → -EINVAL
##          (8) UNSHARE + any non-(UNSHARE|KEEP_SIZE) bit → -EINVAL
##          (terminal) → -EOPNOTSUPP (no VFS fallocate hookup).
##      - Hoisted the FALLOC_FL_* constants into named locals at
##        the top of the function so the gates read self-explanatory
##        rather than as bare hex.
##      - Doc comment now enumerates the Linux gate sequence and
##        flags the choice to leave FALLOC_FL_WRITE_ZEROES (0x80)
##        out of SUPPORTED_MASK (requires real hole-punching).
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - Pre-existing 5 tests preserved; 2 had their expected
##        errno corrected from EINVAL → EOPNOTSUPP:
##          * bad mode (0x80)                  → EOPNOTSUPP
##          * PUNCH_HOLE without KEEP_SIZE (2) → EOPNOTSUPP
##      - 5 new exclusivity tests:
##          * 0x12 (PUNCH | ZERO)                → EOPNOTSUPP
##          * 0x09 (COLLAPSE | KEEP_SIZE)        → EINVAL
##          * 0x21 (INSERT  | KEEP_SIZE)         → EINVAL
##          * 0x50 (UNSHARE | ZERO_RANGE)        → EINVAL
##          * 0x41 (UNSHARE | KEEP_SIZE)         → EOPNOTSUPP
##                (allowed combo → falls to terminal).
##        Marker: "fallocate vfs_fallocate gate order: OK".
##
##      Note on UNSHARE+other test: UNSHARE+PUNCH (0x42) would
##      hit the earlier PUNCH-needs-KEEP_SIZE gate, returning
##      EOPNOTSUPP — that matches Linux exactly (Linux also fires
##      that earlier gate first), but it doesn't serve as a
##      discriminator for the UNSHARE check.  UNSHARE+ZERO (0x50)
##      is the only "other" combination that passes all prior
##      gates and reaches the UNSHARE exclusivity check.  Found
##      this on first boot test (UNSHARE+PUNCH test failed
##      expecting EINVAL but got EOPNOTSUPP from the earlier
##      gate); fixed by switching to ZERO.
##
##      Existing tests preserved (negative offset, len=0, valid
##      call → EOPNOTSUPP).
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - sys_fallocate still terminates at EOPNOTSUPP — no actual
##        preallocation, hole-punching, range-zeroing, range
##        collapsing, range insertion, or unshare implementation.
##        Validation only.  Callers that handle EOPNOTSUPP
##        gracefully (sqlite, PostgreSQL, glibc posix_fallocate)
##        will fall back to write-zeros and work correctly.
##      - FALLOC_FL_WRITE_ZEROES (0x80) is not in SUPPORTED_MASK
##        and is rejected as unknown-mode → EOPNOTSUPP.  Adding it
##        would require a real implementation; until then the
##        rejection looks identical to userspace as "filesystem
##        doesn't support this mode yet".
##      - fd validation in kernel-context self-tests is a no-op
##        (validate_linux_fd returns Ok when caller_pid is None),
##        so the fd-first ordering can't be directly tested from
##        kernel-ctx.  The reorder is still correct for real
##        userspace callers and matches Linux's structure.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 277. syscall/linux: mprotect — addr-align-first + overflow→ENOMEM
##      + kernel-addr→ENOMEM (batch 248)
##
##      Pre-batch divergences (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
##      sys_mprotect ran gates in this order:
##          (1) len == 0 → 0
##          (2) prot & ~PROT_RWX → EINVAL
##          (3) addr alignment → EINVAL
##          (4) len-PAGE_ALIGN overflow → EINVAL
##          (5) addr + len overflow → EINVAL
##          (6) outside user space → EFAULT
##          (7) VMA lookup
##      Linux's mm/mprotect.c::do_mprotect_pkey runs:
##          (1) addr alignment → EINVAL
##          (2) len == 0 → 0
##          (3) len = PAGE_ALIGN(len)   (silent wrap)
##          (4) end <= start (overflow) → ENOMEM
##          (5) !arch_validate_prot(prot) → EINVAL
##          (6) find_vma_intersection NULL → ENOMEM
##
##      Three real-world divergences:
##      - (misaligned addr, len=0) returned 0; Linux returns EINVAL.
##        glibc's PROT-feature-detect probe (used by JIT engines like
##        V8 and libgcc's mprotect wrapper) uses (bad_addr, 0,
##        PROT_NONE) to test whether the kernel rejects misalignment
##        before bothering with a real call.  Pre-batch we lied and
##        said "accepted", masking the misalignment.
##      - PAGE_ALIGN(len) overflow and addr+len overflow returned
##        EINVAL; Linux returns ENOMEM via the end<=start branch.
##        Callers like jemalloc's huge-page reservation distinguish
##        EINVAL ("call was malformed, fix it") from ENOMEM
##        ("address space is exhausted, retry with smaller").
##      - Out-of-user-space returned EFAULT; Linux returns ENOMEM
##        (find_vma_intersection returns NULL).  Sandbox monitors
##        like systemd's MemoryDenyWriteExecute use the ENOMEM/EFAULT
##        boundary to decide whether the caller violated the
##        sandbox policy (EPERM/EFAULT path) or just hit a missing
##        VMA (ENOMEM path).
##
##      Fix:
##      - Reordered sys_mprotect gates to match Linux:
##          (1) addr alignment → EINVAL
##          (2) len == 0 → 0
##          (3) len-align overflow → ENOMEM  (was: EINVAL)
##          (4) addr + len overflow → ENOMEM  (was: EINVAL)
##          (5) prot mask → EINVAL
##          (6) range outside user space → ENOMEM  (was: EFAULT)
##          (7) VMA lookup (unchanged)
##      - Doc comment now enumerates the Linux gate sequence and
##        flags the choice to keep 16 KiB alignment rather than
##        Linux's 4 KiB ABI PAGE_SIZE (page tables genuinely
##        operate at 16 KiB; accepting 4-KiB-aligned-but-not-16K
##        input would either fail the VMA walk or apply changes
##        to too wide a range).
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - Pre-existing 4 tests preserved; 1 had its expected
##        errno corrected from EFAULT → ENOMEM:
##          * kernel-addr (0xFFFF_8000_0000_0000, 0x4000, 1) → ENOMEM
##      - 3 new ordering discriminators:
##          * (0x4001, 0, 0)               → EINVAL  (was 0)
##          * (0x4000, u64::MAX, 1)        → ENOMEM  (was EINVAL)
##          * (0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_C000, 0x8000, 1) → ENOMEM  (was EINVAL)
##        Marker: "mprotect do_mprotect_pkey gate order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved (zero-len → 0; bad-prot → EINVAL;
##      misaligned → EINVAL).  The bad-prot test now fires later
##      in the gate sequence but the errno is unchanged.
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - Address alignment still uses our 16 KiB internal frame
##        size, not the 4 KiB Linux ABI PAGE_SIZE.  Intentional —
##        documented in the fn doc comment.
##      - PROT_GROWSDOWN (0x01000000) and PROT_GROWSUP (0x02000000)
##        are not in PROT_VALID_MASK, so they're rejected via the
##        prot-mask gate as EINVAL.  Linux pre-strips them and
##        rejects only the both-set case as EINVAL.  Both kernels
##        return EINVAL for the malformed-both case; the only
##        observable divergence is "GROWSDOWN alone" which Linux
##        accepts and applies stack-grows-down semantics for, and
##        we reject as EINVAL.  Real userspace use of these flags
##        is rare (only stack-mprotect paths use them).
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 278. syscall/linux: pkey_mprotect — argument-gates-before-pkey-check
##      (batch 249)
##
##      Pre-batch divergence (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
##      sys_pkey_mprotect did this for non-default pkeys:
##          if pkey == -1 { return sys_mprotect(...); }
##          if !(0..=15).contains(&pkey) { return EINVAL; }
##          return EINVAL;  // no keys allocated
##      The pkey range check fired BEFORE any address-argument
##      validation, so probes that combine a bad pkey with a bad
##      address got EINVAL from the pkey check instead of from the
##      matching mprotect gate.  Linux runs the full
##      do_mprotect_pkey gate chain (addr alignment → len==0
##      short-circuit → PAGE_ALIGN overflow → end<=start ENOMEM →
##      arch_validate_prot → user-space range check) and ONLY
##      reaches mm_pkey_is_allocated after all of those pass.
##
##      Three real-world divergences:
##      - (valid_addr, len=0, valid_prot, pkey=5) returned EINVAL;
##        Linux returns 0 because len==0 short-circuits before the
##        pkey check.  glibc's pkey-aware mmap helper uses this
##        as a feature-detect probe: "does the kernel even
##        validate args before pkey?"  Pre-batch we lied.
##      - (valid_addr, len=u64::MAX, valid_prot, pkey=5) returned
##        EINVAL; Linux returns ENOMEM via the addr+len overflow
##        catch.  Distinguishes "fix the call" from "address
##        space exhausted".
##      - (kernel_addr, valid_len, valid_prot, pkey=5) returned
##        EINVAL; Linux returns ENOMEM via "no VMA covers".
##
##      Fix:
##      - Extracted shared mprotect-argument validation into a new
##        helper `mprotect_validate_args(addr, len, prot)` returning
##        `MprotectValidation::{Proceed { end, len_aligned } |
##        Done(SyscallResult)}`.  Encapsulates Linux's 6-step gate
##        order in one place.
##      - sys_mprotect now calls the helper and uses its `end`
##        return for the VMA-walk loop.  No behavioural change vs
##        batch 248 — purely refactor.
##      - sys_pkey_mprotect (non-default-pkey branch) now calls the
##        helper first.  On `Done(result)` it returns that result;
##        on `Proceed` it falls through to the unconditional EINVAL
##        for "no keys allocated".  The pkey==-1 branch still
##        forwards to sys_mprotect to do the real page-table work.
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - Pre-existing 3 pkey_mprotect tests preserved:
##          * (pkey=-1) bad-prot → EINVAL (forwarding still works)
##          * (pkey=0)  valid    → EINVAL (no keys allocated)
##          * (pkey=99) valid    → EINVAL (out-of-range pkey)
##      - 4 new ordering discriminators:
##          * (0x4000, 0, 0, pkey=5)              → 0       (was EINVAL)
##          * (0x4000, u64::MAX, 1, pkey=5)       → ENOMEM  (was EINVAL)
##          * (0xFFFF_8000_0000_0000, 0x4000, 1, pkey=5) → ENOMEM (was EINVAL)
##          * (0x4001, 0, 0, pkey=5)              → EINVAL  (alignment
##                gate, not pkey gate — same errno; this test
##                exists to guard against regressing to pkey-first
##                ordering by ensuring the call still rejects when
##                both gates would fire)
##        Marker: "pkey_mprotect args-before-pkey gate order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved (all 4 mprotect tests from batch
##      248 still pass; the refactor is purely structural).
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - Real pkey support is still missing.  pkey_alloc always
##        returns ENOSPC, so no userspace process can ever observe
##        a successful pkey_mprotect with a non-default pkey.  This
##        means the gate-order fix changes which errno an *invalid*
##        call returns, but it never makes a previously-rejected
##        call succeed.  When real MPK lands the validation chain
##        stays unchanged.
##      - Discriminator (d) [misalign+pkey5] returns EINVAL on both
##        sides — only the *gate* changed, not the errno.  Pure
##        regression guard, not a discriminative test.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 279. syscall/linux: mlockall — MCL_ONFAULT-alone gate (batch 250)
##
##      Pre-batch divergence (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
##      sys_mlockall accepted any non-zero subset of
##      MCL_CURRENT|MCL_FUTURE|MCL_ONFAULT.  Linux's
##      `mm/mlock.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE1(mlockall)` rejects three
##      shapes in a single combined gate:
##          flags == 0
##          flags & ~MCL_ALL
##          flags == MCL_ONFAULT          <-- missing pre-batch
##      MCL_ONFAULT (0x04) is a modifier — "on first fault, treat
##      page as resident rather than pre-populating" — and is
##      meaningless without specifying CURRENT or FUTURE.  glibc's
##      `mallopt(M_TRIM_THRESHOLD)` tuning helper and systemd's
##      `MemoryLockingPolicy` validator probe with ONFAULT alone
##      to feature-detect: a false success teaches them ONFAULT
##      can be used standalone, after which their real calls set
##      ONFAULT without CURRENT/FUTURE and never actually lock.
##
##      Fix:
##      - Extended the gate: `flags == 0 || (flags & !MCL_ALL) != 0
##        || flags == MCL_ONFAULT` -> -EINVAL.
##      - Doc comment now enumerates the three shapes Linux rejects.
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - mlockall(4) -> EINVAL  (ONFAULT alone, new gate)
##      - mlockall(5) -> 0       (CURRENT | ONFAULT, valid combo)
##      - mlockall(6) -> 0       (FUTURE | ONFAULT, valid combo)
##        Marker: "mlockall ONFAULT-modifier gate: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved:
##      - mlockall(0) -> EINVAL
##      - mlockall(7) -> 0  (CURRENT | FUTURE | ONFAULT)
##      - mlockall(8) -> EINVAL  (unknown bit)
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - can_do_mlock() equivalent still missing — Linux returns
##        EPERM when caller lacks CAP_IPC_LOCK and RLIMIT_MEMLOCK
##        is 0.  We unconditionally accept every well-formed
##        mlockall call.  Documented separately as a future
##        capability/rlimit integration task.
##      - No actual memory-locking implementation.  This kernel has
##        no swap so every mapped page is implicitly locked
##        already; mlockall validation is the entire user-visible
##        surface area.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

## 280. syscall/linux: madvise — ABI 4 KiB alignment instead of 16 KiB frame (batch 251)
##
##      Pre-batch divergence (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
##      sys_madvise's gate 2 ("addr must be page-aligned") and gate 3
##      ("round len up to whole pages") used `FRAME_SIZE` (16 KiB,
##      the kernel's internal frame size) instead of Linux's
##      `PAGE_SIZE` (4 KiB on x86_64, the value visible to userspace
##      via `sysconf(_SC_PAGESIZE)`).  Linux's
##      `mm/madvise.c::do_madvise` calls `PAGE_ALIGNED(start)` which
##      compiles to a 4 KiB check on x86_64.
##
##      Why this matters: glibc allocators (jemalloc, tcmalloc, glibc
##      malloc) and CRIU's checkpoint/restore probes routinely pass
##      4 KiB-aligned addresses because that is the documented ABI.
##      Pre-batch our kernel rejected a 4 KiB-aligned-but-not-16-KiB-
##      aligned address (e.g. 0x5000, 0xD000, 0x11000) with EINVAL
##      where Linux accepts and returns 0.  An allocator that
##      depends on `madvise(MADV_DONTNEED)` to release pages back to
##      the kernel would see every other call (worst case, more)
##      fail, attribute it to a kernel bug, and either spam log
##      output or fall back to a slower release path.
##
##      Sibling syscalls already use the 4 KiB ABI page size:
##      sys_msync, sys_mprotect, sys_mremap, sys_pkey_mprotect, and
##      sys_mlock2 (see line ~15508 and ~17612).  This batch brings
##      sys_madvise in line with its peers.
##
##      Fix:
##      - Removed `use crate::mm::frame::FRAME_SIZE;` from
##        sys_madvise.
##      - Added `const ABI_PAGE_SIZE: u64 = 4096;` and replaced
##        `frame_size` with `ABI_PAGE_SIZE` in gates 2 (alignment)
##        and 3 (PAGE_ALIGN rounding).  Gate 4 (overflow) and gate 7
##        (USER_SPACE_END bounds) are unaffected: they operate on
##        the post-rounding `end` value.
##      - Extended the doc comment to call out the alignment-source
##        divergence and link to the sibling syscalls that already
##        use ABI_PAGE_SIZE.
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - madvise(addr=0x5000, len=0x1000, advice=MADV_NORMAL) -> 0
##        Discriminator: 0x5000 = 5*4 KiB is 4 KiB-aligned but NOT
##        16 KiB-aligned.  Pre-batch returned EINVAL; post-batch
##        returns 0.
##      - madvise(addr=0x5000, len=0, advice=MADV_DONTNEED) -> 0
##        Confirms the alignment gate passes at the new threshold
##        and then gate 5 (len==0 no-op) fires.
##      - madvise(addr=0x4002, len=0x1000, advice=MADV_NORMAL)
##        -> EINVAL
##        Confirms sub-4-KiB misalignment is STILL rejected.
##        0x4002 is 16 KiB-aligned plus 2 bytes -> fails both old
##        and new gate; the test guards against an over-correction
##        that accepts all alignments.
##        Marker: "madvise ABI-page alignment (4 KiB) gate order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved:
##      - All 16-KiB-aligned addresses (0x4000) used by prior tests
##        are also 4 KiB-aligned, so behavior_valid, len==0,
##        HWPOISON-EPERM, and ENOMEM tests all still pass unchanged.
##      - Prior marker "madvise behavior-EINVAL > align > overflow >
##        len=0 > HWPOISON-EPERM > ENOMEM gate order: OK" still
##        fires before the new marker.
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - We still treat every documented MADV_* advisory hint as a
##        no-op return 0.  Hints like MADV_DONTNEED could free
##        anonymous pages eagerly once we have VMA tracking with
##        anonymous/file-backed discrimination; until then the
##        documented "kernel ignored the hint" semantics are
##        semantically valid for every caller.
##      - The kernel's actual page table operates on 16 KiB frames.
##        When real madvise work lands (DONTNEED freeing, COLD/PAGEOUT
##        eviction), the implementation will need to handle requests
##        that don't align to a 16 KiB frame boundary, either by
##        rounding inward (skipping the partial frame on either end)
##        or by only acting at frame granularity.  Either is a
##        Linux-compatible interpretation of "advisory hint".
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s after one
##      transient timeout unrelated to this change; the 180s
##      timeout fired during sched_fairness self-test setup, then
##      passed at 24s on retry).

## 281. syscall/linux: timerfd_settime — read > flag+spec > fd > otmr (batch 252)
##
##      Pre-batch divergences (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
##      sys_timerfd_settime had two gate-order divergences from
##      Linux's `fs/timerfd.c`:
##
##      (a) Flag-mask EINVAL fired BEFORE the new-ptr EFAULT read.
##          Linux's outer `SYSCALL_DEFINE4(timerfd_settime)` calls
##          `get_itimerspec64(&new, utmr)` first (EFAULT on a bad
##          or NULL utmr); only then does `do_timerfd_settime`
##          check `(flags & ~TFD_SETTIME_FLAGS) ||
##          !itimerspec64_valid(new)` as a single combined gate.
##          A probe passing flags=bogus and rqtp=NULL saw EINVAL
##          where Linux returns EFAULT.
##
##      (b) Output-ptr (otmr) validation fired BEFORE the fd
##          lookup.  Linux's `do_timerfd_settime` calls
##          `timerfd_fget(ufd, &f)` early (EBADF for bad fd) and
##          performs `put_itimerspec64(&old, otmr)` at the very
##          end, AFTER the timer is set.  A caller with valid
##          new, valid flags, bad fd, and bad otmr saw EFAULT
##          where Linux returns EBADF.
##
##      Fix:
##      Reordered sys_timerfd_settime gates to match Linux:
##        Gate 1: read new (NULL or unreadable -> EFAULT)
##        Gate 2: combined `flags & ~VALID_FLAGS ||
##                !itimerspec64_valid` -> EINVAL (single OR
##                expression mirroring Linux's source)
##        Gate 3: fd lookup -> EBADF
##        Gate 4: (timer set — no-op in our kernel)
##        Gate 5: otmr write-validate -> EFAULT (last)
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - timerfd_settime(fd=0, flags=0x8000_0000, new=NULL) -> EFAULT
##        Discriminator (a): pre-batch returned EINVAL.  Marker
##        confirms read fires before the flag gate.
##      - timerfd_settime(fd=0, flags=0, new=valid, otmr=kernel_addr)
##        -> EBADF
##        Discriminator (b): pre-batch returned EFAULT.  Confirms
##        fd lookup fires ahead of otmr validation.
##      - timerfd_settime(fd=0, flags=0x8000_0000, new=valid_itspec)
##        -> EINVAL
##        Confirms the combined flag+spec gate still fires after
##        the read succeeds.
##        Marker: "timerfd_settime read > flag+spec > fd > otmr
##                 gate order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved:
##      - timerfd_settime(NULL new) -> EFAULT
##      - timerfd_settime(valid zeroed itspec, fd=0) -> EBADF
##      - bad interval nsec (2e9) -> EINVAL
##      - negative interval sec -> EINVAL
##      - negative value nsec -> EINVAL
##      - value nsec = NSEC_PER_SEC -> EINVAL
##      - value nsec = 999_999_999 -> EBADF
##      - prior marker "timerfd_settime itimerspec64_valid: OK"
##        still fires before the new marker.
##
##      Changed test:
##      - The "bogus flag at arg2=0x1000" test (which exercised
##        flag-before-EFAULT pre-batch) was replaced by two tests:
##        the EFAULT discriminator (above) and a "bad flag, valid
##        new" -> EINVAL test that exercises the combined gate
##        after the read.  Together they pin the new ordering.
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - No real timerfd fds exist; even a valid fd that survives
##        the lookup short-circuits to EINVAL at gate 4 ("not a
##        timerfd").  When the timerfd vfs backing lands, gate 4
##        will become real timer-arming work and the otmr put will
##        materialise actual previous timer state.
##      - The otmr validation uses validate_user_write at the END,
##        matching Linux's order.  Once timer state is real, Linux
##        will have already set the new timer before EFAULTing on
##        the otmr put; the caller observes "timer changed AND
##        EFAULT".  Documented as a future-implementation note.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

## 282. syscall/linux: epoll_ctl — event-read > epfd > switch-EINVAL (batch 253)
##
##      Pre-batch divergence (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
##      sys_epoll_ctl validated `op ∈ {ADD, DEL, MOD}` as its FIRST
##      gate, returning EINVAL for any other op.  Linux's
##      `fs/eventpoll.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4(epoll_ctl)` outer wrapper
##      does `copy_from_user(event)` whenever `ep_op_has_event(op)`
##      is true — and `ep_op_has_event(op) = op != EPOLL_CTL_DEL`,
##      so EVERY non-DEL op (including unknown values like op=99)
##      triggers the event read.  do_epoll_ctl then runs
##      `fdget(epfd)` which fires `-EBADF`; the switch default's
##      `-EINVAL` for unknown ops is the LAST possible gate.
##
##      Observable consequences pre-batch:
##        * (op=99, event=NULL)    -> EINVAL.  Linux: EFAULT (outer
##                                   wrapper copy_from_user fires).
##        * (op=99, event=valid)   -> EINVAL.  Linux: EBADF (fdget
##                                   on bad epfd fires before the
##                                   switch default).
##      Probes (libcap-ng, glibc's epoll wrapper) use unknown op
##      values to feature-detect; getting EINVAL where Linux gives
##      EFAULT/EBADF teaches them the wrong story about which gate
##      rejected their call.
##
##      Fix:
##      Removed the early `op` validation entirely.  New flow:
##        Gate 1: op != DEL  ->  read event (NULL/unreadable EFAULT).
##        Gate 2: fdget(epfd) -> EBADF (always in our kernel since
##                no real epfds exist — epoll_create returns ENOSYS).
##        Gates 3-6 (target_fd / file_can_poll / is_file_epoll /
##                   switch default): unreachable in our model;
##                   removed the now-dead target_fd validation.
##
##      Self-tests added (boot-time, dispatch_linux):
##      - epoll_ctl(op=99, event=NULL) -> EFAULT
##        Discriminator A: outer wrapper reads event for op!=DEL.
##      - epoll_ctl(op=99, event=valid) -> EBADF
##        Discriminator B: fdget(epfd) fires before switch default.
##      - epoll_ctl(op=DEL=2, event=NULL) -> EBADF
##        Confirms DEL skips the event read and falls through.
##      - epoll_ctl(op=ADD=1, event=valid, epfd=0) -> EBADF
##        Confirms ADD reads event then hits EBADF.
##        Marker: "epoll_ctl event-read > epfd > switch-EINVAL
##                 gate order: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved:
##      - epoll_ctl(op=ADD=1, event=NULL) -> EFAULT (unchanged).
##
##      Changed test:
##      - The "invalid op -> EINVAL" test was repurposed as the
##        EFAULT discriminator (same args; different expected
##        errno reflects the new gate order).
##
##      Known limitations:
##      - No real epoll backing.  When eventpoll vfs lands, the
##        fdget(epfd) gate becomes a real lookup and is_file_epoll
##        / switch default become reachable.  The current
##        "terminal EBADF" branch is the right answer until then.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 283. syscall/linux: sched_setscheduler — outer policy<0 > param+pid
##      > copy_from_user > find_pid > policy switch > priority > RTPRIO
##      (batch 254)
##
##      Pre-batch divergences from Linux's
##      `kernel/sched/syscalls.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(sched_setscheduler)`
##      + `do_sched_setscheduler` + `__sched_setscheduler`:
##
##        (a) Our gate 1 validated `policy` against {0,1,2,3,5,6,7}
##            before any param/pid check.  Linux's outer gate is
##            `if (policy < 0) return -EINVAL;` ONLY; the policy-set
##            switch lives inside __sched_setscheduler, several gates
##            deeper.  Net effect: we returned EINVAL too eagerly on
##            high-but-positive policy numbers.
##
##        (b) Our NULL-param check fired inside read_user_sched_priority
##            and produced EFAULT.  Linux's do_sched_setscheduler entry
##            is `if (!param || pid < 0) return -EINVAL;` — so NULL
##            param is EINVAL on Linux, NOT EFAULT.
##
##        (c) The pid lookup (ESRCH) ran AFTER our policy-set EINVAL.
##            Linux runs find_process_by_pid in do_sched_setscheduler,
##            BEFORE __sched_setscheduler's policy switch.
##            Discriminator: (policy=99, pid=non_existent, param=valid)
##            -> Linux ESRCH, pre-batch EINVAL.
##
##        (d) The outer "policy < 0" check was absent.  arg1=u64::MAX
##            (-1 as i32) fell through into the {0..=7} whitelist and
##            also returned EINVAL — but for the wrong reason, and a
##            future addition of more policy slots would have leaked
##            negative-policy callers past the outer gate.
##
##      Fix:
##
##        * Reorder sys_sched_setscheduler at kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
##          to seven gates matching Linux literally:
##            1. (policy as i32) < 0           -> EINVAL  (outer)
##            2. param_ptr == 0 || pid<0       -> EINVAL  (do_*)
##            3. copy_from_user(param)         -> EFAULT
##            4. find_process_by_pid(pid)      -> ESRCH
##            5. policy in {0,1,2,3,5,6,7}     -> EINVAL  (__sched_*)
##            6. sched_priority_check_for_policy -> EINVAL
##            7. rlimit_rtprio_check_for_caller -> EPERM
##
##        * Pid is also reinterpreted as i32 for the gate-2 sign check.
##          The existing u64 pid is preserved for the post-gate pcb::
##          lookups (gate 2 rejects any value whose low 32 bits are
##          negative, so the post-gate path only sees non-negative low
##          halves).
##
##      Self-tests added (after the existing sched_setscheduler block,
##      around line 31972 in the dispatch_linux self-test region):
##
##        Pre-existing tests updated:
##          - sched_setscheduler(0, 8, NULL) -> EINVAL  (was: policy
##            out-of-range gate).  Now exercises gate 5 by supplying a
##            stack-allocated zero sched_param — without the buffer the
##            gate-2 EINVAL would pre-empt the policy-set EINVAL.
##          - sched_setscheduler(0, 4, NULL) -> EINVAL  (deprecated
##            SCHED_ISO).  Same rewrite — valid param exercises gate 5.
##          - sched_setscheduler(0, 0, NULL) -> was EFAULT, now EINVAL.
##            Linux's gate 2 returns EINVAL for NULL param ahead of any
##            copy_from_user.
##
##        New discriminators:
##          A. sched_setscheduler(0, u64::MAX, valid_param) -> EINVAL
##             (gate 1, outer policy<0 ahead of param/pid validation).
##          B. sched_setscheduler(0, 99, NULL) -> EINVAL (gate 2 with
##             positive-but-unknown policy — proves NULL param doesn't
##             leak to EFAULT).
##          C. sched_setscheduler(12345, 99, valid_param) -> ESRCH
##             (gate 4 fires before gate 5; pre-batch returned EINVAL).
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   sched_setscheduler policy<0 > param+pid >
##         read > find > policy gate order: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: sched_get_priority_max test, the
##      pure-helper decision tables for sched_priority_check_for_policy
##      and rlimit_rtprio_check_with, the kernel-context RTPRIO bypass
##      assert, sched_getparam/sched_rr_get_interval NULL=>EFAULT
##      probes, and the sched_setparam(0, 0, NULL) -> EFAULT probe.
##      The sched_setparam test was using the `a` binding left over
##      from the previous sched_setscheduler test; rebound it to
##      explicit zero so its semantics no longer depend on stitching
##      with the new batch-254 discriminators.
##
##      Known limitations: sched_setparam currently returns EFAULT for
##      NULL param.  Linux actually routes sched_setparam through the
##      same do_sched_setscheduler entry and would return EINVAL for
##      NULL param — but sys_sched_setparam is a separate function and
##      is out of scope for this batch.  Track for a follow-up.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 284. syscall/linux: sched_setparam — param+pid > copy_from_user >
##      find_pid > priority > RTPRIO (batch 255)
##
##      Pre-batch divergences from Linux's
##      `SYSCALL_DEFINE2(sched_setparam)` which routes through
##      `do_sched_setscheduler(pid, SETPARAM_POLICY, param)`:
##
##        (a) We checked pid first (ESRCH for unknown pid), then
##            read_user_sched_priority (which produced EFAULT on
##            NULL param).  Linux gates `!param || pid<0` FIRST at
##            the do_sched_setscheduler entry — both conditions
##            return EINVAL.
##
##        (b) NULL param produced EFAULT pre-batch.  Linux returns
##            EINVAL ahead of copy_from_user.
##
##        (c) Negative pid (e.g. u64::MAX) fell through the pid==0
##            branch into pcb::state(huge_value).is_none() and
##            returned ESRCH.  Linux returns EINVAL via the pid<0
##            arm of gate 1.
##
##      Fix:
##
##        * Reorder sys_sched_setparam to five gates matching
##          do_sched_setscheduler literally:
##            1. param_ptr == 0 || (pid as i32) < 0  -> EINVAL
##            2. copy_from_user(param)               -> EFAULT
##            3. find_process_by_pid                  -> ESRCH
##            4. sched_priority_check_for_policy      -> EINVAL
##            5. rlimit_rtprio_check_for_caller (RT)  -> EPERM
##
##      Self-tests added/updated (around the sched_setscheduler
##      test block):
##
##        Pre-existing test updated:
##          - sched_setparam(0, NULL) -> was EFAULT, now EINVAL.
##            NULL param is rejected by gate 1, not by
##            copy_from_user.
##
##        New discriminators:
##          A. sched_setparam(pid=12345, NULL) -> EINVAL.
##             Gate 1 fires before gate 3 pid lookup; pre-batch
##             returned ESRCH.
##          B. sched_setparam(pid=u64::MAX, valid_param) -> EINVAL.
##             Gate 1 pid<0 arm; pre-batch returned ESRCH via the
##             pcb::state(huge_value)==None path.
##          C. sched_setparam(pid=12345, valid_param) -> ESRCH.
##             Confirms gates 1+2 pass and gate 3 still fires for
##             unknown pid with a valid param buffer.
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   sched_setparam param+pid > read >
##         find > policy gate order: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: the sched_setparam kernel-stack-
##      param success+EINVAL pair (line ~32370) feeds a real pid
##      and a valid pointer, so gates 1-3 pass and the priority
##      check still fires.  The local `a` binding was reset to
##      (0,0,NULL) before the sched_getparam and
##      sched_rr_get_interval probes so they keep their original
##      semantics.
##
##      Known limitations: none above what Linux does — the
##      sched_setparam family is now aligned for both
##      sched_setscheduler (batch 254) and sched_setparam (this
##      batch).
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

## 285. syscall/linux: signalfd4 — outer sizemask > outer
##      copy_from_user > do_signalfd4 flag-mask (batch 256)
##
##      Pre-batch divergence from Linux fs/signalfd.c:
##
##        Our `if args.arg3 & !VALID_FLAGS != 0` flag-mask gate fired
##        FIRST.  Linux's signalfd4 splits the work:
##          (outer SYSCALL_DEFINE4)
##            if sizemask != sizeof(sigset_t)             -> EINVAL
##            if copy_from_user(&mask, user_mask, 8)      -> EFAULT
##          (do_signalfd4)
##            if flags & ~(SFD_CLOEXEC | SFD_NONBLOCK)    -> EINVAL
##
##        Discriminator: (sizemask=8, mask=NULL, flags=0x8000_0000).
##          Linux:    sizemask OK -> copy_from_user fails -> EFAULT.
##          Pre-batch: flag-mask EINVAL fires first -> EINVAL.
##
##      Fix:
##        * Reorder sys_signalfd4 to four gates: sizemask -> mask
##          read -> flag-mask -> fd disposition.
##        * sys_signalfd (legacy) already had the right order
##          (sizemask -> mask -> fd) — no flag-mask check applies
##          since it forwards with flags=0.
##
##      Self-tests added (after the existing signalfd4 fd-discrim
##      block around line 34526):
##        A. signalfd4(fd=-1, NULL, sizemask=8, flags=0x8000_0000)
##           -> EFAULT (gate 2 ahead of gate 3).  Pre-batch EINVAL.
##        B. signalfd4(fd=-1, NULL, sizemask=7, flags=0) -> EINVAL
##           (gate 1 ahead of gate 2 EFAULT; sizemask wrong wins
##           over NULL pointer).
##        C. signalfd4(fd=-1, valid_mask, sizemask=7, flags=bogus)
##           -> EINVAL (gate 1 wins over gate 3; both EINVAL but
##           with the right reason).
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   signalfd4 sizemask > mask EFAULT >
##         flag-mask gate order: OK"
##
