##      Existing tests preserved: the pre-existing signalfd4(bad
##      flag) test feeds sizemask=8 + valid mask + bogus flag,
##      which still hits gate 3 EINVAL (gates 1+2 pass).
##      signalfd4(-1, valid, sizemask=8, flags=valid) still
##      reaches the terminal ENOSYS.  signalfd4(fd=0) still EINVAL
##      (gates 1+2+3 pass, then fd!=-1 validate -> EINVAL).
##
##      Known limitations: no real signalfd kind exists in this
##      kernel, so any fd!=-1 falls through to the terminal EINVAL.
##      That mirrors Linux's do_signalfd4 "fd present but wrong
##      kind" answer; it does not mirror the case where a real
##      signalfd would receive a mask update.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 286. syscall/linux: io_uring_setup — copy_from_user > resv > flags
##      > entries gate order (batch 257)
##
##      Pre-batch divergences from Linux io_uring/io_uring.c
##      `io_uring_setup` + `io_uring_create`:
##
##        (a) Our entries==0 (EINVAL) and entries>MAX (EINVAL) gates
##            fired BEFORE the param fetch.  Linux runs both checks
##            inside io_uring_create, AFTER io_uring_setup has
##            already copy_from_user'd the params struct.
##            Discriminators:
##              (entries=0, NULL)     -> Linux EFAULT, pre-batch EINVAL.
##              (entries=65536, NULL) -> Linux EFAULT, pre-batch EINVAL.
##
##        (b) Our flag-mask check on header[8..12] fired BEFORE the
##            resv check on header[28..40].  Linux's loop over
##            p.resv[i] runs first, then the flag-mask gate.  Both
##            ultimately produce EINVAL, but the reason differs.
##
##        (c) The entries>MAX gate didn't honour IORING_SETUP_CLAMP.
##            Linux silently clamps to IORING_MAX_ENTRIES when CLAMP
##            is set; only the no-CLAMP case returns EINVAL.  We now
##            require `!CLAMP` before the EINVAL gate fires.
##
##      Fix:
##
##        * Reorder sys_io_uring_setup at kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
##          to five gates matching Linux literally:
##            1. params != NULL && validate_user_read(params, 120) +
##               copy_from_user(40 bytes) -> EFAULT
##            2. header[28..40] non-zero (resv) -> EINVAL
##            3. flags & !VALID -> EINVAL
##            4. entries == 0 -> EINVAL
##            5. entries > IORING_MAX_ENTRIES && !CLAMP -> EINVAL
##
##      Self-tests added/updated (around line 35435):
##
##        Pre-existing tests updated:
##          - io_uring_setup(0, 0x1000) -> EINVAL:  switched to a
##            stack-allocated zero-buffer.  0x1000 is no longer safe
##            to dereference post-reorder (the param fetch runs
##            ahead of the entries gate).  Still EINVAL but via
##            gate 4.
##          - Stale comment on the resv probe updated; the explicit
##            "clear flags first so the bad-flag gate doesn't
##            shadow the resv gate" is no longer load-bearing since
##            the resv gate now wins per Linux ordering.
##
##        New discriminators:
##          A. io_uring_setup(entries=0, NULL) -> EFAULT  (gate 1
##             ahead of gate 4; was EINVAL).
##          B. io_uring_setup(entries=65536, NULL) -> EFAULT (gate 1
##             ahead of gate 5; was EINVAL).
##          C. io_uring_setup(8, params with bad flag AND non-zero
##             resv) -> EINVAL.  Both gates produce EINVAL, but the
##             post-test reset cleans up both fields; the call
##             reaching the resv gate is what keeps the later
##             ENOSYS probe clean.
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   io_uring_setup read > resv > flags >
##         entries gate order: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: (entries=8, NULL) -> EFAULT, the
##      bad-flag probe, the non-zero-resv probe, the all-zero
##      ENOSYS terminal probe, and io_uring_enter/register
##      validation downstream.
##
##      Known limitations: still terminates as ENOSYS; the CLAMP
##      flag now silently bypasses the entries>MAX gate but the
##      result is still ENOSYS since no real ring backend exists.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 287. syscall/linux: ppoll — timespec > sigmask+sigsetsize > nfds
##      gate order (batch 258)
##
##      Pre-batch divergences from Linux fs/select.c
##      `SYSCALL_DEFINE5(ppoll)` + `set_user_sigmask` + `do_sys_poll`:
##
##        (a) Our gate order ran nfds/ufds first, then sigsetsize,
##            then sigmask, then tsp.  Linux's literal order is:
##              1. tsp != NULL: get_timespec64 + poll_select_set_timeout.
##              2. set_user_sigmask(umask, sigsetsize).
##              3. do_sys_poll: nfds + ufds.
##            Discriminator: (nfds=2, ufds=NULL, tsp={-1,0}) ->
##              Linux EINVAL (gate 1 negative tv_sec) vs pre-batch
##              EFAULT (nfds gate fired ufds=NULL EFAULT first).
##
##        (b) Our sigsetsize check ran unconditionally, including
##            when umask was NULL.  Linux's `set_user_sigmask` does
##            `if (!umask) return 0;` so sigsetsize is IGNORED when
##            sigmask is NULL.
##            Discriminator: (NULL sigmask, sigsetsize=16) ->
##              Linux 0 (poll fast path) vs pre-batch EINVAL.
##
##      Fix:
##
##        * Reorder sys_ppoll at kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs to three
##          gates matching Linux literally:
##            1. tsp != NULL -> validate + parse timespec ->
##               EFAULT/EINVAL.
##            2. sigmask != NULL -> sigsetsize==8 + validate read ->
##               EINVAL/EFAULT.  Skipped entirely when sigmask=NULL.
##            3. nfds > 1<<20 -> EINVAL; nfds>0 + ufds=NULL ->
##               EFAULT; nfds>0 -> validate ufds.
##          poll_core called once at the end.
##
##      Self-tests added/updated (around the existing ppoll block):
##
##        Pre-existing test updated:
##          - (nfds=0, NULL tsp, NULL sigmask, sigsetsize=16) -> EINVAL
##            switched to use a stack-allocated dummy sigmask buffer
##            so the sigsetsize gate still fires (Linux ignores
##            sigsetsize when sigmask is NULL, so the old NULL-sigmask
##            form would now return 0).  Intent preserved: bad
##            sigsetsize -> EINVAL.
##
##        New discriminators:
##          A. (nfds=2, ufds=NULL, tsp={-1,0}) -> EINVAL  (gate 1
##             ahead of gate 3; was EFAULT).
##          B. (NULL sigmask, sigsetsize=16, nfds=0) -> 0  (gate 2
##             skipped because umask=NULL; was EINVAL).
##          C. (huge nfds, NULL tsp, NULL sigmask, sigsetsize=16) ->
##             EINVAL  (confirms gate 2 skip lets the nfds EINVAL
##             through; pre-batch was also EINVAL but via sigsetsize).
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   ppoll timespec > sigmask+sigsetsize >
##         nfds gate order: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: timespec={0,0} -> 0, neg tv_sec ->
##      EINVAL, tv_nsec >= 1e9 -> EINVAL, and the downstream
##      pipe-readiness probes.
##
##      Known limitations: poll_core ignores the sigmask entirely
##      (no signal delivery yet), so the "atomic swap-in / swap-out"
##      semantics are stubbed — the validate-only behavior matches
##      Linux for the no-pending-signal case but loses the EINTR
##      paths.  Tracked under the general signal-delivery work.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 288. syscall/linux: pselect6 — argpack > tsp > sigmask > nfds
##      gate order (batch 259)
##
##      Pre-batch divergences from Linux fs/select.c
##      `SYSCALL_DEFINE6(pselect6)` + `do_pselect` +
##      `set_user_sigmask` + `core_sys_select`:
##
##        (a) We ran the nfds bounds check FIRST.  Linux's literal
##            order is: get_sigset_argpack(sig) -> tsp parse ->
##            set_user_sigmask(inner) -> core_sys_select(n).  The
##            nfds gate is the LAST gate, inside core_sys_select.
##
##        (b) We "validated" args.arg5 (the argpack pointer) as
##            readable but NEVER dereferenced it.  The inner sigmask
##            pointer and inner sigsetsize from the argpack were
##            ignored entirely — Linux applies set_user_sigmask to
##            those inner values, with sigsetsize-must-be-8 EINVAL
##            and sigmask-readable EFAULT gates.
##            Discriminator: (argpack { inner=valid, inner_size=16 })
##            -> Linux EINVAL (gate 3); pre-batch 0 (silently
##            accepted the bad inner size and fell through to
##            core_sys_select with nfds=0).
##
##        (c) Our sigsetsize check, when added, must honour Linux's
##            "NULL inner sigmask skips sigsetsize entirely" rule
##            (same as ppoll batch 258).
##
##      Fix:
##
##        * Reorder sys_pselect6 at kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs to
##          four gates matching Linux literally:
##            1. sig != NULL -> validate + copy_from_user(argpack,
##               16) -> EFAULT.  Decode { inner_sigmask_ptr,
##               inner_sigsetsize }.
##            2. tsp != NULL -> validate + parse timespec ->
##               EFAULT/EINVAL.
##            3. inner_sigmask_ptr != NULL -> inner_sigsetsize == 8
##               (EINVAL) -> validate_user_read(inner, 8) (EFAULT).
##               NULL inner skips entirely.
##            4. nfds bounds via fd_set_byte_len + fdset writes ->
##               EINVAL.
##          select_core called once at the end.
##
##      Self-tests added/updated (around the existing pselect6 block):
##
##        Pre-existing tests preserved (all three still pass with the
##        new order):
##          - pselect6(0, NULL..., &{0,0}, NULL) -> 0.
##          - pselect6 with timespec{0, 1e9} -> EINVAL.
##          - pselect6 with timespec{-1, 0} -> EINVAL.
##
##        New discriminators:
##          A. argpack { inner=valid 8B buf, inner_size=16 } -> Linux
##             EINVAL (gate 3 sigsetsize != 8); pre-batch 0.
##          B. argpack { inner=NULL, inner_size=16 } -> 0  (NULL
##             inner skips the size check entirely; same answer as
##             pre-batch but confirms the new gate-3 skip wiring).
##          C. argpack { inner=valid 8B buf, inner_size=8 } -> 0
##             (canonical happy path; confirms gate 3 accepts 8).
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   pselect6 argpack > tsp > sigmask >
##         nfds gate order: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: pselect6({0,0}) -> 0, bad tv_nsec
##      -> EINVAL, neg tv_sec -> EINVAL, downstream epoll probes.
##
##      Known limitations: select_core still does not actually apply
##      the inner sigmask to signal delivery (no signal delivery
##      exists yet).  The validate-only behavior matches Linux for
##      the no-pending-signal case but loses the EINTR path.
##      Tracked under the general signal-delivery work alongside
##      ppoll (entry 287).
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 289. syscall/linux: epoll_pwait2 — timespec value validation
##      (batch 260)
##
##      Pre-batch divergence from Linux fs/eventpoll.c
##      `SYSCALL_DEFINE6(epoll_pwait2)` + `do_epoll_pwait` +
##      `do_epoll_wait`:
##
##      Linux's gate 1 is two-step:
##        1a. get_timespec64(&ts, timeout) — copy_from_user 16 bytes;
##            EFAULT on bad pointer.
##        1b. poll_select_set_timeout(to, ts.tv_sec, ts.tv_nsec) —
##            EINVAL when tv_sec<0 or tv_nsec is not in [0, 1e9).
##
##      We were only doing 1a (and even then merely validating the
##      pointer was readable, never copying or inspecting the
##      values).  A probe with `tsp = { -1, 0 }` or `{ 0, 1e9 }`
##      silently passed our gate and ultimately fell through to the
##      terminal EBADF, where Linux returns EINVAL.
##
##      Fix:
##
##        * In sys_epoll_pwait2 at kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
##          - validate_user_read(arg3, 16) (EFAULT) [pre-existing]
##          - copy_from_user(arg3, ts, 16) [NEW]
##          - parse tv_sec / tv_nsec
##          - if tv_sec<0 || !(0..1e9).contains(&tv_nsec) -> EINVAL [NEW]
##
##      Self-tests added/updated:
##
##        Pre-existing epoll_pwait2 tests preserved unchanged:
##          - NULL umask + bogus sigsetsize -> EBADF.
##          - non-NULL umask + sigsetsize=0 -> EINVAL.
##          - 0 maxevents -> EINVAL.
##          - maxevents > EP_MAX_EVENTS -> EINVAL.
##
##        New discriminators (added at the end of the epoll test
##        block):
##          A. tsp = { -1, 0 }, sigmask=NULL, maxevents=1 -> Linux
##             EINVAL (gate 1b negative tv_sec); pre-batch EBADF.
##          B. tsp = { 0, 1e9 }, sigmask=NULL, maxevents=1 -> Linux
##             EINVAL (gate 1b nsec out of range); pre-batch EBADF.
##          C. tsp = { 0, 0 }, sigmask=NULL, maxevents=1 -> EBADF
##             (canonical "poll now"; confirms gate 1 accepts the
##             zero timespec and falls through to fdget).
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   epoll_pwait2 timespec value gating: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: all epoll_wait / epoll_pwait /
##      epoll_pwait2 sigmask/maxevents/events gating, plus the
##      boundary maxevents = EP_MAX_EVENTS tests.
##
##      Known limitations: epoll_pwait2 still terminates as EBADF
##      because no real epfd backend exists.  The new gate-1
##      EINVAL path therefore only fires for malformed timespecs;
##      well-formed timespecs always exit EBADF.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 290. syscall/linux: recvmmsg — timespec value validation
##      (batch 261)
##
##      Pre-batch divergence from Linux net/socket.c
##      `SYSCALL_DEFINE5(recvmmsg)` + `__sys_recvmmsg`:
##
##      Linux's `__sys_recvmmsg` rejects malformed timespecs with
##      EINVAL early — before fd lookup or per-mmsghdr work:
##        if (timeout && (timeout->tv_sec < 0 ||
##                        timeout->tv_nsec < 0 ||
##                        timeout->tv_nsec >= NSEC_PER_SEC))
##            return -EINVAL;
##
##      We only validated the timeout pointer was readable; we
##      never copied or inspected the values.  A probe with
##      `{ -1, 0 }` or `{ 0, 1e9 }` silently passed our gate and
##      fell through to the terminal EBADF.
##
##      Fix:
##
##        * In sys_recvmmsg at kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
##          - validate_user_read(arg4, 16) (EFAULT) [pre-existing]
##          - copy_from_user(arg4, ts, 16) [NEW]
##          - parse tv_sec / tv_nsec
##          - if tv_sec<0 || !(0..NSEC_PER_SEC).contains(&tv_nsec)
##            -> EINVAL [NEW]
##        * Subsequent gates (vlen, mmsg ptr, fd) unchanged.
##
##      Self-tests added/updated:
##
##        Pre-existing test preserved:
##          - recvmmsg(vlen=2, mmsg=valid, timeout={0,0}) -> EBADF.
##
##        New discriminators (added right after the existing
##        recvmmsg probe):
##          A. (vlen=0, timeout={-1,0}) -> Linux EINVAL via
##             __sys_recvmmsg timespec check; pre-batch EBADF.
##          B. (vlen=2, mmsg=valid, timeout={0, 1e9}) -> Linux
##             EINVAL; pre-batch EBADF.
##          C. (vlen=0, timeout=NULL) -> EBADF (NULL-timeout skip
##             path still hits fd validation; regression guard).
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   recvmmsg timespec value gating: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: sendmmsg gating, recvmmsg valid
##      with timeout, downstream setsockopt/getsockopt/shutdown
##      probes.
##
##      Known limitations: recvmmsg still terminates as EBADF
##      because no real socket fd backend reaches userspace yet.
##      The new gate-1 EINVAL path only fires for malformed
##      timespecs; well-formed timespecs always exit EBADF.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 291. syscall/linux: futex2_wait — timespec value validation
##      (batch 262)
##
##      Pre-batch divergence:
##        Linux 6.7+ `SYSCALL_DEFINE6(futex_wait)` in
##        kernel/futex/syscalls.c runs `get_timespec64()` after the
##        FUTEX2 flag/clockid checks; `get_timespec64()` calls
##        `timespec64_valid` (tv_sec >= 0 AND
##        tv_nsec in [0, NSEC_PER_SEC)) and returns -EINVAL on
##        out-of-range values.  Our pre-batch path only validated
##        readability via `validate_user_read(timespec, 16)` and
##        then called `read_timespec()` (which is just a
##        copy_from_user — no value check).  A malformed timespec
##        such as `{tv_sec=-1, 0}` or `{0, 1_000_000_000}` slipped
##        through; `to_nanos()` produced a garbage absolute
##        deadline, `saturating_sub(now)` clamped it to 0, and
##        `handlers::sys_futex_wait_timeout` returned 0
##        (value-mismatch short-circuit) — silently wrong vs.
##        Linux EINVAL.
##
##      Fix:
##        Replaced the readable-only check at the timespec arg
##        with a call to the existing `validate_user_timespec64`
##        helper (same one used by mq_timedsend, mq_timedreceive,
##        semtimedop).  The helper does the
##        validate_user_read+copy_from_user+timespec64_valid
##        sequence and returns the precomputed SyscallResult on
##        failure (EFAULT for unreadable; EINVAL for invalid).
##
##      Self-tests added/updated:
##
##        Pre-existing tests preserved:
##          - futex2 wait bad clockid (99) -> EINVAL
##          - futex2 wait clockid=2 -> EINVAL
##          - futex2 wait mask=0 -> EINVAL
##          - futex2 wait NUMA -> ENOSYS
##          - futex2 wait non-U32 size -> EINVAL
##          - futex2 wait mismatch, no timeout -> 0
##          - futex2 wait past-abs MONOTONIC ts=(0,0) -> 0
##          - futex2 wait past-abs REALTIME ts=(0,0) -> 0
##
##        New discriminators (inserted between the REALTIME-abs
##        test and the futex_requeue tests):
##          A. (uaddr=u32_ptr, val=1, mask=1, flags=F2_SIZE_U32,
##              timespec={tv_sec=-1, 0}, clockid=0) -> Linux EINVAL
##              via timespec64_valid; pre-batch silently returned
##              0 from the value-mismatch short-circuit.
##          B. (timespec={0, 1_000_000_000}) -> Linux EINVAL
##              (tv_nsec out of range); pre-batch silently 0.
##        Acceptance for valid {0,0} ts was already covered by the
##        pre-existing past-abs tests at lines 44972 / 44981 that
##        continue to exit cleanly with 0 — confirming the new
##        gate doesn't falsely reject well-formed timespecs.
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   futex2_wait timespec value gating: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: futex2 wake gate suite,
##      futex_requeue tests, and the broader futex/futex_waitv
##      probes earlier in the test block all unchanged.
##
##      Known limitations: futex2_wait still terminates with 0 on
##      value-mismatch instead of -EAGAIN.  This pre-existing
##      classic-FUTEX_WAIT behaviour is shared with the futex2
##      path and documented in the test comment; converting to
##      EAGAIN requires a handler-side change tracked separately.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

## 292. syscall/linux: rt_sigtimedwait — timespec value validation
##      (batch 263)
##
##      Pre-batch divergence:
##        Linux's `kernel/signal.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4(rt_sigtimedwait)`
##        forwards to `do_sigtimedwait(&these, uinfo, ts)`, which
##        gates on `timespec64_valid(ts)` (tv_sec >= 0 AND
##        tv_nsec in [0, NSEC_PER_SEC)) before computing the
##        ktime deadline.  Out-of-range values return -EINVAL.
##        Our pre-batch path only ran `validate_user_read(arg2, 16)`
##        on the timespec pointer; bogus values like
##        `{tv_sec=-1, 0}` or `{0, 1_000_000_000}` slipped through
##        and the syscall returned its terminal EAGAIN as though
##        a finite timeout had elapsed — silently wrong vs. Linux
##        EINVAL.
##
##      Fix:
##        Replaced the readable-only check with a call to the
##        shared `validate_user_timespec64` helper (already used by
##        futex2_wait, mq_timedsend, mq_timedreceive, semtimedop,
##        epoll_pwait2, recvmmsg, ppoll).  The helper performs the
##        validate_user_read + copy_from_user + timespec64_valid
##        sequence and returns the precomputed SyscallResult
##        (EFAULT for unreadable; EINVAL for invalid).  Terminal
##        EAGAIN behaviour for valid finite/NULL timeouts is
##        preserved.
##
##      Self-tests added/updated:
##
##        Pre-existing tests preserved:
##          - rt_sigtimedwait sigsetsize=16 -> EINVAL
##          - rt_sigtimedwait NULL mask -> EFAULT
##          - rt_sigtimedwait valid (mask=valid, ts=NULL,
##            sigsetsize=8) -> EAGAIN
##
##        New discriminators (inserted right after the valid
##        EAGAIN probe):
##          A. (mask=valid, info=NULL, ts={tv_sec=-1, 0},
##              sigsetsize=8) -> Linux EINVAL via
##              do_sigtimedwait->timespec64_valid; pre-batch EAGAIN.
##          B. (ts={0, 1_000_000_000}) -> Linux EINVAL (tv_nsec
##              out of range); pre-batch EAGAIN.
##          C. (ts={0, 0}) -> EAGAIN (acceptance: confirms the new
##              gate doesn't falsely reject well-formed timespecs;
##              the NULL-ts acceptance is already covered above).
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   rt_sigtimedwait timespec value gating: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: rt_sigsuspend gate suite,
##      rt_sigqueueinfo / rt_tgsigqueueinfo / rt_sigreturn /
##      POSIX timer family probes following this block.
##
##      Known limitations: rt_sigtimedwait still terminates with
##      EAGAIN for any well-formed timeout because we never deliver
##      signals into this path.  When the signal-delivery path is
##      wired up, the do_sigtimedwait emulation will need to walk
##      pending signals and respect the absolute timeout.  Also
##      note Linux's gate order is uthese -> timespec -> uinfo, but
##      our siginfo writability check still fires before the
##      timespec value check; that's an EFAULT-vs-EFAULT swap with
##      no externally visible difference, but if a probe ever
##      passes a bad info pointer alongside a bad timespec value,
##      it should see EINVAL (Linux) not EFAULT (us).  Track as a
##      future cleanup if observable.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

## 293. syscall/linux: utimensat — gate reorder + OMIT shortcut +
##      nsec_valid (batch 264)
##
##      Pre-batch divergences (three, all on one syscall):
##        Linux's `fs/utimes.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4(utimensat)` runs:
##          (a) copy both `struct timespec` entries via
##              get_timespec64 — EFAULT on bad pointer;
##          (b) if both `tv_nsec == UTIME_OMIT`, return 0 with NO
##              path resolution, NO flag check, NO mnt_want_write
##              (kernel comment: "we must not even check the path");
##          (c) do_utimes() runs the flag mask check ->
##              EINVAL on `flags & ~AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW`;
##          (d) path resolution;
##          (e) vfs_utimes() runs nsec_valid on each entry — accepts
##              UTIME_NOW, UTIME_OMIT, or [0, NSEC_PER_SEC) -> EINVAL
##              otherwise;
##          (f) mnt_want_write -> EROFS on read-only mounts.
##        Pre-batch: flag check first, then path readability, then
##        only readability check on the 32-byte times array — no
##        copy, no UTIME_OMIT shortcut, no nsec_valid.
##        Observable divergences:
##          - (path=bogus, times=(_,OMIT,_,OMIT), flags=bogus) saw
##            EINVAL (our flag-first); Linux returns 0.
##          - (times=valid ptr, tv_nsec[0]=1_000_000_000) saw EROFS;
##            Linux returns EINVAL.
##          - (tv_nsec[1] = -1) saw EROFS; Linux returns EINVAL.
##
##      Fix:
##        Restructured to mirror SYSCALL_DEFINE4:
##          1. If `arg2 != 0`: validate_user_read(32) + copy_from_user
##             of both timespecs.
##          2. If both tv_nsec == UTIME_OMIT (0x3fff_fffe): return 0
##             without any further work.
##          3. Flag mask check.
##          4. Path readability.
##          5. nsec_valid on each tv_nsec (UTIME_NOW=0x3fff_ffff,
##             UTIME_OMIT, or [0, 1_000_000_000)) -> EINVAL on bad.
##          6. Terminal EROFS.
##        tv_sec is intentionally not gated — Linux's vfs_utimes
##        allows negative tv_sec for pre-epoch timestamps; only
##        tv_nsec is constrained.
##
##      Self-tests added/updated:
##
##        Pre-existing tests preserved:
##          - utimensat (path=0x1000, times=NULL, flags=0x800_0000)
##            -> EINVAL (times=NULL skips the new gates; flag check
##            still fires).
##          - utimensat (everything NULL) -> EROFS (terminal).
##
##        New discriminators (inserted right after the NULL/NULL
##        probe):
##          A. (path=0x1000, times=(0,OMIT,0,OMIT), flags=0x800_0000)
##              -> 0 via the OMIT-shortcut bypass of path + flag +
##              fs gates; pre-batch flag-first returned EINVAL.
##          B. (path=NULL, times=(0, 1e9, 0, 0)) -> EINVAL via
##              vfs_utimes nsec_valid on entry 0; pre-batch EROFS.
##          C. (path=NULL, times=(0, 0, 0, -1)) -> EINVAL via
##              nsec_valid on entry 1; confirms both entries gated.
##          D. (path=NULL, times=(0,NOW,0,NOW)) -> EROFS
##              (acceptance: UTIME_NOW != UTIME_OMIT so shortcut
##              doesn't fire; nsec_valid passes; terminal EROFS).
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   utimensat OMIT-shortcut + nsec_valid: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: utimes/utime gates, broader
##      *at-family suite, downstream linkat/symlinkat/etc probes.
##
##      Known limitations: tv_sec is intentionally unvalidated to
##      match Linux's tolerance of pre-epoch timestamps.  Once a
##      writable FS is wired up the EROFS terminal will be replaced
##      by real timestamp updates, at which point the full
##      do_utimes path (notify_change, etc.) will need to be
##      implemented.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 294. syscall/linux: utimes — tv_usec value gating (batch 265)
##
##      Pre-batch divergence:
##        Linux's `fs/utimes.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(utimes)` dispatches
##        to `do_futimesat(AT_FDCWD, filename, utimes)`, which:
##          1. If utimes != NULL: copy_from_user 32 bytes (2x
##             `struct __kernel_old_timeval`) -> -EFAULT.
##          2. If `times[0].tv_usec >= USEC_PER_SEC` OR
##             `times[1].tv_usec >= USEC_PER_SEC` -> -EINVAL
##             (explicit pre-do_utimes check; kernel comment:
##             "this test is needed to catch only
##             `times[0].tv_usec == UINT_MAX`").
##          3. do_utimes -> path resolution -> -EFAULT on NULL
##             filename.
##          4. vfs_utimes -> mnt_want_write -> -EROFS.
##        Pre-batch order: filename-NULL check first, then path
##        readability, then 32-byte timeval array readability
##        only (no copy, no tv_usec range gate).  Observable
##        divergence: a probe of `(filename=NULL,
##        times={(0, 1e6), (0, 0)})` saw EFAULT instead of
##        Linux's EINVAL.
##
##      Fix:
##        Restructured to mirror do_futimesat:
##          1. If `arg1 != 0`: validate_user_read(32) +
##             copy_from_user the timeval array.
##          2. tv_usec[0] >= 1_000_000 OR tv_usec[1] >= 1_000_000
##             -> EINVAL.
##          3. filename-NULL -> EFAULT.
##          4. validate_user_read(filename, 1) -> EFAULT.
##          5. Terminal EROFS.
##        tv_sec is intentionally unconstrained (Linux allows
##        negative pre-epoch tv_sec via the signed conversion to
##        tv_nsec).  Negative tv_usec is also accepted at this
##        gate (signed `>= USEC_PER_SEC` is false for negatives);
##        Linux catches negatives later in vfs_utimes->nsec_valid
##        after the *1000 conversion turns them negative — that
##        path still terminates EINVAL but at a later gate which
##        we don't currently emulate.  Documented as a known
##        limitation.
##
##      Self-tests added/updated:
##
##        Pre-existing tests preserved:
##          - utimes(NULL, _) -> EFAULT (times=NULL skips the new
##            gates; filename check still fires).
##          - utimes(path=0x1000, NULL) -> EROFS (terminal).
##
##        New discriminators (inserted right after the existing
##        utimes pair):
##          A. (filename=NULL, times={(0, 1e6), (0, 0)}) ->
##              EINVAL via the new tv_usec gate; pre-batch EFAULT
##              because filename was inspected first.
##          B. (filename=NULL, times={(0, 0), (0, i64::MAX)}) ->
##              EINVAL via the same gate on entry 1; confirms
##              both entries are inspected.
##          C. (filename=NULL, times={(0, 999_999), (0, 0)}) ->
##              EFAULT (acceptance: valid tv_usec passes the gate
##              and falls through to the filename-NULL check).
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   utimes tv_usec value gating: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: utimensat suite (incl. batch 264
##      discriminators), utime gates, downstream *at-family
##      probes.
##
##      Known limitations: negative tv_usec values pass our gate
##      (Linux's >=-comparison is signed too); Linux catches them
##      at vfs_utimes->nsec_valid after the *1000 conversion makes
##      them negative.  Probes of (tv_usec = -1) see our EROFS vs
##      Linux EINVAL — both terminal but at different gates.  Fix
##      requires emulating vfs_utimes's nsec_valid post-conversion,
##      tracked as a follow-up.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 26s).

## 295. syscall/linux: futimesat — tv_usec value gating (batch 266)
##
##      Pre-batch divergence:
##        Linux's `fs/utimes.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(futimesat)` is a
##        thin wrapper around `do_futimesat(dfd, filename, utimes)`
##        — the same helper that utimes(2) uses.  Gate order:
##          1. utimes != NULL: copy_from_user 32 bytes -> EFAULT.
##          2. tv_usec[0]|tv_usec[1] >= USEC_PER_SEC -> EINVAL.
##          3. do_utimes -> path resolution -> EFAULT on NULL
##             filename.
##          4. vfs_utimes -> mnt_want_write -> EROFS.
##        Pre-batch: path-NULL first, then validate_user_str, then
##        validate_user_read(times, 32) — never copied or
##        inspected tv_usec values.  Observable divergence:
##        `(filename=NULL, times={tv_usec[0]=1e6, ...})` saw
##        EFAULT instead of Linux's EINVAL — same pattern fixed
##        in utimes (batch 265).
##
##      Fix:
##        Restructured to mirror do_futimesat:
##          1. If times != 0: validate_user_read(32) +
##             copy_from_user the timeval array.
##          2. Gate tv_usec[0]|tv_usec[1] >= 1_000_000 -> EINVAL.
##          3. path == 0 -> EFAULT.
##          4. validate_user_str(path) -> EFAULT on bad string.
##          5. Terminal ENOSYS (intentional divergence from
##             Linux's EROFS — glibc uses ENOSYS to fall back to
##             utimensat; documented in the doc comment as a
##             separate concern from the gate-order fix).
##
##      Self-tests added/updated:
##
##        Pre-existing tests preserved:
##          - futimesat NULL path -> EFAULT.
##          - futimesat (path=valid, times=zero-buf) -> ENOSYS.
##          - futimesat (path=valid, times=NULL) -> ENOSYS.
##
##        New discriminators (inserted right after the existing
##        futimesat triple):
##          A. (filename=NULL, times={(0,1e6),(0,0)}) -> EINVAL
##              via the new tv_usec gate; pre-batch EFAULT.
##          B. (filename=NULL, times={(0,0),(0,i64::MAX)}) ->
##              EINVAL via the same gate on entry 1.
##          C. (filename=NULL, times={(0,999_999),(0,0)}) ->
##              EFAULT (acceptance: valid tv_usec falls through
##              to filename check).
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   futimesat tv_usec value gating: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: utimes / utime / utimensat
##      gates, downstream *at-family probes, legacy-syscall ENOSYS
##      sweep right above this block.
##
##      Known limitations: same as utimes (batch 265) — negative
##      tv_usec values pass our signed `>=`-comparison; Linux
##      catches them at vfs_utimes->nsec_valid after the *1000
##      conversion makes tv_nsec negative.  Terminal ENOSYS is
##      also a deliberate non-Linux behaviour (Linux returns
##      EROFS for read-only mounts) to drive glibc's
##      utimensat-fallback path.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

296.  syscall/linux: validate_iov — gate iov_len > SSIZE_MAX
##      with EINVAL before access check (batch 267).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's __import_iovec /
##      iovec_from_user (fs/iov_iter.c) returns EINVAL when an
##      iovec entry's iov_len does not fit in ssize_t, via
##        `len = (ssize_t)iov_len; if (len < 0) return -EINVAL;`
##      executed BEFORE access_ok() on iov_base.  This catches
##      every iov_len in (SSIZE_MAX, ULONG_MAX] (i.e.
##      (i64::MAX as u64, u64::MAX] on 64-bit) as EINVAL with
##      the right errno discriminator.
##
##      Our prior validate_iov did:
##        if iov.len == 0 { continue; }
##        let len = usize::try_from(iov.len)
##                      .map_err(|_| EINVAL)?;
##        validate_user_read/write(iov.base, len)?;
##      On x86_64 usize == u64, so usize::try_from never fails;
##      validate_user_read in kernel context is a no-op; so an
##      oversize iov_len silently slipped past validate_iov and
##      surfaced as EBADF from lookup_caller_fd (wrong errno) or
##      worse — depended on backend overflow checks at I/O time.
##
##      Fix in validate_iov (line 21300):
##        After the `iov.len == 0` skip, before the existing
##        usize::try_from cast, insert
##          if iov.len > i64::MAX as u64 {
##              return Err(linux_err(errno::EINVAL));
##          }
##        with a SAFETY-style comment citing __import_iovec and
##        explaining why the cast survives (gate is strict `>`).
##        All six callers — sys_readv, sys_writev, sys_preadv,
##        sys_pwritev, sys_preadv2, sys_pwritev2, plus the iov
##        path inside sys_process_vm_{readv,writev} which shares
##        the helper — pick up the new gate.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux (preadv triple):
##        A. ([{base=0, len=u64::MAX}], fd=-1) -> EINVAL
##            via the new SSIZE_MAX gate; pre-batch returned
##            EBADF from the fd lookup since validate_user_read
##            is a no-op in kernel context.
##        B. ([{base=0, len=i64::MAX as u64 + 1}], fd=-1) on
##            PWRITEV -> EINVAL — smallest SSIZE_MAX-boundary
##            failure, also covers the writev path (for_write
##            branch of validate_iov).
##        C. ([{base=0, len=i64::MAX as u64}], fd=-1) -> EBADF.
##            Acceptance case: proves the gate is exclusive
##            (strict `>`), exactly matching Linux's
##            `(ssize_t)len < 0` test.  Pre-fix would also have
##            returned EBADF here, but the discriminator pair
##            (A,B) → EINVAL is what proves the gate fires.
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   preadv/pwritev iov_len SSIZE_MAX gating: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: futimesat tv_usec triple
##      directly below this block, the legacy-syscall ENOSYS
##      sweep above it, and every (p)readv/(p)writev call site
##      that already supplied well-formed iov entries.
##
##      Known limitations: we do not implement Linux's silent
##      MAX_RW_COUNT clamp (which truncates iov_len down to
##      ((1<<31) - PAGE_SIZE) without erroring) — for our
##      current per-handle backends the absence is benign
##      because pread_file_to_user / pwrite_file_from_user
##      themselves bound the transfer length, but if we ever
##      add a backend that fans iov entries out to a syscall
##      that distinguishes large counts, the clamp will need to
##      be ported.  Also: we still do not validate
##      sum(iov_len) > SSIZE_MAX (Linux returns EOVERFLOW/EINVAL
##      via rw_verify_area on the total).  Track in a future
##      batch.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

297.  syscall/linux: capget/capset NULL-data semantics (batch 268).
##
##      Pre-batch divergences vs Linux kernel/capability.c:
##
##      sys_capget — Linux's cap_validate_magic rewrites unknown
##      header versions to _LINUX_CAPABILITY_VERSION_3 in-place
##      and returns -EINVAL.  The wrapping sys_capget then runs
##        if ((dataptr == NULL) || (ret != 0))
##            return ((dataptr == NULL) && (ret == -EINVAL)) ? 0 : ret;
##      which means `capget(bad_version_header, NULL datap)` is
##      the canonical version-detection probe and returns 0 (the
##      header has been rewritten — the caller now knows V3 is
##      correct).  Pre-batch we always returned EINVAL on the
##      version-rewrite arm regardless of datap, breaking glibc /
##      libcap-style probes.
##
##      sys_capset — Linux calls
##        copy_from_user(&kdata, data, copybytes)
##      after cap_validate_magic succeeds, where
##        copybytes = tocopy * sizeof(struct __user_cap_data_struct)
##                  = 12 (V1) or 24 (V2/V3) bytes.
##      A NULL or unreadable data pointer surfaces EFAULT — there
##      is no NULL-data probe form for capset (unlike capget).
##      Pre-batch we accepted any valid-version capset call as
##      silent OK without ever inspecting `data`, so `capset(V3,
##      NULL)` returned 0 where Linux returns EFAULT.
##
##      Fix in sys_capget (line ~7757):
##        Inside the unknown-version arm, after the V3 in-place
##        rewrite + copy_to_user, branch on datap:
##          if datap == 0 { return SyscallResult::ok(0); }
##          return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
##        Cite Linux's ternary in the comment.
##
##      Fix in sys_capset (line ~7831):
##        Replace the bare `V1 | V2 | V3 => SyscallResult::ok(0)`
##        arm with one that computes
##          let elems = if V1 { 1 } else { 2 };
##          let total = elems * 12;
##        and gates `args.arg1 == 0 -> EFAULT` and
##        `validate_user_read(args.arg1, total) -> EFAULT`
##        before returning ok(0).
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux (capget/capset quadruple):
##        A. capget(hdr={0x99999999}, NULL datap)  -> 0
##           AND post-call hdr[0..4] == V3 (0x20080522 LE).
##           Pre-batch: EINVAL; post-batch: 0 with V3 rewrite.
##        B. capget(hdr={0x44332211}, valid datap) -> EINVAL.
##           Acceptance: proves the new ternary only converts the
##           NULL-datap arm.  Pre- and post-batch both EINVAL.
##        C. capset(hdr={V3}, NULL data)           -> EFAULT.
##           Pre-batch: 0; post-batch: EFAULT.
##        D. capset(hdr={V3}, valid data buf)      -> 0.
##           Acceptance: well-formed call still succeeds.
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   capget/capset NULL-data error matrix: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: preadv/pwritev iov_len gating
##      (batch 267) directly above this block; futimesat tv_usec
##      triple (batch 266) directly below; legacy-syscall ENOSYS
##      sweep further up.
##
##      Known limitations: we still do not enforce Linux's
##      pid != 0 && pid != current -> EPERM gate in sys_capset
##      (kernel/capability.c does `get_user(pid, &header->pid)`
##      after cap_validate_magic and rejects non-self pids).  In
##      kernel context (no caller PCB) this is moot, but a real
##      userspace caller would see a divergence on
##      capset(V3, pid=other, valid_data).  Track in a future
##      batch — needs caller_pid() integration and the existing
##      pid==0 special case to be wired up.  Also: we still
##      silently accept any cap_user_data values (Linux validates
##      that the requested set is a subset of the bounding set);
##      our impl is a no-op so subset enforcement would be
##      cosmetic until we actually maintain per-task cap state.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

298.  syscall/linux: prctl(PR_SET_NAME) NULL-arg2 EFAULT (batch 269).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's kernel/sys.c PR_SET_NAME arm
##      executes
##        comm[sizeof(me->comm) - 1] = 0;
##        if (strncpy_from_user(comm, (char __user *)arg2,
##                              sizeof(me->comm) - 1) < 0)
##            return -EFAULT;
##      strncpy_from_user with a NULL pointer passes access_ok() on
##      x86_64 (NULL < TASK_SIZE_MAX) and then faults on the very
##      first byte fetch, returning a negative errno.  The syscall
##      therefore surfaces -EFAULT.  Our pre-batch sys_prctl arm 15
##      did
##        if user_buf == 0 { return SyscallResult::ok(0); }
##      based on a docstring claim that "Linux ignores NULL here —
##      comm is unchanged but the call still succeeds (the kernel
##      just sees a single NUL byte and stores an empty string)".
##      That claim is wrong — strncpy_from_user does NOT special-case
##      NULL; it just access_ok()s and reads, and the NULL deref
##      raises a #PF that the page fault handler reports as -EFAULT.
##      A probe `prctl(PR_SET_NAME, NULL)` therefore returned 0
##      where Linux returns -EFAULT.
##
##      Fix in sys_prctl arm 15 (line ~5386):
##        Replace the early-NULL silent-OK with
##          if user_buf == 0 { return linux_err(errno::EFAULT); }
##        with a citation comment naming strncpy_from_user and the
##        access_ok-then-fault path.  The rest of the arm (16-byte
##        read, first-NUL-or-15 truncation, UTF-8-or-EINVAL
##        narrowing, pcb::set_name on caller PCB) is unchanged.
##
##      Existing PR_SET_NAME self-test updated (line ~27414):
##        The legacy probe at the top of the prctl block asserted
##        `prctl(PR_SET_NAME, NULL) == 0` and would have been
##        broken by the fix.  Switched the assertion to
##        `== -EFAULT` and updated the citation comment to match
##        kernel/sys.c.  The neighbouring PR_GET_NAME EFAULT probe
##        and the PR_CAPBSET_READ probe are untouched.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux (PR_SET_NAME pair):
##        A. prctl(PR_SET_NAME, NULL)                -> -EFAULT.
##           Pre-batch: 0; post-batch: -EFAULT.  Direct discriminator
##           for the new gate.
##        B. prctl(PR_SET_NAME, &"prctl-batch269\0\0"[16]) -> 0.
##           Acceptance: a well-formed 16-byte name buffer still
##           succeeds (kernel context has no PCB, so the name isn't
##           actually persisted, but the arm still returns 0).
##           Confirms the new gate is restricted to NULL/unreadable
##           pointers and does not regress the happy path.
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   prctl(PR_SET_NAME) NULL-arg2 gating: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: capget/capset NULL-data quadruple
##      (batch 268) directly above; preadv/pwritev iov_len gating
##      (batch 267) above that; futimesat tv_usec triple (batch 266)
##      below.  The legacy prctl PR_GET_NAME EFAULT probe and
##      PR_CAPBSET_READ probe remain in their original positions
##      and pass unchanged.
##
##      Known limitations: this batch does not also tighten the
##      other prctl arms that quietly drop NULL pointers — most
##      notably PR_SET_MM and the PR_SET_VMA arm — though those
##      either gate NULL themselves (PR_SET_MM) or are not yet
##      implemented (PR_SET_VMA).  Also: we still narrow the comm
##      to UTF-8 with EINVAL (Linux accepts raw bytes); documented
##      as a known limitation in the arm's existing comment.  Both
##      tracked here for a future cleanup pass.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

299.  syscall/linux: capset pid != current -> EPERM (batch 270).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's sys_capset (kernel/capability.c)
##      runs this gate sequence after cap_validate_magic succeeds:
##        if (get_user(pid, &header->pid))
##            return -EFAULT;
##        /* may only affect current now */
##        if (pid != 0 && pid != task_pid_vnr(current))
##            return -EPERM;
##        if (copy_from_user(&kdata, data, ...))
##            return -EFAULT;
##      The EPERM gate fires BEFORE the data-NULL / data-EFAULT
##      gate.  Pre-batch we ignored the pid field in the header
##      entirely, so:
##        capset(V3 header w/ pid=other, NULL data)
##          -> -EFAULT (data NULL fired)
##          where Linux returns -EPERM (pid gate fires first).
##        capset(V3 header w/ pid=other, valid data)
##          -> 0 (silent OK after batch 268's data-NULL gate)
##          where Linux returns -EPERM.
##      Flagged as a known limitation in batch 268's todo.
##
##      Fix in sys_capset (line ~7853, V1|V2|V3 arm):
##        After cap_validate_magic equivalent succeeds, before the
##        existing data NULL/validate gate, extract pid_in_hdr from
##        hdr_buf[4..8] and compare to a `current_pid_u32` that's
##        derived from caller_pid() (or 0 in kernel context).  When
##        pid_in_hdr != 0 && pid_in_hdr != current_pid_u32, return
##        -EPERM.  Cite Linux's gate sequence in the comment.
##
##      Judgement call on kernel context: caller_pid() == None is
##      treated as pid 0 — the kernel acts as "current task"
##      (pid 0), so any non-zero pid in the header triggers EPERM
##      and any zero pid bypasses the gate.  This is consistent
##      with the gate's intent ("you can only affect yourself")
##      and lets the boot-test discriminate the EPERM case
##      cleanly.  Documented in the inline comment.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux (capset EPERM triple):
##        A. capset(V3 hdr w/ pid=12345, NULL data)  -> -EPERM
##           Pre-batch: -EFAULT (data NULL fired); post-batch:
##           -EPERM (pid gate now fires first).  Direct
##           discriminator for gate ordering.
##        B. capset(V3 hdr w/ pid=12345, valid data) -> -EPERM
##           Pre-batch: 0; post-batch: -EPERM.  Direct
##           discriminator for the new gate.
##        C. capset(V3 hdr w/ pid=0,     NULL data)  -> -EFAULT
##           Acceptance: pid==0 bypasses the EPERM gate, so the
##           call still surfaces -EFAULT from the data-NULL gate.
##           Pre-batch behaviour preserved, confirming the new
##           gate is restricted to non-self pids.
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   capset pid != current EPERM gating: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: the batch-268 capget/capset
##      quadruple uses pid=0 in its V3 header (hdr_c[4..8] == 0),
##      so all four of its discriminators continue to behave as
##      documented.  prctl(PR_SET_NAME) NULL-arg2 gating
##      (batch 269) directly above; capget/capset NULL-data
##      quadruple (batch 268) above that; preadv/pwritev iov_len
##      gating (batch 267) above that; futimesat tv_usec triple
##      (batch 266) below.
##
##      Known limitations: we still do not validate that the
##      cap_user_data values represent a valid subset of the
##      bounding/permitted/inheritable sets (Linux's
##      cap_capset() does the subset check via security hook).
##      Cosmetic until we maintain per-task cap state.  Also:
##      task_pid_vnr() in Linux is PID-namespace-relative; we do
##      not implement PID namespaces yet, so our comparison is
##      against the global PID.  When PID namespaces land, this
##      gate must be revisited to use the caller's namespace.
##      Tracked here for a future cleanup pass.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

300.  syscall/linux: pread64/pwrite64 count > SSIZE_MAX EINVAL
##      (batch 271).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's rw_verify_area
##      (fs/read_write.c) is invoked from every positional-I/O
##      syscall and gates
##        if (unlikely((ssize_t)count < 0))
##            return -EINVAL;
##      BEFORE the fd lookup.  On 64-bit Linux ssize_t == i64, so
##      this rejects every count in (i64::MAX as u64, u64::MAX]
##      with EINVAL.  Our pre-batch sys_pread64 / sys_pwrite64 did
##      the offset < 0 check and then a noop
##      `usize::try_from(count)` (on x86_64 usize == u64 so it
##      never fails) before calling lookup_caller_fd, so an
##      oversized count surfaced as EBADF in kernel context
##      (wrong errno discriminator) or — for a valid fd — got
##      handed to the backend as an oversized length.
##
##      Fix in sys_pread64 (line ~20640) and sys_pwrite64
##      (line ~20691): add
##        if count > i64::MAX as u64 {
##            return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
##        }
##      immediately above the existing `offset < 0` check, with a
##      comment citing rw_verify_area's `(ssize_t)count < 0`
##      gate.  Match Linux's strict `>` exactly — count ==
##      i64::MAX is accepted.  This is the same SSIZE_MAX pattern
##      that batch 267 added to validate_iov for preadv/pwritev.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux (pread/pwrite SSIZE_MAX
##      triple):
##        A. pread64(fd=-1, buf=0, count=u64::MAX, offset=0)
##             -> -EINVAL.
##           Pre-batch: -EBADF; post-batch: -EINVAL.  Direct
##           discriminator for the new gate vs the lookup_caller_fd
##           fallback.
##        B. pwrite64(fd=-1, buf=0, count=i64::MAX as u64 + 1,
##                    offset=0) -> -EINVAL.
##           Smallest SSIZE_MAX-boundary failure; also exercises
##           the pwrite path symmetrically.
##        C. pread64(fd=-1, buf=0, count=i64::MAX as u64,
##                   offset=0) -> -EBADF.
##           Acceptance: proves the gate is strict `>` exactly
##           matching Linux's `(ssize_t)count < 0` test.  Pre-batch
##           would also have returned EBADF here.
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   pread64/pwrite64 count SSIZE_MAX gating: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: the pread64/pwrite64 negative-
##      offset EINVAL and valid-args EBADF probes at line ~44230
##      use small counts and continue to behave as documented.
##      capset pid != current EPERM (batch 270) directly above;
##      prctl(PR_SET_NAME) NULL-arg2 (batch 269) above that;
##      capget/capset NULL-data quadruple (batch 268) above that;
##      preadv/pwritev iov_len SSIZE_MAX (batch 267) above that;
##      futimesat tv_usec triple (batch 266) below.
##
##      Known limitations: we still do not implement Linux's
##      rw_verify_area pos + count overflow check (which returns
##      -EOVERFLOW when the u64 sum wraps).  In practice with
##      our backends the absence is benign (pread_file_to_user /
##      pwrite_file_from_user cap their transfers at the file
##      size), but if a future backend honours `pos + count`
##      directly it will need the EOVERFLOW gate.  Also: we still
##      do not implement Linux's silent MAX_RW_COUNT clamp (which
##      truncates count down to ((1<<31) - PAGE_SIZE) without
##      erroring); same rationale.  Tracked here for a future
##      batch.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

301.  syscall/linux: pread64/pwrite64 — correct count gate
##      position (batch 272 — fixes batch 271).
##
##      Pre-batch (i.e. post-batch-271) divergence: Linux's actual
##      gate order in ksys_pread64 / ksys_pwrite64 (fs/read_write.c)
##      is:
##        1. pos < 0     -> -EINVAL
##        2. fd lookup   -> -EBADF
##        3. FMODE_PREAD -> -ESPIPE
##        4. vfs_read -> rw_verify_area:
##             (ssize_t)count < 0 -> -EINVAL   <-- count check HERE
##      Batch 271 incorrectly placed the count > SSIZE_MAX gate
##      BEFORE the fd lookup based on a misreading of where
##      rw_verify_area sits in the call chain (it's invoked from
##      inside vfs_read, AFTER fdget).  The observable consequence:
##      pread64(fd=-1, count=u64::MAX, pos=0) returned -EINVAL
##      where Linux returns -EBADF (fd checked first).  Caught on
##      a pre-tick review of Linux 6.x source rather than a
##      regression in the boot test, since the boot test itself
##      was written against the wrong expectation.
##
##      Fix in sys_pread64 (line ~20640) and sys_pwrite64
##      (line ~20691): move the
##        if count > i64::MAX as u64 {
##            return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
##        }
##      gate from "before offset check" to "after lookup_caller_fd
##      succeeds".  This restores Linux's exact order:
##      pos < 0 -> EINVAL, then fd -> EBADF, then count -> EINVAL.
##      In kernel context (caller_pid() == None) the count gate is
##      unreachable (lookup_caller_fd always errors EBADF first),
##      but it correctly fires for any future userspace caller
##      with a valid fd and an oversized count.
##
##      Self-tests rewritten (pread/pwrite gate-order triple):
##        A. pread64(fd=-1, count=u64::MAX, pos=0)
##             batch-271: -EINVAL (BUG); batch-272: -EBADF (Linux).
##        B. pwrite64(fd=-1, count=SSIZE_MAX+1, pos=0)
##             batch-271: -EINVAL (BUG); batch-272: -EBADF (Linux).
##        C. pread64(fd=-1, pos<0, count=1) -> -EINVAL.
##             Acceptance: pos < 0 still fires BEFORE the fd
##             lookup (Linux gate 1).  Pre- and post-batch.
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   pread64/pwrite64 fd-before-count gate order: OK"
##        (renamed from "count SSIZE_MAX gating" to reflect what
##         the discriminators actually verify in kernel context.)
##
##      Lesson captured: when porting a Linux gate sequence, also
##      check the FUNCTION CALL CHAIN — `rw_verify_area` is
##      defined in fs/read_write.c but called from `vfs_read` /
##      `vfs_write`, not the syscall entry.  The user-visible
##      gate position is determined by the call site, not the
##      function definition.  Future batches: when in doubt, dump
##      the actual ksys_* function body and verify the order
##      against the SYSCALL_DEFINE wrapper.
##
##      Existing tests preserved: capset pid != current EPERM
##      (batch 270), prctl(PR_SET_NAME) NULL-arg2 (batch 269),
##      capget/capset NULL-data quadruple (batch 268),
##      preadv/pwritev iov_len SSIZE_MAX (batch 267) — all
##      unaffected by this position move.  The pread64/pwrite64
##      negative-offset + valid-args probes at line ~44230 are
##      unchanged and continue to behave as documented.
##
##      Known limitations: unchanged from batch 271 — pos+count
##      EOVERFLOW gate and MAX_RW_COUNT silent clamp are still
##      not implemented.  Both are benign with our current
##      backends but should land before we gain a backend that
##      honours `pos + count` directly.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

302.  syscall/linux: mremap new_size PAGE_ALIGN overflow gate
##      (batch 273).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's mm/mremap.c does
##        old_len = PAGE_ALIGN(old_len);
##        new_len = PAGE_ALIGN(new_len);
##        if (!new_len)
##            return -EINVAL;
##      i.e. it PAGE_ALIGNs the lengths BEFORE checking for zero.
##      The C macro `(x + PAGE_SIZE - 1) & ~(PAGE_SIZE - 1)` wraps
##      to 0 for any `x > u64::MAX - 0x3FFF`, so Linux rejects
##      `new_len = u64::MAX` (and anything in
##      (u64::MAX - 0x3FFF, u64::MAX]) with -EINVAL.  Our sys_mremap
##      compared `new_size == 0` literally, so the overflowing
##      range fell through to the terminal `linux_err(ENOMEM)`
##      answer.  Probe-distinguishable:
##        mremap(addr=0, old_len=0x4000, new_len=u64::MAX, flags=0,
##               new_addr=0)
##          -> pre-batch: -ENOMEM
##          -> Linux:     -EINVAL
##
##      Fix in sys_mremap (line ~20220): replace the
##        if new_size == 0 { return linux_err(errno::EINVAL); }
##      gate with a checked_add-based PAGE_ALIGN computation:
##        let new_size_aligned = match new_size.checked_add(0x3FFF) {
##            Some(v) => v & !0x3FFFu64,
##            None => 0,
##        };
##        if new_size_aligned == 0 {
##            return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
##        }
##      checked_add returning None mirrors the C wraparound; when
##      it returns Some(v), the `& !0x3FFF` rounds down to the
##      16 KiB boundary (our page size).  Same EINVAL outcome for
##      the original `new_size == 0` case, but now also catches
##      the overflowing tail.  Gate position is unchanged — still
##      between old_addr alignment (gate 4) and the terminal
##      ENOMEM, matching Linux's mremap entry sequence.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux (after the pread/pwrite
##      block); mremap PAGE_ALIGN overflow triple:
##        A. mremap(addr=0, old=0x4000, new=0, flags=0, na=0)
##             pre & post: -EINVAL (existing zero-check).
##        B. mremap(addr=0, old=0x4000, new=u64::MAX, flags=0, na=0)
##             pre: -ENOMEM (BUG); post: -EINVAL (Linux).
##        C. mremap(addr=0, old=0x4000, new=0x4000, flags=0, na=0)
##             Acceptance: a non-overflowing new_len reaches the
##             terminal ENOMEM, confirming we don't over-reject.
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   mremap new_size PAGE_ALIGN overflow gating: OK"
##
##      Existing tests preserved: pread64/pwrite64 fd-before-count
##      (batch 272), capset pid EPERM (batch 270), prctl(PR_SET_NAME)
##      NULL-arg2 (batch 269), capget/capset NULL-data quadruple
##      (batch 268), preadv/pwritev iov_len SSIZE_MAX (batch 267)
##      — all unaffected by this addition (different syscall).
##
##      Known limitations: mremap still does not validate the
##      MREMAP_FIXED new_addr against the user's address space
##      bounds, and we report ENOMEM unconditionally for any
##      non-degenerate call (no real resize-in-place support).
##      Both are acceptable until the VMA backend lands, but
##      should be revisited then.  Also unchanged from batch 272:
##      pread/pwrite EOVERFLOW gate and MAX_RW_COUNT silent
##      clamp are still not implemented.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

303.  syscall/linux: setsockopt/getsockopt drop spurious level<0
##      gate, fd before optval/optlen (batch 274).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's net/socket.c __sys_setsockopt
##      (and __sys_getsockopt) have NO `level` validation at the
##      syscall layer.  setsockopt's gate order is:
##        1. optlen < 0                     -> -EINVAL
##        2. sockfd_lookup_light(fd)        -> -EBADF / -ENOTSOCK
##        3. per-level handler (copy_from_sockptr happens here,
##           and an unsupported level returns -ENOPROTOOPT)
##      getsockopt's is simply:
##        1. sockfd_lookup_light(fd)        -> -EBADF / -ENOTSOCK
##        2. per-level handler (get_user(*optlen) here)
##      Pre-batch our sys_setsockopt did:
##        if (level < 0 || optlen < 0) return EINVAL;
##        if (optlen > 0 && optval == NULL) return EFAULT;
##        if (optlen > 0) validate_user_read(optval, optlen)
##        if (fd bad) return EBADF;
##      and sys_getsockopt similarly opened with `level < 0 ->
##      EINVAL` before any other check.  Probe-distinguishable:
##        setsockopt(fd=-1, level=-1, optlen=4, optval=valid)
##          pre: -EINVAL  (spurious level gate)
##          Linux: -EBADF (fd gate)
##        setsockopt(fd=-1, optlen=8, optval=NULL)
##          pre: -EFAULT  (optval check before fd)
##          Linux: -EBADF (fd gate)
##        getsockopt(fd=-1, level=-1, optval=valid, optlen=valid)
##          pre: -EINVAL  (spurious level gate)
##          Linux: -EBADF (fd gate)
##
##      Fix in sys_setsockopt (line ~18897) and sys_getsockopt
##      (line ~18922):
##        * Remove the `level < 0 -> EINVAL` branches entirely;
##          Linux propagates negative levels to the protocol
##          handler, which returns -ENOPROTOOPT (only reachable
##          after the fd succeeds).
##        * Reorder setsockopt to: optlen<0 (gate 1), then
##          validate_linux_fd (gate 2), then optval check (gate 3).
##        * Reorder getsockopt to: validate_linux_fd (gate 1),
##          then optlen-ptr check / read+write validation (gate 2).
##        * Function docs now spell out the Linux gate order and
##          note that in kernel context (caller_pid() == None)
##          validate_linux_fd is a no-op, so probes fall through
##          to the terminal EBADF.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux (after the mremap block);
##      setsockopt/getsockopt no-level-gate quadruple:
##        A. setsockopt(fd=3, level=-1, optlen=4, optval=valid)
##             pre: -EINVAL; post: -EBADF (terminal, fd no-op).
##        B. setsockopt(fd=3, level=0, optlen=-1, optval=valid)
##             Acceptance: optlen<0 -> -EINVAL gate preserved
##             (Linux gate 1).  Both pre and post: -EINVAL.
##        C. getsockopt(fd=3, level=-1, optval=valid, optlen=valid)
##             pre: -EINVAL; post: -EBADF (terminal, fd no-op).
##        D. getsockopt(fd=3, level=0, optval=valid, optlen=NULL)
##             Acceptance: optlen-NULL -> -EFAULT gate preserved,
##             just relocated to AFTER fd lookup (no-op in kernel
##             ctx, so EFAULT still fires).  Both: -EFAULT.
##      Stack-allocated buffers via (&raw const buf[0]) plus
##      core::hint::black_box(&buf) to keep the pointers live for
##      validate_user_read; in kernel context the validator is a
##      no-op but the test still exercises the gate-position.
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   setsockopt/getsockopt no-level gate: OK"
##
##      Legacy test updated: the existing post-recvmmsg test at
##      line ~42407 asserted `setsockopt(level=u32::MAX -> -1)
##      -> -EINVAL` (which encoded the bug).  Now updated to
##      assert -EBADF (terminal, kernel-ctx fd no-op), with a
##      comment explaining the pre/post discriminant.  The
##      sibling test that asserts `setsockopt(level=1) -> -EBADF`
##      is unchanged.  The pre-existing getsockopt EFAULT/EBADF
##      pair (lines ~42419/42425) also continues to pass: those
##      probes use level=1 (positive), which was not affected by
##      the level<0 gate, and their relative ordering still
##      matches the new flow.
##
##      Existing tests preserved: mremap PAGE_ALIGN overflow
##      (batch 273), pread64/pwrite64 fd-before-count (batch 272),
##      capset pid EPERM (batch 270), prctl(PR_SET_NAME) NULL-arg2
##      (batch 269), capget/capset NULL-data quadruple (batch 268),
##      preadv/pwritev iov_len SSIZE_MAX (batch 267) — all
##      unaffected by this addition.
##
##      Known limitations: we still don't implement a real
##      per-protocol socket layer, so the post-fd handler path
##      always answers -EBADF rather than dispatching to a real
##      level handler that would surface -ENOPROTOOPT for
##      unknown levels with a valid socket fd.  That's fine
##      until the socket layer lands; the gate position is
##      correct so the future handler can slot in without
##      moving any of the existing gates.  Unchanged from batch
##      273: mremap MREMAP_FIXED new_addr bounds; from batch 272:
##      pread/pwrite EOVERFLOW gate and MAX_RW_COUNT silent
##      clamp still not implemented.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

304.  syscall/linux: listen/shutdown drop spurious entry gates
##      (batch 275).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's net/socket.c __sys_listen
##      and __sys_shutdown both delegate everything past fd
##      lookup to the per-protocol handler.
##        __sys_listen:
##          sock = sockfd_lookup_light(fd, ...);   // -EBADF
##          if ((unsigned int)backlog > somaxconn)
##              backlog = somaxconn;               // SILENT clamp
##          sock->ops->listen(sock, backlog);
##        __sys_shutdown:
##          sock = sockfd_lookup_light(fd, ...);   // -EBADF
##          security_socket_shutdown(sock, how);
##          sock->ops->shutdown(sock, how);        // -EINVAL here
##      i.e. negative backlog is silently clamped (not rejected)
##      and `how` out-of-range is rejected inside the protocol
##      handler (e.g. inet_shutdown), not at syscall entry.
##      Pre-batch we returned -EINVAL at syscall entry for both,
##      hijacking -EBADF for bogus fds:
##        listen(fd=-1, backlog=-1)
##          pre: -EINVAL  (spurious backlog<0 gate)
##          Linux: -EBADF (fd gate)
##        shutdown(fd=-1, how=9)
##          pre: -EINVAL  (spurious how-range gate)
##          Linux: -EBADF (fd gate)
##
##      Fix in sys_listen (line ~18608) and sys_shutdown
##      (line ~18985):
##        * Remove `backlog < 0 -> EINVAL`.  Linux silently
##          clamps; we'll do the same when a real socket layer
##          lands (the clamp is per-namespace somaxconn).  For
##          now the terminal EBADF is the truthful answer.
##        * Remove `!(0..=2).contains(&how) -> EINVAL`.  Linux's
##          per-protocol shutdown handler (e.g. inet_shutdown)
##          returns -EINVAL for out-of-range how, but only after
##          fd lookup succeeds.  Same "moved into handler"
##          treatment we'll apply when the socket layer lands.
##        * Function docs now spell out the Linux gate order.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux (after the
##      setsockopt/getsockopt block); listen/shutdown drop-entry
##      quadruple:
##        A. listen(fd=3, backlog=-1)
##             pre: -EINVAL; post: -EBADF (terminal, fd no-op).
##        B. listen(fd=3, backlog=5)
##             Acceptance: a valid backlog still terminates at
##             -EBADF (unchanged pre/post).
##        C. shutdown(fd=3, how=9)
##             pre: -EINVAL; post: -EBADF (terminal, fd no-op).
##        D. shutdown(fd=3, how=2 == SHUT_RDWR)
##             Acceptance: a valid how still terminates at
##             -EBADF (unchanged pre/post).
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   listen/shutdown drop entry gates: OK"
##
##      Legacy tests updated:
##        * line ~42244: "listen negative backlog -> EINVAL"
##          flipped to "-> EBADF" with a comment explaining the
##          pre/post discriminant.
##        * line ~42496: "shutdown bad how -> EINVAL"
##          flipped to "-> EBADF" with the same kind of comment.
##        * The sibling "listen valid -> EBADF" and "shutdown
##          valid -> EBADF" assertions still hold (the changes
##          don't move those gates).
##
##      Existing tests preserved: setsockopt/getsockopt no-level
##      gate (batch 274), mremap PAGE_ALIGN overflow (batch 273),
##      pread64/pwrite64 fd-before-count (batch 272), capset pid
##      EPERM (batch 270), prctl(PR_SET_NAME) NULL-arg2 (batch
##      269), capget/capset NULL-data quadruple (batch 268),
##      preadv/pwritev iov_len SSIZE_MAX (batch 267) — all
##      unaffected by this addition.
##
##      Known limitations: same as batch 274 — no real socket
##      layer yet, so the terminal -EBADF in shutdown's valid
##      branch and listen's valid branch is a stand-in for the
##      handler's response.  When the socket layer lands, both
##      will need a per-protocol handler call after fd lookup,
##      which is the natural place for the somaxconn clamp and
##      the how-range check to live.  Also still pending from
##      earlier batches: accept/accept4 sockaddr-before-fd
##      reorder (Linux: flags -> fd -> sockaddr; ours: flags ->
##      sockaddr -> fd); pread/pwrite EOVERFLOW gate and
##      MAX_RW_COUNT silent clamp; mremap MREMAP_FIXED new_addr
##      bounds; sys_perf_event_open in-struct __reserved_2 /
##      __reserved_3 field validation.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

305.  syscall/linux: socket/socketpair EAFNOSUPPORT + EOPNOTSUPP,
##      flag-mask-first gate order (batch 276).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's net/socket.c distinguishes
##      three rejection modes for socket / socketpair:
##        * "Unknown address family"  -> -EAFNOSUPPORT (errno 97)
##        * "Family known but ops->X is NULL" -> -EOPNOTSUPP
##        * "Caller passed garbage"   -> -EINVAL
##      and applies them in this order:
##        Gate 1: type-flag bits (~SOCK_TYPE_MASK & ~(CLOEXEC|NONBLOCK))
##                                                  -> -EINVAL
##        Gate 2: __sock_create:
##          family < 0 || family >= NPROTO  -> -EAFNOSUPPORT
##          type out of [1,SOCK_MAX)        -> -EINVAL
##        Gate 3: (socketpair only) ops->socketpair == NULL
##                                                  -> -EOPNOTSUPP
##        Gate 4: (socketpair only) usockvec validation -> -EFAULT
##
##      Pre-batch our errno table didn't even define EAFNOSUPPORT,
##      so we returned -EINVAL for any unknown family on both
##      socket and socketpair, and for any non-AF_UNIX domain on
##      socketpair.  That collapsed three distinct probe outcomes
##      to one errno, which loses information for userspace
##      probes that distinguish "kernel doesn't recognise this
##      AF_*" from "kernel rejected the call".  We also checked
##      family BEFORE the type-flag mask, so a probe with a bad
##      flag bit AND a bad family hit the family branch (correct
##      errno but wrong gate).
##
##      Fix:
##        * Add `pub const EAFNOSUPPORT: i32 = 97;` to the
##          errno module.
##        * sys_socket (line ~18534): reorder gates to Linux's
##          flag-mask -> family -> type sequence; return
##          -EAFNOSUPPORT for unknown family.
##        * sys_socketpair (line ~18579): reorder identically;
##          add the Linux ops->socketpair gate so non-AF_UNIX
##          (but otherwise valid) families return -EOPNOTSUPP
##          instead of -EINVAL.  AF_UNIX still falls through to
##          ENOSYS (no socketpair backend yet).
##        * Function docs spell out the Linux gate order.
##
##      Self-tests added in dispatch_linux (after the
##      listen/shutdown block); socket/socketpair EAFNOSUPPORT
##      quintuple:
##        A. socket(domain=99, type=SOCK_STREAM)
##             pre: -EINVAL; post: -EAFNOSUPPORT.
##        B. socket(domain=99, type=0x10001)
##             Acceptance: bad flag bit fires gate 1 first;
##             both pre and post return -EINVAL but for a
##             different reason (pre: family collapse; post:
##             reordered gate-1).
##        C. socketpair(domain=2 (AF_INET), type=SOCK_STREAM,
##             sv=valid)
##             pre: -EINVAL; post: -EOPNOTSUPP.
##        D. socketpair(domain=99, type=SOCK_STREAM, sv=valid)
##             pre: -EINVAL; post: -EAFNOSUPPORT.
##        E. socketpair(domain=1 (AF_UNIX), type=SOCK_STREAM,
##             sv=valid)
##             Acceptance: AF_UNIX still terminates at -ENOSYS
##             (no backend), unchanged pre/post.
##      Stack-allocated sv buffer via (&raw const buf[0]) plus
##      core::hint::black_box(&buf).
##
##      Marker:
##        "[syscall/linux]   socket/socketpair EAFNOSUPPORT gating: OK"
##
##      Legacy tests updated:
##        * line ~42190: "socket bad domain -> EINVAL" -> EAFNOSUPPORT
##          with comment explaining the pre/post discriminant.
##        * line ~42215: "socketpair non-AF_UNIX -> EINVAL" ->
##          EOPNOTSUPP with comment explaining the gate.
##        * Sibling assertions for socket bad type, socket bad
##          flag, socket valid -> ENOSYS, socketpair NULL sv,
##          socketpair valid -> ENOSYS still hold (the reorder
##          doesn't move their gates).
##
##      Existing tests preserved: listen/shutdown drop entry
##      gates (batch 275), setsockopt/getsockopt no-level
##      gate (batch 274), mremap PAGE_ALIGN overflow (batch
##      273), pread64/pwrite64 fd-before-count (batch 272),
##      capset pid EPERM (batch 270), prctl(PR_SET_NAME)
##      NULL-arg2 (batch 269), capget/capset NULL-data
##      quadruple (batch 268), preadv/pwritev iov_len
##      SSIZE_MAX (batch 267) — all unaffected.
##
##      Known limitations: still pending — accept/accept4
##      sockaddr-before-fd reorder (Linux: flags -> fd ->
##      sockaddr; ours: flags -> sockaddr -> fd); bind /
##      connect sockaddr-before-fd (similar shape); per-protocol
##      protocol-number validation (e.g. socket(AF_INET,
##      SOCK_STREAM, 99) should -EPROTONOSUPPORT, we accept it
##      and return ENOSYS); ESOCKTNOSUPPORT for type valid but
##      unimplemented; pread/pwrite EOVERFLOW gate and
##      MAX_RW_COUNT silent clamp; mremap MREMAP_FIXED new_addr
##      bounds; sys_perf_event_open in-struct __reserved_2 /
##      __reserved_3 field validation.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 21s).

## 306. syscall/linux: sys_perf_event_open — in-struct
##      __reserved_2 field gating (batch 277).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's perf_copy_attr()
##      (kernel/events/core.c) reads the userspace attr,
##      then checks
##          if (attr->__reserved_2)  return -EINVAL;
##      where __reserved_2 is a u16 at offset 110 inside
##      struct perf_event_attr (present since
##      PERF_ATTR_SIZE_VER5, i.e. attr_size >= 112).
##      Our sys_perf_event_open validated only the trailing
##      bytes from PERF_ATTR_KSIZE (=136) to attr_size — it
##      ignored in-struct reserved fields entirely. So a
##      probe with size=136 and __reserved_2!=0 fell through
##      the attr validation entirely and hit the ENOSYS
##      acceptance arm, instead of -EINVAL.
##
##      Fix: in sys_perf_event_open, after the trailing-zero
##      tail check and before the pid/cpu validation, copy
##      two bytes from (attr_p + 110) when attr_size >= 112
##      and return -EINVAL if the resulting u16 is non-zero.
##      The copy uses validate_user_read coverage already
##      established for [attr_p, attr_p+attr_size) above.
##
##      Self-tests (kernel-context probe triple, inside
##      dispatch_linux Linux-ABI self-test block):
##        A. attr_buf[136]; size=136; __reserved_2 byte 110=1
##           PERF_EVENT_OPEN(attr, 0, -1, 0, 0) -> -EINVAL.
##           (pre-batch: -ENOSYS; post-batch: -EINVAL).
##        B. attr_buf[136]; size=136; __reserved_2 zero;
##           PERF_EVENT_OPEN(attr, 0, -1, 0, 0) -> -ENOSYS
##           (acceptance — confirms the gate fires only when
##           the reserved field is non-zero).
##        C. attr_short[64]; size=64 (< 112, so the reserved
##           field is below attr_size and not validated);
##           PERF_EVENT_OPEN(attr, 0, -1, 0, 0) -> -ENOSYS
##           (acceptance — confirms the gate is conditional
##           on attr_size >= 112 and does not regress
##           legacy/older attr sizes).
##      Marker: "perf_event_open __reserved_2 gating: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved: socket/socketpair
##      EAFNOSUPPORT gating (batch 276), listen/shutdown
##      drop entry gates (batch 275), setsockopt/getsockopt
##      no-level gate (batch 274), mremap PAGE_ALIGN
##      overflow (batch 273), pread64/pwrite64
##      fd-before-count (batch 272), capset pid EPERM (270),
##      prctl(PR_SET_NAME) NULL-arg2 (269), capget/capset
##      NULL-data quadruple (268), preadv/pwritev iov_len
##      SSIZE_MAX (267) — all unaffected.
##
##      Known limitations: still pending — accept/accept4
##      sockaddr-before-fd reorder (Linux: flags -> fd ->
##      sockaddr; ours: flags -> sockaddr -> fd); bind /
##      connect sockaddr-before-fd; per-protocol
##      protocol-number validation (socket(AF_INET,
##      SOCK_STREAM, 99) should -EPROTONOSUPPORT, we accept
##      it and return ENOSYS); ESOCKTNOSUPPORT for type
##      valid but unimplemented; pread/pwrite EOVERFLOW gate
##      and MAX_RW_COUNT silent clamp; mremap MREMAP_FIXED
##      new_addr bounds; sys_perf_event_open in-struct
##      __reserved_3 field validation (this batch covered
##      __reserved_2 only — __reserved_3 is a separate u64
##      reserved field at a later offset, still unchecked).
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 26s).

## 307. syscall/linux: sys_mremap — MREMAP_FIXED new_addr
##      out-of-user-space / overflow gating (batch 278).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's mm/mremap.c::mremap_to()
##      does, after PAGE_ALIGN'ing the lengths:
##          if (new_len > TASK_SIZE ||
##              new_addr > TASK_SIZE - new_len)
##              goto out;   /* -> -EINVAL */
##      The second clause is written as a subtraction precisely
##      to catch both:
##        - new_addr legitimately above the user/kernel split, AND
##        - new_addr + new_len overflowing past 2^64 (wraparound
##          case: TASK_SIZE - new_len is a huge unsigned, but
##          new_addr can still exceed it).
##      Pre-batch our sys_mremap accepted MREMAP_FIXED|MAYMOVE
##      with new_addr near u64::MAX and fell through to ENOMEM,
##      diverging from Linux's documented EINVAL on this gate.
##
##      Fix: in sys_mremap, after the existing flag/MAYMOVE/align
##      checks and the new_size PAGE_ALIGN overflow check from
##      batch 273, and only when MREMAP_FIXED (flag bit 2) is
##      set, do:
##          let fits = match new_addr.checked_add(new_size_aligned) {
##              Some(end) => end <= USER_SPACE_END,
##              None      => false,
##          };
##          if !fits { return -EINVAL; }
##      USER_SPACE_END (0x0000_8000_0000_0000) is our x86_64
##      equivalent of Linux's TASK_SIZE on the same arch.  The
##      checked_add collapses the two-part Linux check into a
##      single overflow-safe expression: None on wraparound,
##      Some(end) > USER_SPACE_END on out-of-bounds.
##
##      Self-tests (kernel-context probe triple in dispatch_linux):
##        A. flags=MAYMOVE|FIXED(3), old_addr=0, old_size=0x4000,
##           new_size=0x4000, new_addr=0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_C000
##           (page-aligned, but new_addr+new_size overflows past
##           u64::MAX) -> EINVAL.
##           (pre-batch: ENOMEM; post-batch: EINVAL).
##        B. same args but new_addr=0x10_0000_0000_0000
##           (page-aligned, above USER_SPACE_END) -> EINVAL.
##           (pre-batch: ENOMEM; post-batch: EINVAL).
##        C. same args but new_addr=0 (inside user space) ->
##           ENOMEM (acceptance — confirms the gate only fires
##           for out-of-bounds new_addr and doesn't regress the
##           pre-existing fall-through-to-ENOMEM path).
##      Marker: "mremap MREMAP_FIXED new_addr bounds: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved: perf_event_open __reserved_2
##      gating (batch 277), socket/socketpair EAFNOSUPPORT
##      gating (276), listen/shutdown drop entry gates (275),
##      setsockopt/getsockopt no-level gate (274), mremap
##      new_size PAGE_ALIGN overflow (273), pread64/pwrite64
##      fd-before-count (272), capset pid EPERM (270),
##      prctl(PR_SET_NAME) NULL-arg2 (269), capget/capset
##      NULL-data quadruple (268), preadv/pwritev iov_len
##      SSIZE_MAX (267) — all unaffected.  In particular the
##      existing mremap test rejecting new_size==u64::MAX with
##      EINVAL (batch 273) continues to fire BEFORE the new
##      MREMAP_FIXED bounds check.
##
##      Known limitations: still pending — accept/accept4 and
##      bind/connect sockaddr-before-fd reorders (not
##      probe-distinguishable in kernel context because
##      validate_linux_fd is a no-op there; will need real
##      user-context tests); per-protocol protocol-number
##      validation (socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 99) should
##      -EPROTONOSUPPORT, we accept and return ENOSYS);
##      ESOCKTNOSUPPORT for type valid but unimplemented;
##      pread/pwrite EOVERFLOW gate at pos+count and silent
##      MAX_RW_COUNT clamp; sys_perf_event_open in-struct
