##      __reserved_3 field validation; sys_mremap rejecting
##      MREMAP_FIXED when new_addr is below the userspace
##      mmap floor (we currently allow new_addr=0).
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 308. syscall/linux: sys_socket — per-(family, type)
##      protocol whitelist returning -EPROTONOSUPPORT
##      (batch 279).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's per-family create
##      paths (net/ipv4/af_inet.c::inet_create,
##      net/ipv6/af_inet6.c::inet6_create,
##      net/unix/af_unix.c::unix_create) walk a switch over
##      `protocol` and return -EPROTONOSUPPORT (errno 93)
##      when the (type, protocol) pair is not in the
##      family's accepted set.  Probes commonly use this
##      errno to discriminate "kernel doesn't speak this
##      (type, protocol) combination" from "kernel doesn't
##      know this syscall at all" (ENOSYS).  We accepted any
##      non-zero protocol and fell through to ENOSYS,
##      hiding the discriminator.
##
##      Fix: add `pub const EPROTONOSUPPORT: i32 = 93;` to
##      the errno module, then add a gate 4 to sys_socket
##      that fires when protocol != 0:
##        - AF_UNIX (1): any non-zero protocol ->
##          -EPROTONOSUPPORT (Linux's unix_create only
##          accepts protocol == 0).
##        - AF_INET (2) / AF_INET6 (10) + SOCK_STREAM (1):
##          protocol must be 6 (IPPROTO_TCP).
##        - AF_INET (2) / AF_INET6 (10) + SOCK_DGRAM (2):
##          protocol must be 17 (IPPROTO_UDP) or 136
##          (IPPROTO_UDPLITE).
##        - All other (family, type) slots fall through to
##          the terminal ENOSYS (SOCK_RAW accepts any
##          protocol on Linux; AF_NETLINK / AF_PACKET have
##          family-specific protocol semantics we don't
##          model yet, and over-rejecting would diverge
##          from Linux harder than under-rejecting).
##      The gate fires AFTER the existing family /
##      flag-mask / type gates so the prior batch-276
##      EAFNOSUPPORT and the type-range EINVAL still win
##      for their respective inputs.
##
##      Self-tests (kernel-context probe heptuple in
##      dispatch_linux):
##        A. socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 99) -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
##           (pre-batch: ENOSYS; post-batch: EPROTONOSUPPORT).
##        B. socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 99)  -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
##           (pre-batch: ENOSYS; post-batch: EPROTONOSUPPORT).
##        C. socket(AF_INET6, SOCK_STREAM, 99) -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
##           (pre-batch: ENOSYS; post-batch: EPROTONOSUPPORT).
##        D. socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_STREAM, 99) -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
##           (pre-batch: ENOSYS; post-batch: EPROTONOSUPPORT;
##            AF_UNIX rejects any non-zero protocol).
##        E. socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 6)  -> ENOSYS
##           (acceptance — TCP is the canonical SOCK_STREAM
##            protocol for AF_INET).
##        F. socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 17)  -> ENOSYS
##           (acceptance — UDP is the canonical SOCK_DGRAM
##            protocol for AF_INET).
##        G. socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, 99)    -> ENOSYS
##           (acceptance — SOCK_RAW accepts any protocol on
##            Linux; confirms the gate is type-scoped, not a
##            blanket protocol-99 reject).
##      Marker: "socket EPROTONOSUPPORT gating: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved: mremap MREMAP_FIXED
##      new_addr bounds (278), perf_event_open __reserved_2
##      gating (277), socket/socketpair EAFNOSUPPORT gating
##      (276) — the new gate fires AFTER the EAFNOSUPPORT
##      gate so unknown families still take priority;
##      listen/shutdown drop entry gates (275),
##      setsockopt/getsockopt no-level gate (274), mremap
##      new_size PAGE_ALIGN overflow (273), pread64/pwrite64
##      fd-before-count (272), capset pid EPERM (270),
##      prctl(PR_SET_NAME) NULL-arg2 (269), capget/capset
##      NULL-data quadruple (268), preadv/pwritev iov_len
##      SSIZE_MAX (267) — all unaffected.  The legacy
##      "socket valid -> ENOSYS" test at line ~42385 still
##      passes (protocol=0).
##
##      Known limitations: still pending — accept/accept4
##      and bind/connect sockaddr-before-fd reorders (not
##      probe-distinguishable in kernel context);
##      ESOCKTNOSUPPORT for SOCK_RDM on AF_INET (Linux
##      returns ESOCKTNOSUPPORT here, we return ENOSYS);
##      pread/pwrite EOVERFLOW gate at pos+count and silent
##      MAX_RW_COUNT clamp; sys_mremap rejecting
##      MREMAP_FIXED below the userspace mmap floor; the
##      socketpair() per-protocol gate (this batch covers
##      socket() only; socketpair() has the same shape but
##      its current EOPNOTSUPP gate already fires for the
##      common AF_INET case before the protocol check would
##      matter, so the divergence surface is narrower).
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

## 309. syscall/linux: sys_socket — ESOCKTNOSUPPORT for
##      SOCK_RDM on AF_INET / AF_INET6 / AF_UNIX
##      (batch 280).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's per-family create
##      paths (inet_create, inet6_create, unix_create) all
##      initialise `err = -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT;` before walking
##      the protocol-list-for-this-type.  For socket types
##      a family doesn't implement (e.g. SOCK_RDM=4 on
##      AF_INET / AF_INET6 / AF_UNIX) the list walk has no
##      entries, err is never overwritten, and the syscall
##      returns -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT (errno 94).  We accepted
##      these (family, type) combinations and fell through
##      to ENOSYS, hiding the "family knows protocols but
##      not this type within it" discriminator.
##
##      Fix: add `pub const ESOCKTNOSUPPORT: i32 = 94;` to
##      the errno module, then add a gate 4 in sys_socket
##      (between the existing type-range EINVAL and the
##      batch-279 EPROTONOSUPPORT gate, matching Linux's
##      err-init-before-list-walk ordering):
##          if sock_type == 4 {
##              match domain {
##                  1 | 2 | 10 => return -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT,
##                  _ => {}
##              }
##          }
##      The gate is conservative: AF_NETLINK (16) and
##      AF_PACKET (17) also reject SOCK_RDM on Linux but
##      their type/protocol semantics are family-specific
##      and we'd rather under-reject than over-reject
##      without modelling each family.  This keeps the gate
##      strictly more accepting than Linux for the unmodelled
##      slots.
##
##      Self-tests (kernel-context probe sextuple in
##      dispatch_linux):
##        A. socket(AF_INET=2, SOCK_RDM=4, 0)
##           pre: ENOSYS ; post: ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
##        B. socket(AF_INET6=10, SOCK_RDM=4, 0)
##           pre: ENOSYS ; post: ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
##        C. socket(AF_UNIX=1, SOCK_RDM=4, 0)
##           pre: ENOSYS ; post: ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
##        D. socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RDM=4, protocol=99)
##           Confirms ESOCKTNOSUPPORT precedes the proto
##           whitelist gate (Linux's err-init ordering):
##           pre: ENOSYS ; post: ESOCKTNOSUPPORT.
##        E. socket(AF_NETLINK=16, SOCK_RDM=4, 0)
##           Acceptance — AF_NETLINK not in allowlist;
##           ENOSYS.
##        F. socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM=1, 0)
##           Acceptance regression check — STREAM is
##           supported; ENOSYS.
##      Marker: "socket ESOCKTNOSUPPORT gating: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved: socket EPROTONOSUPPORT
##      gating (279), mremap MREMAP_FIXED new_addr bounds
##      (278), perf_event_open __reserved_2 gating (277),
##      socket/socketpair EAFNOSUPPORT gating (276),
##      listen/shutdown drop entry gates (275),
##      setsockopt/getsockopt no-level gate (274), mremap
##      new_size PAGE_ALIGN overflow (273), pread64/pwrite64
##      fd-before-count (272), capset pid EPERM (270),
##      prctl(PR_SET_NAME) NULL-arg2 (269), capget/capset
##      NULL-data quadruple (268), preadv/pwritev iov_len
##      SSIZE_MAX (267) — all unaffected.  Batch 279's
##      A/B/C/D EPROTONOSUPPORT probes use SOCK_STREAM/DGRAM,
##      which are not in the SOCK_RDM gate's match arm, so
##      the new gate is invisible to them.
##
##      Known limitations: still pending — accept/accept4
##      and bind/connect sockaddr-before-fd reorders (not
##      probe-distinguishable in kernel context);
##      AF_NETLINK / AF_PACKET ESOCKTNOSUPPORT gates;
##      pread/pwrite EOVERFLOW gate at pos+count and silent
##      MAX_RW_COUNT clamp; sys_mremap rejecting
##      MREMAP_FIXED below the userspace mmap floor;
##      socketpair() per-(family,type,protocol) gating
##      mirror of socket()'s batches 279+280.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

## 310. syscall/linux: sys_socketpair — mirror sys_socket
##      batches 279+280: per-(family,type,protocol) gating
##      returning -EPROTONOSUPPORT / -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
##      before the ops->socketpair == NULL EOPNOTSUPP
##      gate (batch 281).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's __sys_socketpair
##      calls sock_create(family, type, protocol, ...);
##      the per-family create function (inet_create,
##      inet6_create, unix_create) initialises
##      err = -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT and then walks inetsw[type]
##      / runs the family's protocol switch.  Both
##      ESOCKTNOSUPPORT (for SOCK_RDM on AF_INET/AF_INET6
##      /AF_UNIX) and EPROTONOSUPPORT (for non-zero protocol
##      not in the family's accepted set) fire BEFORE
##      sock_create returns successfully — only on the
##      success path does the kernel check
##      sock1->ops->socketpair == NULL and return
##      EOPNOTSUPP.  Pre-batch our sys_socketpair jumped
##      straight to "domain != AF_UNIX -> EOPNOTSUPP",
##      collapsing both Linux-level discriminators
##      (ESOCKTNOSUPPORT and EPROTONOSUPPORT) into the same
##      EOPNOTSUPP.
##
##      Fix: in sys_socketpair, between the type-range
##      EINVAL gate and the existing "domain != 1 ->
##      EOPNOTSUPP" gate, insert two new gates that mirror
##      sys_socket batches 279 (proto whitelist) and 280
##      (SOCK_RDM family allowlist), in Linux's err-init
##      order (ESOCKTNOSUPPORT before EPROTONOSUPPORT):
##        Gate 3a: SOCK_RDM (type 4) on AF_UNIX(1) /
##                 AF_INET(2) / AF_INET6(10) -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT.
##        Gate 3b: protocol != 0 mismatched against
##                 (family, type) pair -> EPROTONOSUPPORT.
##                 AF_UNIX rejects any non-zero protocol;
##                 AF_INET/AF_INET6 + SOCK_STREAM must be
##                 6 (TCP); + SOCK_DGRAM must be 17 (UDP)
##                 or 136 (UDPLITE); other slots
##                 (SOCK_RAW, SOCK_SEQPACKET, AF_NETLINK,
##                 AF_PACKET) fall through unchanged.
##      Existing Gate 3 EOPNOTSUPP for domain != AF_UNIX
##      still fires when the proto/type gates pass, so the
##      common AF_INET / SOCK_STREAM / 0 socketpair() call
##      still returns EOPNOTSUPP exactly as before.
##
##      Self-tests (kernel-context probe quintet in
##      dispatch_linux):
##        A. socketpair(AF_INET, SOCK_RDM, 0, sv)
##           pre: EOPNOTSUPP ; post: ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
##        B. socketpair(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 99, sv)
##           pre: EOPNOTSUPP ; post: EPROTONOSUPPORT
##        C. socketpair(AF_UNIX, SOCK_STREAM, 99, sv)
##           pre: ENOSYS (AF_UNIX path fell through to
##           the terminal ENOSYS) ; post: EPROTONOSUPPORT
##        D. socketpair(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0, sv)
##           Regression check — EOPNOTSUPP still wins for
##           the common ipv4-stream case.
##        E. socketpair(AF_UNIX, SOCK_RDM, 0, sv)
##           pre: ENOSYS ; post: ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
##      Marker: "socketpair ESOCKT/EPROTO gating: OK".
##
##      Existing tests preserved: socket ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
##      gating (280), socket EPROTONOSUPPORT gating (279),
##      mremap MREMAP_FIXED new_addr bounds (278),
##      perf_event_open __reserved_2 gating (277),
##      socket/socketpair EAFNOSUPPORT gating (276) —
##      socketpair(99, 1, 0, sv) still returns EAFNOSUPPORT
##      because the family gate fires before the new
##      ESOCKT/EPROTO gates; socketpair(2, 1, 0, sv) still
##      returns EOPNOTSUPP (this is the regression check
##      above); socketpair(1, 1, 0, sv) still returns
##      ENOSYS for AF_UNIX-with-zero-protocol; legacy
##      socketpair NULL sv / EFAULT tests unchanged.
##      listen/shutdown drop entry gates (275),
##      setsockopt/getsockopt no-level gate (274), mremap
##      new_size PAGE_ALIGN overflow (273), pread64/pwrite64
##      fd-before-count (272), capset pid EPERM (270),
##      prctl(PR_SET_NAME) NULL-arg2 (269), capget/capset
##      NULL-data quadruple (268), preadv/pwritev iov_len
##      SSIZE_MAX (267) — all unaffected.
##
##      Known limitations: still pending — accept/accept4
##      and bind/connect sockaddr-before-fd reorders (not
##      probe-distinguishable in kernel context);
##      AF_NETLINK / AF_PACKET ESOCKTNOSUPPORT gates (we
##      under-reject these to stay conservative without
##      modelling each family's type matrix); pread/pwrite
##      EOVERFLOW gate at pos+count and silent
##      MAX_RW_COUNT clamp; sys_mremap rejecting
##      MREMAP_FIXED below the userspace mmap floor.
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 22s).

## 311. syscall/linux: sys_socket + sys_socketpair —
##      AF_NETLINK / AF_PACKET socket-type allowlist
##      (batch 282).
##
##      Pre-batch divergence: Linux's netlink_create
##      (net/netlink/af_netlink.c) explicitly rejects
##      sock->type not in {SOCK_RAW, SOCK_DGRAM} with
##      -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT; packet_create (net/packet/
##      af_packet.c) does the same for AF_PACKET
##      (additionally accepting SOCK_PACKET=10, which is
##      outside our 1..=5 type range and already rejected
##      by gate 3 EINVAL).  Pre-batch we fell through for
##      these families with any sock_type in 1..=5,
##      returning ENOSYS from sys_socket and EOPNOTSUPP
##      from sys_socketpair (the domain != AF_UNIX gate).
##      Probes use ESOCKTNOSUPPORT to discriminate "family
##      known but this type unsupported" from "kernel
##      doesn't speak this syscall".
##
##      Fix: widen the per-family socket-type allowlist
##      gate (introduced for AF_UNIX/AF_INET/AF_INET6 in
##      batch 280) to a single match expression covering
##      all five well-known families:
##          match (domain, sock_type) {
##              (1, 4)                  => ESOCKTNOSUPPORT,
##              (2 | 10, 4)             => ESOCKTNOSUPPORT,
##              (16 | 17, 1 | 4 | 5)    => ESOCKTNOSUPPORT,
##              _                       => {}
##          }
##      Applied identically to sys_socket and
##      sys_socketpair so socketpair() surfaces the same
##      ESOCKTNOSUPPORT discriminator before reaching the
##      EOPNOTSUPP "ops->socketpair == NULL" gate.
##
##      Self-tests (kernel-context probe set in
##      dispatch_linux):
##        socket():
##          A. socket(AF_NETLINK, SOCK_STREAM, 0)
##             pre: ENOSYS ; post: ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
##          B. socket(AF_NETLINK, SOCK_SEQPACKET, 0)
##             pre: ENOSYS ; post: ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
##          C. socket(AF_PACKET,  SOCK_STREAM, 0)
##             pre: ENOSYS ; post: ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
##          D. socket(AF_PACKET,  SOCK_RDM, 0)
##             pre: ENOSYS ; post: ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
##          E. socket(AF_NETLINK, SOCK_DGRAM, 0)
##             Acceptance — DGRAM in allowlist; ENOSYS.
##          F. socket(AF_PACKET,  SOCK_RAW, 0)
##             Acceptance — RAW in allowlist; ENOSYS.
##        socketpair():
##          G. socketpair(AF_NETLINK, SOCK_STREAM, 0, sv)
##             pre: EOPNOTSUPP ; post: ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
##          H. socketpair(AF_PACKET, SOCK_RDM, 0, sv)
##             pre: EOPNOTSUPP ; post: ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
##          I. socketpair(AF_NETLINK, SOCK_DGRAM, 0, sv)
##             Acceptance — DGRAM in allowlist, falls
##             through to the EOPNOTSUPP gate (netlink
##             ops->socketpair == NULL).
##      Marker: "AF_NETLINK/AF_PACKET ESOCKT gating: OK".
##
##      Updated test in batch 280's ESOCKTNOSUPPORT block:
##      discriminator E (AF_NETLINK / SOCK_RDM) was
##      "ENOSYS (acceptance — not in allowlist)" pre-282;
##      it now expects ESOCKTNOSUPPORT because batch 282
##      widens the gate to include AF_NETLINK.
##
##      Existing tests preserved: socketpair ESOCKT/EPROTO
##      gating (281) — discriminators A (AF_INET/RDM/0/sv)
##      and E (AF_UNIX/RDM/0/sv) still return
##      ESOCKTNOSUPPORT (now via the merged match arm);
##      D (AF_INET/STREAM/0/sv) regression still
##      EOPNOTSUPP; B/C still EPROTONOSUPPORT.  socket
##      ESOCKTNOSUPPORT (280), socket EPROTONOSUPPORT
##      (279), mremap MREMAP_FIXED new_addr bounds (278),
##      perf_event_open __reserved_2 gating (277), and the
##      legacy socket/socketpair tests at lines ~42367+
##      and the EAFNOSUPPORT gating (276) all unaffected.
##
##      Known limitations: still pending — accept/accept4
##      and bind/connect sockaddr-before-fd reorders (not
##      probe-distinguishable in kernel context);
##      pread/pwrite EOVERFLOW gate at pos+count and silent
##      MAX_RW_COUNT clamp; sys_mremap rejecting
##      MREMAP_FIXED below the userspace mmap floor;
##      SOCK_PACKET (type 10) support on AF_PACKET (we
##      reject it as out-of-range EINVAL where Linux
##      accepts it as a deprecated raw-packet type).
##
##      Build clean, boot test PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s on
##      retry; first run hit a known intermittent hang
##      between the [rcu] "Callback registration: OK"
##      step and BOOT_OK — same machine, same kernel,
##      passes on rerun).  See entry 312 below for the
##      RCU self-test flake.

## 312. [bug] kernel/src/rcu: intermittent hang between
##      "[rcu]   Callback registration: OK" and BOOT_OK.
##
##      Observed during batch 282 boot test: the first run
##      timed out at 240s with the serial log frozen at
##      "[rcu]   Callback registration: OK" — no panic, no
##      further output.  Re-running the same kernel binary
##      with no changes booted to BOOT_OK in 23s.  The
##      hang is not caused by this batch's syscall edits
##      (the [syscall/linux] block above it completed all
##      its self-tests including the new
##      "AF_NETLINK/AF_PACKET ESOCKT gating: OK" marker
##      before the hang).
##
##      Reproducer: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
##      --timeout=240 in a loop; the hang reproduces at
##      something like 1-in-10 frequency on this machine.
##
##      Suspected cause: a race between the RCU callback
##      registration self-test step and either the
##      scheduler picking up a callback-worker task or a
##      per-CPU RCU state-machine transition.  The next
##      test phase ([futex] in successful runs) starts on
##      a different code path, so the hang sits squarely
##      in the RCU subsystem between "Callback
##      registration: OK" and whatever the next [rcu]
##      step is meant to log.
##
##      Investigation TODO (not blocking the autonomous
##      loop on the Linux ABI translation batch series):
##        - Run the kernel under qemu -d int,cpu_reset to
##          see whether the CPU is spinning, halted, or
##          deadlocked on a lock.
##        - Add a serial trace at the start of each [rcu]
##          self-test sub-step so the hung step is
##          identifiable.
##        - If reproduction stays flaky, add a watchdog
##          timer that panics with a stack trace if the
##          [rcu] self-test runs for > 30s, converting
##          the silent hang into an observable panic.
##

313. [2026-06-06] Batch 283: Linux ABI translation —
     sys_mincore: introduce explicit ENOMEM gate for
     (addr+length) leaving user address space, ordered
     BEFORE the NULL-vec EFAULT gate so probes see Linux's
     documented errno.

     Pre-batch divergence
     --------------------
     Linux's mm/mincore.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(mincore) runs
     gates in this order:
       1. start & ~PAGE_MASK                -> -EINVAL
       2. !access_ok(start, len)             -> -ENOMEM
       3. pages = compute_from(len)
       4. !access_ok(vec, pages)             -> -EFAULT
     Our pre-batch stub ran them as:
       1. addr % PAGE_SIZE                   -> -EINVAL
       2. length == 0                        -> 0
       3. vec_ptr == 0                       -> -EFAULT
       4. validate_user_write(vec, pages)    -> errno
       5. validate_user_read(addr, length)   -> -ENOMEM
     The ENOMEM gate corresponded to Linux's gate 2 but
     fired LAST, after the NULL-vec gate.  Worse, it went
     through validate_user_read which is a no-op in
     kernel context — meaning kernel-context probes never
     observed the ENOMEM path at all.  Two probe shapes
     surfaced the divergence:
       - (USER_SPACE_END, 0x4000, NULL):
           pre-batch -> -EFAULT
           Linux     -> -ENOMEM (access_ok fails on a
                        range crossing TASK_SIZE)
       - (u64::MAX & !0x3FFF, 0x4000, NULL):
           pre-batch -> -EFAULT
           Linux     -> -ENOMEM (access_ok fails on a
                        range that wraps past 2^64)

     Fix
     ---
     Inserted a new gate between the alignment check and
     the length==0 short-circuit:
         if length != 0 {
             let fits = match addr.checked_add(length) {
                 Some(end) => end
                              <= USER_SPACE_END,
                 None => false,
             };
             if !fits {
                 return linux_err(errno::ENOMEM);
             }
         }
     USER_SPACE_END = 0x0000_8000_0000_0000 is our x86_64
     TASK_SIZE equivalent.  checked_add(...) returning
     None catches the addr+len > 2^64 wraparound case;
     `end > USER_SPACE_END` catches the more common
     "range crosses into kernel half" case.  The gate is
     pre-bypass (no validate_user_* call), so
     kernel-context self-tests can drive it.  The
     length==0 short-circuit was preserved so callers
     that legitimately pass len=0 (a documented "this
     does nothing" idiom on Linux) keep getting 0 back.

     Tail of the routine is unchanged: vec_ptr == 0
     -> EFAULT, validate_user_write(vec, pages),
     residency byte-stream writes, etc.  The
     validate_user_read(addr, length) call that
     previously stood for the ENOMEM gate is removed —
     the new pre-bypass check supersedes it, and keeping
     a bypassable check downstream would have been dead
     code in self-tests.

     New self-tests (probe-distinguishable in kernel
     context, fire before validate_user_* bypass):
       - mincore(USER_SPACE_END, 0x4000, NULL) -> ENOMEM
         (addr+len just barely exits user space; pre-fix
         this returned EFAULT)
       - mincore(u64::MAX & !0x3FFF, 0x4000, NULL)
         -> ENOMEM
         (addr+len overflows past u64::MAX; pre-fix this
         returned EFAULT)

     Marker: "mincore addr+len ENOMEM gating: OK"

     Preserved self-tests (still pass post-batch):
       - mincore misaligned addr (0x4001) -> EINVAL
         (gate 1 still fires first)
       - mincore length=0 -> 0
         (the new gate is conditional on length != 0;
         length==0 short-circuits before the NULL-vec
         gate)
       - mincore(0x4000, 0x4000, NULL) -> EFAULT
         (in-bounds backing range, so the new ENOMEM gate
         passes, then NULL-vec gate fires)
       - mincore(0x4000, 0x4000, vec) -> 0 with vec[0]==1
         and untouched 0xCC sentinel beyond page 0
       - mincore(0x4000, 5*0x4000, vec) -> 0 with all
         five vec bytes == 0x01 and untouched sentinel

     Known limitations
     -----------------
     - Page size for the bounds-check granularity is
       still 16 KiB (matching our PAGE_SIZE), not Linux's
       4 KiB.  An addr that's 16 KiB-aligned but not
       4 KiB-aligned can't exist (16 is a multiple of 4),
       so the alignment gate is strictly stricter than
       Linux's — calls Linux would accept with a
       4 KiB-aligned but not 16 KiB-aligned addr still
       see EINVAL here.  Documented under "Page size
       divergences" in earlier batches; not addressed
       by this batch.
     - validate_user_read(addr, length) is no longer
       called.  In a real-user-context process this had
       no observable effect: it ran AFTER the routine
       had already decided to write resident bytes (we
       had already validated the vec buffer and started
       writing).  Linux's loop walks the actual VMA and
       can return ENOMEM mid-write for the unmapped
       case; our kernel has no swap and no demand
       paging, so every mapped page is unconditionally
       resident and any unmapped page would be visible
       earlier through the explicit bounds check.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean.
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

314. [2026-06-06] Batch 284: Linux ABI translation —
     sys_msync: mirror Linux's C unsigned-long wrap
     semantics for the page-aligned length computation,
     so probes with len = u64::MAX see 0 (success) and
     probes with addr+len overflowing past u64::MAX see
     ENOMEM, matching the real kernel.

     Pre-batch divergence
     --------------------
     Linux's mm/msync.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(msync) computes
     the rounded length and end pointer with native C
     unsigned-long arithmetic, which wraps silently:
         error = -ENOMEM;
         len = (len + ~PAGE_MASK) & PAGE_MASK;
         end = start + len;
         if (end < start) goto out;          // ENOMEM
         error = 0;
         if (end == start) goto out;         // success
     For len = u64::MAX, `len + 0xFFF` wraps to 0xFFE,
     `& ~0xFFF` zeroes to 0.  end = start + 0 = start, so
     end < start is false and end == start fires the
     success exit — the syscall returns 0.

     Our pre-batch stub used checked_add for both the
     rounding and the addr+len computation, returning
     ENOMEM for either overflow.  A probe
       msync(0x4000, u64::MAX, MS_ASYNC)
     therefore saw -ENOMEM where Linux returns 0.  An
     existing self-test (batch 109 era) asserted ENOMEM
     for exactly this input — it correctly described our
     stub's behaviour but mis-described Linux's, and was
     updated to the Linux value as part of this batch.

     Fix
     ---
     Replace the two checked_add calls with
     wrapping_add, then keep the explicit `end < addr`
     test for the genuine "end overflowed but didn't
     wrap to zero" case:
         let len_aligned = len
             .wrapping_add(ABI_PAGE_SIZE - 1)
             & !(ABI_PAGE_SIZE - 1);
         let end = addr.wrapping_add(len_aligned);
         if end < addr {
             return linux_err(errno::ENOMEM);
         }
         if end == addr {
             return SyscallResult::ok(0);
         }
     This is a single-instruction rewrite that mirrors
     Linux's C semantics exactly, and keeps the rest of
     the routine (the validate_user_read range check)
     untouched.

     Updated self-test:
       - msync(0x4000, u64::MAX, MS_ASYNC):
           pre-fix expected/observed: -ENOMEM
           post-fix expected/observed: 0
           (matches Linux's wrap-to-zero behaviour).
     New self-test:
       - msync(0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_F000, 0x4000, MS_ASYNC):
           addr+len overflows past u64::MAX (end wraps to
           0x3000, end < addr) -> ENOMEM
           (exercises the genuine end<start path, distinct
           from the wrap-to-zero path).

     Marker: "msync wrap-to-zero / end<start: OK"

     Preserved self-tests (still pass):
       - msync(0, 0, 0) -> 0
       - msync(0, 0, MS_INVALIDATE) -> 0
       - msync(0, 0, MS_SYNC|MS_ASYNC) -> EINVAL
       - msync(0, 0, 0x10) -> EINVAL (unknown flag)
       - msync(0x1234, 4096, MS_ASYNC) -> EINVAL (addr
         not 4 KiB-aligned)
       - msync(0, 0, MS_ASYNC) -> 0
       - msync(0x1000, 0, MS_ASYNC) -> 0 (4K-aligned
         sub-frame addr)

     Known limitations
     -----------------
     - The ABI page size used for alignment and rounding
       is 4 KiB, matching Linux's x86_64 ABI.  Our
       internal frame size is 16 KiB, but msync doesn't
       expose frame boundaries to userspace — the
       alignment gate is therefore purely an ABI check,
       not a backing-store check.
     - validate_user_read(addr, len) is still called at
       the end for in-bounds (non-trivial-end) ranges.
       In kernel context it's a no-op; in user context
       it surfaces EFAULT for unmapped ranges.  Linux's
       VMA walk would surface ENOMEM for unmapped ranges
       and ENOMEM for unmapped sub-ranges within a
       mapped span; our single-shot validate_user_read
       maps both cases to EFAULT.  Tracked separately
       (the ENOMEM-on-unmapped-VMA gap requires a real
       VMA tree, not just a page-table walk).

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean.
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 22s).

315. [2026-06-06] Batch 285: Linux ABI translation —
     sys_sched_get_priority_max and sys_sched_get_priority_min
     now truncate `policy` to int before matching, mirroring
     Linux's syscall stub which declares `int policy` and
     thereby drops the high 32 bits of the syscall register.

     Pre-batch divergence
     --------------------
     Linux's kernel/sched/syscalls.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE1(
       sched_get_priority_max, int, policy)
     casts the 64-bit syscall register to the declared `int`
     before entering the switch, so a probe with
       policy = 0x0000_0001_0000_0001
     truncates to (int)0x0000_0001 = 1 = SCHED_FIFO and
     returns 99 (MAX_USER_RT_PRIO - 1).

     Our pre-batch stub matched on `args.arg0 as u64`
     directly, with no truncation:
         match args.arg0 {
             1 | 2 => ok(99),
             0 | 3 | 5 | 6 | 7 => ok(0),
             _ => EINVAL,
         }
     so 0x0000_0001_0000_0001 fell into the catch-all arm and
     returned EINVAL.  Same divergence for
     sys_sched_get_priority_min — the two routines are
     symmetric and shared the same bug.

     This is probe-distinguishable in kernel context: the
     routines are pure value-based, no validate_user_*, no
     fd lookup.  A fuzzer or libc shim that happens to leave
     dirty high bits in the syscall register (which can
     occur when the C-level prototype is `long` rather than
     `int`, common in older glibc stubs and in hand-rolled
     assembly callers) would see a Linux-only "policy 1
     works" / our "EINVAL" split.

     Fix
     ---
     Cast args.arg0 to i32 BEFORE the match, with the
     standard truncation/wrap allowances:
         let policy = args.arg0 as i32;
         match policy {
             1 | 2 => SyscallResult::ok(99),
             0 | 3 | 5 | 6 | 7 => SyscallResult::ok(0),
             _ => linux_err(errno::EINVAL),
         }
     Identical change in sys_sched_get_priority_min.  This is
     the same `int` truncation pattern we already use for
     sys_sched_setscheduler / sys_sched_getscheduler /
     sys_sched_rr_get_interval and most other "int-arg-0"
     syscalls — both routines were outliers.

     New self-tests:
       - sched_get_priority_max(0x1_0000_0001) -> 99
         (Linux truncates to SCHED_FIFO)
       - sched_get_priority_min(0x1_0000_0001) -> 1
       - sched_get_priority_max(0x1_0000_0063) -> EINVAL
         (Linux truncates to 99 == invalid policy; high
         bits must not "rescue" the value)
       - sched_get_priority_max(u64::MAX) -> EINVAL
         (confirms the (int)-1 path; this happens to work
         pre-fix too via the same catch-all, but is included
         so the truncation contract is documented in tests).

     Marker:
       "sched_get_priority_{max,min} int truncation: OK"

     Preserved self-tests (still pass):
       - sched_get_priority_max(SCHED_OTHER=0) -> 0
       - sched_get_priority_max(SCHED_FIFO=1)  -> 99
       - sched_get_priority_min(SCHED_FIFO=1)  -> 1
       - sched_get_priority_max(99)            -> EINVAL

     Known limitations
     -----------------
     - i32 truncation is signed.  For policy values in
       [2^31, 2^32), the low 32 bits are interpreted as a
       negative int (e.g. 0x80000000 -> -2147483648).  This
       matches Linux's `int policy` semantics — the kernel
       doesn't accept any negative policy value, so all of
       these correctly fall to EINVAL.
     - SCHED_EXT (policy 7) was added in Linux 6.12.  We
       continue to accept it (returning max=0, min=0) to
       match the modern kernel; older kernels return EINVAL
       here.  Same call shape as before the batch — no
       change to SCHED_EXT acceptance.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean.
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 22s).

316. [2026-06-06] Batch 286: Linux ABI translation —
     sys_getpriority and sys_setpriority now truncate
     `which` and `who` to int before validating, mirroring
     Linux's `SYSCALL_DEFINE2(getpriority, int, int)` and
     `SYSCALL_DEFINE3(setpriority, int, int, int)`
     signatures.  Follow-up to batch 285's `int` truncation
     audit.

     Pre-batch divergences
     ---------------------
     Linux's kernel/sys.c handlers declare both args as
     `int`, so the x86_64 syscall ABI truncates the 64-bit
     registers to the low 32 bits before the body runs.
     Our pre-batch stubs held `which = args.arg0` and
     `who = args.arg1` as raw u64, so two distinct probes
     diverged:

       Probe A — `which` truncation:
         getpriority(0x1_0000_0001, 0)
           Linux : (int)which = 1 = PRIO_PGRP
                   → caller's pgrp nice
                   → returns 20 (= 20 - 0)
           Ours  : raw u64 > 2 → EINVAL

       Probe B — `who` truncation:
         getpriority(0, 0x1_0000_0000)
           Linux : (int)who = 0 → "the caller" path
                   → returns 20
           Ours  : raw u64 != 0 → pcb::state(0x1_0000_0000)
                   = None → ESRCH

     Same divergences in setpriority via the symmetric
     args.  prio was already cast to i32 (it was the
     existing model), so its truncation was already
     correct.

     Fixes
     -----
     Cast args.arg0 and args.arg1 to i32 at the top of
     both routines.  Tightened the `which` gate from
     `if which > 2` (raw u64) to
     `if !(0..=2).contains(&which)` so the explicit
     negative-int case mirrors Linux's
       `if (which > 2 || which < 0) goto out;`
     (the raw-u64 form caught negatives implicitly via
     "huge unsigned > 2", but the post-truncation int
     form needs the lower bound spelled out).

     For the PRIO_PROCESS path, `who < 0` is now an
     explicit ESRCH (no valid pid is negative; the
     find_task_by_vpid path in Linux returns NULL
     for any pid <= 0 except the special pid==0 "self"
     semantics).  Casts to u64 for the pcb::state /
     pcb::set_nice lookups are gated by the positive-int
     branch.

     New self-tests:
       - getpriority(0x1_0000_0001, 0) -> 20
       - setpriority(0x1_0000_0001, 0, 0) -> 0
         (which truncates to PRIO_PGRP; pre-batch saw
          EINVAL)
       - getpriority(0, 0x1_0000_0000) -> 20
       - setpriority(0, 0x1_0000_0000, 0) -> 0
         (who truncates to 0 = "caller"; pre-batch saw
          ESRCH)
       - getpriority(u64::MAX, 0) -> EINVAL
         (regression guard for the explicit lower bound
          on `which`; same errno as pre-batch via the
          "huge unsigned > 2" implicit catch)

     Marker:
       "get/setpriority int truncation: OK"

     Preserved self-tests (still pass):
       - getpriority(PRIO_PROCESS, 0) -> 20
       - setpriority(PRIO_PROCESS, 0, 0) -> 0
       - setpriority(PRIO_PROCESS, 0, 19) -> 0
       - setpriority(PRIO_PROCESS, 0, 100) -> 0 (silent
         clamp to 19 — Linux saturates, doesn't error)
       - setpriority(PRIO_PROCESS, u64::MAX, 0) -> ESRCH
         (who truncates to -1, who<0 gate → ESRCH)
       - getpriority(PRIO_PROCESS, u64::MAX) -> ESRCH
         (same path)
       - get/setpriority(3, 0) -> EINVAL
         (which==3 is out of range either pre- or post-
         truncation)

     Known limitations
     -----------------
     - PRIO_PGRP and PRIO_USER are still accepted but
       not actually applied — we don't model process
       groups or per-uid nice tables.  Both paths fall
       through to caller_pid() for the per-PCB nice
       store, matching the documented behaviour from
       earlier batches.
     - For setpriority's PRIO_PROCESS path with `who > 0`
       referring to a pid that exists but has no PCB
       nice slot, we silently no-op the set (the
       pcb::set_nice call returns an Err which we
       discard).  Linux would set the nice and return 0;
       we return 0 too, so externally indistinguishable
       — but a `getpriority` follow-up would see the
       old nice.  Tracked in earlier batches as a "no
       per-pid nice storage" limitation; not addressed
       here.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean.
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 22s).

317. [2026-06-06] Batch 287: Linux ABI translation —
     sys_getpgid, sys_getsid, and sys_setpgid now truncate
     their pid_t / pgid args to int before validating,
     mirroring Linux's
       SYSCALL_DEFINE1(getpgid, pid_t, pid)
       SYSCALL_DEFINE1(getsid,  pid_t, pid)
       SYSCALL_DEFINE2(setpgid, pid_t, pid, pid_t, pgid)
     signatures.  Continuation of the `int` truncation
     audit started in batches 285/286.

     Pre-batch divergences
     ---------------------
     All three handlers held their pid_t args as raw u64
     and matched them directly.  Probes with high bits
     set diverged from Linux's post-truncation view:

       Probe A — getpgid pid_t truncation:
         getpgid(0x1_0000_0000)
           Linux : (int)pid = 0 → "the caller" path
                   → returns the caller's pgid (= caller
                     pid in our 1:1 stub)
           Ours  : raw u64 != 0 → pcb::state(0x1_0000_0000)
                   = None → ESRCH

       Probe B — getsid pid_t truncation:
         getsid(0x1_0000_0000)
           Linux : (int)pid = 0 → caller's sid
                   (= caller pid in our 1:1 stub)
           Ours  : same wrong ESRCH as getpgid

       Probe C — setpgid pgid sign-bit truncation:
         setpgid(0, 0x0000_0000_8000_0000)
           Linux : (int)pgid = -2_147_483_648
                   → EINVAL (negative pgid is rejected
                   before any group lookup)
           Ours  : (pgid as i64) = +0x8000_0000 > 0
                   → ok(0).  The old `(pgid as i64) < 0`
                   guard only caught bit-63-set values
                   because it widened first then tested
                   the i64 sign; bit-31-set inputs slipped
                   through.

     Fixes
     -----
     - sys_getpgid: cast args.arg0 to i32 at entry; reject
       `pid < 0` with ESRCH (no valid pid_t is negative;
       Linux's find_task_by_vpid returns NULL for any
       pid < 0).  Only on the positive-int branch do we
       widen to u64 for the pcb::state lookup.
     - sys_getsid: symmetric treatment of `pid` (same gate
       semantics, same ESRCH for `pid < 0`).  No backing
       session table either way — we still synthesize
       sid = target pid as before.
     - sys_setpgid: cast args.arg1 to i32 at entry and
       test `pgid < 0` after the cast.  Replaces the old
       `(pgid as i64) < 0` widening that missed bit-31
       negatives.

     The pid arg to setpgid is still passed through to
     "ok(0)" — we don't model process groups, so any
     non-negative pgid (after truncation) is accepted as
     before.  Same call shape as the rest of the
     process-group stubs.

     New self-tests (added after the existing setsid
     test block):
       - getpgid(0x1_0000_0000) -> > 0
         (pid truncates to 0 → caller path; pre-batch
          returned -ESRCH which is < 0)
       - getsid(0x1_0000_0000)  -> > 0
         (same path)
       - setpgid(0, 0x0000_0000_8000_0000) -> EINVAL
         (pgid truncates to INT_MIN → reject; pre-batch
          returned 0 because the i64 widening saw the
          value as positive)

     Marker:
       "getpgid/getsid/setpgid int truncation: OK"

     Preserved self-tests (still pass):
       - All earlier setsid / getsid / getpgid / setpgid
         tests in the dispatch_linux block continue to
         exercise the same call shapes as before.  Inputs
         whose high bits are zero hit the same code paths
         post-cast (signed-positive int = original u64),
         so the previously-asserted return values are
         unchanged.

     Known limitations
     -----------------
     - We still don't track process groups or sessions
       separately from PIDs.  getsid / getpgid return
       the target pid as both pgid and sid (the 1:1
       "every process is its own session leader" model
       documented in earlier batches).  Not addressed
       here; tracked elsewhere.
     - setpgid does not actually move processes between
       groups; it just validates the pgid sign after
       truncation and returns 0.  Same behaviour as
       pre-batch for the accepted-input case.
     - The pid arg to setpgid is not range-checked
       against int after truncation; only pgid is.  This
       matches Linux's handler ordering: do_setpgid
       starts with the pgid check before any pid lookup,
       and we have no pid lookup to perform either way.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean.
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 22s).

318. [2026-06-06] Batch 288: Linux ABI translation —
     sys_kill now truncates its `int sig` argument to i32
     before the existence-probe / signal-number gates,
     mirroring Linux's
       SYSCALL_DEFINE2(kill, pid_t, pid, int, sig)
     signature.  Continuation of the `int` truncation
     audit started in batches 285/286/287.  This batch
     narrows to sig only; pid truncation is deferred
     because the existing kill self-tests use a
     "definitely-nonexistent" probe pid (0xDEAD_BEEF)
     whose post-truncation interpretation (process group)
     would change the gate-ordering semantics and require
     restructuring those tests.  Tracked in this todo as
     a follow-up.

     Pre-batch divergence
     --------------------
     Linux's `kill(pid_t pid, int sig)` truncates the
     64-bit args.arg1 register to its low 32 bits before
     any gate runs.  Pre-batch we held `sig` as raw u64,
     so probes with the high half set diverged:

       Probe A — sig truncates to 0 (existence probe):
         kill(PROBE_PID, 0x1_0000_0000)
           Linux : (int)sig = 0 → existence/perm probe
                   → ESRCH for nonexistent PROBE_PID
           Ours  : raw sig != 0 → signal_send with sig =
                   0x1_0000_0000 → EINVAL (sig > NSIG)

       Probe B — sig truncates to a valid signal:
         kill(PROBE_PID, 0x1_0000_0009)
           Linux : (int)sig = 9 = SIGKILL → signal-number
                   gate accepts → target lookup → ESRCH
           Ours  : raw sig = 0x1_0000_0009 > NSIG → EINVAL

     For sig = 0x0000_0000_8000_0000 (bit-31 only) the
     pre-batch path also produced EINVAL ("huge unsigned
     > NSIG"), but post-truncation it is INT_MIN, which
     must still produce EINVAL via the new explicit
     lower-bound gate.  Linux rejects any sig < 0 with
     EINVAL before touching the target, so both pre- and
     post-batch behave the same on bit-31-only probes;
     the regression-guard test below documents the
     invariant.

     Fixes
     -----
     - Cast args.arg1 to i32 at the top of sys_kill.
     - Add explicit `sig < 0 → EINVAL` gate (mirrors
       Linux's `valid_signal()` lower bound).
     - Move the existing `sig == 0` existence-probe
       branch to operate on the post-cast value.
     - For the real-signal delegation to signal_send,
       rebuild SyscallArgs with the truncated sig value
       widened back to u64 (positive int → u64 is the
       same bit pattern), so the inner gate sees the
       post-ABI signal number, not the raw register.

     New self-tests (appended to the existing kill
     dispatch block):
       - kill(PROBE_PID, 0x1_0000_0000) -> ESRCH
         (sig truncates to 0 → existence probe; pre-batch
          returned EINVAL because the raw sig missed the
          sig==0 gate)
       - kill(PROBE_PID, 0x1_0000_0009) -> not EINVAL
         (sig truncates to 9 = SIGKILL; pre-batch
          rejected EINVAL because raw sig > NSIG)
       - kill(PROBE_PID, 0x0000_0000_8000_0000) -> EINVAL
         (sig truncates to INT_MIN < 0; regression guard
          that a future "fix" doesn't widen-before-test
          and accidentally accept the value)

     Marker:
       "kill sig int truncation: OK"

     Preserved self-tests (still pass):
       - kill(PROBE, sig=0) -> not EINVAL (existence
         probe path — same code, same answer post-cast)
       - kill(PROBE, sig=65) -> EINVAL (NSIG+1; post-
         cast still 65 > NSIG)
       - kill(PROBE, sig=u64::MAX) -> EINVAL (post-cast
         (int)-1 < 0 → caught by new explicit lower
         bound; pre-batch caught implicitly by raw >
         NSIG; same answer either way)
       - kill(PROBE, sig in [9,15,17,64]) -> not EINVAL
         (positive valid signals; post-cast = original)

     Known limitations
     -----------------
     - pid is still held as raw u64 — sys_kill does NOT
       yet apply int truncation to args.arg0.  The
       existing tests probe with pid = 0xDEAD_BEEF whose
       post-truncation int value is negative, which would
       trigger Linux's process-group semantics (pid < -1
       → send to pgroup |pid|; pid == -1 → send to all).
       We have no pgroup model, so adding pid truncation
       would have to either reject all negative pids as
       EINVAL (breaking the existing sig-gate tests that
       expect ESRCH from the nonexistent-pid path) or
       restructure the existing tests around a positive
       probe value like 0x7FFF_FFFE.  Doing it right is a
       separate, larger batch.  Tracked here.
     - sys_tkill and sys_tgkill also still hold tid /
       tgid / sig as raw u64 (tkill / tgkill currently
       forward the raw args to sys_kill, so they pick up
       the sig fix transitively only along paths where
       owner_process(tid) succeeds — in kernel-context
       boot self-tests with u64::MAX tids this is moot
       because the lookup bails ESRCH before reaching
       sys_kill's sig gate).  Same audit, deferred to
       its own batch alongside the existing-test
       restructuring.
     - sig == 0 existence probe still hard-rolls the
       target-pid / parent / capability checks against
       the raw target pid (args.arg0).  After a future
       pid-truncation batch, the target lookup will use
       the post-cast pid; for now it sees raw u64.  The
       SIGHUP placeholder for signal_send is unchanged
       and remains documented as a no-op marker.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean.
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 22s).

319. [2026-06-06] Batch 289: Linux ABI translation —
     sys_tkill and sys_tgkill now truncate their pid_t
     args (tid / tgid) to i32 and reject `tid <= 0` /
     `tgid <= 0` with EINVAL before any owner_process
     lookup, mirroring Linux's
       SYSCALL_DEFINE2(tkill,  pid_t, pid, int, sig)
       SYSCALL_DEFINE3(tgkill, pid_t, tgid, pid_t, pid, int, sig)
     bodies, which both open with
       `if (pid <= 0) return -EINVAL`
     (tgkill additionally `|| tgid <= 0`).  Continuation
     of the `int` truncation audit started in batches
     285/286/287/288.  Sig handling is delegated to
     sys_kill, which already truncates sig (batch 288).

     Pre-batch divergences
     ---------------------
     Linux's tkill/tgkill ABI truncates each register to
     int before the body runs.  Pre-batch we held tid /
     tgid as raw u64, so two distinct probes diverged:

       Probe A — tid truncates to 0 (Linux EINVAL gate):
         tkill(0x1_0000_0000, 1)
           Linux : (int)tid = 0 → EINVAL
           Ours  : raw tid != 0 →
                   owner_process(0x1_0000_0000) = None
                   → ESRCH
         tgkill(1, 0x1_0000_0000, 1) same path → EINVAL.
         tgkill(0x1_0000_0000, 1, 1) tgid path → EINVAL.

       Probe B — tid truncates to -1 (same gate):
         tkill(u64::MAX, 1)
           Linux : (int)tid = -1 → EINVAL
           Ours  : owner_process miss → ESRCH

     Same `tid <= 0` gate for tgkill applied to both
     tgid and tid columns.

     Fixes
     -----
     - sys_tkill: cast args.arg0 to i32 at entry; reject
       `tid <= 0` with EINVAL; widen the surviving
       positive int back to u64 for the owner_process
       lookup.  Pass args.arg1 (raw u64 sig) unchanged
       to sys_kill — sys_kill already truncates sig
       (batch 288) so the inner gate sees the post-ABI
       value either way.
     - sys_tgkill: cast args.arg0 and args.arg1 to i32
       at entry; reject `tgid <= 0 || tid <= 0` with
       EINVAL; widen both surviving positive ints back
       to u64 for the owner_process / tgid-mismatch
       checks.  Pass args.arg2 unchanged to sys_kill
       for the same reason.

     Existing self-tests restructured
     --------------------------------
     The previous tkill/tgkill self-tests used `u64::MAX`
     to mean "definitely nonexistent tid" and asserted
     ESRCH from the owner_process lookup miss.  After
     truncation, u64::MAX → -1 → the new `tid <= 0`
     gate fires first and returns EINVAL.  We replaced
     the probe value with `0x7FFF_FFFE` (positive
     INT_MAX-1, definitely no registered thread) so the
     same ESRCH path is still exercised — the test
     intent (ensure non-existence yields ESRCH, not the
     wrong errno) is preserved without depending on the
     old wrong gate ordering.

     Restructured tests (still pass post-batch):
       - tkill(0x7FFF_FFFE, 1)  -> ESRCH
       - tkill(0x7FFF_FFFE, 0)  -> ESRCH (sig=0 probe
         on nonexistent tid, after the tid lookup miss)
       - tgkill(1, 0x7FFF_FFFE, 1) -> ESRCH

     New self-tests demonstrating the truncation gate:
       - tkill(0x1_0000_0000, 1)  -> EINVAL
         (tid truncates to 0)
       - tkill(u64::MAX, 1)       -> EINVAL
         (tid truncates to -1; this is the old probe
         flipped to its new correct expected value)
       - tgkill(0x1_0000_0000, 1, 1) -> EINVAL
         (tgid truncates to 0)
       - tgkill(1, 0x1_0000_0000, 1) -> EINVAL
         (tid truncates to 0)
       - tgkill(u64::MAX, u64::MAX, 1) -> EINVAL
         (both truncate to -1)

     Marker:
       "tkill/tgkill pid_t int truncation: OK"

     Known limitations
     -----------------
     - sig is still passed forward as raw u64 from
       tkill/tgkill to sys_kill.  sys_kill truncates it
       there (batch 288), so post-ABI behaviour is
       correct; the only observable artefact is that
       sys_kill sees the raw register value for its
       initial `sig < 0 / sig == 0 / signal-send`
       decision.  No behavioural divergence.
     - sys_kill still does not truncate pid (the
       0xDEAD_BEEF probes in the kill self-tests would
       need restructuring along the same lines as this
       batch did for tkill/tgkill).  Tracked under
       entry 318's "Known limitations" and unchanged
       here.
     - tgkill's tgid-mismatch check (`if pid != tgid`)
       now operates on the post-truncation positive int
       widened back to u64.  This is observationally
       identical to the pre-batch raw u64 comparison
       for inputs whose tgid was already a small
       positive int (the only inputs that can reach
       this point post-batch).

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean.
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 22s).

320. [2026-06-06] Batch 290: Linux ABI translation —
     sched_* family pid_t truncation, applied end-to-end
     across sys_sched_getscheduler, sys_sched_getparam,
     sys_sched_setscheduler, sys_sched_setparam, and
     sys_sched_rr_get_interval.  All five now cast pid to
     i32 at entry, reject pid<0 with EINVAL (or already
     did), and use the truncated value for ALL downstream
     decisions — caller-vs-explicit branch, pcb::state
     lookup, target_pid resolution.  Pre-batch only the
     EINVAL negative-check gate had been truncated;
     subsequent lookups still saw the raw u64.

     Pre-batch divergences
     ---------------------
     Linux's sched_* syscalls all declare pid as pid_t
     (int), so the x86_64 ABI truncates args.arg0 to its
     low 32 bits before find_process_by_pid runs.  The
     mixed state (truncated for the <0 gate, raw for the
     lookup) gave us two distinct families of probe
     mismatches:

       Probe A — pid truncates to 0 (caller path):
         pid = 0x1_0000_0000
           Linux : (int)pid = 0 → caller's policy/prio
                   → SCHED_OTHER = 0 (sched_getscheduler)
                   → writes 0 to buf (sched_getparam)
                   → no-op caller-store (sched_set*)
           Ours  : raw u64 != 0 →
                   pcb::state(0x1_0000_0000) = None →
                   ESRCH

       Probe B — pid truncates to -1 (Linux EINVAL):
         pid = u64::MAX
           Linux : (int)pid = -1 → EINVAL via
                   `if (pid < 0)` body-opener
           Ours  : (pre-existing for set*/rr_get_interval
                   only) the truncated gate already fired
                   EINVAL.  For sched_getscheduler /
                   sched_getparam, which had no <0 gate
                   pre-batch, the raw u64 fell through to
                   pcb::state(u64::MAX) = None → ESRCH.

     Fixes
     -----
     - sys_sched_getscheduler: new — cast pid to i32 at
       entry, reject pid<0 with EINVAL, widen back to u64
       only for the pcb::state lookup on the explicit-pid
       branch.
     - sys_sched_getparam: new — same cast + pid<0
       EINVAL gate.  The NULL-param branch still returns
       EFAULT (Linux returns EINVAL there per the
       `!param || pid<0` combined opener); that's a
       separate, pre-existing semantic divergence and is
       left untouched in this batch to keep the scope on
       int truncation.  Documented under Known
       Limitations below.
     - sys_sched_setscheduler: gate-1/2 truncation was
       already present.  Switched gate-4 (pcb::state)
       and the target_pid resolution to use pid_i32
       widened back to u64, so probes that pass the
       gates with a high-bit-set raw pid now resolve to
       the post-ABI int instead of the raw register.
     - sys_sched_setparam: identical treatment to
       sched_setscheduler at the lookup/target_pid step.
     - sys_sched_rr_get_interval: removed the dead
       `let pid = args.arg0` binding; switched the
       pcb::state lookup to the pid_u widened from the
       already-validated pid_i32.

     Existing self-tests restructured
     --------------------------------
     The dispatch-side coverage block (after the per-PCB
     round-trip test) used `pid = 0xdead_beef` as its
     "bogus pid for ESRCH" probe against
     sched_getscheduler.  Post-batch 0xdead_beef
     truncates to a negative int and now produces EINVAL
     via the new gate, masking the ESRCH path the test
     wanted to assert.  Switched the probe to
     `0x7fff_fffe` (positive INT_MAX-1, no registered
     PCB) so the same lookup-miss → ESRCH path is still
     exercised.

     New self-tests demonstrating the truncation:
       - sched_getscheduler(0x1_0000_0000) -> 0
         (pid truncates to 0 → caller path)
       - sched_getscheduler(u64::MAX)       -> EINVAL
         (pid truncates to -1 → new <0 gate)
       - sched_getparam(0x1_0000_0000, valid) -> 0
       - sched_getparam(u64::MAX, valid)      -> EINVAL
       - sched_setscheduler(0x1_0000_0000, 0, valid) -> 0
       - sched_setparam(0x1_0000_0000, valid)        -> 0
       - sched_rr_get_interval(0x1_0000_0000, valid) -> 0

     Marker:
       "sched_{get,set}{scheduler,param} / rr_get_interval
        pid_t truncation: OK"

     Preserved self-tests (still pass):
       - sched_getscheduler(0) -> 0 (caller path,
         kernel-context fallback to SCHED_OTHER)
       - sched_get_priority_max/min on known policies
         (untouched by this batch)
       - sched_setscheduler gate-order discriminators
         A/B/C from batch 254 (policy<0 → EINVAL,
         NULL param → EINVAL, unknown pid → ESRCH).
         All three use pid=0 or pid=12345 with the
         high bits clean, so post-truncation behaviour
         is identical to pre-batch.
       - sched_setparam gate-order tests from batch 255
         (same reasoning).
       - sched_rr_get_interval batch-236 gate order
         (pid=-1, valid ts; pid=-1, bogus ts; pid=
         0x7fff_ffff, NULL ts).  The pid=-1 cases were
         already truncation-aware; the bogus pid case
         used `0x7fff_ffff` which is post-truncation
         positive INT_MAX, unchanged.
       - Real-PCB dispatch coverage (sched_getscheduler
         / sched_getparam on a created test_pid) —
         unaffected since the test_pid value is a small
         positive int.

     Known limitations
     -----------------
     - sched_getparam still returns EFAULT for NULL
       param where Linux returns EINVAL via the combined
       `!param || pid<0` body-opener.  The existing
       self-test `sched_getparam(0, NULL) -> EFAULT`
       documents the divergence and is preserved.
       Fixing this requires moving the NULL check above
       the EFAULT path and updating that test;
       deliberately deferred to keep this batch focused
       on int truncation.
     - The sched_setscheduler / sched_setparam tests
       above that assert "ok(0)" for the truncate-to-0
       caller path don't actually verify a per-PCB
       priority/policy update.  In kernel context
       caller_pid()=None, so target_pid resolves to
       None and the helpers silently no-op.  This is
       consistent with the rest of this file's
       kernel-context behaviour and matches the comment
       on the function.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean.
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

321. Linux ABI batch 291 — sys_sched_getaffinity /
     sys_sched_setaffinity: pid_t and unsigned int
     register truncation.

     Pre-batch divergences
     ---------------------
     Linux declares
       long sys_sched_getaffinity(pid_t pid,
                                  unsigned int len,
                                  unsigned long __user *user_mask_ptr);
       long sys_sched_setaffinity(pid_t pid,
                                  unsigned int len,
                                  unsigned long __user *user_mask_ptr);
     The x86_64 syscall ABI delivers args in 64-bit
     registers but truncates each one to its declared
     C type before the body runs.  Our stubs held
     args.arg0 / args.arg1 as raw u64 and matched
     against them, so probes with garbage in the high
     32 bits exercised the wrong control-flow paths:

       Probe A — pid truncates to 0 (caller path):
         pid = 0x1_0000_0000
           Linux : (int)pid = 0 → resolves to "current"
                   → fills the affinity mask for the
                   caller (return = effective len)
                   or no-ops the setter (return 0)
           Ours  : raw u64 != 0 → pcb::state(0x1_0000_0000)
                   = None → ESRCH

       Probe B — len truncates to a small value:
         len = 0x1_0000_0008
           Linux : (unsigned int)len = 8 → bookkeeps 8
                   bytes of the mask
           Ours  : raw u64 → flowed through as a giant
                   length, hitting our EINVAL "too
                   large" gate

       Probe C — alignment invariant after truncation:
         len = 0x1_0000_000F
           Linux : (unsigned int)len = 0xF → fails the
                   "len % sizeof(long) == 0" check →
                   EINVAL
           Ours  : raw u64 0x1_0000_000F mod 8 == 7 →
                   incidentally hit EINVAL, but for the
                   wrong reason and unstable under
                   future refactors

       Probe D — negative pid:
         pid = u64::MAX
           Linux : (int)pid = -1.  Linux affinity has no
                   explicit `if (pid < 0)` gate; it
                   falls through to find_task_by_vpid()
                   which returns NULL → ESRCH.
           Ours  : raw u64 = u64::MAX → pcb::state
                   returns None → ESRCH (accidentally
                   correct errno, wrong reasoning).

     Fixes
     -----
     - sys_sched_getaffinity: cast args.arg0 to i32 at
       entry; cast args.arg1 to u32 then widen to usize
       (so the high 32 bits are dropped before any
       size/alignment math).  The size cap and
       alignment-divisible-by-sizeof(long) checks now
       run against the truncated len, matching Linux's
       gate order.  The pid resolution branch:
         pid == 0 → caller mask
         pid  > 0 → pcb::state(pid as u64), ESRCH on miss
         pid  < 0 → ESRCH directly (matches Linux's
                    find_task_by_vpid → NULL path
                    without faking an EINVAL we don't
                    have evidence Linux returns)
     - sys_sched_setaffinity: identical truncation
       treatment.  The len==0 short-circuit, mask
       validate_user_read, and per-PCB no-op semantics
       are preserved against the truncated len.
     - The existing `validate_user_write(mask_ptr,
       cpusetsize)` etc. now run against the truncated
       cpusetsize, so we no longer touch usermem with
       the raw 64-bit length.

     Existing self-tests restructured
     --------------------------------
     None — the prior sched_*affinity gate-order tests
     used small literal pids (0, valid test_pid,
     small bogus pid) and small literal lens, so they
     pass unchanged.

     New self-tests demonstrating the truncation
     -------------------------------------------
       - sched_getaffinity(0x1_0000_0000, 8, mask) > 0
         (pid truncates to 0 → caller fills mask)
       - sched_setaffinity(0x1_0000_0000, 8, mask) == 0
         (pid truncates to 0 → no-op setter in kernel
          context)
       - sched_getaffinity(0, 0x1_0000_0008, mask) == 8
         (len truncates to 8 → 8 bytes of mask copied)
       - sched_getaffinity(0, 0x1_0000_000F, 0) ==
         EINVAL (after truncation 0xF is not divisible
         by sizeof(long), alignment gate)
       - sched_getaffinity(u64::MAX, 8, mask) == ESRCH
         (pid truncates to -1 → Linux NULL-task
          fallback)

     Marker:
       "sched_{get,set}affinity pid_t/uint truncation: OK"

     Preserved self-tests
     --------------------
     - All previously documented "sched_getaffinity ...
       gate order: OK" probes (NULL mask EFAULT, len==0
       short-circuit, mask too-large EINVAL, mask
       misalignment EINVAL, unknown small-pid ESRCH).
     - The dispatch-level real-PCB round-trip from
       earlier batches.

     Known limitations
     -----------------
     - The MAX_CPU_MASK_BYTES cap is our own (8 bytes,
       64 CPUs).  Linux's NR_CPUS is configurable.
       This is a deliberate, pre-existing scope choice
       documented under entry 215 and unchanged here.
     - sys_personality (`unsigned long`, high bits
       carry meaningful flags on Linux) was audited
       in this batch and already truncates correctly
       via `args.arg0 as u32` at the entry point —
       no change required.
     - sys_sched_setattr / sys_sched_getattr (pid_t pid,
       unsigned int flags) still hold args as raw u64.
       Deferred to a future batch since they have
       independent gate-order work (flags validation
       against SCHED_FLAG_*) that should land in the
       same change.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (42.45s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 26s).

322. Linux ABI batch 292 — sys_sched_setattr /
     sys_sched_getattr: pid_t, unsigned int flags, and
     unsigned int size register truncation.

     Pre-batch divergences
     ---------------------
     Linux declares
       long sys_sched_setattr(pid_t pid,
                              struct sched_attr __user *attr,
                              unsigned int flags);
       long sys_sched_getattr(pid_t pid,
                              struct sched_attr __user *attr,
                              unsigned int size,
                              unsigned int flags);
     The x86_64 syscall ABI delivers args in 64-bit
     registers but truncates each one to its declared
     C type before the body runs.  Batch 217 had
     already added the pid_i32 cast to the entry of
     both stubs for the <0 EINVAL gate, but the rest
     of each body still touched the raw args.argN u64:

       Probe A — setattr flags truncates to 0:
         flags = 0x1_0000_0000
           Linux : (unsigned int)flags = 0 → passes
                   the combined `if (!uattr || pid<0
                   || flags)` gate → v0 attr installs
                   → 0
           Ours  : raw u64 != 0 → EINVAL

       Probe B — setattr pid truncates to 0:
         pid = 0x1_0000_0000, valid v0 attr
           Linux : (int)pid = 0 → caller path → 0
           Ours  : pid_i32 = 0 passed the <0 gate,
                   then args.arg0 raw = 0x1_0000_0000
                   != 0 → pcb::state(raw) = None →
                   ESRCH

       Probe C — getattr flags truncates to 0:
         flags = 0x1_0000_0000, attr=valid, size=48
           Linux : (unsigned int)flags = 0 → passes
                   combined gate → writes v0 attr → 0
           Ours  : raw u64 != 0 → EINVAL

       Probe D — getattr size truncates to 48:
         size = 0x1_0000_0030 (= 0x30 after u32 trunc)
           Linux : truncated size = 48; passes both
                   `size > PAGE_SIZE` and
                   `size < SCHED_ATTR_SIZE_VER0`
                   gates → writes 48-byte attr → 0,
                   with attr.size = 0x30 (the value
                   userspace requested, post-truncation)
           Ours  : raw u64 = 0x1_0000_0030 > 4096 →
                   EINVAL

       Probe E — getattr pid truncates to 0:
         pid = 0x1_0000_0000, valid attr
           Linux : (int)pid = 0 → caller path → 0
           Ours  : pid_i32 = 0 passed <0 gate, then
                   args.arg0 raw != 0 →
                   pcb::state(raw) = None → ESRCH

     Fixes
     -----
     - sys_sched_setattr:
         * cast args.arg2 to u32 → use as `flags` in
           the combined EINVAL gate
         * use the existing pid_i32 (cast from
           args.arg0) for the resolution branch:
             pid_i32 == 0 → caller_pid()
             pid_i32 >  0 → pcb::state(pid_u) lookup
                            then target_pid = Some(pid_u)
           where pid_u = pid_i32 as u64 (sign-loss
           allowed since the <0 case is rejected
           upstream).
     - sys_sched_getattr:
         * cast args.arg2 to u32, widen to u64 as
           `size` — all subsequent gates (PAGE_SIZE,
           SCHED_ATTR_SIZE_VER0), the validate_user_write
           call, the user_size attr.size write-back, and
           the trailing-zero-fill loop now run against
           the truncated value
         * cast args.arg3 to u32 → use as `flags` in
           the combined EINVAL gate
         * use pid_i32 for the resolution branch the
           same way as setattr

     Existing self-tests
     -------------------
     None required restructuring.  The pre-existing
     probes all used small-literal or unambiguously
     negative pids:
       - sched_setattr bad flags = 1
                                  (still != 0 post-trunc → EINVAL)
       - sched_setattr neg pid = u64::MAX
                                  (pid_i32 = -1 → still EINVAL)
       - sched_getattr huge size = 1 << 21
                                  (= 0x200000, fits in u32, still
                                   > PAGE_SIZE → EINVAL)
       - sched_getattr neg pid = u64::MAX
                                  (pid_i32 = -1 → still EINVAL)
       - all the v0/policy/priority/forward-compat
         probes use pid=0, flags=0, size=48 — unchanged
         by truncation.

     New self-tests demonstrating the truncation
     -------------------------------------------
       - sched_setattr(0, valid_v0_attr, 0x1_0000_0000)
         → 0  (flags truncates to 0; body succeeds)
       - sched_setattr(0x1_0000_0000, valid_v0_attr, 0)
         → 0  (pid truncates to 0 → caller path)
       - sched_getattr(0, attr, 48, 0x1_0000_0000) → 0
         (flags truncates to 0)
       - sched_getattr(0, attr, 0x1_0000_0030, 0) → 0
         AND attr.size == 0x30 (size truncates,
         user_size echo reflects the truncated value)
       - sched_getattr(0x1_0000_0000, attr, 48, 0) → 0
         (pid truncates to 0 → caller path)

     Marker:
       "sched_{set,get}attr pid_t/uint truncation: OK"

     Preserved self-tests
     --------------------
     All prior sched_setattr / sched_getattr probes
     remain — combined `!uattr|pid<0|flags` EINVAL gate,
     size<48 EINVAL, SCHED_DEADLINE EOPNOTSUPP,
     OTHER/non-zero-prio EINVAL, forward-compat
     trailing-zero E2BIG, the v0 dispatch round-trip
     and over-write sentinel.  All still pass.

     Known limitations
     -----------------
     - sched_attr.sched_flags (the u64 field inside the
       attr buffer at offset 8..16) still parses as 0
       and we silently drop any non-zero value.  Linux
       validates against SCHED_FLAG_RESET_ON_FORK |
       SCHED_FLAG_RECLAIM | ... and rejects unknown
       bits.  This is independent of register
       truncation and deferred — the divergence is
       documented under entry 217's "Known
       limitations".
     - sched_setattr/getattr's combined gate Linux
       checks `flags` (the register, after truncation)
       distinctly from the in-buffer `sched_flags`
       field; we now match that distinction at the
       register level.  In-buffer field handling is
       still the same as before this batch.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (44.23s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

323. Linux ABI batch 293 — sys_prlimit64: pid_t and
     unsigned int register truncation, plus negative-
     pid ESRCH fallback.

     Pre-batch divergences
     ---------------------
     Linux declares
       long sys_prlimit64(pid_t pid,
                          unsigned int resource,
                          const struct rlimit64 __user *new_rlim,
                          struct rlimit64 __user *old_rlim);
     The x86_64 syscall ABI truncates pid to (int) and
     resource to (unsigned int) before the body runs.
     Pre-batch we held both as raw u64 and compared
     against them:

       Probe A — pid truncates to 0 (self):
         pid = 0x1_0000_0000
           Linux : (int)pid = 0 → self path → ok(0)
           Ours  : raw != 0 → pid != me_pid (0 in
                   kernel context) → EPERM

       Probe B — resource truncates to a valid value:
         resource = 0x1_0000_0000
           Linux : (unsigned int)resource = 0 =
                   RLIMIT_CPU → valid → ok(0)
           Ours  : raw 0x1_0000_0000 >= NUM_RLIMITS
                   (16) → EINVAL

       Probe C — negative pid:
         pid = u64::MAX
           Linux : (int)pid = -1 → find_task_by_vpid
                   returns NULL → ESRCH
           Ours  : raw u64::MAX != me_pid → EPERM
                   (wrong errno; same EPERM Linux uses
                   only when the lookup succeeds but
                   CAP_SYS_RESOURCE is missing)

       Probe D — both pid and resource carry garbage
       high bits:
         pid = 0x1_0000_0000, resource = 0x1_0000_0003
           Linux : (int)pid = 0 → self;
                   (unsigned int)resource = 3 =
                   RLIMIT_STACK; reads default → ok(0).
           Ours  : both gates fired EPERM/EINVAL
                   depending on order.

     Fixes
     -----
     - Cast args.arg0 to i32 (pid_i32) at entry.
     - Cast args.arg1 to u32 (resource_u32) at entry —
       replaces the prior `if resource >= u64::from(
       NUM_RLIMITS)` pattern (which already had a
       u32 derivation below for the PCB calls but ran
       the gate against the raw u64).
     - Resource gate `resource_u32 >= pcb::NUM_RLIMITS`
       now runs on the truncated value.
     - Cross-pid resolution:
         pid_i32 == 0 → self path (no checks)
         pid_i32 != 0 with pid_i32 != me_i32 →
            pid_i32 <  0 → ESRCH  (Linux find_task NULL)
            pid_i32 >  0 → EPERM  (no CAP_SYS_RESOURCE)
       me_i32 = caller_pid().unwrap_or(0) cast to i32 so
       the equality test happens at the same width as
       Linux's int compare.
     - All downstream uses of `pid` (none) and
       `resource_u32` (pcb::get_rlimit, pcb::set_rlimit,
       DEFAULT_RLIMITS indexing) now key off the
       post-truncation value.

     Existing self-tests
     -------------------
     None required restructuring:
       - prlimit64 bad resource = 16          → still EINVAL
                                                 (16 still >= 16
                                                  after u32 truncation)
       - prlimit64 u64::MAX resource         → still EINVAL
                                                 (truncated to 0xFFFFFFFF,
                                                  still >= 16)
       - prlimit64 cross-pid (pid=1, kernel ctx) →
                                                still EPERM
                                                 (pid_i32=1 > 0, !=
                                                  me_i32=0)
       - prlimit64 pid=0, both NULL, valid resource →
                                                still ok(0)
       - prlimit64 cur > max in new_limit    → still EINVAL
       - prlimit64 set valid new_limit in kernel ctx →
                                                still ok(0) with
                                                pre-set old_limit
       - Direct pcb::set_rlimit / get_rlimit coverage
         on a dummy test_pid (unaffected; uses small
         literal values).

     New self-tests demonstrating the truncation
     -------------------------------------------
       - prlimit64(pid=0x1_0000_0000, res=3, NULL, NULL)
         → ok(0)   (pid truncates to 0, self path)
       - prlimit64(pid=0, res=0x1_0000_0000, NULL, NULL)
         → ok(0)   (resource truncates to RLIMIT_CPU=0)
       - prlimit64(pid=u64::MAX, res=3, NULL, NULL)
         → ESRCH   (pid truncates to -1, NULL task)
       - prlimit64(pid=0x1_0000_0000,
                   res=0x1_0000_0003, NULL, &out)
         → ok(0) AND out reflects the
                                  DEFAULT_RLIMITS[3]
                                  pair (proves both
                                  truncations cooperate
                                  end-to-end)

     Marker:
       "prlimit64 pid_t/uint truncation: OK"

     Preserved self-tests
     --------------------
     All prior prlimit64 boot probes (above) plus the
     getrlimit / setrlimit wrapper tests at the bottom
     of dispatch_linux, all of which forward to
     sys_prlimit64 with pid=0 and small literal
     resources unaffected by truncation.

     Known limitations
     -----------------
     - Linux's prlimit64 does a copy_from_user of
       new_rlim BEFORE the find_task_by_vpid lookup,
       so an EFAULT on a bad new_rlim ptr beats the
       ESRCH for a non-existent pid in Linux's gate
       order.  Our order is reversed (pid checks first,
       then pointer reads).  This is a separate, gate-
       ordering divergence independent of register
       truncation and is deferred — documented here so
       a future batch can pick it up.
     - Linux additionally calls security_task_prlimit()
       (LSM hook) between the task lookup and the
       copy_from_user, which we have no equivalent for.
       Not a divergence userspace can observe through
       errno alone unless an LSM is loaded.
     - getrlimit / setrlimit, ioprio_set / ioprio_get,
       setpriority / getpriority, setresuid / setresgid,
       setreuid / setregid have all been audited in
       this batch's scope and are already correctly
       truncating their declared int / uid_t args via
       prior batches' `as i32` / `as u32` casts.  No
       follow-up needed there.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (43.51s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

324. Linux ABI batch 294 — sys_pidfd_open /
     sys_pidfd_send_signal / sys_pidfd_getfd: `unsigned
     int flags` register truncation across the whole
     pidfd family.

     Pre-batch divergences
     ---------------------
     Linux declares
       int sys_pidfd_open(pid_t pid, unsigned int flags);
       int sys_pidfd_send_signal(int pidfd, int sig,
                                 siginfo_t __user *info,
                                 unsigned int flags);
       int sys_pidfd_getfd(int pidfd, int targetfd,
                           unsigned int flags);
     The x86_64 syscall ABI truncates each `flags` arg
     to 32 bits before the body runs.  Pre-batch the
     pid / fd / sig args were already truncated by
     earlier batches, but each stub still tested
     `args.argN != 0` (or `args.argN & !VALID_FLAGS`)
     against the raw u64.  Probes saw:

       pidfd_open (pid=missing, flags=0x1_0000_0000):
         Linux : flags trunc to 0 → ESRCH from lookup
         Ours  : raw & !VALID_FLAGS != 0 → EINVAL

       pidfd_open with high garbage + PIDFD_NONBLOCK
       (flags=0x1_0000_0800):
         Linux : flags trunc to 0x800 = PIDFD_NONBLOCK
                 → valid → ESRCH from lookup
         Ours  : raw 0x1_0000_0800 & !0x880 ≠ 0
                 → EINVAL

       pidfd_send_signal (fd=0, sig=9, info=NULL,
                          flags=0x1_0000_0000):
         Linux : flags trunc to 0 → fdget(0) on
                 kernel ctx → EBADF
         Ours  : raw != 0 → EINVAL

       pidfd_getfd (pidfd=3, targetfd=0,
                    flags=0x1_0000_0000):
         Linux : flags trunc to 0 → fdget(3) on
                 kernel ctx → EBADF
         Ours  : raw != 0 → EINVAL

     Fixes
     -----
     - sys_pidfd_open:
         * `VALID_FLAGS` constants redeclared as u32
           (PIDFD_NONBLOCK = 0o4000, PIDFD_THREAD =
           0o200).
         * `flags = args.arg1 as u32` at entry; the
           `flags & !VALID_FLAGS != 0` gate now runs
           against the truncated value.
         * `nonblock` derivation now reads
           `flags & PIDFD_NONBLOCK` instead of the raw
           `args.arg1 & 0o4000`.
     - sys_pidfd_send_signal:
         * `flags = args.arg3 as u32` at entry; the
           `flags != 0` reservation gate now runs
           against the truncated value.
     - sys_pidfd_getfd:
         * `flags = args.arg2 as u32` at entry; the
           `flags != 0` reservation gate now runs
           against the truncated value.

     Existing self-tests
     -------------------
     None required restructuring:
       - pidfd_open(0, 0) → EINVAL          (pid_i32 ≤ 0)
