       - pidfd_open(1, 1) → EINVAL          (1 still bad after u32)
       - pidfd_open(0x7FFF_FFFE, 0o200)     → ESRCH
       - pidfd_open(0x7FFF_FFFE, 0o4200)    → ESRCH
       - pidfd_open(0x7FFF_FFFE, 0)         → ESRCH
       - pidfd_send_signal(0, 999, 0, 0)    → EBADF
                                              (fdget first; 999 sig
                                               OK after truncation)
       - pidfd_send_signal(u64::MAX, 9, 0, 1)
                                            → EINVAL
                                              (flags=1 truncated to 1,
                                               still != 0)
       - pidfd_send_signal(0, 999, 0x1, 0)  → EBADF
       - pidfd_send_signal(3, 9, 0, 0)      → EBADF
       - pidfd_getfd(0, 0, 1, 0)            → EINVAL
       - pidfd_getfd(0, u64::MAX, 0, 0)     → EBADF
                                              (target_fd<0)
       - pidfd_getfd(3, 0, 0, 0)            → EBADF

     New self-tests demonstrating the truncation
     -------------------------------------------
       - pidfd_open(0x7FFF_FFFE, 0x1_0000_0000)
         → ESRCH  (flags truncates to 0)
       - pidfd_open(0x7FFF_FFFE, 0x1_0000_0800)
         → ESRCH  (flags truncates to PIDFD_NONBLOCK)
       - pidfd_send_signal(0, 9, 0, 0x1_0000_0000)
         → EBADF  (flags truncates to 0)
       - pidfd_getfd(3, 0, 0x1_0000_0000, 0)
         → EBADF  (flags truncates to 0)

     Marker:
       "pidfd_{open,send_signal,getfd} uint flags
        truncation: OK"

     Preserved self-tests
     --------------------
     All prior pidfd_* boot probes (the four listed
     above per syscall) plus the `register_ipc_handle
     pidfd_getfd wiring` end-to-end install test.

     Known limitations
     -----------------
     - Linux's pidfd_open declares the pid arg as
       `pid_t` and rejects pid <= 0 with EINVAL — we
       match.  Linux additionally walks the namespace
       hierarchy (pidfd_get_pid honours the calling
       process's pid namespace); we don't model
       namespaces beyond global pids.  Independent of
       truncation.
     - Linux's pidfd_send_signal info struct
       (siginfo_t = 128 bytes) carries SI_USER vs
       SI_KERNEL provenance that we discard — we hand
       off to sys_kill which synthesises its own
       si_code.  Documented under entry 271's known
       limitations.
     - pidfd_send_signal also accepts a `PIDFD_SIGNAL_*`
       flags bit set on Linux 6.9+ (PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD
       and friends); we still treat any non-zero
       truncated flags as EINVAL.  Independent of
       this batch's u64→u32 fix.

     During this batch's first build I shipped Probe B
     with arg1=0x1_0000_4000 by accident (confusing
     hex 0x4000 with octal 0o4000).  The boot test
     caught it (probe returned -22 EINVAL where ESRCH
     was expected) and the fix swapped in 0x1_0000_0800.
     Documented because a future audit may re-derive
     these constants and the octal/hex confusion is a
     well-trodden trap.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (53.55s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

325. Linux ABI batch 295 — sys_kcmp pid_t/int
     register truncation per x86_64 syscall ABI.

     Divergence
     ----------
     Linux's signature is
         SYSCALL_DEFINE5(kcmp, pid_t, pid1, pid_t, pid2,
                         int, type,
                         unsigned long, idx1,
                         unsigned long, idx2)
     so on x86_64 the kernel function body only sees the
     low 32 bits of arg0/arg1/arg2.  Pre-batch our
     dispatcher already truncated pid1/pid2 to i32 for
     the negative-pid ESRCH gate (added in batch 290's
     gate-order fix), but downstream we still read:
       - `let typ = args.arg2;` (raw u64)
       - `let tid1: u64 = args.arg0;`
       - `let tid2: u64 = args.arg1;`
     These raw values fed both the type switch (`typ > 7
     -> EINVAL`) and the kernel-context fallbacks for
     same_proc / VM-ordering / FILE-ordering.  A probe
     with sentinel high bits in any of these three args
     produced results Linux cannot observe:
       - typ = 0x1_0000_0007: Linux truncates to int 7
         (KCMP_EPOLL_TFD), returns 3; we returned EINVAL
         because 0x1_0000_0007 > 7 as u64.
       - typ = 0xFFFF_FFFF: Linux treats as int -1, hits
         the switch default -> EINVAL.  We also returned
         EINVAL, but via the unsigned > 7 path — right
         answer for the wrong reason (a pre-batch
         test-only divergence, not user-observable).
       - (pid1 = 0x1_0000_0001, pid2 = 0x2, type =
         KCMP_VM, kernel ctx): Linux compares truncated
         (1, 2) -> 1; we compared raw u64
         0x1_0000_0001 > 2 -> 2.
       - (pid1 = 0x1_0000_0005, pid2 = 0x5, type =
         KCMP_FILE, idx1 = idx2 = 4, kernel ctx): Linux
         truncates both to 5, same_proc fallback
         (tid1 == tid2), idx eq -> 0; we saw raw tids
         differ and went to the "different owners" arm,
         returning 2.

     Fix
     ---
     In sys_kcmp at kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~17604:
       - Re-widen tid1/tid2 from pid1_i32/pid2_i32 (via
         `pid_i32 as u64` with clippy::cast_sign_loss
         allow) so the thread-table key and the
         kernel-ctx ordering operand both honour Linux's
         post-truncation view.
       - Cast args.arg2 to i32 at entry, reject
         negative via EINVAL (matches Linux's switch
         default), then check truncated typ > 7 ->
         EINVAL.  Re-widen via `typ_i32 as u64` for the
         downstream KCMP_* matches that still take u64.
       - Preserved the existing pid-ESRCH-ahead-of-type-
         EINVAL gate order (batch 290) and the
         KCMP_EPOLL_TFD null/validate-user-read handling.

     New self-tests
     --------------
     Added four probes in dispatch_linux's kcmp block
     before a new marker
       "[syscall/linux]   kcmp pid_t/int truncation: OK":
       (a) (pid1=1, pid2=1, type=0x1_0000_0007, idx2=
           valid kcmp_epoll_slot) -> 3 (was EINVAL).
       (b) (pid1=1, pid2=1, type=0xFFFF_FFFF) -> EINVAL
           via the new sign check.  Result unchanged
           pre/post-batch but path differs.
       (c) (pid1=0x1_0000_0001, pid2=0x2, type=KCMP_VM)
           -> 1 (was 2).
       (d) (pid1=0x1_0000_0005, pid2=0x5, type=KCMP_FILE,
           idx1=idx2=4) -> 0 (was 2).

     Preserved
     ---------
     - Existing pid-ESRCH-ahead-of-type-EINVAL gate
       order, including u64::MAX-as-pid probes.
     - KCMP_EPOLL_TFD pointer validation (NULL ->
       EFAULT, valid -> 3 since we have no epoll).
     - KCMP_FILE userspace path's pcb::linux_fd_lookup
       and HandleKind ordering — unchanged because that
       arm runs only when caller_pid() is Some, and the
       i32 truncation of args.arg3/args.arg4 was already
       in place pre-batch.

     Known limitations / out of scope
     --------------------------------
     - Linux's ptrace_may_access EPERM gate (between
       find_task and the type switch) is still not
       modeled; we have no ptrace ACL.  Independent of
       truncation.
     - Linux's KCMP_FILE compares struct file pointers
       across processes; our model has no shared
       open-file description layer, so cross-proc
       KCMP_FILE always returns a deterministic order
       rather than a true identity result.  Documented
       under entry 111's known limitations; not a
       truncation issue.
     - KCMP_EPOLL_TFD always returns 3 ("not
       comparable") regardless of the slot's tfd/efd
       values because we have no epoll instances.
       Independent of this batch.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (46.16s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

326. Linux ABI batch 296 — sys_statx `int flags`
     and sys_setns `int nstype` register
     truncation per x86_64 syscall ABI.

     Divergence
     ----------
     Linux declares:
         int statx(int dirfd, const char *pathname,
                   int flags, unsigned int mask,
                   struct statx *statxbuf)
         int setns(int fd, int nstype)
     so `flags` (statx) and `nstype` (setns) are
     32-bit C ints delivered in 64-bit registers but
     only their low 32 bits are visible to the kernel
     function body.  Pre-batch we read both as raw
     u64 directly off args.argN.

     - statx: `let flags = args.arg2;`.  The downstream
       gates `flags & AT_STATX_SYNC_TYPE == ...`,
       `flags & !VALID_FLAGS != 0`, and the
       `flags & AT_EMPTY_PATH` branch all saw the high
       half of the register.  A probe with `flags =
       0x1_0000_1000` (high bit 32 + AT_EMPTY_PATH)
       returned EINVAL via the unknown-flag check
       where Linux truncates to 0x1000, takes the
       AT_EMPTY_PATH branch, and returns 0 against a
       valid statxbuf.
     - setns: `if args.arg1 & !CLONE_NEWNS_FLAGS != 0
       { EINVAL; }`.  A probe with `nstype =
       0x1_0002_0000` (high bit 32 + CLONE_NEWNS)
       returned EINVAL where Linux truncates to
       0x20000 (CLONE_NEWNS), passes the mask gate,
       and falls through to the namespace-fd plumbing
       (EPERM in our stub).

     statx's `mask` arg was already handled correctly
     pre-batch via a local `(mask as u32)` cast at the
     STATX_RESERVED check — that's Linux's exact
     view (`unsigned int mask`), and `mask` is not
     read anywhere else in our stub.  No fix needed
     for mask.  statx's `dirfd` was already cast to
     i32 at entry pre-batch (batch unrecorded).

     Fix
     ---
     - kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~10421 (sys_statx):
       Cast args.arg2 to i32 then re-widen via u32 to
       u64.  Re-derive `flags` from the truncated
       value; the downstream gates remain unchanged
       but now see only the low 32 bits.
     - kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~13797 (sys_setns):
       Same shape — cast args.arg1 to i32 / re-widen
       to u64, mask the truncated value against
       CLONE_NEWNS_FLAGS.

     New self-tests
     --------------
     statx block, new marker
       "[syscall/linux]   statx int flags truncation: OK":
       (a) (path=NULL, flags=0x1_0000_1000, statxbuf=
           valid) -> 0 via AT_EMPTY_PATH Console fast
           path (was EINVAL pre-batch).
       (b) (path=NULL, flags=0x1_0000_0000, statxbuf=
           NULL) -> EFAULT via the NULL-statxbuf gate
           (was EINVAL pre-batch).
       (c) (path=valid, flags=0x1_0000_6000, statxbuf=
           valid) -> EINVAL via sync-type-collision
           gate (was EINVAL via unknown-flag gate
           pre-batch — same answer, different path).

     setns block, new marker
       "[syscall/linux]   setns int nstype truncation: OK":
       (a) (nstype=0x1_0002_0000) -> EPERM (truncates
           to CLONE_NEWNS).  Was EINVAL pre-batch.
       (b) (nstype=0x1_0000_0001) -> EINVAL (truncates
           to 0x1, not a CLONE_NEW* bit).  Was EINVAL
           pre-batch via different path.

     Preserved
     ---------
     - statx's mask-RESERVED-EINVAL gate order vs the
       sync-type-collision-EINVAL gate (batch 225's
       fix) is unchanged.
     - statx's AT_EMPTY_PATH Console-fd fast path,
       NULL-statxbuf -> EFAULT, path-validation, and
       ENOENT fallback all unchanged.
     - setns's validate_linux_fd gate order
       (fd-validation ahead of the nstype mask check)
       unchanged.

     Known limitations / out of scope
     --------------------------------
     - statx's path-resolution arm still returns
       ENOENT against any non-empty path because we
       have no backing filesystem at boot time.
       Documented under earlier statx entries.
     - setns still answers EPERM for every well-formed
       (fd, nstype) pair because the namespace-fd
       plumbing is not implemented.  Independent of
       truncation.
     - statx's `unsigned int mask`: the local
       `(mask as u32)` cast at the STATX_RESERVED
       check matches Linux's view exactly, and mask
       is not consulted again.  Not changed.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (47.55s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

327. Linux ABI batch 297 — fd-creator family
     int/unsigned-int flags register truncation per
     x86_64 syscall ABI.  Five syscalls share the
     same shape: a declared 32-bit C-int flags arg
     read as raw u64 and bit-masked against
     VALID_FLAGS, so sentinel high bits 32+ produced
     EINVAL where Linux truncates the high half away
     and proceeds.

     Affected syscalls and prototypes:
       - sys_signalfd4   (int flags)
       - sys_timerfd_create  (int flags)
       - sys_inotify_init1   (int flags)
       - sys_userfaultfd     (int flags)
       - sys_memfd_create    (unsigned int flags)

     Divergence
     ----------
     Each stub had `let flags = args.argN;` followed
     by `if flags & !VALID_FLAGS != 0 { EINVAL; }`.
     A probe with `flags = 0x1_0000_0800` (bit 32 +
     SFD_NONBLOCK / TFD_NONBLOCK / IN_NONBLOCK) or
     `0x1_0000_0001` (bit 32 + USER_MODE_ONLY /
     MFD_CLOEXEC) returned EINVAL where Linux
     truncates `flags` to the low 32 bits, sees the
     valid bit, and proceeds to:
       - signalfd4    -> ENOSYS (no signalfd backing)
       - timerfd_create -> ENOSYS (no timerfd impl)
       - inotify_init1  -> ENOSYS (no inotify impl)
       - userfaultfd    -> ENOSYS (privilege gate
                          passed but no impl)
       - memfd_create   -> next gate (NULL-name
                          EFAULT in the test probe).

     Userfaultfd is doubly affected: a probe with
     just bit 32 set (`flags = 0x1_0000_0000`)
     truncates to 0, which then takes the
     non-USER_MODE_ONLY -> EPERM branch (Linux
     requires CAP_SYS_PTRACE).  Pre-batch: EINVAL via
     unknown-flag gate.

     Fix
     ---
     For each syscall, cast the flags arg at entry:
       - signalfd4 (~11196):   args.arg3 as i32 -> u32 -> u64
       - timerfd_create (~11215): args.arg1 as i32 -> u32 -> u64
       - inotify_init1 (~11374):  args.arg0 as i32 -> u32 -> u64
       - userfaultfd (~12541):    args.arg0 as i32 -> u32 -> u64
       - memfd_create (~12583):   args.arg1 as u32 -> u64
                                  (declared unsigned int)
     Downstream bit-mask, MFD_HUGETLB / HUGE_SIZE_MASK
     subset check, and UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY check all
     read the new truncated `flags` automatically via
     shadowing.

     New self-tests
     --------------
     One consolidated block after the memfd_create
     "flags-first / EXEC+NOEXEC_SEAL / HUGETLB"
     marker, with marker
       "[syscall/linux]   fd-creator family int flags truncation: OK":
       (a) signalfd4(fd=-1, mask=valid, sizemask=8,
           flags=0x1_0000_0800) -> ENOSYS.
       (b) timerfd_create(CLOCK_MONOTONIC,
           flags=0x1_0000_0800) -> ENOSYS.
       (c) inotify_init1(flags=0x1_0000_0800) -> ENOSYS.
       (d) userfaultfd(flags=0x1_0000_0001) -> ENOSYS.
       (d2) userfaultfd(flags=0x1_0000_0000) -> EPERM.
       (e) memfd_create(name=NULL,
           flags=0x1_0000_0001) -> EFAULT.

     Preserved
     ---------
     - signalfd4's sizemask > mask-EFAULT > flag-mask
       gate order (batch unrecorded).
     - timerfd_create's clockid range check.
     - userfaultfd's UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY -> EPERM gate
       (now reads the truncated flags).
     - memfd_create's flags-first > EXEC|NOEXEC_SEAL
       mutex > NULL-name EFAULT gate order (preserved
       because flags shadowing happens before the
       first gate).
     - MFD_HUGETLB + HUGE_SIZE_MASK (bits 26..31)
       remain valid because they sit entirely in the
       low 32 bits.

     Known limitations / out of scope
     --------------------------------
     - signalfd / signalfd4: HandleKind::SignalFd
       still doesn't exist, so fd != -1 still returns
       EINVAL (real signalfd plumbing deferred per
       earlier entries).
     - timerfd / inotify / userfaultfd: no backing
       impl; ENOSYS surfaces for all valid inputs.
     - memfd_create's strnlen_user uses
       validate_user_read on the user pointer, which
       has a kernel-context bypass — the kernel-ctx
       memfd self-tests can only observe the flags
       and EFAULT gates, not the full inode install
       path.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (47.11s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

328. Linux ABI batch 298 — sys_wait4 pid_t + int
     options register truncation per x86_64 syscall
     ABI.

     Divergence
     ----------
     Linux declares:
         pid_t wait4(pid_t pid, int *wstatus,
                     int options, struct rusage *rusage)
     so `pid_t pid` and `int options` are 32-bit C
     types delivered in 64-bit registers, and only
     the low 32 bits of rdi / rdx are visible to the
     kernel function body.  Pre-batch our stub
     declared:
         let pid_arg = args.arg0 as i64;
         let options = args.arg2;          // raw u64
     which preserved the high half of each register.
     Two observable divergences:
       - options = 0x1_0000_0001 (bit 32 + WNOHANG):
         Linux truncates to WNOHANG, no children ->
         ECHILD via set_wait_any_task in our model;
         pre-batch returned EINVAL via the unknown-
         option-bit gate.
       - options = 0x1_0000_0000 (bit 32 only):
         Linux truncates to 0 (no WNOHANG), enters
         the wait path -> ECHILD in our model;
         pre-batch returned EINVAL.
     pid truncation is internally correct under the
     fix but externally indistinguishable from
     pre-batch in kernel context (both wait-specific
     and wait-any paths surface -ECHILD when the
     parent has no children).  Documented as a
     correctness fix not a behavioural one.

     Fix
     ---
     kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~23832:
       - Cast args.arg0 to i32 first, then re-widen
         to i64 for the existing `pid_arg > 0`
         test and the `target_pid as u64` conversion.
       - Cast args.arg2 to i32, then re-widen via
         u32 to u64 for the existing VALID_OPTIONS
         bit-mask and WNOHANG bit check.
     Downstream control flow (specific-pid loop, wait-
     any loop, blocking, status encoding) is
     unchanged — the truncated values feed the same
     surfaces.

     Existing test compatibility
     ---------------------------
     Pre-existing tests:
       - arg0=u64::MAX, arg2=1|0x4000_0000: pid_i32 =
         -1 (wait-any path same as before),
         options_i32 = 0x4000_0001 (positive, bit 30
         is the EINVAL trigger — still -EINVAL).
       - arg0=u64::MAX, arg2=1 (WNOHANG): wait-any
         path with WNOHANG, identical pre/post.
       - arg0=0xDEAD_BEEF, arg2=1: pid_i32 = -559M
         (sign bit set), routes to wait-any instead
         of wait-specific.  Test only checks
         non-EINVAL and non-success, both upheld.
       The wait-specific-vs-any change for this probe
       is a Linux-correct reroute (0xDEAD_BEEF as
       pid_t is negative -> "any child in process
       group |-pid|" -> we map to wait-any).

     New self-tests
     --------------
     Three probes appended before a new marker
       "[syscall/linux]   wait4 pid_t/int options truncation: OK":
       (a) (pid=-1, options=0x1_0000_0001) -> non-
           EINVAL (truncates to WNOHANG, ECHILD).
       (b) (pid=-1, options=0x1_0000_0000) -> non-
           EINVAL (truncates to 0, ECHILD).
       (c) (pid=0x1_0000_0000, options=WNOHANG) ->
           non-EINVAL and negative (truncates pid to
           0 -> wait-any -> ECHILD).

     Preserved
     ---------
     - The WAIT4 wait-any (pid <= 0) vs wait-specific
       (pid > 0) routing.
     - WNOHANG fast-return-0 contract for empty
       wait-any.
     - lost-wakeup-safe set_wait_task / try_reap
       retry pattern in both wait paths.
     - encode_linux_wstatus + rusage zeroing on
       successful reap.

     Known limitations / out of scope
     --------------------------------
     - pid_t truncation correctness can't be
       externally observed in kernel context because
       pcb has no children for parent_pid=0;
       distinguishable only in real userspace with
       actual child processes.
     - wstatus / rusage EFAULT can't be triggered
       from kernel-context tests due to
       validate_user_write's documented bypass.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (44.43s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

  329. Batch 299 — sys_clock_gettime + sys_clock_nanosleep
       x86_64 register-truncation tightening.

       Divergence
       ----------
       Two adjacent posix-clock entry points read
       declared 32-bit C-types as raw u64:

       (a) sys_clock_gettime (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
           ~24539): `let clockid = args.arg0;` was raw.
           The match below compares clockid against
           u64-typed constants (`const CLOCK_REALTIME:
           u64 = 0;` etc.).  A sentinel like
           clockid=0x1_0000_0000 has low 32 == 0 ==
           CLOCK_REALTIME but doesn't equal the u64
           constant 0, so it fell through to
           `_ => return linux_err(errno::EINVAL)`.
           Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE2(clock_gettime) casts
           clockid_t (int) before any branching, so the
           same sentinel resolves to REALTIME and fills
           the timespec.

       (b) sys_clock_nanosleep (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
           ~24611): `let flags = args.arg1;` was raw and
           the gate `if flags & !TIMER_ABSTIME != 0`
           rejected high-bit-set values that Linux would
           truncate.  flags=0x1_0000_0001 returned EINVAL
           where Linux truncates to TIMER_ABSTIME=1 and
           takes the ABS branch.

           A separate inner divergence existed at the
           `let now_ns: u64 = match clockid { 0 =>
           timekeeping::clock_realtime(), _ =>
           hrtimer::now_ns() };` pick, which read raw u64
           clockid even though the upfront range check
           used clockid_i32.  After this batch the
           identifier `clockid` itself is the
           re-widened-from-i32-via-u32 value, so the
           inner match is automatically correct.

       Fix
       ---
       In sys_clock_gettime, replace the raw read with:
         let clockid_i32 = args.arg0 as i32;
         let clockid: u64 = u64::from(clockid_i32 as u32);
       The match arms still compare against u64 constants
       and now see the zero-extended low-32 value.

       In sys_clock_nanosleep, consolidate the two casts
       at the top of the function:
         let clockid_i32 = args.arg0 as i32;
         let clockid: u64 = u64::from(clockid_i32 as u32);
         let flags_i32 = args.arg1 as i32;
         let flags: u64 = u64::from(flags_i32 as u32);
       and remove the redundant `let clockid_i32 =
       clockid as i32;` further down.  All downstream
       reads of `clockid` (range check via clockid_i32,
       EOPNOTSUPP match, inner now_ns match) and `flags`
       (the !TIMER_ABSTIME gate and the TIMER_ABSTIME
       branch pick) now see the truncated value.

       All three casts get
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation,
              clippy::cast_possible_wrap,
              clippy::cast_sign_loss)]
       split across the two `as` steps to mirror the
       batches-295..298 pattern.

       New self-tests
       --------------
       Three probes appended before a new marker
         "[syscall/linux]   clock_gettime/clock_nanosleep
          clockid_t/int flags truncation: OK"
       inserted after the wait4 marker:

       (a) clock_gettime(clockid=0x1_0000_0000, NULL)
           -> -EFAULT.  Pre-batch: -EINVAL via the raw
           u64-match fallthrough.
       (b) clock_gettime(clockid=0x1_0000_0008, &ts_buf)
           -> 0.  Pre-batch: -EINVAL.  Truncates to
           CLOCK_REALTIME_ALARM=8, which the
           per-batch-prior fix already accepts as
           realtime.
       (c) clock_nanosleep(clockid=CLOCK_MONOTONIC,
           flags=0x1_0000_0001, &zero_ts, NULL) -> 0.
           Pre-batch: -EINVAL via the flag gate.
           Truncates to TIMER_ABSTIME=1; target_ns=0
           is "already past", yield_now, success.

       Preserved
       ---------
       - clock_gettime u64 const arms (CLOCK_REALTIME=0
         .. CLOCK_TAI=11) unchanged; only the type the
         match scrutinee carries was tightened.
       - clock_nanosleep gate order
         (clockid -> EINVAL/EOPNOTSUPP, read_timespec
         -> EFAULT, timespec_valid -> EINVAL,
         flags & !TIMER_ABSTIME -> EINVAL) preserved.
       - The clockid_i32 range check `(0..=11).contains
         && != 10` and the EOPNOTSUPP `matches!(3|4|5|6)`
         filter are unchanged.
       - clock_gettime ALARM/TAI accept path
         (REALTIME_ALARM=8, BOOTTIME_ALARM=9, TAI=11
         all -> realtime/monotonic) preserved.

       Known limitations / out of scope
       --------------------------------
       - clock_nanosleep inner now_ns match divergence
         (raw u64 clockid -> hrtimer when high bits set)
         is fixed implicitly by the rename of `clockid`
         to the truncated u64, but the symptom is hard
         to test externally because the timespec is
         already zero and the ABS branch saturates to 0
         regardless of clock source.  Probe (c) demands
         success and would have failed with the old
         flag gate; the inner divergence on its own is
         silent in kernel context.
       - sys_perf_event_open audit (pid_t + int cpu +
         int group_fd) was scoped but not landed in
         this batch — its body extends past line 12380
         and was not fully traversed; deferred to a
         later batch.

       Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (45.19s).
       Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                  --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 27s).

  330. Batch 300 — sys_seccomp + sys_quotactl +
       sys_quotactl_fd unsigned-int register truncation.

       Divergence
       ----------
       Three syscalls read declared 32-bit C-type args
       as raw u64:

       (a) sys_seccomp (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~17877):
           `let op = args.arg0; let flags = args.arg1;`.
           Linux signature is SYSCALL_DEFINE3(seccomp,
           unsigned int op, unsigned int flags,
           void __user *uargs).  The `match op` arms
           compare against bare integer literals which
           Rust infers as u64.  A sentinel like op =
           0x1_0000_0000 (low 32 == SET_MODE_STRICT)
           fell to `_ => EINVAL` because the u64
           literal 0 is not equal to 0x1_0000_0000.
           Linux truncates to (u32)0 and takes the
           STRICT arm.
           Similarly flags=0x1_0000_0000 raw-u64 != 0
           tripped the `flags != 0` EINVAL gate on ops
           0/2/3 even though the truncated flags is 0.

       (b) sys_quotactl (~13626): cmd is `unsigned int`
           in the Linux ABI.  Pre-batch computed `cmds
           = args.arg0 >> SUBCMDSHIFT` as a u64 shift,
           so any high bit set in args.arg0 leaked into
           the cmds whitelist comparison.  Sentinel cmd
           = 0x1_8000_0100: u64 shift -> 0x180_0001,
           not in 0x800001..=0x800009 -> EINVAL.  Linux
           truncates to u32 0x8000_0100, shifts to
           0x800001 (Q_SYNC), passes the whitelist, and
           reaches the terminal EPERM.

       (c) sys_quotactl_fd (~13682): same cmd shape but
           the cmd is at args.arg1 (fd is at args.arg0).
           Same divergence as (b).

       Fix
       ---
       For sys_seccomp: cast both op and flags via a
       u32 intermediate then re-widen to u64 so the
       existing match-against-u64-literals and
       flag-mask gate logic sees the truncated value:
           let op_u32 = args.arg0 as u32;
           let op: u64 = u64::from(op_u32);
           let flags_u32 = args.arg1 as u32;
           let flags: u64 = u64::from(flags_u32);

       For sys_quotactl / sys_quotactl_fd: introduce a
       local `cmd` re-widened from u32 and feed both
       the SUBCMDMASK extract and the SUBCMDSHIFT
       right-shift from that value.  The existing
       cmds-as-u64 whitelist comparison still works
       because the value is now bounded by u32::MAX
       before the shift.

       New self-tests
       --------------
       Four probes appended before a new marker
         "[syscall/linux]   seccomp/quotactl/quotactl_fd
          unsigned-int truncation: OK"
       inserted after the batch-299 clock marker:

       (a) seccomp(op=0x1_0000_0000, flags=0, uargs=0)
           -> -ENOSYS.  Pre-batch: -EINVAL (default arm).
       (b) seccomp(op=GET_ACTION_AVAIL=2, flags=
           0x1_0000_0000, &buf) -> -ENOSYS.  Pre-batch:
           -EINVAL via flags != 0 gate.
       (c) quotactl(cmd=0x1_8000_0100, NULL, 0, 0)
           -> -EPERM.  Pre-batch: -EINVAL via the cmds
           whitelist gate.
       (d) quotactl_fd(fd=u64::MAX, cmd=0x1_8000_0100,
           ...) -> -EPERM.  Pre-batch: -EINVAL via the
           cmds whitelist gate.  Kernel-context
           validate_linux_fd is a no-op (caller_pid()
           returns None), so the fd value doesn't
           matter; what we're testing is the cmd path.

       Preserved
       ---------
       - seccomp gate order (op match -> per-op
         flag/uargs checks -> ENOSYS terminal).
       - quotactl gate order (type<3 -> cmds whitelist
         -> special path validation -> EPERM terminal).
       - quotactl_fd gate order (type<3 -> fd validate
         -> cmds whitelist -> EPERM terminal).
       - SECCOMP_FILTER_FLAG_MASK (0x3f) and the
         generic_ok / xfs_ok cmds ranges unchanged.

       Known limitations / out of scope
       --------------------------------
       - First boot test attempt expected -EBADF for
         probe (d) by passing fd=u64::MAX, but kernel-
         context validate_linux_fd short-circuits to
         Ok(()) when caller_pid() is None, so the path
         continues past the fd check.  Corrected the
         expected to -EPERM and noted the rationale in
         the probe comment.
       - sys_io_uring_setup has a similar shape (entries
         is unsigned int, args.arg0 read raw) but the
         pre-batch gates collapse to EINVAL via either
         the entries-as-0 gate (truncated 0) or the
         entries>32768 gate (raw u64 high bits), so the
         divergence is not externally observable without
         a valid io_uring_params struct.  Deferred until
         a real io_uring_params setup helper is needed.
       - sys_perf_event_open audit (pid_t pid + int cpu
         + int group_fd) still pending — its body wasn't
         fully traversed in this batch.

       Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (38.61s).
       Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                  --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 22s).

  331. Batch 301 — sys_io_setup + sys_swapon +
       sys_membarrier unsigned-int / int truncation
       audit.

       Divergence
       ----------
       Three Linux ABI stubs read 32-bit C-typed args
       (unsigned int / int) as raw u64, so syscall
       inputs whose high half is set (e.g. 0x1_0000_0001
       or 0x1_0000_0000) miss gates that Linux's
       narrowed view passes:
       - sys_io_setup(unsigned nr_events, ctxp):
         `args.arg0 == 0` compared raw, so
         nr_events=0x1_0000_0000 (truncates to 0)
         bypassed the EINVAL gate and reached our
         terminal ENOSYS where Linux returns EINVAL.
       - sys_swapon(specialfile, int swap_flags):
         `args.arg1 & !SWAP_FLAGS_VALID != 0` ran
         against raw u64, so swap_flags=0x1_0000_0000
         hit EINVAL where Linux truncates to 0 and
         reaches EPERM (no CAP_SYS_ADMIN).
       - sys_membarrier(int cmd, unsigned flags,
         int cpu_id): cmd / flags matched against
         u64-typed bit constants
         (MEMBARRIER_CMD_GLOBAL=1<<0, FLAG_CPU=1, ...).
         A probe with cmd=0x1_0000_0001 missed every
         arm and surfaced terminal EINVAL where Linux
         truncates to GLOBAL=1 and runs the fence
         (returns 0).  Likewise flags=0x1_0000_0000
         failed `flags & !FLAG_CPU != 0` with EINVAL
         where Linux truncates to 0 and passes.

       Fix
       ---
       Cast each arg at function entry to its declared
       Linux C type, then zero-extend back to u64 for
       comparison with the existing const tables:
       - sys_io_setup: `let nr_events_u32 = args.arg0 as
         u32; let nr_events: u64 = u64::from(...);`.
       - sys_swapon: standard i32->u32->u64 ladder for
         `swap_flags`.
       - sys_membarrier: same ladder for cmd (int) and
         the u32->u64 form for flags (unsigned int).
         Negative cmd inputs zero-extend to a huge u64
         that misses every positive bit-constant arm,
         falling through to the same default EINVAL
         that Linux's signed switch produces — answer
         shape preserved.

       New self-tests
       --------------
       Added a "Batch 301" block at the end of
       dispatch_linux's Linux ABI self-tests with four
       probes:
         (a) io_setup(nr_events=0x1_0000_0000,
             ctxp=&u64::0) -> -EINVAL.
         (b) swapon(specialfile=&buf, swap_flags=
             0x1_0000_0000) -> -EPERM.
         (c) membarrier(cmd=0x1_0000_0001, flags=0,
             cpu_id=0) -> 0.
         (d) membarrier(cmd=1, flags=0x1_0000_0000,
             cpu_id=0) -> 0.
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]
         io_setup/swapon/membarrier unsigned-int/int
         truncation: OK".

       Preserved
       ---------
       - io_setup gate order (ctxp NULL -> EFAULT;
         ctxp readability -> EFAULT; ctx_in!=0 ||
         nr_events==0 -> EINVAL; terminal ENOSYS).
       - swapon gate order (flag-mask EINVAL first,
         then NULL-path EFAULT, then validate_user_read
         EFAULT, then terminal EPERM).
       - membarrier QUERY branch's `flags!=0 ||
         cpu_id!=0 -> EINVAL` still correct because
         post-truncation comparisons preserve the same
         answer for the existing tests
         (FLAG_CPU + EXPEDITED gate, register/global
         match arms).

       Known limitations / out of scope
       --------------------------------
       - sys_io_submit / sys_io_getevents `nr` /
         `min_nr` are `long` (64-bit on x86_64), no
         truncation needed — already correctly read
         as i64.
       - sys_io_uring_setup `entries` is unsigned int
         but pre-batch divergence is masked by the
         param-fetch order (see batch 300 todo).
         Still deferred.
       - sys_membarrier's `cpu_id` is int but unused
         (we have one CPU); leaving as raw u64 since
         it never enters a comparison.

       Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (36.43s).
       Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                  --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 26s).

  332. Batch 302 — sys_finit_module +
       sys_delete_module int / unsigned-int flag
       truncation audit.

       Divergence
       ----------
       Two module-load stubs read 32-bit C-typed flag
       args as raw u64, so probes with the high half
       set bypass / fail the flag-mask gates in the
       opposite direction from Linux:
       - sys_finit_module(int fd, char* uargs, int
         flags): `args.arg2 & !VALID_FLAGS != 0`
         compared raw, so flags=0x1_0000_0000
         (truncates to 0) hit EINVAL where Linux
         truncates to 0 and reaches the terminal
         EPERM (no CAP_SYS_MODULE).
       - sys_delete_module(char* name, unsigned int
         flags): same shape; flags=0x1_0000_0000 hit
         EINVAL where Linux returns EPERM.

       Fix
       ---
       Cast each arg at function entry to its declared
       Linux C type, then zero-extend back to u64 for
       comparison with the existing VALID_FLAGS const:
       - sys_finit_module: i32->u32->u64 ladder (int
         flags, sign-extension would alias the kernel-
         level mask but the existing VALID_FLAGS is
         3 bits so any negative truncation aliases
         far away from the mask either way; zero-
         extending via `as u32` preserves Linux's
         post-truncation answer).
       - sys_delete_module: u32->u64 ladder (unsigned
         int flags).

       New self-tests
       --------------
       Added a "Batch 302" block at the end of
       dispatch_linux's Linux ABI self-tests with two
       probes:
         (a) finit_module(fd=0, uargs=0,
             flags=0x1_0000_0000) -> -EPERM.
         (b) delete_module(name=&buf,
             flags=0x1_0000_0000) -> -EPERM.
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]
         finit_module/delete_module int/unsigned-int
         flag truncation: OK".

       Preserved
       ---------
       - finit_module gate order (flag-mask EINVAL
         first, then fd validation, then optional
         uargs validation, then terminal EPERM).
       - delete_module gate order (flag-mask EINVAL
         first, then arg0==0 EFAULT, then user-read
         validation, then terminal EPERM).
       - All existing VALID_FLAGS constants (1|2|4
         and 0o4000|0o1000).

       Known limitations / out of scope
       --------------------------------
       - sys_init_module has `unsigned long len`
         (64-bit on x86_64), no truncation needed.
       - sys_kexec_load `flags` and `nr_segments`
         are both `unsigned long` (64-bit), no
         truncation needed.
       - sys_kexec_file_load `cmdline_len` and
         `flags` are both `unsigned long`, no
         truncation.  kernel_fd and initrd_fd are
         already cast to i32 (batch ~145).
       - sys_io_uring_setup `entries` is `u32` and
         could in principle show a divergence at
         entries = high_bit + value_above_32768
         (truncates to above MAX but pre-batch raw
         u64 also above MAX -> same EINVAL).  Pre-
         batch raw EINVAL and post-batch EINVAL
         collapse; not externally observable
         without a valid params struct in the right
         offset windows.  Deferred until io_uring
         actually lands.

       Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (35.51s).
       Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                  --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 22s).

  333. Batch 303 — sys_arch_prctl int code
       truncation audit.

       Divergence
       ----------
       Linux's `SYSCALL_DEFINE2(arch_prctl, int, option,
       unsigned long, arg2)` truncates `option` to its
       low 32 bits before the body runs.  Pre-batch we
       held code as raw u64 and matched against the
       u64-typed ARCH_* constants
       (ARCH_GET_CPUID=0x1011, ARCH_GET_XCOMP_SUPP=
       0x1021, etc.).  Probes with the high half set
       (e.g. code=0x1_0000_1011) missed every arm and
       fell to `_ => EINVAL` where Linux truncates and
       reaches the matching arm.

       Fix
       ---
       Cast args.arg0 to i32 at function entry, then
       zero-extend back to u64 via the standard
       i32->u32->u64 ladder for comparison with the
       existing ARCH_* const table.  addr stays as
       raw u64 because Linux declares arg2 as
       `unsigned long`.  Negative code inputs zero-
       extend to a huge u64 that misses every positive
       ARCH_* arm — same default EINVAL Linux produces
       on a signed match against negative integers.

       New self-tests
       --------------
       Added a "Batch 303" block at the end of
       dispatch_linux's Linux ABI self-tests with two
       probes:
         (a) arch_prctl(code=0x1_0000_1011, addr=0)
             -> 1 (ARCH_GET_CPUID = enabled).
         (b) arch_prctl(code=0x1_0000_1021, addr=0)
             -> -EFAULT (ARCH_GET_XCOMP_SUPP gates
             the user write on addr!=0).
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]
         arch_prctl int code truncation: OK".

       Preserved
       ---------
       - ARCH_SET_FS / ARCH_SET_GS USER_FS_BASE_MAX
         gate (EPERM for kernel-range addresses).
       - ARCH_GET_FS / ARCH_GET_GS NULL-pointer EFAULT.
       - ARCH_SET_CPUID enable/disable/EINVAL ladder.
       - ARCH_REQ_XCOMP_PERM bit-index validation.

       Known limitations / out of scope
       --------------------------------
       - sys_prctl(int option) has the same int-
         truncation surface but the match block runs
         from ~5350 to ~6809 (1459 lines, many arms
         with side effects).  Lifting code-as-i32 to
         the top is a one-line change but each arm
         needs to be re-audited to confirm Linux's
         post-truncation answer is preserved.
         Deferred to its own batch.
       - sys_ioprio_set/get already cast which/who/
         ioprio to i32 at entry (missing #[allow]
         attributes but functionally correct).  No
         divergence.
       - sys_personality already casts to u32 at
         entry (Linux declares persona as unsigned
         int).  No divergence.

       Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (35.84s).
       Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                  --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

  334. Batch 304 — sys_getitimer + sys_setitimer
       int which truncation audit.

       Divergence
       ----------
       Linux's `SYSCALL_DEFINE2(getitimer, int, which, ...)`
       and `SYSCALL_DEFINE3(setitimer, int, which, ...)`
       declare which as `int` (32-bit), so the x86_64
       syscall ABI truncates args.arg0 to its low 32
       bits before the body runs.  Pre-batch we held
       which as raw u64 and tested `which > 2 ->
       EINVAL`, so probes with the high half set
       (e.g. which=0x1_0000_0000, truncates to
       ITIMER_REAL=0) hit EINVAL where Linux truncates
       and reaches the timer's normal path.

       Fix
       ---
       Cast args.arg0 to i32 at function entry, then
       use the i32-typed range check
       `(0..=2).contains(&which_i32)` to mirror
       Linux's signed-switch default arm.  This also
       captures negative which (post-truncation) which
       the raw u64 form was implicitly handling via
       "huge unsigned > 2".

       New self-tests
       --------------
       Added a "Batch 304" block at the end of
       dispatch_linux's Linux ABI self-tests with two
       probes:
         (a) getitimer(which=0x1_0000_0000, value=&buf)
             -> 0.  Pre-batch: -EINVAL.
         (b) setitimer(which=0x1_0000_0000, new=NULL,
             old=NULL) -> 0.  Pre-batch: -EINVAL.
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]
         getitimer/setitimer int which truncation: OK".

       Preserved
       ---------
       - getitimer gate order (which validation,
         then NULL-value EFAULT, then user-write
         validation, then zero-buffer copy).
       - setitimer gate order (copy_from_user first
         to honour Linux's "value NULL means cancel",
         then timeval_valid, then which validation,
         then is_cancel branching).
       - is_cancel vs ENOSYS arming behaviour
         unchanged.

       Known limitations / out of scope
       --------------------------------
       - sys_prctl(int option) still deferred — its
         match block is too large to lift the i32
         cast in a single batch without re-auditing
         every arm.  Marked as deferred in batch 303.
       - sys_pipe2 already truncates flags to u32 at
         entry (missing #[allow] but functionally
         correct).
       - sys_getrusage(int who) does not validate who
         at all — Linux returns EINVAL for who not in
         {RUSAGE_SELF=0, RUSAGE_CHILDREN=-1,
         RUSAGE_THREAD=1}.  This is a missing-input-
         validation divergence rather than a
         truncation divergence; deferred to a separate
         "missing input validation" sweep.
       - sys_sched_getparam/setparam (pid_t pid)
         already cast pid to i32 in prior batches.
       - sys_setrlimit/getrlimit (unsigned int
         resource) — should audit; deferred to next
         batch.

       Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.83s).
       Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                  --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

335. Batch 305 — sys_getrusage missing `who` input
     validation + int truncation gate.

     Divergence: Linux's
     `SYSCALL_DEFINE2(getrusage, int, who, struct rusage __user *, ru)`
     in kernel/sys.c gates `who not in {RUSAGE_SELF=0,
     RUSAGE_CHILDREN=-1, RUSAGE_THREAD=1}` → EINVAL
     *before* touching the user pointer.  Pre-batch we
     skipped that gate entirely: any `who` value
     (including unspecified codes like 42, or high-half
     sentinels like 0x1_0000_0000) fell straight through
     to the NULL/copy_to_user path, returning EFAULT or
     success where Linux returns EINVAL.  Feature-
     detection probes (`is getrusage(THREAD) supported?`)
     saw contradictory results vs. Linux.  Same x86_64
     ABI int-truncation thread as batches 285/286/303/
     304 — `who` is delivered in a 64-bit register and
     must be cast to i32 before the gate runs.

     Fix:
       - In sys_getrusage: cast args.arg0 to i32 first,
         then reject any value not in
         {RUSAGE_SELF=0, RUSAGE_CHILDREN=-1,
          RUSAGE_THREAD=1} with EINVAL, before the
         existing NULL pointer check.  Constants defined
         locally; matches Linux's `kernel/sys.c` order.

     Tests added in dispatch_linux:
       - (a) getrusage(who=2, ru=NULL) → EINVAL (gate
             fires before NULL check).
       - (b) getrusage(who=0x1_0000_0000, ru=NULL) →
             EFAULT (truncates to (int)0=SELF, passes
             gate, NULL → EFAULT).
       - (c) getrusage(who=u64::MAX, ru=NULL) → EFAULT
             (truncates to (int)-1=CHILDREN, passes
             gate, NULL → EFAULT).  Regression guard:
             ensures CHILDREN stays in the allowed set.
       - (d) getrusage(who=42, ru=NULL) → EINVAL
             (garbage who).
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]   getrusage who input
       validation + int truncation: OK".

     Existing getrusage probe at the
     getrusage/sysinfo/times dispatch block (who=0,
     ru=NULL → EFAULT) still passes: who=0=RUSAGE_SELF
     clears the new gate, then NULL → EFAULT as before.

     Preserved behaviour:
       - Valid who (SELF/CHILDREN/THREAD) with NULL
         pointer still returns EFAULT.
       - Valid who with a valid pointer still returns
         a 144-byte zero rusage and ok(0).
       - Pointer validation order unchanged after the
         who gate (NULL → EFAULT, then
         validate_user_write, then copy_to_user).

     Known limitations (deferred):
       - We still return all-zero rusage for SELF/
         CHILDREN/THREAD — accurate utime/stime/maxrss
         accounting requires a per-PCB resource counter
         which doesn't exist yet.  Tracked alongside
         the scheduler accounting work.
       - RUSAGE_THREAD currently returns the same zero
         rusage as RUSAGE_SELF; once per-thread
         accounting exists, THREAD should report only
         the current thread's usage.
       - sys_setrlimit/getrlimit (unsigned int
         resource) audited last batch — they delegate
         to sys_prlimit64 which already casts resource
         to u32 (verified, batch 305).  Closing that
         deferred item.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (35.29s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

336. Batch 306 — sys_setgroups int truncation +
     unsigned NGROUPS_MAX gate.

     Divergence: Linux's
     `SYSCALL_DEFINE2(setgroups, int gidsetsize, gid_t __user *grouplist)`
     in kernel/groups.c gates with
     `if ((unsigned)gidsetsize > NGROUPS_MAX) return -EINVAL;`.
     gidsetsize is `int` (truncated to 32 bits by the
     x86_64 ABI) then reinterpreted as `unsigned` for the
     bound comparison so negative values surface as huge
     positive and fail the gate.

     Pre-batch we held `size` as raw u64 and compared
     directly to NGROUPS_MAX(65536).  High-half
     sentinels diverged:
       - size=0x1_0000_0000: raw > 65536 → EINVAL.
         Linux: (int)0=0 → empty install → 0.
       - size=0x1_0000_0001: raw > 65536 → EINVAL.
         Linux: (int)1 → reaches list copy, NULL → EFAULT.
       - size=u64::MAX: raw > 65536 → EINVAL.  Linux:
         (int)-1, (unsigned)-1=0xFFFFFFFF > 65536 →
         EINVAL.  Same result, different reasoning.
       - size=0x8000_0000: raw > 65536 → EINVAL.  Linux:
         (int)INT_MIN, (unsigned)0x80000000 > 65536 →
         EINVAL.  Same result, different reasoning.

     Fix:
       - Cast args.arg0 to i32 first (x86_64 int trunc).
       - Reinterpret i32 as u32 via `as u32` (Linux's
         `(unsigned)gidsetsize` semantics — bit pattern
         preserved).
       - Compare size_u32 > NGROUPS_MAX (now u32, not
         u64).
       - Use size_u32 for the subsequent `==0` empty-
         install branch and `as usize` for the buffer
         allocation length.
       - Existing setgroups probes at the gid section
         (size=65537→EINVAL, size=0→ok) still pass:
         65537 > 65536 still EINVAL, 0 still 0.

     Tests added in dispatch_linux:
       - (a) setgroups(0x1_0000_0000, NULL) → 0 (trunc
             to 0, kernel-context empty install).
       - (b) setgroups(0x1_0000_0001, NULL) → 0 (trunc
             to 1, kernel-context bypass returns 0
             before reaching the list copy).
       - (c) setgroups(0x8000_0000, NULL) → EINVAL
             (INT_MIN reinterprets to 0x80000000 >
             NGROUPS_MAX).  Regression guard for the
             int→u32 sign-preserving reinterpret.
       - (d) setgroups(u64::MAX, NULL) → EINVAL (trunc
             to -1, unsigned-reinterpret to 0xFFFFFFFF).
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]   setgroups int
       truncation + unsigned NGROUPS_MAX: OK".

     Preserved behaviour:
       - Real userspace caller (uid==0) with size=2
         and a valid list still installs 2 groups.
       - Real userspace caller (uid!=0) still returns
         EPERM.
       - size=0 still drops all supplementary groups
         on a real PCB caller.

     Known limitations (unchanged):
       - Linux's actual gate order is EPERM
         (may_setgroups) BEFORE the (unsigned)>
         NGROUPS_MAX gate.  We currently do NGROUPS_MAX
         first, then EPERM.  For uid==0 callers and
         kernel context the ordering is invisible; for
         non-root callers with garbage size, we
         return EINVAL where Linux returns EPERM.
         Tracked here as a future gate-order
         alignment item — defer because it requires
         changing the "kernel-context bypass" semantics
         to not skip EPERM, which is a larger surgery
         than this batch's int-truncation scope.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (35.81s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

337. Batch 307 — sys_dup3 missing flags-gate validation
     + int truncation.

     Divergence: Linux's
     `fs/file.c::ksys_dup3(unsigned int oldfd, unsigned int newfd,
                          int flags)`
     gate order:
       1. `(flags & ~O_CLOEXEC) != 0`           -> -EINVAL
       2. `oldfd == newfd`                      -> -EINVAL
       3. `newfd >= rlimit(RLIMIT_NOFILE)`      -> -EBADF
       4. fdget(oldfd) / install at newfd

     Pre-batch we skipped gate 1 entirely: any flag value was
     silently accepted and we used only the O_CLOEXEC bit
     when calling sys_dup2_impl.  Probes:
       - dup3(oldfd=5, newfd=6, flags=0x10): Linux EINVAL via
         flags gate; pre-batch dup2_impl → kernel-context
         EBADF.  **Divergence.**
       - dup3(oldfd=5, newfd=6, flags=0x1_0000_0010): Linux
         truncates int flags to 0x10 → EINVAL; pre-batch
         `as u32` gave 0x10 but no gate → EBADF.
         **Divergence.**

     Also fixes the x86_64 ABI int-truncation thread for
     `flags`: cast args.arg2 to i32 first (matches Linux's
     `int flags` declaration), then reinterpret as u32 for
     the bit-mask comparison.  Bit pattern is preserved
     either way for in-range values; the explicit cast
     ladder documents the ABI step.  Also added explicit
     #[allow] attributes for oldfd/newfd i32 casts (pre-
     batch had silent casts without lint suppressions).

     Fix:
       - Cast args.arg2 → i32 → u32 (int truncation +
         unsigned reinterpret).
       - Add `if flags & !O_CLOEXEC != 0 -> EINVAL` BEFORE
         the existing `oldfd == newfd` check.
       - Pass `flags & O_CLOEXEC != 0` to dup2_impl
         unchanged (cloexec semantics preserved).

     Tests added in dispatch_linux:
       - (a) dup3(5, 6, flags=0x10) → EINVAL (bogus bit
             trips the flags gate).
       - (b) dup3(5, 6, flags=0x1_0000_0010) → EINVAL
             (high-half sentinel + bogus low bit; verifies
             int truncation preserves the bogus 0x10 bit).
       - (c) dup3(5, 6, flags=0x1_0000_0000) → EBADF
             (high-half sentinel only; int truncation
             yields flags=0 → flags gate passes → kernel-
             context EBADF).  Regression guard against an
             over-eager flag check that forgets to mask
             before testing.
       - (d) dup3(5, 5, flags=0x10) → EINVAL (the existing
             dup3(0,0,0) probe at the fd-table block
             confirms zero-flag same-fd → EINVAL; this
             probe additionally locks in that bogus flags
             with same-fd is still EINVAL).
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]   dup3 flags-gate validation
       + int truncation: OK".

     Preserved behaviour:
       - Existing dup3(0,0,0) probe at the dup3 same-fd
         block still returns EINVAL (flags=0 passes new
         gate; oldfd==newfd triggers same-fd EINVAL).
       - Real userspace caller: dup3(5, 6, flags=O_CLOEXEC)
         still successfully duplicates 5 onto 6 with
         FD_CLOEXEC set.
       - dup3(oldfd_invalid, newfd_valid, flags=0) still
         returns EBADF via the dup2_impl path.

     Known limitations (deferred):
       - Linux's gate 3 (`newfd >= rlimit(RLIMIT_NOFILE)
         → EBADF`) runs before fdget(oldfd) in Linux.  We
         delegate to dup2_impl which checks `newfd < 0` →
         EBADF but doesn't surface the RLIMIT_NOFILE
         interlock — for in-range newfd values the dup
         path's own table-capacity check (MAX_FDS) catches
         it as EBADF (same errno).  Tracked alongside
         broader RLIMIT_NOFILE enforcement work.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (38.22s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=240 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 26s).

338. sys_mlockall int truncation — high-half register garbage
     must be stripped before the flags mask check.

     Divergence:
       Linux signature: `int mlockall(int flags)`.  The C
       `int` parameter forces the kernel function body to
       observe only the low 32 bits of the syscall register
       (Linux uses SYSCALL_DEFINE1(mlockall, int, flags),
       which the macro expands to a function taking
       `int flags`).  The high 32 bits of the x86_64 RDI
       register are caller-uninitialised garbage — neither
       glibc nor musl zero them before issuing the syscall
       (for an int argument, only the low 32 bits carry
       semantic meaning).  Linux's `flags & ~MCL_VALID_MASK`
       check therefore operates at 32-bit width.

       Pre-batch we held `flags` as `u64` and ran the mask
       check at 64-bit width:
           let flags = args.arg0;
           if flags == 0 || (flags & !MCL_ALL) != 0 ...
       so a caller passing `0x1_0000_0001` (high-half
       garbage + MCL_CURRENT in the low half) saw EINVAL
       (the high bit trips `!MCL_ALL`) where Linux returns
       0 (high bits are truncated away, low bits =
       MCL_CURRENT, valid).

       The bug surfaces for any caller that relies on the C
       ABI's natural int truncation — glibc's
       sysdeps/unix/syscalls.list entry for mlockall does
       NOT zero-extend before issuing the syscall, so
       random RDI garbage from the prior register state
       (e.g. a recent strncmp return) becomes visible to
       our kernel as ill-formed flags.

     Fix:
       kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_mlockall:
         * Cast args.arg0 to i32 (matching Linux's int
           parameter), then reinterpret as u32 for the
           mask check.  Convert MCL_CURRENT / MCL_FUTURE /
           MCL_ONFAULT / MCL_ALL from u64 to u32 since
           the comparison is now at 32-bit width.
         * Existing flags==0 / mask / ONFAULT-alone gates
           preserved exactly; only the carrier width
           changes.

     Tests (dispatch_linux self-test, after the dup3 block):
       - (a) mlockall(0x1_0000_0001) → 0
             (high-half garbage + MCL_CURRENT; verifies
             int truncation strips the high bits and the
             flags are accepted as valid).
       - (b) mlockall(0x1_0000_0007) → 0
             (high-half garbage + MCL_ALL; verifies the
             full valid mask survives truncation).
       - (c) mlockall(0x1_0000_0000) → EINVAL
             (high-half garbage only, low half zero;
             verifies the flags==0 short-circuit fires at
             32-bit width — pre-batch this was already
             EINVAL but via the wrong gate, post-batch
             it's via the documented flags==0 path).
       - (d) mlockall(0x1_0000_0004) → EINVAL
             (high-half garbage + MCL_ONFAULT alone;
             verifies the ONFAULT-modifier-alone gate from
             batch 250 still fires after truncation).
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]   mlockall int truncation
       (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved behaviour:
       - Existing mlockall(0) → EINVAL probe still passes.
       - Existing mlockall(7) → 0 probe still passes.
       - Existing mlockall(8) → EINVAL (unknown bit) still
         passes.
       - Existing batch-250 ONFAULT-alone probes
         (mlockall(4) → EINVAL, mlockall(5) → 0,
         mlockall(6) → 0) all still pass.
       - munlockall still returns 0 unconditionally.

     Known limitations (deferred):
       - We accept every valid flags value with success(0)
         without actually pinning pages.  The kernel has
         no userspace memory pin tracking yet — mlockall
         is a no-op success, matching how it has been
         since the syscall was first added.  Real lock-
         counting and OOM-killer interaction with mlock'd
         pages are roadmap items, not Linux ABI parity.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (45.10s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 26s)
                — initial 240s attempt timed out at the
                rcu self-test before our marker was
                emitted, but the second run with extra
                headroom completed normally; the mlockall
                marker fires regardless.

339. sys_msync int truncation — high-half register garbage
     must be stripped before the flags mask check.

     Divergence:
       Linux signature: `int msync(void *addr, size_t length,
       int flags)`.  SYSCALL_DEFINE3(msync, unsigned long,
       start, size_t, len, int, flags) narrows the third
       parameter to (int) on entry; the kernel function body
       observes only the low 32 bits of RDX.  The high 32
       bits are caller-uninitialised garbage (neither glibc
       nor musl zero-extend int arguments before issuing the
       syscall — for an int param only the low 32 bits carry
       semantic meaning).

       Pre-batch we held `flags` as `u64` and ran the mask
       check at 64-bit width:
           let flags = args.arg2;
           if (flags & !MS_ALL) != 0 { return EINVAL; }
       so a caller passing `0x1_0000_0001` (high-half
       garbage + MS_ASYNC) saw EINVAL where Linux returns 0
       (after the high half is truncated away the low half
       is MS_ASYNC, mask passes, len=0 → end==addr → 0).

       Same shape as the batch-308 mlockall fix.

     Fix:
       kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_msync:
         * Convert MS_ASYNC / MS_INVALIDATE / MS_SYNC /
           MS_ALL constants from u64 to u32 to match the
           Linux int width.
         * Cast args.arg2 to i32 (matching Linux's int flags
           parameter), then reinterpret as u32 for the mask
           and mutual-exclusion checks.
         * addr (arg0) and len (arg1) remain at u64 — those
           are unsigned long / size_t in Linux's ABI and
           full 64-bit width is correct.

     Tests (dispatch_linux self-test, after the mlockall
     batch-308 block):
       - (a) msync(0, 0, 0x1_0000_0001) → 0
             (high-half garbage + MS_ASYNC; truncates to 1,
             mask passes, len=0 → end==addr → success).
       - (b) msync(0, 0, 0x1_0000_0007) → EINVAL
             (high-half garbage + MS_ALL with SYNC|ASYNC;
             truncates to 7, mask passes, mutual-exclusion
             fires; verifies the mutual-exclusion gate still
             operates on the truncated value).
       - (c) msync(0, 0, 0x1_0000_0000) → 0
             (high-half garbage only, low half zero;
             truncates to 0, flags==0 is accepted, len=0 →
             success).
       - (d) msync(0, 0, 0x1_0000_0010) → EINVAL
             (high-half garbage + unknown low bit 0x10;
             truncates to 0x10, mask gate rejects bogus low
             bits; verifies the mask gate still rejects
             unknown bits after high half is stripped).
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]   msync int truncation
       (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved behaviour:
       - Existing msync(0,0,0) → 0 probe still passes
         (flags==0 path).
       - Existing msync(0,0,2) → 0 probe still passes
         (MS_INVALIDATE alone path).
       - Existing msync(0,0,1|4) → EINVAL still passes
         (SYNC|ASYNC mutual exclusion).
       - Existing msync(0,0,0x10) → EINVAL still passes
         (unknown bit path).
       - Existing msync(0x1234, 4096, 1) → EINVAL still
         passes (misaligned-addr path).
       - Existing wrapping_add overflow semantics for len
         near u64::MAX preserved (carrier width of the
         addition path unchanged; only `flags` was narrowed).

     Known limitations (deferred):
       - We still no-op a successful msync: there is no
         per-VMA fsync_range call because we have no
         file-backed VMA tracking.  Real msync semantics
         (forcing dirty pages out to backing storage) await
         the VMA + page-cache subsystems.  Linux ABI parity
         for the mask/alignment/overflow gates is what's
         covered here.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (44.00s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

340. sys_mlock2 int truncation — high-half register garbage
     must be stripped before the flags mask check.

     Divergence:
       Linux signature: `int mlock2(const void *addr, size_t
       len, int flags)`.  SYSCALL_DEFINE3(mlock2,
       unsigned long, start, size_t, len, int, flags)
       narrows the third parameter to (int) on entry; the
       mask check `flags & ~MLOCK_ONFAULT` runs at 32-bit
       width.  The high 32 bits of RDX are caller-
       uninitialised garbage (the C calling convention for
       int args does not zero-extend before issuing the
       syscall).

       Pre-batch we held `flags` as `u64` and ran the mask
       check at 64-bit width:
           let flags = args.arg2;
           if flags & !1 != 0 { return EINVAL; }
       so a caller passing `0x1_0000_0001` (high-half
       garbage + MLOCK_ONFAULT) saw EINVAL where Linux
       returns 0 (truncates to 1, mask passes).  Third
       instance of the same shape after the batch-308
       mlockall and batch-309 msync fixes.

     Fix:
       kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_mlock2:
         * Cast args.arg2 to i32 (matching Linux's int
           flags parameter), then reinterpret as u32 for
           the mask check.
         * addr (arg0) and len (arg1) remain at u64 —
           those are unsigned long / size_t in Linux's
           ABI and full 64-bit width is correct.

     Tests (dispatch_linux self-test, after the existing
     mlock2 valid probe):
       - (a) mlock2(0x4000, 0x4000, 0x1_0000_0001) → 0
             (high-half garbage + MLOCK_ONFAULT;
             truncates to 1, mask passes, validate_user_read
             bypassed in kernel context, success).
       - (b) mlock2(0x4000, 0, 0x1_0000_0001) → 0
             (high-half garbage + MLOCK_ONFAULT, len=0;
             truncates to 1, mask passes, len=0 short-
             circuit fires before validate_user_read).
       - (c) mlock2(0x4000, 0, 0x1_0000_0000) → 0
             (high-half garbage only, low half zero;
             truncates to 0, flags=0 is valid for mlock2
             which has no required bits, len=0 → 0).
       - (d) mlock2(0x4000, 0, 0x1_0000_0002) → EINVAL
             (high-half garbage + unknown low bit 0x2;
             truncates to 0x2, mask gate rejects bit
             outside MLOCK_ONFAULT; verifies the mask gate
             still rejects bogus low bits after the high
             half is stripped).
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]   mlock2 int truncation
       (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved behaviour:
       - Existing mlock2(0x4000, 0x4000, 2) → EINVAL probe
         still passes (bad low flag with no high bits).
       - Existing mlock2(0x4000, 0, 0) → 0 probe still
         passes (len=0 short-circuit).
       - Existing mlock2(0x4000, 0x4000, 1) → 0 probe
         still passes (valid MLOCK_ONFAULT + non-zero len).

     Known limitations (deferred):
       - We accept successful mlock2 without actually
         pinning pages (no swap subsystem yet — all memory
         is implicitly locked).  Same caveat as mlockall:
         real lock-counting and OOM-killer interaction
         await the broader VM work.
       - The kernel-context validate_user_read bypass
         means probe (a) doesn't actually exercise the
         user-pointer validation surface; the bypass is
         well-established (it lets self-tests address
         "fake" addresses like 0x4000) so this is a
         testing convenience, not an ABI compromise.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (45.61s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

341. sys_fchmodat int truncation — high-half register garbage
     must be stripped before the flags mask check.

     Divergence:
       Linux signature: `int fchmodat(int dirfd, const
       char *pathname, mode_t mode, int flags)`.  The
       fourth parameter is C `int`, so the kernel function
       body observes only the low 32 bits of R10; the high
       32 bits are caller-uninitialised garbage (no zero-
       extension for int args in the AMD64 syscall ABI).

       Pre-batch we held the mask check at 64-bit width:
           const VALID_FLAGS: u64 = 0x100 | 0x1000;
           if args.arg3 & !VALID_FLAGS != 0 {
               return EINVAL;
           }
       so a caller passing `0x1_0000_0100` (high-half
       garbage + AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW) saw EINVAL where
       Linux returns EROFS (after truncation the low half
       is 0x100, mask passes, the call proceeds to the
       readonly-fs gate).  Fourth instance of the same
       shape after the batch-308 mlockall, batch-309
       msync, and batch-310 mlock2 fixes.

       Note: sys_fchmodat2 already does the truncation
       correctly (uses `as i32` then i32 mask) so this
       batch only touches the original fchmodat.

     Fix:
       kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_fchmodat:
         * Cast args.arg3 to i32 (matching Linux's int
           flags parameter), then reinterpret as u32 for
           the mask check.
         * Convert VALID_FLAGS from u64 to u32 since the
           comparison is now at 32-bit width.

     Tests (dispatch_linux self-test, after the existing
     fchmodat EROFS probe):
       - (a) fchmodat(0, 0x1000, 0, 0x1_0000_0100) → EROFS
             (high-half garbage + AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW;
             truncates to 0x100, mask passes,
             validate_user_str succeeds in kernel context
             via validate_user_read bypass, EROFS terminal
             from the read-only fs surface).
       - (b) fchmodat(0, 0x1000, 0, 0x1_0000_1100) → EROFS
             (high-half garbage + NOFOLLOW | EMPTY_PATH;
             both valid bits combined; truncates to 0x1100,
             mask passes, EROFS terminal).
       - (c) fchmodat(0, 0x1000, 0, 0x1_0000_0000) → EROFS
             (high-half garbage only, low half zero;
             truncates to 0, flags=0 is valid (no required
             bits for fchmodat), EROFS terminal).
       - (d) fchmodat(0, 0x1000, 0, 0x1_0000_0010) → EINVAL
             (high-half garbage + unknown low bit 0x10;
             truncates to 0x10, mask check rejects bit
             outside VALID_FLAGS; verifies the mask gate
             still rejects bogus low bits after high half
             is stripped).
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]   fchmodat int truncation
       (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved behaviour:
       - Existing fchmodat(_,path,_,0x800_0000) → EINVAL
         probe still passes (huge bogus low bit, no high
         bits — same EINVAL gate fires post-batch).
       - Existing fchmodat(_,path,_,0) → EROFS probe still
         passes (flags=0 with no high bits).
       - sys_fchmodat2 unchanged (already correct).

     Known limitations (deferred):
       - sys_fchownat has the identical mask-check shape
         (`args.arg4 & !(0x100|0x1000)` at u64 width).
         Tracked for a follow-up batch — keeping batches
         tight means landing one syscall at a time even
         when the fix is mechanical.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (40.02s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

342. sys_fchownat int truncation — high-half register garbage
     must be stripped before the flags mask check.

     Divergence:
       Linux signature: `int fchownat(int dirfd, const
       char *pathname, uid_t owner, gid_t group, int
       flags)`.  SYSCALL_DEFINE5 narrows the fifth
       parameter to (int) on entry; the mask check
       `flags & ~(AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW|AT_EMPTY_PATH)` runs
       at 32-bit width.

       Pre-batch we held the mask check at 64-bit width
       (identical shape to batch-311 fchmodat):
           const VALID_FLAGS: u64 = 0x100 | 0x1000;
           if args.arg4 & !VALID_FLAGS != 0 {
               return EINVAL;
           }
           if args.arg4 & 0x1000 != 0 { ... EMPTY_PATH ... }
       so a caller passing `0x1_0000_0100` (high-half
       garbage + AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW) saw EINVAL where
       Linux returns EROFS.  Fifth instance of the same
       shape after batches 308 (mlockall), 309 (msync),
       310 (mlock2), and 311 (fchmodat).

       Note: the AT_EMPTY_PATH branch test also used
       `args.arg4 & 0x1000` at u64 width; replacing with
       the truncated u32 `flags` ensures the EMPTY_PATH
       branch fires for callers whose high-half garbage
       coincidentally clears bit 0x1000 — pre-batch this
       branch could spuriously NOT fire when high bits
       were present (less common; the EINVAL gate fired
       first for any non-empty high half).

     Fix:
       kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_fchownat:
         * Cast args.arg4 to i32 (matching Linux's int
           flags parameter), then reinterpret as u32 for
           the mask and EMPTY_PATH-branch checks.
         * Convert VALID_FLAGS from u64 to u32.
         * Replace `args.arg4 & 0x1000` with `flags &
           0x1000` so the EMPTY_PATH branch dispatch is
           consistent with the mask gate's truncated view.

     Tests (dispatch_linux self-test, after the existing
     fchownat EROFS probe):
       - (a) fchownat(0, 0x1000, 0, 0, 0x1_0000_0100) →
             EROFS (high|NOFOLLOW; truncates to 0x100, mask
             passes, no EMPTY_PATH branch fires, EROFS
             terminal via validate_user_str + read-only
             surface).
       - (b) fchownat(0, 0x1000, 0, 0, 0x1_0000_1100) →
             EROFS (high|NOFOLLOW|EMPTY_PATH; truncates to
             0x1100, mask passes, EMPTY_PATH branch fires,
             dirfd=0 in kernel context → caller_pid()=None
             → EROFS via the AT_EMPTY_PATH branch's
             kernel-context fallback).
       - (c) fchownat(0, 0x1000, 0, 0, 0x1_0000_0000) →
             EROFS (high-half only, low half zero;
             truncates to 0, flags=0, no branches, EROFS
             via validate_user_str path).
       - (d) fchownat(0, 0x1000, 0, 0, 0x1_0000_0010) →
             EINVAL (high|bad-low 0x10; truncates to 0x10,
             mask rejects bit outside VALID_FLAGS).
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]   fchownat int truncation
       (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved behaviour:
       - Existing fchownat(_,path,_,_,0x800_0000) → EINVAL
         probe still passes (huge bogus low bit, no high
         bits — same EINVAL gate post-batch).
       - Existing fchownat(_,path,_,_,0) → EROFS probe
         still passes.

     Known limitations (deferred):
       - sys_chown / sys_lchown have no flags parameter,
         so they have no equivalent truncation surface.
         The fchown/lchown surface in linux.rs already
         casts fd to i32.
       - Remaining unaudited `*at` syscalls with int flags
         that follow this pattern: sys_linkat (existing
         AT_SYMLINK_FOLLOW), sys_renameat2 (existing
         RENAME_* bits), sys_unlinkat (existing AT_REMOVEDIR),
         sys_utimensat (existing AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW),
         sys_statx (existing AT_NO_AUTOMOUNT etc.).  Each
         is a candidate for future int-truncation batches.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.62s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 26s).

343. sys_unlinkat int truncation — high-half register garbage
     must be stripped before the flags mask check.

     Divergence:
       Linux signature: `int unlinkat(int dirfd, const
       char *pathname, int flags)`.  SYSCALL_DEFINE3 narrows
       the third parameter to (int) on entry; the mask
       check `flags & ~AT_REMOVEDIR` runs at 32-bit width.

       Pre-batch we held the mask check at 64-bit width:
           const AT_REMOVEDIR: u64 = 0x200;
           const VALID_FLAGS: u64 = AT_REMOVEDIR;
           if args.arg2 & !VALID_FLAGS != 0 {
               return EINVAL;
           }
       so a caller passing `0x1_0000_0200` (high-half
       garbage + AT_REMOVEDIR) saw EINVAL where Linux
       returns ENOENT (after truncation the low half is
       0x200, mask passes, the call proceeds to the no-
       such-file gate).  Sixth instance of the same shape
       after batches 308 (mlockall), 309 (msync), 310
       (mlock2), 311 (fchmodat), and 312 (fchownat).

     Fix:
       kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_unlinkat:
         * Cast args.arg2 to i32 (matching Linux's int
           flags parameter), then reinterpret as u32 for
           the mask check.
         * Convert AT_REMOVEDIR / VALID_FLAGS from u64 to
           u32.

     Tests (dispatch_linux self-test, after the existing
     unlinkat ENOENT probe):
       - (a) unlinkat(0, 0x1000, 0x1_0000_0200) → ENOENT
             (high|AT_REMOVEDIR; truncates to 0x200, mask
             passes, validate_user_str succeeds in kernel
             context, ENOENT terminal).
       - (b) unlinkat(0, 0x1000, 0x1_0000_0000) → ENOENT
             (high-half only, low half zero; truncates to
             0, mask passes, ENOENT terminal).
       - (c) unlinkat(0, 0, 0x1_0000_0200) → EFAULT
             (high|AT_REMOVEDIR with NULL path; truncates
             to 0x200, mask passes, validate_user_str
             returns InvalidAddress for ptr=0 → EFAULT.
             Critically locks in the EFAULT-after-mask
             gate order: pre-batch this returned EINVAL
             because the 64-bit mask rejected the high bit
             before the path was examined; post-batch the
             NULL-path EFAULT gate fires as Linux's path-
             lookup gate would).
       - (d) unlinkat(0, 0x1000, 0x1_0000_0010) → EINVAL
             (high|bad-low 0x10; truncates to 0x10, mask
             rejects bit outside AT_REMOVEDIR; verifies
             mask gate still rejects bogus low bits after
             high half is stripped).
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]   unlinkat int truncation
       (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved behaviour:
       - Existing unlinkat(_,path,0x800_0000) → EINVAL
         probe still passes (huge bogus low bit, no high
         bits).
       - Existing unlinkat(_,NULL,0) → EFAULT still passes.
       - Existing unlinkat(_,0x1000,AT_REMOVEDIR) → ENOENT
         still passes.

     Known limitations (deferred):
       - sys_renameat2 (RENAME_NOREPLACE|EXCHANGE|WHITEOUT
         u64 mask) has the same shape; tracked as a
         follow-up batch.
       - sys_linkat (AT_SYMLINK_FOLLOW|AT_EMPTY_PATH u64
         mask) has the same shape; tracked.
       - sys_utimensat (AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW); tracked.
       - sys_statx (full AT_STATX_* mask) has a richer
         flags surface and may need bespoke handling.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (44.27s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

344. sys_renameat2 unsigned-int truncation — high-half register
     garbage must be stripped before the flags mask check.

     Divergence:
       Linux signature: `int renameat2(int olddirfd, const
       char *oldpath, int newdirfd, const char *newpath,
       unsigned int flags)`.  SYSCALL_DEFINE5 narrows the
       fifth parameter to (unsigned int) on entry; the mask
       check `flags & ~(RENAME_NOREPLACE|EXCHANGE|
       WHITEOUT)` runs at 32-bit width.  The C calling
       convention does not zero-extend unsigned int args
       any more than int args, so the high 32 bits of R8
       are caller-uninitialised garbage.

       Pre-batch we held the mask check at 64-bit width:
           const VALID_FLAGS: u64 = 1 | 2 | 4;
           if args.arg4 & !VALID_FLAGS != 0 {
               return EINVAL;
           }
       so a caller passing `0x1_0000_0001` (high-half
       garbage + RENAME_NOREPLACE) saw EINVAL where Linux
       returns ENOENT (after truncation the low half is 1,
       mask passes, rename_impl validates pointers and
       returns ENOENT for our empty FS).  Seventh instance
       of the same shape after batches 308 (mlockall), 309
       (msync), 310 (mlock2), 311 (fchmodat), 312
       (fchownat), and 313 (unlinkat).

     Fix:
       kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_renameat2:
         * Cast args.arg4 to u32 (matching Linux's unsigned
           int flags parameter).
         * Convert VALID_FLAGS from u64 to u32.

     Tests (dispatch_linux self-test, after the existing
     renameat2 ENOENT probe):
       - (a) renameat2(0, 0x1000, 0, 0x2000, 0x1_0000_0001)
             → ENOENT (high|RENAME_NOREPLACE; truncates to
             1, mask passes, rename_impl returns ENOENT).
       - (b) renameat2(0, 0x1000, 0, 0x2000, 0x1_0000_0007)
             → ENOENT (high|all-three-valid-bits;
             truncates to 7; locks in truncation behaviour
             — note this combination is NOT mutually-
             exclusive-valid per Linux, see Limitations).
       - (c) renameat2(0, 0x1000, 0, 0x2000, 0x1_0000_0000)
