             → ENOENT (high-half only, low half zero;
             truncates to 0 → equivalent to plain renameat
             behaviour).
       - (d) renameat2(0, 0x1000, 0, 0x2000, 0x1_0000_0008)
             → EINVAL (high|bad-low 0x8; truncates to 0x8,
             mask rejects bit outside valid set).
       Marker: "[syscall/linux]   renameat2 unsigned-int
       truncation (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved behaviour:
       - Existing renameat2(_,_,_,_,0x800_0000) → EINVAL
         probe still passes (bogus low bit, no high bits).
       - Existing renameat2(_,_,_,_,RENAME_NOREPLACE) →
         ENOENT probe still passes.

     Known limitations (deferred):
       - Linux's mutual-exclusion gate:
           if ((flags & (RENAME_NOREPLACE | RENAME_WHITEOUT))
               && (flags & RENAME_EXCHANGE))
               return -EINVAL;
         is NOT enforced by our stub.  Pre-batch and post-
         batch we accept e.g. RENAME_NOREPLACE|EXCHANGE and
         return ENOENT; Linux returns EINVAL.  This is a
         separate divergence from the int-truncation fix
         and should be addressed in a future batch
         dedicated to renameat2's mutual-exclusion gates.
         (Real callers like libuv/Tokio/golang runtime
         typically don't combine these flags, but
         filesystem fuzzers do.)

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (59.23s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

345. sys_linkat int truncation — high-half register garbage
     leaked into the AT_SYMLINK_FOLLOW|AT_EMPTY_PATH mask
     check (batch 315).

     Divergence: Linux signature is
       `int linkat(int olddirfd, const char *oldpath,
                   int newdirfd, const char *newpath,
                   int flags)`.
     The fifth parameter is C int → only the low 32 bits
     are defined; the AMD64 syscall ABI does NOT zero-extend
     int args before issuing the syscall, so the high 32
     bits of %r8 are caller-uninitialised garbage.
     SYSCALL_DEFINE5(linkat, int, olddfd, ..., int, flags)
     narrows to (int) on entry; the mask check
     `flags & ~(AT_SYMLINK_FOLLOW | AT_EMPTY_PATH)` in
     fs/namei.c::do_linkat runs at 32-bit width.

     Pre-batch we held flags as u64 and ran the mask check
     against the full 64-bit register, so a caller with
     non-zero high bits saw spurious EINVAL where Linux
     accepts the call and proceeds to EROFS (no writable
     FS in kernel context after validate_user_read
     succeeds for non-NULL paths).

     Eighth instance of the *at int-flags truncation
     pattern after batches 308 (mlockall), 309 (msync),
     310 (mlock2), 311 (fchmodat), 312 (fchownat), 313
     (unlinkat), and 314 (renameat2).

     Fix: cast args.arg4 (u64) to i32, then re-widen to
     u32 for the mask check.  Matches the i32→u32 pattern
     used in batches 308, 311, 312, 313 (all flags=int).
     renameat2 used direct u32 cast because its flags is
     unsigned int; linkat is signed int so it goes through
     i32 first to mirror exactly what (int) narrowing does
     in SYSCALL_DEFINE5.

     Tests: four boot-time probes added in dispatch_linux
     after the existing LINKAT EINVAL/EROFS probes, each
     covering one shape:
       (a) linkat(0, 0x1000, 0, 0x2000, 0x1_0000_0400)
           → EROFS (high|AT_SYMLINK_FOLLOW; truncates to
            0x400, mask passes, paths validated, EROFS
            terminal in kernel context).
       (b) linkat(0, 0x1000, 0, 0x2000, 0x1_0000_1400)
           → EROFS (high|AT_SYMLINK_FOLLOW|AT_EMPTY_PATH;
            truncates to 0x1400, mask passes, EROFS).
       (c) linkat(0, 0x1000, 0, 0x2000, 0x1_0000_0000)
           → EROFS (high-half only, low half zero;
            truncates to 0, mask passes, EROFS).
       (d) linkat(0, 0x1000, 0, 0x2000, 0x1_0000_0010)
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 0x10; truncates to 0x10,
            mask rejects bit outside the valid set;
            verifies mask gate still rejects invalid bits
            after the high half is stripped).
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   linkat int truncation
              (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing linkat(bad flag) EINVAL and
     linkat(valid AT_SYMLINK_FOLLOW) EROFS probes still
     fire — the 64-bit mask check `& !VALID_FLAGS != 0`
     and the 32-bit mask check `& !VALID_FLAGS != 0`
     return identical results when args.arg4 fits in
     32 bits, which is the case in those probes
     (0x800_0000 and 0x400 respectively).

     Known limitations:
     - sys_linkat in our kernel returns EROFS uncondi-
       tionally after pointer validation because we have
       no writable FS.  Real Linux runs ten more gates
       between mask check and EROFS (do_filp_open on
       oldpath, follow_or_not_to_follow logic on
       AT_SYMLINK_FOLLOW vs symlink target, ESTALE
       handling, vfs_link permission checks, target
       directory validation, EXDEV cross-device check,
       ELOOP for symlink chains, ENAMETOOLONG, EACCES on
       restricted dirs, and ENOSPC).  Our EROFS short-
       circuit is correct for the empty-FS case but does
       not exercise those gates.  When we land a writable
       FS the linkat self-tests will need to grow probes
       for each terminal errno.
     - AT_EMPTY_PATH (0x1000) requires olddirfd to be an
       open fd referring to the source file (Linux's
       hardlinks-to-fd feature, CAP_DAC_READ_SEARCH or
       O_PATH).  In kernel context we can't install a
       real fd so the (b) probe with AT_EMPTY_PATH only
       exercises the mask gate, not the
       resolve-from-fd path.  When fd installation lands
       a follow-up test should cover the AT_EMPTY_PATH
       semantics end-to-end.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (47.67s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

346. sys_utimensat int truncation — high-half register
     garbage leaked into the AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW mask
     check at gate 3 (batch 316).

     Divergence: Linux signature is
       `int utimensat(int dirfd, const char *pathname,
                      const struct timespec times[2],
                      int flags)`.
     The fourth parameter is C int → only the low 32 bits
     are defined; the AMD64 syscall ABI does NOT zero-
     extend int args before issuing the syscall, so the
     high 32 bits of %r10 are caller-uninitialised garbage.
     SYSCALL_DEFINE4(utimensat, ..., int, flags) narrows
     to (int) on entry; the mask check
     `flags & ~AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW` in fs/utimes.c::
     do_utimes runs at 32-bit width.

     Pre-batch we held flags as u64 and ran the mask check
     against the full 64-bit register, so a caller with
     non-zero high bits saw spurious EINVAL where Linux
     either reaches the EROFS terminal (with valid times
     and a known-good path) or returns 0 via the gate-2
     (OMIT, OMIT) short-circuit (whose gate runs BEFORE
     the flag check, so high-half garbage in arg3 with
     valid OMIT-OMIT times must still return 0 even when
     the truncated flag would also pass the mask).

     Ninth instance of the *at int-flags truncation
     pattern after batches 308 (mlockall), 309 (msync),
     310 (mlock2), 311 (fchmodat), 312 (fchownat), 313
     (unlinkat), 314 (renameat2), and 315 (linkat).

     Fix: cast args.arg3 (u64) to i32, then re-widen to
     u32 for the mask check at gate 3.  Matches the
     i32→u32 pattern used in batches 308, 311, 312, 313,
     315 (all flags=int).  Also changed VALID_FLAGS const
     from u64 to u32 (no behavioural difference; the mask
     is the same value 0x100 either way).

     Tests: four boot-time probes added in dispatch_linux
     after the existing utimensat OMIT-shortcut/nsec_valid
     discriminator probes, each covering one shape:
       (a) utimensat(0, NULL, NULL, 0x1_0000_0100) → EROFS
           (high|AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW; truncates to 0x100,
            mask passes, NULL path is legal, EROFS at
            gate 5).
       (b) utimensat(0, NULL, &(OMIT,OMIT), 0x1_0000_0100)
           → 0 (high|AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW with OMIT-OMIT
            times; gate 2 short-circuits before the flag
            check.  This locks in that the OMIT-shortcut
            still fires even when the flag has high-half
            garbage — important because the gate ordering
            puts gate 2 BEFORE the flag mask, so the
            truncation only matters for shapes where
            gate 2 doesn't fire).
       (c) utimensat(0, NULL, NULL, 0x1_0000_0000) → EROFS
           (high-half only, low half zero; truncates to 0,
            mask passes, EROFS terminal).
       (d) utimensat(0, NULL, NULL, 0x1_0000_0010) → EINVAL
           (high|bad-low 0x10; truncates to 0x10, mask
            rejects bit outside AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW;
            verifies mask gate still rejects invalid bits
            after the high half is stripped).
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   utimensat int truncation
              (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing utimensat probes all still pass:
       - bogus flag (0x800_0000) → EINVAL: works because
         0x800_0000 fits in 32 bits, so the i32 cast is
         identity and the mask still rejects it.
       - (_,NULL,NULL,0) → EROFS: works because 0 is 0
         in either width.
       - OMIT-shortcut discriminator A (bogus flag +
         bogus path + OMIT-OMIT) → 0: works because
         gate 2 fires before the flag check.
       - nsec_valid discriminator B (NSEC_PER_SEC) →
         EINVAL: works because gate 4 fires after the
         (now-truncated) gate-3 flag check passes.

     Known limitations:
     - utimensat's full Linux behaviour requires a
       writable FS to mutate atime/mtime.  Our EROFS
       terminal is correct for the empty-FS case but
       does not exercise mnt_want_write/vfs_utimes
       permission checks (need_capability(CAP_FOWNER)
       for changing times on files not owned by caller;
       EACCES when the inode's flags include S_IMMUTABLE
       or S_APPEND).  Follow-up needed when writable FS
       lands.
     - args.arg3 used in the flag check; args.arg2
       (times[2] pointer) and args.arg1 (path pointer)
       are correctly u64 (pointers, not int).  No
       truncation concerns there.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (45.06s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

347. sys_umount2 int truncation — high-half register garbage
     leaked into the MNT_FORCE|MNT_DETACH|MNT_EXPIRE|
     UMOUNT_NOFOLLOW mask check (batch 317).

     Divergence: Linux signature is
       `int umount2(const char *target, int flags)`.
     The second parameter is C int → only the low 32 bits
     are defined; the AMD64 syscall ABI does NOT zero-
     extend int args before issuing the syscall, so the
     high 32 bits of %rsi are caller-uninitialised garbage.
     SYSCALL_DEFINE2(umount, ..., int, flags) narrows to
     (int) on entry; the mask check
     `flags & ~(MNT_FORCE|MNT_DETACH|MNT_EXPIRE|
                UMOUNT_NOFOLLOW)` in fs/namespace.c::
     SYSCALL_DEFINE2(umount) runs at 32-bit width before
     the CAP_SYS_ADMIN check.

     Pre-batch we held flags as u64 and ran the mask check
     against the full 64-bit register, so a caller with
     non-zero high bits saw spurious EINVAL where Linux
     accepts the flag value and proceeds to the EPERM
     terminal (no CAP_SYS_ADMIN in kernel context after
     path validation).

     Tenth instance of the int-flags truncation pattern
     after batches 308 (mlockall), 309 (msync), 310
     (mlock2), 311 (fchmodat), 312 (fchownat), 313
     (unlinkat), 314 (renameat2), 315 (linkat), and 316
     (utimensat).

     Fix: cast args.arg1 (u64) to i32, then re-widen to
     u32 for the mask check.  Matches the i32→u32 pattern
     used in batches 308, 311, 312, 313, 315, 316.

     Tests: four boot-time probes added in dispatch_linux
     after the existing UMOUNT2 EPERM probe, each
     covering one shape:
       (a) umount2(0x1000, 0x1_0000_0001) → EPERM
           (high|MNT_FORCE; truncates to 1, mask passes,
            path validated, EPERM terminal in kernel
            context).
       (b) umount2(0x1000, 0x1_0000_000F) → EPERM
           (high|all-valid 0xF; truncates to 0xF, mask
            passes, EPERM).
       (c) umount2(0x1000, 0x1_0000_0000) → EPERM
           (high-half only, low half zero; truncates to 0,
            mask passes, EPERM).
       (d) umount2(0x1000, 0x1_0000_0010) → EINVAL
           (high|bad-low 0x10; truncates to 0x10, mask
            rejects bit outside the valid set; verifies
            mask gate still rejects invalid bits after
            the high half is stripped).
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   umount2 int truncation
              (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing umount2 probes still pass:
       - umount2(0x1000, 0x8000_0000) → EINVAL: works
         because 0x8000_0000 has bit 31 set, which after
         i32 cast becomes negative-i32, and (negative as
         u32) keeps bit 31 set → mask rejects (0x8000_0000
         & !(MNT_FORCE|...|UMOUNT_NOFOLLOW) != 0).  The
         truncation doesn't change this outcome.
       - umount2(0x1000, 0) → EPERM: works because 0 is 0
         in either width.

     Known limitations:
     - Real Linux umount2 runs many gates between mask
       check and the FS hit: lookup_mnt(target), per-mount
       MNT_LOCKED check, mnt namespace check, MNT_EXPIRE
       requires zero refcount, MNT_FORCE only for FUSE
       filesystems by current uid, MNT_DETACH lazy-unmount
       semantics.  Our EPERM short-circuit is correct for
       the no-namespace/no-capability case but does not
       exercise those gates.  When CAP_SYS_ADMIN
       enforcement and a writable VFS land, the umount2
       self-tests will need to grow probes for each
       terminal errno.
     - Selected umount2 over splice/tee/vmsplice (which
       share the SPLICE_F_* mask 1|2|4|8) because all
       three of those return EINVAL at the terminal AND at
       the mask gate, so the truncation fix is correct
       but cannot be observed via a boot probe.  Will
       revisit those when fd-installation infrastructure
       lets us reach the success path.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (37.50s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 26s).

348. sys_memfd_secret unsigned-int truncation — high-half
     register garbage leaked into the O_CLOEXEC mask check
     (batch 318).

     Divergence: Linux signature is
       `int memfd_secret(unsigned int flags)`.
     The sole parameter is C unsigned int → only the low
     32 bits are defined; the AMD64 syscall ABI does NOT
     zero-extend int args before issuing the syscall, so
     the high 32 bits of %rdi are caller-uninitialised
     garbage.  SYSCALL_DEFINE1(memfd_secret, unsigned int,
     flags) narrows to (unsigned int) on entry; the mask
     check `flags & ~FD_CLOEXEC` in mm/secretmem.c runs at
     32-bit width.

     Pre-batch we held flags as u64 and ran the mask check
     against the full 64-bit register, so a caller with
     non-zero high bits saw spurious EINVAL where Linux
     returns ENOSYS in our kernel (we don't implement
     secretmem at all — terminal ENOSYS after the mask
     gate is the truthful answer).

     Eleventh instance of the int/unsigned-int truncation
     pattern after batches 308-317.  Also reconfirmed
     that sys_statx (already truncated since batch 225)
     and sys_unshare (kernel signature is unsigned long,
     not int, so no truncation needed) are NOT candidates
     for this audit pass.

     Pivot from initial batch-318 candidate sys_unshare:
     verified that Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE1(unshare,
     unsigned long, unshare_flags) uses unsigned long for
     the flags parameter (not int as the userspace
     glibc/man page declaration suggests), so the high
     32 bits are NOT caller-uninitialised — they're
     meaningfully zero by the userspace caller widening
     int→long via the C calling convention.  No
     truncation divergence in unshare.

     Fix: cast args.arg0 (u64) to u32 directly (matches
     batch 314's renameat2 pattern for unsigned int —
     no i32 intermediate needed for unsigned types).

     Tests: four boot-time probes added in dispatch_linux
     after the existing MEMFD_SECRET ENOSYS probe, each
     covering one shape:
       (a) memfd_secret(0x1_0008_0000) → ENOSYS
           (high|O_CLOEXEC; truncates to 0o2_000_000 =
            0x8_0000, mask passes, ENOSYS terminal).
       (b) memfd_secret(0x1_0000_0000) → ENOSYS
           (high-half only, low half zero; truncates to 0,
            mask passes, ENOSYS).
       (c) memfd_secret(0x1_0000_0001) → EINVAL
           (high|bad-low 0x1; truncates to 0x1, mask
            rejects bit outside O_CLOEXEC; verifies mask
            gate still rejects invalid bits after the
            high half is stripped).
       (d) memfd_secret(0x0008_0000) → ENOSYS
           (plain O_CLOEXEC with no high half; control
            case to ensure the valid flag still flows
            past the mask post-batch).
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   memfd_secret unsigned-int
              truncation (high-half ignored): OK".

     Build attempt 1 failed: first cut of probe (a) used
     0x1_0020_0000 with the comment claiming O_CLOEXEC =
     0x20_0000.  Recomputed: 0o2_000_000 octal = 2 *
     8^6 = 524288 = 0x80000.  Rewrote (a) to
     0x1_0008_0000 and (d) to 0x0008_0000.

     Preserved: existing memfd_secret(0) → ENOSYS probe
     still passes: 0 truncates to 0, mask passes, ENOSYS.

     Known limitations:
     - We never plan to implement secretmem (Linux's
       secretmem_file_operations require a writable backing
       memory region that the kernel removes from the
       direct map and the page cache; this is fundamentally
       at odds with our shared-page IPC model).  ENOSYS
       is the permanent terminal — no follow-up probes
       needed when more infrastructure lands.
     - The probe set covers flag truncation but does not
       exercise the `prctl(PR_SET_DUMPABLE, 0)` privilege
       gate that Linux runs after the mask check (Linux's
       memfd_secret refuses if the caller has set
       PR_SET_DUMPABLE=0 because secretmem pages must
       survive a fork-with-dump).  Our ENOSYS terminal
       fires before that gate, which is correct because
       the feature is unsupported.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (37.68s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

349. sys_timerfd_settime int truncation — high-half register
     garbage leaked into the combined gate-2 flag+itimerspec
     EINVAL check (batch 319).

     Divergence: Linux signature is
       `int timerfd_settime(int fd, int flags,
                            const struct itimerspec *new_value,
                            struct itimerspec *old_value)`.
     The second parameter is C int → only the low 32 bits are
     defined; the AMD64 syscall ABI does NOT zero-extend int
     args before issuing the syscall, so the high 32 bits of
     %rsi are caller-uninitialised garbage.  SYSCALL_DEFINE4
     (timerfd_settime, int, ufd, int, flags, ...) narrows to
     (int) on entry; do_timerfd_settime's combined gate-2
     check `(flags & ~TFD_SETTIME_FLAGS) ||
     !itimerspec64_valid(new)` runs at 32-bit width.

     Pre-batch we held flags as u64 and ran the mask check
     against the full 64-bit register, so a caller with
     non-zero high bits saw spurious EINVAL where Linux either
     returns EBADF (in kernel context after read+itimerspec
     gate passes with no real fd) or progresses to the (no-op)
     timer-set step on a real timerfd.

     Twelfth instance of the int-flags truncation pattern
     after batches 308 (mlockall), 309 (msync), 310 (mlock2),
     311 (fchmodat), 312 (fchownat), 313 (unlinkat), 314
     (renameat2), 315 (linkat), 316 (utimensat), 317
     (umount2), and 318 (memfd_secret).

     Fix: cast args.arg1 (u64) to i32, then re-widen to u32
     for the mask check.  Same pattern as batches 308, 311,
     312, 313, 315-317.  Also changed VALID_FLAGS const from
     u64 to u32 (no behavioural difference; mask 1|2=0x3 fits
     either width).

     Tests: four boot-time probes added in dispatch_linux
     after the existing TIMERFD_SETTIME gate-order markers,
     each covering one shape (all four use a valid all-zero
     itimerspec so the combined gate's timespec check is
     trivially satisfied):
       (a) timerfd_settime(0, 0x1_0000_0001, &valid, NULL)
           → EBADF (high|TFD_TIMER_ABSTIME; truncates to 1,
            mask passes, itimerspec valid, EBADF at gate 3
            in kernel context).
       (b) timerfd_settime(0, 0x1_0000_0003, &valid, NULL)
           → EBADF (high|all-valid 0x3 =
            ABSTIME|CANCEL_ON_SET; truncates to 3, mask
            passes, EBADF).
       (c) timerfd_settime(0, 0x1_0000_0000, &valid, NULL)
           → EBADF (high-half only, low half zero; truncates
            to 0, mask passes, EBADF).
       (d) timerfd_settime(0, 0x1_0000_0004, &valid, NULL)
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 0x4; truncates to 0x4,
            mask rejects bit outside ABSTIME|CANCEL_ON_SET;
            verifies mask gate still rejects invalid bits
            after the high half is stripped via the
            combined-OR semantics).
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   timerfd_settime int truncation
              (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing timerfd_settime probes still pass:
       - (bad flag 0x8000_0000, valid new) → EINVAL: bit 31
         survives the i32→u32 round-trip (i32::MIN cast back
         to u32 is still 0x8000_0000), so the mask still
         rejects.
       - (bad flag, NULL new) → EFAULT: NULL gate fires before
         the truncation matters (gate 1, ahead of gate 2).
       - (0, 0, NULL new) → EFAULT: same — gate 1 fires first.
       - Various itimerspec-validity probes (bad nsec, neg
         sec, boundary nsec=1e9, just-below 999_999_999) all
         still return EINVAL/EBADF as before; truncation
         doesn't affect the timespec path.
       - (valid new, valid flags, bad fd, bad otmr) → EBADF:
         gate-5 ordering preserved; truncation in gate 2 ran
         and passed.

     Known limitations:
     - The probes drive every fall-through to EBADF rather
       than to the "timer set" success path, because in kernel
       context there is no real timerfd to operate on.  When
       we land a real timerfd_create + fd-installation
       infrastructure, the int-truncation behaviour should
       also be verified end-to-end (valid flags + valid new
       + real fd + valid otmr → 0).
     - args.arg0 (fd) is already i32-truncated at line 11580
       — no separate fix needed for the fd parameter, just
       the flags.
     - args.arg2 (new_value) and args.arg3 (old_value) are
       pointers (correctly u64); no truncation concerns.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (43.80s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

350. sys_landlock_restrict_self unsigned-int truncation —
     high-half register garbage leaked into the
     LANDLOCK_RESTRICT_SELF_FLAGS mask check (batch 320).

     Divergence: Linux signature is
       `int landlock_restrict_self(int ruleset_fd,
                                   __u32 flags)`.
     The second parameter is C __u32 → only the low 32
     bits are defined; the AMD64 syscall ABI does NOT
     zero-extend int args before issuing the syscall, so
     the high 32 bits of %rsi are caller-uninitialised
     garbage.  SYSCALL_DEFINE2(landlock_restrict_self,
     ..., __u32, flags) narrows to (__u32) on entry; the
     mask check `flags & ~LANDLOCK_RESTRICT_SELF_FLAGS`
     in security/landlock/syscalls.c runs at 32-bit width.

     Pre-batch we held flags as u64 and ran the mask
     check against the full 64-bit register, so a caller
     with non-zero high bits saw spurious EINVAL where
     Linux returns EBADF (no landlock ruleset fd exists
     in our kernel — we have no landlock infrastructure
     at all, so EBADF is the permanent terminal).

     Thirteenth instance of the int/unsigned-int
     truncation pattern after batches 308 (mlockall),
     309 (msync), 310 (mlock2), 311 (fchmodat), 312
     (fchownat), 313 (unlinkat), 314 (renameat2), 315
     (linkat), 316 (utimensat), 317 (umount2), 318
     (memfd_secret), and 319 (timerfd_settime).

     Pivot from initial batch-320 candidate sys_swapon:
     verified that sys_swapon was already truncated in
     batch 301 (see lines 14293-14296: `swap_flags_i32 =
     args.arg1 as i32; swap_flags: u64 = u64::from(
     swap_flags_i32 as u32)`).  No further work needed
     there.

     Fix: cast args.arg1 (u64) to u32 directly (matches
     batches 314 and 318 patterns for unsigned int — no
     i32 intermediate needed for unsigned types).

     Tests: four boot-time probes added in dispatch_linux
     after the existing landlock_restrict_self log flags
     marker, each covering one shape:
       (a) landlock_restrict_self(0, 0x1_0000_0001)
           → EBADF (high|LOG_SAME_EXEC_OFF; truncates
            to 1, mask passes, fd check passes in kernel
            context, EBADF terminal).
       (b) landlock_restrict_self(0, 0x1_0000_0007)
           → EBADF (high|all-valid 0x7 = SAME_EXEC_OFF|
            NEW_EXEC_ON|SUBDOMAINS_OFF; truncates to 7,
            mask passes, EBADF).
       (c) landlock_restrict_self(0, 0x1_0000_0000)
           → EBADF (high-half only, low half zero;
            truncates to 0, mask passes, EBADF).
       (d) landlock_restrict_self(0, 0x1_0000_0008)
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 0x8; truncates to 0x8,
            mask rejects bit outside the valid set;
            verifies mask gate still rejects invalid
            bits after the high half is stripped).
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   landlock_restrict_self
              unsigned-int truncation (high-half
              ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing landlock_restrict_self probes
     still pass:
       - undef flag 0x8 → EINVAL: works because 0x8 fits
         in 32 bits, the u32 cast is identity, mask still
         rejects.
       - valid (flags=0) → EBADF: works because 0 is 0
         in either width.
       - LOG_SAME_EXEC_OFF=0x1, LOG_NEW_EXEC_ON=0x2,
         LOG_SUBDOMAINS_OFF=0x4, all three (0x7) → EBADF:
         all low-32-bit values, behave identically post-
         truncation.
       - high bit (0x101) → EINVAL: works because 0x101
         truncates to itself (already in low 32 bits),
         and 0x100 is outside the valid set.

     Known limitations:
     - Landlock is fundamentally incompatible with our
       capability-based security model (we have no
       per-thread/per-process LSM hook infrastructure and
       no plan to add one — capabilities replace landlock
       in our architecture).  EBADF is the permanent
       terminal after the mask gate — no follow-up probes
       needed when more infrastructure lands.
     - args.arg0 (ruleset_fd) is already i32-truncated at
       the cast in line 17950 — no separate fix needed
       for the fd parameter.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (51.01s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

351. Batch 321 — sys_map_shadow_stack int truncation
     (high-half of flags must be masked before bit
     validation).

     Divergence: Linux's sys_map_shadow_stack signature
     is `SYSCALL_DEFINE3(map_shadow_stack, unsigned long,
     addr, unsigned long, size, unsigned int, flags)`.
     The `flags` parameter is C `unsigned int`, but on
     x86_64 the syscall ABI delivers it in r10 (a 64-bit
     register) and the AMD64 ABI does NOT zero-extend
     `unsigned int` arguments before the syscall
     instruction.  So Linux observes only the low 32 bits
     of r10; the high 32 bits are caller-controlled
     garbage that must be ignored.

     Our pre-fix implementation read args.arg2 as raw u64
     and did `if flags & !1 != 0 { return EINVAL; }`.
     That meant any high-half garbage (e.g.
     0x1_0000_0001) was rejected with EINVAL where Linux
     would accept (truncated low half is 1 =
     SHADOW_STACK_SET_TOKEN, valid) and return ENOSYS on
     our stub.

     Fix: in sys_map_shadow_stack (kernel/src/syscall/
     linux.rs line ~18952), insert:
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let flags = args.arg2 as u32;
     before the mask check.  Mask compares against u32
     truncated value so high-half garbage is dropped
     before the SHADOW_STACK_SET_TOKEN=1 bit gate.  addr
     (arg0) and size (arg1) are C `unsigned long` and
     stay at u64 — no truncation needed for them.

     Tests added to dispatch_linux (after existing valid/
     undef-flag probes around line 45925):
       (a) map_shadow_stack(0, 4096, 0x1_0000_0001)
           → ENOSYS (high|SHADOW_STACK_SET_TOKEN;
            truncates to 1, mask passes, falls through
            to ENOSYS stub).
       (b) map_shadow_stack(0, 4096, 0x1_0000_0000)
           → ENOSYS (high-half only, low half zero;
            truncates to 0, mask passes, ENOSYS).
       (c) map_shadow_stack(0, 4096, 0x1_0000_0002)
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 0x2; truncates to 0x2,
            mask rejects bit outside the SET_TOKEN bit;
            verifies mask gate still rejects invalid
            bits after the high half is stripped).
       (d) map_shadow_stack(0, 13, 0x1_0000_0001)
           → EINVAL (bad size 13; not multiple of 8;
            verifies the size gate fires before the
            truncated flag gate so size validation is
            not masked by truncation).
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   map_shadow_stack
              unsigned-int truncation (high-half
              ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing map_shadow_stack probes still
     pass:
       - valid (size=4096, flags=0) → ENOSYS: works
         because 0 is 0 in either width.
       - valid (size=4096, flags=SET_TOKEN=1) → ENOSYS:
         works because 1 fits in 32 bits, u32 cast is
         identity.
       - undef flag 0x2 → EINVAL: works because 0x2 fits
         in 32 bits and the mask still rejects.
       - size=0 → EINVAL: gate fires before flag check.
       - size=7 (not multiple of 8) → EINVAL: gate fires
         before flag check.

     Known limitations:
     - x86 CET shadow stacks require user-mode hardware
       support and a real address-space mapper to back
       the SSP-page allocation.  We have neither — the
       syscall remains an ENOSYS stub.  When CET lands,
       the flag-mask gate continues to guard the entry,
       but the ENOSYS return is replaced by the actual
       allocation path.  Probes (a) and (b) will need to
       be updated to expect the new terminal errno (or a
       success fd) at that time.
     - addr (arg0) is currently ignored entirely (no
       address-hint plumbing).  When CET allocation lands
       this becomes a placement hint matched against the
       process address space.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (42.97s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 27s).

352. Batch 322 — sys_listmount int truncation (high-half
     of flags must be masked before bit validation).

     Divergence: Linux's sys_listmount signature is
     `SYSCALL_DEFINE4(listmount, const struct mnt_id_req
     __user *, req, u64 __user *, mnt_ids, size_t,
     nr_mnt_ids, unsigned int, flags)`.  The `flags`
     parameter is C `unsigned int`, delivered in r10.
     The AMD64 ABI does not zero-extend `unsigned int`
     across the syscall instruction, so Linux observes
     only the low 32 bits — the high half is caller-
     controlled garbage that must be ignored.

     Our pre-fix implementation did `if args.arg3 &
     !LISTMOUNT_REVERSE != 0 { return EINVAL; }` with
     LISTMOUNT_REVERSE declared as u64, so the mask
     check ran at 64-bit width.  Any high-half garbage
     (e.g. 0x1_0000_0001) was rejected with EINVAL even
     though the truncated low half (1 = REVERSE) is
     valid.

     Fix: in sys_listmount (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
     line ~21005), redeclare LISTMOUNT_REVERSE as u32
     and cast args.arg3 to u32 before the mask check:
       const LISTMOUNT_REVERSE: u32 = 1;
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let flags = args.arg3 as u32;
       if flags & !LISTMOUNT_REVERSE != 0 {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }
     req (arg0) is a pointer and nr_mnt_ids (arg2) is
     size_t = u64 on x86_64 — no truncation needed.

     Tests added to dispatch_linux (after existing
     listmount flags-first / size gates marker around
     line 47972):
       (a) listmount(req=NULL, buf=NULL, nr=0,
                     flags=0x1_0000_0001)
           → EFAULT (high|REVERSE; truncates to 1, mask
            passes, downstream gate 2a EFAULT on NULL
            req).  Pre-fix returned EINVAL because the
            high bit was caught by the mask.
       (b) listmount(req=NULL, buf=NULL, nr=0,
                     flags=0x1_0000_0000)
           → EFAULT (high-half only, low half zero;
            truncates to 0, mask passes, EFAULT on
            NULL req).
       (c) listmount(req=NULL, buf=NULL, nr=0,
                     flags=0x1_0000_0002)
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 0x2; truncates to 2,
            mask rejects bit outside LISTMOUNT_REVERSE;
            verifies mask gate still rejects invalid
            bits after the high half is stripped).
       (d) listmount(req=small_mnt_req (size=16),
                     buf=mnt_outbuf, nr=8,
                     flags=0x1_0000_0001)
           → EINVAL (high|REVERSE with too-small size;
            truncates to 1, mask passes, downstream
            gate 2c EINVAL for size<MNT_ID_REQ_SIZE_VER0
            = 24; verifies flag truncation does not
            mask the downstream size gate).
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   listmount unsigned-int
              truncation (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing listmount probes still pass:
       - flags=2 (bit 1) → EINVAL: works because 0x2 in
         the low 32 bits is rejected by the mask
         (LISTMOUNT_REVERSE=1 is bit 0).
       - flags=LISTMOUNT_REVERSE (1) with valid req+buf
         → ENOENT: works because 1 fits in 32 bits and
         u32 cast is identity.
       - req->size = 16 → EINVAL (size<VER0): unaffected
         by flag truncation.
       - req->size = 8192 → E2BIG (size>PAGE): unaffected.
       - valid req+buf → ENOENT: unaffected.

     Known limitations:
     - We have no mount-tree infrastructure yet, so the
       terminal errno after all validation gates pass is
       always ENOENT (unknown mnt_id).  When the VFS
       mount tree lands, the terminal probes will need
       to be updated to expect successful enumeration
       behavior, but the flag-mask gate continues to
       guard the entry.
     - nr_mnt_ids (arg2) and req (arg0) are correctly
       sized at u64 — no separate fixes needed.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (43.86s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

353. Batch 323 — sys_cachestat int truncation (high-half
     of flags must be masked before the != 0 reject gate).

     Divergence: Linux's sys_cachestat signature is
     `SYSCALL_DEFINE4(cachestat, unsigned int, fd,
     struct cachestat_range __user *, cstat_range,
     struct cachestat __user *, cstat, unsigned int,
     flags)`.  The `flags` parameter is C `unsigned int`,
     delivered in r10.  The AMD64 ABI does not zero-
     extend `unsigned int` across the syscall
     instruction, so Linux observes only the low 32 bits
     — the high half is caller-controlled garbage that
     must be ignored.

     Our pre-fix implementation did `if args.arg3 != 0 {
     return EINVAL; }` at u64 width, so any high-half
     garbage (e.g. 0x1_0000_0000) was rejected with
     EINVAL even though Linux's truncated flags=0
     advances to the pointer gates.  Userspace probes
     stress-testing the syscall by sweeping the high half
     of r10 saw the wrong errno discriminator.

     Fix: in sys_cachestat (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
     line ~18847), cast args.arg3 to u32 before the
     != 0 gate:
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let flags = args.arg3 as u32;
       if flags != 0 {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }
     fd is also `unsigned int` per Linux's prototype but
     the existing `as i32` cast at line ~18867 already
     truncates to 32 bits; no separate fix needed.

     Tests added to dispatch_linux (after existing
     cachestat valid/zero-fill probes around line 45886):
       (a) cachestat(fd=0, range=NULL, cstat=NULL,
                     flags=0x1_0000_0000)
           → EFAULT (high-only; truncates to 0, gate
            passes, downstream EFAULT on the NULL
            cstat_range pointer).  Pre-fix returned
            EINVAL because the high bit set the raw u64
            non-zero.
       (b) cachestat(fd=0, range=NULL, cstat=NULL,
                     flags=0x1_0000_0001)
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 1; truncates to 1,
            gate still rejects nonzero low half;
            verifies the mask still rejects after the
            high-bit strip).
       (c) cachestat(fd=0, range=NULL, cstat=NULL,
                     flags=0x1_0000_0002)
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 2; independent low
            bit confirms the mask covers more than the
            LSB).
       (d) cachestat(fd=0, range=valid (cstat_range_buf
                     16 bytes), cstat=valid (40-byte
                     destination), flags=0x1_0000_0000)
           → 0 (high-only with valid pointers; full
            success path runs, zero-fills the 40-byte
            cstat buffer, leaves the 8-byte trailing
            sentinel intact).  Verifies flag truncation
            does not mask downstream behaviour.
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   cachestat unsigned-int
              truncation (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing cachestat probes still pass:
       - flags=1 → EINVAL: works because 1 fits in 32
         bits and the != 0 gate still rejects.
       - range=NULL, cstat=valid, flags=0 → EFAULT:
         range gate fires first regardless of flag
         path.
       - valid range+cstat, flags=0 → 0 with 40-byte
         zero-fill: unchanged success path.

     Known limitations:
     - There is no page cache in this kernel — every
       counter returned is honestly zero.  When a real
       page-cache subsystem lands, the success path
       will surface per-range cache residency / dirty /
       writeback / evicted counters, but the flag-mask
       gate continues to guard the entry.
     - fd (arg0) is C `unsigned int` per Linux's
       prototype, but we cast to i32 (line ~18867) for
       compatibility with validate_linux_fd's signed-fd
       contract.  All u32 values up to 2_147_483_647 fit
       both representations identically; values above
       that wrap to negative i32 and are rejected as
       invalid fds, matching Linux's `fd < 0` rejection
       (Linux rejects any fd whose underlying file
       lookup fails, which any high-bit fd does via the
       fdtable bounds).  No truncation needed for fd.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (57.98s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 26s).

354. Batch 324 — sys_landlock_add_rule int truncation
     (high-half of flags must be masked before the != 0
     rejection gate).

     Divergence: Linux's sys_landlock_add_rule signature
     is `SYSCALL_DEFINE4(landlock_add_rule, const int,
     ruleset_fd, const enum landlock_rule_type,
     rule_type, const void __user *, rule_attr,
     const __u32, flags)`.  The `flags` parameter is C
     `__u32` (unsigned int), delivered in r10.  The
     AMD64 ABI does not zero-extend unsigned int across
     the syscall instruction, so Linux observes only
     the low 32 bits — the high half is caller-
     controlled garbage that must be ignored.

     Our pre-fix implementation did `if args.arg3 !=
     0 { return EINVAL; }` at u64 width, so any high-
     half garbage (e.g. 0x1_0000_0000) was rejected
     with EINVAL even though Linux's truncated flags=0
     advances to the rule_type / attr gates.

     Fix: in sys_landlock_add_rule (kernel/src/syscall/
     linux.rs line ~17852), cast args.arg3 to u32
     before the != 0 gate:
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let flags = args.arg3 as u32;
       if flags != 0 {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }

     Tests added to dispatch_linux (after existing
     landlock_add_rule attr validation marker around
     line 44785):
       (a) landlock_add_rule(ruleset_fd=0, rule_type=1
                              (PATH_BENEATH), attr=NULL,
                              flags=0x1_0000_0000)
           → EFAULT (high-only; truncates to 0, flag
            gate passes, downstream attr=NULL gate
            returns EFAULT).  Pre-fix returned EINVAL.
       (b) landlock_add_rule(ruleset_fd=0, rule_type=1,
                              attr=NULL,
                              flags=0x1_0000_0001)
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 1; truncates to 1,
            gate still rejects nonzero low half;
            verifies the mask continues to reject after
            the high-bit strip).
       (c) landlock_add_rule(ruleset_fd=0, rule_type=99
                              (bad), attr=NULL,
                              flags=0x1_0000_0000)
           → EINVAL (high-only; truncates to 0, flag
            gate passes, downstream rule_type gate
            fires EINVAL; verifies subsequent gates
            still work).  Same errno pre/post-fix but
            different gate.
       (d) landlock_add_rule(ruleset_fd=0, rule_type=1,
                              attr=lpb_attr (valid,
                              allowed_access=EXECUTE=
                              0x1), flags=0x1_0000_0000)
           → EBADF (high-only with valid attr;
            truncates to 0, flag gate passes, full
            pre-validate flow runs to the terminal
            EBADF since no real ruleset_fd exists).
            Pre-fix returned EINVAL on the flag gate.
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   landlock_add_rule
              unsigned-int truncation (high-half
              ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing landlock_add_rule probes
     still pass:
       - bad rule_type=99, flags=0 → EINVAL: unaffected.
       - valid PATH_BENEATH (allowed_access=1) → EBADF:
         unaffected because flags=0 in low 32 bits.
       - PATH_BENEATH allowed_access=0 → ENOMSG:
         unaffected.
       - PATH_BENEATH allowed_access=0x10000 (outside
         FS mask) → EINVAL: unaffected.
       - valid NET_PORT (allowed_access=1, port=8080)
         → EBADF: unaffected.
       - NET_PORT allowed_access=0x4 (outside NET mask)
         → EINVAL: unaffected.
       - NET_PORT port=65536 → EINVAL: unaffected.

     Known limitations:
     - Landlock is fundamentally incompatible with our
       capability-based security model (see entry 350
       on landlock_restrict_self for the architecture
       rationale).  EBADF is the permanent terminal
       after all pre-validation gates pass — no follow-
       up probes will be needed when more
       infrastructure lands.
     - rule_type (arg1) is C `enum landlock_rule_type`
       which is also int-sized; the `matches!(args.arg1,
       1 | 2)` gate at line ~17869 currently compares
       at u64 width.  This is a separate truncation
       divergence — a probe with rule_type=
       0x1_0000_0001 would fail to match `1` at u64
       width but Linux truncates to int and accepts.
       Deferred to a follow-up batch (would need a
       second discriminator and probe template here).

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (38.90s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

355. Batch 325 — sys_landlock_add_rule rule_type int
     truncation (high-half of rule_type must be masked
     before the enum-match gate).  This closes the
     deferred limitation from entry 354.

     Divergence: Linux's sys_landlock_add_rule signature
     declares `const enum landlock_rule_type, rule_type`
     for arg1.  The C enum is int-sized, delivered in
     rsi.  The AMD64 syscall ABI does not zero-extend
     C int across the syscall instruction, so Linux
     observes only the low 32 bits — the high half is
     caller-controlled garbage that must be ignored.

     Our pre-fix implementation did `if !matches!(args.
     arg1, 1 | 2) { return EINVAL; }` at u64 width, so
     a probe like rule_type=0x1_0000_0001 failed the
     match and returned EINVAL where Linux's truncated
     rule_type=1 matches PATH_BENEATH and advances to
     the attr gate.  The downstream per-arm dispatcher
     `match args.arg1 { 1 => ..., 2 => ... }` had the
     same divergence.

     Fix: in sys_landlock_add_rule (kernel/src/syscall/
     linux.rs line ~17869), cast args.arg1 to i32 once
     and use it for both the validation match! and the
     per-arm dispatcher:
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation,
                clippy::cast_possible_wrap)]
       let rule_type = args.arg1 as i32;
       if !matches!(rule_type, 1 | 2) {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }
       ...
       match rule_type { 1 => ..., 2 => ..., _ => ... }

     Tests added to dispatch_linux (after the unsigned-
     int truncation marker from batch 324):
       (a) landlock_add_rule(ruleset_fd=0,
                              rule_type=0x1_0000_0001,
                              attr=NULL, flags=0)
           → EFAULT (high|PATH_BENEATH; truncates to 1,
            type matches, downstream attr=NULL gate
            returns EFAULT).  Pre-fix returned EINVAL
            because the u64 match failed.
       (b) landlock_add_rule(ruleset_fd=0,
                              rule_type=0x1_0000_0002,
                              attr=NULL, flags=0)
           → EFAULT (high|NET_PORT; truncates to 2,
            type matches, attr=NULL EFAULT).  Pre-fix
            returned EINVAL.
       (c) landlock_add_rule(ruleset_fd=0,
                              rule_type=0x1_0000_0000,
                              attr=NULL, flags=0)
           → EINVAL (high|0 = invalid; truncates to 0,
            not in {1,2}, rule_type gate fires EINVAL).
            Verifies the gate still rejects unknown
            rule_type after the high-bit strip.
       (d) landlock_add_rule(ruleset_fd=0,
                              rule_type=0x1_0000_0001,
                              attr=lpb_attr (valid,
                              allowed_access=EXECUTE=
                              0x1), flags=0)
           → EBADF (high|PATH_BENEATH with valid attr;
            truncates to 1, full pre-validate flow
            runs to the terminal EBADF since no real
            ruleset_fd exists).  Pre-fix returned
            EINVAL.
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   landlock_add_rule
              rule_type int truncation (high-half
              ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing landlock_add_rule probes
     still pass:
       - bad rule_type=99, flags=0 → EINVAL: works
         because 99 fits in 32 bits and is not in
         {1,2}.
       - valid PATH_BENEATH (allowed_access=1) → EBADF:
         works because 1 in i32 == 1 in u64.
       - PATH_BENEATH allowed_access=0 → ENOMSG:
         unaffected.
       - PATH_BENEATH allowed_access=0x10000 (outside
         FS mask) → EINVAL: unaffected.
       - valid NET_PORT (allowed_access=1, port=8080)
         → EBADF: unaffected.
       - NET_PORT allowed_access=0x4 (outside NET mask)
         → EINVAL: unaffected.
       - NET_PORT port=65536 → EINVAL: unaffected.
       - flags truncation probes (entry 354) all pass.

     Known limitations:
     - Landlock continues to be fundamentally
       incompatible with our capability-based security
       model (see entry 350).  EBADF is the permanent
       terminal after pre-validation — no follow-up
       probes will be needed when more infrastructure
       lands.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (44.83s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

356. Batch 326 — sys_lsm_set_self_attr unsigned-int
     truncation (high-half of flags must be masked
     before the != 0 rejection gate).

     Divergence: Linux's sys_lsm_set_self_attr signature
     is `SYSCALL_DEFINE4(lsm_set_self_attr, unsigned
     int, attr, struct lsm_ctx __user *, ctx, u32, size,
     u32, flags)`.  `flags` is C `u32`, delivered in
     r10.  The AMD64 syscall ABI does not zero-extend
     `unsigned int` across the syscall instruction, so
     Linux observes only the low 32 bits — the high
     half is caller-controlled garbage that must be
     ignored.

     Our pre-fix implementation did `if args.arg3 != 0
     { return EINVAL; }` at u64 width, so any high-half
     garbage (e.g. 0x1_0000_0000) was rejected with
     EINVAL even though Linux's truncated flags=0
     advances to the size and ctx gates.

     Fix: in sys_lsm_set_self_attr (kernel/src/syscall/
     linux.rs line ~21184), cast args.arg3 to u32
     before the != 0 gate:
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let flags = args.arg3 as u32;
       if flags != 0 {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }

     Tests added to dispatch_linux (after existing
     lsm_set_self_attr flags/size/E2BIG gating marker
     around line 48385):
       (a) lsm_set_self_attr(attr=100, ctx=NULL,
                              size=32,
                              flags=0x1_0000_0000)
           → EFAULT (high-only; truncates to 0, flag
            gate passes, size gate passes (32 ==
            LSM_CTX_SIZE), downstream NULL-ctx gate
            returns EFAULT).  Pre-fix returned EINVAL.
       (b) lsm_set_self_attr(attr=100, ctx=lsm_ctx_ptr,
                              size=32,
                              flags=0x1_0000_0001)
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 1; truncates to 1,
            gate still rejects nonzero low half;
            verifies the mask still rejects after the
            high-bit strip).
       (c) lsm_set_self_attr(attr=100, ctx=lsm_ctx_ptr,
                              size=32,
                              flags=0x1_0000_0002)
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 2; independent low
            bit confirms the mask covers more than the
            LSB).
       (d) lsm_set_self_attr(attr=100, ctx=lsm_ctx_ptr,
                              size=16385,
                              flags=0x1_0000_0000)
           → E2BIG (high-only with size > PAGE_SIZE;
            truncates to 0, flag gate passes, size
            gate's lower bound passes, then size >
            16384 trips the E2BIG gate; verifies
            subsequent gates still fire after flag
            truncation).  Pre-fix returned EINVAL on
            the flag gate.
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   lsm_set_self_attr
              unsigned-int truncation (high-half
              ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing lsm_set_self_attr probes still
     pass:
       - flags=1, size=32, valid ctx → EINVAL: works
         because 1 fits in 32 bits.
       - flags=1 with huge size (100_000) → EINVAL:
         unchanged.
       - size=0 with NULL ctx → EINVAL: unaffected.
       - size=16 → EINVAL (< LSM_CTX_SIZE): unaffected.
       - size=31 → EINVAL: unaffected.
       - size=16385 with valid ctx → E2BIG: unaffected.
       - NULL ctx, size=32 → EFAULT: unaffected.
       - attr=50, valid ctx → EOPNOTSUPP: unaffected.
       - valid → EOPNOTSUPP: unaffected.

     Known limitations:
     - `attr` (arg0) is C `unsigned int` per Linux's
       prototype but is currently consumed at u64 width
       only to fall through to the LSM_RET_DEFAULT(
       setselfattr) = EOPNOTSUPP terminal.  No LSM is
       attached, so attr truncation does not affect
       any observable behaviour today.  When the first
       LSM lands and attr-keyed dispatch matters,
       arg0 needs the same u32 cast — tracked as a
       separate batch when LSM dispatch arrives.
     - `size` (arg2) is C `u32` per Linux's prototype
       but is consumed at u64 width via direct
       comparisons.  In practice the LSM_MAX_CTX_SIZE
       (16384) ceiling means any u32 value behaves
       identically to its u64 widened form, and values
       above 2^32 are well past the ceiling and trip
       E2BIG regardless.  Future tightening is
       possible but yields no observable behaviour
       difference today.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (37.77s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

357. Batch 327 — sys_lsm_list_modules unsigned-int
     truncation (high-half of flags must be masked
     before the != 0 rejection gate).

     Divergence: Linux's sys_lsm_list_modules signature
     is `SYSCALL_DEFINE3(lsm_list_modules, u64 __user *,
     ids, size_t __user *, size, u32, flags)`.  `flags`
     is C `u32`, delivered in rdx.  The AMD64 syscall
     ABI does not zero-extend `unsigned int` across the
     syscall instruction, so Linux observes only the
     low 32 bits — the high half is caller-controlled
     garbage that must be ignored.

     Our pre-fix implementation did `if args.arg2 != 0
     { return EINVAL; }` at u64 width, so any high-half
     garbage (e.g. 0x1_0000_0000) was rejected with
     EINVAL even though Linux's truncated flags=0
     advances to the size-pointer gates.

     Fix: in sys_lsm_list_modules (kernel/src/syscall/
     linux.rs line ~23909), cast args.arg2 to u32
     before the != 0 gate:
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let flags = args.arg2 as u32;
       if flags != 0 {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }

     Tests added to dispatch_linux (after existing
     lsm_list_modules flags-first gating marker around
     line 48490):
       (a) lsm_list_modules(ids=NULL, size=NULL,
                              flags=0x1_0000_0000)
           → EFAULT (high-only; truncates to 0, flag
            gate passes, downstream NULL-size gate
            returns EFAULT).  Pre-fix returned EINVAL.
       (b) lsm_list_modules(ids=NULL, size=size_ptr,
                              flags=0x1_0000_0001)
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 1; truncates to 1,
            gate still rejects nonzero low half;
            verifies the mask still rejects after the
            high-bit strip).
       (c) lsm_list_modules(ids=NULL, size=size_ptr,
                              flags=0x1_0000_0002)
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 2; independent low
            bit confirms the mask covers more than the
            LSB).
       (d) lsm_list_modules(ids=NULL, size=size_ptr,
                              flags=0x1_0000_0000)
           → EOPNOTSUPP (high-only with valid size
            pointer; truncates to 0, flag gate passes,
            full pre-validate flow runs to the
            terminal EOPNOTSUPP since no LSM modules
            are registered).  Pre-fix returned EINVAL
            on the flag gate.
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   lsm_list_modules
              unsigned-int truncation (high-half
              ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing lsm_list_modules probes still
     pass:
       - NULL size, flags=0 → EFAULT: unaffected.
       - NULL size, flags=1 → EINVAL (flags-first):
         works because 1 fits in 32 bits.
       - valid size, flags=1 → EINVAL: unaffected.
       - valid size, flags=0xFFFF_FFFF (max u32) →
         EINVAL: unaffected because this fits in 32
         bits exactly.
       - valid size, flags=0 → EOPNOTSUPP: unaffected.

     Known limitations:
     - There is no LSM infrastructure in this kernel
       (capabilities replace LSM in our architecture;
       see entry 350).  EOPNOTSUPP is the permanent
       terminal — no follow-up probes will be needed
       when more infrastructure lands.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.56s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 15s).

358. Batch 328 — sys_lsm_get_self_attr unsigned-int
     truncation (high-half of flags must be masked
     before the LSM_FLAG_SINGLE comparison).

     Divergence: Linux's sys_lsm_get_self_attr signature
     is `SYSCALL_DEFINE4(lsm_get_self_attr, unsigned
     int, attr, struct lsm_ctx __user *, ctx, u32 __user
     *, size, u32, flags)`.  `flags` is C `u32`,
     delivered in r10.  The AMD64 syscall ABI does not
     zero-extend `unsigned int` across the syscall
     instruction, so Linux observes only the low 32
     bits.

     Our pre-fix implementation declared
     `const LSM_FLAG_SINGLE: u64 = 1;` and compared
     `args.arg3 != LSM_FLAG_SINGLE` at u64 width.  A
     caller passing flags=0x1_0000_0001 hit the
     comparison with the high-half garbage still in
     place: u64 0x1_0000_0001 != 1, so we rejected
     with EINVAL where Linux's truncated
     flags=LSM_FLAG_SINGLE advances past the flag
     gate into the SINGLE-without-ctx check (and
     ultimately into the per-LSM dispatch with
     EOPNOTSUPP terminal).

     Symmetric issue on the all-zero-low path:
     flags=0x1_0000_0000 with low=0 hit `args.arg3
     != 0` and was also rejected with EINVAL where
     Linux's truncated flags=0 advances unconditionally.

     Fix: in sys_lsm_get_self_attr (kernel/src/syscall/
     linux.rs line ~21113), change LSM_FLAG_SINGLE to
     u32 and cast args.arg3 to u32 before the
     comparisons:
       const LSM_FLAG_SINGLE: u32 = 1;
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let flags = args.arg3 as u32;
       if flags != 0 && flags != LSM_FLAG_SINGLE {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }
       if flags == LSM_FLAG_SINGLE && args.arg1 == 0 {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }

     Tests added to dispatch_linux (after the existing
     "lsm_get_self_attr UNDEF/SINGLE gating: OK"
     marker around line 48322):
       (a) lsm_get_self_attr(attr=100, ctx=valid,
                             size=valid,
                             flags=0x1_0000_0000)
           → EOPNOTSUPP (high-only; truncates to 0,
            flag gate passes, full gate flow runs to
            terminal EOPNOTSUPP).  Pre-fix returned
            EINVAL.
       (b) lsm_get_self_attr(attr=100, ctx=valid,
                             size=valid,
                             flags=0x1_0000_0001)
           → EOPNOTSUPP (high|SINGLE; truncates to
            LSM_FLAG_SINGLE, with valid ctx the
            downstream SINGLE-without-ctx gate
            passes, full flow runs to terminal
            EOPNOTSUPP).  Pre-fix returned EINVAL
            because u64 0x1_0000_0001 != 1.
       (c) lsm_get_self_attr(attr=100, ctx=valid,
                             size=valid,
                             flags=0x1_0000_0002)
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 2; truncates to
            2 which is neither 0 nor SINGLE; flag
            gate still rejects; confirms mask
            preserves correct rejection of unknown
            bits).
       (d) lsm_get_self_attr(attr=100, ctx=NULL,
                             size=valid,
                             flags=0x1_0000_0001)
           → EINVAL (high|SINGLE with NULL ctx;
            verifies the downstream SINGLE-without-
            ctx gate is reached on the truncated
            flag value).
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   lsm_get_self_attr
              unsigned-int truncation (high-half
              ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing lsm_get_self_attr probes still
     pass:
       - attr=0 (LSM_ATTR_UNDEF) → EINVAL.
       - attr=50 and attr=200 (out-of-range) →
         EOPNOTSUPP (Linux does not range-check attr;
         falls through to the per-LSM default).
       - NULL size → EINVAL.
       - flags=2 → EINVAL (bad bit pattern).
       - flags=3 (SINGLE + extra bit) → EINVAL.
       - flags=1 with NULL ctx → EINVAL.
       - flags=1 with valid ctx → EOPNOTSUPP.
       - flags=0 with valid args → EOPNOTSUPP.

     Known limitations:
     - `attr` (arg0) was already cast to u32 in a
       prior batch — the LSM_ATTR_UNDEF comparison
       remains correct against the truncated form.
     - `size*` (arg2) is a pointer (u64) — not
       subject to int truncation.
     - There is no LSM infrastructure in this kernel
       (capabilities replace LSM in our architecture;
       see entry 350).  EOPNOTSUPP is the permanent
       terminal — no follow-up probes will be needed
       when more infrastructure lands.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (36.52s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

359. Batch 329 — sys_futex_waitv unsigned-int
     truncation (high-half of flags must be masked
     before the != 0 reject gate).

     Divergence: Linux's sys_futex_waitv signature is
     `SYSCALL_DEFINE5(futex_waitv, struct futex_waitv
     __user *, waiters, unsigned int, nr_futexes,
     unsigned int, flags, struct __kernel_timespec
     __user *, timeout, clockid_t, clockid)`.  `flags`
     is C `unsigned int`, delivered in rdx.  The AMD64
     syscall ABI does not zero-extend `unsigned int`
     across the syscall instruction, so Linux observes
     only the low 32 bits.

     Our pre-fix implementation did `if args.arg2 != 0
     { return EINVAL; }` at u64 width.  A caller
     passing flags=0x1_0000_0000 (high-only) hit the
     u64 gate and was rejected with EINVAL where
     Linux's truncated flags=0 advances past the flag
     gate into the nr/waiters check.  More importantly,
     in the nr=1 fast path success route, this
     pre-mature EINVAL prevented the dispatched
     sys_futex_wait from being reached.

     Fix: in sys_futex_waitv (kernel/src/syscall/
     linux.rs line ~20248), cast args.arg2 to u32
     before the != 0 gate:
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let flags = args.arg2 as u32;
       if flags != 0 {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }

     Tests added to dispatch_linux (after existing
     "futex_waitv nr=1 fast path wiring: OK" marker
     around line 47486).  All four probes reuse the
     SIZE_U32 `valid` WaitvEntry from the fast-path
     block (uaddr=0) so the dispatched sys_futex_wait
     returns EFAULT as the success-path discriminator:
       (a) flags=0x1_0000_0000, nr=1, waitv=v_ptr
           → EFAULT (high|0; truncates to 0, advances
            through gates to fast-path uaddr=0
            EFAULT).  Pre-fix returned EINVAL via the
            un-truncated flag gate.
       (b) flags=0x1_0000_0001, nr=1, waitv=v_ptr
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 1; truncates to 1,
            flag gate still rejects nonzero low half).
       (c) flags=0x1_0000_0002, nr=1, waitv=v_ptr
           → EINVAL (high|bad-low 2; independent low
            bit confirms the mask covers more than the
            LSB).
       (d) flags=0x1_0000_0000, nr=2, waitv=v_ptr
           → ENOSYS (high|0; truncates to 0,
            nr/waiters gate accepts nr=2, nr>1 path
            returns ENOSYS for multi-key wait).
            Distinct terminal from (a) confirms the
            flag mask doesn't accidentally re-trip an
            EINVAL downstream.
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   futex_waitv unsigned-
              int truncation (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing futex_waitv probes still
     pass:
       - nr=0, flags=0 → EINVAL (combined gate).
       - nr=200, flags=0 → EINVAL (FUTEX_WAITV_MAX
         exceeded).
       - waiters=valid, nr=1, flags=1 → EINVAL (flag
         gate rejects 1 as bad bit).
       - NULL waiters, nr=1, flags=0 → EINVAL.
       - nr=0, flags=0xdead_beef → EINVAL (flag gate
         fires first; mask truncates 0xdead_beef
         which is still nonzero so still rejects).
       - bad clockid with timeout → EINVAL.
       - SIZE_U16/U8 → EINVAL (per-entry gate).
       - reserved!=0 → EINVAL.
       - unknown per-entry flag bit → EINVAL.
       - FUTEX2_NUMA bit → EINVAL.
       - SIZE_U32 entry with uaddr=0 → EFAULT.
       - SIZE_U32 | PRIVATE with uaddr=0 → EFAULT.

     Known limitations:
     - `nr_futexes` (arg1) was already cast to u32 in
       a prior batch — the FUTEX_WAITV_MAX comparison
       remains correct against the truncated form.
     - `clockid` (arg4) is C int and was already cast
       to i32 in a prior batch.
     - The `nr > 1` path still returns ENOSYS until
       multi-key wait infrastructure lands (tracked
       separately).

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (36.25s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 26s).

360. Batch 330 — sys_membarrier int truncation
     (cpu_id, the third arg, must be masked to i32
     before the QUERY != 0 reject gate).

     Divergence: Linux's sys_membarrier signature is
     `SYSCALL_DEFINE3(membarrier, int, cmd, unsigned
     int, flags, int, cpu_id)`.  `cpu_id` is C `int`,
     delivered in rdx.  The AMD64 syscall ABI does not
     sign- or zero-extend `int` across the syscall
     instruction, so Linux observes only the low 32
     bits.

     Batch 301 already truncated cmd (i32) and flags
     (u32) but left cpu_id as raw u64.  The QUERY arm
     (cmd == MEMBARRIER_CMD_QUERY) checks
     `args.arg2 != 0` directly at u64 width to enforce
     "cpu_id must be 0 for QUERY".  A caller passing
     cpu_id=0x1_0000_0000 (high-only) hit the u64 gate
     and was rejected with EINVAL where Linux's
     truncated cpu_id=0 advances past the gate and
     returns the QUERY bitmask.

     Outside the QUERY arm cpu_id is unused (the
     bind-to-CPU FLAG_CPU path forwards the value to
     the per-CPU IPI which we don't implement), so the
     divergence is QUERY-only.

     Fix: in sys_membarrier (kernel/src/syscall/
     linux.rs line ~18451), add an i32 cast and reroute
     the QUERY gate through it:
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation,
               clippy::cast_possible_wrap)]
       let cpu_id_i32 = args.arg2 as i32;
       ...
       if cmd == MEMBARRIER_CMD_QUERY {
           if flags != 0 || cpu_id_i32 != 0 {
               return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
           }
           ...
       }

     Tests added to dispatch_linux (after existing
     batch-301 "io_setup/swapon/membarrier unsigned-
     int/int truncation: OK" marker around line 28239):
       (a) cmd=0 (QUERY), flags=0,
           cpu_id=0x1_0000_0000
           → 0x3FF (QUERY bitmask; truncates to 0,
            advances past gate).  Pre-fix returned
            EINVAL.
       (b) cmd=0, flags=0, cpu_id=0x1_0000_0001
           → EINVAL (truncates to 1, gate still
            rejects nonzero low half).
       (c) cmd=0, flags=0,
           cpu_id=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0000
           → 0x3FF (all-high pattern, low=0;
            independent confirmation of the truncation).
       (d) cmd=0, flags=0,
           cpu_id=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0001
           → EINVAL (all-high + low=1; mask still
            trips the gate regardless of high half).
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   membarrier cpu_id
              int-truncation (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing membarrier probes still pass:
       - QUERY (cmd=0, flags=0, cpu_id=0) → 0x3FF.
       - QUERY with flags=1 → EINVAL.
       - QUERY with cpu_id=1 → EINVAL (low-half low
         bit set rejects via the truncated value).
       - GLOBAL (cmd=1) → 0.
       - PRIVATE_EXPEDITED + FLAG_CPU → 0.
       - REGISTER_GLOBAL → 0.
       - REGISTER + FLAG_CPU → EINVAL.
       - GLOBAL + FLAG_CPU → EINVAL (FLAG_CPU only
         legal on PRIVATE_EXPEDITED variants).
       - GET_REGISTRATIONS → 0.
       - unknown flag bit → EINVAL.
       - bogus cmd → EINVAL.
       - huge cmd (high-half) truncated → EINVAL via
         cmd-truncation from batch 301.

     Known limitations:
     - For non-QUERY commands cpu_id is unused: the
       bind-to-CPU FLAG_CPU path forwards the value to
       a per-CPU IPI which we don't implement, so even
       a "valid" cpu_id is silently ignored.  When SMP
       IPI lands, additional cpu_id validation
       (range-check against nr_cpus) will be needed.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (35.85s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

361. Batch 331 — sys_mq_timedsend unsigned-int
     truncation (msg_prio, the fourth arg, must be
     masked to u32 before the MQ_PRIO_MAX gate).

     Divergence: Linux's sys_mq_timedsend signature is
     `SYSCALL_DEFINE5(mq_timedsend, mqd_t, mqdes,
     const char __user *, u_msg_ptr, size_t, msg_len,
     unsigned int, msg_prio, const struct
     __kernel_timespec __user *, u_abs_timeout)`.
     `msg_prio` is C `unsigned int`, delivered in r10.
     The AMD64 syscall ABI does not zero-extend
     `unsigned int` across the syscall instruction.

     Our pre-fix implementation did `if args.arg3 >=
     MQ_PRIO_MAX { return EINVAL; }` at u64 width
     (MQ_PRIO_MAX = 32768).  A caller passing
     msg_prio=0x1_0000_0000 (high-only) hit the u64
     gate (0x1_0000_0000 >= 32768) and was rejected
     with EINVAL where Linux's truncated msg_prio=0
     advances to the msg-pointer NULL check.

     Fix: in sys_mq_timedsend (kernel/src/syscall/
     linux.rs line ~14762), narrow MQ_PRIO_MAX to u32
     and cast args.arg3 to u32 before the comparison:
       const MQ_PRIO_MAX: u32 = 32768;
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let msg_prio = args.arg3 as u32;
       if msg_prio >= MQ_PRIO_MAX {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }

     Tests added to dispatch_linux (after existing
     "mq_timedsend prio validation: OK" marker around
     line 42597):
       (a) prio=0x1_0000_0000, msg=NULL, ts=0
           → EFAULT (truncates to 0, prio gate
            passes, NULL msg check fires).  Pre-fix
            returned EINVAL.
       (b) prio=0x1_0000_0001, msg=NULL, ts=0
           → EFAULT (truncates to 1 < MQ_PRIO_MAX,
            advances to NULL msg).  Pre-fix returned
            EINVAL.
       (c) prio=0x1_0000_8000 (high|MQ_PRIO_MAX),
           msg=NULL, ts=0
           → EINVAL (truncates to 32768; gate still
            rejects values >= MAX).  Both pre and
            post-batch return EINVAL — confirms the
            mask doesn't accidentally let MAX-equal
            values through.
       (d) prio=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0000 (all-high,
           low=0), msg=NULL, ts=0
           → EFAULT.  Independent confirmation of the
            truncation with an alternate high-half
            pattern.  Pre-fix returned EINVAL.
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   mq_timedsend msg_prio
              unsigned-int truncation (high-half
              ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing mq_timedsend probes still
     pass:
       - mq_timedsend(NULL) → EFAULT.
       - mq_timedsend(prio=MQ_PRIO_MAX) → EINVAL.
       - mq_timedsend(prio=70000) → EINVAL (well
         above MAX; fits in u32 so the mask is
         transparent).
       - mq_timedsend(prio=32767, NULL) → EFAULT
         (highest valid value; advances to NULL msg).
       - mq_timedsend(bad tv_nsec) → EINVAL.
       - mq_timedsend(neg tv_sec) → EINVAL.
       - mq_timedsend(good ts, bad prio) → EINVAL.

     Known limitations:
     - mqd (arg0) is C `mqd_t` (int) — already cast
       to i32 at the fd-validation step.
     - msg_len (arg2) is C `size_t` (u64) — no
       truncation issue.
     - There is no real mq implementation; terminal
       EBADF stands in for the fd lookup.  When mq
       lands, the full receive/send/notify paths will
       need their own truncation audits.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (37.13s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

362. Batch 332 — sys_io_uring_enter unsigned-int
     truncation (high-half ignored).

     Divergence: Linux's prototype is
       SYSCALL_DEFINE6(io_uring_enter,
                       unsigned int, fd,
                       u32, to_submit,
                       u32, min_complete,
                       u32, flags,
                       const sigset_t __user *, sig,
                       size_t, sigsz)
     where `flags` is `u32`, delivered in r10.  The
     AMD64 syscall ABI does not zero-extend `u32` args
     before issuing syscall, so only the low 32 bits
     of `flags` are defined; the high half is caller-
     supplied garbage that the kernel must mask off
     before any flag-bit comparison.

     Pre-batch sys_io_uring_enter read flags as a raw
     `u64` (`let flags = args.arg3;`) and gated against
     `VALID_FLAGS: u64 = 0x7F` with
     `if flags & !VALID_FLAGS != 0 { ... EINVAL }`.  Any
     bit set above bit 31 — i.e. any caller-supplied
     high-half garbage — tripped the reject and
     surfaced EINVAL where Linux would mask the high
     half, accept the call, and fall through to the
     fd lookup (EBADF in this kernel, since no
     io_uring fd can exist).

     Fix: cast `args.arg3` to `u32` at entry with
     `#[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]` and
     change `IORING_ENTER_GETEVENTS`,
     `IORING_ENTER_EXT_ARG`, and `VALID_FLAGS` from
     `u64` to `u32` so the downstream EXT_ARG /
     GETEVENTS bit checks operate on the same masked
     value.

     Tests: four boot-time probes added after the
     existing "io_uring_enter/register validation: OK"
     marker, all with fd=0, to_submit=0, min_complete=0,
     sig=NULL, sigsz=0 so the discriminator is solely
     the post-mask flag value:
       (a) flags=0x1_0000_0000 (high-only, low=0)
           -> EBADF (no invalid bit set after mask).
       (b) flags=0x1_0000_0080 (high|bit-7 reserved)
           -> EINVAL preserved (low half still bad).
       (c) flags=0x1_0000_0001 (high|GETEVENTS)
           -> EBADF (low half is the valid GETEVENTS
           bit, sig=NULL skips pointer block, fd=0
           falls through).
       (d) flags=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0000 (all-high,
           low=0) -> EBADF.
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   io_uring_enter flags
              u32-truncation (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing io_uring_enter validation
     probes still pass:
       - fd=0, flags=0, sig=NULL -> EBADF.
       - flags=0x80 (unknown bit) -> EINVAL.
       - sig=non-NULL, EXT_ARG clear, sigsz!=8
         -> EINVAL.
       - EXT_ARG without GETEVENTS -> EINVAL.
       - sig=NULL, EXT_ARG|GETEVENTS -> EBADF.

     Known limitations:
     - `fd` (arg0) is C `unsigned int` (Linux uses
       `unsigned int` for io_uring fds at this syscall
       boundary); already cast to i32 at the
       validate_linux_fd step.
     - `to_submit` and `min_complete` (arg1, arg2) are
       both C `u32` but are not consulted by the
       current handler (no io_uring fds exist), so no
       gate observes them — no truncation visible
       today.  When io_uring lands, they will need
       their own u32 mask before any range comparison.
     - `sigsz` (arg5) is C `size_t` (u64) — no
       truncation issue.
     - sys_io_uring_setup also takes `entries: u32`
       and gates against `IORING_MAX_ENTRIES: u64 =
       32768`; this is a separate truncation candidate
       deferred to a future batch (the existing
       max-entries probe uses a 65536 sentinel that
       fits in u32 so the bug isn't visible there
       yet — high-bit-set entries would currently
       surface EINVAL via the `>` comparison instead
       of being masked).  Logged here for follow-up.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (40.84s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

363. Batch 333 — sys_io_uring_setup unsigned-int
     truncation (high-half ignored).

     Divergence: Linux's prototype is
       SYSCALL_DEFINE2(io_uring_setup,
                       u32, entries,
                       struct io_uring_params __user *, params)
     where `entries` is `u32`, delivered in rdi.  The
     AMD64 syscall ABI does not zero-extend `u32` args
     before issuing syscall, so only the low 32 bits
     of `entries` are defined; the high half is
     caller-supplied garbage that the kernel must mask
     off before any range comparison.

     Pre-batch sys_io_uring_setup gated entries at u64
     width:
       if args.arg0 == 0 { ... EINVAL }
       const IORING_MAX_ENTRIES: u64 = 32768;
       if args.arg0 > IORING_MAX_ENTRIES && ... { EINVAL }
     Any high-bit garbage in arg0 (e.g.
     0x1_0000_0008) made the raw u64 > 32768 comparison
     trip and surface EINVAL where Linux would mask
     entries to 8 (a valid count below MAX), accept the
     call, and fall through to ENOSYS in this kernel.

     Fix: cast `args.arg0` to `u32` at entry with
     `#[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]`,
     change `IORING_MAX_ENTRIES` from `u64` to `u32` so
     the comparison happens at the correct width, and
     run both entries==0 and entries>MAX gates against
     the masked value.  This was logged as a known
     limitation in batch 332's todo entry (entry 362).

     Tests: four boot-time probes added after the
     existing "io_uring_setup read > resv > flags >
     entries gate order: OK" marker, all using a fresh
     120-byte zero-filled params buffer so copy_from_user,
     resv, and flag-mask gates all pass and the
     discriminator is solely the post-mask entries
     value:
       (a) entries=0x1_0000_0000 (high-only, low=0)
           -> EINVAL via gate 4 (entries==0).  Pre-batch
           tripped gate 5 (raw u64 > MAX) with the same
           errno but via the wrong gate; post-batch the
           correct gate fires.
       (b) entries=0x1_0000_0008 (high|valid-8)
           -> ENOSYS (was EINVAL pre-batch).  Primary
           discriminator.
       (c) entries=0x1_0000_8001 (high|low=32769>MAX)
           -> EINVAL preserved (low-half still exceeds
           IORING_MAX_ENTRIES).
       (d) entries=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0008 (all-high|
           valid-8) -> ENOSYS (was EINVAL pre-batch).
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   io_uring_setup entries
              u32-truncation (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing io_uring_setup gate-order
     probes still pass:
       - (entries=0, valid params) -> EINVAL (gate 4).
       - (entries=8, NULL params) -> EFAULT (gate 1).
       - (entries=0, NULL params) -> EFAULT (gate 1
         beats gate 4).
       - (entries=65536, NULL params) -> EFAULT (gate 1
         beats gate 5).
       - (entries=65536, valid params) -> EINVAL via
         gate 5 — 65536 fits in u32, so the mask is
         transparent.
       - (entries=8, valid params, bad flag) -> EINVAL.
       - (entries=8, valid params, non-zero resv)
         -> EINVAL.
       - (entries=8, valid params, both bad flag and
         non-zero resv) -> EINVAL (resv gate fires
         first).
       - (entries=8, valid params, all zero) -> ENOSYS.

     Known limitations:
     - `params` (arg1) is a user pointer (u64) — no
       truncation issue.
     - Cleared one item from the batch 332 follow-up
       list.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (35.53s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
