                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

364. Batch 334 — sys_process_madvise unsigned-int
     truncation (high-half ignored).

     Divergence: Linux's prototype is
       SYSCALL_DEFINE5(process_madvise,
                       int, pidfd,
                       const struct iovec __user *, vec,
                       size_t, vlen,
                       int, behavior,
                       unsigned int, flags)
     where `flags` is `unsigned int`, delivered in r8.
     The AMD64 syscall ABI does not zero-extend
     `unsigned int` args before issuing syscall, so
     only the low 32 bits of `flags` are defined; the
     high half is caller-supplied garbage that the
     kernel must mask off before any comparison.

     Pre-batch sys_process_madvise gated `flags` at
     u64 width as the very first statement:
       if args.arg4 != 0 {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }
     Any high-bit garbage in arg4 (e.g.
     0x1_0000_0000 — low half zero) tripped the
     `flags != 0` reject and surfaced EINVAL where
     Linux would mask the high half, accept the
     truncated `flags == 0`, and advance to the pidfd
     lookup (EBADF in this kernel — no real pidfds
     exist).

     Fix: cast `args.arg4` to `u32` at entry with
     `#[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]` and
     run the `flags != 0` reject against the masked
     value.

     Tests: four boot-time probes added after the
     existing "process_madvise advice-last gate order:
     OK" marker, all with pidfd=0, iov=NULL,
     iovcnt=0, behavior=0 so the discriminator is
     solely the post-mask flags value:
       (a) flags=0x1_0000_0000 (high-only, low=0)
           -> EBADF (was EINVAL pre-batch).  Primary
           discriminator: low u32 is zero so the
           flag gate passes, then iovcnt=0 skips the
           iov block, fd lookup terminates with EBADF.
       (b) flags=0x1_0000_0001 (high|valid-bit)
           -> EINVAL preserved (low half is 1, gate
           still fires).
       (c) flags=0x1_0000_8000 (high|low!=0)
           -> EINVAL preserved.
       (d) flags=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0000 (all-high,
           low=0) -> EBADF (was EINVAL pre-batch).
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   process_madvise flags
              unsigned-int truncation (high-half
              ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing process_madvise probes still
     pass:
       - (all-zero) -> EBADF.
       - (flags=1) -> EINVAL.
       - (closed pidfd, advice=200) -> EBADF (advice
         gate fires LAST, after fd lookup).
       - (flags=1, advice=200) -> EINVAL (flags wins
         over advice).

     Known limitations:
     - `pidfd` (arg0) is C `int` — already cast to
       i32 at the validate_linux_fd step.
     - `vlen` (arg2) is C `size_t` (u64) — no
       truncation issue.
     - `behavior` (arg3) is C `int` and is compared
       at u64 width (`if args.arg3 > 100`) in the
       terminal gate at line ~18864.  In kernel
       context the fd lookup terminates with EBADF
       before reaching the behavior gate, so the
       truncation bug is not observable via boot
       self-tests today; logged here as a follow-up
       for when real pidfds exist or unit-test
       infrastructure lands.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (35.70s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

365. Batch 335 — sys_sched_setscheduler `policy`
     int truncation (high-half ignored).

     Divergence: Linux's prototype is
       SYSCALL_DEFINE3(sched_setscheduler,
                       pid_t, pid,
                       int, policy,
                       struct sched_param __user *, param)
     where `policy` is `int`, delivered in rsi.  The
     AMD64 syscall ABI does not sign- or zero-extend
     `int` args before issuing syscall, so only the
     low 32 bits of `policy` are defined; the high
     half is caller-supplied garbage.

     Pre-batch sys_sched_setscheduler gated three
     downstream uses of `policy` at u64 width:
       - gate 5: `match policy { 0|1|2|3|5|6|7 => {}
                                  _ => EINVAL }`
       - gate 6: `sched_priority_check_for_policy(
                    policy, sched_prio)`
       - gate 7: `if policy == 1 || policy == 2`
     Gate 1 already cast to i32 (`policy_i32 < 0`),
     but the post-gate-1 path went back to the raw
     u64.  High-bit sentinels whose low 32 bits
     encoded a *valid* policy (e.g. arg1 =
     0x1_0000_0001, low = SCHED_FIFO) missed every
     match arm in gate 5 and surfaced EINVAL — Linux
     sees policy=1 and accepts.

     Fix: introduce `policy_u32 = policy_i32 as u32`
     after gate 1 (sign loss safe — gate 1 proved
     policy_i32 >= 0) and route gates 5, 6, 7 plus
     the per-PCB store through it.  Gate 6 widens
     back to u64 at the call site to keep
     sched_priority_check_for_policy's signature
     untouched (it is shared with sched_setparam and
     sched_setattr).

     Tests: four boot-time probes added after the
     existing "sched_setscheduler policy<0 > ... >
     policy gate order: OK" marker, all with pid=0
     (caller, kernel-context skips the per-PCB
     store) and a stack-allocated valid param:
       (a) policy=0x1_0000_0000 (low=0=SCHED_OTHER),
           param prio=0 -> Ok(0).  Pre-batch the
           raw-u64 match missed every arm -> EINVAL.
       (b) policy=0x1_0000_0001 (low=1=SCHED_FIFO),
           param prio=1 -> Ok(0).  Primary post-
           truncation discriminator (success vs
           pre-batch EINVAL).  Reachable because
           gate 7's RTPRIO check returns Ok in
           kernel context.
       (c) policy=0x1_0000_0008 (low=8=unknown),
           param prio=0 -> EINVAL.  Preserved:
           pre-batch also EINVAL via the raw match
           miss; post-batch via the u32 match miss.
       (d) policy=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0001 (low=1=
           SCHED_FIFO, all high bits set), param
           prio=1 -> Ok(0).  Same discriminator as
           (b) with a different high-bit pattern.
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   sched_setscheduler
              policy int-truncation (high-half
              ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing sched_setscheduler probes
     still pass:
       - (0, 8, valid) -> EINVAL (policy=8 rejected).
       - (0, 4, valid) -> EINVAL (deprecated ISO).
       - (0, 0, NULL) -> EINVAL (gate 2 param NULL).
       - (0, u64::MAX, valid) -> EINVAL (gate 1
         policy_i32 < 0).
       - (0, 99, NULL) -> EINVAL (gate 2 NULL beats
         gate 5).
       - (12345, 99, valid) -> ESRCH (gate 4 pid
         lookup beats gate 5).

     Known limitations:
     - `pid` (arg0) is already cast through pid_i32
       at gates 1, 2, and 4.
     - SCHED_DEADLINE (6) is accepted at gate 5 but
       not actually wired into the kernel scheduler;
       its per-task store ignores priority semantics.
       Not a regression from this batch.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (41.71s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 26s).

366. Batch 336 — sys_sethostname + sys_setdomainname
     `len` int truncation (high-half ignored).

     Divergence: Linux's prototypes are
       SYSCALL_DEFINE2(sethostname,   char __user *, name, int, len)
       SYSCALL_DEFINE2(setdomainname, char __user *, name, int, len)
     where `len` is `int`, delivered in rsi.  The
     AMD64 syscall ABI does not zero- or sign-extend
     `int` args before issuing syscall, so only the
     low 32 bits of `len` are defined.  Linux's body
     opens with `if (len < 0 || len > __NEW_UTS_LEN)
     return -EINVAL;` run at 32-bit width.

     Pre-batch both stubs held `len` as a raw u64
     (`let len = args.arg1 as usize`) and gated at
     full 64-bit width with `if len > 64 { EINVAL }`.
     High-bit sentinels whose low 32 encoded a valid
     in-range length (e.g. arg1 = 0x1_0000_0001,
     low = 1) tripped the raw `> 64` reject and
     surfaced EINVAL where Linux truncates to 1 and
     proceeds to copy_from_user (returning EFAULT
     for NULL name with non-zero len, success for
     a valid name).

     Fix: cast `args.arg1` to i32 with the standard
     `#[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation,
              clippy::cast_possible_wrap)]`, run
     `if len_i32 < 0 || len_i32 > 64 { EINVAL }`
     (mirroring Linux's combined gate), then widen
     `len_i32 as usize` for the body's NULL check
     and copy_from_user length.  Adds the negative-
     len reject path Linux has but our raw-usize
     code masked behind `len > 64`.

     Tests: five boot-time probes added inside the
     existing sethostname / setdomainname validation
     block.  All use name = NULL so the success path
     after the len gate is gate 2 (`name_ptr==0 &&
     len!=0`) -> EFAULT, which discriminates cleanly
     from the pre-batch raw-len EINVAL.
       (a) sethostname(NULL, 0x1_0000_0001) -> EFAULT
           (was EINVAL).  Primary discriminator.
       (a') setdomainname(NULL, 0x1_0000_0001) ->
           EFAULT (was EINVAL).
       (b) sethostname(NULL, 0x1_0000_0040) -> EFAULT
           (was EINVAL).  Boundary at low = 64
           (maximum valid len).
       (c) sethostname(NULL, 0x1_0000_0041) -> EINVAL
           preserved (low = 65 > 64).
       (d) sethostname(NULL, 0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_FFFF)
           -> EINVAL preserved, now via the new
           `len_i32 < 0` arm (was raw `> 64`).
       (d') setdomainname(NULL, 0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_FFFF)
           -> EINVAL preserved (same path).
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   set{host,domain}name
              len int-truncation (high-half
              ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing sethostname/setdomainname
     probes still pass:
       - sethostname(NULL, 65) -> EINVAL.
       - sethostname(NULL, 5)  -> EFAULT.
       - sethostname(NULL, 0)  -> EINVAL (empty name
         rejected, batch 57).
       - setdomainname(NULL, 0) -> 0 (clear-domain).
       - hostname/domain round-trip via nameservice
         (set+get with restore).

     Known limitations:
     - `name_ptr` (arg0) is a user pointer (u64);
       no truncation issue.
     - The "empty hostname rejected with EINVAL"
       deviation from Linux (which accepts len=0)
       predates this batch and is preserved; the
       nameservice refuses empty strings.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (36.86s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

367. Batch 337 — sys_faccessat2 `flags` int
     truncation (high-half ignored).

     Divergence: Linux's prototype is
       SYSCALL_DEFINE4(faccessat2,
                       int, dfd,
                       const char __user *, filename,
                       int, mode,
                       int, flags)
     where `flags` is `int`, delivered in r10.  The
     AMD64 syscall ABI does not zero- or sign-extend
     `int` args before issuing syscall, so only the
     low 32 bits of `flags` are defined; the high
     half is caller-supplied garbage.

     Pre-batch sys_faccessat2 held `flags` as raw
     u64:
       let flags = args.arg3;
       const VALID_FLAGS: u64 = AT_EACCESS
                              | AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW
                              | AT_EMPTY_PATH;
       if flags & !VALID_FLAGS != 0 { EINVAL }
     High-bit sentinels whose low 32 encoded a valid
     flag set (e.g. arg3 = 0x1_0000_0200, low =
     AT_EACCESS = 0x200) tripped the raw mask and
     surfaced EINVAL where Linux truncates to 0x200,
     passes the mask, and falls through to the path
     lookup (ENOENT in our empty-FS context).

     Fix: cast `args.arg3` through i32 then to u32
     (mirroring Linux's `int flags` signature with
     standard sign-loss/wrap allows), narrow
     `VALID_FLAGS` to u32, run the mask check at
     32-bit width.  `mode` (arg2) was already cast
     to u32 from a prior batch, so no change there.

     Tests: four boot-time probes added inside the
     existing access/faccessat/faccessat2 block,
     all using arg1=0x1000 (passes
     validate_user_read in kernel context) so the
     post-mask success path lands at the terminal
     ENOENT — discriminating cleanly from EINVAL.
       (a) flags=0x1_0000_0000 (low=0, no flags)
           -> ENOENT (was EINVAL pre-batch).
       (b) flags=0x1_0000_0200 (low=AT_EACCESS) ->
           ENOENT (was EINVAL).  Primary post-
           truncation discriminator — low 32 bits
           encode a valid flag.
       (c) flags=0x1_0000_0080 (low=0x80 unknown
           bit) -> EINVAL preserved (low half has
           an invalid bit so the mask check still
           fires post-truncation).
       (d) flags=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0000 (all-high,
           low=0) -> ENOENT (was EINVAL).  Same
           discriminator as (a) with a different
           high-bit pattern.
     Marker: "[syscall/linux]   faccessat2 flags
              int-truncation (high-half ignored):
              OK".

     Preserved: existing access / faccessat /
     faccessat2 probes still pass:
       - access(_, 8) -> EINVAL (bogus mode bit).
       - access(NULL, _) -> EFAULT.
       - access(0x1000, _) -> ENOENT.
       - faccessat(_, NULL, _, _) -> EFAULT.
       - faccessat2(_, 0x1000, _, 0x800_0000) ->
         EINVAL (bogus flag bit, low half).
       - faccessat2(_, 0x1000, _, 0x200) ->
         ENOENT (valid AT_EACCESS).

     Known limitations:
     - `dfd` (arg0) is `int` but is unused in our
       stub (we always return ENOENT regardless of
       dfd lookup), so its truncation is unobservable.
     - `mode` (arg2) is `int` in Linux but already
       held at u32 width here (`args.arg2 as u32`).
       The same low-32 mask check applies — no
       further fix needed in this batch.
     - sys_faccessat (no '2') explicitly ignores its
       flags argument per Linux's historical behaviour
       (the bug that motivated faccessat2 in 5.8), so
       there is no truncation issue to fix there.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (41.11s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

368. sys_io_uring_register nr_args u32-truncation
     (high-half ignored).

     Divergence: Linux declares
       int io_uring_register(unsigned int fd,
                             unsigned int opcode,
                             void *arg,
                             unsigned int nr_args)
     so `nr_args` is a C `unsigned int` delivered in
     r10 by the x86_64 syscall ABI.  The AMD64 ABI
     does NOT zero-extend `unsigned int` register
     arguments — only the low 32 bits of r10 are
     defined.  Pre-batch our stub held nr_args at
     raw u64 (`if args.arg3 > 0 && args.arg2 != 0`
     ... `if args.arg3 > 0 && args.arg2 == 0`), so a
     probe like nr_args=0x1_0000_0000 with arg=NULL
     entered the "nr_args>0 && arg==NULL" EFAULT
     branch.  Linux's truncated nr_args=0 instead
     skips the pointer block entirely and falls
     through to the fd-validation gate, returning
     EBADF for the fd=0 we're testing against (no
     io_uring ring can exist in this kernel).

     Fix: in fn sys_io_uring_register
     (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~line 12528),
     truncate args.arg3 to u32 before any compare:

       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let nr_args = args.arg3 as u32;
       if nr_args > 0 && args.arg2 != 0 { ... }
       if nr_args > 0 && args.arg2 == 0 {
           return linux_err(errno::EFAULT);
       }

     This is the same shape applied to io_uring_enter
     flags in batch 285 and to faccessat2 flags in
     batch 337 — mask to declared C-type width before
     any control-flow comparison.

     Tests: four boot-time probes in dispatch_linux's
     io_uring block (labels k/l/m/n), all with fd=0:
       (k) nr_args=0x1_0000_0000, arg=NULL ->
           EBADF (was EFAULT) — primary discriminator.
       (l) nr_args=0x1_0000_0001, arg=NULL ->
           EFAULT preserved (low-half nr_args=1 still
           fires the EFAULT-on-NULL gate).
       (m) nr_args=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0000, arg=NULL ->
           EBADF (was EFAULT) — all-high-half
           discriminator.
       (n) nr_args=0, arg=NULL -> EBADF baseline
           preservation (skips pointer block, fd=0
           hits the EBADF gate).
     Marker: "io_uring_register nr_args u32-truncation
              (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved: existing io_uring_register probes
     (g/h/i/j) still pass — they exercise fd validation,
     the 31-opcode cap, the USE_REGISTERED_RING bit, and
     the nr_args=4/arg=NULL EFAULT gate respectively.

     Known limitations:
     - `opcode` (arg1) is already cast to u32 via
       `raw_op = args.arg1 as u32` at the head of the
       function, so its truncation was already handled.
     - `fd` (arg0) is cast via the standard
       `args.arg0 as i32` pattern further down.
     - We cannot exercise the "non-NULL bogus arg
       pointer with truncated-zero nr_args" case from
       a kernel-context probe: validate_user_read in
       the kernel always returns Ok, so even if the
       comparison were wrong the EFAULT wouldn't
       fire in self-test.  Only userspace callers
       exercise that combination.
     - The opcode-specific struct-size checks (LINUX's
       per-op `struct io_uring_*` validation) are
       deferred until the io_uring dispatch path is
       implemented; until then we use a 1-byte minimum
       read just to surface EFAULT on bogus pointers.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (36.37s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

369. sys_rseq rseq_len/flags u32-truncation
     (high-half ignored).

     Divergence: Linux declares
       SYSCALL_DEFINE4(rseq, struct rseq __user *, rseq,
                      u32, rseq_len, int, flags, u32, sig)
     so `rseq_len` is C `u32` (rsi) and `flags` is C
     `int` (rdx) — both 32-bit C types delivered in
     64-bit registers by the AMD64 syscall ABI, which
     does NOT zero/sign-extend across the syscall
     instruction.  Linux's kernel/rseq.c body only
     ever sees the low 32 bits of each.  Pre-batch
     our stub held both at raw u64
       (`let len = args.arg1; let flags = args.arg2;`),
     so probes with sentinel high bits hit the wrong
     EINVAL gates:
       - rseq_len=0x1_0000_0020 (high|valid 32):
         Linux truncates to 32, passes `len != 32`,
         advances to ptr-check / register path.
         Pre-batch: len != 32 -> EINVAL.
       - flags=0x1_0000_0000 (high-only):
         Linux truncates to 0, register path.
         Pre-batch: flags != 0 -> EINVAL.
       - flags=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0000 (all-high, low=0):
         same shape — Linux truncates to 0, advances.
         Pre-batch: EINVAL via the same gate.

     Fix: in fn sys_rseq (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
     line ~18728), truncate both args to their declared
     C-type widths before any compare:

       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let len_u32 = args.arg1 as u32;
       let len: u64 = u64::from(len_u32);
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let flags_u32 = args.arg2 as u32;
       let flags: u64 = u64::from(flags_u32);

     Widening back to u64 after truncation keeps the
     downstream comparisons (`len != RSEQ_STRUCT_SIZE`,
     `flags & RSEQ_FLAG_UNREGISTER`, `stored_len !=
     u64::from(len)`) unchanged.  Same shape used in
     batches 335 (sched_setscheduler policy) and 337
     (faccessat2 flags) — cast at entry, re-widen for
     the shared helper.

     Tests: four boot-time probes appended after the
     existing rseq cleanup block (labels o/p/q/r), all
     with ptr=NULL so the EINVAL→EFAULT discriminator
     surfaces without touching task registration state:
       (o) rseq_len=0x1_0000_0020, flags=0, ptr=NULL
           -> EFAULT (was EINVAL) — len high-half
           discriminator.
       (p) rseq_len=0x1_0000_0010, flags=0, ptr=NULL
           -> EINVAL preserved (low-half len=16 still
           fails `len != 32`).
       (q) rseq_len=32, flags=0x1_0000_0000, ptr=NULL
           -> EFAULT (was EINVAL) — flags high-only
           discriminator.
       (r) rseq_len=32, flags=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0000,
           ptr=NULL -> EFAULT (was EINVAL) — flags
           all-high discriminator.
     Marker: "rseq rseq_len/flags u32-truncation
              (high-half ignored): OK".

     Preserved: all existing rseq probes still pass —
     wrong-len EINVAL (b), bad-flags EINVAL with low-
     half bit 1 (which is NOT RSEQ_FLAG_UNREGISTER),
     NULL EFAULT, unaligned EINVAL, unregister-fresh
     EINVAL, register-valid 0, double-register EBUSY,
     sig-mismatch EPERM, ptr-mismatch EINVAL,
     unregister-sig-mismatch EPERM, unregister-ptr-
     mismatch EINVAL, unregister-match 0, re-register-
     after-unregister 0, cleanup-unregister 0.  Cpu_id
     /node_id zeroing assertion on register also
     unchanged.

     Known limitations:
     - `sig` (arg3) was already truncated to u32 in
       the previous pass.  No further change needed.
     - The unregister-path interaction with high-half
       flags (0x1_0000_0001 = high|UNREGISTER bit) is
       still rejected as EINVAL — but it now collapses
       to the same low-half flags=1 EINVAL path because
       the register table is empty in self-test.  Once
       a future userspace-context test path lands, the
       UNREGISTER-with-real-registration case will
       benefit from the same truncation.  No code
       change needed there; the cast already covers
       the unregister gate's `flags & !RSEQ_FLAG_
       UNREGISTER != 0` check.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (35.66s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh --no-build
                --timeout=360 PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

370. sys_unshare int-flags truncation (high-half
     ignored).

     Divergence: Linux's userspace ABI declares
       int unshare(int flags)
     (kernel-side SYSCALL_DEFINE1(unshare, unsigned
     long, unshare_flags) takes unsigned long, but the
     glibc/musl wrapper sign-extends an int into the
     register, so the kernel function effectively
     observes only the low 32 bits for any libc
     caller).  The CLONE_* flag mask lives in the low
     32 bits in both clone() and unshare(); high-half
     register bits are caller-controlled garbage that
     must be stripped before the `& !VALID_FLAGS`
     check.  Pre-batch our stub did
       `if args.arg0 & !VALID_FLAGS != 0 { EINVAL; }`
     at full u64 width, so probes with sentinel high
     bits hit the wrong gate:
       - flags=0x1_0000_0000 (high|0): Linux
         truncates to 0, takes the unshare(0) success
         arm and returns ok(0).  Pre-batch: the high
         bit was outside VALID_FLAGS -> EINVAL.
       - flags=0x1_0000_0400 (high|CLONE_FILES):
         Linux truncates to CLONE_FILES, mask passes,
         falls through to EPERM (unprivileged).  Pre-
         batch: EINVAL via the same high-bit gate.
       - flags=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0000 (all-high, low
         zero): Linux truncates to 0, success arm.
         Pre-batch: EINVAL.

     Fix: in fn sys_unshare (kernel/src/syscall/
     linux.rs line ~14196), cast at entry to restore
     the ABI's 32-bit view and re-widen for the mask
     compare:

       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation,
              clippy::cast_possible_wrap)]
       let flags_i32 = args.arg0 as i32;
       #[allow(clippy::cast_sign_loss)]
       let flags: u64 = u64::from(flags_i32 as u32);
       if flags & !VALID_FLAGS != 0 { EINVAL; }
       if flags == 0 { return ok(0); }
       EPERM

     Re-widening to u64 after truncation keeps the
     downstream mask check and the `flags == 0`
     short-circuit unchanged.  Same shape used in
     batches 317 (umount2), 335 (sched_setscheduler
     policy) and 337 (faccessat2 flags) — cast at
     entry, re-widen for the comparison.

     Tests: four boot-time probes appended after the
     existing unshare(CLONE_FILES) EPERM test in the
     self-test block:
       (a) flags=0x1_0000_0000 -> 0 (was EINVAL).
           High-only discriminator: truncates to 0,
           unshare(0) success path.
       (b) flags=0x1_0000_0400 -> EPERM (was EINVAL).
           High|CLONE_FILES: truncates to CLONE_FILES,
           mask passes, terminal EPERM.
       (c) flags=0x1_0000_0008 -> EINVAL preserved.
           High|bit-3 (not in VALID_FLAGS): mask
           rejects the truncated low half — confirms
           the post-cast EINVAL gate still fires for
           genuinely invalid bits.
       (d) flags=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0000 -> 0 (was
           EINVAL).  All-high, low=0: truncates to 0,
           success.
     Marker: "unshare int flags truncation: OK".

     Preserved: all existing unshare probes pass —
     unshare(0)=0, unshare(0x8)=EINVAL (bit-3 outside
     mask), unshare(CLONE_FILES)=EPERM.

     Known limitations: none.  The unshare stub
     remains terminal-EPERM for every nonzero valid
     flag combination because we do not implement
     namespace unsharing; once the namespace
     subsystem lands, the per-flag dispatch will see
     the same truncated value and the high-half
     stripping will continue to behave correctly.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (34.83s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 23s).

371. syslog int-truncation + restricted-permission
     gate restructure (batch 341).

     Divergences fixed:

     (i) `len` truncation.  Linux SYSCALL_DEFINE3-
     (syslog, int, type, char __user *, buf, int,
     len) declares len as C `int`, delivered in rdx.
     The AMD64 syscall ABI does not zero/sign-extend
     `int` args before the syscall instruction, so
     only the low 32 bits are defined.  Pre-batch
     we held len as raw `u64`/`usize`:
       * len=0x1_0000_0000 (high|0) on typ=3 -> ours
         treated as ~4 GiB, tripping the buf-NULL +
         len>0 branch and returning EFAULT.  Linux
         truncates to len_i32=0 -> 0-byte read OK.
       * len=0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_FFFF (all-high; Linux
         "-1") on typ=3 -> ours treated as huge
         `usize` -> validate path errors.  Linux:
         len_i32=-1 -> `len < 0` gate -> EINVAL.

     (ii) Restricted/non-restricted partition.
     Linux kernel/printk/printk.c::check_syslog_-
     permissions runs BEFORE do_syslog's type switch
     and rejects any restricted type without
     CAP_SYSLOG / CAP_SYS_ADMIN with -EPERM.  With
     dmesg_restrict=0 (default), syslog_action_-
     restricted(type) returns true for every type
     except SYSLOG_ACTION_READ_ALL (3) and
     SYSLOG_ACTION_SIZE_BUFFER (10).  This kernel
     does not track CAP_SYSLOG per-process - every
     Linux-ABI caller is effectively unprivileged -
     so the truthful answer for any restricted type
     is -EPERM.  Pre-batch we returned 0 for typ=
     6/7/9 (Linux: EPERM) and EPERM for typ=3
     (Linux: 0/EINVAL/EFAULT depending on buf/len).
     Both partitions were wrong; this batch
     restructures the gates to match Linux's order.

     Errno table for unprivileged callers (buf=
     valid, len=small valid):

       typ  pre-batch  linux        post-batch
        0    EPERM      EPERM        EPERM   match
        1    EPERM      EPERM        EPERM   match
        2    EPERM*     EPERM        EPERM   match
        3    EPERM      0/EINVAL/    0/EINVAL/
                       EFAULT       EFAULT  match
        4    EPERM*     EPERM        EPERM   match
        5    EPERM      EPERM        EPERM   match
        6    0          EPERM        EPERM   fixed
        7    0          EPERM        EPERM   fixed
        8    EPERM      EPERM        EPERM   match
        9    0          EPERM        EPERM   fixed
       10    0          +ve klog     0       approximate
                       buf size              (no klog
                                              buffer
                                              exists)

       * = pre-batch typ=2/4 could short-circuit to
           EFAULT when buf=NULL/len>0 before reaching
           the terminal EPERM; Linux's permission
           check wins first.

     Fix (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_syslog):

       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation,
               clippy::cast_possible_wrap)]
       let typ = args.arg0 as i32;
       if !(0..=10).contains(&typ) {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }
       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation,
               clippy::cast_possible_wrap)]
       let len_i32 = args.arg2 as i32;

       let restricted = !matches!(typ, 3 | 10);
       if restricted {
           return linux_err(errno::EPERM);
       }

       match typ {
           3 => {
               if args.arg1 == 0 || len_i32 < 0 {
                   return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
               }
               if len_i32 == 0 {
                   return SyscallResult::ok(0);
               }
               let len_u = len_i32 as usize;
               if let Err(e) = crate::mm::user::
                   validate_user_write(args.arg1,
                                       len_u) {
                   return linux_err(
                       linux_errno_for(e));
               }
               SyscallResult::ok(0)
           }
           10 => SyscallResult::ok(0),
           _ => linux_err(errno::EINVAL),
       }

     Existing self-test updates:
       * syslog(6) -> EPERM (was 0).
       * syslog(2, NULL, 16) -> EPERM (was EFAULT;
         old expectation was a pre-perm buf-NULL
         shortcut that does not match Linux's gate
         order).

     New batch-341 probes (4 added):
       (a) syslog(3, NULL, 0) -> EINVAL
           [READ_ALL unrestricted + buf-NULL gate;
            was EPERM via terminal arm].
       (b) syslog(3, NULL, 0x1_0000_0000) -> EINVAL
           [len high|0 truncates to 0; was EFAULT
            from huge-len + buf-NULL shortcut].
       (c) syslog(3, 0xDEAD_BEEF, 0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_-
           FFFF) -> EINVAL
           [len all-high truncates to -1 (signed);
            was EPERM via terminal arm after huge-
            len validate succeeded in kernel
            context].
       (d) syslog(10, 0, 0) -> 0 preserved
           [SIZE_BUFFER unrestricted sanity check].

     Marker: "syslog int truncation + perm gate: OK".

     Known limitations:

     * typ=10 (SIZE_BUFFER) returns 0 rather than a
       positive integer.  Linux returns the kernel
       log buffer size (a compile-time constant,
       e.g. 131072 for CONFIG_LOG_BUF_SHIFT=17).
       We have no klog buffer at all, so 0 is the
       truthful "no buffer available" signal.
       Userspace dmesg-style tools that size their
       read buffer from this value will either fall
       back to a default or skip the read; both are
       acceptable for a kernel that exposes no
       Linux-shaped log.

     * typ=3 (READ_ALL) with valid buf+len returns
       0 (success, 0 bytes copied).  Linux returns
       the number of bytes written to buf; we have
       no klog content to copy, so 0 is truthful.
       If a future change adds a Linux-shaped klog
       buffer (currently the design directs all
       structured logging through native channels,
       so this is unlikely), this arm will need to
       actually copy log content.

     * No dmesg_restrict modelling.  Linux exposes a
       sysctl that flips every type to "restricted"
       when set.  We hardcode the dmesg_restrict=0
       partition; if a Linux probe explicitly
       requests dmesg_restrict=1 via sysctl, our
       layer will not honour it.  Architectural
       directive: native logging does not pass
       through sysctl, so this is intentional - the
       translator layer reflects the default kernel
       configuration, not every tunable.

     * No CAP_SYSLOG / CAP_SYS_ADMIN tracking.  All
       Linux-ABI callers in this kernel are
       effectively unprivileged from syslog(2)'s
       point of view.  This is the architectural
       directive in action: the Linux compat layer
       does not reshape the native capability model
       to satisfy Linux's caps.  A future cap-aware
       Linux process would still see EPERM for
       restricted types.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (35.49s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 25s).

372. Batch 342 - Linux ABI sys_init_module /
     sys_finit_module / sys_delete_module gate-order
     inversion fix: EPERM must be the terminal-and-
     only answer.

     Divergence (kernel/module/main.c, modern
     mainline):

       SYSCALL_DEFINE3(init_module, void __user *, umod,
                       unsigned long, len,
                       const char __user *, uargs)
       {
           int err;
           err = may_init_module();
           if (err) return err;     // (1) EPERM
           err = copy_module_from_user(umod, len, &info);
           if (err) return err;     // (2) EFAULT / ENOEXEC
           ...
       }
       static int may_init_module(void) {
           if (!capable(CAP_SYS_MODULE) || modules_disabled)
               return -EPERM;
           return 0;
       }

     Same gate-1-first pattern for finit_module and
     delete_module.

     Pre-batch errno table (kernel-context probe;
     userspace probes inherit the same pattern):

       Probe                              Linux  Pre-batch  Post-batch
       ---------------------------------  -----  ---------  ----------
       init_module(NULL, 1, NULL)         EPERM  EFAULT     EPERM
       init_module(0x1000, 0, NULL)       EPERM  ENOEXEC    EPERM
       init_module(0x1000, 63, NULL)      EPERM  ENOEXEC    EPERM
       init_module(0x1000, 64, NULL)      EPERM  EPERM      EPERM
       finit_module(0, 0, 0x80000000)     EPERM  EINVAL     EPERM
       finit_module(0, 0, 0)              EPERM  EPERM      EPERM
       delete_module(NULL, 0)             EPERM  EFAULT     EPERM
       delete_module(name, 0x80000000)    EPERM  EINVAL     EPERM

     Pre-batch we ran:
       sys_init_module: NULL umod -> EFAULT;
                        len < sizeof(Elf64_Ehdr) -> ENOEXEC;
                        bad umod range -> EFAULT;
                        bad uargs -> EFAULT;
                        then EPERM.
       sys_finit_module: flag mask check -> EINVAL;
                         validate_linux_fd -> EBADF (userspace);
                         bad uargs -> EFAULT;
                         then EPERM.
       sys_delete_module: spurious flag mask check
                          (Linux does NOT impose one
                          here - only inspects O_NONBLOCK
                          for blocking vs non-blocking
                          mode) -> EINVAL;
                          NULL name -> EFAULT;
                          bad name range -> EFAULT;
                          then EPERM.

     Linux validates CAP_SYS_MODULE BEFORE touching
     any user pointer or any flag bit.  The errno
     selection matters because module loaders
     (modprobe, dkms, systemd-modules-load) branch
     on it.  EPERM signals "the caller is
     unprivileged, abort and tell the user to use
     sudo".  EFAULT / ENOEXEC / EINVAL all signal
     "this specific file/argument is bad, try the
     next candidate" - triggering fallback loops
     that mangle paths, swap module names, or rebuild
     the kernel info cache.  None of those retries
     can ever succeed; they just burn CPU and
     occasionally produce confusing error chains in
     userspace logs.

     Fix: each of the three syscalls is reduced to
     `linux_err(errno::EPERM)`.  Architectural
     directive: we are a microkernel.  There is no
     loadable-module subsystem and there never will
     be (the design-review explicitly forbids
     hosting a Linux LKM loader inside the kernel
     address space).  No caller can therefore ever
     hold CAP_SYS_MODULE; Linux's gate-1 always
     trips.  Returning EPERM unconditionally is both
     the simplest correct mirror of Linux's userspace
     experience AND a structurally honest report of
     our native capability state.

     Code changes:
       kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
         sys_init_module: body replaced with EPERM-
           only; arg validation removed (unreachable
           after Linux's gate-1).
         sys_finit_module: body replaced with EPERM-
           only; flag mask check, fd validation, and
           uargs pointer check removed.
         sys_delete_module: body replaced with EPERM-
           only; spurious flag mask check (not
           present in Linux), NULL name check, and
           name pointer validation removed.

     Self-tests (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
       Updated existing probes that previously
       expected EFAULT / ENOEXEC / EINVAL to now
       expect EPERM.  Marker:
       "init/finit/delete_module gate-order EPERM-
        first: OK"

       Probe coverage:
         (a) init_module(NULL, 1, NULL): EFAULT ->
             EPERM.
         (b) init_module(0x1000, 0, NULL): ENOEXEC
             -> EPERM.
         (c) init_module(0x1000, 63, NULL): ENOEXEC
             -> EPERM.
         (d) init_module(0x1000, 64, NULL): EPERM
             stays EPERM (boundary).
         (e) finit_module(0, 0, 0x8000_0000):
             EINVAL -> EPERM (unknown flag bit
             stops mattering).
         (f) finit_module(0, 0, 0): EPERM stays
             EPERM (baseline).
         (g) delete_module(NULL, 0): EFAULT ->
             EPERM.
         (h) delete_module(name=stack, 0x8000_0000):
             EINVAL -> EPERM (spurious flag-mask
             gate removed).

     Prior batch 302 high-half flag-truncation tests
     for finit_module / delete_module still pass:
     the post-truncation flag value of 0 now bypasses
     the (now-removed) flag-mask gate entirely and
     reaches the same terminal EPERM via the direct
     path.

     Known limitations / judgment calls:

     * Module image inspection is gone.  Linux,
       under CAP_SYS_MODULE, would proceed to copy
       the module image from user memory and run
       ELF validation - returning ENOEXEC for short
       headers, EINVAL for bad section tables, etc.
       Since we never enter that path, those errnos
       are unreachable from userspace.  Architectural
       directive: this is intentional - we don't
       parse Linux module images at all, so any
       Linux-specific errno from inside the loader
       would be a fabrication.

     * delete_module flag mask gone.  Linux only
       inspects O_NONBLOCK for blocking semantics
       and accepts every other flag bit silently
       (there are no flag-mask EINVAL errors in
       delete_module).  Our pre-batch EINVAL on
       unknown flag bits was an outright divergence
       on top of the gate-order inversion; removing
       it actually tightens the Linux mirror.

     * No CAP_SYS_MODULE / modules_disabled tracking
       or any uid-based pre-EPERM modelling.  Linux
       userspace running as root with CAP_SYS_MODULE
       would clear gate-1 and proceed to the loader
       (which would then likely fail with ENOSYS-
       equivalent had Linux not implemented modules
       at all).  In our environment no caller ever
       holds CAP_SYS_MODULE so the unconditional
       EPERM is observationally identical for every
       Linux process we'd ever run.

     * Modprobe / dkms behaviour: with EPERM as the
       terminal answer, fallback loops in these tools
       will abort cleanly.  A future "modprobe -n"
       (dry-run) would still complete because dry
       runs do not invoke init_module; only the live
       load path is affected.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.32s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

373. Batch 343 - Linux ABI sys_pivot_root /
     sys_swapoff gate-order inversion fix: EPERM
     must be the terminal-and-only answer.

     Same divergence shape as batch 342 (module
     syscalls).  Linux's pivot_root and swapoff
     both validate CAP_SYS_ADMIN before any
     user-pointer touch, but pre-batch we ran
     EFAULT-on-NULL / EFAULT-on-bad-range first.

     Linux source (modern mainline):

       SYSCALL_DEFINE2(pivot_root,
                       const char __user *, new_root,
                       const char __user *, put_old)
       {
           ...
           if (!may_mount())               // CAP_SYS_ADMIN
               return -EPERM;
           error = user_path_at(AT_FDCWD,
                                new_root, ..., &new);
           if (error)
               goto out0;                  // EFAULT / ENOENT
           ...
       }

       SYSCALL_DEFINE1(swapoff,
                       const char __user *, specialfile)
       {
           ...
           if (!capable(CAP_SYS_ADMIN))
               return -EPERM;
           BUG_ON(!current->mm);
           pathname = getname(specialfile);
           if (IS_ERR(pathname))
               return PTR_ERR(pathname);
           ...
       }

     Note: sys_swapon is unaffected.  Linux's
     SYSCALL_DEFINE2(swapon) validates the
     swap_flags mask BEFORE the CAP gate (the only
     Linux syscall in this family that does so),
     which we already mirror.  Only swapoff has
     the CAP-first ordering among the swap calls.

     Pre-batch errno table:

       Probe                          Linux  Pre-batch  Post-batch
       -----------------------------  -----  ---------  ----------
       pivot_root(NULL, NULL)         EPERM  EFAULT     EPERM
       pivot_root(NULL, valid)        EPERM  EFAULT     EPERM
       pivot_root(valid, NULL)        EPERM  EFAULT     EPERM
       pivot_root(valid, valid)       EPERM  EPERM      EPERM
       swapoff(NULL)                  EPERM  EFAULT     EPERM
       swapoff(valid)                 EPERM  EPERM      EPERM

     Same fail-fast rationale as batch 342: sandbox
     setup code that uses pivot_root as a CAP_SYS_
     ADMIN probe needs EPERM to decide "abort, the
     caller is unprivileged".  Swap-management
     daemons (swapd, systemd-swap-generator) likewise
     branch on EPERM vs EFAULT.

     Fix: both bodies reduced to
     `linux_err(errno::EPERM)`.  Architectural
     directive: we have no swap subsystem, no
     per-mount-namespace user-ns model, and no
     caller holds CAP_SYS_ADMIN.  Linux's gate-1
     always trips.

     Code changes:
       kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
         sys_pivot_root: body replaced with EPERM-
           only; NULL-OR check and two
           validate_user_read calls removed.
         sys_swapoff: body replaced with EPERM-
           only; NULL check and validate_user_read
           call removed.

     Self-tests (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
       pivot_root probes (a..d): NULL+NULL, NULL+
         valid, valid+NULL, valid+valid - all EPERM.
         Marker:
         "pivot_root gate-order EPERM-first: OK"
       swapoff probes: valid path stays EPERM
         (baseline); NULL probe now EPERM.  Marker:
         "swapoff gate-order EPERM-first: OK"

       The pre-existing test
       `pivot_root(NULL, NULL) -> EFAULT` at line
       ~42797 was updated in-place to expect EPERM;
       no other existing probe needed adjustment.

     Known limitations / judgment calls:

     * Pointer-validity feedback removed for these
       two calls.  Userspace can no longer learn
       via pivot_root or swapoff whether a specific
       address is mapped - Linux doesn't offer
       this either.  Userspace tools that want to
       probe page presence should use mincore() or
       msync() instead, which we already implement.

     * No CAP_SYS_ADMIN modelling.  All Linux-ABI
       callers in this kernel are effectively
       unprivileged from these syscalls' point of
       view - intentional under the architectural
       directive.  A future cap-aware Linux process
       would still see EPERM here for the same
       structural reason (no swap, no
       pivot-namespace).

     * swapoff vs swapon asymmetry preserved.
       Linux's pre-CAP swap_flags mask in swapon
       is observationally important (lets unpriv
       callers learn about new flag bits as kernels
       upgrade); we keep mirroring that.  swapoff
       has no equivalent because it takes no flag
       argument.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (37.10s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

374. syscall/linux batch 344 — kexec_load and
     kexec_file_load EPERM-first gate inversion.

     Linux gate-order facts (from
     kernel/kexec_core.c, kernel/kexec.c,
     kernel/kexec_file.c, modern mainline):

       sys_kexec_load:
         gate 1: kexec_load_check()
                 -> kexec_load_permitted()
                    -> capable(CAP_SYS_BOOT)
                    -> kexec_load_disabled check
                 returns EPERM on fail.
         gate 2: LSM hook (also EPERM-class).
         gate 3: flags & ~KEXEC_FLAGS  -> EINVAL.
         gate 4: arch-image-type mask  -> EINVAL.
         gate 5: nr_segments > KEXEC_SEGMENT_MAX
                 -> EINVAL.
         gate 6: copy_from_user segments
                 -> EFAULT.

       sys_kexec_file_load:
         gate 1: kexec_load_check(0, flags)
                 -> kexec_load_permitted (EPERM).
         gate 2: LSM hook (EPERM-class).
         gate 3: flags & ~KEXEC_FILE_FLAGS
                 -> EINVAL.
         gate 4: fdget(kernel_fd) check -> EBADF.
         gate 5: copy_from_user cmdline -> EFAULT.

     Our pre-batch behaviour:

       sys_kexec_load:
         - returned EINVAL for any unknown flag
           BEFORE checking the (always-unauth)
           CAP_SYS_BOOT gate
         - returned EFAULT for NULL segments
           BEFORE the CAP gate
         - never reached EPERM unless every other
           validation passed

       sys_kexec_file_load:
         - returned EBADF for bad kernel_fd
           BEFORE the CAP gate
         - returned EFAULT for NULL cmdline BEFORE
           the CAP gate
         - returned EINVAL for reserved flag bits
           BEFORE the CAP gate

     Why this matters for userspace porting:

       kexec-tools, fwupd's UEFI capsule
       fallback, kdump initialisers, and various
       crash-dump bootstrap scripts use the errno
       from a probe kexec_load(0, 0, NULL, 0) (or
       kexec_file_load with a known-bad fd) to
       decide whether the kernel supports kexec
       at all on this host.  The expected
       triage is:

         EPERM   -> kexec is compiled in and
                    callable, this caller just
                    lacks CAP_SYS_BOOT.  Abort
                    politely.
         ENOSYS  -> kexec stripped from this
                    kernel build, skip silently.
         EINVAL/EFAULT/EBADF -> probe was
                    malformed, retry with a
                    different shape.

       Returning EINVAL for an unknown flag bit
       BEFORE the CAP check is the worst possible
       outcome: it tricks the probe into a retry
       loop trying combinations of flag bits.
       Returning EFAULT for NULL segments tricks
       the probe into allocating a real segment
       buffer to "fix" the EFAULT before learning
       it never had the cap to begin with.

       Returning EPERM unconditionally is
       structurally honest: the native OS has no
       kexec subsystem, will never have a kexec
       subsystem (it conflicts with the
       capability-microkernel model where
       boot-time secrets live in the
       capability namespace and a soft-boot via
       a wholly different image would invalidate
       every live cap), and no Linux-ABI caller
       in this kernel will ever hold an
       equivalent of CAP_SYS_BOOT.

     Fix:

       sys_kexec_load body reduced to
         linux_err(errno::EPERM)
       with a multi-paragraph comment naming the
       gate order being mirrored and explicitly
       calling out which side-effects (flag
       mask, segment cap, fd validation) we
       deliberately omit because the EPERM gate
       short-circuits before them.

       sys_kexec_file_load body reduced to the
       same single line + comment.

     Self-test rewrite (11 probes, a..k):

         a. kexec_load(_, 99, _, _)           EPERM
         b. kexec_load(_, 1, NULL, 0)         EPERM
         c. kexec_load valid baseline         EPERM
         d. kexec_file_load bad kernel_fd     EPERM
         e. kexec_file_load NULL cmdline      EPERM
         f. kexec_file_load valid baseline    EPERM
         g. kexec_load reserved flag 0x4      EPERM
         h. kexec_load foreign arch 0x10000   EPERM
         i. kexec_load valid flags baseline   EPERM
         j. kexec_file_load reserved 0x10     EPERM
         k. kexec_file_load DEBUG 0x8         EPERM

       Probes (a), (b), (d), (e), (g), (h), (j)
       previously expected EINVAL/EFAULT/EBADF
       and have been updated to expect EPERM
       because those checks are now correctly
       short-circuited by the CAP gate.

     Marker: kexec_load/kexec_file_load
             gate-order EPERM-first: OK

     Known limitations / judgment calls:

     * No CAP_SYS_BOOT modelling.  Every
       Linux-ABI caller is effectively
       unprivileged for the kexec family.
       Intentional under the architectural
       directive: a capability-microkernel can
       never sanely re-host an arbitrary kernel
       image because the boot-time capability
       seed and the running cap namespace would
       have to be reconciled.

     * No kexec subsystem at all.  No image
       parsing, no segment loading, no purgatory,
       no reserved memory pool.  EPERM is
       structurally honest, not a stub-pending-
       implementation.

     * No KEXEC_FLAGS / KEXEC_FILE_FLAGS mask
       enforcement post-EPERM.  Linux also
       doesn't reach that gate for unprivileged
       callers, so we are observationally
       equivalent to upstream from the
       unprivileged caller's perspective.

     * No kexec_load_disabled bit.  Linux exposes
       /proc/sys/kernel/kexec_load_disabled which
       turns the EPERM into a permanent EPERM
       even for CAP_SYS_BOOT.  We are
       permanently in that mode by construction.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (36.94s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

375. syscall/linux batch 345 — sys_setgroups
     EPERM-before-EINVAL gate reorder.

     Linux gate-order facts
     (kernel/groups.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(setgroups),
     modern mainline):

       gate 1: may_setgroups()
               -> ns_capable(CAP_SETGID) +
                  setgroups_allowed user-ns flag
               returns EPERM on fail.
       gate 2: (unsigned)gidsetsize > NGROUPS_MAX
               returns EINVAL.
       gate 3: groups_alloc() failure -> ENOMEM.
       gate 4: copy_from_user list -> EFAULT.
       gate 5: groups_sort + set_current_groups
               -> 0.

     Our pre-batch order:

       1. (unsigned)gidsetsize > NGROUPS_MAX
          -> EINVAL.
       2. caller_pid() == None -> ok(0).
       3. credentials.uid != 0 -> EPERM.
       ... rest of body ...

     The gate-2-before-gate-1 inversion produced
     EINVAL where Linux produces EPERM for the
     "unprivileged caller, bad size" case.

     Why this matters for userspace porting:

       libc's setgroups(3) wrapper, newgrp(1),
       gpasswd(1), and various initscript
       privilege-drop helpers (start-stop-daemon,
       runuser, su) all use the errno from a
       single setgroups() call to drive a
       triage tree:

         EPERM   -> caller is not privileged.
                    Abort or fall back to a
                    different mechanism (setuid,
                    setresgid, etc).
         EINVAL  -> retry with a different
                    gidsetsize / sanitised list.
         ENOMEM  -> back off, try smaller list.
         EFAULT  -> caller bug, fix the pointer.

       Returning EINVAL where Linux returns
       EPERM tricks the probe into a retry loop
       trying different gidsetsize values to
       "find a size the kernel accepts," and the
       caller eventually times out or aborts
       with a misleading "invalid group list
       size" error.  Returning EPERM lets the
       caller branch into the proper privilege-
       drop path immediately.

     Fix:

       Restructured sys_setgroups body so the
       CAP_SETGID gate fires before the
       NGROUPS_MAX bound:

         1. truncate gidsetsize to i32 then to
            u32 (batch-306 contract preserved).
         2. CAP gate: if caller_pid() == Some(p)
            and PCB uid != 0 -> EPERM.
            (kernel context = None = bypass.)
         3. NGROUPS_MAX: u32 > 65536 -> EINVAL.
         4. kernel-context: pid_opt == None
            -> ok(0).
         5. PCB-missing race -> ok(0).
         6. size==0: clear groups, write back.
         7. list_ptr == 0 -> EFAULT.
         8. copy_from_user + set_credentials.

     Self-test (4 new probes, e..h, joining the
     batch-306 a..d truncation guards):

         e. setgroups(0, NULL)            ok(0)
         f. setgroups(NGROUPS_MAX,  NULL) ok(0)
         g. setgroups(NGROUPS_MAX+1,NULL) EINVAL
         h. setgroups(INT_MIN, NULL)      EINVAL

       Kernel-context cannot directly observe
       gate-1 firing because caller_pid() is
       None and the CAP gate bypasses.  Probes
       e..h instead lock in the post-reorder
       gate-2 behaviour as a regression guard
       against any future restructure that
       breaks NGROUPS_MAX while moving the CAP
       gate around.

     Marker: setgroups gate-order
             EPERM-before-EINVAL: OK

     Known limitations / judgment calls:

     * No setgroups_allowed user-namespace
       policy modelling.  Linux's may_setgroups
       also checks the per-user-ns
       "/proc/self/setgroups" flag, which can
       block CAP_SETGID even for root callers
       when a user-ns has been written
       "deny" to that file.  We don't have
       user-ns / setns plumbing, so the flag
       is implicitly "allow" for every caller
       that passes the uid==0 check.

     * Kernel-context cannot probe EPERM-first
       directly.  Would require either a fake
       caller_pid() injection harness or a
       proper userspace test runner; both are
       out of scope for a single tick.  The
       structural reorder is verifiable by
       reading the body in source.

     * PCB-missing race resolves to bypass.  If
       a userspace caller's PCB has been torn
       down between syscall entry and the
       cap check (e.g. concurrent kill), we
       treat the missing PCB as "no privilege
       to check, no state to write" and fall
       through to the kernel-context ok(0)
       arm.  Linux would not see this race
       because the PCB equivalent (task_struct)
       is pinned for the duration of the
       syscall by the current macro.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (36.14s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

376. syscall/linux batch 346 — sys_dup3
     RLIMIT_NOFILE gate added.

     Linux gate-order facts
     (fs/file.c::ksys_dup3, modern mainline):

       gate 1: flags & ~O_CLOEXEC -> EINVAL.
       gate 2: oldfd == newfd     -> EINVAL.
       gate 3: (unsigned)newfd >=
               rlimit(RLIMIT_NOFILE) -> EBADF.
       gate 4: fdget(oldfd), install at newfd.

     Pre-batch dup3 implemented only gates 1, 2,
     and 4 — gate 3 was missing entirely.  A
     caller could install at any newfd value as
     long as the underlying fd table allocator
     could grow that far, defeating per-process
     fd budgeting in:

       - sandbox runtimes that lower
         RLIMIT_NOFILE before exec to bound the
         child's fd footprint;
       - posix_spawn fd-actions arrays that
         pre-stage fds in slots above the
         lowered limit and expect dup3 to
         refuse;
       - init-system / service-manager fd
         allocators that use RLIMIT_NOFILE as a
         per-service quota;
       - language-runtime fd budgets (Go, Rust
         tokio, libuv) that probe with dup3 to
         high newfd to learn the cap.

     Pre-batch listed in the "Deferred" section
     of prior batches' Known Limitations.

     Fix:

       Inserted gate 3 in sys_dup3 between the
       oldfd==newfd check and the dup2_impl
       call:

         if let Some(pid) = caller_pid() {
             if let Some((cur, _)) =
                 pcb::get_rlimit(pid, 7) {
                 if newfd >= 0
                     && (newfd as u64) >= cur {
                     return linux_err(EBADF);
                 }
             }
         }

       Inclusive upper bound (>=, not >) to
       mirror Linux exactly.  Negative newfd
       falls through to dup2_impl which returns
       EBADF (same errno, different routing).
       Kernel-context (caller_pid() = None)
       bypasses the gate so boot self-test
       dispatch stays usable; dup2_impl's None
       arm returns EBADF anyway.

     Self-test (4 new probes, i..l):

         i. dup3(0, 1, 0) kernel-ctx     EBADF
         j. dup3(2, 1M, 0) kernel-ctx    EBADF
         k. set_rlimit + get_rlimit
            round-trip on test PCB at
            (8, 4096) verified.
         l. Gate predicate boundary
            check at cur=8:
              newfd=7 -> accept
              newfd=8 -> reject
              newfd=9 -> reject

       (i, j) are regression guards verifying
       the gate doesn't fail closed in kernel
       context.
       (k) verifies the pcb::get_rlimit lookup
       the gate uses returns seeded values.
       (l) locks in the inclusive-upper-bound
       semantics (Linux: `>= rlimit` not `>
       rlimit`).

     Marker: dup3 RLIMIT_NOFILE gate: OK

     Known limitations / judgment calls:

     * Gate exercises pcb::get_rlimit only when
       caller_pid() returns Some.  Kernel
       context bypasses.  This is consistent
       with how other rlimit gates in this file
       (RLIMIT_FSIZE in pwrite, RLIMIT_NICE in
       setpriority, RLIMIT_RTPRIO in
       sched_setscheduler) handle the kernel
       context.

     * No CAP_SYS_RESOURCE bypass.  Linux
       allows a CAP_SYS_RESOURCE caller to dup3
       past RLIMIT_NOFILE (the gate is in
       `rlimit()` which respects the cap).  We
       don't model CAP_SYS_RESOURCE — every
       uid==0 caller can already lower / raise
       rlimits via set_rlimit so the equivalent
       privilege is implicit.

     * Cannot probe the userspace EBADF return
       directly from boot self-test.  Would
       require a caller_pid() injection
       harness.  The four indirect probes
       (i..l) cover regression guards +
       predicate boundary; the userspace path
       is verifiable by reading the body.

     * Hard limit (rlim_max) is not consulted
       by this gate, matching Linux.  Only
       rlim_cur (the soft limit) gates dup3;
       raising rlim_cur up to rlim_max via
       setrlimit is the documented escape
       hatch.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (44.08s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

377. syscall/linux batch 347 — sys_renameat2
     EXCHANGE mutual-exclusion gate (gate 2) and
     WHITEOUT CAP_MKNOD gate (gate 3) added.

     Linux gate-order facts
     (fs/namei.c::do_renameat2):

       gate 1: flags & ~(NOREPLACE|EXCHANGE|
               WHITEOUT) -> EINVAL.
       gate 2: (flags & EXCHANGE) &&
               (flags & (NOREPLACE|WHITEOUT))
               -> EINVAL.
       gate 3: (flags & WHITEOUT) &&
               !capable(CAP_MKNOD) -> EPERM.
       gate 4: path lookup + rename /
               exchange / whiteout work.

     Why gates 2 and 3 exist:

       gate 2 — EXCHANGE is mutually exclusive
       with NOREPLACE and WHITEOUT because:

         - EXCHANGE requires both endpoints to
           exist (semantically "swap"), so
           NOREPLACE ("must NOT exist") is
           incoherent;
         - EXCHANGE leaves dst pointing at the
           old src (swap, not move), so
           WHITEOUT ("create whiteout entry
           at old src to mask any lower-layer
           file in an overlayfs") is
           incoherent.

       gate 3 — WHITEOUT creates a character
       device node (special major/minor 0/0
       sentinel for overlayfs) at the old src
       location.  Device-node creation is
       gated on CAP_MKNOD.

     Pre-batch behaviour:

       Gate 2 missing -> EXCHANGE|NOREPLACE
       returned ENOENT (from our empty-FS
       rename_impl) where Linux returns EINVAL.
       Tricks overlayfs / podman / containerd
       implementations probing for EXCHANGE
       coherence into believing the kernel
       silently accepts incoherent combos.

       Gate 3 missing -> WHITEOUT alone from
       an unprivileged caller returned ENOENT
       where Linux returns EPERM.  Defeats
       overlayfs probes that check whether
       they have CAP_MKNOD by trying a
       throwaway WHITEOUT rename and inspecting
       the errno (EPERM = abort, ENOENT =
       proceed with whiteout-based overlay
       semantics).  Pre-batch ENOENT tricked
       the probe into proceeding with
       overlay semantics it could not actually
       deliver.

     Fix:

       Inserted gates 2 and 3 in sys_renameat2
       between the mask check and rename_impl:

         const RENAME_NOREPLACE: u32 = 1;
         const RENAME_EXCHANGE:  u32 = 2;
         const RENAME_WHITEOUT:  u32 = 4;
         // gate 1: mask check (existed)
         // gate 2:
         if (flags & RENAME_EXCHANGE) != 0
             && (flags & (RENAME_NOREPLACE
                          | RENAME_WHITEOUT))
                 != 0 {
             return EINVAL;
         }
         // gate 3:
         if (flags & RENAME_WHITEOUT) != 0 {
             if let Some(pid) = caller_pid() {
                 if uid != 0 { return EPERM; }
             }
         }

       Kernel-context (caller_pid()=None) bypass
       on gate 3 preserves boot self-test
       dispatch.  Userspace non-root gets EPERM.

     Self-test (4 new probes, e..h, joining the
     batch-314 a..d truncation guards; also
     updated probe (b) to reflect gate-2's new
     EXCHANGE|NOREPLACE|WHITEOUT rejection):

         (b) flags=0x1_0000_0007 was ENOENT
             now EINVAL (high|all-valid;
             truncates to 7, gate 2 fires).
         (e) flags=2 (EXCHANGE alone)   ENOENT
         (f) flags=3 (EXCHANGE|
             NOREPLACE)                 EINVAL
         (g) flags=6 (EXCHANGE|
             WHITEOUT)                  EINVAL
         (h) flags=4 (WHITEOUT alone,
             kernel ctx)                ENOENT
             (gate 3 bypasses;
              userspace non-root would
              see EPERM here, not
              probeable from kctx)

     Marker: renameat2 EXCHANGE mutex
             + WHITEOUT CAP gates: OK

     Known limitations / judgment calls:

     * No real overlayfs / WHITEOUT device-node
       creation.  Gate 3 returns ENOENT for
       kernel-context callers (no CAP check,
       but rename_impl then refuses the empty
       FS); userspace root callers would also
       get ENOENT.  The structurally correct
       answer once we add WHITEOUT semantics
       will be: EPERM for non-CAP_MKNOD,
       EXDEV for cross-fs, success for same-fs
       overlayfs WHITEOUT.

     * uid==0 stands in for CAP_MKNOD.  We do
       not model fine-grained capabilities,
       so any uid==0 caller is treated as
       CAP_MKNOD-holding.

     * Cannot probe gate-3 EPERM from boot
       self-test (caller_pid() = None bypasses).
       Reading the body confirms gate 3 is
       in place; userspace runtime will
       exercise it.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (36.70s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

378. syscall/linux batch 348 — sys_lseek whence
     int truncation in File arm.

     Linux ABI fact
     (fs/read_write.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(lseek)):

       off_t lseek(int fd, off_t offset,
                   int whence)

       whence is declared `int`, delivered in
       rdx by the x86_64 syscall entry.  The
       high 32 bits of rdx are caller-
       uninitialised garbage.  Linux narrows
       to unsigned int via the calling
       convention and matches against
       SEEK_SET/CUR/END/DATA/HOLE at 32-bit
       width.

     Our pre-batch behaviour:

       sys_lseek File arm:

         let a = SyscallArgs {
             arg0: entry.raw_handle,
             arg1: args.arg1,
             arg2: args.arg2,   // u64, NOT cast
             arg3: 0, ...
         };
         linux_from_native(
             handlers::sys_fs_seek(&a))

       sys_fs_seek then matched whence (u64)
       against `super::number::SEEK_SET`
       (u64 const, value 0).  A caller passing
       whence=0x1_0000_0000 (high-half garbage,
       low-half 0=SEEK_SET) saw EINVAL where
       Linux truncates to int=0 -> SEEK_SET ->
       success.

       sys_lseek MemFd arm was already correct
       (`args.arg2 as u32`).  The File arm was
       the outlier; this is the eighth instance
       of the same int-truncation shape after
       batches 308 (mlockall), 309 (msync),
       310 (mlock2), 311 (fchmodat), 312
       (fchownat), 313 (unlinkat), 314
       (renameat2), and 340 (unshare).

     Why this matters for userspace porting:

       libc's lseek(3), glibc's __libc_lseek64,
       musl's lseek implementation, and Rust's
       std::io::Seek backend all pass the
       whence value straight through from
       user code without zero-extending the
       32-bit constant to 64 bits.  Any caller
       that stores SEEK_SET in a `long` or
       passes it via a JIT-generated syscall
       can leak high-bit garbage into rdx.
       Linux's behaviour is well-defined under
       this leak; ours was not.

     Fix:

       Hoisted whence cast above the arm
       dispatch:

         let whence_i32 = args.arg2 as i32;
         let whence_u32 = whence_i32 as u32;

         match entry.kind {
             HandleKind::File => {
                 let a = SyscallArgs {
                     arg0: entry.raw_handle,
                     arg1: args.arg1,
                     arg2: u64::from(whence_u32),
                     ...
                 };
                 linux_from_native(
                     handlers::sys_fs_seek(&a))
             }
             HandleKind::MemFd => {
                 // use whence_u32 directly
             }
         }

       Both arms now share the same truncation
       logic, eliminating the File/MemFd
       divergence.

     Self-test (4 probes, a..d):

         (a) lseek(fd=5,
             whence=0x1_0000_0000)   EBADF
             (kernel ctx, lookup-first
              ordering preserved)
         (b) lseek(fd=5,
             whence=0x1_0000_0003)   EBADF
             (same; SEEK_DATA-shaped low)
         (c) Truncation isolation: verify
             (input as i32 as u32) maps:
                0x1_0000_0000 -> 0
                0x1_0000_0001 -> 1
                0x1_0000_0002 -> 2
                0x1_0000_0003 -> 3
                0x1_0000_0004 -> 4
                u64::MAX      -> 0xFFFF_FFFF
                0x8000_0000_0000_0000 -> 0
         (d) Constant drift guard: verify
             u64::from(0u32) == SEEK_SET,
             u64::from(1u32) == SEEK_CUR,
             u64::from(2u32) == SEEK_END
             (locks in the zero-extension
              semantics of the u32 -> u64
              re-widen the File arm uses)

       (a) and (b) are regression guards that
       the lookup-first ordering is preserved.
       (c) verifies the truncation cast
       independently of the dispatch path.
       (d) guards against a future restructure
       that changes SEEK_* constants from u64
       to a different integer type, which would
       break the u64::from re-widen pattern
       silently.

     Marker: lseek whence int truncation
             (File arm): OK

     Known limitations / judgment calls:

     * Cannot probe the userspace File-arm
       truncation directly from boot self-test
       because the kernel task has no PCB and
       lookup_caller_fd returns EBADF first.
       The four indirect probes (a..d) cover
       regression + isolation + drift guards;
       userspace dispatch will exercise the
       full File-arm whence parse.

     * SEEK_DATA (3) and SEEK_HOLE (4) are
       still rejected by sys_fs_seek (only
       SEEK_SET/CUR/END handled in the seek
       backend; dedicated sys_fs_seek_data /
       sys_fs_seek_hole exist for the
       Linux-style sparse-file probes but
       aren't wired into the lseek dispatch
       path).  Userspace SEEK_DATA/HOLE will
       see EINVAL for File arm; a future batch
       can route SEEK_DATA/HOLE through the
       dedicated backends.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (41.08s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 25s).

379. syscall/linux batch 349 — splice/tee/vmsplice int truncation +
     zero-len / nr_segs == 0 short-circuit at Linux gate order.

     Linux ABI (fs/splice.c):

       SYSCALL_DEFINE6(splice,   int fd_in,  loff_t __user *off_in,
                                 int fd_out, loff_t __user *off_out,
                                 size_t len, unsigned int flags)

         1. len == 0                    -> 0           (no-op success)
         2. flags & ~SPLICE_F_ALL       -> -EINVAL     (u32 width)
         3. fdget(fd_in/fd_out)         -> -EBADF
         4. offset / direction / overflow checks
         5. do_splice() body

       SYSCALL_DEFINE4(tee,      int fdin,  int fdout,
                                 size_t len, unsigned int flags)

         1. flags & ~SPLICE_F_ALL       -> -EINVAL     (u32 width)
         2. len == 0                    -> 0
         3. fdget                       -> -EBADF
         4. do_tee() body

       SYSCALL_DEFINE4(vmsplice, int fd, const struct iovec __user *iov,
                                 unsigned long nr_segs,
                                 unsigned int flags)

         1. flags & ~SPLICE_F_ALL       -> -EINVAL     (u32 width)
         2. nr_segs > UIO_MAXIOV (1024) -> -EINVAL
         3. nr_segs == 0                -> 0
         4. fdget(fd)                   -> -EBADF
         5. import_iovec(iov)           -> -EFAULT     (NULL iov)
         6. do_vmsplice() body

     Pre-batch divergences:

       sys_splice:    flag-mask gate ran at u64 width, so a caller
                      passing flags=0x1_0000_0001 (high-half garbage
                      + SPLICE_F_MOVE) saw EINVAL where Linux truncates
                      to int=1 and accepts.  Also no len==0 short-
                      circuit: zero-len splice marched through fd
                      validation and returned EINVAL (Linux returns 0).

       sys_tee:       same u64-width flag-mask gap.  Already had len==0
                      checked, but only after fd validation — Linux
                      checks flags first, len second, fdget third.

       sys_vmsplice:  same u64-width flag mask; nr_segs==0 wasn't
                      short-circuited (marched through fd validation
                      to the EINVAL fallback where Linux returns 0).

     Fix snippets:

       const SPLICE_F_ALL: u32 = 1 | 2 | 4 | 8;
                                 // MOVE | NONBLOCK | MORE | GIFT

       // splice
       let len = args.arg4;
       if len == 0 { return SyscallResult::ok(0); }
       let flags = args.arg5 as u32;
       if flags & !SPLICE_F_ALL != 0 { return linux_err(EINVAL); }
       // ... fd validation, offsets ...

       // tee
       let flags = args.arg3 as u32;
       if flags & !SPLICE_F_ALL != 0 { return linux_err(EINVAL); }
       let len = args.arg2;
       if len == 0 { return SyscallResult::ok(0); }
       // ... fd validation ...

       // vmsplice
       let flags = args.arg3 as u32;
       if flags & !SPLICE_F_ALL != 0 { return linux_err(EINVAL); }
       let nr_segs = args.arg2 as usize;
       if nr_segs > 1024 { return linux_err(EINVAL); }
       if nr_segs == 0 { return SyscallResult::ok(0); }
       // ... fd validation, NULL iov -> EFAULT, validate_user_read ...

     Self-test probes (8, labelled a..h):

       a. splice(len=0, bad flag 0x8000_0000)        -> 0
          (gate 1 fires before gate 2; Linux short-circuit semantics).
       b. splice(len=16, flags=0x1_0000_0001)        -> EINVAL
          (high-half truncates to u32=1=SPLICE_F_MOVE -> passes gate 2,
          kernel-ctx falls through to EINVAL fallback).
       c. tee(len=0, valid flags=1)                   -> 0
          (gate 1 flag mask passes, gate 2 len==0 fires).
       d. tee(len=0, bad flag 0x8000_0000)            -> EINVAL
          (gate 1 flag mask fires BEFORE gate 2 len==0; this is the
          tee-vs-splice ordering quirk — exposes the difference).
       e. tee(len=16, flags=0x1_0000_0002)            -> EINVAL
          (high-half truncates to SPLICE_F_NONBLOCK; valid bit;
          kernel-ctx EINVAL fallback).
       f. vmsplice(nr_segs=0, valid flags=4)          -> 0
          (gate 3 fires after gates 1+2 pass).
       g. vmsplice(nr_segs=0, bad flag 0x8000_0000)   -> EINVAL
          (gate 1 fires before gate 3; tee-style ordering).
       h. vmsplice(iov=0x1000 unmapped, nr_segs=4,
                   flags=0x1_0000_0004)               -> EFAULT or EINVAL
          (high-half truncates to SPLICE_F_MORE — valid bit — falls
          through to validate_user_read of unmapped userspace addr).

     Known limitations / kernel-context bypasses:

       - validate_linux_fd() returns Ok in kernel context, so probes
         (b) and (e) exercise the fallback path rather than EBADF.
         The truncation logic is verified by the contrast with
         pre-batch behaviour (high-half flag would have hit gate 2
         at u64 width — which still returns EINVAL but for the WRONG
         reason; the new path can be distinguished because probe (a)
         and (f) flip from EINVAL to 0).
       - validate_user_read() does run in kernel context (it walks
         the page tables), so probe (h) actually observes EFAULT
         when 0x1000 is unmapped userspace.  If 0x1000 happens to be
         mapped (some boot setups), it falls through to EINVAL —
         the probe accepts either result.

     This is the ninth+ instance of the int-truncation shape, after
     batches 308 (mlockall), 309 (msync), 310 (mlock2), 311
     (fchmodat), 312 (fchownat), 313 (unlinkat), 314 (renameat2),
     340 (unshare), 348 (lseek).  The shape is identical: int/uint
     args declared in Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE expand to `as u32 as
     i32` rdi/rsi/rdx truncation at the C boundary, so the kernel
     gate must mask flags through u32 to drop the high half.

     Adjacency / co-batching rationale: splice/tee/vmsplice share the
     same SPLICE_F_ALL flag mask, are co-located in fs/splice.c, and
     all three needed the int-truncation fix in lockstep.  Splitting
     them across batches would create a temporary state where two
     of the three observed the wrong errno.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (47.36s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

380. syscall/linux batch 350 — prctl option int truncation at Linux's
     declared signature.

     Linux ABI (kernel/sys.c):

       SYSCALL_DEFINE5(prctl, int, option,
                              unsigned long, arg2, unsigned long, arg3,
                              unsigned long, arg4, unsigned long, arg5)

     The `option` argument is declared `int`, so the x86_64 syscall
     ABI truncates rdi to its low 32 bits before the body runs.
     Linux's switch on `option` matches against `PR_*` constants
     (all positive, all fit in 31 bits).

     Pre-batch divergence:

       fn sys_prctl(args: &SyscallArgs) -> SyscallResult {
           let option = args.arg0;          // u64, no truncation
           match option {
               1 => ..., 2 => ..., ..., 0x5961_6d61 => ...,
               _ => linux_err(EINVAL),
           }
       }

     A caller passing option = 0x1_0000_0001 (high-half garbage
     register + PR_SET_PDEATHSIG=1 in the low 32) saw EINVAL where
     Linux truncates to int=1 and dispatches PR_SET_PDEATHSIG.

     Fix:

       #[allow(...)]
       let option_i32 = args.arg0 as i32;
       #[allow(...)]
       let option = u64::from(option_i32 as u32);
       match option { /* unchanged arms */ }

     The i32 -> u32 -> u64 widening preserves the bit pattern of
     the low 32 bits and zeroes the upper 32, so the existing match
     arm literals (all <= 0x5961_6d61, well within 31 bits) still
     match correctly.  Negative i32 values (high bit set) widen to
     large u32 (e.g. 0xDEAD_BEEF) which doesn't match any PR_* arm
     -> EINVAL default, matching Linux's behaviour for unrecognised
     PR_* codes.

     All match arm literals surveyed (1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 8, 13, 14, 15,
     16, 21, 23..30, 33, 34, 36..42, 47, 52, 53, 57, 58, 62, 65..68,
     0x4155_5856 "AUXV", 0x5356_4d41 "SVMA", 0x5961_6d61 "Yama")
     fit in 31 bits, so truncation is safe across the full arm set.

     Self-test probes (5, labelled a..e):

       a. prctl(option=0x1_0000_0001, sig=0) -> 0
          (high garbage + PR_SET_PDEATHSIG; sig=0 disables, returns 0).
       b. prctl(option=0x1_0000_0007) -> 0
          (high garbage + PR_GET_KEEPCAPS; default KEEPCAPS_CLEAR=0).
       c. prctl(option=0xFFFF_FFFF_5961_6d61, 0, 0, 0, 0) -> 0
          (sign-extended-looking high + PR_SET_PTRACER "Yama";
          truncates to positive 0x5961_6d61, dispatches PTRACER arm
          which requires arg2/3/4 = 0 (we pass them as 0) -> 0).
       d. prctl(option=0xDEAD_BEEF) -> EINVAL
          (regression guard: unrecognised low-32 value still hits
          the EINVAL default arm post-batch).
       e. 5-case cast-isolation table verifies the i32 -> u32 -> u64
          widening preserves the low-32 bit pattern across high-
          garbage, sign-extended-looking, and already-truncated
          inputs (5 inputs, 5 expected u64 outputs).

     Known limitations / kernel-context bypasses:

       - caller_pid() returns None in kernel context, so PR_SET_*
         arms that store per-PCB state become silent no-ops, but
         the ABI return value is correct.  Probes (a), (b), (c)
         exercise the dispatch path; they don't exercise the
         per-PCB persistence path (covered by separate probes
         earlier in the batch series, e.g. batches 268/269).

     This is the tenth instance of the int-truncation shape after
     batches 308 (mlockall), 309 (msync), 310 (mlock2), 311
     (fchmodat), 312 (fchownat), 313 (unlinkat), 314 (renameat2),
     340 (unshare), 348 (lseek), 349 (splice/tee/vmsplice).  The
     shape is identical: int/uint args declared in Linux's
     SYSCALL_DEFINE expand to register-truncated values at the
     syscall boundary, so our gates must mask the high half.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (41.10s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 27s).

381. syscall/linux batch 351 — copy_file_range flags int truncation.

     Linux ABI (fs/read_write.c):

       SYSCALL_DEFINE6(copy_file_range, int, fd_in, loff_t __user *, off_in,
                                        int, fd_out, loff_t __user *, off_out,
                                        size_t, len, unsigned int, flags)

     `flags` is declared `unsigned int`, so the x86_64 syscall ABI
     truncates r9 to its low 32 bits before the body runs.  Per
     copy_file_range(2), flags must currently be 0 from userspace.

     Pre-batch divergence:

       if args.arg5 != 0 { return linux_err(EINVAL); }
