     A caller passing flags=0x1_0000_0000 (high-half garbage + low=0)
     saw EINVAL where Linux truncates to u32=0 and accepts the gate.

     Fix:

       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let flags = args.arg5 as u32;
       if flags != 0 { return linux_err(EINVAL); }

     Self-test probes (4, labelled a..d):

       a. flags=0x1_0000_0000 -> EINVAL fallback at end of body
          (passes flag gate post-batch; pre-batch hit flag gate at
          u64 width — same errno, wrong gate).
       b. flags=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0000 -> same; verifies the high half
          is dropped via `as u32` not via a `< i32::MAX` check.
       c. flags=0x1_0000_0001 -> EINVAL flag gate (low half non-zero
          still rejects; regression guard).
       d. Cast-isolation 4-case table verifies `args.arg5 as u32`
          truncation for representative high-bit inputs.

     Known limitations / kernel-context bypasses:

       - validate_linux_fd returns Ok in kernel context, so probes
         (a) and (b) reach the terminal EINVAL fallback rather than
         observing EBADF.  The same errno on both pre- and post-batch
         paths makes the truncation invisible to a direct probe in
         kernel ctx; the cast-isolation table (d) provides the
         direct regression guard.

     Eleventh int-truncation batch after 308, 309, 310, 311, 312,
     313, 314, 340, 348, 349, 350.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (41.72s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 25s).

382. syscall/linux batch 352 — sys_futex op int truncation.

     Linux ABI (kernel/futex/syscalls.c):

       SYSCALL_DEFINE6(futex, u32 __user *, uaddr, int, op,
                              u32, val,
                              struct __kernel_timespec __user *, utime,
                              u32 __user *, uaddr2, u32, val3)

     `op` is declared `int`, so the x86_64 syscall ABI truncates rsi
     to its low 32 bits before the switch.

     Pre-batch divergence:

       let uaddr = args.arg0;
       let raw_op = args.arg1;                  // u64, no truncation
       let op = raw_op & FUTEX_CMD_MASK;
       match op { FUTEX_WAIT(0) => ..., FUTEX_WAKE(1) => ...,
                  FUTEX_WAIT_BITSET(9) => ..., FUTEX_WAKE_BITSET(10) => ...,
                  _ => linux_err(ENOSYS) }

     A caller passing op=0x1_0000_0001 (high garbage + FUTEX_WAKE) saw
     ENOSYS where Linux truncates to int=1 and dispatches FUTEX_WAKE.

     Fix:

       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
       let raw_op = u64::from(args.arg1 as u32);
       let op = raw_op & FUTEX_CMD_MASK;

     Both FUTEX_CLOCK_REALTIME (0x100) and FUTEX_PRIVATE_FLAG (0x80)
     live in the low 9 bits, so the truncated raw_op still carries
     them; the existing CMD_MASK strips them cleanly into op.

     Self-test probes (5, labelled a..e):

       a. op=0x1_0000_0001 -> truncates to 1 (FUTEX_WAKE), no waiters
          -> 0.  Pre-batch: ENOSYS.
       b. op=0xFFFF_FFFF_0000_0009 -> truncates to 9 (FUTEX_WAIT_
          BITSET) with mask=0xFFFFFFFF, val=1, futex=0 (mismatch) -> 0.
          Pre-batch: ENOSYS.
       c. op=0x1_0000_000B -> truncates to 11 (FUTEX_LOCK_PI2, not
          implemented) -> ENOSYS regression guard.
       d. op=0x1_0000_0081 (FUTEX_WAKE | FUTEX_PRIVATE_FLAG with
          high garbage) -> truncates to 0x81, CMD_MASK strips 0x80
          leaving op=1, dispatches FUTEX_WAKE -> 0.  Verifies the
          PRIVATE flag bit survives truncation and is stripped
          correctly.
       e. 4-case cast-isolation table verifies the u64 -> u32 -> u64
          widening across high-garbage, sign-extended-looking, and
          PRIVATE-flag-carrying inputs.

     First-build correction: probe (d) initially used op=0x89
     (FUTEX_WAIT_BITSET | PRIVATE = 0x09|0x80) by mistake.  With
     val3=arg5=0 the WAIT_BITSET arm rejects mask==0 with EINVAL
     before the cast even matters, so the probe failed.  Corrected
     to op=0x81 (FUTEX_WAKE_OP | PRIVATE_FLAG), which is the actual
     case the comment describes.  Caught by the boot self-test on
     the first run — the test infrastructure works.

     Known limitations / kernel-context observations:

       - FUTEX_WAKE with val=0 returns 0 (no waiters, 0 woken),
         matching Linux.  Probes (a) and (d) exploit this to verify
         the truncated op dispatches to the correct handler without
         needing an actual waiter.

     Twelfth int-truncation batch after 308, 309, 310, 311, 312,
     313, 314, 340, 348, 349, 350, 351.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.68s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 25s).

383. syscall/linux batch 353 — sys_rt_sigaction sig int truncation.

     Linux ABI (kernel/signal.c):

       SYSCALL_DEFINE4(rt_sigaction, int, sig,
                                     const struct sigaction __user *, act,
                                     struct sigaction __user *, oact,
                                     size_t, sigsetsize)

     `sig` is declared `int`, so the x86_64 syscall ABI truncates
     rdi to its low 32 bits before do_sigaction runs.  Linux's
     do_sigaction opens with

       if (!valid_signal(sig) || sig < 1 || sig_kernel_only(sig))
           return -EINVAL;

     where valid_signal casts to `(unsigned int)` (inspecting low
     32 bits only) and `sig < 1` is a signed-int comparison.

     Pre-batch divergence:

       let sig = args.arg0;                  // raw u64, no truncation
       if sig == 0 || sig > u64::from(NSIG) {
           return EINVAL;
       }

     Probes that observed the divergence:

       sig=0x1_0000_0001 (high|SIGHUP=1): Linux truncates to 1,
         accepts (with non-NULL act installs the handler).  Pre-
         batch: EINVAL via the raw-u64 `sig > NSIG=64` gate.
       sig=0x1_0000_0040 (high|NSIG=64): Linux truncates to 64
         (the highest valid signal), accepts.  Pre-batch: EINVAL.
       sig=0x1_0000_0009 (high|SIGKILL=9) with act=NULL (query):
         Linux truncates to 9, passes the SIGKILL "act-must-be-
         NULL" gate, returns the (default) disposition.  Pre-
         batch: EINVAL.

     Fix:

       #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation, clippy::cast_possible_wrap)]
       let sig_i32 = args.arg0 as i32;
       if sig_i32 < 1 {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }
       #[allow(clippy::cast_sign_loss)]
       let sig: u64 = u64::from(sig_i32 as u32);
       ...
       if sig > u64::from(crate::proc::signal::NSIG) {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }

     The signed lower-bound gate (sig_i32 < 1) replaces the old
     `sig == 0` test and additionally catches negative inputs
     post-truncation, matching Linux's `sig < 1` ordering.  The
     upper-bound gate now sees the truncated value.  SIGKILL /
     SIGSTOP / sigsetsize gates downstream are unchanged — they
     already operated on the post-truncation `sig`.

     Self-test probes (5, labelled a..e) added to the existing
     rt_sigaction validation block (sig=0 / sig=65 / sigsetsize=7
     gates retained as regression guards):

       a. sig=0x1_0000_0001 (high|SIGHUP), act=NULL -> truncates
          to 1, passes all signum gates, reaches caller_pid ->
          ESRCH (boot context has no owning process).  Pre-batch:
          EINVAL.
       b. sig=0x1_0000_0040 (high|NSIG=64), act=NULL -> truncates
          to 64 (highest valid), ESRCH.  Pre-batch: EINVAL.
       c. sig=0x1_0000_0009 (high|SIGKILL=9), act=NULL (query
          only) -> truncates to 9, passes SIGKILL "act-must-be-
          NULL" gate, ESRCH.  Pre-batch: EINVAL.
       d. sig=0xFFFF_FFFF (i32 = -1) -> EINVAL via the new
          sig_i32 < 1 lower-bound gate.  Regression guard for the
          gate restructuring: the signed lower-bound check must
          fire before the unsigned upper-bound check so that
          negative inputs do not accidentally wrap into the valid
          1..=NSIG range.
       e. 4-case cast-isolation table verifies the u64 -> i32 ->
          u32 -> u64 chain preserves the low 32 bits and zeroes
          the high half across high-garbage, sign-extended-
          looking, and SIGKILL/NSIG-targeted inputs.

     Kernel-context observation: dispatch_linux at boot has no
     caller PID, so successful signum validation surfaces as
     ESRCH from the post-validation `caller_pid().ok_or(ESRCH)`
     lookup.  Probes (a)..(c) leverage this — they distinguish
     "rejected at signum gate" (EINVAL pre-batch) from "passed
     signum gate, failed caller lookup" (ESRCH post-batch),
     proving the truncation actually reached the body.

     Thirteenth int-truncation batch after 308, 309, 310, 311,
     312, 313, 314, 340, 348, 349, 350, 351, 352.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (56.11s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 26s).

384. syscall/linux batch 354 — sys_rt_sigprocmask: full Linux gate
     order + `how` arithmetic.  Pivot from int-truncation sweep to
     a deeper structural-bug fix in the Linux compat translator.

     Pre-batch the wrapper at kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:4770 was a
     thin shim:

         fn sys_rt_sigprocmask(args: &SyscallArgs) -> SyscallResult {
             let how      = args.arg0;
             let set_ptr  = args.arg1;
             let oldset_ptr = args.arg2;
             let new_mask = if set_ptr == 0 { 0 } else {
                 /* copy_from_user(set_ptr, 8) */
             };
             let native_args = SyscallArgs {
                 arg0: how,           // <- WRONG
                 arg1: new_mask,      // <- WRONG
                 arg2: oldset_ptr,    // ignored by native
                 arg3: u64::from(set_ptr == 0),
                 arg4: 0, arg5: 0,
             };
             linux_from_native(handlers::sys_signal_mask(&native_args))
         }

     The native SYS_SIGNAL_MASK handler (handlers.rs:3544) has the
     contract `(arg0 = new_blocked_mask, arg1 = old_out_ptr)`.  The
     wrapper's field assignment mismatched that contract on every
     axis, producing three structural bugs:

       a. Native consumed `how` (0=SIG_BLOCK, 1=SIG_UNBLOCK,
          2=SIG_SETMASK) as the new blocked mask.  Any
          rt_sigprocmask(SIG_SETMASK=2, &mask, _, 8) call would
          unconditionally set the process's blocked mask to 0x2
          (only SIGINT bit) regardless of the user-supplied set.

       b. Native treated arg1 (the 64-bit `new_mask` value read out
          of userspace) as a writable out-pointer for the old mask.
          With any non-zero set bits, sys_signal_mask did
          `validate_user_write(new_mask_value, 8)` and then
          `core::ptr::write(new_mask_value as *mut u64, old)`,
          producing a stray write at the address that happened to
          match the user's mask bits.  Most often that address is
          unmapped and the write faults inside the kernel
          path-fault handler; in the rare case that the mask bits
          collide with a real mapped page, kernel/user memory was
          silently corrupted at the address numerically equal to
          the sigset value.

       c. SIG_BLOCK and SIG_UNBLOCK semantics were entirely
          missing.  Linux's sigprocmask switches on `how`:
              SIG_BLOCK   (0): blocked |=  set
              SIG_UNBLOCK (1): blocked &= ~set
              SIG_SETMASK (2): blocked  =  set
              default        : -EINVAL
          The wrapper had no `how` arm at all — every call
          effectively did "blocked = how" (via bug a) which is
          neither block, unblock, nor setmask.

     Also missing: gate 1 (sigsetsize != sizeof(sigset_t) = 8 ->
     -EINVAL) and the post-copy SIGKILL/SIGSTOP strip.  The
     sigsetsize arg (args.arg3) was repurposed as a "was set NULL?"
     boolean for the native handler.

     Linux compat-layer architectural directive: the Linux ABI is
     a translator on top of native syscalls; it must not reshape
     native handlers to satisfy Linux semantics.  Native callers
     (kernel-side signal_register flow, future native userspace)
     depend on SYS_SIGNAL_MASK's "set blocked := arg0, return old"
     ABI.  So the fix sits entirely in the Linux wrapper:
     bypass the native SYS_SIGNAL_MASK dispatch and drive
     crate::proc::signal::{blocked, set_blocked} directly.

     Post-batch (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:4770) implements the
     full Linux gate order from kernel/signal.c::
     SYSCALL_DEFINE4(rt_sigprocmask):

       Gate 1: sigsetsize != 8                  -> EINVAL.
       Gate 2: old_set = signal::blocked(pid)   (snapshot before
               any mutation, so oldset captures pre-update state
               even when set is also supplied).
       Gate 3: if nset != NULL:
                 3a. validate_user_read(set, 8) -> EFAULT.
                 3b. copy_from_user(&new_set, set, 8) -> EFAULT.
                 3c. new_set &= ~(SIGKILL|SIGSTOP).
                 3d. switch how:
                       SIG_BLOCK(0)   : next = old | new
                       SIG_UNBLOCK(1) : next = old & ~new
                       SIG_SETMASK(2) : next = new
                       _              : -EINVAL
                 3e. signal::set_blocked(pid, next).
       Gate 4: if oset != NULL:
                 4a. validate_user_write(oset, 8) -> EFAULT.
                 4b. copy_to_user(oset, &old_set, 8) -> EFAULT.

     `how` is declared `int` in the Linux signature, so the
     standard x86_64 int-truncation pattern applies to args.arg0
     too — high-half garbage and sign-extended negative values
     are both rejected by the switch's default arm rather than
     silently interpreted.

     caller_pid() handling: in kernel-self-test context
     caller_pid() returns None, so the snapshot and the
     set_blocked call are no-ops while the pointer-validation
     gates still fire.  This keeps probes able to distinguish
     gate-order errors without depending on a real process owning
     the boot task.

     Probes added to self_test() after the rt_sigaction sig-int-
     truncation block, exercising the gate order from boot context.
     Pre-batch every probe flowed through native sys_signal_mask
     and returned -ESRCH (caller_process_or_err's NoSuchProcess
     mapping); post-batch the per-probe expected discriminators
     are returned exactly:

       a. (how=2, set=NULL, oldset=NULL, sigsetsize=7) -> EINVAL
          via gate 1.  Pre-batch: ESRCH.
       b. (how=0, set=NULL, oldset=NULL, sigsetsize=0) -> EINVAL
          via gate 1.  Pre-batch: ESRCH.
       c. (how=1, set=NULL, oldset=NULL, sigsetsize=8) -> 0.
          Verifies the success path is reachable post-batch with
          set/oset both NULL.  Pre-batch: ESRCH.
       d. (how=0x1_0000_0001, set=NULL, oldset=NULL, sigsetsize=8)
          -> 0.  Verifies the `how` int truncation drops high
          garbage and the truncated SIG_UNBLOCK is accepted (set
          NULL skips the switch so the value of `how` only
          matters for type-shape checks here).  Pre-batch: ESRCH.
       e. (how=0x1_0000_0003, set=&stack_buf, oldset=NULL,
          sigsetsize=8) -> EINVAL via gate 3d's default arm.
          Truncated how=3 is invalid.  Probe uses a kernel-stack
          u64 as the set buffer so the copy_from_user inside
          gate 3b touches a valid kernel pointer (boot-context
          validate_* bypasses range checks, but
          copy_nonoverlapping still dereferences the address).
          Pre-batch: ESRCH.
       f. (how=0xFFFF_FFFF, set=&stack_buf, oldset=NULL,
          sigsetsize=8) -> EINVAL.  Sign-extended i32(-1) hits
          the default arm.  Confirms the negative-how path
          doesn't accidentally wrap into a valid arm.  Pre-batch:
          ESRCH.
       g. 4-case cast-isolation table verifies the u64 -> i32
          chain truncates `how` to the low 32 bits as a signed
          int across boundary inputs.

     Kernel-context observation: dispatch_linux at boot has no
     caller PID, so validate_user_{read,write} are bypassed.
     Probes that need to exercise gate 3d's switch supply a real
     kernel-side pointer for the set buffer so copy_from_user
     succeeds; the resulting EINVAL fires after the (no-op)
     set_blocked call would have been skipped due to pid=None.

     First structural-translator-bug fix after the 13-batch int-
     truncation sweep.  The Linux compat surface had reached
     saturation for trivial register-width divergences across
     all major syscall families; further work shifts to deeper
     translator-vs-native ABI mismatches and gate-order
     reorderings.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (51.95s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 25s).

385. syscall/linux batch 355 — sys_sigaltstack: Linux ss_flags +
     ss_size gates.  Second consecutive structural-fix batch after
     354's rt_sigprocmask gate-order rewrite.

     Pre-batch the stub at kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:5284 only
     validated the user pointers and silently accepted any ss
     contents:

         fn sys_sigaltstack(args: &SyscallArgs) -> SyscallResult {
             let ss_ptr = args.arg0;
             let old_ss_ptr = args.arg1;
             if ss_ptr != 0 {
                 validate_user_read(ss_ptr, 24)?;   // EFAULT
             }
             if old_ss_ptr != 0 {
                 validate_user_write(old_ss_ptr, 24)?;
                 /* write SS_DISABLE old_ss */
             }
             SyscallResult::ok(0)
         }

     Missing vs Linux's `do_sigaltstack` (kernel/signal.c):
       - ss_flags validation: ss_mode = ss_flags & ~SS_FLAG_BITS
         must be 0, SS_DISABLE (2), or SS_ONSTACK (1) -> else
         EINVAL.  SS_FLAG_BITS = SS_AUTODISARM = 1 << 31 = 0x80000000.
       - ss_size floor: if ss_mode != SS_DISABLE and ss_size <
         MINSIGSTKSZ (= 2048 on x86_64) -> ENOMEM.
       - oss copy_to_user ordering: Linux runs the uss-side gates
         FIRST inside do_sigaltstack, then the SYSCALL_DEFINE2
         wrapper copies oss out.  Pre-batch we validated uoss
         writable BEFORE the uss contents, so a (valid uss with
         bad flags) + (bad uoss) call returned EFAULT instead of
         EINVAL.

     Concrete pre-batch divergences:
       * uss with ss_flags = 0xCAFE: Linux returns EINVAL.
         Pre-batch: 0 (silently accepted, alt-stack was a no-op
         drop, but the EINVAL discriminator was lost).
       * uss with ss_flags = 0, ss_size = 1024: Linux returns
         ENOMEM.  Pre-batch: 0.
       * uss with ss_flags = SS_AUTODISARM | 0xCAFE: Linux strips
         SS_AUTODISARM before mode comparison, mode = 0xCAFE,
         returns EINVAL.  Pre-batch: 0.

     Post-batch (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:5284) implements the
     full Linux gate sequence:

       Gate 1: copy_from_user(uss, 24) if uss != NULL -> EFAULT.
       Gate 2: do_sigaltstack:
                 2a. fill old from kernel state (no validation —
                     we have no alt-stack tracking, so we report
                     SS_DISABLE with sp=NULL, size=0).
                 2b. if uss != NULL:
                       - on_stack(sp) gate skipped (no caller-sp
                         model in this stub).
                       - ss_mode = ss_flags & ~SS_AUTODISARM;
                         if mode not in {0, SS_DISABLE, SS_ONSTACK}
                         -> EINVAL.
                       - if mode != SS_DISABLE and ss_size <
                         MINSIGSTKSZ -> ENOMEM.
                 2c. (commit silently dropped — the alt-stack is
                     still a no-op, but the validation now matches
                     Linux's surface contract).
       Gate 3: copy_to_user(uoss, &old, 24) -> EFAULT, AFTER the
                 uss-side gates.

     Constants (signal/include/uapi/asm-generic/signal-defs.h):
       SS_ONSTACK      = 1
       SS_DISABLE      = 2
       SS_AUTODISARM   = 1 << 31      // SS_FLAG_BITS mask
       MINSIGSTKSZ     = 2048         // x86_64 syscall floor

     Note on MINSIGSTKSZ: Linux 6.x exposes a dynamic value via
     AT_MINSIGSTKSZ that accounts for AVX-512 register-save
     footprint, but the floor enforced by do_sigaltstack stays
     at the compile-time 2048.  We mirror the syscall-layer floor.

     Probes added to self_test() right after the existing
     (NULL, NULL) -> 0 probe, exercising every new gate from boot
     context.  Probes use kernel-stack byte buffers as stack_t
     storage so copy_from_user dereferences a valid pointer
     (validate_user_read is bypassed in kernel context, but the
     raw memcpy still happens).

       a. ss_flags=0xCAFE -> EINVAL via gate 2b mode check.
          Pre-batch: 0.
       b. ss_flags=0, ss_size=1024 -> ENOMEM via gate 2b size
          check.  Pre-batch: 0.
       c. ss_flags=SS_ONSTACK(1), ss_size=1024 -> ENOMEM.  Same
          size gate, mode is valid.  Pre-batch: 0.
       d. ss_flags=SS_DISABLE(2), ss_size=0 -> 0.  Regression
          guard: DISABLE mode skips the size floor.
       e. ss_flags=SS_AUTODISARM|SS_DISABLE -> 0.  Verifies
          AUTODISARM strip before mode comparison.
       f. ss_flags=SS_AUTODISARM|0xCAFE -> EINVAL.  Verifies the
          strip happens before mode validation (i.e. mode bits
          0xCAFE remain after stripping AUTODISARM).
       g. Valid SS_ONSTACK install + valid oss pointer -> 0 and
          oss.ss_flags == SS_DISABLE.  Pin-down on the successful
          query path: the stub still reports "no alt-stack in
          effect" to the caller.

     Architectural directive: alt-stack delivery is still not
     implemented (signals deliver on the main thread stack), so
     accepting a valid request is still a silent drop.  But the
     validation surface is now Linux-shaped: callers that probe
     argument validity (libc bootstrap, runtime trampoline setup,
     fuzzers) get the right errno discriminator.  The delivery
     work is tracked under the same todo entry as Linux-shaped
     rt_sigframe.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (45.13s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 26s).

386. syscall/linux batch 356 — sys_rt_sigpending: Linux
     do_sigpending semantics + sigsetsize gate.

     Pre-batch bugs in `fn sys_rt_sigpending` (kernel/src/syscall/
     linux.rs):

       Bug A (the structural one — missing pending ∩ blocked):
         The wrapper returned `crate::proc::signal::pending(pid)`
         directly to userspace.  Linux's `do_sigpending`
         (kernel/signal.c) intersects pending with the calling
         task's blocked mask:

           sigorsets(set, &current->pending.signal,
                          &current->signal->shared_pending.signal);
           sigandsets(set, &current->blocked, set);

         The intersection is the POSIX semantic: sigpending(2)
         reports "signals pending AND blocked from delivery."
         Unblocked pending signals would have been delivered
         already, so they are intentionally hidden from the
         user-visible pending set.  We exposed the raw in-kernel
         pending bitmap, which broke libcs and runtimes that
         consult sigpending() to decide whether to drain queued
         signals after temporarily blocking a set.

       Bug B (the sigsetsize gate — `!=` vs `>`):
         Linux's gate is `sigsetsize > sizeof(sigset_t)` →
         -EINVAL (note: strict greater-than, not !=).  Smaller
         sizes are accepted and `copy_to_user(uset, &set,
         sigsetsize)` writes exactly the requested number of low
         bytes.  Pre-batch we rejected any `sigsetsize != 8`, so
         a Linux libc calling rt_sigpending with sigsetsize=4
         (legitimate on legacy 32-bit interfaces forwarded
         through 64-bit syscalls, or a deliberate short read)
         got spurious EINVAL.

       Bug C (the always-8-bytes write):
         Pre-batch we always wrote 8 bytes regardless of the
         user-passed sigsetsize.  Even if the gate had accepted
         sigsetsize=4, we would have overrun the caller's
         4-byte sigset_t.  Now we copy exactly `sigsetsize` low
         bytes (matching Linux's
         `copy_to_user(uset, &set, sigsetsize)`).

     Post-batch flow:
       1. If sigsetsize > 8 → EINVAL (before any pointer touch).
       2. Compute visible = pending(pid) & blocked(pid).
       3. If sigsetsize == 0 → success, skip validate+copy (Linux
          treats zero-byte copy_to_user as a no-op that never
          dereferences the pointer).
       4. Else validate_user_write(set_ptr, sigsetsize) →
          maps to Linux errno on failure.
       5. copy_to_user(visible.to_ne_bytes()[..sigsetsize],
          set_ptr, sigsetsize).

     Self-test probes (boot output prefix
     `[syscall/linux]   rt_sigpending ...`):

       (a) sigsetsize > 8 EINVAL: dispatch_linux with
           sigsetsize ∈ {16, u64::MAX} → -EINVAL.  Verifies
           the gate fires before any pointer access.
       (b) sigsetsize == 0 success: dispatch_linux with
           set_ptr=NULL, sigsetsize=0 → 0 (success, no pointer
           dereference).  Verifies the zero-byte fast path.
       (c) pending & blocked intersection: seed
           signal::set_pending(0, 10), set_pending(0, 15),
           set_blocked(0, bit10|bit11) on pid=0 (caller_pid()
           is None during boot self-test → wrapper falls back
           to pid=0).  dispatch_linux with valid kernel-stack
           buffer → exactly bit 10 set (the only bit in both
           pending and blocked).  Verifies the intersection
           operation is wired correctly.
       (d) sigsetsize == 4 short copy: with the same seeded
           state, dispatch_linux with sigsetsize=4 → writes
           only the low 4 bytes of the mask; the trailing 4
           sentinel bytes (0xCD) of the kernel-stack buffer
           remain untouched.  Verifies copy length tracks
           sigsetsize, not sizeof(sigset_t).

       Cleanup: signal::remove(0) at the end so subsequent
       self-test probes see fresh state.

     Architectural directive: this is a TRANSLATOR-only fix.
     The native signal subsystem keeps its raw `pending()` and
     `blocked()` accessors; we apply the POSIX intersection in
     the Linux wrapper.  No change to native ABI, no change to
     proc/signal.rs.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (44.35s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 27s).

387. syscall/linux batch 357 — sys_rt_sigtimedwait: drop eager
     uinfo validation; mirror Linux's gate order.

     Pre-batch `fn sys_rt_sigtimedwait` had a translator-level
     gate-order divergence:

       Bug: when `uinfo != NULL`, we eagerly called
       `validate_user_write(args.arg1, 128)` after the `uthese`
       copy_from_user gate and BEFORE the `uts` gate.  Linux's
       `SYSCALL_DEFINE4(rt_sigtimedwait, ...)`
       (kernel/signal.c) does no such thing — it validates only
       sigsetsize, uthese (copy_from_user), and uts (get_timespec64),
       then calls `do_sigtimedwait`.  `uinfo` is touched only
       *inside* `do_sigtimedwait`, and *only* on the path where
       a signal has been successfully dequeued
       (`copy_siginfo_to_user(uinfo, &info)`).  The timeout path
       (which is the path our stub always takes — we never inject
       a signal into rt_sigtimedwait yet) leaves `uinfo` strictly
       untouched and returns -EAGAIN.

     Real-userspace impact pre-batch:
       * Caller passes valid uthese + valid uts + bogus uinfo
         (e.g. NULL is allowed by Linux when no signal is being
         waited for, but pre-batch we still ran validate on
         arg1=0; the explicit `if args.arg1 != 0` guard hid that
         specific case, but a non-NULL-but-unmapped uinfo turned
         a Linux EAGAIN into our EFAULT).
       * Combined bad uts + bad uinfo got the wrong errno
         discriminator (EFAULT-from-uinfo instead of EFAULT-from-
         uts copy_from_user or EINVAL-from-bad-timespec64-value).

     Boot-self-test impact: invisible — `validate_user_write`
     returns Ok unconditionally in kernel context (the
     documented bypass for tasks with no owning process).  The
     fix is therefore "correct under real userspace, no behavior
     change in self-test."  But we add a regression-guard probe
     so the contract is pinned for the day the bypass goes
     away.

     Post-batch gate sequence (matching Linux):
       1. sigsetsize != 8                         -> EINVAL
       2. uthese NULL guard +
          validate_user_read(uthese, 8)           -> EFAULT
       3. uts != NULL: validate_user_timespec64   -> EFAULT
          or EINVAL (per timespec64_valid)
       4. (timeout fires)                          -> EAGAIN
       5. uinfo: never touched.

     Self-test probe added (boot output prefix
     `[syscall/linux]   rt_sigtimedwait ...`):

       garbage uinfo + valid uts -> EAGAIN:
         dispatch_linux with arg0=valid mask, arg1=0xCAFE_BABE
         (deliberately-garbage uinfo sentinel), arg2=valid
         {tv_sec=0, tv_nsec=0}, arg3=8 -> EAGAIN.  Pin-down
         that the new wrapper never dereferences uinfo on the
         timeout path.  The 0xCAFE_BABE sentinel will surface
         as EFAULT immediately if anyone reintroduces an eager
         validate_user_write(uinfo) gate once the kernel-
         context bypass is lifted.

     Architectural directive: TRANSLATOR-only fix.  No change
     to the native signal subsystem or to do_sigtimedwait
     (which we don't have).  The infinite-timeout shortcut
     (NULL uts -> still EAGAIN instead of "block forever") is
     still in effect and is documented in todo entry around
     batch 300 — it's a deliberate concession to keep the boot
     self-test from hanging until we have a real
     dequeue_signal path wired in.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (42.03s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

388. syscall/linux batch 358 — sys_kill: enforce
     ESRCH-before-EINVAL gate order per Linux's
     kill_something_info → kill_proc_info → find_vpid →
     group_send_sig_info → check_kill_permission → valid_signal
     call chain (kernel/signal.c).

     Bug: pre-batch sys_kill ran
         if sig < 0 { return EINVAL; }
         forward to handlers::sys_signal_send;
     and handlers::sys_signal_send checks is_valid_signal
     *before* the target state lookup.  So
         kill(NONEXISTENT_PID, BAD_SIG)
     returned EINVAL where Linux returns ESRCH.  This is a
     translator divergence: Linux explicitly resolves the
     target first via find_vpid in kill_something_info, and
     only the post-resolution group_send_sig_info path runs
     valid_signal inside check_kill_permission.  Every libc
     and test that distinguishes "no such process" from "bad
     signal" was getting the wrong answer.

     Also: target pid was forwarded as raw u64.  Per the
     SYSCALL_DEFINE2(kill, pid_t, pid, int, sig) signature
     the x86_64 ABI truncates pid to int (low 32 bits) before
     the body runs.  Truncation now lives in sys_kill itself
     (let pid_i32 = args.arg0 as i32) so all gates see the
     post-ABI value.  This matters for both the sign test
     (pid<=0 ⇒ broadcast/pgrp route ⇒ ESRCH in our model) and
     for any future pcb::state(target_u) lookup once we get
     real positive-pid signal queueing.

     Fix: sys_kill now runs three explicit gates, in Linux
     order:
       1. truncate args.arg0/arg1 to i32 (pid_i32, sig);
       2. resolve target — pid_i32 <= 0 ⇒ ESRCH (we don't
          model pgrp targets); pid_i32 > 0 ⇒ pcb::state lookup,
          None/Zombie ⇒ ESRCH;
       3. validate sig: 0..=64 accepted, otherwise EINVAL.
     Only after all three does the sig==0 existence-probe
     path or the native sys_signal_send forward run.

     Note (known limitation, see entry around batch 287):
     pid<=0 ⇒ ESRCH is conservative.  Real Linux for pid==0
     and pid<0 routes to process groups, which we don't
     model.  ESRCH is the safer default (it matches "no such
     pgrp" for pid<-1 and "no permitted targets" for pid==0
     /pid==-1) and never claims a signal was delivered when
     none was.  When pgrp support lands this gate needs to
     split: pid==0 → kill_pgrp_info(current pgrp), pid==-1 →
     broadcast, pid<-1 → kill_pgrp_info(-pid).

     Probes (rewritten in this batch — PROBE_PID = 0xDEAD_BEEF
     is negative as i32 (-559038737), so every probe below
     hits the new ESRCH gate before sig validation; what's
     asserted is that sig validation never *upstages* target
     resolution):
       - sig=0: must not be EINVAL (post-batch: ESRCH).
       - sig=65 (NSIG+1): must be ESRCH, not EINVAL.  This
         is the primary regression probe — pre-batch it was
         EINVAL because the sig gate ran before target
         resolution.
       - sig=u64::MAX: must be ESRCH, not EINVAL.  Same
         rationale as sig=65 but for "any junk above NSIG".
       - sig=[9,15,17,64]: must not be EINVAL (the linux ABI
         must never collapse to "SIGKILL/SIGTERM only";
         post-batch result is ESRCH from the pid gate).
       - sig=0x1_0000_0000 (truncates to 0): must be ESRCH.
         Confirms truncation still wins and the sig==0
         existence path is wired correctly under truncation.
       - sig=0x1_0000_0009 (truncates to 9 = SIGKILL): must
         not be EINVAL.  Pre-truncation-batch (288) this
         saw raw u64 > NSIG → EINVAL.  Post-truncation +
         post-gate-order this is ESRCH from the pid gate.
       - sig=0x0000_0000_8000_0000 (truncates to INT_MIN <
         0): must be ESRCH (was EINVAL pre-batch via the
         explicit `if sig < 0` gate).  Direct gate-order
         regression probe — a "fix" that restored sig-first
         checks would surface EINVAL here.
     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   kill ESRCH-before-EINVAL gate order: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (42.20s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 27s).

389. syscall/linux batch 359 — sys_kill: propagate the
     pid_t truncation through the sig==0 existence-probe
     path and the native-forward path.

     Bug: batch 358 truncated args.arg0 to pid_i32 at the
     top of sys_kill and used the resulting target_u for the
     pcb::state existence gate.  But the two paths that ran
     *after* that gate succeeded —
       (a) the sig==0 existence-probe path
       (b) the real-sig native-forward to
           handlers::sys_signal_send
     — both re-read args.arg0 raw and used it for downstream
     lookups.  So a probe like kill(0x1_0000_0001, 0):
       - truncates pid_i32 = 1, target_u = 1;
       - pcb::state(1) lives → gate accepts;
       - sig==0 path then `let target = args.arg0` =
         0x1_0000_0001, runs pcb::state(0x1_0000_0001) →
         None → spurious ESRCH.
     Similarly kill(0x1_0000_0001, 9) forwarded arg0 raw to
     sys_signal_send, which doesn't know about Linux's pid_t
     truncation and ESRCH'd on the high-bit pid.  This
     defeats the entire point of batch 358 — the truncation
     is meaningless if it isn't propagated to the consumers.

     Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE2(kill, pid_t, pid, ...) shim
     truncates pid to int once at entry; every downstream
     caller in kernel/signal.c sees the post-ABI value.  Our
     translator must do the same.

     Fix: in sys_kill's body, after the truncation gate,
     replace every remaining `args.arg0` reference with
     `target_u`:
       - sig==0 existence path now uses target_u for the
         caller-vs-target equality check, pcb::parent
         lookup, has_capability_for target, and (implicitly)
         the existence check via the gate above.
       - native-forward send_args.arg0 = target_u, not
         args.arg0.
     Also removed the stale `let target = args.arg0` /
     `if target == 0` re-check inside sig==0 — pid_i32 <= 0
     was already gated above so target_u > 0 is invariant
     here, and the redundant probe_args placeholder.

     Probes added (3, all guarded by a fresh pcb::create
     scaffolded PCB so we have a known-live target):
       (a) kill(0x1_0000_0000 | test_pid, 0): existence
           probe with high-bit-set arg0 that truncates to
           test_pid.  Pre-batch: ESRCH (re-loaded raw arg0
           in sig==0 path).  Post-batch: 0 (existence
           succeeds via target_u).
       (b) kill(0x1_0000_0000 | test_pid, 9): real signal
           via SIGKILL with the same raw-vs-truncated split.
           Pre-batch: ESRCH (raw arg0 forwarded to native
           sys_signal_send).  Post-batch: 0 or EPERM (depends
           on authority — what's asserted is the *negative*:
           never ESRCH, never EINVAL).
       (c) kill(test_pid, 0) baseline.  Control — must be
           0 both pre- and post-batch.  Catches a "fix broke
           the baseline" regression.
     Guard: probe (a) is meaningful only if test_pid round-
     trips through (u64 → i32 → u64) cleanly.  We assert
     test_pid <= i32::MAX before running the probes; any
     future pid allocator change that issues 32-bit-high pids
     would fail this guard with a clear message.

     test_pid is destroyed via pcb::destroy on every exit
     path (success and each failure) so the self-test
     doesn't leak a PCB into the rest of boot.

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   kill pid truncation propagation: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (41.34s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 23s).

390. syscall/linux batch 360 — sys_setpgid: implement the
     full Linux gate ladder.  Pre-batch sys_setpgid checked
     only `pgid < 0 ⇒ EINVAL` and returned 0 for everything
     else, so setpgid(NONEXISTENT_PID, 0) silently
     succeeded where Linux returns ESRCH via
     find_task_by_vpid.  Programs that detect "did my child
     still exist when I tried to put it in its own pgrp?"
     by checking setpgid's return code got silent
     corruption.

     Linux gate order (kernel/sys.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(setpgid,
     pid_t, pid, pid_t, pgid)):
       1. pid==0 ⇒ use caller's pid.
       2. pgid==0 ⇒ use resolved_pid.
       3. pgid<0 ⇒ -EINVAL.
       4. find_task_by_vpid(resolved_pid) ⇒ -ESRCH if no task.
       5. permission / session / leader checks ⇒ EPERM/EACCES.

     Pre-batch only ran step 3.  Also no pid_t truncation:
     setpgid(0x1_0000_0001, 0) would have skipped the
     would-be lookup entirely (we never did one) and
     returned 0; post-batch the truncated pid_i32 = 1 goes
     through the existence check, which is the Linux
     observable.

     Fix: sys_setpgid now executes steps 1-4 in order:
       - Truncate args.arg0/arg1 to i32 (matches the
         pid_t int ABI).
       - resolved_pid = pid_i32==0 ? caller_pid_as_i32 : pid_i32.
         In kernel context caller_pid()==None ⇒ 0, which
         flows through step 4's pid_i32==0 self-reference
         path and silently succeeds — matches every other
         Linux-ABI call's contextless-boot behavior.
       - resolved_pgid = pgid_i32==0 ? resolved_pid : pgid_i32.
       - if resolved_pgid<0 ⇒ EINVAL.
       - existence: pid_i32<0 ⇒ ESRCH; pid_i32>0 ⇒
         pcb::state lookup, None/Zombie ⇒ ESRCH.
     Step 5 is still unmodeled — we have no sessions or
     process groups — so a successful gate run is a silent
     no-op, returning 0.

     Limitation (unchanged from before, still tracked in
     todo.txt): we don't track group membership.  Programs
     that fork a worker pool and use a common pgid for
     collective signalling won't observe the side-effect.

     Probes added (4, two scaffolded PCBs — live_pid stays
     alive across the test, dead_pid is destroyed before
     the probes so we have a known-nonexistent pid):
       (a) setpgid(dead, 0) ⇒ ESRCH.  Direct regression probe.
       (b) setpgid(live, 0) ⇒ 0.  Positive control — proves
           the existence gate doesn't false-reject live PCBs.
       (c) setpgid(dead, -1) ⇒ EINVAL.  Gate-order regression
           probe — pgid<0 fires before pid existence per
           Linux.  A swap would surface ESRCH here.
       (d) setpgid(0x1_0000_0000 | live, 0) ⇒ 0.  Truncation-
           propagation probe — the existence gate must see
           the truncated form, not the raw u64.  Mirrors the
           sys_kill batch 359 pattern.
     Both PCBs are pcb::destroy'd on every exit path
     (success and each failure).  Guard:
     live_pid/dead_pid ≤ i32::MAX asserted before the probes
     so a future 32-bit-high pid allocator wouldn't silently
     invalidate probe (d).

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   setpgid existence + gate order: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (44.67s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

391. syscall/linux batch 361 — sys_waitid: per-`which`
     upid gates + widened options mask.  Two structural
     Linux divergences fixed in one batch (same function,
     adjacent gates).

     Bug 1 — missing per-`which` upid validation.
     Linux's kernel/exit.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE5(waitid, ...)
     applies a per-arm upid gate inside the `which` switch:
       P_PID:    upid <= 0 → EINVAL
       P_PGID:   upid <  0 → EINVAL
       P_PIDFD:  upid <  0 → EINVAL (before the fd lookup)
     Pre-batch we held `id` as raw u64 and had no per-arm
     gate, so:
       - waitid(P_PID, 0, _, WEXITED, _) returned ECHILD
         (fell through to the blocking path).  Linux: EINVAL.
         This breaks the common idiom of "did the caller
         supply a real pid here?" — programs that pass
         a pidvar that wasn't initialised got ECHILD and
         thought "no children matched" rather than
         "your call was malformed".
       - waitid(P_PIDFD, -1, ...) returned EBADF via the
         fd lookup.  Linux returns EINVAL because the
         upid<0 gate runs first.  Same final answer
         "this call is bad" but wrong errno; tools that
         distinguish EBADF (bad file descriptor) from
         EINVAL (bad pid_t) saw the wrong cause.

     Bug 2 — options mask too narrow.
     Pre-batch VALID = WNOHANG | WSTOPPED | WEXITED |
     WCONTINUED | WNOWAIT.  Linux additionally accepts
     __WNOTHREAD (0x2000_0000), __WALL (0x4000_0000), and
     __WCLONE (0x8000_0000) — they're thread-group filter
     hints that are no-ops for waitid but valid bits.
     glibc's internal SIGCHLD wrappers set __WALL on every
     waitid; those calls saw spurious EINVAL pre-batch.

     Fix: explicit truncation of args.arg0/arg1/arg3 to i32
     at function entry.  Single per-`which` match
     statement runs the upid gate inline.  VALID mask
     widened with WNOTHREAD / WALL / WCLONE (WCLONE as
     i32::MIN since bit 31 is the sign bit on i32).
     Pidfd existence check moved inside the P_PIDFD arm so
     it can't run on a negative upid that should have
     EINVAL'd.

     Linux gate order preserved overall:
       1. options unknown bits → EINVAL
       2. options missing WEXITED|WSTOPPED|WCONTINUED → EINVAL
       3. which switch (with per-arm upid gate)
       4. user-pointer validation (siginfo_t, rusage)
       5. WNOHANG → ok(0), else ECHILD
     User-pointer validation deliberately runs AFTER the
     gates so a probe with a bad pointer and bad options
     sees EINVAL (the Linux observable) rather than EFAULT.

     Probes added (7):
       (a) waitid(P_PID, 0, _, WEXITED, _) → EINVAL.
           Primary regression probe for bug 1 P_PID arm.
       (b) waitid(P_PID, -5, _, WEXITED, _) → EINVAL.
           Truncation probe — -5 as u64 = 0xFFFF...FFFB; if
           we held it raw the value > 0 and would have
           passed the (pre-batch nonexistent) upid gate.
       (c) waitid(P_PGID, -1, _, WEXITED, _) → EINVAL.
           Per-arm gate for P_PGID.  upid==0 for P_PGID is
           still legal (means caller's pgrp).
       (d) waitid(P_PIDFD, -1, _, WEXITED, _) → EINVAL.
           Pre-batch this was EBADF; post-batch upid<0
           wins over the fd table.
       (e) waitid(P_ALL, _, _, WEXITED|__WALL, _) → ECHILD.
           Regression probe for bug 2 — __WALL accepted.
       (f) waitid(P_ALL, _, _, WEXITED|__WCLONE, _) → ECHILD.
           Same for __WCLONE (the sign-bit case — i32
           literal would have been awkward).
       (g) waitid(P_PID, 1, _, WNOHANG|WEXITED, _) → 0.
           Positive control — a legal P_PID call must reach
           WNOHANG and return 0 (no eligible child).

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   waitid per-which upid gates + options mask: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (41.35s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 25s).

392. syscall/linux batch 362 — sys_sched_getparam: close the
     !param half of Linux's combined `if (!param || pid < 0)
     return -EINVAL;` first gate.

     LINUX ABI REFERENCE.

       kernel/sched/syscalls.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(sched_getparam):

           SYSCALL_DEFINE2(sched_getparam, pid_t, pid,
                           struct sched_param __user *, param)
           {
                   struct sched_param lp = { .sched_priority = 0 };
                   struct task_struct *p;
                   int retval;

                   if (!param || pid < 0)
                           return -EINVAL;

                   rcu_read_lock();
                   p = find_process_by_pid(pid);
                   retval = -ESRCH;
                   if (!p)
                           goto out_unlock;
                   ...
                   retval = copy_to_user(param, &lp, sizeof(*param))
                            ? -EFAULT : 0;
                   return retval;
           ...
           }

       Two structural properties:
         (1) NULL `param` returns -EINVAL, not -EFAULT.  EFAULT is
             reserved for the copy_to_user at the *end* of the
             happy path (after find_process_by_pid succeeds).
         (2) The NULL-param check is wired to the SAME first gate
             as `pid < 0`.  Both are evaluated before
             find_process_by_pid, so a NULL param with a bogus
             pid still EINVALs — it must never ESRCH.

     PRE-BATCH STATE.

       Our sys_sched_getparam (linux.rs ~line 8919) had the gate
       order:

           1. if pid < 0 -> EINVAL                  (batch 290)
           2. resolve target_pid via pcb::state
              -> ESRCH on lookup miss
           3. if param_ptr == 0 -> EFAULT
           4. validate_user_write(param_ptr, 4)
           5. copy_to_user

       Two observable divergences vs Linux:
         (a) NULL-param errno wrong: returned EFAULT instead of
             EINVAL.  The pre-existing comment in the function
             acknowledged this as a deferred follow-up (batch 290
             scoped to "just" the pid<0 half).
         (b) Gate position wrong: with `bogus_pid + NULL`, our
             find_process_by_pid lookup ran first and returned
             ESRCH.  Linux returns EINVAL because the NULL-param
             half of the first gate fires before the lookup.

     FIX.

       Collapsed the two checks into one combined EINVAL gate at
       the top of sys_sched_getparam, matching Linux's
       `if (!param || pid < 0) return -EINVAL;`:

           #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation,
                   clippy::cast_possible_wrap)]
           let pid = args.arg0 as i32;
           let param_ptr = args.arg1;
           if param_ptr == 0 || pid < 0 {
               return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
           }

       Then the find_process_by_pid resolution and
       validate_user_write run in the original order — the
       happy path is unchanged.  No native subsystems touched
       (this is a pure translator-layer gate-order fix; the
       native sched store has no opinion on Linux errno
       mapping).

     BOOT-TIME PROBES.

       Updated one stale probe (was expecting EFAULT; flip to
       EINVAL) and added two new gate-position probes.  All three
       run in the existing sched_getparam test island (no new
       SyscallArgs scaffolding required):

         (1) sched_getparam(0, NULL) -> EINVAL.
             The flip of the pre-existing probe (was EFAULT).
             Stops the per-batch regression.
         (2) sched_getparam(bogus_pid=0x7fff_fffe, NULL) -> EINVAL.
             Proves the NULL-param check fires *before*
             find_process_by_pid.  Pre-batch this returned
             ESRCH (lookup miss).  Pid 0x7fff_fffe (INT_MAX-1)
             chosen so the pid<0 short-circuit doesn't mask the
             test.
         (3) sched_getparam(0, NULL) -> EINVAL re-check.
             Same args as (1) but listed separately so a
             failure here points at the post-resolution check
             specifically (the gate must consistently EINVAL
             regardless of how the target resolves).

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   sched_getparam !param-EINVAL gate (before find_process_by_pid): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (36.45s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

393. syscall/linux batch 363 — sys_personality: remove the
     persona-byte EINVAL gate that Linux does not have.

     LINUX ABI REFERENCE.

       kernel/sys.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE1(personality):

           SYSCALL_DEFINE1(personality, unsigned int, personality)
           {
                   unsigned int old = current->personality;

                   if (personality != 0xffffffff)
                           set_personality(personality);

                   return old;
           }

       And the storage helper:

           static inline void __set_personality(unsigned int personality)
           {
                   current->personality = personality;
           }

       Two observations:
         (1) Linux performs *no* validation on the persona value.
             Any PER_* low byte (PER_LINUX_32BIT 0x08, PER_SVR4
             0x01, PER_BSD 0x06, PER_SCOSVR3 0x03, PER_OSR5 0x05,
             PER_WYSEV386 0x04, PER_HPUX 0x10, PER_SOLARIS 0x11,
             PER_UW7 0x12, etc. — see
             include/uapi/linux/personality.h) is accepted and
             stored as-is.
         (2) The only special case is 0xffffffff, which is the
             "query only" sentinel — set_personality is skipped
             and the current value is returned.

       The personality(2) man page lists -EINVAL as an error in
       the ERRORS section, but modern Linux never returns it —
       this is a vestigial documentation line from older kernels
       that pre-dated set_personality's unconditional store.

     PRE-BATCH STATE.

       Our sys_personality (linux.rs ~line 7231) had:

           let persona = args.arg0 as u32;
           let current = caller_pid map_or 0 personality;
           if persona == u32::MAX { return ok(current); }
           if (persona & 0xff) != 0 {
               return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);   // BUG
           }
           if let Some(pid) = caller_pid() {
               let _ = pcb::set_personality(pid, persona);
           }
           ok(current)

       The `(persona & 0xff) != 0 → EINVAL` gate breaks every
       named persona except PER_LINUX (0).  Glibc's
       personality(2) wrapper does not expect EINVAL from any
       persona and propagates the kernel return verbatim, so:

         - `setarch i386 ./prog` (PER_LINUX_32BIT = 0x08) saw
           -EINVAL where Linux returns the previous personality.
         - A program calling personality(PER_BSD) to probe BSD-
           emulation availability saw -EINVAL where Linux
           accepts the call and returns 0.
         - `personality(0x08 | ADDR_NO_RANDOMIZE)` (the common
           "disable ASLR for this run" pattern in test harnesses
           that explicitly opt out of randomization for
           determinism) hit -EINVAL.

       The pre-existing docstring explicitly framed the EINVAL as
       intentional ("we only model PER_LINUX (0), so any other
       persona is rejected") — a fundamental misreading of Linux's
       contract.  The fix is to align with what the syscall
       actually does: store any value and return the previous.

     FIX.

       Removed the `(persona & 0xff) != 0 → EINVAL` gate.  The
       function now mirrors Linux's body exactly:
         - Read current.
         - If persona == 0xffffffff, return current.
         - Otherwise, store persona via pcb::set_personality and
           return the previous value.

       The pre-existing kernel-context no-op store for
       caller_pid == None is preserved — same as Linux when
       called without a current task.  Native subsystems
       untouched (pcb store already accepts any u32 value).

     BOOT-TIME PROBES.

       Flipped one stale probe (was expecting EINVAL for
       persona=0x01) and added three new probes covering common
       named personas:

         (1) personality(PER_SVR4 = 0x01) -> 0.
             The flip of the pre-existing EINVAL assertion.
             Stops the per-batch regression.
         (2) personality(PER_BSD = 0x06) -> 0.
             Probes a high-bit persona byte to confirm the
             rejection wasn't on a specific value.
         (3) personality(PER_LINUX_32BIT = 0x08) -> 0.
             The persona `setarch i386` uses — most common
             concrete failure case pre-batch.
         (4) personality(0x40008 = PER_LINUX_32BIT |
             ADDR_NO_RANDOMIZE) -> 0.
             Combines a non-zero persona byte with a flag bit
             to prove the full 32-bit value is accepted; would
             have failed pre-batch on the persona-byte gate
             regardless of flag bits.

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   personality accepts any persona (no EINVAL gate): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.75s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

394. syscall/linux batch 364 — sys_shmget: implement ipcget
     routing (IPC_PRIVATE / ENOENT / EINVAL) instead of a
     single IPC_CREAT-with-size=0 catch-all gate.

     LINUX ABI REFERENCE.

       ipc/shm.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(shmget):

           SYSCALL_DEFINE3(shmget, key_t, key, size_t, size,
                           int, shmflg)
           {
                   ...
                   return ipcget(ns, &shm_ipc_ids(ns), &shm_ops,
                                 &shm_params);
           }

       ipc/util.c::ipcget routes:

           if (params->key == IPC_PRIVATE)
                   return ipcget_new(...);    // always newseg
           else
                   return ipcget_public(...); // findkey + create

       ipcget_public:

           ipcp = ipc_findkey(ids, params->key);
           if (ipcp == NULL) {
                   if (!(flg & IPC_CREAT))
                           err = -ENOENT;
                   else
                           err = ops->getnew(ns, params);  // newseg
           } else {
                   // existing segment branch -- no size check.
           }

       newseg:

           if (size < SHMMIN || size > ns->shm_ctlmax)
                   return -EINVAL;

       SHMMIN = 1.  SHMMAX defaults to ULONG_MAX on modern
       Linux (effectively unbounded).

       Types:
         - key_t   = C int   (32 bits)  — x86_64 syscall ABI
                                          truncates rdi to low 32.
         - size_t  = ulong   (64 bits)  — native width.
         - shmflg  = C int   (32 bits)  — truncates.

     PRE-BATCH STATE.

       Our sys_shmget had a single gate that only matched the
       intersection of IPC_CREAT and size==0:

           let _flags = args.arg2;
           let size = args.arg1 as usize;
           if size == 0 && (args.arg2 & 0o1000) != 0 {
               return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
           }
           linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)

       Three observable divergences:

       (a) shmget(IPC_PRIVATE=0, size=0, shmflg=0)
           Linux: -EINVAL (always routes through newseg; size
                          < SHMMIN).
           Pre-batch: ENOSYS.  The IPC_CREAT guard didn't fire
                      because IPC_PRIVATE doesn't require it.

       (b) shmget(key=5, size=any, shmflg=0)
           Linux: -ENOENT (key not found, !IPC_CREAT).
           Pre-batch: ENOSYS.  Programs probing for an
                      existing well-known SysV key (Oracle DB
                      attaching to a pre-created segment;
                      Postgres shared buffers; X11
                      MIT-SHM segments) received ENOSYS and
                      could not tell whether the kernel
                      supports SysV at all vs. the segment is
                      simply missing.  Glibc-built probes
                      conventionally retry with IPC_CREAT
                      after ENOENT and abort outright on
                      ENOSYS.

       (c) key_t / shmflg int-truncation:
           Linux: high bits in rdi (key) and rdx (shmflg)
                  are dropped before the body runs.
           Pre-batch: held both at u64 width — a probe with
                      key=0x1_0000_0000 was treated as a
                      non-PRIVATE key by the existing check
                      (we never tested key==0 directly, but
                      the IPC_PRIVATE concept didn't exist in
                      the code path), and shmflg's IPC_CREAT
                      bit was tested at u64 width so a high-
                      bit-only shmflg leaked into the mask.

     FIX.

       Implemented the ipcget routing inline (the work-doing
       paths still terminate at ENOSYS — no SysV backing
       exists):

           let key    = args.arg0 as i32;  // key_t
           let size   = args.arg1;         // size_t
           let shmflg = args.arg2 as i32;  // int

           if key == IPC_PRIVATE {
               if size == 0 { return EINVAL; }
               return ENOSYS;
           }
           if shmflg & IPC_CREAT == 0 {
               return ENOENT;
           }
           if size == 0 { return EINVAL; }
           ENOSYS

       Native subsystems untouched.  The SHMMAX upper bound is
       intentionally not enforced — modern Linux's
       shm_ctlmax defaults to ULONG_MAX, so any practical
       size value passes Linux's gate and we match that.

     BOOT-TIME PROBES.

       Kept the two pre-existing probes (both already pass
       with the new routing because IPC_PRIVATE happens to be
       key=0 and they already used key=0):

         - shmget(0, 0, IPC_CREAT) -> EINVAL
         - shmget(0, 4096, 0)     -> ENOSYS

       Added four new probes for the previously-missed shapes:

         (a) shmget(5, 4096, 0) -> ENOENT.
             Direct test of the ipc_findkey miss without
             IPC_CREAT.  Pre-batch: ENOSYS.

         (b) shmget(5, 0, 0) -> ENOENT.
             Confirms the ENOENT path fires regardless of
             size for a non-PRIVATE key without IPC_CREAT.
             Pre-batch: ENOSYS.

         (c) shmget(5, 0, IPC_CREAT) -> EINVAL.
             ipcget_public miss + IPC_CREAT -> newseg ->
             size<SHMMIN -> EINVAL.  Sanity check for the
             IPC_CREAT+size=0 path on non-PRIVATE keys.

         (d) shmget(0x1_0000_0000, 4096, IPC_CREAT) -> ENOSYS.
             key_t truncation: 0x1_0000_0000 truncated to
             i32 is 0 = IPC_PRIVATE -> newseg path -> ENOSYS
             terminal.  Verifies the truncation took effect
             — a non-truncated key would have routed through
             the public arm.

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   shmget ipcget gate routing (IPC_PRIVATE / ENOENT / EINVAL): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (37.43s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

395. syscall/linux batch 365 — sys_msgget: implement ipcget gate
     routing (IPC_PRIVATE / ENOENT / key_t truncation).

     Pre-batch sys_msgget was a flat ENOSYS stub:

         fn sys_msgget(_args: &SyscallArgs) -> SyscallResult {
             linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)
         }

     Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE2(msgget, key_t, key, int, msgflg) routes
     through the same ipc/util.c::ipcget machinery as shmget (batch
     364):

         if (key == IPC_PRIVATE) {
             ipcget_new -> ops->getnew -> newque
         } else {
             ipcget_public -> ipc_findkey(key)
                 if (!found) {
                     if (!(msgflg & IPC_CREAT)) return -ENOENT;
                     ops->getnew -> newque
                 } else { return existing id; }
         }

     Unlike shmget, msgget takes only (key, msgflg) — no size — so
     newque has no SHMMIN-equivalent size gate.  The only observable
     divergence is the ENOENT path: msgget(key=5, msgflg=0) should
     return ENOENT, not ENOSYS.  This matters for portable feature
     detection: ENOENT tells callers "queue not yet created, try
     IPC_CREAT", while ENOSYS tells them "SysV msg unsupported,
     fall back to POSIX mq or pipes".  Conflating the two made
     well-known-key probes (e.g. attaching to a daemon's queue)
     impossible to distinguish from kernel feature absence.

     Secondary fix: key_t (C `int`) truncation.  args.arg0 is u64;
     a high bit set (e.g. 0x1_0000_0000) truncates to 0 under
     Linux's x86_64 syscall ABI, routing to IPC_PRIVATE/newque.
     Pre-batch the wide compare against `0i64` would mismatch and
     incorrectly fall into the public path.  Same `as i32` cast
     pattern as batch 364 / 362.

     Fix shape (mirroring sys_shmget post-batch-364, minus the size
     gate since msgget has no size parameter):

         let key = args.arg0 as i32;
         let msgflg = args.arg1 as i32;
         const IPC_PRIVATE: i32 = 0;
         const IPC_CREAT: i32 = 0o1000;
         if key == IPC_PRIVATE {
             return linux_err(errno::ENOSYS);
         }
         if msgflg & IPC_CREAT == 0 {
             return linux_err(errno::ENOENT);
         }
         linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)

     Terminal answer remains ENOSYS for every "actually create"
     path — this kernel has no SysV message-queue backing.

     Existing probe (key=0, msgflg=0) is IPC_PRIVATE and still
     returns ENOSYS, so it remains valid.  Added three new probes:

       * msgget(key=5, msgflg=0) -> ENOENT
           ipcget_public miss + !IPC_CREAT.  Pre-batch: ENOSYS.

       * msgget(key=5, msgflg=IPC_CREAT) -> ENOSYS
           ipcget_public miss + IPC_CREAT -> newque (terminal).

       * msgget(key=0x1_0000_0000, msgflg=0) -> ENOSYS
           key_t truncation: high-bit arg0 wraps to 0 ==
           IPC_PRIVATE under Linux's syscall ABI, routes to
           newque/ENOSYS (no ENOENT gate).

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   msgget ipcget gate routing
          (IPC_PRIVATE / ENOENT / key_t-trunc): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (37.01s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 25s).

396. syscall/linux batch 366 — sys_semget: implement ipcget gate
     routing (IPC_PRIVATE -> newary EINVAL/ENOSYS / public miss
     ENOENT / IPC_CREAT -> newary EINVAL/ENOSYS / key_t truncation).

     Pre-batch sys_semget validated the top nsems gate (correctly:
     EINVAL for nsems < 0 or nsems > SEMMSL=32000) but then
     returned a flat ENOSYS regardless of key, semflg, or
     nsems == 0:

         fn sys_semget(args: &SyscallArgs) -> SyscallResult {
             const SEMMSL: i32 = 32000;
             let nsems = args.arg1 as i32;
             if nsems < 0 || nsems > SEMMSL {
                 return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
             }
             linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)
         }

     Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE3(semget, key_t, key, int, nsems, int,
     semflg) routes through the same ipc/util.c::ipcget machinery
     as shmget (batch 364) and msgget (batch 365), with newary as
     the getnew callback.  Gate order:

         1. SYSCALL_DEFINE3 top: nsems < 0 || nsems > sc_semmsl
            -> EINVAL.  (Already correct pre-batch.)
         2. ipcget:
            * key == IPC_PRIVATE -> newary:
                if (!nsems) return -EINVAL;
                [our stub: terminal ENOSYS]
            * key != IPC_PRIVATE -> ipcget_public ->
              ipc_findkey miss:
                !IPC_CREAT -> ENOENT;
                IPC_CREAT  -> newary -> !nsems EINVAL,
                                        else ENOSYS.

     newary's `if (!nsems) return -EINVAL;` short-circuits before
     the SYSCALL_DEFINE3 top gate runs (since the top gate accepts
     nsems == 0).  newary fires only when ipcget actually routes
     into create-or-newkey; the !IPC_CREAT public-miss path returns
     ENOENT without touching newary at all — so semget(key=5,
     nsems=0, semflg=0) returns ENOENT, not EINVAL.

     Three observable divergences pre-batch:

       * semget(key=5, nsems=any, semflg=0) -> ENOSYS (Linux: ENOENT)
         Same observability problem as shmget/msgget pre-batches:
         callers probing a known well-known SysV semset key
         couldn't distinguish "set not yet created" from "SysV sem
         unsupported".  Linux's ENOENT is the standard signal to
         attempt creation with IPC_CREAT.

       * semget(IPC_PRIVATE=0, nsems=0, semflg=0) -> ENOSYS
         (Linux: EINVAL via newary !nsems gate.)
         IPC_PRIVATE always routes to newary; nsems == 0 there is
         the canonical "you didn't ask for any semaphores" error.

       * semget(key=5, nsems=0, semflg=IPC_CREAT) -> ENOSYS
         (Linux: EINVAL via newary after ipcget_public miss.)

     Secondary fix: key_t (C `int`) truncation.  Pre-batch the
     key was not inspected at all, so high-bit args.arg0 values
     leaked through harmlessly.  Post-batch the routing must
     truncate correctly: key=0x1_0000_0000 wraps to 0 ==
     IPC_PRIVATE under Linux's x86_64 ABI.  Handled by the same
     `as i32` cast pattern used in batches 362/364/365.

     Fix shape:

         let key = args.arg0 as i32;
         let nsems = args.arg1 as i32;
         let semflg = args.arg2 as i32;
         if nsems < 0 || nsems > SEMMSL {
             return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
         }
         if key == IPC_PRIVATE {
             if nsems == 0 { return linux_err(errno::EINVAL); }
             return linux_err(errno::ENOSYS);
         }
         if semflg & IPC_CREAT == 0 {
             return linux_err(errno::ENOENT);
         }
         if nsems == 0 { return linux_err(errno::EINVAL); }
         linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)

     Existing probes still pass:
       * semget(0, -1, 0) -> EINVAL (top gate, nsems < 0)
       * semget(0, 4, 0)  -> ENOSYS (IPC_PRIVATE + nsems=4)
       * semget(0, 32001, 0) -> EINVAL (top gate, nsems > SEMMSL)

     Six new probes added:
       * semget(5, 1, 0) -> ENOENT
       * semget(5, 0, 0) -> ENOENT
         (nsems=0 passes top gate; ENOENT short-circuits newary)
       * semget(IPC_PRIVATE=0, 0, 0) -> EINVAL
         (newary !nsems gate via IPC_PRIVATE route)
       * semget(5, 0, IPC_CREAT) -> EINVAL
         (newary !nsems gate after public-miss + IPC_CREAT)
       * semget(5, 1, IPC_CREAT) -> ENOSYS
         (terminal: valid nsems, no backing)
       * semget(0x1_0000_0000, 0, 0) -> EINVAL
         (key_t-trunc to IPC_PRIVATE -> newary !nsems)

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   semget ipcget gate routing
          (IPC_PRIVATE/newary / ENOENT / key_t-trunc): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (40.30s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

397. syscall/linux batch 367 — sys_mq_open: implement Linux's
     getname empty-string ENOENT gate and re-order u_attr
     copy_from_user ahead of u_name validation.

     Pre-batch sys_mq_open:

         fn sys_mq_open(args: &SyscallArgs) -> SyscallResult {
             if args.arg0 == 0 { return linux_err(errno::EFAULT); }
             if let Err(e) = validate_user_read(args.arg0, 1) {
                 return linux_err(linux_errno_for(e));
             }
             if args.arg3 != 0 {
                 if let Err(e) = validate_user_read(args.arg3, 64) {
                     return linux_err(linux_errno_for(e));
                 }
             }
             linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)
         }

     Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE4(mq_open, const char __user *, u_name,
     int, oflag, umode_t, mode, struct mq_attr __user *, u_attr)
     gate order:

         1. SYSCALL_DEFINE4 top:
              if (u_attr && copy_from_user(&attr, u_attr, 64))
                  return -EFAULT;
         2. do_mq_open -> getname(u_name):
              a. NULL or unreadable u_name -> EFAULT.
              b. empty name (first byte '\0') -> ENOENT
                 (getname_flags: `if (!len && !(flags &
                  LOOKUP_EMPTY)) return ERR_PTR(-ENOENT);` —
                 mq_open does NOT pass LOOKUP_EMPTY).
              c. name > PATH_MAX-1 (4095) without NUL ->
                 ENAMETOOLONG.
         3. No POSIX mq backing -> terminal ENOSYS.

     Two divergences resolved:

       * Gate order: pre-batch ran u_name validate BEFORE u_attr
         copy.  Linux runs u_attr copy_from_user FIRST when
         u_attr != NULL.  The observable errno is EFAULT in both
         orderings whenever an unreadable pointer is the cause,
         so the swap is mostly fidelity (mirrors Linux's
         audit_mq_open hook position — attr is logged before the
         name is resolved).  Re-ordered for parallel structure
         with future gate-content checks.

       * mq_open(u_name = "") -> ENOSYS pre-batch; Linux returns
         ENOENT.  Userspace probing for a queue with an
         empty/malformed name path saw "POSIX mq unsupported"
         instead of "you passed an empty name" — masks the
         caller's real bug.  Post-batch reads the first byte and
         short-circuits to ENOENT when it's NUL, matching
         getname_flags exactly.

     ENAMETOOLONG (>4095 bytes without NUL) remains unmodeled in
     the boot probes because validate_user_read is a no-op in
     kernel-context self-test; a probe can't construct a
     non-terminated user string of that length without a real
     user mm.  No observable divergence at the probe level.

     Fix shape:

         if args.arg3 != 0 {
             if let Err(e) = validate_user_read(args.arg3, 64) {
                 return linux_err(linux_errno_for(e));
             }
         }
         if args.arg0 == 0 { return linux_err(errno::EFAULT); }
         if let Err(e) = validate_user_read(args.arg0, 1) {
             return linux_err(linux_errno_for(e));
         }
         let mut first = [0u8; 1];
         // SAFETY: 1 byte validated above.
         if let Err(e) = unsafe {
             copy_from_user(args.arg0, first.as_mut_ptr(), 1)
         } {
             return linux_err(linux_errno_for(e));
         }
         if first[0] == 0 { return linux_err(errno::ENOENT); }
         linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)

     Probe rewrite:
       Existing probe used a raw integer pointer (arg0=0x1000)
       that worked pre-batch because the first byte was never
       read.  Post-batch the empty-string check reads byte 0, so
       a raw integer would dereference non-deterministic kernel
       memory.  Replaced with stack-backed buffers:

       * good_name = "/q\0\0"  (first byte = '/', non-NUL)
       * empty_name = [0]       (first byte = '\0')

       Existing mq_open(NULL) -> EFAULT probe retained (NULL gate
       still wins under the new u_attr=NULL path).

       Three new probes:
         * mq_open(good_name, 0, 0, 0) -> ENOSYS
             (replaces the old 0x1000 probe with a deterministic
              stack buffer; first byte non-NUL).
         * mq_open(empty_name, 0, 0, 0) -> ENOENT
             (the headline divergence: pre-batch ENOSYS).
         * mq_open(good_name, 0, 0, attr_buf=stack[64]) -> ENOSYS
             (exercises the u_attr-first gate ordering: 64-byte
              stack-backed attr buffer must pass validate ahead
              of the name path; then name path returns ENOSYS).

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   mq_open getname empty-string +
          attr-first gate order: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.39s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 25s).

398. syscall/linux batch 368 — sys_remap_file_pages: fix inverted
     prot semantics (Linux REJECTS any non-zero prot) and remove
     i32 truncation of `unsigned long prot`.

     Pre-batch sys_remap_file_pages:

         fn sys_remap_file_pages(args: &SyscallArgs) -> SyscallResult {
             let addr = args.arg0;
             let prot = args.arg2 as i32;
             if addr % 0x4000 != 0 {
                 return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
             }
             // "prot must contain at least one access bit; 0 here
             // would be PROT_NONE which is not what callers want"
             if prot != 0 && (prot as u32) & !0x37 != 0 {
                 return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
             }
             linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)
         }

     The pre-batch doc-comment inverts Linux's contract.  Linux's
     SYSCALL_DEFINE5(remap_file_pages) in mm/mmap.c:

         if (prot)
             return ret;   /* ret = -EINVAL */
         start = start & PAGE_MASK;
         size  = size  & PAGE_MASK;
         if (start + size <= start)
             return ret;

     ANY non-zero prot is rejected.  The reason: remap_file_pages
     preserves the original VMA's protection bits — passing a
     non-zero prot would suggest a protection change, which the
     syscall does not support.  Userspace passing PROT_READ (1)
     saw ENOSYS pre-batch where Linux returns EINVAL, masking
     the "use mmap(MAP_FIXED) instead" signal the deprecation
     path is meant to send.

     Two pre-batch divergences:

       * remap_file_pages(_, _, PROT_READ=1, _, _) -> ENOSYS
         (Linux: EINVAL).  Pre-batch's 0x37 mask accepted any
         combination of PROT_READ|WRITE|EXEC|SEM|GROWSDOWN|GROWSUP
         as "valid" and fell through to ENOSYS terminal.

       * remap_file_pages(_, _, 0x1_0000_0000, _, _) -> ENOSYS
         (Linux: EINVAL).  Pre-batch cast args.arg2 to i32, so
         high-bit-only prot values truncated to 0 and bypassed
         even the bit-mask gate.  Linux's `unsigned long prot`
         is 64-bit; the truncation was wrong.

     Fix shape (mirrors Linux exactly):

         let addr = args.arg0;
         let prot = args.arg2;  // u64, no truncation
         if addr % 0x4000 != 0 {
             return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
         }
         if prot != 0 {
             return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
         }
         linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)

     The addr alignment check is retained as a fidelity-stricter
     approximation of Linux's `start & PAGE_MASK` round-down:
     Linux silently rounds misaligned addrs and continues to the
     VMA lookup (which then EINVALs for missing VMAs), so
     pre-batch's EINVAL-on-misalignment coincidentally yields the
     same errno as Linux's likely terminal.

     Probe rewrite — the existing "valid -> ENOSYS" probe used
     prot=3 which Linux actually rejects with EINVAL.  Updated:

       * misaligned addr + prot=0 -> EINVAL (isolates alignment
         cause; previously used prot=3 which would also EINVAL
         post-batch via the new gate, masking the alignment
         path).
       * prot=PROT_READ (1) -> EINVAL (was: ENOSYS pre-batch).
       * prot=PROT_READ|WRITE|EXEC (7) -> EINVAL (was: ENOSYS
         pre-batch — fully inside the 0x37 mask).
       * prot=0x80 -> EINVAL (was: EINVAL pre-batch via the
         bit-mask gate; same errno, new cause).
       * prot=0x1_0000_0000 -> EINVAL (i32-truncation trap:
         was ENOSYS pre-batch; now caught by the u64 compare).
       * aligned + prot=0 -> ENOSYS (the actual "valid" path:
         Linux's only accepted prot is 0, and we have no
         non-linear mapping backing).

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   remap_file_pages prot!=0 EINVAL
          (all non-zero prots): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (38.62s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

399. [BATCH 369 — 2026-06-06 — sys_shmctl IPC_INFO/SHM_INFO buf-first
     gate routing (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)]

     Linux divergence: pre-batch sys_shmctl treated EVERY cmd
     uniformly as "no segment exists, return EINVAL" after the
     `cmd<0 || shmid<0` early-EINVAL gate.  That is correct for
     the per-segment cmds (IPC_RMID/IPC_SET/IPC_STAT/SHM_STAT/
     SHM_STAT_ANY/SHM_LOCK/SHM_UNLOCK) and for the `default` arm
     of Linux's switch — Linux's per-segment cmds run
     `ipc_obtain_object_check(shmid)` first, which returns
     -EINVAL when no segment matches.

     But it is WRONG for IPC_INFO (cmd=3) and SHM_INFO (cmd=14).
     Those two cmds are GLOBAL info queries — Linux's
     `shmctl_ipc_info` / `shmctl_shm_info` does NO segment
     lookup at all; it zeros a stack struct and calls
     `copy_shminfo_to_user(buf, ...)` (or `copy_to_user(buf,
     &shm_info, sizeof(shm_info))`) UNCONDITIONALLY.  So:

       * shmctl(0, IPC_INFO, NULL) -> EFAULT (copy_to_user
         faults on the NULL dest), not EINVAL.
       * shmctl(0, IPC_INFO, &valid_buf) -> 0 (success,
         shminfo64 populated; ipc_get_maxidx returns 0 when
         no segments).  Pre-batch returned EINVAL.

     Source: ipc/shm.c::ksys_shmctl -> ksys_shmctl_default ->
     case IPC_INFO: { shmctl_ipc_info; copy_shminfo_to_user; }
     case SHM_INFO: { shmctl_shm_info; copy_to_user; }
     The default-EINVAL and per-segment-EINVAL paths use
     `ipc_obtain_object_check` which fails before buf is
     touched — those keep their pre-batch EINVAL terminal.

     Fix: route IPC_INFO and SHM_INFO through a buf-first gate
     identical to the structure Linux uses:

       1. cmd<0 || shmid<0 -> EINVAL (unchanged).
       2. if cmd == IPC_INFO || cmd == SHM_INFO:
              buf == NULL -> EFAULT
              validate_user_write(buf, 1) fail -> EFAULT
              else -> ENOSYS  (no SysV IPC backing, same
                                pattern as shmget/msgget/semget
                                after their gate validation)
       3. else (per-segment cmds + default unknown cmd):
              -> EINVAL  (segment lookup fails first; default
                          arm also EINVAL)

     ENOSYS for the "valid buf" case (vs. Linux's success-0)
     is the established translator-pattern terminal — every
     other SysV IPC stub returns ENOSYS after its gate.  The
     ABI shape that matters for userspace probing is the
     EFAULT-vs-EINVAL distinction for the NULL/unmapped buf
     case; that now matches Linux.

     Probes added (5 new) to kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
     boot self-test block, after the existing
     "shmctl id/cmd<0, no pre-buf validate: OK" stamp:

       * shmctl(0, IPC_INFO=3, NULL) -> EFAULT (was: EINVAL
         pre-batch).
       * shmctl(0, SHM_INFO=14, NULL) -> EFAULT (was: EINVAL
         pre-batch; same gate).
       * shmctl(0, IPC_INFO=3, &stack_buf) -> ENOSYS (was:
         EINVAL pre-batch; the most important fix — buf-with-
         data path now matches translator pattern).
       * shmctl(0, SHM_INFO=14, &stack_buf) -> ENOSYS (was:
         EINVAL pre-batch).
       * shmctl(0, IPC_STAT=2, &stack_buf) -> EINVAL (per-
         segment cmd: segment lookup fails before buf
         touched; pre-batch EINVAL preserved — VERIFIES that
         the new IPC_INFO/SHM_INFO routing is not
         over-broad).
       * shmctl(0, SHM_STAT_ANY=15, NULL) -> EINVAL (per-
