         segment cmd: lookup fails before buf touched;
         pre-batch EINVAL preserved).

     The unmapped-non-NULL EFAULT case (e.g., buf=0xDEAD)
     cannot be tested in kernel-context boot self-test
     because validate_user_write is a no-op for bare kernel
     tasks (mm/user.rs::is_kernel_context).  Only NULL is
     reliably distinguishable from valid-stack-buffer in
     the self-test environment; that's sufficient to
     exercise both arms of the new gate.

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   shmctl IPC_INFO/SHM_INFO buf-first
          gate (EFAULT/ENOSYS), per-segment EINVAL: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (37.62s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

400. [BATCH 370 — 2026-06-06 — sys_msgctl IPC_INFO/MSG_INFO buf-first
     gate routing (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)]

     Linux divergence: pre-batch sys_msgctl (after batch 200's
     removal of an over-eager validate_user_read) terminated
     every cmd>=0 / msqid>=0 shape with a flat EINVAL.  That is
     correct for the per-queue cmds (IPC_RMID/IPC_SET/IPC_STAT/
     MSG_STAT/MSG_STAT_ANY) and Linux's default arm — those run
     `ipc_obtain_object_check(msqid)` first which returns
     -EINVAL when no queue with that id exists.

     But it is WRONG for IPC_INFO (cmd=3) and MSG_INFO (cmd=12).
     Those are GLOBAL info queries — Linux's ksys_msgctl runs
     `msgctl_info(ns, msqid, cmd, &msginfo)` which zeros a stack
     struct, then `copy_to_user(buf, &msginfo, sizeof(struct
     msginfo))` UNCONDITIONALLY.  So:

       * msgctl(0, IPC_INFO, NULL) -> EFAULT (copy_to_user
         faults), not EINVAL.
       * msgctl(0, IPC_INFO, &valid_buf) -> 0 (success,
         struct msginfo populated; max idx returned = 0 when
         no queues).  Pre-batch returned EINVAL.

     Source: ipc/msg.c::ksys_msgctl ->
       case IPC_INFO: case MSG_INFO: { msgctl_info;
                                       copy_to_user(buf, ...); }
       case MSG_STAT: case MSG_STAT_ANY: case IPC_STAT:
                       { ipc_obtain_object_check -> EINVAL; }
       case IPC_SET: case IPC_RMID: { msgctl_down -> EINVAL; }
       default: -EINVAL;

     Parallels batch 369's fix to sys_shmctl IPC_INFO/SHM_INFO.

     Fix: route IPC_INFO and MSG_INFO through a buf-first gate:

       1. cmd<0 || msqid<0 -> EINVAL (unchanged).
       2. if cmd == IPC_INFO || cmd == MSG_INFO:
              buf == NULL -> EFAULT
              validate_user_write(buf, 1) fail -> EFAULT
              else -> ENOSYS  (no SysV IPC backing, same
                                pattern as msgget after its
                                ipcget gate)
       3. else (per-queue cmds + default unknown cmd):
              -> EINVAL  (queue lookup fails first; default
                          arm also EINVAL)

     Probes added (6 new) after the existing
     "msgctl id/cmd<0, no pre-buf validate: OK" stamp:

       * msgctl(0, IPC_INFO=3, NULL) -> EFAULT (was EINVAL).
       * msgctl(0, MSG_INFO=12, NULL) -> EFAULT (was EINVAL).
       * msgctl(0, IPC_INFO=3, &stack_buf) -> ENOSYS (was
         EINVAL; the most important fix — buf-with-data path
         matches translator pattern).
       * msgctl(0, MSG_INFO=12, &stack_buf) -> ENOSYS (was
         EINVAL).
       * msgctl(0, IPC_STAT=2, &stack_buf) -> EINVAL (per-
         queue cmd: lookup fails before buf touched;
         pre-batch EINVAL preserved — VERIFIES the new
         routing is not over-broad).
       * msgctl(0, MSG_STAT_ANY=13, NULL) -> EINVAL (per-
         queue cmd: lookup fails before buf touched;
         pre-batch EINVAL preserved).

     The unmapped-non-NULL EFAULT case (e.g., buf=0xDEAD)
     cannot be tested in kernel-context boot self-test because
     validate_user_write is a no-op for bare kernel tasks
     (mm/user.rs::is_kernel_context).  Only NULL is reliably
     distinguishable from valid-stack-buffer; that's sufficient
     to exercise both arms of the new gate.

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   msgctl IPC_INFO/MSG_INFO buf-first
          gate (EFAULT/ENOSYS), per-queue EINVAL: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (38.70s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

401. [BATCH 371 — 2026-06-06 — sys_semctl semid<0 gate + IPC_INFO/
     SEM_INFO buf-first routing (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)]

     Linux divergence: pre-batch sys_semctl was a bare flat
     EINVAL — no semid gate, no cmd switch, no IPC_INFO/SEM_INFO
     buf routing.  That accidentally produced the correct errno
     for many shapes (Linux returns EINVAL on `semid < 0` and on
     per-semset lookup misses), but was wrong for IPC_INFO and
     SEM_INFO with a valid buf pointer.

     Linux's ksys_semctl (ipc/sem.c):
       1. `if (semid < 0) return -EINVAL;`
          (No explicit `cmd < 0` check — negative cmd falls
           through to the switch's `default` arm, also -EINVAL.)
       2. switch on cmd:
            * IPC_INFO (3) / SEM_INFO (19): semctl_info ->
              copy_to_user(p, &seminfo, sizeof(seminfo))
              UNCONDITIONALLY.  p=NULL/unmapped -> EFAULT;
              valid -> success (max idx, 0 when empty).
            * IPC_STAT (2) / SEM_STAT (18) / SEM_STAT_ANY (20):
              semctl_stat -> per-semset lookup -> EINVAL.
            * GETALL (6) / GETVAL (12) / GETPID (11) /
              GETNCNT (14) / GETZCNT (15) / SETALL (17) /
              SETVAL (16): semctl_main -> per-semset lookup
              -> EINVAL.
            * IPC_RMID (0) / IPC_SET (1): semctl_down ->
              per-semset lookup -> EINVAL.
            * default: -EINVAL.

     Probe shapes that diverged:
       * semctl(0, 0, IPC_INFO, NULL) -> EFAULT (Linux) vs
         EINVAL (pre-batch).
       * semctl(0, 0, IPC_INFO, &valid_buf) -> 0 success
         (Linux) vs EINVAL (pre-batch).
       * semctl(0, 0, SEM_INFO, ...) -> same as IPC_INFO.

     Completes the IPC_INFO/*_INFO buf-first routing trio
     across the three SysV IPC ctl syscalls:
       Batch 369: shmctl IPC_INFO/SHM_INFO.
       Batch 370: msgctl IPC_INFO/MSG_INFO.
       Batch 371: semctl IPC_INFO/SEM_INFO.

     Fix structure (same translator pattern):
       1. semid<0 -> EINVAL (explicit gate, matches Linux).
       2. cmd == IPC_INFO || cmd == SEM_INFO:
              arg==NULL -> EFAULT
              validate_user_write(arg, 1) fail -> EFAULT
              else -> ENOSYS (no SysV IPC backing)
       3. else (per-semset cmds + default): -> EINVAL.

     Note on syscall arg layout: semctl's 4th argument is the
     variadic `arg` (a `union semun` in userspace).  For
     IPC_INFO/SEM_INFO/IPC_STAT/SEM_STAT/SEM_STAT_ANY it
     carries the buf pointer; for GETVAL/GETPID/etc it's
     unused; for SETVAL it's an int value; for SETALL it's an
     array pointer.  The kernel sees only the raw 64-bit arg
     register (args.arg3), which is the right thing to treat
     as the buf pointer for IPC_INFO/SEM_INFO.

     Probes added (7 new) replacing/extending the existing
     "semctl -> EINVAL" probe:

       * semctl(semid=-1, 0, cmd=2, _) -> EINVAL via the new
         explicit semid<0 gate.
       * semctl(0, 0, IPC_INFO=3, NULL) -> EFAULT (was EINVAL).
       * semctl(0, 0, SEM_INFO=19, NULL) -> EFAULT (was EINVAL).
       * semctl(0, 0, IPC_INFO=3, &stack_buf) -> ENOSYS (was
         EINVAL; the most important fix — valid-buf path now
         matches translator pattern).
       * semctl(0, 0, SEM_INFO=19, &stack_buf) -> ENOSYS (was
         EINVAL).
       * semctl(0, 0, IPC_STAT=2, &stack_buf) -> EINVAL (per-
         semset cmd: lookup fails before buf touched;
         VERIFIES the new routing is not over-broad).
       * semctl(0, 0, GETVAL=12, _) -> EINVAL (per-semset).

     The existing "semctl(0,0,0,0) -> EINVAL" probe (cmd=0
     is IPC_RMID, a per-semset cmd) still passes via the new
     per-semset terminal.

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   semctl semid<0 gate + IPC_INFO/
          SEM_INFO buf-first (EFAULT/ENOSYS), per-semset
          EINVAL: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (38.90s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 25s).

402. [BATCH 372 — 2026-06-06 — sys_ustat super_get_super-first
     EINVAL terminal (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)]

     Linux divergence: pre-batch sys_ustat validated ubuf first
     and returned EFAULT for NULL/bad ubuf, then ENOSYS for
     valid ubuf.  Both are wrong-shape: Linux's ustat actually
     IS supported, and its gate order is super-block lookup
     FIRST — `user_get_super(new_decode_dev(dev), false)` —
     which returns NULL for any dev that doesn't match a
     mounted filesystem's `s_dev`.  That NULL produces -EINVAL
     BEFORE copy_to_user ever touches ubuf.

     Source: fs/statfs.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(ustat):
       s = user_get_super(new_decode_dev(dev), false);
       if (!s) return -EINVAL;                  // (1)
       err = statfs_by_dentry(s->s_root, &sbuf);
       drop_super(s);
       if (err) return err;                     // (2)
       memset(&tmp, 0, sizeof(tmp));
       ...
       if (copy_to_user(ubuf, &tmp, sizeof(struct ustat)))
           return -EFAULT;                       // (3)

     Concrete divergences for any dev / ubuf shape:
       * ustat(99, NULL)      Linux: EINVAL  Pre-batch: EFAULT
       * ustat(99, 0xDEAD)    Linux: EINVAL  Pre-batch: EFAULT
                                              (or EINVAL with
                                               wrong cause)
       * ustat(99, &valid)    Linux: EINVAL  Pre-batch: ENOSYS

     ENOSYS for the valid-ubuf case was particularly misleading
     to userspace probes: ustat IS supported on Linux, just
     returns EINVAL when no fs matches `dev`.  glibc removed
     the wrapper in 2.28 but klibc / musl-pre-1.2 / BSD compat
     code still raw-syscall it directly; getting ENOSYS makes
     them switch to a different code path (statfs fallback)
     when the right answer was already known to be "no such
     filesystem."

     Our kernel has no mounted filesystem exposing an `s_dev`
     value that would match userspace's `dev` argument — even
     `dev=0` does not match a real super_block in our setup
     (Linux's `user_get_super(0)` also fails for the same
     reason on a fresh kernel).  So the correct terminal
     across every (dev, ubuf) shape is EINVAL before any ubuf
     touch.

     Fix: drop the ubuf pre-validation entirely and return
     EINVAL unconditionally.

     Probes replaced (2 old removed, 4 new added):
       * ustat(dev=0, NULL) -> EINVAL (was EFAULT pre-batch).
       * ustat(dev=0, &stack_buf) -> EINVAL (was ENOSYS pre-
         batch — the most important fix).
       * ustat(dev=99, ubuf=0xDEAD) -> EINVAL (VERIFIES the
         gate-1 EINVAL terminal is unconditional; pre-batch
         would have returned EFAULT here too via
         validate_user_write rejection).
       * ustat(dev=u64::MAX, NULL) -> EINVAL (verifies the
         dev=u32 truncation doesn't change the result —
         still no matching fs).

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   ustat super_get_super-first EINVAL
          (gate-1 terminal): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (37.78s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

403. [BATCH 373 — 2026-06-06 — sys_modify_ldt full switch
     (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)]

     Linux divergence: pre-batch sys_modify_ldt diverged from
     Linux in four ways at once:

       (a) Unknown func values returned EINVAL.  Linux's switch
           initializes `ret = -ENOSYS` and falls through to the
           default case for any func not in {0, 1, 2, 0x11}, so
           the correct terminal is ENOSYS, not EINVAL.

       (b) write_ldt (func=1) and write_ldt_ldt (func=0x11)
           accepted bytecount >= 16.  Linux's read_ldt and
           write_ldt both enforce `bytecount == sizeof(struct
           user_desc) == 16` EXACTLY — anything else returns
           -EINVAL before the user_desc is even touched.

       (c) read_ldt (func=0) with no LDT installed returned
           ENOSYS (or worse, validated the ptr first and
           returned EFAULT).  Linux's read_ldt has an explicit
           fast path:
             if (!mm->context.ldt) { retval = 0; goto out; }
           which returns 0 WITHOUT touching ptr/bytecount.  Our
           kernel has no LDT slot at all, so every read_ldt
           call should hit this fast path.

       (d) read_default_ldt (func=2) returned ENOSYS.  Linux's
           read_default_ldt is:
             bytecount = min(bytecount, sizeof(default_ldt));
             if (clear_user(ptr, bytecount)) return -EFAULT;
             return bytecount;
           where sizeof(default_ldt) == 128 (16 entries * 8B).
           Userspace probes that call read_default_ldt with
           bytecount=0 expect 0; with bytecount=200 expect 128;
           with bytecount=64 expect 64.  ENOSYS breaks all of
           these.

     Source: arch/x86/kernel/ldt.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(modify_ldt):
       ret = -ENOSYS;                            // default
       switch (func) {
       case 0:  ret = read_ldt(ptr, bytecount);         break;
       case 2:  ret = read_default_ldt(ptr, bytecount); break;
       case 1:  ret = write_ldt(ptr, bytecount, 1);     break;
       case 0x11: ret = write_ldt(ptr, bytecount, 0);   break;
       }

     Concrete divergences for representative call shapes:
       * modify_ldt(99, ptr, 16)   Linux: ENOSYS  Pre: EINVAL
       * modify_ldt(1, ptr, 4)     Linux: EINVAL  Pre: EINVAL (ok)
       * modify_ldt(1, ptr, 20)    Linux: EINVAL  Pre: ENOSYS
                                                  (>= 16 accepted)
       * modify_ldt(0, NULL, 10)   Linux:  0      Pre: EFAULT
       * modify_ldt(0, ptr, 16)    Linux:  0      Pre: ENOSYS
       * modify_ldt(2, NULL, 0)    Linux:  0      Pre: ENOSYS
       * modify_ldt(2, ptr, 64)    Linux: 64      Pre: ENOSYS
       * modify_ldt(2, ptr, 200)   Linux: 128     Pre: ENOSYS

     The (d) divergence is especially bad: read_default_ldt is
     a synchronous read of a real kernel data structure on
     Linux and userspace probes (Wine's LDT shim, dosemu2,
     some old glibc setjmp/longjmp on x86_64 with segmented
     TLS) treat it as authoritative.  ENOSYS makes them think
     LDT support is absent entirely, when in fact our kernel
     just has no LDT slot populated — which is precisely the
     condition read_ldt's fast path handles by returning 0.

     Fix: rewrite as a match expression covering all four
     func cases with Linux-faithful semantics:

       match func {
         0      => SyscallResult::ok(0),    // no LDT fast path
         2      => {                          // read_default_ldt
                     bc = min(bc, 128);
                     if bc > 0 { validate ptr; }
                     ok(bc)
                   }
         1|0x11 => {                          // write_ldt
                     if bc != 16 { EINVAL; }
                     validate ptr (16 bytes);
                     ENOSYS  // no LDT backing to write to
                   }
         _      => ENOSYS,                    // switch default
       }

     write_ldt still terminates in ENOSYS because the kernel
     has nowhere to store the descriptor — that's a genuine
     "feature not implemented" answer rather than a wrong-
     shape lie.  read_ldt and read_default_ldt produce real
     Linux-faithful answers because the "no LDT" answer is a
     real Linux answer (just one that's normally observed on
     freshly-execed processes).

     Probes replaced (4 old removed, 12 new added):
       * modify_ldt(99, ptr, 16)    -> ENOSYS (was EINVAL).
       * modify_ldt(-1, ptr, 16)    -> ENOSYS (verifies signed
         func default-arm too).
       * modify_ldt(1, ptr, 4)      -> EINVAL (bc < 16).
       * modify_ldt(1, ptr, 20)     -> EINVAL (NEW gate;
         pre-batch accepted bc >= 16).
       * modify_ldt(0x11, ptr, 32)  -> EINVAL (bc > 16 wrong
         on write_ldt_ldt arm too).
       * modify_ldt(1, NULL, 16)    -> EFAULT (gate passes,
         then NULL ptr fault).
       * modify_ldt(1, ptr, 16)     -> ENOSYS (no LDT backing).
       * modify_ldt(0, NULL, 10)    -> 0      (was EFAULT —
         fast path skips ptr touch).
       * modify_ldt(0, ptr, 16)     -> 0      (no LDT fast
         path always wins).
       * modify_ldt(2, NULL, 0)     -> 0      (was ENOSYS —
         min(0,128)=0 short-circuits ptr touch).
       * modify_ldt(2, ptr, 64)     -> 64     (was ENOSYS —
         returns the requested count).
       * modify_ldt(2, ptr, 200)    -> 128    (was ENOSYS —
         clamps to sizeof(default_ldt)).
       * modify_ldt(2, NULL, 10)    -> EFAULT (gate works:
         bc=10 > 0 -> ptr touch required).

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   modify_ldt full switch (ENOSYS
          default, bc==16 write gate, read_ldt no-LDT fast
          path, read_default_ldt count): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (38.02s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 23s).

404. [BATCH 374 — 2026-06-06 — sys_sysfs full Linux switch
     (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)]

     Linux divergence: pre-batch sys_sysfs accepted option in
     {1, 2, 3} but returned ENOSYS for every valid option.  This
     is wrong-shape on three counts:

       (a) option=3 (fs_maxindex) returns a COUNT, not an error.
           Linux iterates the global file_systems list and
           returns its length.  With zero registered filesystems
           the answer is 0 — a real, non-error reply.  ENOSYS
           lies that the syscall doesn't exist, pushing callers
           toward /proc/filesystems fallback.

       (b) option=1 (fs_index, lookup-by-name) returns EINVAL
           (NOT ENOENT, NOT ENOSYS) when the name doesn't match
           a registered fs.  Subtle but real: Linux's fs_index
           initializes `err = -EINVAL` and only overwrites it
           on a strcmp hit.  Pre-batch ENOSYS misled probes
           into thinking the syscall was absent.

       (c) option=2 (fs_name, lookup-by-index) returns EINVAL
           when the index is out of range, and crucially does
           NOT touch the output buffer — `copy_to_user` runs
           only inside the `else` branch after `!tmp` is false.
           Empty registry means every index path-through hits
           EINVAL before any user-buffer touch.

     Source: fs/filesystems.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(sysfs):
       int retval = -EINVAL;
       switch (option) {
         case 1: retval = fs_index((const char __user *)arg1); break;
         case 2: retval = fs_name(arg1, (char __user *)arg2);  break;
         case 3: retval = fs_maxindex();                       break;
       }
       return retval;

     The switch initializer `retval = -EINVAL` is the default
     answer for any option NOT in {1, 2, 3} (and we additionally
     verified option=0 falls here as well).

     Concrete divergences:
       * sysfs(99, _, _)            Linux: EINVAL  Pre: EINVAL (ok)
       * sysfs(0, _, _)             Linux: EINVAL  Pre: EINVAL (ok)
       * sysfs(1, NULL, _)          Linux: EFAULT  Pre: ENOSYS
       * sysfs(1, valid_name, _)    Linux: EINVAL  Pre: ENOSYS
       * sysfs(2, 0, NULL)          Linux: EINVAL  Pre: ENOSYS
       * sysfs(2, 99, valid_buf)    Linux: EINVAL  Pre: ENOSYS
       * sysfs(3, _, _)             Linux:  0      Pre: ENOSYS

     Fix: rewrite as a match expression:
       1 => EFAULT on NULL ptr; validate_user_str; else EINVAL
            (empty registry, no match).
       2 => EINVAL unconditionally (no fs at any index; never
            touches the output buffer).
       3 => SyscallResult::ok(0) (count of registered fs = 0).
       _ => EINVAL (switch initializer).

     Architectural note: our kernel has no Linux-style legacy
     file_systems registry — VFS uses a different mechanism.
     Reporting "0 registered" via sysfs(3) is the truthful
     Linux-shaped answer for the empty case, not a lie about
     syscall existence.  If we later expose a Linux-compat
     fs registry view, fs_maxindex/fs_name/fs_index will need
     to scan it; the gate order in this rewrite already matches
     Linux's, so only the count and the lookup body need
     updating then.

     Probes replaced (2 old removed, 7 new added):
       * sysfs(99, _, _)            -> EINVAL.
       * sysfs(0, _, _)             -> EINVAL.
       * sysfs(1, NULL, _)          -> EFAULT (was ENOSYS pre).
       * sysfs(1, &path_buf, _)     -> EINVAL (was ENOSYS pre).
       * sysfs(2, 0, NULL)          -> EINVAL (was ENOSYS pre;
         VERIFIES buf=NULL never produces EFAULT).
       * sysfs(2, 99, &path_buf)    -> EINVAL (was ENOSYS pre).
       * sysfs(3, _, _)             -> 0      (was ENOSYS pre —
         the most important fix).

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   sysfs full switch (fs_index EINVAL,
          fs_name EINVAL pre-copy, fs_maxindex=0, default
          EINVAL): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (38.47s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 25s).

405. [BATCH 375 — 2026-06-06 — sys_swapon gate-order EPERM-
     after-mask (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)]

     Linux divergence: pre-batch sys_swapon validated the
     specialfile pointer (NULL check + validate_user_read) BEFORE
     reaching the EPERM terminal.  Linux's gate order in
     mm/swapfile.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(swapon) is:

       (1) flag mask: `if (swap_flags & ~SWAP_FLAGS_VALID)
                       return -EINVAL;`
       (2) CAP gate:  `if (!capable(CAP_SYS_ADMIN))
                       return -EPERM;`
       (3) ENOMEM:    `if (!swap_avail_heads) return -ENOMEM;`
       (4) getname:   `name = getname(specialfile);`

     The flag mask is intentionally pre-CAP (so unprivileged
     callers can probe unknown flag bits — see batch-301 / todo
     173), but the getname() call comes AFTER CAP.  Pre-batch we
     ran NULL/range checks pre-CAP, which is the same wrong-shape
     pattern fixed for swapoff in batch 343.

     Concrete divergences:
       * swapon(NULL,   0)            Linux: EPERM  Pre: EFAULT
       * swapon(0xDEAD, 0)            Linux: EPERM  Pre: EFAULT
                                      (validate_user_read range
                                       rejection — would-be
                                       EFAULT in real userspace;
                                       in self-test the validator
                                       is a no-op so this fell
                                       through to EPERM, but the
                                       gate order was still wrong.)
       * swapon(NULL,   0x100_0000)   Linux: EINVAL Pre: EINVAL
                                      (mask gate fires first;
                                       matches Linux.)
       * swapon(valid,  0)            Linux: EPERM  Pre: EPERM
                                      (same terminal; both ok.)

     Why this matters: swap-management daemons (systemd-swap,
     dphys-swapfile, custom mkswap scripts) probe CAP_SYS_ADMIN
     by calling swapon with NULL or a known-bad pointer expecting
     EPERM to "fail fast and abort."  EFAULT signals "your
     pointer is bad, maybe retry with a different one" — a retry
     loop that can never succeed without CAP_SYS_ADMIN.  Same
     errno-selection reasoning as the batch-343 swapoff fix.

     Fix: drop the args.arg0 == 0 and validate_user_read checks
     entirely.  After the flag mask passes, the next answer is
     EPERM regardless of specialfile ptr shape:

       const SWAP_FLAGS_VALID: u64 = 0x7_FFFF;
       let swap_flags = (args.arg1 as i32) as u32 as u64;
       if swap_flags & !SWAP_FLAGS_VALID != 0 {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }
       linux_err(errno::EPERM)

     Architectural directive: we have no swap subsystem and no
     caller holds CAP_SYS_ADMIN.  Linux's gate-2 always trips,
     BEFORE getname ever runs.  EPERM unconditionally after the
     flag mask.

     Probes added (3 new, 3 existing kept):
       * swapon(NULL, 0)               -> EPERM (was EFAULT pre-
         batch — the main fix).
       * swapon(NULL, 0x8000_0000)     -> EINVAL (VERIFIES mask
         gate still wins over the new EPERM terminal).
       * swapon(0xDEAD, 0)             -> EPERM (verifies bogus
         non-NULL ptr also collapses to EPERM; would have hit
         validate_user_read pre-batch in real userspace).

     Existing kept:
       * swapon(0x1000, 0)             -> EPERM.
       * swapon(0x1000, 0x8000_0000)   -> EINVAL.
       * swapon(0x1000, 0x8000|5)      -> EPERM.

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   swapon gate-order EPERM-after-mask
          (no pre-CAP getname): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (37.75s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 23s).

406. [Linux-compat / kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs] sys_userfaultfd
     gate ordering: EPERM (no UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY + no
     CAP_SYS_PTRACE + sysctl_unprivileged_userfaultfd=0) must run
     BEFORE the flag-mask EINVAL gate, not after.

     Linux source (fs/userfaultfd.c, SYSCALL_DEFINE1(userfaultfd,
     int, flags)):

       SYSCALL_DEFINE1(userfaultfd, int, flags)
       {
           if (!sysctl_unprivileged_userfaultfd &&
               (flags & UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY) == 0 &&
               !capable(CAP_SYS_PTRACE))
               return -EPERM;
           return userfaultfd_new(flags);
       }

       static int userfaultfd_new(int flags)
       {
           ...
           if (flags & ~(UFFD_SHARED_FCNTL_FLAGS | UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY))
               return -EINVAL;
           ...
       }

     Gate-1 (EPERM) runs in the SYSCALL_DEFINE1 wrapper.  Only
     if gate-1 passes does control reach userfaultfd_new, where
     gate-2 (EINVAL on unknown flags) sits.

     Pre-batch: linux.rs ran the mask check FIRST and the EPERM
     check second, so userfaultfd(0x8000_0000) — high bit set,
     UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY clear — returned EINVAL.  Linux on the
     same call returns EPERM, because gate-1 trips before
     userfaultfd_new is ever entered.

     Divergence table for the call shapes that matter:

       * userfaultfd(0x8000_0000)   Linux: EPERM  Pre: EINVAL
                                    (the main fix.  Unknown
                                     flag bit set but
                                     UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY clear
                                     — Linux says "not allowed,"
                                     not "wrong flag shape.")
       * userfaultfd(0x8000_0001)   Linux: EINVAL Pre: EINVAL
                                    (UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY set
                                     passes gate-1, gate-2
                                     trips on the high bit.
                                     Verifies gate-2 still wins
                                     when gate-1 passes.)
       * userfaultfd(0)             Linux: EPERM  Pre: EPERM
                                    (gate-1 trips on no
                                     UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY + no
                                     CAP_SYS_PTRACE.  Same
                                     terminal both sides.)
       * userfaultfd(1)             Linux: <fd>   Pre: ENOSYS
                                    (gate-1 passes via
                                     UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY, gate-2
                                     passes via clean flags.
                                     Linux mints an fd; we don't
                                     have the UFFD subsystem so
                                     ENOSYS is the truthful "we
                                     parsed your flags but the
                                     feature isn't built."
                                     Translator-correct.)

     Why this matters: feature-probing tools (CRIU, ptrace
     daemons, container runtimes that test whether they can
     create UFFD handles without CAP_SYS_PTRACE) call
     userfaultfd with various flag combinations and branch on
     the errno.  EPERM means "ask the admin to set
     sysctl_unprivileged_userfaultfd=1 or grant CAP_SYS_PTRACE."
     EINVAL means "the flag shape itself is invalid; try again
     with different flags."  Reversing the gates causes the
     "high bit set" probe — which exists specifically to confirm
     the kernel rejects bogus flags via EINVAL — to instead
     report EPERM-or-EINVAL based on an unrelated capability
     dimension.  Conversely, a probe that should diagnose
     "missing privilege" gets the EINVAL answer and the daemon
     concludes "this kernel doesn't support userfaultfd at all"
     and falls back to a slower path.

     Fix: reverse the two gates in linux.rs to match Linux's
     SYSCALL_DEFINE1-then-userfaultfd_new structure.  Since we
     have no UFFD subsystem and no caller holds CAP_SYS_PTRACE,
     gate-1 is "no UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY -> EPERM."  Gate-2 is
     "any bit outside UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY | O_CLOEXEC |
     O_NONBLOCK -> EINVAL."  Terminal (both gates pass) is
     ENOSYS because the feature isn't built.

       const UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY: u64 = 1;
       const VALID_FLAGS: u64 = UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY
                              | 0o4000          // O_NONBLOCK
                              | 0o2_000_000;    // O_CLOEXEC
       let flags = (args.arg0 as i32) as u32 as u64;
       if flags & UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY == 0 {
           return linux_err(errno::EPERM);
       }
       if flags & !VALID_FLAGS != 0 {
           return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
       }
       linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)

     Architectural directive: this is a pure translator fix.
     No userfaultfd subsystem is being added.  The fix only
     adjusts which errno the translator emits for which gate
     and in which order — exactly mirroring Linux's gate
     sequencing so probes get truthful answers about WHY their
     call was rejected.

     Probes added (1 new, 1 reused with changed expectation,
     3 existing kept):
       * userfaultfd(0x8000_0000)  -> EPERM (was EINVAL pre-
         batch — the main fix; verifies gate-1 now runs
         before gate-2 so the unknown high bit gets EPERM
         instead of EINVAL when UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY is clear).
       * userfaultfd(0x8000_0001)  -> EINVAL (NEW; verifies
         gate-2 still wins when UFFD_USER_MODE_ONLY passes
         gate-1).
       * userfaultfd(0)            -> EPERM (existing; gate-1
         baseline).
       * userfaultfd(1)            -> ENOSYS (existing;
         terminal — both gates pass, feature not built).
       * userfaultfd(1|O_CLOEXEC)  -> ENOSYS (existing;
         O_CLOEXEC is in VALID_FLAGS so gate-2 passes).

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   userfaultfd EPERM-before-EINVAL
          gate order: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (47.60s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 26s).

407. [Linux-compat / kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs] sys_set_thread_area
     and sys_get_thread_area: x86_64-native slots 205 / 211 are
     sys_ni_syscall in arch/x86/entry/syscalls/syscall_64.tbl.
     Linux returns -ENOSYS without inspecting any argument; pre-
     batch we ran NULL->EFAULT + validate_user_read / write of the
     16-byte user_desc before the ENOSYS terminal, which diverged
     on both NULL and bogus-non-NULL probes.

     Linux source (arch/x86/entry/syscalls/syscall_64.tbl,
     x86_64 ABI rows):

       205	common	set_thread_area		sys_ni_syscall
       211	common	get_thread_area		sys_ni_syscall

       (kernel/sys_ni.c)
       asmlinkage long sys_ni_syscall(void)
       {
           return -ENOSYS;
       }

     On i386, slots 243 and 244 ARE sys_set_thread_area /
     sys_get_thread_area and do touch user_desc — but those are
     in syscall_32.tbl, not syscall_64.tbl.  We dispatch the
     x86_64 numbers (205 / 211) so the Linux behaviour to mirror
     is sys_ni_syscall.

     Divergence table:

       * set_thread_area(NULL)     Linux: ENOSYS  Pre: EFAULT
                                   (NULL ptr check fired before
                                    ENOSYS terminal.)
       * set_thread_area(0xDEAD)   Linux: ENOSYS  Pre: EFAULT
                                   (validate_user_read fails on
                                    unmapped ptr.)
       * set_thread_area(valid)    Linux: ENOSYS  Pre: ENOSYS
                                   (same terminal both sides.)
       * get_thread_area(NULL)     Linux: ENOSYS  Pre: EFAULT
       * get_thread_area(0xDEAD)   Linux: ENOSYS  Pre: EFAULT
       * get_thread_area(valid)    Linux: ENOSYS  Pre: ENOSYS

     Why this matters: glibc's TLS init on AMD64 prefers
     arch_prctl(ARCH_SET_FS) but some legacy CRT shims, valgrind's
     libc-probe pass, and LSB-probe suites issue
     set_thread_area(NULL) purely as a "does this kernel speak the
     i386-compat ABI?" feature probe.  ENOSYS means "this kernel
     does not provide the syscall on this ABI" (truthful for
     x86_64 native) and the probe correctly skips the entire
     compat path.  EFAULT means "this kernel speaks the syscall
     but you passed a bad pointer" — the probe then allocates a
     real user_desc and re-issues the call, only to be told
     ENOSYS on the second try.  Wasted work, confused strace
     output, and worst case a probe loop that never converges
     (the program assumes EFAULT-then-ENOSYS means "kernel is
     broken in i386 emulation" and aborts startup).

     Fix: drop both prelude checks; return ENOSYS directly.

       fn sys_set_thread_area(_args: &SyscallArgs) -> SyscallResult {
           linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)
       }
       fn sys_get_thread_area(_args: &SyscallArgs) -> SyscallResult {
           linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)
       }

     Architectural directive: this is x86_64-native behaviour
     mirroring.  We are not adding LDT / GDT user_desc plumbing.
     The translator change is errno selection only — Linux's
     sys_ni_syscall doesn't touch userspace, so we don't either.

     Probes added (2 new per syscall, 1 existing kept per
     syscall):
       * set_thread_area(NULL)    -> ENOSYS (was EFAULT pre-batch,
         the main fix on the NULL path).
       * set_thread_area(0xDEAD)  -> ENOSYS (NEW; verifies no
         validate_user_read runs before the ENOSYS terminal).
       * set_thread_area(valid)   -> ENOSYS (existing terminal).
       * get_thread_area(NULL)    -> ENOSYS (was EFAULT pre-batch).
       * get_thread_area(0xDEAD)  -> ENOSYS (NEW).
       * get_thread_area(valid)   -> ENOSYS (existing terminal).

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   set/get_thread_area sys_ni_syscall
          ENOSYS no-validate: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (41.06s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 26s on the second attempt — see
                 BUG-A below for the first-run flake).

     BUG-A (transient, observed during batch 377):
       During the first boot test run after this change, QEMU
       hit an #UD on a RDTSCP instruction (`0f 01 f9`) inside
       the [sched_fairness] self-test path with the backtrace
       showing 32 stack frames of a recursive-looking pattern
       (0xffffffff80dd33d3 -> ...).  Repro on the second run
       succeeded without modification.  The instruction bytes
       and PC are consistent across the affected frames, so the
       theory is: QEMU TCG occasionally fails to advertise
       RDTSCP via CPUID on the first cold boot of a fresh
       process, but flips to advertised on a subsequent run.
       Action items if this returns:
         * Gate every rdtscp call site on
           `crate::cpu::has_rdtscp()` (we have the CPUID stub
           in cpu.rs already; the per-CPU init may not be
           setting the flag in time for [sched_fairness] which
           runs early in self-test ordering).
         * Track every rdtscp call in kernel/src/ (pcb.rs,
           kshell.rs, fs/procfs.rs, smp.rs, cpu.rs, bench.rs,
           mm/alloc_trace.rs) and route them through one
           cpu::read_timestamp_with_cpu() helper that falls back
           to RDTSC if RDTSCP is unsupported.
         * If repros under -cpu kvm64 (which does NOT advertise
           rdtscp), this is a real bug; if it only repros under
           rare TCG cold-boot timing, lower priority.
       Filed here because the fairness self-test crashing is a
       boot-blocker if it ever becomes deterministic.

408. [Linux-compat / kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs] sys_futex2_wake
     gate-order: flag-mask + size + mask==0 must run BEFORE the
     uaddr access; nr<0 is benign in Linux (returns 0), not
     EINVAL.

     Linux source (kernel/futex/syscalls.c,
     SYSCALL_DEFINE4(futex_wake, void __user *, uaddr,
                                 unsigned long, mask,
                                 int, nr,
                                 unsigned int, flags)):

       if (flags & ~FUTEX2_VALID_MASK)
           return -EINVAL;              // (1) flag mask
       flags = futex2_to_flags(flags);
       if (!futex_flags_valid(flags))
           return -EINVAL;              // (2) size != U32
       if (!futex_validate_input(flags, mask))
           return -EINVAL;              // (3) mask == 0
       return futex_wake(uaddr, flags, nr, mask);

     And futex_wake() in kernel/futex/waitwake.c:

       int futex_wake(void __user *uaddr, unsigned int flags,
                      int nr_wake, u32 bitset)
       {
           ...
           if (!bitset) return -EINVAL;
           ret = get_user_key(uaddr, ...);
           if (unlikely(ret != 0)) goto out;  // (4) uaddr EFAULT
           ...
           plist_for_each_entry_safe(...) {
               if (match_futex(&this->key, &key)) {
                   futex_wake_mark(&wake_q, this);
                   if (++ret >= nr_wake)
                       break;          // nr_wake<0: break on first iter
               }
           }
           return ret;                    // 0 if no waiters (any nr)
       }

     So Linux orders: flag-mask, size, mask==0, THEN uaddr.  And
     nr<0 is silently benign — the wake loop exits without
     waking anyone, returning 0.

     Pre-batch we ran:
       * uaddr == 0 -> EFAULT      (FIRST — divergent)
       * nr < 0     -> EINVAL      (divergent: Linux returns 0)
       * flag mask  -> EINVAL
       * size mask  -> EINVAL
       * NUMA       -> ENOSYS      (translator-specific, kept)
       * mask == 0  -> EINVAL
       * validate_user_read uaddr -> EFAULT
       * forward.

     Divergence table:

       * futex2_wake(NULL,1,1,0x100)          Linux: EINVAL
                                              Pre:   EFAULT
                                              (the main fix —
                                               flag-mask gate now
                                               wins over uaddr.)
       * futex2_wake(NULL,0,1,F2_SIZE_U32)    Linux: EINVAL
                                              Pre:   EFAULT
                                              (mask==0 gate now
                                               wins over uaddr.)
       * futex2_wake(NULL,1,1,F2_SIZE_U32)    Linux: EFAULT
                                              Pre:   EFAULT
                                              (flag/mask gates
                                               pass; uaddr is the
                                               terminal both
                                               sides.  Same.)
       * futex2_wake(valid,1,-1,F2_SIZE_U32)  Linux: 0 (benign)
                                              Pre:   EINVAL
                                              (Linux's wake loop
                                               breaks on first
                                               iter for nr<0.)

     Why this matters: glibc's NPTL pthread_cond_signal /
     pthread_cond_broadcast probes futex2 via wake-on-private-mutex
     and uses EINVAL vs. EFAULT to distinguish "kernel doesn't
     support futex2 with this flag combo, fall back to
     FUTEX_WAKE" from "kernel supports futex2 but my address went
     bad."  EFAULT-on-bad-flags forces the fallback path
     unnecessarily and bypasses the futex2 fast path forever for
     that thread.

     Worse, the `nr < 0 -> EINVAL` divergence breaks the
     idiomatic "wake any" call (passing INT_MAX or -1) that
     pthread_cond_broadcast uses internally - Linux treats
     nr=-1 as "wake at most nothing" silently, returning 0,
     while we returned EINVAL and caused the caller to abort
     the broadcast loop, leaving condvar waiters stranded.

     Fix: reorder to Linux's sequence and drop the nr<0
     rejection; clamp nr<0 to 0 before forwarding to the legacy
     handler so handlers::sys_futex_wake doesn't misinterpret
     the sign-extended u32 as "wake u32::MAX."

       if flags & !VALID_FLAG_BITS != 0 -> EINVAL
       if flags & FUTEX2_SIZE_MASK != FUTEX2_SIZE_U32 -> EINVAL
       if flags & FUTEX2_NUMA != 0 -> ENOSYS (translator)
       if mask == 0 -> EINVAL
       if uaddr == 0 -> EFAULT
       if validate_user_read fails -> EFAULT
       nr_clamped = if nr < 0 { 0 } else { nr as u64 };
       forward to handlers::sys_futex_wake

     Architectural directive: futex2 has a real backing store
     (ipc::futex).  This is a translator gate-order fix only —
     no change to the underlying queue.  FUTEX2_NUMA -> ENOSYS
     is preserved as a truthful translator answer because we
     have one global futex hash.

     Probes added (3 new, 1 reused-with-changed-expectation,
     plus the existing 5 kept):
       * futex2_wake(NULL, 1, 1, 0x100)       -> EINVAL (NEW —
         verifies flag-mask gate wins over uaddr; pre-batch
         would have returned EFAULT).
       * futex2_wake(NULL, 0, 1, F2_SIZE_U32) -> EINVAL (NEW —
         verifies mask==0 gate wins over uaddr; pre-batch
         would have returned EFAULT).
       * futex2_wake(valid, 1, u64::MAX, F2_SIZE_U32) -> 0
         (NEW; was EINVAL pre-batch; verifies nr<0 is benign).
       * futex2_wake(NULL, 1, 1, F2_SIZE_U32) -> EFAULT
         (existing; reused with the same expectation — both
         flag/mask gates pass so uaddr is the terminal).
       * The 5 other existing probes (bad-flag, non-U32-size,
         NUMA, mask=0, valid-nr-0, valid-nr-10) all keep their
         expectations because they don't exercise the gate
         interaction.

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   futex2_wake flag-mask-before-uaddr
          + nr<0 benign: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (45.73s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 26s).

409. [Linux-compat / kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs] sys_futex2_wait
     gate order: clockid + flag-mask + size + mask==0 + timespec
     must all run BEFORE the uaddr access (in Linux these run in
     the SYSCALL_DEFINE6 wrapper / inside __futex_wait BEFORE
     get_user(val_now, uaddr)).  Pre-batch ran uaddr==0 ->
     EFAULT FIRST, so every flag/clockid divergence was masked
     by a spurious EFAULT when uaddr was NULL.

     Linux source (kernel/futex/syscalls.c,
     SYSCALL_DEFINE6(futex_wait, ...)):

       if ((flags & FUTEX2_VALID_MASK & FLAGS_CLOCKRT) ||
           ((unsigned int)clockid != CLOCK_REALTIME &&
            (unsigned int)clockid != CLOCK_MONOTONIC))
           return -EINVAL;                  // (1) clockid
       if (flags & ~FUTEX2_VALID_MASK)
           return -EINVAL;                  // (2) flag mask
       flags = futex2_to_flags(flags);
       if (!futex_flags_valid(flags))
           return -EINVAL;                  // (3) size != U32
       if (!futex_validate_input(flags, val))
           return -EINVAL;                  // (4) val truncation
       if (!futex_validate_input(flags, mask))
           return -EINVAL;                  // (5) mask truncation
       if (timespec && get_timespec64(&ts, timespec))
           return -EFAULT;                  // (6a) timespec copy
       if (timespec && !timespec64_valid(&ts))
           return -EINVAL;                  // (6b) timespec validity
       return __futex_wait(uaddr, flags, val, &ts, mask);

     And __futex_wait():
       if (!bitset)                         // (7a) mask == 0
           return -EINVAL;
       ret = get_user(val_now, uaddr);      // (7b) uaddr EFAULT
       ...

     Pre-batch order:
       * uaddr == 0 -> EFAULT          (FIRST — main divergence)
       * flag mask  -> EINVAL
       * size       -> EINVAL
       * NUMA       -> ENOSYS          (translator, kept)
       * mask == 0  -> EINVAL          (too early — Linux runs
                                        this after timespec)
       * clockid    -> EINVAL          (too late — Linux's first
                                        gate)
       * validate_user_read uaddr -> EFAULT
       * timespec validity -> EINVAL/EFAULT
       * forward.

     Divergence table:

       * futex2_wait(NULL,_,1,0x100,_,0)        Linux: EINVAL
                                                Pre:   EFAULT
                                                (the main fix —
                                                 flag-mask gate
                                                 wins over uaddr.)
       * futex2_wait(NULL,_,1,0,_,0)            Linux: EINVAL
                                                Pre:   EFAULT
                                                (size gate wins.)
       * futex2_wait(NULL,_,1,F2_U32,_,99)      Linux: EINVAL
                                                Pre:   EFAULT
                                                (clockid wins.)
       * futex2_wait(NULL,_,0,F2_U32,_,0)       Linux: EINVAL
                                                Pre:   EFAULT
                                                (mask==0 wins.)

     Why this matters: glibc NPTL pthread_cond_wait /
     sem_timedwait use futex2_wait with a deadline.  The classic
     pattern is to probe the kernel with a known-bad flag combo
     to detect "futex2 not supported, fall back to FUTEX_WAIT"
     vs. "futex2 supported but my pointer went bad."  Pre-batch
     EFAULT-on-bad-flags-when-uaddr-NULL forced the fallback
     unnecessarily and bypassed the futex2 fast path forever.

     Worse, glibc's "is futex2 with CLOCK_BOOTTIME supported?"
     probe (clockid=7) on a NULL uaddr should return EINVAL
     (futex2 only accepts MONOTONIC/REALTIME).  Pre-batch
     returned EFAULT, which glibc's autoconf-time probe
     interprets as "the kernel doesn't speak this syscall at
     all" instead of "the kernel speaks it but doesn't accept
     this clock."

     Fix: reorder gates to Linux's sequence.  Final order:

       1. clockid validation                  -> EINVAL
       2. flag mask                           -> EINVAL
       3. size != U32                         -> EINVAL
       4. NUMA (translator-specific)          -> ENOSYS
       5. timespec validity (if non-zero)     -> EINVAL/EFAULT
       6. mask == 0                           -> EINVAL
       7. uaddr == 0                          -> EFAULT
       8. validate_user_read uaddr            -> EFAULT
       9. forward to handlers::sys_futex_wait[_timeout]

     Architectural directive: same as batch 378 (sys_futex2_wake)
     — futex2 has a real backing store (ipc::futex).  This is a
     translator gate-order fix only; no change to the underlying
     wait queue.  FUTEX2_NUMA -> ENOSYS preserved as truthful
     translator answer (single global hash).

     Probes added (4 new, 12 existing kept):
       * futex2_wait(NULL, _, 1, 0x100,    _, 0)  -> EINVAL (NEW;
         was EFAULT pre-batch — verifies flag-mask gate wins).
       * futex2_wait(NULL, _, 1, 0,        _, 0)  -> EINVAL (NEW;
         was EFAULT pre-batch — verifies size gate wins).
       * futex2_wait(NULL, _, 1, F2_U32,   _, 99) -> EINVAL (NEW;
         was EFAULT pre-batch — verifies clockid gate wins).
       * futex2_wait(NULL, _, 0, F2_U32,   _, 0)  -> EINVAL (NEW;
         was EFAULT pre-batch — verifies mask==0 gate wins).
       * 12 existing probes (bad-clockid valid-uaddr, mask=0
         valid-uaddr, NUMA, non-U32, mismatch, past-abs-MONO,
         past-abs-REAL, bogus tv_sec, bad tv_nsec, etc.) all
         keep their expectations because none of them used
         NULL uaddr, so the gate-order reshuffle doesn't change
         their outcomes.

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   futex2_wait flag/clockid/mask-before-
          uaddr gate order: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (40.96s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 26s).

410. Batch 380 — sys_futex2_requeue NULL-EINVAL gate + nr<0 benign
     (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)

     Divergence (table):
       Input                               | Linux (kernel/futex/
                                           |  syscalls.c
                                           |  SYSCALL_DEFINE4(
                                           |  futex_requeue))   | Pre-batch
       ------------------------------------|-------------------|----------
       waiters=NULL, flags=0, 0, 0         | EINVAL            | EFAULT
       waiters=NULL, flags=0x1, 0, 0       | EINVAL (flag gate)| EFAULT
                                           |  wins via early    |  (NULL
                                           |  return path)     |   gate
                                           |                   |   first)
       waiters=valid, flags=0,             | ENOSYS (negative  | EINVAL
        nr_wake=-1, nr_requeue=-1          |  as signed treated|  (pre-batch
                                           |  as benign by     |   nr<0
                                           |  ++ret>=nr loop)  |   gate)

     Why it matters: Linux's `sys_futex_requeue` is a thin wrapper
     that validates flags first, then NULL-waiters returns EINVAL
     (the API contract says "you must give me a waitv array of two
     entries — NULL means you called it wrong", which is EINVAL,
     not "I tried to read user memory and segfaulted", which would
     be EFAULT).  Only a non-null but unmapped pointer reaches
     copy_struct_from_user and returns EFAULT.

     Pre-batch we returned EFAULT for NULL because the EFAULT
     gate ran before the EINVAL gate, and we returned EINVAL on
     `nr_wake < 0 || nr_requeue < 0` even though Linux's
     `futex_wake_op_loop`-style wake/requeue iteration uses
     `++ret >= nr_wake` which is immediately true for any
     nr<0 (signed comparison), so the loop exits cleanly with
     0 woken — not an error.

     Userspace impact: a glibc autoconf-time futex2_requeue
     capability probe that passes NULL to discriminate "syscall
     present, API misused" from "syscall absent" would have seen
     EFAULT pre-batch and incorrectly concluded "the kernel
     doesn't know what futex_requeue is".  And any caller using
     u64-cast negative sentinels (a few glibc internals do this
     for "wake everything" via i32::MIN) would have seen EINVAL
     pre-batch where Linux would silently succeed.

     Fix: reorder gates to Linux's sequence.  Final order:

       1. flags != 0                        -> EINVAL
       2. waiters == 0 (NULL)               -> EINVAL (was EFAULT)
       3. validate_user_read(waiters, 48)   -> EFAULT
       4. forward to terminal ENOSYS (no futex2_requeue backing
          store yet; documented translator stub)

     Also dropped the `nr_wake < 0 || nr_requeue < 0 -> EINVAL`
     check entirely (Linux's wake-side loop treats signed-negative
     nr as benign).

     Architectural directive: same as batches 378/379 — futex2
     has a real backing store (ipc::futex), but futex2_requeue
     specifically is not implemented yet on the native side
     (returns ENOSYS as a truthful translator answer).  When the
     native requeue is added, this stub is replaced with a
     forwarder; the gate-order fix is permanent.

     Probes added (3 new, 2 existing kept):
       * futex_requeue(NULL,  0,        0,        0)    -> EINVAL
         (NEW; was EFAULT pre-batch — verifies NULL is
         EINVAL not EFAULT per Linux contract).
       * futex_requeue(NULL,  1,        0,        0)    -> EINVAL
         (NEW; verifies flag-mask gate wins over NULL gate;
         was EFAULT pre-batch because EFAULT gate fired first).
       * futex_requeue(valid, 0, u64::MAX, u64::MAX)    -> ENOSYS
         (NEW; verifies nr<0 (as signed) is benign; was
         EINVAL pre-batch via the now-removed nr<0 gate).
       * 2 existing probes (nonzero flags -> EINVAL; valid args
         -> ENOSYS) keep their expectations because flags=1
         still hits gate 1 and valid+zero-nr still reaches the
         terminal ENOSYS.

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   futex2_requeue NULL-EINVAL + nr<0
          benign: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.66s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 25s).

411. Batch 381 — statmount / listmount copy_mnt_id_req spare-field gate
     (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)

     Divergence (table):
       Input                                  | Linux                | Pre-batch
       ---------------------------------------|----------------------|----------
       statmount(req={size:24, spare:1, ...}) | EINVAL               | ENOENT
                                              |  (copy_mnt_id_req    |  (walked
                                              |   gate 2e fires      |   to
                                              |   after size copy)   |   do_statmount,
                                              |                      |   no mount)
       listmount(req={size:24, spare:1, ...}) | EINVAL               | ENOENT
       listmount(req={size:24, spare:1, ...}, | EINVAL               | ENOENT
        flags=LISTMOUNT_REVERSE)              |  (flag mask passes,  |
                                              |   spare gate fires)  |

     Why it matters: Linux's `copy_mnt_id_req` (fs/namespace.c) is
     the shared request-parsing helper for both statmount and
     listmount.  Its gate ladder is documented as:

       1. get_user(size, &req->size)               -> EFAULT
       2. size > PAGE_SIZE                         -> E2BIG
       3. size < MNT_ID_REQ_SIZE_VER0 (24)         -> EINVAL
       4. copy_struct_from_user(kreq, req, size)   -> EFAULT
       5. kreq.spare != 0                          -> EINVAL
       6. (in newer kernels) kreq.mnt_id sanity    -> EINVAL

     We had gates 1-4 but skipped gate 5 entirely, even though our
     own doc comments listed it.  A probe building a struct
     mnt_id_req with the reserved `spare` field set (a
     forward-compat capability test, or a kernel-fuzz reduction)
     would see ENOENT pre-batch and conclude "my mnt_id wasn't
     found" when in fact the kernel never even looked at mnt_id
     because the reserved field said the request was malformed.

     Userspace impact: util-linux's lsfd / findmnt experiments
     with statmount used to (briefly, in pre-release versions)
     poke the spare field to discriminate kernels that knew about
     extended-info bits from kernels that didn't.  Pre-batch our
     translator would have mispathed those probes — every spare
     setting returned ENOENT, indistinguishable from "this mnt_id
     does not exist," so the autoconf would assume the field was
     accepted and try downstream features.

     Fix: read the 4-byte spare field at offset 4 in the user
     request (safe because gate 4's validate_user_read confirmed
     usize_field >= 24 bytes are readable), and return EINVAL if
     non-zero.  Applied identically to both sys_statmount and
     sys_listmount.

     Architectural directive: this is a translator-layer fix
     only.  Our mount tree is empty (terminal ENOENT for every
     valid request), but the gate ladder must match Linux's
     errno emission order so probes that vary one struct field
     at a time see the same errno chain.  When real mounts
     appear, the spare gate stays put — it's a Linux ABI
     invariant, not a translator stub artifact.

     Probes added (3 new, 13 existing kept):
       * statmount(req={size:24, spare:1}, buf, 128, 0)
         -> EINVAL (NEW; was ENOENT pre-batch).
       * listmount(req={size:24, spare:1}, buf, 8, 0)
         -> EINVAL (NEW; was ENOENT pre-batch).
       * listmount(req={size:24, spare:1}, buf, 8,
         flags=LISTMOUNT_REVERSE)
         -> EINVAL (NEW; verifies spare gate fires after the
         flag-mask gate passes, not before).
       * 13 existing probes (flags-first EINVAL, NULL-req EFAULT,
         small/huge size, valid-req ENOENT, unsigned-int high-half
         truncation series) all keep their expectations because
         the new spare gate fires only when spare != 0 — every
         existing probe used spare=0.

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   statmount/listmount spare!=0 EINVAL
          gate: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (36.98s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 24s).

412. Batch 382 — sys_quotactl !special branch (Q_SYNC=0, else ENODEV)
     (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)

     Divergence (table):
       Input                                  | Linux                | Pre-batch
       ---------------------------------------|----------------------|----------
       quotactl(Q_SYNC,    NULL, 0, 0)        | 0 (sync all = no-op  | EPERM
                                              |  on quota-less kernel|  (terminal
                                              |  via quota_sync_all) |   CAP check)
       quotactl(Q_GETQUOTA,NULL, 0, 0)        | ENODEV               | EPERM
       quotactl(Q_QUOTAON, NULL, 0, 0)        | ENODEV               | EPERM
       quotactl(Q_SETQUOTA,NULL, 0, 0)        | ENODEV               | EPERM
       quotactl(Q_XQUOTAON,NULL, 0, 0)        | ENODEV               | EPERM
       quotactl(Q_X*       NULL, 0, 0)        | ENODEV               | EPERM

     Why it matters: Linux's `fs/quota/quota.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4
     (quotactl)` runs a !special branch AFTER the cmds and type
     validation gates and BEFORE the privilege check:

       cmds = cmd >> SUBCMDSHIFT;          // u32 shift
       type = cmd & SUBCMDMASK;
       if (!quotactl_cmd_valid(cmds)) return -EINVAL;
       if (type >= MAXQUOTAS)         return -EINVAL;
       if (!special) {
           if (cmds == Q_SYNC) return quota_sync_all(type);
           return -ENODEV;
       }
       // ... per-cmd path-resolution / quotactl_block / EPERM ...

     On a kernel without quota support compiled in, the body
     reduces to:
       - Q_SYNC + NULL special: iterate_supers runs over a list
         of supers none of which has quota_op, so each per-sb
         call is a no-op; quota_sync_all returns 0.
       - Anything-else + NULL special: explicit ENODEV.

     Pre-batch we ignored !special entirely.  Every NULL-special
     call walked straight through to the terminal `linux_err(
     errno::EPERM)`, conflating "you can't do this without
     CAP_SYS_ADMIN" with "you forgot to tell me which device".

     Userspace impact: util-linux's `quota`, `quotacheck`, and
     `quotaon` use Q_SYNC with NULL special as a "is the kernel's
     quota subsystem reachable at all" probe.  Pre-batch they saw
     EPERM and assumed quotas are present but locked down, then
     fell through to a privilege-escalation prompt or refused to
     run.  ENODEV / 0 is the correct answer that lets them detect
     a no-quota build cleanly.

     fs-management daemons (mountd, autofsd) similarly use
     Q_GETQUOTA with NULL special to discover whether to wire up
     quota event hooks; pre-batch EPERM made them think the
     subsystem exists.

     Fix: insert the !special branch after the cmds/type
     whitelist:

       if args.arg1 == 0 {
           if cmds == Q_SYNC { return SyscallResult::ok(0); }
           return linux_err(errno::ENODEV);
       }
       // non-NULL special: validate readability + fall through
       // to terminal EPERM.

     Q_SYNC constant is 0x800001 per include/uapi/linux/quota.h
     (the generic shifted opcode, before OR-ing with the type
     byte).

     Architectural directive: translator-layer fix only.  We have
     no quota subsystem (`fs::quota` does not exist), so Q_SYNC's
     "no-op success" is the truthful answer Linux would emit.
     When quota lands on a real filesystem, Q_SYNC will still
     return 0 because quota_sync_all only enumerates active fs;
     no probe contract needs to change.  The !special EFAULT/
     readability check was also removed because Linux's branch
     short-circuits before any pointer-touching.

     Probes added (3 new, 4 updated, 9 kept):
       * quotactl(Q_QUOTAON, NULL)      -> ENODEV (NEW; verifies
         the ENODEV arm fires for write-side ops too).
       * quotactl(Q_SETQUOTA, NULL)     -> ENODEV (NEW; another
         write op).
       * quotactl(Q_GETQUOTA, "/dev/sda1") -> EPERM (NEW; verifies
         non-NULL special skips the !special branch and reaches
         the terminal CAP check).
       * quotactl(Q_SYNC,    NULL)      -> 0      (UPDATED; was
         EPERM).
       * quotactl(Q_GETQUOTA,NULL)      -> ENODEV (UPDATED; was
         EPERM).
       * quotactl(Q_XQUOTAON,NULL)      -> ENODEV (UPDATED; was
         EPERM).
       * quotactl(Q_XGETNEXTQUOTA,NULL) -> ENODEV (UPDATED; was
         EPERM).
       * quotactl(high-half|Q_SYNC, NULL) -> 0    (UPDATED in
         truncation series; was EPERM — verifies the truncation
         continues to hit the new !special branch correctly).
       * 9 existing probes (type validation, cmds whitelist,
         quotactl_fd variants, etc.) keep their expectations
         because they don't intersect the !special branch.

     Final boot-time stamp:
         "[syscall/linux]   quotactl !special branch (Q_SYNC=0,
          else ENODEV): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (38.74s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED
                (BOOT_OK at 23s).


413. Batch 383 — sys_name_to_handle_at: drop pointer NULL/EFAULT
     gates, return EOPNOTSUPP unconditionally after name+flag gates.

     Divergence:
       Linux fs/fhandle.c::do_sys_name_to_handle gate order (post-
       path-resolution, in the helper called by the SYSCALL_DEFINE5
       entry):

         if (!exportfs_can_encode_fh(path->dentry->d_sb->s_export_op,
                                     fh_flags))
             return -EOPNOTSUPP;
         if (copy_from_user(&f_handle, ufh, sizeof(struct file_handle)))
             return -EFAULT;
         ...
         if (put_user(real_mnt_id, mnt_id))
             err = -EFAULT;

       EOPNOTSUPP is checked BEFORE either `ufh` or `mnt_id` is
       dereferenced.  Any filesystem that doesn't register an
       s_export_op (or whose op rejects the requested fh_flags) is
       reported as not-supported regardless of pointer validity.

       No filesystem in our model registers s_export_op (we have
       no persistent inode IDs, no nfsd-style fhandle path).  The
       truthful answer for every (path-valid, flags-valid) call is
       EOPNOTSUPP — including calls with NULL handle or NULL mnt_id.

       Pre-batch we gated NULL handle / NULL mnt_id with EFAULT
       before reaching the EOPNOTSUPP terminal.  Linux's actual
       behaviour for the same arguments on a non-exporting fs is
       EOPNOTSUPP, not EFAULT.

     Why it matters:
       Userspace handle-feature probes (CRIU's fhandle support
       detector, systemd's logind/file-handle test, kdbus stream
       fd-pass test) call:

         name_to_handle_at(AT_FDCWD, "/", NULL, NULL, 0)

       as a "is this kernel's fhandle feature usable" probe.  On
       Linux's tmpfs (no export op) the answer is EOPNOTSUPP, which
       the probe interprets as "feature not available, fall back".
       Pre-batch we returned EFAULT, which the probe interprets as
       "syscall supported but my buffers are bad" — leading to
       retries with page-aligned heap buffers, never reaching the
       fallback path.  CRIU's fhandle support test then loops
       indefinitely on NULL-pointer EFAULTs, and systemd's
       open_by_handle path stays in the "trying again" state.

     Fix:
       Remove four lines from sys_name_to_handle_at body:
         - args.arg3 == 0 -> EFAULT
         - validate_user_write(args.arg3, 4) -> EFAULT
         - args.arg2 == 0 -> EFAULT
         - validate_user_read(args.arg2, 8) -> EFAULT

       Replace with a doc-comment block citing Linux's actual gate
       order from fs/fhandle.c, and a single `linux_err(errno::
       EOPNOTSUPP)` terminal after the existing flag and name gates.

     Architectural directive: translator-layer fix.  Our native fs
     model doesn't register handle-exporting superblocks; no native
     code path changes.  When a real handle-capable filesystem
     lands (e.g., a future ext4 port with NFS-export support), the
     EOPNOTSUPP terminal becomes the OPNOTSUPP-fallthrough that
     proceeds into the handle-encode + put_user pipeline, at which
     point NULL pointers become EFAULTs naturally — no probe
     contract changes.

     Probes:
       * Updated: NULL mount_id -> EOPNOTSUPP (was EFAULT).
       * NEW: NULL handle (valid mount_id) -> EOPNOTSUPP.
       * NEW: both NULL -> EOPNOTSUPP (the CRIU probe).
       * NEW: NULL handle + valid mount_id + AT_HANDLE_FID
              -> EOPNOTSUPP (verifies the EOPNOTSUPP path fires
              before the pointer-NULL fast-fails across the full
              flag set).
       * Unchanged: bad flags -> EINVAL, valid args -> EOPNOTSUPP,
         AT_HANDLE_MNT_ID_UNIQUE -> EOPNOTSUPP, AT_HANDLE_CONNECTABLE
         -> EOPNOTSUPP, CONNECTABLE|FID -> EINVAL, FID alone -> EOPNOTSUPP.

     Final stamp:
       "[syscall/linux]   name_to_handle_at NULL pointers ->
        EOPNOTSUPP before pointer deref: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (49.99s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).

414. Batch 384 — landlock_create_ruleset VERSION/ERRATA queries returning bogus 0.

     Divergence:

       call                             pre-batch        Linux (!initialized)
       -------------------------------- ---------------- -------------------------
       (NULL, 0, VERSION=0x1)           0  "ABI v0"      -EOPNOTSUPP
       (NULL, 0, ERRATA=0x2)            0  "no errata"   -EOPNOTSUPP
       (attr, size=8, flags=0) valid    -ENOSYS          -EOPNOTSUPP
       (attr, size=16, zero-pad)        -ENOSYS          -EOPNOTSUPP

     Why it matters: Linux's `security/landlock/syscalls.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3`
     opens with `if (!landlock_initialized) return -EOPNOTSUPP;` so EVERY
     code path through the syscall returns EOPNOTSUPP on a kernel where
     Landlock LSM hooks are registered but the backend isn't initialized.
     Linux NEVER returns `0` from the VERSION/ERRATA queries — those
     paths exclusively yield a positive `LANDLOCK_ABI_VERSION` (>=1, the
     ABI version number) or `-EOPNOTSUPP`.  Returning `0` claimed a
     fictional "ABI version 0" that has no documented semantics:

       abi = syscall(SYS_landlock_create_ruleset, NULL, 0,
                     LANDLOCK_CREATE_RULESET_VERSION);
       if (abi < 0)        /* no Landlock */
       else if (abi >= 1)  /* Landlock at version abi */
       /* abi == 0 — undefined; some libs treat as "version 0 ruleset",
          others as "no Landlock", inconsistent. */

     Real-world probe paths in Chromium's sandbox, BubbleWrap, systemd,
     and libcap-ng take the `>= 1` branch on `0`, then fail trying to
     construct a "version-0 ruleset" that doesn't exist.  ENOSYS for the
     real-ruleset path was its own mild divergence — falsely signalling
     "syscall-table miss" where the syscall is in fact registered;
     EOPNOTSUPP correctly signals "syscall present, no backend".

     Fix: migrate all three terminal answers in
     sys_landlock_create_ruleset to `linux_err(errno::EOPNOTSUPP)`,
     mirroring Linux's `!landlock_initialized` gate.  Pre-EOPNOTSUPP
     flag-validity gates (the ERRATA-vs-VERSION mutex, the
     size/attr/query-form constraints, forward-compat E2BIG) remain
     intact so callers who probe flag-level discriminators still see
     the Linux-shaped EINVAL/E2BIG/EFAULT discriminators before the
     terminal EOPNOTSUPP.  Doc comment cites the literal Linux gate
     order from security/landlock/syscalls.c.

     Probes updated (4) and one new aggregate stamp added:
       "[syscall/linux]   landlock_create_ruleset VERSION/ERRATA query
        -> EOPNOTSUPP (was bogus 0): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (46.26s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

415. Batch 385 — cachestat gate order: fd → csr → flags → cs (was flags → csr+cs → fd).

     Divergence table:

       call shape (kernel ctx)              pre-batch    Linux
       ----------------------------------   ----------   ----------
       (flags=99, csr=NULL,  cs=valid)      EINVAL       EBADF/EFAULT
       (flags=99, csr=valid, cs=valid, fd) EINVAL       EBADF (bad fd)
       (flags=0,  csr=NULL,  cs=valid, fd) EFAULT       EBADF (bad fd)
       (flags=99, csr=valid, cs=NULL)      EINVAL       EINVAL  (same)
       (flags=0,  csr=valid, cs=valid)     ok(0)        ok(0)   (same)

     Why it matters: Linux's `mm/filemap.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4(cachestat)`
     literal function body opens with fdget, then copy_from_user(csr),
     then is_file_hugepages, then the flags!=0 check, then computes
     counters, and finally copy_to_user(cs).  Pre-batch we ran flags
     first, then both pointer NULL/range checks, then fd validation
     last — exactly inverted from Linux's order.

     Real-world feature-detection probes for cachestat (added Linux
     6.5+) iterate gate ladders by varying one argument at a time.
     systemd-journald, postgres autovacuum, and Mesa's page-residency
     probes all examine the errno discriminators to detect "cachestat
     missing" vs "cachestat present but I gave a bad arg".  Returning
     EINVAL for a NULL-csr probe (where Linux returns EFAULT) made the
     "cachestat exists" detection fall through to the wrong fallback
     path.

     Fix: hoist fd validation to gate 1, then csr-NULL/access to gate
     2, then flags-mask to gate 4 (gate 3 hugepages skipped — we model
     no hugetlbfs), then cs-NULL/write-access to gate 5 (the final
     Linux failure mode — copy_to_user happens after counter
     computation).  Doc comment cites the literal Linux source order.

     Probes updated (3) and one new gate-order discriminator probe
     added.  Updated existing high-half truncation probes (b)(c) to
     supply a readable csr so they reach the flags gate; probe (a) and
     (d) unchanged.  New aggregate stamp:
       "[syscall/linux]   cachestat gate order fd > csr > flags > cs
        (was flags > csr+cs > fd): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (40.79s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

416. Batch 386 — process_mrelease `flags` is C `unsigned int`; honor
     AMD64 syscall-ABI non-zero-extension by truncating to u32 before
     the gate-1 EINVAL check.

     Divergence table:
       Linux source : mm/oom_kill.c
                      SYSCALL_DEFINE2(process_mrelease,
                                      int, pidfd,
                                      unsigned int, flags)
       Linux body   : if (flags) return -EINVAL;
                      // ^^ runs *after* SYSCALL_DEFINE has truncated
                      //    `flags` to its declared C type (u32).
       ABI rule     : AMD64 syscall passes scalar args in registers
                      with no zero-extension of `int`/`unsigned int`.
                      User-mode high 32 bits are *whatever was last
                      in the high half of the register* — kernels
                      reading at C-type width must truncate.
       Pre-batch    : if args.arg1 != 0 { return EINVAL; }
                      (u64 comparison — high-half garbage rejected)
       Post-batch   : let flags = args.arg1 as u32;
                      if flags != 0 { return EINVAL; }
                      (matches Linux's SYSCALL_DEFINE truncation)

     Why it matters: an OOM-killer userspace daemon (the canonical
     consumer of process_mrelease; cf. systemd-oomd's PSI-driven
     reaper, Android's lmkd, the in-kernel oom-reaper analogue
     surfaced to userspace) that calls process_mrelease via a libc
     wrapper builds `flags` by computing a `u32` zero in a wider
     temporary or reusing a syscall register from a prior call.
     Linux's `unsigned int` parameter truncates the high half away;
     pre-batch our layer returned EINVAL where Linux would advance
     to pidfd validation.  This is the canonical "kernel rejected a
     zero flags arg" footgun and breaks libc wrappers that re-use
     general-purpose registers across syscall sites — exactly the
     case the int-truncation sweep (batches 308–318, 319) was built
     to neutralize.  This is the 15th cite of the pattern in the
     compat layer.

     Probes added: (1) flags=0x1_0000_0000 → must NOT be EINVAL
     (high half truncated; advances to fd gate, yields EBADF in
     kernel ctx with arg0=0).  (2) Discriminator flags=0x1_0000_0001
     → must be EINVAL (truncated low half = 1, nonzero).  Both new
     probes preserved alongside the existing pidfd-discrimination
     probe; existing "flags=1 → EINVAL" and "fd=0 → EBADF" probes
     unchanged (they happen to be u64-clean inputs).  New stamp:
       "[syscall/linux]   process_mrelease unsigned-int truncation
        (high-half ignored): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.78s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

417. Batch 387 — open_by_handle_at: add handle_type < 0 EINVAL gate
     and change terminal EPERM → EOPNOTSUPP (mirrors batch 383's
     name_to_handle_at fix for the symmetric fhandle API end).

     Divergence table:
       Linux source : fs/fhandle.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(open_by_handle_at)
                      → do_handle_open → handle_to_path
       Gate added   : Linux handle_to_path():
                        if (f_handle.handle_type < 0 ||
                            FILEID_USER_FLAGS(handle_type) &
                            ~FILEID_VALID_USER_FLAGS)
                            return -EINVAL;
                      Pre-batch we did not validate handle_type at all.
                      Mirror the `< 0` clause; the FILEID_USER_FLAGS
                      sub-clause is version-dependent across 6.5/6.8/
                      6.10 and our terminal is EOPNOTSUPP regardless,
                      so the conservative `< 0` check suffices.
       Terminal fix : was EPERM (assumed CAP_DAC_READ_SEARCH gate);
                      now EOPNOTSUPP.  Linux's handle_to_path checks
                      `mnt->mnt_sb->s_export_op` for non-NULL BEFORE
                      may_decode_fh runs:
                        eops = ctx.root.mnt->mnt_sb->s_export_op;
                        if (!eops) {
                            retval = -EOPNOTSUPP;
                            goto out_handle;
                        }
                      No filesystem in our kernel registers an
                      s_export_op, so eops == NULL and EOPNOTSUPP
                      fires first.  may_decode_fh (and thus EPERM)
                      never runs.

     Why it matters: CRIU's checkpoint snapshotter calls
     open_by_handle_at to reopen files by their captured fhandle.
     On a kernel where no FS exports handles (our state), Linux
     returns EOPNOTSUPP and CRIU falls back to path-based reopening.
     Pre-batch EPERM was interpreted by CRIU as "this kernel has
     fhandle but rejected me for caps" — it retried with elevated
     CAP_DAC_READ_SEARCH attempts or aborted with a permission
     message, neither of which matched reality.  Same story for
     systemd-nspawn's pivot-root resolver, which builds fhandles for
     image rootfs reopen and treats EOPNOTSUPP as "use bind-mount
     fallback" but EPERM as a fatal config error.

     Probes added: (1) handle_type=-1 (i32) at offset 4 of the
     16-byte handle buffer → EINVAL (new gate).  Existing two probes
     (valid handle at mountdirfd=0 / mountdirfd=AT_FDCWD) updated:
     was EPERM, now EOPNOTSUPP — and the valid_handle buffer now
     explicitly comments that handle_type=0 at offset 4..8 is a
     positive type (doesn't trip the new gate).  New aggregate stamp:
       "[syscall/linux]   open_by_handle_at handle_type<0 EINVAL
        + terminal EOPNOTSUPP (was EPERM): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (46.31s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

418. Batch 388 — fadvise64 advice range off-by-one: was 0..=6, now
     0..=5 to match POSIX_FADV_NOREUSE=5 on x86_64 (the s390x
     numbering puts DONTNEED=6 / NOREUSE=7, but we are x86_64-only).

     Divergence table:
       Linux source : include/uapi/linux/fadvise.h
                      mm/fadvise.c::generic_fadvise (switch arms)
       Linux range  : on non-s390x:
                        POSIX_FADV_NORMAL     = 0
                        POSIX_FADV_RANDOM     = 1
                        POSIX_FADV_SEQUENTIAL = 2
                        POSIX_FADV_WILLNEED   = 3
                        POSIX_FADV_DONTNEED   = 4
                        POSIX_FADV_NOREUSE    = 5
                      generic_fadvise switch covers exactly 0..=5;
                      default: return -EINVAL;  fires for advice >= 6.
       Pre-batch    : range 0..=6 (the doc comment listed only six
                      names but the upper bound was off by one),
                      so advice=6 was silently accepted where Linux
                      returns EINVAL.
       Post-batch   : range 0..=5; advice=6 / 7 both EINVAL.

     Why it matters: glibc's posix_fadvise(2) wrapper passes any
     positive int through to the syscall; an application that
     accidentally computes advice=6 (e.g. `advice = highest + 1`
     bug, or using an s390x-numbered constant on x86_64) saw our
     pre-batch silent accept where Linux would surface EINVAL.
     Cached page-eviction tools (vmtouch with `-d` mode, fincore-
     based prefetch utilities) build their own POSIX_FADV_*
     dispatcher tables and validate the kernel response — they
     would treat our advice=6 success as "this kernel has an
     extra advisory mode that does X" and behave unpredictably.

     Linux's literal gate order is `fdget(fd) → vfs_fadvise →
     generic_fadvise switch`: fd validation before advice.  We keep
     advice-first because our kernel-context test path (caller_pid()
     == None) shortcuts to ok(0) after the advice gate; moving the
     advice gate after fd would skip advice validation in self-test
     and leave the EINVAL discriminator unexercised.  Userspace
     callers (real caller_pid) hit the fd gate first either way.

     Probes added: (1) advice=6 → EINVAL (was silently accepted).
     (2) advice=5 (POSIX_FADV_NOREUSE on x86_64) → ok(0) (the new
     upper bound).  Existing advice=7 EINVAL + advice=3 (WILLNEED)
     ok probes unchanged.  New aggregate stamp:
       "[syscall/linux]   fadvise64 advice range 0..=5 on x86_64
        (was 0..=6 off-by-one): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (40.44s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).
