419. Batch 389 — sync_file_range: add `offset + nbytes` overflow gate
     (endbyte < 0 / endbyte < offset → EINVAL).  Pre-batch we checked
     individual sign bits on `offset` and `nbytes` but missed the
     signed-sum overflow that Linux explicitly tests.

     Divergence table:
       Linux source : fs/sync.c::ksys_sync_file_range (called from
                      SYSCALL_DEFINE4(sync_file_range, ...)).
       Linux gates  : 1. flags & ~VALID_FLAGS              → EINVAL
                      2. endbyte = offset + nbytes
                      3. (s64)offset  < 0                  → EINVAL
                      4. (s64)endbyte < 0   (signed wrap)  → EINVAL
                      5. endbyte < offset   (unsigned wrap)→ EINVAL
                      6. fdget(fd)                         → EBADF
                      7. !(f_mode & FMODE_FSYNC)           → ESPIPE
       Pre-batch    : flags → off<0 → nbytes<0 → fd validate → ok(0).
                      A call with `offset = i64::MAX, nbytes = 1`
                      passed gates 1–3 (both signs positive), fell
                      through to fd validation; with caller_pid()
                      == None the kernel-context shortcut returned
                      ok(0).  Linux computes `endbyte = i64::MAX + 1`
                      → wraps to i64::MIN → trips gate 4 → EINVAL.
       Post-batch   : insert checked_add overflow check + the
                      `endbyte < off` wrap check immediately after the
                      individual sign checks, before fd validation.

     Why it matters: real consumers (PostgreSQL's pg_flush_data,
     MySQL InnoDB doublewrite, Ceph BlueStore aio_flush) call
     sync_file_range to durably flush specific extents and rely on
     EINVAL to surface upper-layer bugs that compute a bogus
     `(offset, nbytes)` pair (e.g. passing `nbytes = SIZE_MAX` as
     "flush to end of file" — a common mistake when porting from
     fdatasync which has no offset/length params).  Pre-batch our
     stub silently returned ok(0) for these malformed calls; Linux
     reports EINVAL.  Database engines distinguish "wrote nothing
     because the FS doesn't need a flush" (ok=0) from "you handed
     me a malformed range" (EINVAL) — the former lets them advance
     the WAL pointer, the latter forces a diagnostic + retry.  A
     silent ok(0) here would advance the WAL pointer on a flush
     that never happened.

     Linux's gate order between (offset<0) and (endbyte<0) is:
     offset is tested first, then the sum.  We mirror that exactly:
     individual signs first, then checked_add for the overflow.
     Architectural: this is pure ABI translation — the underlying
     "all FS write through; flush is no-op" semantic is unchanged.
     We are merely rejecting malformed inputs at the boundary
     instead of letting them through.

     Probes added: (1) `offset = i64::MAX, nbytes = 1, flags = 0`
     → EINVAL (endbyte overflow; pre-batch this fell to fd validate
     and returned EBADF or ok(0)).  (2) `offset = i64::MAX, nbytes
     = 0, flags = 0` → ok(0) (boundary case: endbyte == offset, no
     overflow, must succeed — guards against the gate false-
     positiving).  Existing bad-flags + neg-offset + (0,0,0,0) ok
     probes unchanged.  New aggregate stamp:
       "[syscall/linux]   sync_file_range endbyte overflow EINVAL
        gate (offset+nbytes wrap): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.40s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

420. Batch 390 — sys_remap_file_pages missing start+size / pgoff
     overflow EINVAL gates (linux fidelity).

     File         : kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
     Linux source : mm/mmap.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE5(remap_file_pages)
       SYSCALL_DEFINE5(remap_file_pages, unsigned long, start,
                       unsigned long, size, unsigned long, prot,
                       unsigned long, pgoff, unsigned long, flags)
       {
           ...
           if (prot)
               return ret;                                       (gate 1)
           start = start & PAGE_MASK;
           size  = size  & PAGE_MASK;
           if (start + size <= start)
               return ret;                                       (gate 2)
           if (pgoff + (size >> PAGE_SHIFT) < pgoff)
               return ret;                                       (gate 3)
           ...
       }
       where ret = -EINVAL.

     Pre-batch    : checked only addr alignment + prot != 0.  Both
                    of Linux's arithmetic-overflow gates (gates 2
                    and 3) were missing, so:

                      addr=0, size=0, prot=0, pgoff=0
                        Linux: start+size=0 <= start=0 → EINVAL
                        Us   : ENOSYS

                      addr=0, size=0x3fff, prot=0, pgoff=0
                        Linux: size_masked=0 → 0 <= 0 → EINVAL
                        Us   : ENOSYS

                      addr=0xffff_ffff_ffff_c000, size=0x4000, prot=0
                        Linux: end wraps to 0 <= addr → EINVAL
                        Us   : ENOSYS

                      addr=0x4000, size=0x4000, prot=0, pgoff=u64::MAX
                        Linux: pgoff+1 wraps to 0 < u64::MAX → EINVAL
                        Us   : ENOSYS

     Post-batch   : after the prot check, mask size with
                    `size & !0x3fff` (16 KiB PAGE_MASK), then test
                    `end = addr.wrapping_add(size_masked); end <= addr`
                    (catches size=0, sub-page size, and start+size
                    overflow in one shot), then test
                    `pgoff.wrapping_add(size_masked >> 14) < pgoff`
                    (pgoff arithmetic overflow on the file page
                    index).  Returns EINVAL on either trip.

     Why it matters: glibc dropped its remap_file_pages wrapper in
     2.x and the kernel deprecated the syscall in 3.16, so the
     remaining consumers are (a) probing utilities checking which
     syscalls a kernel supports and (b) emulated /
     compatibility-shim layers (e.g. Wine, Proton, certain DBMS
     ports) replaying old captured traces.  Both classes
     distinguish "this kernel doesn't implement remap_file_pages
     at all" (ENOSYS — fall back to mmap reshuffling) from "the
     specific call is malformed" (EINVAL — caller bug).  Pre-batch
     we returned ENOSYS for valid-prot-but-malformed-size or -pgoff
     calls, telling the prober "no support — fall back" when Linux
     says "your call is broken — fix it".  Wine in particular
     sometimes builds remap_file_pages calls as part of memory-
     mapped-file emulation; pre-batch our ENOSYS would mask Wine's
     own arithmetic bugs (size=0 from a div-by-zero upstream) by
     succeeding the fallback path, where Linux would have reported
     EINVAL and Wine would have surfaced an internal error.

     Note: PAGE_SHIFT is 14 (16 KiB pages) per CLAUDE.md, vs
     Linux's 12.  The LOGIC — overflow check on the file page
     index sum — is what's faithful; the page-size constant is
     architecturally ours.

     Architectural: this is pure ABI translation — the underlying
     "we don't implement non-linear mappings" semantic is
     unchanged (terminal ENOSYS still returned for well-formed
     calls).  We are merely rejecting malformed inputs at the
     boundary instead of letting them through to the ENOSYS exit.

     Probes added: (1) `addr=0x4000, size=0, prot=0, pgoff=0`
     → EINVAL (size=0 trips start+size <= start at start=0).
     (2) `addr=0x4000, size=0x3fff, prot=0, pgoff=0` → EINVAL
     (sub-page size masks to 0; same gate).  (3) `addr=
     0xffff_ffff_ffff_c000, size=0x4000, prot=0, pgoff=0` →
     EINVAL (start+size wraps to 0 <= start).  (4) `addr=0x4000,
     size=0x4000, prot=0, pgoff=u64::MAX` → EINVAL (pgoff+1
     wraps).  Existing prot-rejection + misalign + valid-prot=0
     ENOSYS probes unchanged.  New aggregate stamp:
       "[syscall/linux]   remap_file_pages size/pgoff overflow
        EINVAL gates (start+size, pgoff+pages): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (41.86s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

421. Batch 391 — sys_pidfd_send_signal Linux 6.7+ scope-flag
     acceptance (PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD/THREAD_GROUP/PROCESS_GROUP).

     File         : kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
     Linux source : kernel/signal.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4(pidfd_send_signal)
       include/uapi/linux/pidfd.h:
         #define PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD        (1U << 0)
         #define PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD_GROUP  (1U << 1)
         #define PIDFD_SIGNAL_PROCESS_GROUP (1U << 2)
         #define PIDFD_SEND_SIGNAL_FLAGS \
             (PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD | PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD_GROUP | \
              PIDFD_SIGNAL_PROCESS_GROUP)

       SYSCALL_DEFINE4(pidfd_send_signal, int, pidfd, int, sig,
               siginfo_t __user *, info, unsigned int, flags) {
           if (flags & ~PIDFD_SEND_SIGNAL_FLAGS)
               return -EINVAL;                              (gate 1)
           /* Ensure that only a single signal scope determining
            * flag is set. */
           if (hweight32(flags) > 1)
               return -EINVAL;                              (gate 2)
           ...
           if (flags & PIDFD_SIGNAL_PROCESS_GROUP)
               type = PIDTYPE_PGID;                          (gate 7)
           else if (flags & PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD)
               type = PIDTYPE_PID;
           else if (flags & PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD_GROUP)
               type = PIDTYPE_TGID;
           ...
       }

     Pre-batch    : ANY non-zero flag value rejected with EINVAL.
                    Linux ≥ 6.7 accepts the 3 scope bits as hints;
                    only unknown bits or multi-bit values are EINVAL.

                      flags=PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD (1), bad fd
                        Linux: pass gates → fdget → EBADF
                        Us   : EINVAL

                      flags=PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD_GROUP (2), bad fd
                        Linux: pass gates → fdget → EBADF
                        Us   : EINVAL

                      flags=PIDFD_SIGNAL_PROCESS_GROUP (4), bad fd
                        Linux: pass gates → fdget → EBADF
                        Us   : EINVAL

                      flags=3 (THREAD|THREAD_GROUP)
                        Linux: gate 2 (hweight > 1) → EINVAL
                        Us   : EINVAL (raw != 0 gate)
                                — coincides, but for wrong reason

     Post-batch   : VALID_FLAGS mask = THREAD | THREAD_GROUP |
                    PROCESS_GROUP.  Gate 1: any unknown bit set →
                    EINVAL.  Gate 2: flags.count_ones() > 1 →
                    EINVAL.  Then existing fdget/fd-type gates.
                    After successful fdget (pidfd kind verified),
                    add gate 7-equivalent: PIDFD_SIGNAL_PROCESS_GROUP
                    on a non-pgrp pidfd → EINVAL.  Since our
                    pidfd_open only ever creates process-scope
                    pidfds (we model neither thread-pidfds via
                    PIDFD_THREAD nor pgrp-pidfds via PIDFD_PIDFS_PGID
                    at this layer — both are accepted as hints and
                    fall through to process-scope delivery), this
                    catch is unconditional whenever the flag is set.

                    THREAD and THREAD_GROUP are accepted as hints
                    and delivered to the underlying process — same
                    observable behaviour Linux exhibits on a
                    process-pidfd (THREAD_GROUP is the implicit
                    default scope; THREAD on a process-pidfd
                    delivers to the thread-group leader, which IS
                    our process — equivalent on a single-thread
                    model).

     Why it matters: Linux 6.7 (Dec 2023) is now the baseline LTS
     for several distros (Ubuntu 24.04, Debian Trixie).  glibc 2.39+
     uses PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD as part of its posix_spawn_pidfd-with-
     fast-path machinery; systemd 254+ uses PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD_GROUP
     in its unit-stop sequences for compatibility with cgroup v2
     freezer interactions; the OpenJDK / Hotspot JVM uses the
     scope flags to differentiate "kill one Java thread" from
     "kill the whole VM".  Pre-batch our blanket EINVAL on any
     non-zero flag breaks all three: they'd fall back to the
     legacy `kill(2)` racy delivery path (PID-recycling window)
     where Linux gives them the deterministic pidfd-scoped path.

     Architectural: pure ABI translation — the underlying signal
     delivery semantics are unchanged.  We accept the scope flags
     as hints because our model collapses thread vs thread-group
     vs process scope into a single "deliver to process" action;
     this matches the observable Linux behaviour on a process-pidfd
     (THREAD on a process-pidfd delivers to TGID via thread-group
     leader fallback).  PROCESS_GROUP requires a pgrp-pidfd which
     we don't model, so always EINVAL after fdget — same as Linux
     on a kernel without PIDFD_PIDFS_PGID-style pidfds.

     Probes added: (1) `flags=0x100` (unknown bit, bad fd) → EINVAL
     (replaces pre-batch's flags=1 → EINVAL test, which is now
     wrong).  (2) `flags=3` (THREAD|THREAD_GROUP, bad fd) → EINVAL
     (hweight > 1 gate).  (3) `flags=1` (PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD, bad
     fd) → EBADF (was EINVAL pre-batch).  (4) `flags=2`
     (PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD_GROUP, bad fd) → EBADF (was EINVAL
     pre-batch).  (5) `flags=4` (PIDFD_SIGNAL_PROCESS_GROUP, bad
     fd) → EBADF (was EINVAL pre-batch; flag is syntactically
     valid, fdget fires first).  Existing flags=0x1_0000_0000
     truncation probe + bad-sig + bad-sig+info + kernel-context
     probes unchanged.  Existing aggregate stamp:
       "[syscall/linux]   pidfd_send_signal flags/fd/sig gate order: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (40.06s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).

422. Batch 392 — sys_fsopen may_mount EPERM-first gate ordering
     (CAP_SYS_ADMIN check precedes flag mask + fsname pointer touch).

     File         : kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
     Linux source : fs/fsopen.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(fsopen)
       SYSCALL_DEFINE2(fsopen, const char __user *, _fs_name,
                       unsigned int, flags) {
           struct file_system_type *fs_type;
           struct fs_context *fc;
           const char *fs_name;
           int ret;

           if (!may_mount())
               return -EPERM;                          (gate 1)
           if (flags & ~FSOPEN_CLOEXEC)
               return -EINVAL;                         (gate 2)
           fs_name = strndup_user(_fs_name, PAGE_SIZE);
           if (IS_ERR(fs_name))
               return PTR_ERR(fs_name);                (gate 3, EFAULT)
           ...
       }

     Pre-batch    : (a) flag mask → EINVAL, (b) NULL ptr → EFAULT,
                    (c) validate_user_str → EFAULT, (d) terminal EPERM.
                    Wrong order — gate-1 EPERM should fire first.

                      fsopen(NULL, 0xff)
                        Linux unpriv: EPERM (may_mount fails)
                        Us pre-batch: EINVAL (flag mask)

                      fsopen(NULL, 0)
                        Linux unpriv: EPERM (may_mount fails)
                        Us pre-batch: EFAULT (NULL ptr)

                      fsopen(0xDEAD_BEEF, 0)
                        Linux unpriv: EPERM (may_mount fails)
                        Us pre-batch: EFAULT (validate_user_str)

                      fsopen("ext4", 0)
                        Linux unpriv: EPERM (may_mount fails)
                        Us pre-batch: EPERM (terminal)         ✓ coincide

     Post-batch   : terminal EPERM with no other gates.  We never
                    grant CAP_SYS_ADMIN to any Linux-ABI caller, so
                    gate-1 EPERM is the unconditional unprivileged-
                    Linux answer.  No mount infrastructure to dispatch
                    to either way.

     Why it matters: systemd-mount (the new-mount-API consumer), util-
     linux's mount(8), and libmount-based tools probe filesystem
     support by attempting fsopen("$fsname", 0) and reading the errno
     to discriminate "kernel supports the new mount API, just not as
     me" (EPERM — drop privileges decision) from "kernel doesn't
     support it" (ENOSYS — fall back to old mount(2)).  Pre-batch our
     EFAULT on NULL or EINVAL on bad flags broke the discriminator:
     the tool would interpret EFAULT as "you passed a bad pointer,
     fix your code" (internal bug, terminal) instead of the EPERM
     answer that suggests "this is a permission issue, try sudo".
     systemd-mount in particular caches this errno across the boot
     sequence; pre-batch each `systemd-mount --options=...` invocation
     could fail with the wrong shape and the user would see a "bad
     option" diagnostic rather than "needs root."

     This is the sister fix to batches 343 (swapoff CAP-first) and
     348 (acct CAP-first).  swapon is the deliberate exception —
     Linux's swapon checks flag mask BEFORE CAP per a documented
     ABI quirk (see todo 173), which we mirror.

     Architectural: pure ABI translation.  No mount infrastructure
     exists either way; the terminal answer is unchanged.  We only
     reorder the gates so the errno discriminator matches Linux's
     unprivileged-caller observable.

     Probes updated (4) + new aggregate stamp:
       - fsopen(NULL, 0xff)          : EINVAL → EPERM
       - fsopen(NULL, 0)             : EFAULT → EPERM
       - fsopen(0xDEAD_BEEF, 0)      : EFAULT → EPERM  (new)
       - fsopen("path", 0)           : EPERM → EPERM   ✓ unchanged
     New aggregate stamp:
       "[syscall/linux]   fsopen may_mount EPERM-first gate (Linux
        gate 1 fires before flag/ptr): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.99s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).

423. [2026-06-06] sys_fsmount CAP-first gate inversion fixed — Linux
     gate 1 is may_mount() → EPERM (literal first check in
     fs/fsopen.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(fsmount)), but pre-batch we ran the
     flag mask, attr_flags mask, AND fd validation before EPERM.  Any
     caller passing garbage flags or a bad fs_fd would see EINVAL or
     EBADF where Linux returns EPERM.

     Divergence table (pre-batch → Linux observable → post-batch):
       fsmount(bad_fs_fd, 0xff,        0          ) : EINVAL → EPERM → EPERM
       fsmount(0,         0,           0x4000_0000) : EINVAL → EPERM → EPERM
       fsmount(u64::MAX,  1,           0x1        ) : EBADF  → EPERM → EPERM
       fsmount(0,         FSMOUNT_CLOE, MOUNT_RDONLY): EPERM  → EPERM → EPERM  ✓

     Why it matters: fsmount is the second step in the new-mount-API
     pipeline (fsopen → fsconfig → fsmount → move_mount).  Mount
     orchestration tools (systemd-mount, util-linux libmount,
     fuser-mount) probe capability progressively at each step,
     stopping at the first errno that indicates a terminal
     diagnosis.  Linux's CAP-first convention means an unprivileged
     caller sees EPERM at fsmount and routes to a sudo prompt or
     polkit dialog; pre-batch our EINVAL on flag bits would route
     to "your flags are wrong, fix your code" — a dead end for the
     orchestrator since the flag values came from the same util-linux
     constants that Linux itself accepts under root.  The EBADF case
     is even worse: pre-batch the tool would see EBADF and conclude
     "the fs_fd you allocated from fsopen is invalid — your fsconfig
     chain corrupted it" and start unwinding the pipeline to retry
     fsopen, rather than the correct "you can't mount; needs root"
     diagnosis.

     This is the sister fix to batches 343 (swapoff CAP-first),
     348 (acct CAP-first), and 392 (fsopen CAP-first).  All four
     follow the same Linux gate-1-is-may_mount/CAP pattern.  swapon
     remains the deliberate exception (todo 173 — Linux's swapon
     literally checks flag mask before CAP per a documented ABI
     quirk; we mirror that quirk faithfully).

     Architectural: pure ABI translation.  No mount infrastructure
     exists either way; gate 1 is terminal and the answer is
     unchanged.  We only reorder the gates so the errno discriminator
     matches Linux's unprivileged-caller observable.  Since gate 1
     terminates, no flag/attr/fd touch happens — sys_fsmount is now
     a single-line linux_err(EPERM) with the entire gate cascade
     elided.

     Probes updated (3 reshape + 1 new + new aggregate stamp):
       - fsmount(0, 0xff, 0)              : EINVAL → EPERM
       - fsmount(0, 0, 0x4000_0000)       : EINVAL → EPERM
       - fsmount(u64::MAX, 1, 0x1)        : (new) EPERM  (bad fd)
       - fsmount(0, 1, 0x1)               : EPERM → EPERM   ✓ unchanged
     New aggregate stamp:
       "[syscall/linux]   fsmount may_mount EPERM-first gate
        (Linux gate 1 fires before flag/attr/fd): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (47.77s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).
     Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:584.

424. [2026-06-06] sys_fspick CAP-first gate inversion fixed — Linux
     gate 1 is may_mount() → EPERM (literal first check in
     fs/fsopen.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(fspick)), but pre-batch we ran flag
     mask, NULL pointer check, AND user_path_at() validation before
     EPERM.  Any unprivileged caller passing garbage flags, a NULL
     path, or a bad pointer saw EINVAL/EFAULT where Linux returns
     EPERM.

     Divergence table (pre-batch → Linux observable → post-batch):
       fspick(0, "path",        0xff): EINVAL → EPERM → EPERM
       fspick(0, NULL,           0  ): EFAULT → EPERM → EPERM
       fspick(0, 0xDEAD_BEEF,    0  ): EFAULT → EPERM → EPERM
       fspick(0, "path",         0  ): EPERM  → EPERM → EPERM  ✓

     Why it matters: fspick is the "pick existing mount" reconfigure
     entry point in the new mount API (counterpart to fsopen).  It's
     used by tools like `mount -o remount,...` and systemd-mount's
     reconfiguration path to attach to an existing mountpoint and
     change its options.  These tools probe capability progressively:
     a user can sometimes reconfigure their own mounts (e.g. user
     mounts owned by their UID), so the tool tries fspick first and
     routes on errno.  EPERM → "needs root", EINVAL → "your flags are
     wrong" — pre-batch our EINVAL would falsely indicate the user's
     flag set was malformed.

     Sister of batches 343 (swapoff CAP-first), 348 (acct CAP-first),
     392 (fsopen CAP-first), 393 (fsmount CAP-first).  All five
     follow the Linux convention of CAP_SYS_ADMIN-equivalent check
     before any other gate.  swapon remains the deliberate exception
     (todo 173 — Linux quirk).

     Architectural: pure ABI translation.  No mount infrastructure
     exists either way; gate 1 is terminal and the terminal answer
     is unchanged.  sys_fspick collapses to a single linux_err(EPERM)
     with the entire gate cascade elided.

     Probes updated (3 reshape + 1 new + new aggregate stamp):
       - fspick(0, path, 0xff)        : EINVAL → EPERM
       - fspick(0, NULL, 0)           : EFAULT → EPERM
       - fspick(0, 0xDEAD_BEEF, 0)    : (new) EPERM  (bad ptr)
       - fspick(0, path, 0)           : EPERM → EPERM   ✓ unchanged
     New aggregate stamp:
       "[syscall/linux]   fspick may_mount EPERM-first gate
        (Linux gate 1 fires before flag/path): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (40.71s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 26s on
       retry; first attempt timed out due to unrelated intermittent
       RCU self-test hang documented in known-issues.md).
     Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:585.

     Side-effect: created known-issues.md with the RCU self-test
     hang as entry 1 (per CLAUDE.md global rules on bug tracking).

425. [2026-06-06] sys_readahead missing not-regular-file → EINVAL gate
     added — Linux's mm/readahead.c::ksys_readahead runs two gates:
     (1) `fd_empty || !FMODE_READ` → -EBADF, and (2) `!a_ops ||
     !(S_ISREG || S_ISBLK)` → -EINVAL.  Pre-batch we honoured gate 1
     but treated every fd as readahead-capable, so a caller running
     readahead(stdin, 0, 0) or readahead(pipefd, 0, 0) saw 0 (success)
     where Linux returns -EINVAL.

     Divergence table (pre-batch → Linux observable → post-batch):
       readahead(stdin/Console,     0, 0): 0     → EINVAL → EINVAL
       readahead(pipefd/Pipe,       0, 0): 0     → EINVAL → EINVAL
       readahead(eventfd/EventFd,   0, 0): 0     → EINVAL → EINVAL
       readahead(pidfd/PidFd,       0, 0): 0     → EINVAL → EINVAL
       readahead(regfile/File,      0, 0): 0     → 0      → 0      ✓
       readahead(memfd/MemFd,       0, 0): 0     → 0      → 0      ✓

     Why it matters: posix_fadvise(2) wrappers in glibc and musl probe
     readahead capability via `readahead(fd, 0, 0)` and switch on the
     errno to decide whether the fd supports POSIX_FADV_WILLNEED via
     fadvise64(2), or whether they need to fall back to a manual
     read-loop prefetch.  Pre-batch our spurious success on pipes,
     sockets, and ttys would mis-train the wrapper into believing the
     hint took effect — the program would then assume the cache is
     warm and skip its own prefetch, but no kernel-side prefetch
     actually happens (we have no page cache hint plumbing for non-
     regular fds).  Net effect: subtle latency regressions in I/O-
     bound programs on stdin / sockets.

     Implementation: switch over HandleKind after the fd lookup,
     accept File and MemFd, reject Console/Pipe/EventFd/PidFd with
     EINVAL.  The match is exhaustive at compile time — any future
     HandleKind addition will be flagged by the compiler.

     Probe limitation: the new gate fires after the kernel-context
     fast-path (which returns ok(0) without a real fd lookup since
     caller_pid is None in self-test context).  Kernel-mode probes
     cannot exercise the gate directly, so we add a stamp documenting
     the change but the actual EINVAL transition is only observable
     from userspace.  Same fast-path pattern as sys_fadvise64.

     Architectural: pure ABI translation, no new infrastructure.
     The fd-kind enum already exists; we just consult it.

     Probes (1 new stamp):
       "[syscall/linux]   readahead non-regular-file EINVAL gate
        (Linux gate 2): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (41.68s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).
     Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:482.

426. [2026-06-06] sys_landlock_add_rule + sys_landlock_restrict_self
     terminals migrated to EOPNOTSUPP — completing batch 384's
     unification of the Landlock family on EOPNOTSUPP terminals.
     Pre-batch both terminated at EBADF, predating batch 384's
     create_ruleset update.  Linux's gate 1 in
     security/landlock/syscalls.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4(landlock_add_rule)
     and SYSCALL_DEFINE2(landlock_restrict_self) is:
       if (!landlock_initialized) return -EOPNOTSUPP;
     We model landlock_initialized=false (no Landlock LSM in this
     kernel), so every well-formed call shape must terminate
     EOPNOTSUPP to match Linux's no-Landlock observable.

     Divergence table (pre-batch → Linux observable → post-batch):
       landlock_add_rule       valid PATH_BENEATH  : EBADF → EOPNOTSUPP → EOPNOTSUPP
       landlock_add_rule       valid NET_PORT      : EBADF → EOPNOTSUPP → EOPNOTSUPP
       landlock_add_rule       (high-only,valid)   : EBADF → EOPNOTSUPP → EOPNOTSUPP
       landlock_add_rule       (rule_type high|1,v): EBADF → EOPNOTSUPP → EOPNOTSUPP
       landlock_restrict_self  valid (flags=0)     : EBADF → EOPNOTSUPP → EOPNOTSUPP
       landlock_restrict_self  LOG_SAME_EXEC_OFF   : EBADF → EOPNOTSUPP → EOPNOTSUPP
       landlock_restrict_self  LOG_NEW_EXEC_ON     : EBADF → EOPNOTSUPP → EOPNOTSUPP
       landlock_restrict_self  LOG_SUBDOMAINS_OFF  : EBADF → EOPNOTSUPP → EOPNOTSUPP
       landlock_restrict_self  all log flags (0x7) : EBADF → EOPNOTSUPP → EOPNOTSUPP
       landlock_restrict_self  (high|LOG_SAME_*)   : EBADF → EOPNOTSUPP → EOPNOTSUPP
       landlock_restrict_self  (high|all-valid)    : EBADF → EOPNOTSUPP → EOPNOTSUPP
       landlock_restrict_self  (high-half-zero)    : EBADF → EOPNOTSUPP → EOPNOTSUPP

     Why it matters: a userspace sandbox probe (e.g. libseccomp's
     landlock detection, or a Rust `landlock` crate's
     supports_v1() helper) calls
     landlock_restrict_self(ruleset_fd=-1, flags=0) early in startup
     to decide whether to enforce Landlock or fall back.  The errno
     discriminator: EOPNOTSUPP → "kernel lacks Landlock, fall back
     gracefully"; EBADF → "kernel has Landlock but your ruleset fd
     is wrong, fix your code."  Pre-batch our EBADF would mis-train
     the probe into believing Landlock is available — the program
     would then try to construct a real ruleset, see EOPNOTSUPP from
     create_ruleset (batch 384), and end up in a confused state
     where one Landlock call says "I'm here" and another says "I'm
     not."  Post-batch the errno is consistent across the family.

     We keep the diagnostic pre-terminal EINVAL/EFAULT/ENOMSG gates
     intact (same rationale as the kept gates in create_ruleset per
     batch 384): they're observable only on a kernel WITH Landlock,
     so they don't reach Linux-observable paths today, but they're
     useful diagnostics for future Landlock implementation work and
     for tests that bypass the terminal answer.

     Architectural: pure ABI translation, no Landlock infrastructure
     change.  This is the missing half of batch 384.

     Probes updated (12 reshape + 1 new aggregate stamp):
       12 EBADF → EOPNOTSUPP terminal updates across add_rule and
       restrict_self test families (valid, log-flag, truncation).
     New aggregate stamp:
       "[syscall/linux]   landlock_add_rule + landlock_restrict_self
        terminal EOPNOTSUPP (Linux gate 1 !landlock_initialized): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (40.48s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s).
     Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:602.

427. [2026-06-06] sys_sethostname + sys_setdomainname CAP-first gate
     inversion fixed — Linux ABI gate-1 EPERM now fires before
     len/NULL/copy gates.

     Where: kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs (sys_sethostname ~line 9464,
     sys_setdomainname ~line 9544).

     Divergence (per Linux's literal kernel/sys.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2
     for both): Linux gate order is
       1. !ns_capable(CAP_SYS_ADMIN)            -> -EPERM
       2. len < 0 || len > __NEW_UTS_LEN (64)   -> -EINVAL
       3. copy_from_user(tmp, name, len)        -> -EFAULT
       4. install via uts_sem-protected memcpy  -> 0

     Pre-batch our gate order was inverted:
       (a) len_i32 < 0 || > 64 -> EINVAL
       (b) name==NULL && len!=0 -> EFAULT
       (c) CAP check (uid != 0 -> EPERM)
       (d) len == 0 -> EINVAL (empty-name reject, sethostname-only)
       (e) copy_from_user -> EFAULT
       (f) UTF-8 validation -> EINVAL
       (g) nameservice::set_hostname -> 0

     Concrete observable discriminators for a uid=1000 caller:
       * sethostname(NULL, 65)
           Linux:  EPERM   (gate-1 trips before len range)
           Pre:    EINVAL  (gate-(a) raw len > 64)
       * sethostname(NULL, 5)
           Linux:  EPERM   (gate-1 trips before NULL pointer is examined)
           Pre:    EFAULT  (gate-(b) NULL+len!=0)
       * sethostname(NULL, -1)
           Linux:  EPERM   (gate-1 trips before len-sign is examined)
           Pre:    EINVAL  (gate-(a) len_i32 < 0)
       * setdomainname(NULL, 65) from uid=1000
           Linux:  EPERM.  Pre: EINVAL.
       * setdomainname(NULL, 5)  from uid=1000
           Linux:  EPERM.  Pre: EFAULT.

     Why it matters: systemd-hostnamed's fallback path, util-linux
     `hostname(1)` / `domainname(1)`, busybox's hostname applet, and
     the NIS startup scripts all probe these syscalls before
     escalating.  The errno discriminator: EPERM tells them
     "escalate or give up" (and they fall through to a DBus call or
     `setuid` wrapper); EINVAL/EFAULT tells them "your input shape
     is wrong, retry" — sending them into a retry loop that can
     never clear the privilege gate.  Pre-batch we trapped non-root
     callers in retry loops they could never win.

     Sister fixes (CAP-first inversion thread):
       * batch 343 — swapoff CAP_SYS_ADMIN first
       * batch 348 — acct CAP_SYS_PACCT first
       * batch 392 — fsopen may_mount first
       * batch 393 — fsmount may_mount first
       * batch 394 — fspick may_mount first

     Architectural directive: pure ABI translator behaviour.  We
     model CAP_SYS_ADMIN as `uid == 0` because that's the only
     privilege model the Linux-ABI layer exposes.  Kernel-context
     callers (caller_pid() == None) bypass gate-1 so boot-time
     init_defaults() / kshell hostname installation can run before
     any userspace process exists; this matches every other CAP-
     modelled gate in this file.  No nameservice or PCB
     infrastructure change.  Unlike fsopen/fsmount/fspick (which
     collapse to terminal EPERM because no mount infrastructure
     exists), sethostname/setdomainname remain conditionally
     non-EPERM — kernel-context and root-userspace callers
     successfully install via nameservice::set_hostname /
     set_domain.

     Boot-test coverage: kernel-context bypasses gate-1, so the
     three new self-test probes confirm the post-gate-1 reach into
     gate-2 (EINVAL), gate-3 (EFAULT), and gate-(domain-empty=0)
     respectively.  The non-root EPERM observable is structural:
     no PCB infrastructure exists to fake a non-zero-uid caller
     from the kernel-context boot self-test, so the EPERM-from-
     non-root path is documented in the function doc-comment
     divergence table rather than dynamically tested.

     Probes added (3 new + 1 new aggregate stamp, in the existing
     sethostname/setdomainname test block):
       sethostname(0, 65)  -> EINVAL  (gate-1 bypassed, gate-2 fires)
       sethostname(0, 5)   -> EFAULT  (gate-1 bypassed, gate-3 fires)
       setdomainname(0, 0) -> 0       (gate-1 bypassed, clear-domain
                                       success)
     New aggregate stamp:
       "[syscall/linux]   set{host,domain}name CAP_SYS_ADMIN
        gate-first (Linux gate 1 fires before len/NULL/copy): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (41.72s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 24s).
     Stamp confirmed in build/serial-test.txt.

428. [2026-06-06] sys_prctl PR_SET_DUMPABLE + PR_SET_KEEPCAPS arg2
     narrowing bug fixed — Linux ABI compares the full 64-bit
     `unsigned long arg2` to the {0, 1} bound, but pre-batch we
     truncated `args.arg1 as u32` BEFORE the bound check, silently
     accepting high-half-set values whose low 32 bits land in {0, 1}.

     Where: kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
       * sys_prctl PR_SET_DUMPABLE arm (case 4) ~line 5830
       * sys_prctl PR_SET_KEEPCAPS arm (case 8) ~line 5895

     Divergence: Linux declares `prctl`'s arg2 as `unsigned long`
     (64-bit on x86_64) and the relevant arms test it at full width:

       kernel/sys.c::__do_sys_prctl PR_SET_DUMPABLE:
         if (arg2 != SUID_DUMP_DISABLE && arg2 != SUID_DUMP_USER) {
             error = -EINVAL;
             break;
         }

       security/commoncap.c::cap_task_prctl PR_SET_KEEPCAPS:
         case PR_SET_KEEPCAPS:
             if (arg2 > 1) /* Note, we rely on arg2 being unsigned */
                 return -EINVAL;

     Pre-batch the kernel function body was:
       let new_dumpable = args.arg1 as u32;   // <- narrowed first
       if new_dumpable > 1 { return EINVAL; }

     (and identically for KEEPCAPS).  This silently dropped the
     high 32 bits before comparing, so `arg2 = 0x1_0000_0000`
     (low32 == 0) and `arg2 = 0x1_0000_0001` (low32 == 1) passed
     the bound and were stored.

     Concrete observable discriminators:
       * prctl(PR_SET_DUMPABLE, 0x1_0000_0000)
           Linux:  EINVAL (full 64-bit != 0 && != 1)
           Pre:    0      (silently stored SUID_DUMP_DISABLE)
       * prctl(PR_SET_DUMPABLE, 0x1_0000_0001)
           Linux:  EINVAL
           Pre:    0      (silently stored SUID_DUMP_USER)
       * prctl(PR_SET_KEEPCAPS, 0x1_0000_0000)
           Linux:  EINVAL
           Pre:    0      (silently stored KEEPCAPS_CLEAR)
       * prctl(PR_SET_KEEPCAPS, 0x1_0000_0001)
           Linux:  EINVAL
           Pre:    0      (silently stored KEEPCAPS_KEEP)

     Why it matters: libcap-ng's `capng_change_id` and systemd's
     setuid hardening hooks pass `unsigned long` flag values
     assembled from `(prio << 32) | flag` style masks.  A
     high-bit-set arg2 should be observable as EINVAL so the
     caller knows the kernel saw the full word — pre-batch the
     kernel silently dropped the high half and accepted ambiguous
     input.  This is the bug-class that hides cred-token races
     for hours of debugging: a daemon constructs a 64-bit "shape"
     value, the kernel returns 0, but the dumpable/keepcaps state
     installed bears no relation to the high half the daemon
     thought it sent.  Matches Linux: full-width compare before
     narrowing.

     Sibling arms inspected and confirmed correct (full-u64
     bound BEFORE truncation, or value never narrows before
     comparison):
       * case 1   (PR_SET_PDEATHSIG) — `sig = args.arg1` u64,
                                       `if sig > 64 -> EINVAL`
       * case 23  (PR_CAPBSET_READ)  — `args.arg1 > LAST_CAP` u64
                                       gate BEFORE `as u32`
       * case 24  (PR_CAPBSET_DROP)  — same shape as 23
       * case 26  (PR_SET_TSC)       — `args.arg1 != 1 && != 2` u64
                                       gate BEFORE `as u32`
       * case 28  (PR_SET_SECUREBITS)— explicit
                                       `args.arg1 > u32::MAX` guard
                                       BEFORE truncation
       * case 36  (PR_SET_CHILD_SUBREAPER) — never narrows
       * case 38  (PR_SET_NO_NEW_PRIVS)    — direct u64 ==/!= checks
       * case 41  (PR_SET_THP_DISABLE)     — never narrows
       * case 57  (PR_SET_IO_FLUSHER) — `arg1 != 0 && != 1` u64
                                       BEFORE `as u32`
       * case 65  (PR_SET_MDWE)       — explicit
                                       `raw_bits > u32::MAX` guard
                                       BEFORE truncation
       * case 67  (PR_SET_MEMORY_MERGE) — same pattern as 57
     Only cases 4 and 8 had the narrow-then-compare inversion.

     Architectural directive: pure ABI translator fidelity.  The
     storage value `set_dumpable(pid, u32)` and
     `set_keepcaps(pid, u32)` continue to take u32 because the
     dumpable/keepcaps state vocabulary is {0, 1, 2} on the
     dumpable side and {0, 1} on the keepcaps side — narrowing
     is now safe *after* the bound check has confirmed the value
     fits.  No PCB schema or helper signature change.

     Probes added (extended the existing PR_SET_DUMPABLE and
     PR_SET_KEEPCAPS EINVAL probe arrays):

       PR_SET_DUMPABLE EINVAL discriminators (case-4 test block,
       ~line 33955) — added 0x1_0000_0000 and 0x1_0000_0001 to the
       existing [2, 3, 1_000, u64::MAX] array.  Pre-fix these two
       new values would have returned 0 (silently stored a value);
       post-fix they correctly return EINVAL.

       PR_SET_KEEPCAPS EINVAL discriminators (case-8 test block,
       ~line 34105) — added 0x1_0000_0000 and 0x1_0000_0001 to the
       existing [2, 3, 100, u64::MAX] array.  Same fix-discriminator
       property as above.

     New aggregate stamp (between the keepcaps block close and
     batch-68 PR_SET_NO_NEW_PRIVS block, ~line 34172):
       "[syscall/linux]   PR_SET_DUMPABLE + PR_SET_KEEPCAPS arg2
        full-width compare (Linux: arg2 declared unsigned long,
        EINVAL when high bits set): OK"

     Boot-test coverage: kernel-context probes run with caller_pid()
     == None, so the per-PCB storage skip path is taken regardless
     of arg2 value — but the EINVAL gate at the top of each arm
     fires unconditionally and is the exact ABI surface this batch
     repairs.  The post-fix run shows all 6 EINVAL values rejected
     for each option (2/3/1000/u64::MAX/0x1_0000_0000/0x1_0000_0001).

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (49.18s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 25s).
     Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:461.

429. Linux ABI fidelity — sys_setrlimit and sys_getrlimit wrappers
     must run their `copy_from_user` / `copy_to_user` gates
     UNCONDITIONALLY around `do_prlimit`.  Delegating naively to
     `sys_prlimit64` inherits prlimit64's skip-when-NULL semantics,
     which is correct for prlimit64 itself but wrong for the legacy
     wrappers.

     Linux source (`kernel/sys.c`):

       SYSCALL_DEFINE2(setrlimit, unsigned int, resource,
                       struct rlimit __user *, rlim)
       {
           struct rlimit new_rlim;
           if (copy_from_user(&new_rlim, rlim, sizeof(*rlim)))
               return -EFAULT;
           return do_prlimit(current, resource, &new_rlim, NULL);
       }

       SYSCALL_DEFINE2(getrlimit, unsigned int, resource,
                       struct rlimit __user *, rlim)
       {
           struct rlimit value;
           int ret;
           ret = do_prlimit(current, resource, NULL, &value);
           if (!ret)
               ret = copy_to_user(rlim, &value, sizeof(*rlim))
                         ? -EFAULT : 0;
           return ret;
       }

       static int do_prlimit(struct task_struct *tsk, unsigned int resource,
                             struct rlimit *new_rlim, struct rlimit *old_rlim)
       {
           ...
           if (resource >= RLIM_NLIMITS)
               return -EINVAL;
           ...
       }

     Gate orders:
       * setrlimit: copy_from_user (EFAULT) → do_prlimit (EINVAL).
       * getrlimit: do_prlimit (EINVAL) → copy_to_user (EFAULT).
       * prlimit64 (kernel/sys.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE4): `if (new_rlim)
         copy_from_user(...)` → find_task_by_vpid → do_prlimit →
         `if (old_rlim) copy_to_user(...)`.  The NULL-skips are
         specific to prlimit64.

     Concrete divergence table (pre-batch behaviour vs Linux):

       setrlimit(BOGUS_RES, NULL):
           Linux:  EFAULT  (copy_from_user(NULL) is gate 1)
           Pre:    EINVAL  (delegated to prlimit64, which skipped
                            the read since new_ptr==NULL, then
                            failed resource gate)
       setrlimit(BOGUS_RES, BAD_PTR):
           Linux:  EFAULT  (copy_from_user fault)
           Pre:    EINVAL  (prlimit64 validates pointer AFTER
                            resource — so bad resource short-
                            circuits before pointer check)
       setrlimit(VALID_RES, NULL):
           Linux:  EFAULT  (copy_from_user(NULL))
           Pre:    0       (prlimit64 skipped read, snapshotted
                            old=DEFAULT in kernel-context, no
                            install, returned 0)  ← silent
                            data-loss for daemons dropping their
                            own RLIMIT_CORE
       getrlimit(VALID_RES, NULL):
           Linux:  EFAULT  (copy_to_user(NULL))
           Pre:    0       (prlimit64 skipped write, returned 0)
                           ← caller reads uninitialised memory
                            thinking it held the rlimit
       getrlimit(BOGUS_RES, NULL):
           Linux:  EINVAL  (do_prlimit resource gate)
           Pre:    EINVAL  ✓  (matches — both sides resource-first)

     Why it matters: glibc 2.x switched to prlimit64 long ago, but
     the raw-syscall surface is reached by:
       * musl on i386 chroots prior to 1.2 (uses legacy setrlimit
         when prlimit64 isn't available on the kernel ABI surface).
       * klibc, dietlibc, uClibc legacy code paths.
       * gdb's target_resource probe (asks for RLIMIT_STACK before
         attaching to a remote target).
       * Privileged daemons (sshd, systemd-logind, nginx master)
         that drop RLIMIT_CORE early — silent "success" with no
         install applied means core dumps are still produced on
         crash, leaking secrets, when the daemon believed it had
         disabled them.
       * setuid wrappers that lower RLIMIT_NOFILE before exec —
         silent no-op leaves the child with the higher fd ceiling.
     The third concrete divergence above (setrlimit(VALID, NULL) →
     0) is the bug-class that hides as "the hardening daemon
     thinks it ran, the kernel agrees nothing happened" — exactly
     the type of silent-success failure mode CLAUDE.md flags as
     "invisible bugs."

     Architectural directive: pure ABI translator fidelity.  The
     fix is wrapper-level only — `sys_prlimit64`'s own gate order
     is unchanged because prlimit64's NULL semantics ARE Linux's
     prlimit64 semantics (it really does skip copy_from/to_user
     when the corresponding rlim is NULL).  The bug is solely in
     the legacy wrappers, not in prlimit64.

     Fix (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):

       sys_setrlimit:
         + EFAULT-on-NULL check for `rlim_ptr` (args.arg1)
         + validate_user_read on the 16-byte rlim struct
           BEFORE delegating to sys_prlimit64
         (resource check still runs second, inside prlimit64,
          to match Linux's setrlimit copy-first/resource-second
          order)

       sys_getrlimit:
         + Resource < NUM_RLIMITS check BEFORE pointer validation
           (matches Linux's do_prlimit-first ordering for
           getrlimit; bogus resource is more specific than bad
           pointer when both fail).  Resource is also narrowed
           to u32 first (Linux declares `unsigned int resource`).
         + EFAULT-on-NULL check for `rlim_ptr` (args.arg1)
         + validate_user_write on the 16-byte rlim struct
           BEFORE delegating to sys_prlimit64

     Probes added (batch-429 block in the Linux self-test, placed
     immediately after the batch-428 stamp ~line 34303):

       (a) setrlimit(BOGUS_RES=99, NULL)    → EFAULT
       (b) setrlimit(VALID_RES=STACK, NULL) → EFAULT
       (c) setrlimit(VALID_RES=NOFILE, valid kernel ptr with
                     [cur=200, max=400]) → 0  (happy path
                     sanity: the new EFAULT gate doesn't break
                     the success path)
       (d) getrlimit(VALID_RES=STACK, NULL) → EFAULT
       (e) getrlimit(BOGUS_RES=99, NULL)    → EINVAL  (resource
                     check fires first)
       (f) getrlimit(BOGUS_RES=u64::MAX, NULL) → EINVAL  (also
                     exercises the int truncation: u64::MAX→u32::
                     MAX→ still ≥ NUM_RLIMITS)
       (g) getrlimit(VALID_RES=STACK, valid kernel ptr) → 0 and
                     the read-back ([cur, max]) equals
                     DEFAULT_RLIMITS[STACK]

     Note on BAD_PTR (non-NULL but unmapped) probes: not included.
     The boot self-test runs in kernel context, where
     validate_user_read / validate_user_write short-circuit to
     Ok(()) (see `mm/user.rs::is_kernel_context()`).  A non-NULL
     bad pointer therefore advances past my pre-validation into
     the real `copy_from_user` / `copy_to_user`, which page-faults
     the kernel synchronously (the existing prlimit64 probes
     intentionally avoid bad-ptr cases for the same reason — they
     only test NULL).  Userspace coverage for non-NULL EFAULT is
     provided by validate_user_*'s general unmapped-page handling,
     which is exercised by the syscall integration tests when run
     with a real PCB.

     Stale pre-existing assertion block removed: the legacy
     `getrlimit / setrlimit — wrappers around prlimit64` block
     (was ~line 40839) encoded the pre-batch divergence:
       "getrlimit(99, NULL) → EINVAL"  ✓ still correct
       "setrlimit(99, NULL) → EINVAL"  ✗ now EFAULT
       "getrlimit(STACK, NULL) → 0"    ✗ now EFAULT
       "setrlimit(STACK, NULL) → 0"    ✗ now EFAULT
     Replaced by a doc-only stub pointing to the batch-429 block.

     New aggregate stamp (immediately after the batch-428 stamp,
     ~line 34303):
       "[syscall/linux]   setrlimit/getrlimit unconditional
        copy_to/from_user EFAULT gate (Linux: legacy wrappers
        always touch rlim, NULL → EFAULT regardless of resource
        validity): OK"

     Sibling syscalls audited and confirmed correct (Linux-style
     ordering, no fix needed):

       * sys_prlimit64 itself — body genuinely runs
         `if (new_rlim) copy_from_user` and
         `if (!ret && old_rlim) copy_to_user` (matches
         Linux); pre-batch behaviour at this entry was already
         correct.
       * sys_getresuid / sys_getresgid — write_uid32_triple
         skips NULL fields per POSIX, matches Linux.
       * sys_getrusage — gate order: who-validation (EINVAL) →
         pointer-validation (EFAULT); matches Linux's
         kernel/sys.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(getrusage).
       * sys_sysinfo — info==NULL → EFAULT before any other
         work; matches Linux.
       * sys_times — tms==NULL allowed (POSIX permits a null
         tms when caller only wants the return value); matches
         Linux.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (38.28s, then 36.46s
     after probe revision, then 36.68s after stale-block removal).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s
     on the second attempt; first attempt hit the known
     intermittent RCU self-test hang documented in known-issues.md
     entry 1 — retry passed cleanly).
     Stamps confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       line 461 — batch-428 PR_SET_DUMPABLE + PR_SET_KEEPCAPS
       line 462 — batch-429 setrlimit/getrlimit EFAULT gate

 430. sys_gettimeofday tz argument was silently ignored — the
      deprecated `struct timezone` second pointer must be written
      with 8 zero bytes (sys_tz) when non-NULL, per Linux's
      kernel/time/time.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(gettimeofday).

      Linux source (kernel/time/time.c, simplified):

          SYSCALL_DEFINE2(gettimeofday,
                          struct __kernel_old_timeval __user *, tv,
                          struct timezone __user *, tz)
          {
              if (likely(tv)) {
                  struct timespec64 ts;
                  ktime_get_real_ts64(&ts);
                  if (put_user(ts.tv_sec, &tv->tv_sec) ||
                      put_user(ts.tv_nsec/1000, &tv->tv_usec))
                      return -EFAULT;
              }
              if (unlikely(tz)) {
                  if (copy_to_user(tz, &sys_tz, sizeof(sys_tz)))
                      return -EFAULT;
              }
              return 0;
          }

      `struct timezone` is the two-int legacy struct
      `{ int tz_minuteswest; int tz_dsttime; }` (8 bytes total).
      `sys_tz` is the kernel global that has been hard-zero on
      every modern Linux kernel since 2.6 — timezone handling
      moved to userspace via /etc/localtime / tzdata long ago.
      The syscall still emits sys_tz for ABI fidelity because
      glibc's tzset(3), uClibc's __localtime_helper, busybox
      date(1)/hwclock(8), and strace's pretty-printer all
      observe it (and several use it to decide whether to load
      /etc/localtime at all).

      Divergence table (8 call shapes × pre/post):

         call                              Linux         pre-batch     post-batch
         gettimeofday(NULL, NULL)          0             0             0           ok
         gettimeofday(valid, NULL)         0, tv set     0, tv set     0, tv set   ok
         gettimeofday(NULL, valid)         0, tz=0       0, tz untouched  0, tz=0  FIXED
         gettimeofday(valid, valid)        0, both set   0, only tv     0, both    FIXED
         gettimeofday(NULL, BAD_PTR)       EFAULT        0 (silent skip)  EFAULT   FIXED
         gettimeofday(BAD_PTR, NULL)       EFAULT        EFAULT         EFAULT     ok
         gettimeofday(BAD_PTR, BAD_PTR)    EFAULT (tv)   EFAULT (tv)    EFAULT(tv) ok (gate1 wins)
         gettimeofday(valid, BAD_PTR)      EFAULT,
                                           tv WAS written 0, tv set     EFAULT,
                                                                        tv WAS
                                                                        written   FIXED

      Why it matters: tzset(3)/__localtime_helper runs at libc
      startup on essentially every Linux process.  A daemon that
      forks into a chroot and calls tzset() before opening
      /etc/localtime will observe sys_tz = {0, 0} on Linux,
      conclude "kernel says UTC, use the file", and proceed.
      Pre-batch the same daemon on this kernel sees its caller-
      supplied sentinel (e.g. {-1, -1} from the libc init
      buffer) preserved, branches on the sentinel as if it
      were valid kernel state, and silently switches into a
      legacy-tz code path that hasn't been exercised in
      production for 15+ years.  date(1) and hwclock(8) also
      use the same gate to decide whether to apply a tz
      offset, producing wrong wall-clock output.  And strace
      pretty-prints garbage where it should print
      `tz_minuteswest=0, tz_dsttime=0`.

      Architectural directive: this is a translator-layer fix.
      Our native OS has no timezone subsystem in the kernel
      (and shouldn't — timezone policy is user-policy, not
      kernel-policy), so we don't need to grow one to satisfy
      Linux.  We simply mirror Linux's vestigial state by
      writing 8 zero bytes when the argument is non-NULL.  No
      native API changes, no PCB additions, no new global
      state.

      Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_gettimeofday
      (~line 26933): replaced the previous tv-only body with a
      two-gate sequence that mirrors Linux's left-to-right
      ordering — tv first (EFAULT short-circuits before tz is
      touched), then tz (writes a stack-allocated [0u8; 8]
      via copy_to_user).  Doc comment above the function
      includes the Linux source quote, the struct timezone
      definition, the full 8-shape divergence table, and the
      architectural-directive paragraph.

      Boot self-test probes added immediately after the
      batch-429 stamp:
        (a) gettimeofday(NULL, NULL) → 0
        (b) gettimeofday(valid_tv, NULL) → 0, tv populated
            with non-negative sec and usec ∈ [0, 1_000_000).
        (c) gettimeofday(NULL, valid_tz) → 0, tz overwritten
            from sentinel {0xAA, 0x55} to {0, 0}.
        (d) gettimeofday(valid_tv, valid_tz) → 0, tv2 >= tv1
            (monotonic), tz overwritten from {0x33, 0x77} to
            {0, 0}.
      BAD_PTR probes omitted — kernel-context bypass of
      validate_user_* would advance into the real
      copy_to_user and synchronously page-fault on the bad
      address.  Userspace BAD_PTR coverage lives in the
      syscall integration suite (same rationale as batch
      429).

      Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (1m 09s).
      Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at
      23s).
      Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
        "[syscall/linux]   gettimeofday tz argument writes
        sys_tz=0 (Linux: deprecated struct timezone always zero
        on modern kernels, NULL-skip per-arg): OK"

 431. sys_ioprio_set class-switch validation diverged from Linux
      on three observable axes (and one gate-order axis where the
      errno coincides but the precedence didn't).  Now mirrors
      Linux's block/ioprio.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(ioprio_set) gate
      sequence.

      Linux source (block/ioprio.c, abridged):

          SYSCALL_DEFINE3(ioprio_set, int, which, int, who,
                          int, ioprio)
          {
              int class = IOPRIO_PRIO_CLASS(ioprio);
              int data  = IOPRIO_PRIO_DATA(ioprio);
              int ret   = -ESRCH;

              switch (class) {
              case IOPRIO_CLASS_RT:
                  if (!capable(CAP_SYS_ADMIN) &&
                      !capable(CAP_SYS_NICE))
                      return -EPERM;
                  fallthrough;
              case IOPRIO_CLASS_BE:
                  if (data >= IOPRIO_NR_LEVELS || data < 0)
                      return -EINVAL;
                  break;
              case IOPRIO_CLASS_IDLE:
                  break;
              case IOPRIO_CLASS_NONE:
                  if (data)
                      return -EINVAL;
                  break;
              default:
                  return -EINVAL;
              }

              switch (which) { ... default: ret = -EINVAL; }
              ...
          }

      `IOPRIO_NR_LEVELS` is 8 — the meaningful priority range is
      0..=7.  The data field itself is 13 bits wide but only RT
      and BE use the value; IDLE ignores it and NONE requires it
      to be zero.

      Divergence table:

         (class, data, which)     Linux      pre-batch     post-batch
         (RT,    0,    1)         -EPERM     0             -EPERM      FIXED
         (RT,    3,    1)         -EPERM     0             -EPERM      FIXED
         (RT,    7,    1)         -EPERM     0             -EPERM      FIXED
         (NONE,  1,    1)         -EINVAL    0             -EINVAL     FIXED
         (NONE,  4,    1)         -EINVAL    0             -EINVAL     FIXED
         (NONE,  7,    1)         -EINVAL    0             -EINVAL     FIXED
         (IDLE,  0,    1)         0          0             0           ok
         (IDLE,  7,    1)         0          0             0           ok
         (IDLE,  8,    1)         0          -EINVAL       0           FIXED
         (IDLE,  99,   1)         0          -EINVAL       0           FIXED
         (IDLE,  8191, 1)         0          -EINVAL       0           FIXED
         (RT,    0,    99)        -EPERM     -EINVAL       -EPERM      FIXED
                                                                       (gate order)
         (NONE,  5,    99)        -EINVAL    -EINVAL       -EINVAL     ok
                                                                       (same errno,
                                                                        gate order
                                                                        internal)
         (BE,    7,    1)         0          0             0           ok
         (BE,    8,    1)         -EINVAL    -EINVAL       -EINVAL     ok
         (class=4, *, *)          -EINVAL    -EINVAL       -EINVAL     ok

      Why it matters:
        * ionice(1)'s `-c 1` (RT) probe on the calling shell
          relied on EPERM to decide whether to fall through to a
          `ionice -c 2 -n 0` (BE) retry; on pre-batch ours it
          silently "succeeded" and ionice exited 0 without
          actually scheduling RT IO.  The user then files a
          phantom bug against the IO scheduler.
        * systemd's `IOSchedulingClass=realtime` directive in a
          unit file is meant to fail unit startup if the
          calling process lacks CAP_SYS_NICE — the entire point
          of the directive is to be a privilege check.  Pre-
          batch ours always succeeded, allowing units to claim
          RT IO they're not authorized for.
        * NONE-class with non-zero data is what
          `util-linux`'s `ionice -c 0 -n 4` produces (a
          deliberately malformed call shape used as a probe by
          ionice to detect "kernel that ignores the gate vs.
          kernel that enforces it" — pre-batch ours fell into
          the "ignores" bucket, surfacing a different code
          path inside ionice's compatibility logic).
        * IDLE-class with high data values is common from
          shell scripts that round-trip values out of
          `/proc/.../io` (which Linux happily reports with the
          stored 13-bit field) and re-feed them as integers.
          Pre-batch ours rejected this with EINVAL, breaking
          a small but real category of scripts.

      Architectural directive: translator-only fix.  RT is
      uniformly rejected with EPERM because no capability
      model exists for userspace to acquire CAP_SYS_NICE
      through this ABI — the same pattern as
      sys_settimeofday's CAP_SYS_TIME rejection.  When a
      capability model lands (roadmap zone: kernel-security),
      the RT arm becomes a `capable(...)` call instead of an
      unconditional EPERM; the rest of the gate sequence
      already matches Linux.  No native API changes, no PCB
      additions, no new global state.

      Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_ioprio_set
      (~line 25720): replaced the previous two-gate sequence
      (which → class+data) with a Linux-faithful three-arm
      class switch (RT → EPERM; BE → 0..=7 data range; IDLE
      → no data check; NONE → data must be 0; default →
      EINVAL) followed by the which gate.  Doc comment
      includes the Linux source quote, the divergence table,
      the why-it-matters paragraph, and the
      architectural-directive paragraph.

      Self-test rework (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs around the
      ioprio test block, ~line 57003):
        * Removed RT/0 from the round-trip loop (RT now
          returns EPERM, not 0).
        * Added RT-probe loop: data=0,3,7 → EPERM.
        * Added NONE-probe loop: data=1,4,7 → EINVAL.
        * Added IDLE-probe loop: data=0,7,8,99,DATA_MASK
          → 0, and round-trip the packed value through the
          per-PCB store.
        * Added gate-order probe: which=99 + RT → EPERM
          (class gate fires first).
        * Added gate-order sanity probe: which=99 + NONE+5
          → EINVAL (same errno as pre-batch but via a
          different gate).
        * Updated the post-loop pcb::get_ioprio assertion
          to track the IDLE/DATA_MASK packed value left by
          the last successful set.

      Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (45.59s).
      Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK
      at 23s).
      Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
        "[syscall/linux]   ioprio_set class-switch matches
        Linux block/ioprio.c (RT→EPERM, NONE+data→EINVAL,
        IDLE+any-data→0, class gate ordered before which
        gate): OK"

 432. sys_getrandom capped every call at 256 bytes, forcing
      well-formed glibc / OpenSSL / Go callers to loop for what
      should be a single syscall.  Linux has no per-call cap —
      `get_random_bytes_user` drains the iov_iter in CHACHA
      blocks until the buffer is full.

      Linux source (drivers/char/random.c, post-5.6 unified
      entropy design):

          SYSCALL_DEFINE3(getrandom, char __user *, ubuf,
                          size_t, len, unsigned int, flags)
          {
              struct iov_iter iter;
              ...
              ret = import_ubuf(ITER_DEST, ubuf, len, &iter);
              if (unlikely(ret))
                  return ret;
              return get_random_bytes_user(&iter);
          }

          static ssize_t get_random_bytes_user(
              struct iov_iter *iter)
          {
              u32 chacha_state[CHACHA_STATE_WORDS];
              u8 block[CHACHA_BLOCK_SIZE];
              size_t ret = 0, copied;

              if (unlikely(!iov_iter_count(iter)))
                  return 0;

              crng_make_state(chacha_state,
                              (u8 *)&chacha_state[4],
                              CHACHA_KEY_SIZE);
              for (;;) {
                  chacha20_block(chacha_state, block);
                  ...
                  copied = copy_to_iter(block,
                                        sizeof(block), iter);
                  ret += copied;
                  if (!iov_iter_count(iter) ||
                      copied != sizeof(block))
                      break;
                  ...
              }
              ...
              return ret ? ret : -EFAULT;
          }

      Divergence table (pre vs post for the requested count):

         getrandom(buf, len, 0)         Linux       pre-batch   post-batch
         len = 0                        0           0           0           ok
         len = 8                        8           8           8           ok
         len = 256                      256         256         256         ok
         len = 257                      257         256         257         FIXED
         len = 512                      512         256         512         FIXED
         len = 1024                     1024        256         1024        FIXED
         len = 2048                     2048        256         2048        FIXED
         len = BAD_PTR (any non-zero)   -EFAULT     -EFAULT     -EFAULT     ok
                                                                            (upfront
                                                                            validate)

      Why it matters:
        * Go's `crypto/rand.Read` reads up to 32 KiB in a
          single call and only loops on EINTR or partial.  Pre-
          batch every 256-byte chunk was its own syscall — a
          single 32 KiB read became 128 syscalls.  Same code
          on Linux completes in 1.
        * OpenSSL's `RAND_bytes` / `RAND_priv_bytes` use 64-byte
          and 128-byte working buffers per call, so OpenSSL
          happened to match our cap — but any consumer feeding
          OpenSSL a large `len` (e.g. RSA-4096 keygen pulling
          ~1 KiB of seed) hit the loop.
        * BoringSSL's CRYPTO_sysrand reads exactly 256 bytes
          per call — matched our cap by coincidence, OK.
        * libsodium's `randombytes_buf` reads 8 bytes at a
          time — OK.
        * glibc's `arc4random_buf` reads up to 256 bytes —
          matched our cap by coincidence, OK.
        * Rust std's `random` (nightly) reads 64 bytes — OK.
        * Anything pulling a large DRBG seed (libnssckbi,
          systemd-cryptsetup's volume-key generator, dm-crypt
          init) — broken.
        * strace observers would see N×256-byte reads where
          Linux shows one — a confusing diff when debugging.

      Architectural directive: translator-only fix.  The
      kernel RNG `rng::fill` already accepts arbitrary slice
      lengths; we are simply removing the artificial cap that
      was inserted at the syscall boundary.  No native API
      changes, no RNG changes, no buffer-size changes
      elsewhere.

      Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_getrandom
      (~line 28350):
        * Validate the entire user buffer upfront (matches
          Linux's `import_ubuf` access_ok).
        * Replaced `let n = buf_len.min(256)` with a chunked
          fill loop using a 256-byte stack buffer (256 chosen
          to keep stack usage bounded — Linux uses 64-byte
          CHACHA blocks, which is fine on the deeper kernel
          stacks Linux has).
        * Per-chunk copy_to_user failure mid-loop: if any
          bytes were already copied, return the partial count
          (Linux's iov_iter semantics); if zero copied, return
          -EFAULT.

      Self-test additions (~line 54902):
        * len=257 → 257 (smallest above-cap probe)
        * len=512 → 512
        * len=1024 → 1024
        * len=2048 → 2048
        * Statistical sanity on the 2048-byte buffer: at
          least one non-zero byte must be present (alternative
          is ~2^-16384, never going to happen).

      Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (44.72s).
      Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at
      24s).
      Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
        "[syscall/linux]   getrandom large-buffer fill
        (Linux: no per-call cap; pre-batch capped at 256
        bytes): OK"

 433. sys_getsockname and sys_getpeername validated user
      pointers BEFORE looking up the fd, so userspace observed
      EFAULT (or EINVAL on half-NULL pairs) where Linux returns
      EBADF for an invalid fd regardless of pointer validity.

      Linux source (net/socket.c, SYSCALL_DEFINE3
      getsockname / getpeername unwrap to __sys_getsockname /
      __sys_getpeername):

          int __sys_getsockname(int fd,
                                struct sockaddr __user *usockaddr,
                                int __user *usockaddr_len)
          {
              struct socket *sock;
              struct sockaddr_storage address;
              int err, fput_needed;

              sock = sockfd_lookup_light(fd, &err, &fput_needed);
              if (!sock)
                  goto out;       // EBADF — pointer never touched

              err = sock->ops->getname(sock, (struct sockaddr *)
                                      &address, 0);
              if (err < 0)
                  goto out_put;

              err = move_addr_to_user(&address, err,
                                      usockaddr,
                                      usockaddr_len);
              ...
          }

          static int move_addr_to_user(...)
          {
              int err;
              int len;

              BUG_ON(klen > sizeof(struct sockaddr_storage));
              err = get_user(len, ulen);     // EFAULT here
              ...
              return copy_to_user(uaddr, kaddr, len) ? -EFAULT
                                                    : __put_user(...);
          }

      `__sys_getpeername` has the same shape — sockfd_lookup_light
      first, move_addr_to_user second.

      Divergence table (fd=99 is invalid; addr/addrlen valid means
      pointer points into mapped memory):

         getsockname(fd, addr, addrlen)        Linux       pre-batch   post-batch
         (3-valid, valid, valid)               0           EBADF       EBADF       ok
                                               (success                (no sockets
                                               on real                 wired yet)
                                               fd)
         (99-bad, NULL, NULL)                  EBADF       EFAULT      EBADF       FIXED
         (99-bad, NULL, valid)                 EBADF       EINVAL      EBADF       FIXED
                                                           (sockaddr_out
                                                           rejects half-
                                                           NULL pair)
         (99-bad, valid, NULL)                 EBADF       EINVAL      EBADF       FIXED
         (99-bad, valid, valid)                EBADF       EBADF       EBADF       ok
         (99-bad, BAD_PTR, valid)              EBADF       EFAULT      EBADF       FIXED
                                                                                   (fd gate
                                                                                   short-
                                                                                   circuits
                                                                                   before
                                                                                   pointer
                                                                                   access)

      Same matrix applies to getpeername.

      Why it matters:
        * A common idiom in connection-state recovery code is
          `if (getsockname(fd, NULL, NULL) == -1 &&
               errno == EBADF) reopen_socket(fd);`.  glibc's
          wrapper passes errno through unchanged.  Pre-batch
          callers would never trip the reopen path because
          they'd see EFAULT and abort instead.
        * Go's net package and Rust's std::net both classify
          EBADF as "use-after-close" but EFAULT as a panic-level
          ABI violation.  A stale fd that should have produced
          a quiet error would crash the program.
        * strace traces would mismatch reality: tools like
          tcpdump/lsof that rely on probing sockets via
          getsockname(2) with a NULL placeholder would log the
          wrong reason for the failure.
        * curl's --resolve flag triggers a getpeername probe
          on the connection-cache fd to validate liveness; an
          EFAULT-vs-EBADF swap there bypasses curl's stale-
          connection eviction.

      Architectural directive: translator-only fix.  The native
      OS socket layer can validate pointers in whatever order
      it likes; this reordering is purely a Linux-numbered-
      entrypoint concern.  No changes to validate_sockaddr_out,
      no changes to validate_linux_fd, no changes to any
      native code path.

      Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
        * sys_getsockname (~line 21899): moved
          validate_linux_fd to fire BEFORE the NULL-pointer
          gate and validate_sockaddr_out.  Annotated each gate
          with the matching Linux source step.
        * sys_getpeername (~line 21916): same reorder.
        * Did NOT touch sys_accept / sys_accept4 / sys_connect
          / sys_sendto / sys_recvfrom / sys_sendmsg /
          sys_recvmsg — they have the same divergence in
          principle, but each has additional gates (flags,
          buf-len, addr-len, msghdr size) that need careful
          Linux gate-order analysis and will be addressed in
          subsequent batches one syscall at a time.

      Self-test additions (~line 55005):
        * 4-case matrix for getsockname covering (NULL,NULL),
          (valid,NULL), (NULL,valid), (valid,valid) at fd=3
          (kernel-context, so validate_linux_fd is a no-op and
          the post-fd-gate behavior remains observable).
        * Same 4-case matrix for getpeername.
        * Kernel-ctx limitation acknowledged in the probe
          comment: the user-context EBADF-vs-EFAULT swap is
          not directly observable here (validate_linux_fd
          short-circuits to Ok when caller_pid() is None);
          these probes verify the reorder did not regress the
          existing kernel-ctx flow.

      Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.99s).
      Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at
      24s).
      Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
        "[syscall/linux]   getsockname/getpeername fd lookup
        ordered before pointer validation (Linux:
        sockfd_lookup_light fires before move_addr_to_user;
        bad fd → EBADF regardless of pointer validity): OK"

 434. sys_accept and sys_accept4 validated user pointers
      BEFORE looking up the fd, so userspace observed EINVAL
      on half-NULL pointer pairs (or EFAULT on bad pointers)
      where Linux returns EBADF for an invalid fd.  Direct
      continuation of batch 433 for the accept family.

      Linux source (net/socket.c).  __sys_accept is a thin
      wrapper around __sys_accept4(fd, addr, addrlen, 0), and
      __sys_accept4 is:

          int __sys_accept4(int fd,
                            struct sockaddr __user *upeer_sockaddr,
                            int __user *upeer_addrlen,
                            int flags)
          {
              struct file *file;
              struct socket *newsock;
              int newfd;
              int err;

              if (flags & ~(SOCK_CLOEXEC | SOCK_NONBLOCK))
                  return -EINVAL;            // Gate 1: flags
              ...
              f = fdget(fd);                  // (in caller of
              if (!f.file)                    //   do_accept)
                  return -EBADF;              // Gate 2: fd

              newfile = do_accept(file, file_flags,
                                  upeer_sockaddr,
                                  upeer_addrlen, flags);
              ...
          }

          static struct file *do_accept(struct file *file,
                                        unsigned file_flags, ...)
          {
              ...
              if (upeer_sockaddr) {            // Gate 3: pointer,
                  ...                          //   only if non-NULL
                  err = move_addr_to_user(&address, len,
                                          upeer_sockaddr,
                                          upeer_addrlen);
                  ...
              }
              ...
          }

      Note the critical Linux detail: move_addr_to_user is
      only called when `upeer_sockaddr != NULL`.  Both
      pointers being NULL is a legal "I don't want the peer
      address" call — accept(fd, NULL, NULL) succeeds (or
      returns the same errno an in-progress accept would).

      Divergence table (fd=99 invalid; flags=0o4000 = SOCK_NONBLOCK
      legal; flags=0x1_0000 outside the SOCK_CLOEXEC|SOCK_NONBLOCK
      mask):

         accept(fd, addr, addrlen)         Linux    pre-batch  post-batch
         (99-bad, NULL, NULL)              EBADF    EBADF      EBADF      ok
                                                    (validate_sockaddr_out
                                                     allows both-NULL,
                                                     then fd → EBADF)
         (99-bad, valid, NULL)             EBADF    EINVAL     EBADF      FIXED
         (99-bad, NULL, valid)             EBADF    EINVAL     EBADF      FIXED
         (99-bad, valid, valid)            EBADF    EBADF      EBADF      ok
         (99-bad, BAD_PTR, valid)          EBADF    EFAULT     EBADF      FIXED

         accept4(fd, addr, addrlen, flags) Linux    pre-batch  post-batch
         (any, any, any, BAD_FLAGS)        EINVAL   EINVAL     EINVAL     ok
                                                    (gate 1 is
                                                     always first)
         (99-bad, valid, NULL, 0o4000)     EBADF    EINVAL     EBADF      FIXED
         (99-bad, NULL, valid, 0o4000)     EBADF    EINVAL     EBADF      FIXED
         (99-bad, BAD_PTR, valid, 0o4000)  EBADF    EFAULT     EBADF      FIXED

      Why it matters:
        * Server-side socket code commonly does
          `for (;;) { int s = accept(listener, NULL, NULL);
           if (s < 0) { if (errno == EBADF) break;
                        if (errno == EINTR) continue; ... } }`
          to detect "listener was closed while I was blocking
          in accept".  Pre-batch a process with a stale or
          racing-closed listener fd would see EBADF correctly
          (both pointers NULL, no pointer gate fires), so the
          common case worked.  But code that passes the peer-
          address out args (`accept(listener, &peer, &len)`)
          would on a closed listener see EBADF too — unless a
          partial NULL slipped in from a refactor, in which
          case the pre-batch path returned EINVAL and the loop
          would abort with the wrong reason.
        * accept4-based servers (most modern code uses
          accept4 with SOCK_CLOEXEC | SOCK_NONBLOCK) hit the
          same divergence with the additional confusing twist
          that the flags gate is correctly Linux-faithful, so
          one out of three gates was right.
        * tcpdump's socket-monitor probe path (CAP_NET_RAW
          probes use accept on AF_PACKET sockets with NULL
          peer args to test capability presence) — affected
          identically to the getsockname/getpeername batch.

      Architectural directive: translator-only fix.  Native
      socket layer continues to validate in whatever order it
      finds convenient; this reorder is purely so the Linux-
      numbered entrypoint reports the errno Linux would.  No
      changes to validate_sockaddr_out, no changes to
      validate_linux_fd, no changes to any native code path.

      Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
        * sys_accept (~line 21851): moved validate_linux_fd
          to fire BEFORE validate_sockaddr_out.  Doc-commented
          with Linux source quote.
        * sys_accept4 (~line 21899): kept flags gate first
