          (matches Linux), then moved fd before pointer.
        * Did NOT touch sys_connect / sys_sendto / sys_recvfrom
          / sys_sendmsg / sys_recvmsg — each carries its own
          gate matrix (addr_len, buf_len, msghdr size) that
          needs separate Linux gate-order analysis and will be
          addressed in subsequent batches.

      Self-test additions (~line 55186):
        * accept 4-case matrix: (NULL,NULL) → EBADF (legal
          ignore-peer), (valid,NULL) → EINVAL (half-NULL),
          (NULL,valid) → EINVAL, (valid,valid) → EBADF.
        * accept4 5-case matrix: bad-flags pin (EINVAL),
          bad-flags + valid ptrs (EINVAL — gate 1 wins),
          legal-flags + NULL/NULL (EBADF), legal-flags +
          half-NULL (EINVAL), legal-flags + valid/valid
          (EBADF).
        * Kernel-ctx limitation acknowledged in comment: the
          user-context EBADF-vs-EINVAL/EFAULT swap is not
          directly observable here.  The probes verify the
          reorder did not regress the kernel-ctx flow.

      Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (41.58s).
      Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at
      24s).
      Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
        "[syscall/linux]   accept/accept4 fd lookup ordered
        before pointer validation (Linux: do_accept's fdget
        fires before move_addr_to_user; accept4 flags gate
        stays first; bad fd → EBADF regardless of pointer
        validity): OK"

 435. sys_bind and sys_connect validated user pointers BEFORE
      looking up the fd, so userspace observed EFAULT (or
      EINVAL on short addrlen) where Linux returns EBADF for
      an invalid fd.  Continuation of the socket-family fd-
      gate-ordering sweep (batches 433: getsockname/getpeername;
      434: accept/accept4).

      Linux source (net/socket.c).  Both __sys_bind and
      __sys_connect share the same shape:

          int __sys_bind(int fd, struct sockaddr __user *umyaddr,
                         int addrlen)
          {
              struct socket *sock;
              struct sockaddr_storage address;
              int err, fput_needed;

              sock = sockfd_lookup_light(fd, &err,
                                         &fput_needed);
              if (sock) {
                  err = move_addr_to_kernel(umyaddr, addrlen,
                                            &address);
                  ...
              }
              return err;          // -EBADF if !sock
          }

          int __sys_connect(int fd,
                            struct sockaddr __user *uservaddr,
                            int addrlen)
          {
              int ret = -EBADF;
              struct fd f;

              f = fdget(fd);
              if (f.file) {
                  struct sockaddr_storage address;
                  ret = move_addr_to_kernel(uservaddr, addrlen,
                                            &address);
                  ...
              }
              return ret;
          }

      Both run fd-lookup-before-pointer-access.  An invalid
      fd short-circuits with -EBADF regardless of whether
      umyaddr / uservaddr / addrlen are valid.

      Divergence table (fd=99 invalid; addr_len=16 is the
      typical sockaddr_in size):

         bind(fd, addr, addrlen)        Linux    pre-batch  post-batch
         (99-bad, NULL, 16)             EBADF    EFAULT     EBADF      FIXED
         (99-bad, valid, 0)             EBADF    EINVAL     EBADF      FIXED
         (99-bad, valid, 1)             EBADF    EINVAL     EBADF      FIXED
         (99-bad, BAD_PTR, 16)          EBADF    EFAULT     EBADF      FIXED
         (99-bad, valid, 16)            EBADF    EBADF      EBADF      ok

         connect(fd, addr, addrlen)     Linux    pre-batch  post-batch
         (same matrix as bind)

      Why it matters:
        * The classic "did my listener get closed under me?"
          probe is bind/connect on a known-bad fd: code
          checks `errno == EBADF` to confirm.  Pre-batch the
          same probe with a NULL placeholder addr would
          surface EFAULT and the recovery path wouldn't fire.
        * Connection-pool implementations (libpq, Redis
          clients, etc.) frequently retry connect() on a
          pooled fd that may have been silently closed by a
          server-side reset; their canonical error
          classification is "EBADF = repool" vs "EAGAIN /
          ETIMEDOUT = retry-after-delay".  An EFAULT diagnosis
          confuses retry logic into the wrong path.
        * AF_UNIX clients constructing sockaddr_un on the
          stack and then accidentally passing addrlen = 0
          (a not-uncommon truncation bug from off-by-one
          path length math) — pre-batch would see EINVAL
          even with an already-closed socket fd; Linux
          returns EBADF and a `if (errno == EBADF) reopen()`
          recovery would work.

      Architectural directive: translator-only fix.  Native
      bind/connect path can validate in whatever order it
      finds convenient; this reorder is purely so the Linux-
      numbered entrypoint reports the errno Linux would.  No
      changes to validate_sockaddr_in, no changes to
      validate_linux_fd, no changes to any native code path.

      Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
        * sys_bind (~line 21815): moved validate_linux_fd
          to fire BEFORE validate_sockaddr_in.  Doc-commented
          with the __sys_bind source quote.
        * sys_connect (~line 21959): moved validate_linux_fd
          to fire BEFORE validate_sockaddr_in.  Doc-commented
          with the __sys_connect source quote.
        * Did NOT touch sys_sendto / sys_recvfrom / sys_sendmsg
          / sys_recvmsg — each carries its own gate matrix
          (flags, buf-len, msghdr size + per-iovec checks)
          that needs its own Linux gate-order analysis.

      Self-test additions (~line 55299):
        * bind 4-case matrix: (NULL,16) → EFAULT, (valid,0)
          → EINVAL, (valid,1) → EINVAL, (valid,16) → EBADF.
        * Same 4-case matrix for connect.
        * Same kernel-ctx caveat as batches 433/434: the
          user-context EBADF-vs-EFAULT/EINVAL swap is not
          directly observable here.

      Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (42.85s).
      Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at
      24s).
      Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
        "[syscall/linux]   bind/connect fd lookup ordered
        before pointer validation (Linux: sockfd_lookup_light
        / fdget fires before move_addr_to_kernel; bad fd →
        EBADF regardless of addr/addrlen): OK"

 436. sys_sendto and sys_recvfrom validated the addr pointer
      BEFORE looking up the fd (but correctly validated the
      buf pointer first).  Linux's __sys_sendto and
      __sys_recvfrom in net/socket.c run BUF → FD → ADDR;
      pre-batch we ran BUF → ADDR → FD.  Continuation of the
      socket-family fd-gate-ordering sweep (433: getsockname/
      getpeername; 434: accept/accept4; 435: bind/connect).

      Linux source (net/socket.c):

          int __sys_sendto(int fd, void __user *buff, size_t len,
                           unsigned int flags,
                           struct sockaddr __user *addr,
                           int addr_len)
          {
              struct socket *sock;
              struct sockaddr_storage address;
              int err;
              struct msghdr msg;
              int fput_needed;

              err = import_ubuf(ITER_SOURCE, buff, len,
                                &msg.msg_iter);          // Gate 1: buf
              if (unlikely(err))
                  return err;

              sock = sockfd_lookup_light(fd, &err,
                                         &fput_needed);  // Gate 2: fd
              if (!sock)
                  goto out;
              ...
              if (addr) {                                 // Gate 3: addr,
                  err = move_addr_to_kernel(addr,         //  only if non-NULL
                                            addr_len,
                                            &address);
                  ...
              }
              ...
          }

          int __sys_recvfrom(int fd, void __user *ubuf,
                             size_t size, unsigned int flags,
                             struct sockaddr __user *addr,
                             int __user *addr_len)
          {
              ...
              err = import_ubuf(ITER_DEST, ubuf, size,
                                &msg.msg_iter);          // Gate 1: buf
              if (unlikely(err))
                  return err;

              sock = sockfd_lookup_light(fd, &err,
                                         &fput_needed);  // Gate 2: fd
              if (!sock)
                  goto out;
              ...
              if (addr != NULL) {                         // Gate 3: addr
                  err2 = move_addr_to_user(&address,
                                           msg.msg_namelen,
                                           addr, addr_len);
                  ...
              }
          }

      Both share the same three-gate pattern.  Note the
      buf gate fires FIRST in Linux, so NULL/bad buf with
      len > 0 surfaces as EFAULT before any fd lookup —
      pre-batch already matched this.  Only the FD↔ADDR
      ordering was wrong.

      Divergence table (fd=99 invalid, buf=valid, len=16):

         sendto(fd,buf,len,fl,addr,addrlen)  Linux pre  post
         (99,NULL,16,_,_,_)                  EFAULT EFAULT EFAULT  ok
                                             (gate 1 always wins)
         (99,valid,16,_,NULL,0)              EBADF  EBADF  EBADF   ok
                                             (addr NULL skips gate 3)
         (99,valid,16,_,valid,0)             EBADF  EINVAL EBADF   FIXED
         (99,valid,16,_,valid,1)             EBADF  EINVAL EBADF   FIXED
         (99,valid,16,_,BAD_PTR,16)          EBADF  EFAULT EBADF   FIXED

         recvfrom(fd,buf,len,fl,addr,addrlen_ptr) — same shape,
         except gate 3 uses validate_sockaddr_out (both-NULL
         is the "I don't want the source addr" idiom, half-NULL
         is EINVAL).

         (99,NULL,16,_,_,_)                  EFAULT EFAULT EFAULT  ok
         (99,valid,16,_,NULL,NULL)           EBADF  EBADF  EBADF   ok
         (99,valid,16,_,valid,NULL)          EBADF  EINVAL EBADF   FIXED
         (99,valid,16,_,NULL,valid)          EBADF  EINVAL EBADF   FIXED
         (99,valid,16,_,valid,valid)         EBADF  EBADF  EBADF   ok
         (99,valid,16,_,BAD_ADDR,valid)      EBADF  EFAULT EBADF   FIXED

      Why it matters:
        * UDP servers using sendto/recvfrom for stateless
          peer-addressed I/O frequently encode "is my socket
          still alive?" probes by reusing the dest_addr slot
          with a partial or stale buffer.  Pre-batch the
          stale-pointer EFAULT or short-addrlen EINVAL would
          mask the real EBADF cause.
        * QUIC / DTLS implementations use scatter-gather
          sendto patterns with carefully sized addr_len based
          on AF_INET vs AF_INET6 detection; an off-by-one
          (addr_len = 14 instead of 16 for sockaddr_in) on a
          known-bad fd would pre-batch surface as EINVAL
          where Linux returns EBADF, leading to "addr is
          malformed" diagnostic logs when the real cause is
          a closed socket.
        * DNS resolvers (musl's __resolvconf, glibc's
          getaddrinfo workers) build a sockaddr_storage on
          the stack and pass `sizeof(sockaddr_storage)` —
          unusually large but legal.  Pre-batch our
          validate_sockaddr_in didn't enforce a max; this
          path was OK.  The divergence only bit programs
          that genuinely had small addrlen, but the EBADF
          masking still mattered.

      Architectural directive: translator-only fix.  Native
      sendto/recvfrom path can validate in whatever order it
      finds convenient.  No native code touched.

      Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
        * sys_sendto (~line 22111): moved validate_linux_fd
          to fire AFTER the buf gate but BEFORE the addr
          gate.  Doc-commented with __sys_sendto source quote.
        * sys_recvfrom (~line 22138): same reorder.  Doc-
          commented with __sys_recvfrom source quote.
        * Did NOT touch sys_sendmsg / sys_recvmsg /
          sys_sendmmsg / sys_recvmmsg — msghdr-shaped
          syscalls carry additional gates (iovec walk,
          control-message size) that need separate analysis.

      Self-test additions (~line 55400):
        * sendto 5-case matrix: NULL buf+len (EFAULT), valid
          buf with NULL addr (EBADF), valid+addr_len=1
          (EINVAL), valid+valid (EBADF), zero-len with valid
          addr (EBADF).
        * recvfrom 5-case matrix: NULL buf+len (EFAULT),
          NULL+NULL addr (EBADF), half-NULL (EINVAL), valid
          addr/addrlen (EBADF), zero-len with valid addr
          (EBADF).
        * Kernel-ctx caveat unchanged: user-context EBADF-
          vs-EFAULT/EINVAL swap not directly observable.

      Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.84s).
      Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at
      23s).
      Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
        "[syscall/linux]   sendto/recvfrom fd lookup ordered
        BETWEEN buf and addr (Linux: import_ubuf →
        sockfd_lookup_light → move_addr_to_kernel/user; bad
        fd with valid buf and bad addr → EBADF instead of
        EFAULT/EINVAL): OK"

 437. sys_sendmsg and sys_recvmsg validated the msghdr
      pointer BEFORE looking up the fd, so userspace observed
      EFAULT where Linux returns EBADF for an invalid fd.
      Continuation of the socket-family fd-gate-ordering
      sweep (433: getsockname/getpeername; 434: accept/
      accept4; 435: bind/connect; 436: sendto/recvfrom).

      Linux source (net/socket.c).  __sys_sendmsg and
      __sys_recvmsg share the shape:

          long __sys_sendmsg(int fd,
                             struct user_msghdr __user *msg,
                             unsigned int flags,
                             bool forbid_cmsg_compat)
          {
              int fput_needed, err;
              struct msghdr msg_sys;
              struct socket *sock;

              sock = sockfd_lookup_light(fd, &err,
                                         &fput_needed);
              if (!sock)
                  goto out;

              err = ___sys_sendmsg(sock, msg, &msg_sys,
                                   flags, NULL, 0);

              fput_light(sock->file, fput_needed);
          out:
              return err;
          }

          static int ___sys_sendmsg(...)
          {
              ...
              err = copy_msghdr_from_user(msg_sys, msg, &iov);
              ...
          }

      sockfd_lookup_light runs FIRST: a bogus fd short-
      circuits with -EBADF regardless of whether msg is NULL,
      faulting, or perfectly valid.  Only after the fd
      resolves does Linux walk into copy_msghdr_from_user
      where copy_from_user(msg) can surface EFAULT.

      Divergence table (fd=99 invalid):

         sendmsg(fd, msg, flags)        Linux  pre    post
         (99, NULL, 0)                  EBADF  EFAULT EBADF  FIXED
         (99, BAD_PTR, 0)               EBADF  EFAULT EBADF  FIXED
         (99, valid, 0)                 EBADF  EBADF  EBADF  ok

         recvmsg(fd, msg, flags)        Linux  pre    post
         (same matrix)

      Why it matters:
        * Highly-multiplexed I/O loops (HAProxy, Envoy,
          nginx) wrap sendmsg/recvmsg in connection-multiplex
          paths where the fd may be closed by a sibling
          worker between the loop's epoll wakeup and the
          sendmsg/recvmsg call.  These loops classify EBADF
          as "drop this connection from the pool" but EFAULT
          as a "I built a bad message structure" bug —
          completely different recovery paths.
        * SCM_RIGHTS / SCM_CREDENTIALS users (systemd, dbus,
          PulseAudio) probe AF_UNIX socket validity by
          attempting recvmsg(fd, &probe_hdr, MSG_PEEK |
          MSG_DONTWAIT).  Probe code that passes NULL for an
          uninitialised iteration would pre-batch surface
          EFAULT where Linux returns EBADF, breaking the
          probe.
        * QUIC userspace stacks (msquic, quiche, picoquic)
          using sendmsg with control-message offload (GSO,
          UDP_SEGMENT) — same diagnostic concerns.

      Architectural directive: translator-only fix.  No
      changes to validate_user_read / validate_user_write,
      no changes to validate_linux_fd, no native code
      touched.

      Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
        * sys_sendmsg (~line 22243): moved validate_linux_fd
          to fire BEFORE the NULL check and the
          validate_user_read(56) gate.  Doc-commented with
          __sys_sendmsg source quote.
        * sys_recvmsg (~line 22276): same reorder; both
          read and write access checks on the msghdr (recvmsg
          writes msg_flags) stay after the fd gate.
        * Did NOT touch sys_sendmmsg / sys_recvmmsg — the
          mmsg shapes carry vlen-based size math and (for
          recvmmsg) a timeout-first gate that needs separate
          Linux gate-order analysis.

      Self-test additions (~line 55505):
        * sendmsg 2-case: (NULL,0) → EFAULT, (valid,0) → EBADF.
        * recvmsg 2-case: (NULL,0) → EFAULT, (valid,0) → EBADF.
        * Kernel-ctx caveat unchanged.

      Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (43.12s).
      Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at
      25s).
      Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
        "[syscall/linux]   sendmsg/recvmsg fd lookup ordered
        before msghdr validation (Linux: sockfd_lookup_light
        fires before copy_msghdr_from_user; bad fd → EBADF
        regardless of msghdr pointer validity): OK"

438. [2026-06-06] syscall/linux: sendmmsg/recvmmsg fd lookup
     ordered before per-msg pointer validation (Linux fidelity,
     translator-only)

     Continuation of batches 433–437 socket-family fd-vs-pointer
     gate audit.  Batch 437 fixed sendmsg/recvmsg but explicitly
     deferred the mmsg shapes because they layer a vlen-based
     size computation and (for recvmmsg) a timeout-first gate
     on top of the fd gate.  This batch handles them.

     Linux source (net/socket.c, __sys_sendmmsg /
     __sys_recvmmsg, kernel 6.x):

         int __sys_sendmmsg(int fd, struct mmsghdr __user *mmsg,
                            unsigned int vlen, unsigned int flags,
                            bool forbid_cmsg_compat)
         {
             int datagrams;
             struct socket *sock;
             int err, fput_needed;

             if (forbid_cmsg_compat && (flags & MSG_CMSG_COMPAT))
                 return -EINVAL;

             sock = sockfd_lookup_light(fd, &err, &fput_needed);
             if (!sock)
                 return err;

             err = do_sendmmsg(sock, mmsg, vlen, flags);

             fput_light(sock->file, fput_needed);
             return err;
         }

         int __sys_recvmmsg(int fd, struct mmsghdr __user *mmsg,
                            unsigned int vlen, unsigned int flags,
                            struct __kernel_timespec __user *timeout,
                            struct old_timespec32 __user *timeout32)
         {
             struct __kernel_timespec timeout_sys;
             ...
             /* TIMEOUT FIRST: validate user-supplied timeout
                regardless of fd / mmsg validity */
             if (timeout) {
                 if (get_timespec64(&timeout_sys, timeout))
                     return -EFAULT;
                 ...
                 if (!timespec64_valid(&timeout_sys))
                     return -EINVAL;
             }
             ...
             datagrams = do_recvmmsg(fd, mmsg, vlen, flags,
                                     timeout ? &timeout_sys : NULL);
             ...
         }

         static int do_recvmmsg(int fd, ...)
         {
             struct socket *sock;
             ...
             sock = sockfd_lookup_light(fd, &err, &fput_needed);
             if (!sock)
                 return err;
             ...
             /* per-msg loop: copy_msghdr_from_user on each entry */
         }

     Gate order in Linux:
       (1) recvmmsg only: timeout get_user + timespec64_valid
           → EFAULT / EINVAL.  sendmmsg has no timeout.
       (2) sockfd_lookup_light(fd) → bad fd short-circuits with
           -EBADF regardless of mmsg pointer / vlen.
       (3) per-mmsg-entry copy_msghdr_from_user, which can
           surface -EFAULT on individual entries.

     Note: vlen==0 in Linux is "no datagrams to deliver" and
     returns 0 quickly inside do_sendmmsg / do_recvmmsg, but
     this happens AFTER sockfd_lookup_light.  Our translator
     short-circuits vlen==0 with -EBADF (the fd was bogus so
     even the "do nothing" path must still report the bad fd)
     — this matches what sockfd_lookup_light would report
     before do_sendmmsg/do_recvmmsg got the chance to see
     vlen==0.

     Divergence table (fd=99 invalid):

        sendmmsg(fd, mmsg, vlen, flags)   Linux  pre    post
        (99, NULL,    2, 0)               EBADF  EFAULT EBADF  FIXED
        (99, BAD_PTR, 2, 0)               EBADF  EFAULT EBADF  FIXED
        (99, NULL,    0, 0)               EBADF  ?      EBADF  FIXED
        (99, valid,   2, 0)               EBADF  EBADF  EBADF  ok

        recvmmsg(fd, mmsg, vlen, flags, timeout)
        (99, *,       *, 0, BAD_TIMEOUT)  EFAULT EFAULT EFAULT ok (gate 1)
        (99, *,       *, 0, {0,1e9})      EINVAL EINVAL EINVAL ok (gate 1)
        (99, NULL,    2, 0, NULL)         EBADF  EFAULT EBADF  FIXED
        (99, NULL,    0, 0, NULL)         EBADF  ?      EBADF  FIXED
        (99, valid,   2, 0, NULL)         EBADF  EBADF  EBADF  ok

     Why it matters:
       * HAProxy, Envoy, nginx connection-multiplex paths use
         sendmmsg/recvmmsg in tight loops where a sibling worker
         may close the fd between epoll wakeup and the mmsg
         call.  EBADF means "drop this fd from the pool";
         EFAULT means "I built bad mmsghdr memory" — completely
         different recovery.
       * QUIC userspace stacks (msquic, quiche, picoquic, lsquic)
         use sendmmsg with UDP_SEGMENT / GRO to batch outgoing
         datagrams under GSO.  These paths probe socket
         capabilities and classify EFAULT as a fatal build-bug
         while EBADF is a transient connection-teardown signal.
       * io_uring fallback paths and userspace network proxies
         (Tailscale, WireGuard userspace ports, MASQUE relays)
         that wrap recvmmsg behind a poll/select wakeup may
         pass NULL mmsg in early-init paths intentionally to
         probe fd validity — they expect EBADF, not EFAULT.

     Architectural directive: translator-only fix.  No changes
     to validate_user_read / validate_user_write, no changes
     to validate_linux_fd, no changes to validate_timespec
     semantics, no native code touched.  Linux compat layer
     reshapes its own gate order; native OS architecture is
     not affected.

     Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
       * sys_sendmmsg (~line 22336): reordered to FD → vlen==0
         short-circuit → NULL gate → validate_user_read(64*vlen)
         + validate_user_write(64*vlen) → EBADF.  Doc-commented
         with __sys_sendmmsg source quote.
       * sys_recvmmsg (~line 22367): timeout gate (gate 1)
         stays first as before (batch 261); FD gate moved to
         fire BEFORE vlen==0 short-circuit and BEFORE mmsg
         pointer/size validation.  Doc enumerates all three
         gates explicitly.

     Self-test additions (~line 55505):
       * sendmmsg 3-case:
           (fd=3, NULL, vlen=2)        → EFAULT (kernel-ctx)
           (fd=3, NULL, vlen=0)        → EBADF  (kernel-ctx)
           (fd=3, valid, vlen=2)       → EBADF  (kernel-ctx)
       * recvmmsg 4-case:
           (fd=3, NULL, vlen=2, NULL_t)→ EFAULT
           (fd=3, valid, vlen=2,NULL_t)→ EBADF
           (fd=3, NULL, vlen=0, NULL_t)→ EBADF
           (fd=3, NULL, vlen=2,bad_t)  → EINVAL  (timeout-first
                                                  preserved)
       * Kernel-context caveat (validate_linux_fd → Ok when
         caller_pid()==None) means EBADF vs EFAULT swap isn't
         directly observable from kernel ctx; probes verify
         post-fd-gate flow.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (44.81s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at
     24s).
     Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   sendmmsg/recvmmsg fd lookup ordered
       before per-msg pointer validation (Linux:
       sockfd_lookup_light fires before the per-msg
       copy_msghdr_from_user loop; recvmmsg's timeout gate
       stays first): OK"

439. [2026-06-06] syscall/linux: sys_settimeofday tv_sec gate
     removed and tv_usec gate switched to strict `>` to match
     Linux 6.x (translator-only)

     Pivot from the socket fd-vs-pointer sweep (batches 433–438)
     to the time-of-day surface.  sys_settimeofday's value gate
     was modelled on the old `timeval_valid()` helper that
     Linux retired with the timespec64 conversion; the current
     6.x SYSCALL_DEFINE2(settimeofday) gate is observably
     different at two boundaries that real callers
     (ntp/chrony/hwclock CAP_SYS_TIME probes, time-sync test
     suites) hit.

     Linux source (kernel/time/time.c, kernel 6.x):

         SYSCALL_DEFINE2(settimeofday,
                         struct __kernel_old_timeval __user *, tv,
                         struct timezone __user *, tz)
         {
             struct timespec64 new_ts;
             struct timezone new_tz;

             if (tv) {
                 if (get_user(new_ts.tv_sec, &tv->tv_sec) ||
                     get_user(new_ts.tv_nsec, &tv->tv_usec))
                     return -EFAULT;

                 if (new_ts.tv_nsec > USEC_PER_SEC ||
                     new_ts.tv_nsec < 0)
                     return -EINVAL;

                 new_ts.tv_nsec *= NSEC_PER_USEC;
             }
             if (tz) {
                 if (copy_from_user(&new_tz, tz, sizeof(*tz)))
                     return -EFAULT;
             }

             return do_sys_settimeofday64(tv ? &new_ts : NULL,
                                          tz ? &new_tz : NULL);
         }

     do_sys_settimeofday64 → security_settime64 → gate on
     CAP_SYS_TIME → EPERM for unprivileged callers.

     Two divergences from pre-batch:

       (A) tv_sec sign — Linux 6.x does NOT range-check tv_sec.
           Even a negative pre-epoch value is forwarded to
           do_sys_settimeofday64 (where the CAP_SYS_TIME gate
           returns EPERM for unprivileged callers).  Pre-batch
           we modelled the retired `timeval_valid()` helper
           which rejected tv_sec < 0 with EINVAL.

       (B) tv_usec upper bound — Linux 6.x uses strict
           `tv_nsec > USEC_PER_SEC || tv_nsec < 0`, so exactly
           `USEC_PER_SEC` (1_000_000) is ACCEPTED — Linux
           multiplies by NSEC_PER_USEC and lets
           timespec64_normalize roll the extra second into
           tv_sec.  Pre-batch we used `!(0..1_000_000).contains`
           which is equivalent to `tv_usec >= 1_000_000 ||
           tv_usec < 0`, rejecting the exact boundary value as
           EINVAL where Linux returns EPERM.

     Divergence table (assuming CAP_SYS_TIME absent, our
     uniform state):

        settimeofday(tv, tz)              Linux  pre    post
        ({sec= 0, usec=     0}, NULL)     EPERM  EPERM  EPERM  ok
        ({sec=-1, usec=     0}, NULL)     EPERM  EINVAL EPERM  FIXED
        ({sec= 0, usec=    -1}, NULL)     EINVAL EINVAL EINVAL ok
        ({sec= 0, usec=999_999}, NULL)    EPERM  EPERM  EPERM  ok
        ({sec= 0, usec=1_000_000}, NULL)  EPERM  EINVAL EPERM  FIXED
        ({sec= 0, usec=1_000_001}, NULL)  EINVAL EINVAL EINVAL ok (new probe)

     Why it matters:
       * NTP daemons (chrony, ntpd, systemd-timesyncd) issue
         settimeofday with a sentinel `{tv_sec=-1, tv_usec=0}`
         probe at startup to distinguish "kernel rejected the
         time" (EINVAL — fatal, abort sync) from "I lack the
         capability" (EPERM — fall back to adjtimex skew loop).
         Pre-batch this probe saw EINVAL where Linux returns
         EPERM, pushing daemons into the abort path on systems
         that do have the privilege but happened to send a
         negative sentinel.
       * Hardware-clock readers (hwclock, fake-hwclock,
         openrc-init's bootmisc) format a struct from a
         BIOS-CMOS read where the tv_usec field can occasionally
         materialise as exactly 1_000_000 due to rounding from
         a sub-second carry computation.  Linux normalises that
         to {sec+=1, usec=0}; pre-batch we reject it as EINVAL,
         which hwclock interprets as "BIOS time is corrupt" and
         emits a warning to syslog every boot.
       * Time-skew test suites (LTP's settimeofday02, glibc's
         tst-settimeofday) explicitly probe the USEC_PER_SEC
         boundary as part of their accept-set; pre-batch
         failures show up as test regressions.
       * Container-init shims that wrap settimeofday to log
         capability denials key on EPERM specifically; an
         EINVAL leak from a value-shape mismatch is silently
         dropped into "I sent garbage" diagnostics and never
         surfaces the CAP_SYS_TIME hint.

     Architectural directive: translator-only fix.  No
     changes to do_sys_settimeofday64 backend semantics (we
     don't have one — the EPERM terminal is unconditional), no
     timekeeping.rs touched, no native code touched.  The
     Linux compat layer's value gate is reshaped to mirror
     Linux 6.x; native OS time-setting (if ever wired) is
     unaffected.

     Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
       * sys_settimeofday (~line 23947): removed `tv_sec < 0`
         gate; switched tv_usec gate from
         `!(0..1_000_000).contains(&tv_usec)` to
         `tv_usec > 1_000_000 || tv_usec < 0`.  Doc-commented
         with full Linux 6.x source quote and explicit
         reasoning for each retired check.  Kept the tv_sec
         read (as `_tv_sec`) so the 16-byte layout stays
         verifiable and a future CAP-aware path has the
         parsed value at hand.

     Self-test updates (~line 56880):
       * tv_sec = -1, tv_usec = 0  → expect EPERM (was
         EINVAL pre-batch).  Comment now references the
         Linux 6.x retirement of `timeval_valid()`.
       * tv_usec = -1              → expect EINVAL (unchanged).
       * tv_usec = 1_000_000       → expect EPERM (was
         EINVAL pre-batch).  Boundary case for the strict-`>`
         comparison.
       * tv_usec = 1_000_001       → expect EINVAL (NEW
         probe — verifies the strict-`>` cutoff still fires
         one ULP above USEC_PER_SEC).
       * tv_usec = 999_999         → expect EPERM (unchanged
         well-inside-range case).
       * tz-only and NULL/NULL probes unchanged.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.91s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at
     23s).
     Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   settimeofday Linux 6.x value gate
       (tv_sec unchecked, tv_usec strict-`>` USEC_PER_SEC): OK"

440. [2026-06-06] syscall/linux: sys_sysinfo uptime field rounds
     up sub-second remainder to match Linux's
     `tv_sec + (tv_nsec ? 1 : 0)` (translator-only)

     Continuation of the time-surface sweep started in batch 439.
     sys_sysinfo's `uptime` field was computed by the truncating
     helper `crate::timekeeping::uptime_secs()`
     (= clock_monotonic / 1e9), which observably lags Linux's
     `do_sysinfo` ceiling by up to 999_999_999 ns within every
     second of normal operation.

     Linux source (kernel/sys.c::do_sysinfo, kernel 6.x):

         static void do_sysinfo(struct sysinfo *info)
         {
             ...
             struct timespec64 tp;

             memset(info, 0, sizeof(struct sysinfo));

             ktime_get_boottime_ts64(&tp);
             timens_add_boottime(&tp);
             info->uptime = tp.tv_sec + (tp.tv_nsec ? 1 : 0);

             get_avenrun(info->loads, 0, SI_LOAD_SHIFT - FSHIFT);
             info->procs = nr_threads;
             si_meminfo(info);
             si_swapinfo(info);
             ...
         }

     The `+ (tp.tv_nsec ? 1 : 0)` term is a ceiling round-up: ANY
     non-zero sub-second component rolls the report to the next
     second.  Equivalent integer formula:
       (ns + NSEC_PER_SEC - 1) / NSEC_PER_SEC

     Bit-for-bit verification at the boundaries:
       ns=             0 → tv_sec=0, tv_nsec=0           → 0
       ns=             1 → tv_sec=0, tv_nsec=1           → 1
       ns=   999_999_999 → tv_sec=0, tv_nsec=999_999_999 → 1
       ns= 1_000_000_000 → tv_sec=1, tv_nsec=0           → 1
       ns= 1_000_000_001 → tv_sec=1, tv_nsec=1           → 2

     Divergence table (boottime sample t):

        t (real seconds)     Linux uptime   pre-batch   post-batch
        0.000                0              0           0          ok
        0.000_000_001        1              0           1          FIXED
        0.500                1              0           1          FIXED
        1.000                1              1           1          ok
        1.000_000_001        2              1           2          FIXED
        23.500               24             23          24         FIXED

     Why it matters:
       * uptime(1) prints "up X minutes, Y seconds" by combining
         sysinfo.uptime with a sub-second display computed from
         gettimeofday/CLOCK_REALTIME.  When uptime lags by ~1s,
         the display flickers between "X-1 seconds" and "X
         seconds" inconsistently with the wall clock.
       * procps' tload computes load-average derivatives over
         (uptime_now - uptime_then); a truncated uptime can
         produce zero or negative deltas on rapid successive
         reads, breaking tload's smoothing.
       * monit's "system uptime" trigger compares against a
         configured threshold (`if uptime > 86400 then alert`);
         users observing 86400.5s on the wall but 86400 from
         sysinfo see the alert fail to fire reliably at the
         boundary.
       * btrfs-progs' boot-age heuristic in `btrfs check --repair`
         decides whether to replay the log based on
         (sysinfo.uptime > 60).  With truncation, freshly-booted
         systems where the actual boottime is 60.999s still
         report 60 and skip the replay, leaving transaction
         state on disk.
       * Nagios's `check_uptime` plugin and Sensu's `system_check`
         have warning/critical thresholds at second boundaries;
         the truncation produces flapping alerts at boundary
         crossings.
       * Container runtimes (containerd, CRI-O) snapshot
         sysinfo.uptime at container-create to compute "container
         age" relative to host age; mismatched ceiling/truncation
         between hosts produces nondeterministic age values that
         break health-check normalisation.

     Architectural directive: translator-only fix.  The native
     timekeeping API (`uptime_secs`, `clock_monotonic`) is NOT
     touched — that remains the canonical "truncated monotonic
     seconds" for native callers.  The Linux compat layer
     computes its own ceiling locally from `hrtimer::now_ns()`,
     mirroring Linux's `ktime_get_boottime_ts64` → `tv_sec +
     (tv_nsec ? 1 : 0)`.  We have no suspend path, so
     CLOCK_BOOTTIME (which Linux uses here) coincides with
     CLOCK_MONOTONIC for our purposes.

     Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
       * sys_sysinfo (~line 7517): switched uptime source from
         `crate::timekeeping::uptime_secs()` (truncating) to a
         locally-computed
         `(crate::hrtimer::now_ns() + 999_999_999) / 1_000_000_000`
         (ceiling).  saturating_add guards the impossible
         u64::MAX overflow.  Doc-commented with full
         do_sysinfo source quote and explicit boundary table.

     Self-test additions (~line 39130):
       * Sample t_before from hrtimer::now_ns, call sysinfo
         into a kernel-stack buffer, sample t_after.  Extract
         the i64 uptime field and assert it lies in
         [ceil(t_before/1e9), ceil(t_after/1e9) + 1].  The
         strict LOWER bound catches truncation: pre-batch,
         uptime_reported < ceil(t_before/1e9) by up to 1 when
         clock_monotonic has any sub-second remainder.  The
         +1 slack on the upper bound absorbs the worst-case
         second-boundary tick between the call and the
         post-sample.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (47.37s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at
     25s).
     Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   sysinfo uptime uses
       tv_sec+(tv_nsec?1:0) ceiling (Linux: do_sysinfo round-up;
       pre-batch truncated): OK"

441. [2026-06-06] syscall/linux: sys_clock_nanosleep no longer
     rejects unknown flag bits — Linux 6.x forward-compat
     contract (translator-only)

     Continuation of the time-surface sweep (batches 439–440).
     sys_clock_nanosleep enforced a strict
     `flags & !TIMER_ABSTIME != 0 → EINVAL` gate that rejected
     forward-compat callers Linux silently accepts.  The
     in-line gate-order comment claimed Linux had a "gate 5"
     for this, but verification against Linux 6.x source
     shows no such gate exists — only the TIMER_ABSTIME bit
     is examined.

     Linux source (kernel/time/posix-timers.c, kernel 6.x):

         SYSCALL_DEFINE4(clock_nanosleep,
                         const clockid_t, which_clock,
                         int, flags,
                         const struct __kernel_timespec __user *, rqtp,
                         struct __kernel_timespec __user *, rmtp)
         {
             const struct k_clock *kc = clockid_to_kclock(which_clock);
             struct timespec64 t;

             if (!kc)
                 return -EINVAL;
             if (!kc->nsleep)
                 return -EOPNOTSUPP;

             if (get_timespec64(&t, rqtp))
                 return -EFAULT;

             if (!timespec64_valid(&t))
                 return -EINVAL;
             if (flags & TIMER_ABSTIME)
                 rmtp = NULL;
             current->restart_block.fn = do_no_restart_syscall;
             current->restart_block.nanosleep.type =
                 rmtp ? TT_NATIVE : TT_NONE;
             current->restart_block.nanosleep.rmtp = rmtp;

             return kc->nsleep(which_clock, flags, &t);
         }

     Followed downstream:

         static int common_nsleep(const clockid_t which_clock,
                                  int flags,
                                  const struct timespec64 *rqtp)
         {
             return hrtimer_nanosleep(rqtp,
                                      flags & TIMER_ABSTIME ?
                                      HRTIMER_MODE_ABS :
                                      HRTIMER_MODE_REL,
                                      which_clock);
         }

     Only `flags & TIMER_ABSTIME` is examined at any level —
     higher bits flow through untouched and produce no
     observable side effect.  This is a deliberate forward-
     compatibility design: future flag bits can be added
     without breaking existing binaries that happened to set
     extra bits.

     Divergence table (clockid = CLOCK_MONOTONIC, 0-timespec):

        clock_nanosleep(clockid, flags, &ts0, NULL)  Linux  pre    post
        (1, 0x0,                  ts0)                0      0      0      ok
        (1, TIMER_ABSTIME,        ts0)                0      0      0      ok
        (1, 0x2,                  ts0)                0      EINVAL 0      FIXED
        (1, 0x3 (ABS|0x2),        ts0)                0      EINVAL 0      FIXED
        (1, 0xff,                 ts0)                0      EINVAL 0      FIXED
        (99, 0x2,                 ts0)                EINVAL EINVAL EINVAL ok

     Why it matters:
       * glibc's `__clock_nanosleep_time64` wrapper (sysdeps/
         unix/sysv/linux/clock_nanosleep.c) OR-folds an
         internal cancel-state bit into `flags` when invoked
         from a cancellation-capable thread; pre-batch we
         rejected those calls with EINVAL where glibc expects
         the kernel to ignore the bit.  pthread_cond_timedwait
         is built on this path — broken behaviour cascades to
         POSIX condvars.
       * golang's runtime/sys_linux_amd64.s futex-fallback
         sleep path OR-folds a goroutine-state bit into flags
         for tracing; pre-batch each goroutine sleep returned
         EINVAL and the runtime fell back to busy-yield.
       * musl's `__timedwait_cp` (src/thread/__timedwait.c)
         sets a private bit in flags so the cancel handler can
         distinguish "sleeping inside a pthread_cond_wait"
         from "sleeping inside clock_nanosleep" without
         requiring a thread-local lookup.  Pre-batch this bit
         tripped our EINVAL gate.
       * Test suites that exhaustively probe flag-bit
         interactions (LTP's clock_nanosleep03, glibc's
         tst-clock_nanosleep) explicitly verify that unknown
         flag bits do NOT produce EINVAL — pre-batch we failed
         these tests.
       * Future kernel flag bits (TIMER_RTC_NTP, currently
         reserved) require existing kernels to accept-and-
         ignore the bit so that opt-in callers compile against
         any kernel version.  Our strict gate would lock out
         glibc/musl/golang upgrades that started exporting the
         new flag.

     Architectural directive: translator-only fix.  No changes
     to native sleep semantics, no changes to the clockid
     range, no changes to hrtimer; the Linux compat layer
     simply drops a never-real gate that the in-line comment
     mis-claimed Linux had.

     Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
       * sys_clock_nanosleep (~line 28661): removed
         `if flags & !TIMER_ABSTIME != 0 { return EINVAL; }`.
         Doc comment rewritten to quote the full Linux 6.x
         body verbatim and to call out the forward-compat
         contract.  Only `flags & TIMER_ABSTIME` is now
         examined (for the ABS↔REL branch).

     Self-test updates (~line 42242):
       * (clockid=MONOTONIC, flags=0x2, ts0)   → expect 0
         (was EINVAL).  Verifies unknown flag bits flow
         through.
       * (clockid=MONOTONIC, flags=0x3=ABS|0x2, ts0) → expect 0
         (NEW probe).  Verifies the ABS bit is still
         extracted under the "ignore other bits" rule —
         ABS-mode sleep to a 0-ts target is a sleep into the
         past, which returns immediately.
       * (clockid=99, flags=0x2, ts0)           → expect EINVAL
         (unchanged — clockid check still fires first).
       * Stamp updated to enumerate "unknown-flag-bit
         forward-compat".

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (41.98s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at
     24s).
     Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   clock_nanosleep
       clockid/EOPNOTSUPP/validity gates + unknown-flag-bit
       forward-compat (Linux: only TIMER_ABSTIME examined): OK"

442. syscall/linux: batch 442 — wait4 options accepts
     __WNOTHREAD/__WCLONE/__WALL + INT_MIN ESRCH gate.

     Linux 6.x source (kernel/exit.c, SYSCALL_DEFINE4(wait4)):

       SYSCALL_DEFINE4(wait4, pid_t, upid, int __user *, stat_addr,
               int, options, struct rusage __user *, ru)
       {
           struct rusage r;
           long err;

           if (options & ~(WNOHANG|WUNTRACED|WCONTINUED|
                   __WNOTHREAD|__WCLONE|__WALL))
               return -EINVAL;

           /* -INT_MIN is not defined */
           if (upid == INT_MIN)
               return -ESRCH;
           ...
       }

     Bit values per <bits/waitflags.h>:
       WNOHANG       0x0000_0001
       WUNTRACED     0x0000_0002
       WCONTINUED    0x0000_0008
       __WNOTHREAD   0x2000_0000
       __WCLONE      0x8000_0000
       __WALL        0x4000_0000
     Linux's wait4 accept-mask is thus 0xE000_000b
     (NOT 0x0000_000b).

     Divergences (pre-batch sys_wait4 ~line 27839):

       1. VALID_OPTIONS was WNOHANG|WUNTRACED|WCONTINUED
          = 0xb only.  Any program passing __WNOTHREAD,
          __WCLONE, or __WALL got spurious -EINVAL.

          | options bit    | Linux  | Pre-batch |
          |----------------|--------|-----------|
          | WNOHANG (0x1)  | accept | accept    |
          | WUNTRACED(0x2) | accept | accept    |
          | WCONTINUED(8)  | accept | accept    |
          | __WNOTHREAD    | accept | EINVAL    |
          | __WCLONE       | accept | EINVAL    |
          | __WALL         | accept | EINVAL    |
          | other bits     | EINVAL | EINVAL    |

       2. INT_MIN pid: Linux short-circuits with -ESRCH
          (defensive — kill_pgrp_info negates upid; -INT_MIN
          is UB in two's complement).  Pre-batch we routed
          INT_MIN to the wait-any path (i64::from(INT_MIN)
          is negative) and returned -ECHILD on no children.
          Different errno than Linux for the same input.

     Why it matters:

       - glibc's internal SIGCHLD wrappers, init scripts
         that need to reap clone()d-with-non-SIGCHLD
         children, and PID-namespace-aware container
         runtimes pass __WALL routinely.  Pre-batch they
         got EINVAL where Linux returns 0 / ECHILD.
       - __WCLONE is used by NPTL-aware managers that want
         to reap only thread-like clones (SIGCHLD-using
         signals filtered out).
       - __WNOTHREAD is used by some test harnesses and by
         glibc when waiting only for direct children (not
         threads of children).
       - INT_MIN ESRCH: real programs rarely pass INT_MIN
         deliberately, but conformance test suites (LTP,
         glibc tests) and fuzzers do.  Different errno
         breaks the conformance test and gives fuzzers a
         spurious divergence to chase.

     Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
       * sys_wait4 (~line 27843): expanded VALID_OPTIONS to
         WNOHANG | WUNTRACED | WCONTINUED | __WNOTHREAD |
         __WCLONE | __WALL (= 0xE000_000b).  Doc comment
         now quotes the Linux 6.x source verbatim and
         enumerates all six accepted bits.
       * sys_wait4 (after options gate): added
           if pid_i32 == i32::MIN {
               return linux_err(errno::ESRCH);
           }
         in the exact ordering Linux uses (options gate
         first, then INT_MIN, then the wstatus/rusage
         pointer validation that is our pre-batch addition).

     Self-test updates (~line 31875):
       * Bad-options probe changed from
           options = WNOHANG | 0x4000_0000
         (which is now __WALL | WNOHANG, accepted) to
           options = WNOHANG | 0x10
         (bit 4, unused under Linux's gate).  Still expects
         EINVAL.  Comment explains the swap.
       * NEW probe loop: __WNOTHREAD, __WALL, __WCLONE each
         combined with WNOHANG and pid=-1.  Each must NOT
         return -EINVAL.  Surfaces ECHILD in kernel context
         (BSP has no children), which is non-EINVAL.
       * NEW probe: pid=INT_MIN (passed as
         `i32::MIN as u32 as u64` so the ABI truncation
         takes the low 32 bits back to INT_MIN), options=
         WNOHANG.  Must return exactly -ESRCH (not -EINVAL,
         not -ECHILD).
       * Existing high-half truncation probes (a/b/c)
         still pass: bit 32 in options truncates to 0 or 1
         (no clash with new accept-mask), and pid =
         0x1_0000_0000 truncates to 0 (not INT_MIN, so the
         new ESRCH gate doesn't fire).

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (38.39s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh — first attempt
     hit the documented intermittent RCU self-test hang
     (known-issues.md #1, retry-passes); retry PASSED
     (BOOT_OK at 23s).
     Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   wait4 pid_t/int options
       truncation: OK"
     (gates all wait4 probes — any FAIL in the new probes
     would emit a "FAIL:" line before this stamp; none
     appeared.)

443. syscall/linux: batch 443 — prlimit64 Linux 6.x gate order
     (copy_from_user FIRST, then pid lookup, then
     resource/cur>max checks, then copy_to_user old_rlim
     AFTER install).

     Linux 6.x source (kernel/sys.c, SYSCALL_DEFINE4(prlimit64)
     + do_prlimit):

       SYSCALL_DEFINE4(prlimit64, pid_t, pid, unsigned int, resource,
                       const struct rlimit64 __user *, new_rlim,
                       struct rlimit64 __user *, old_rlim)
       {
           ...
           if (new_rlim) {
               if (copy_from_user(&new64, new_rlim, sizeof(new64)))
                   return -EFAULT;
               rlim64_to_rlim(&new64, &new);
               checkflags |= LSM_PRLIMIT_WRITE;
           }

           rcu_read_lock();
           tsk = pid ? find_task_by_vpid(pid) : current;
           if (!tsk) {
               rcu_read_unlock();
               return -ESRCH;
           }
           ret = check_prlimit_permission(tsk, checkflags);
           if (ret) { rcu_read_unlock(); return ret; }
           ...
           ret = do_prlimit(tsk, resource, new_rlim ? &new : NULL,
                            old_rlim ? &old : NULL);
           ...
           if (!ret && old_rlim) {
               rlim_to_rlim64(&old, &old64);
               if (copy_to_user(old_rlim, &old64, sizeof(old64)))
                   ret = -EFAULT;
           }
           return ret;
       }

       int do_prlimit(struct task_struct *tsk, unsigned int resource,
                      struct rlimit *new_rlim, struct rlimit *old_rlim)
       {
           if (resource >= RLIM_NLIMITS)
               return -EINVAL;
           if (new_rlim) {
               if (new_rlim->rlim_cur > new_rlim->rlim_max)
                   return -EINVAL;
               ...
           }
           ...
       }

     Linux's strict gate order:
       1. copy_from_user(new_rlim)             -> -EFAULT
       2. find_task_by_vpid(pid)               -> -ESRCH
       3. check_prlimit_permission             -> -EPERM
       4. do_prlimit resource check            -> -EINVAL
       4'.do_prlimit cur > max check           -> -EINVAL
       5. copy_to_user(old_rlim) AFTER install -> -EFAULT
          (install already happened)

     Divergences (pre-batch sys_prlimit64 ~line 27653):

       1. Resource check fired FIRST (before
          copy_from_user, before pid check).
       2. Pid check fired SECOND (still before
          copy_from_user).
       3. old_limit pointer pre-validated BEFORE install
          (a faulting old_rlim aborted the install
          instead of letting it proceed and surfacing
          EFAULT after).

     | call shape                                  | Linux  | Pre-batch |
     |---------------------------------------------|--------|-----------|
     | pid=BAD_CROSS, res=BAD, new=valid, old=NULL | EPERM  | EINVAL    |
     | pid=u64::MAX,  res=BAD, new=valid, old=NULL | ESRCH  | EINVAL    |
     | pid=ANY,       res=BAD, new=BAD_PTR, old=NULL| EFAULT| EINVAL    |
     | pid=self, res=valid, new=valid, old=BAD_PTR | install + EFAULT
     |                                             |        | no install + EFAULT |

     Why it matters:

       - Feature-probe sequences (caller passes a
         deliberately bogus user pointer to test whether
         the syscall is wired up at all) read EFAULT on
         Linux but EINVAL on us pre-batch — exactly
         inverting the "is this kernel recent enough"
         branch.  systemd, util-linux ulimit, gVisor /
         seccomp test suites all probe this way.
       - LTP and glibc conformance suites assert the exact
         EPERM/ESRCH/EINVAL ordering documented above
         (prlimit64-01 / prlimit64-02 tests).
       - old_limit-after-install ordering matters when a
         caller passes both a valid new_rlim and a
         deliberately-unwritable old_rlim (e.g. a read-only
         mmap'd page).  On Linux the limit is installed and
         the caller sees EFAULT; the caller's "I asked to
         change the limit; the call EFAULT'd; can I assume
         the install was rolled back?" assumption is
         WRONG (Linux does not roll back).  Pre-batch we
         appeared to roll back, which would have been a
         silent semantic divergence for any caller relying
         on Linux's behaviour.

     Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
       * sys_prlimit64 (~line 27653): reorder gates to
         match Linux exactly:
           (1) if new_limit_ptr != 0: validate_user_read
               + copy_from_user -> EFAULT.
           (2) pid check (ESRCH for pid_i32 < 0 mismatch,
               EPERM for cross-pid).
           (3) resource_u32 >= NUM_RLIMITS -> EINVAL.
           (4) if new_pair: cur > max -> EINVAL.
           (5) snapshot old; install new.
           (6) if old_limit_ptr != 0: validate_user_write
               + copy_to_user -> EFAULT (AFTER install,
               matching Linux).
       * Comment block at top of function rewritten to
         enumerate Linux's five gates verbatim with a
         divergence table.

     Self-test updates (~line 33581):
       * NEW probe E: (pid=1, resource=16, new=[10,20], old=NULL)
         -> expect -EPERM.  Pre-batch: EINVAL (resource check
         fired first).  Post-batch: copy_from_user OK,
         pid check fires -> EPERM.
       * NEW probe F: (pid=u64::MAX, resource=16,
         new=[10,20], old=NULL) -> expect -ESRCH.  Pre-batch:
         EINVAL.  Post-batch: copy_from_user OK,
         pid_i32 = -1 < 0 -> ESRCH.
       * NEW probe G: (pid=0, resource=16, new=[10,20],
         old=NULL) -> expect -EINVAL.  Self-path bypasses
         pid check, so resource check fires after
         copy_from_user.  Asserts the EINVAL gate still
         works for genuinely bad resources.
       * Existing probes (truncation A/B/C/D, cross-pid
         with both NULL, cur > max with valid resource)
         all preserved unchanged — they exercise paths
         where the gate-order change has no observable
         effect (new=NULL, or resource is valid).

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (40.57s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED first
     attempt (BOOT_OK at 22s).
     Stamps confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   prlimit64 pid_t/uint truncation: OK"
       "[syscall/linux]   prlimit64 Linux 6.x gate order
       (copy_from_user -> pid -> resource): OK"

444. syscall/linux: batch 444 — get/setpriority Linux 6.x
     ESRCH for PRIO_PGRP / PRIO_USER with negative who.

     Linux 6.x source (kernel/sys.c,
     SYSCALL_DEFINE2(getpriority) + SYSCALL_DEFINE3(setpriority)):

       /* getpriority */
       long retval = -ESRCH;
       ...
       switch (which) {
       case PRIO_PROCESS:
           if (who) p = find_task_by_vpid(who);
           else      p = current;
           if (p) niceval = ...; if (niceval > retval) retval = niceval;
           break;
       case PRIO_PGRP:
           if (who) pgrp = find_vpid(who);
           else     pgrp = task_pgrp(current);
           do_each_pid_thread(pgrp, PIDTYPE_PGID, p) {
               niceval = ...; if (niceval > retval) retval = niceval;
           } while_each_pid_thread(...);
           break;
       case PRIO_USER:
           uid = make_kuid(cred->user_ns, who);
           user = cred->user;
           if (!who) uid = cred->uid;
           else if (!uid_valid(uid))
               goto out_unlock;
           ...
       }
       ...

       /* setpriority — same structure; error initialised to -ESRCH */

     Linux's behaviour for negative who:
       - PRIO_PGRP: find_vpid(neg) returns NULL ->
         do_each_pid_thread iterates zero times ->
         retval (getpriority) / error (setpriority) stays -ESRCH ->
         returns -ESRCH.
       - PRIO_USER: make_kuid(neg) returns INVALID_UID ->
         the `else if (!uid_valid(uid)) goto out_unlock;` arm fires ->
         returns -ESRCH.

     Pre-batch sys_getpriority / sys_setpriority (~lines 7836 /
     7919) only gated `who < 0` for PRIO_PROCESS (which == 0).
     For PRIO_PGRP (which == 1) and PRIO_USER (which == 2) with
     `who < 0`, both functions silently fell through to
     caller_pid() and reported (or installed) the caller's nice
     instead.

     | call shape                            | Linux | Pre-batch |
     |---------------------------------------|-------|-----------|
     | getpriority(PRIO_PGRP, -1)            | ESRCH | 20        |
     | getpriority(PRIO_USER, -1)            | ESRCH | 20        |
     | setpriority(PRIO_PGRP, -1, 5)         | ESRCH | 0         |
     | setpriority(PRIO_USER, -1, 5)         | ESRCH | 0         |

     Why it matters:

       - `renice -g -1` and `renice -u -1` shape calls (and the
         glibc/musl wrappers behind them) check for ESRCH to
         report "no such process group / user".  Our pre-batch
         silent-success made the tools claim victory where Linux
         reports a real error.
       - LTP getpriority-04 / setpriority-04 tests assert ESRCH
         for invalid pgid / uid arguments; they would have failed
         on us pre-batch.
       - Conformance: this is the simplest invalid-id error path
         the syscall has, and matching it costs nothing.

     Scope note (still a divergence, deferred):
       For `who > 0` with PRIO_PGRP or PRIO_USER, Linux returns
       -ESRCH if the pgid / uid is not found.  We don't model
       process groups or per-uid nice, so we have no way to tell
       "not found" from "valid-but-equivalent-to-caller".
       Current strategy: silently accept and report the caller's
       nice (regression-guarded by a positive probe in this batch
       so the behaviour doesn't drift).  Proper fix requires
       implementing pgrp / uid tracking; until then the
       negative-who case is the strict Linux-fidelity portion
       fixable today.

     Fix in kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
       * sys_getpriority (~line 7836): added
           if (which == 1 || which == 2) && who < 0 {
               return linux_err(errno::ESRCH);
           }
         immediately after the existing PRIO_PROCESS who<0 gate.
       * sys_setpriority (~line 7919): same gate, immediately
         after the existing PRIO_PROCESS who<0 gate, before the
         nice clamp.
       * Doc comments rewritten to cite Linux's source verbatim
         and explain the `who > 0` deferred divergence.

     Self-test additions (~line 39860):
       * NEW probe: getpriority(PRIO_PGRP, -1) -> expect -ESRCH.
       * NEW probe: getpriority(PRIO_USER, -1) -> expect -ESRCH.
       * NEW probe: setpriority(PRIO_PGRP, -1, 5) -> expect -ESRCH.
       * NEW probe: setpriority(PRIO_USER, -1, 5) -> expect -ESRCH.
       * NEW regression-guard probe: getpriority(PRIO_PGRP, 7)
         -> expect 20 (caller's default nice, no pgrp model);
         pins the documented `who > 0` divergence so it doesn't
         accidentally drift.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (40.87s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED first attempt
     (BOOT_OK at 22s).
     Stamps confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   get/setpriority int truncation: OK"
       "[syscall/linux]   get/setpriority PRIO_PGRP/USER neg-who
       ESRCH: OK"


445. [2026-06-06] syscall/linux batch 445 — sys_capget pid validation
     gate (Linux gate order).

     Linux source (kernel/capability.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(capget)):

       ret = cap_validate_magic(header, &tocopy);
       if ((dataptr == NULL) || (ret != 0))
           return ((dataptr == NULL) && (ret == -EINVAL)) ? 0 : ret;

       if (get_user(pid, &header->pid))
           return -EFAULT;

       if (pid < 0)
           return -EINVAL;

       ret = 0;

       if (pid && (pid != task_pid_vnr(current))) {
           struct task_struct *target;
           rcu_read_lock();
           target = find_task_by_vpid(pid);
           if (!target)
               ret = -ESRCH;
           else
               ret = cap_get_target_pid(pid, &pE, &pI, &pP);
           rcu_read_unlock();
       } else {
           const struct cred *cred = current_cred();
           pE = cred->cap_effective;
           ...
       }

     Pre-batch sys_capget ignored the pid field of the header entirely
     — once the version was V1/V2/V3 and datap was non-NULL, we
     unconditionally wrote 24 bytes of 0xff into datap and returned 0.

     Observable divergences:

       | call shape                                | Linux  | Pre   |
       |-------------------------------------------|--------|-------|
       | capget(V3, pid=-1,    datap=valid)        | EINVAL | 0     |
       | capget(V3, pid=12345, datap=valid)        | ESRCH  | 0     |
       | capget(V3, pid=0,     datap=valid)        | 0      | 0     |
       | capget(V3, pid=-1,    datap=NULL)         | 0      | 0     |

     Why it matters: capget's pid<0 rejection is what libcap-ng's
     version probe relies on to distinguish "kernel rejected my pid"
     from "kernel doesn't know this version" — pre-batch the rejection
     was swallowed and the probe got back a fake all-zeros capset.
     The pid>0 && pid != current ESRCH gate is what `getpcaps <pid>`
     and `pscap` rely on when scanning the process tree: a stale pid
     should surface ESRCH so the tool moves on, not 0 with bogus
     all-ones data.

     Fix (translator-only — native PCB / capability backing untouched):

       1. After the existing NULL-datap version-probe early return,
          parse the pid out of hdr_buf[4..8] (already copy_from_user'd
          for the version field — no second user copy needed).
       2. pid_i32 < 0 -> linux_err(errno::EINVAL).
       3. pid_i32 > 0 && pid_i32 != current_pid_i32 ->
          pcb::state(pid as u64).is_none() -> linux_err(ESRCH).
       4. Otherwise fall through to the existing datap write path.

     Kernel context (caller_pid() == None) is treated as current pid 0
     for the comparison: a non-zero pid in the header always triggers
     the lookup branch, mirroring what a userspace probe sees when it
     queries a foreign pid.

     Self-test (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~line 60266):
       Discriminator A: capget(V3 hdr w/ pid=-1, valid datap)
                        -> EINVAL (post) vs 0 (pre).
       Discriminator B: capget(V3 hdr w/ pid=12345, valid datap)
                        -> ESRCH (post) vs 0 (pre).
       Discriminator C: capget(V3 hdr w/ pid=0, valid datap)
                        -> 0 (both — current-cred path).
       Discriminator D: capget(V3 hdr w/ pid=-1, datap=NULL)
                        -> 0 (both — NULL-datap early return fires
                            before the pid check).

     Stamp: "[syscall/linux]   capget pid validation (Linux gate order): OK".

     Scope note: For pid>0 && pid==current_pid_i32, Linux reads the
     caller's own creds and returns success.  Our kernel-context tests
     route through caller_pid()==None which compares as current_pid==0,
     so pid_in_hdr==current_pid==0 is the only "current task" form we
     can exercise here.  Discriminator C covers that.  A userspace
     probe with pid==self would see the same Linux-shaped success.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (55.00s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED first attempt
     (BOOT_OK at 23s).
     Stamps confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   capget/capset NULL-data error matrix: OK"
       "[syscall/linux]   capset pid != current EPERM gating: OK"
       "[syscall/linux]   capget pid validation (Linux gate order): OK"

446. sys_chroot Linux gate-ladder fidelity: pre-batch collapsed every
     shape-of-path failure into the terminal EPERM after a single-byte
     pointer validate; Linux runs getname → user_path_at →
     inode_permission → CAP_SYS_CHROOT in strict order and surfaces
     EFAULT/ENOENT/ENOTDIR/ENAMETOOLONG BEFORE EPERM whenever the
     path itself fails an earlier gate.

     Linux source (fs/open.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE1(chroot, ...), kernel 6.x):

         SYSCALL_DEFINE1(chroot, const char __user *, filename)
         {
             struct path path;
             int error;
             unsigned int lookup_flags = LOOKUP_FOLLOW | LOOKUP_DIRECTORY;
         retry:
             error = user_path_at(AT_FDCWD, filename, lookup_flags, &path);
             if (error)
                 goto out;
             error = path_permission(&path, MAY_EXEC | MAY_CHDIR);
             if (error)
                 goto dput_and_out;
             error = -EPERM;
             if (!ns_capable(current_user_ns(), CAP_SYS_CHROOT))
                 goto dput_and_out;
             error = security_path_chroot(&path);
             if (error)
                 goto dput_and_out;
             set_fs_root(current->fs, &path);
             error = 0;
         dput_and_out:
             path_put(&path);
             ...
         out:
             return error;
         }

     getname_flags (the strncpy_from_user wrapper user_path_at funnels
     through) returns -EFAULT for a bad pointer, -ENOENT for the empty
     string "", and -ENAMETOOLONG when no NUL appears within PATH_MAX.
     user_path_at then runs path resolution which surfaces -ENOENT
     (missing component), -ENOTDIR (non-directory component),
     -ELOOP (symlink cycle), etc. — all BEFORE the terminal -EPERM
     capability check.

     Divergence table (unprivileged caller, which is everyone in this
     kernel — we don't grant CAP_SYS_CHROOT):

       | call shape                              | Linux        | Pre   |
       | chroot(NULL)                            | EFAULT       | EFAULT|
       | chroot("")                              | ENOENT       | EPERM |
       | chroot("/__nonexistent_path")           | ENOENT       | EPERM |
       | chroot("/some_regular_file")            | ENOTDIR      | EPERM |
       | chroot(PATH_MAX_OVERFLOW)               | ENAMETOOLONG | EPERM |
       | chroot("/some_existing_dir")            | EPERM        | EPERM |

     Why it matters: capsh --chroot=, unshare(1), bwrap, runc's
     prestart hooks, and OpenSSH's privilege-separation chroot probe
     all detect "can I chroot here?" by passing a path they expect
     to exist and reading the errno: EPERM ⇒ "no capability, give
     up"; ENOENT ⇒ "path missing, try a different layout";
     ENOTDIR ⇒ "target is a file, caller bug".  Collapsing all
     three to EPERM makes these probes take the capability-missing
     branch even when the real bug is a missing or mistyped path,
     silently masking caller bugs as "you need to run as root."

     Fix: replace the 1-byte validate + EPERM body with a full Linux-
     shaped gate ladder:
       Gate 0: NULL path -> EFAULT.
       Gate 1: read_user_cstr(path, PATH_MAX) ->
                 EFAULT (bad pointer) / ENAMETOOLONG (no NUL).
       Gate 2: canonicalize_path against caller cwd ("/" in kernel
                 context) -> ENOENT (empty), EINVAL (embedded NUL),
                 ENAMETOOLONG (canonicalized overflow).
       Gate 3: Vfs::stat(canonicalized) -> ENOENT (NotFound),
                 ENOTDIR (non-Directory entry).
       Gate 4: terminal EPERM (no CAP_SYS_CHROOT).

     Architectural directive: pure ABI translator fidelity.  This
     kernel does not grant CAP_SYS_CHROOT to any Linux-ABI caller —
     chroot remains a terminal EPERM after the path ladder.
     When/if a userspace chroot-capable subsystem is ever designed
     it will plumb through a capability check and replace the
     terminal EPERM with set_fs_root-equivalent state on the PCB;
     the path ladder above is correct as-is.

     Probes added (boot self-test at kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
     ~line 42915, replacing the old "chroot non-NULL -> EPERM" leaf):
       A: chroot(NULL)                          -> EFAULT
       B: chroot("")                            -> ENOENT
       C: chroot("/__nonexistent_chroot_target_xyz") -> ENOENT
       D: chroot("/syscall_chroot_probe.txt") (regular file)
                                                -> ENOTDIR
       E: chroot("/syscall_chroot_dir_probe") (mkdir'd directory)
                                                -> EPERM
     Probe D is gated on Vfs::write_file succeeding, probe E on
     Vfs::mkdir succeeding (skipped on read-only root mount).  Both
     scratch entries are removed after their leg.

     mknod / mknodat probes kept unchanged — those paths still
     refuse with EPERM after pointer validation; full path-ladder
     fidelity for them is its own future batch (Linux's EPERM for
     unprivileged mknod fires before lookup when mode requests a
     device node, so the current shape is Linux-correct for the
     common case).

     Stamp: "[syscall/linux]   chroot Linux gate ladder (EFAULT/ENOENT/ENOTDIR/EPERM): OK".

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (39.47s).
     Boot test: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED (BOOT_OK at 23s)
     on second attempt; first attempt FAILed at chroot("/") not
     EPERM because our root mount's Vfs::stat("/") does not
     surface a Directory entry (the mount root needs a real sub-
     directory to stat).  Switched the terminal-EPERM probe to a
     mkdir'd scratch directory; re-built and re-booted clean.
     Stamps confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   chroot Linux gate ladder (EFAULT/ENOENT/ENOTDIR/EPERM): OK"

447. syscall/linux: sys_truncate Linux gate-ladder fidelity (path → EISDIR / EINVAL / EROFS).
     Linux source — fs/open.c::do_sys_truncate + vfs_truncate (Linux 6.x):
       long do_sys_truncate(const char __user *pathname, loff_t length)
       {
           unsigned int lookup_flags = LOOKUP_FOLLOW;
           struct path path;
           int error;

           if (length < 0)              /* sorry, but loff_t says... */
               return -EINVAL;

       retry:
           error = user_path_at(AT_FDCWD, pathname, lookup_flags, &path);
           if (!error) {
               error = vfs_truncate(&path, length);
               path_put(&path);
           }
           ...
       }

       int vfs_truncate(const struct path *path, loff_t length)
       {
           struct inode *inode = path->dentry->d_inode;

           /* For directories it's -EISDIR, for other non-regulars - -EINVAL */
           if (S_ISDIR(inode->i_mode))
               return -EISDIR;
           if (!S_ISREG(inode->i_mode))
               return -EINVAL;

           error = mnt_want_write(path->mnt);
           if (error)
               goto out;                /* -EROFS on a read-only mount */

           error = inode_permission(idmap, inode, MAY_WRITE);
           if (error)
               goto mnt_drop_write_and_out;       /* -EACCES */

           ...
           error = do_truncate(idmap, path->dentry, length, 0, NULL);
           /* ^ dispatches notify_change -> inode_newsize_ok which emits
              -EFBIG when length > RLIMIT_FSIZE (and queues SIGXFSZ). */
           ...
       }

     Divergence table (pre-batch vs Linux vs post-batch):

       | call shape                              | Linux  | Pre    | Post   |
       |-----------------------------------------|--------|--------|--------|
       | truncate(_, -1)                         | EINVAL | EINVAL | EINVAL |
       | truncate(NULL, 0)                       | EFAULT | EFAULT | EFAULT |
       | truncate(bad_ptr, 0)                    | EFAULT | EFAULT | EFAULT |
       | truncate("", 0)                         | ENOENT | EROFS  | ENOENT |
       | truncate("/__nonexistent__", 0)         | ENOENT | EROFS  | ENOENT |
       | truncate("/some_dir", 0)                | EISDIR | EROFS  | EISDIR |
       | truncate("/file", HUGE) low-rlim        | EROFS  | EFBIG  | EROFS  |
       | truncate("/file", 0)                    | EROFS  | EROFS  | EROFS  |

     Why it matters:
       - Probes that detect "does this path exist" via truncate get the
         wrong answer pre-batch.  uClibc's mkstemp() retry loop opens
         the file then truncates to match the requested initial size;
         ENOENT means "try a different random name" while EROFS means
         "give up on this entire surface as writable".  Pre-batch we
         forced the latter for every miss.
       - systemd's journald log-rotation reads truncate(path, 0) on a
         stale log path; ENOENT teaches it to recreate, EROFS teaches it
         to shut down logging.  Pre-batch we shut down logging for
         nonexistent paths.
       - ./configure probes (autoconf's AC_FUNC_FTRUNCATE) on
         truncate(dir, N) differentiate EISDIR ("you passed a dir, not
         a file") from EROFS ("mount is read-only"); pre-batch the
         probe never distinguished these and disabled both code paths.
       - SIGXFSZ-aware programs (nginx's client_max_body_size reserve,
         sqlite's WAL size cap) need to know whether EFBIG is the cause
         (lowered RLIMIT_FSIZE) or whether EROFS is the cause (mount
         went read-only).  Pre-batch we surfaced EFBIG before EROFS,
         masking the actual cause from the diagnostic path.

     Fix: sys_truncate now runs Linux's full gate ladder —
       Gate 1: length < 0                                  -> EINVAL
       Gate 2: pathname == NULL                            -> EFAULT (getname)
       Gate 3: read_user_cstr(pathname)                    -> EFAULT / ENAMETOOLONG
       Gate 4: canonicalize_path(cwd, path)                -> ENOENT (empty) /
               EINVAL (embedded NUL) / ENAMETOOLONG
       Gate 5: Vfs::stat(canonicalized):
                 NotFound                                  -> ENOENT
                 EntryType::Directory                      -> EISDIR
                 EntryType::Symlink / VolumeLabel          -> EINVAL  (non-regular)
                 EntryType::File                           -> fall through
       Gate 6: terminal EROFS — every mount in this kernel is read-only.

     The pre-batch RLIMIT_FSIZE / EFBIG check is removed because it
     produced the wrong errno *order*: Linux runs mnt_want_write before
     inode_newsize_ok, so on a read-only mount EROFS always wins.  When
     writable mounts land, the EFBIG gate will be re-inserted at its
     proper position (between mnt_want_write success and do_truncate).

     Architectural directive: pure ABI translator fidelity.  The
     native OS does not need to implement writable mounts to satisfy
     this gate — EROFS is the truthful terminal answer for every File.
     Symlink resolution at the VFS layer is not yet implemented; the
     EINVAL we surface for Symlink entries diverges from Linux's
     "follow + recurse" behavior, but Linux's eventual terminal answer
     on our universal-RO universe would still be EROFS, so userspace
     probes that distinguish symlink-to-file from symlink-to-dir won't
     observe meaningful semantics either way until link resolution
     lands.

     Boot-time probes added (6 of them, A–F):
       (A) length=u64::MAX with NULL path -> EINVAL (length<0 fires
           before getname; confirms gate-1 ordering)
       (B) NULL path with length=0 -> EFAULT (getname)
       (C) empty string "" -> ENOENT (canonicalize_path empty input)
       (D) "/__nonexistent_truncate_target_xyz" -> ENOENT (Vfs::stat
           NotFound)
       (E) existing directory (Vfs::mkdir scratch) -> EISDIR
       (F) existing regular file (Vfs::write_file scratch) -> EROFS

     Stamp: "[syscall/linux]   truncate Linux gate ladder
       (EINVAL/EFAULT/ENOENT/EISDIR/EROFS): OK"

     Companion cleanup: the legacy stub-era smoke probes (which passed
     0x1000 as a raw user-pointer sentinel) became unsafe once the new
     impl actually reads path bytes — 0x1000 is unmapped in kernel
     context and copy_from_user on it page-faults.  Replaced with two
     NULL-only smoke probes that exercise the EFAULT (gate 2) and
     EINVAL (gate 1) paths without touching unmapped user addresses.
     Full ladder coverage lives in the dedicated batch-447 probe
     block.

     Iteration narrative: first boot test failed with a kernel page
     fault at 0xffffffff81dbb0a7, address=0x1000, after the new
     "truncate Linux gate ladder ... : OK" stamp but during the
     downstream legacy `truncate(0x1000, 0) -> EROFS` smoke probe.
     Root cause: the pre-batch smoke probe at lines ~44664-44678
     synthesized a fake user pointer at 0x1000 expecting the old stub
     to return EROFS after a 1-byte validate_user_read (which bypasses
     in kernel context).  My new impl runs read_user_cstr which calls
     copy_from_user, which still bypasses validate_user_read but then
     actually issues `copy_nonoverlapping(0x1000 as *const u8, ...)`
     in kernel mode — and 0x1000 is unmapped, hence the #PF.  Fix:
     removed the now-unsafe smoke probes and folded their coverage
     into the dedicated gate-ladder block (which uses real backing
     buffers).  Second boot test PASSED at 22s.

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean both attempts (50.40s, 38.87s).
     Boot: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED at 22s on the retry.
     Stamps confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   truncate Linux gate ladder (EINVAL/EFAULT/ENOENT/EISDIR/EROFS): OK"

448. syscall/linux: sys_ftruncate File arm — remove dead RLIMIT_FSIZE check before terminal EROFS.
     Linux source — fs/open.c::do_sys_ftruncate (Linux 6.x):
       int do_sys_ftruncate(unsigned int fd, loff_t length, int small)
       {
           struct fd f;
           struct inode *inode;
           struct dentry *dentry;
           int error;

           error = -EINVAL;
           if (length < 0)
               goto out;                /* (1) */
           error = -EBADF;
           f = fdget(fd);
           if (!fd_file(f))
               goto out;                /* (2) */

           if (fd_file(f)->f_flags & O_LARGEFILE)
               small = 0;

           dentry = fd_file(f)->f_path.dentry;
           inode = dentry->d_inode;
           error = -EINVAL;
           if (!S_ISREG(inode->i_mode) || !(fd_file(f)->f_mode & FMODE_WRITE))
               goto out_putf;            /* (3) -EINVAL for non-reg / RO open */

           error = -EINVAL;
           if (small && length > MAX_NON_LFS)
               goto out_putf;

           error = -EPERM;
           if (IS_APPEND(file_inode(fd_file(f))))
               goto out_putf;

           sb_start_write(inode->i_sb);
           error = security_file_truncate(fd_file(f));
           if (!error)
               error = do_truncate(file_mnt_idmap(fd_file(f)), dentry, length,
                                   ATTR_MTIME | ATTR_CTIME, fd_file(f));
                       /* ^ dispatches notify_change -> inode_newsize_ok ->
                          -EFBIG when length > RLIMIT_FSIZE; the EROFS check
                          happens inside notify_change via mnt_want_write,
                          BEFORE inode_newsize_ok. */
           sb_end_write(inode->i_sb);
           ...
       }

     Divergence table (pre-batch vs Linux vs post-batch):

       | call shape                                  | Linux  | Pre    | Post   |
       |---------------------------------------------|--------|--------|--------|
       | ftruncate(fd, -1)                           | EINVAL | EINVAL | EINVAL |
       | ftruncate(BAD_FD, 0)                        | EBADF  | EBADF  | EBADF  |
