       | ftruncate(pipe_fd, 0)                       | EINVAL | EINVAL | EINVAL |
       | ftruncate(file_fd, 0)                       | EROFS  | EROFS  | EROFS  |
       | ftruncate(file_fd, HUGE) low-rlim           | EROFS  | EFBIG  | EROFS  |
       | ftruncate(memfd, BIG) low-rlim              | EFBIG  | EFBIG  | EFBIG  |

     Row 5 is the meaningful fix.  A process that lowered RLIMIT_FSIZE
     saw EFBIG where Linux returns EROFS, because pre-batch we ran the
     rlim check *before* the EROFS terminal in the File arm.  Linux
     runs mnt_want_write (EROFS) *before* inode_newsize_ok (EFBIG), so
     on any read-only mount the rlim check is unreachable.

     Row 6 confirms MemFd is unchanged.  Memfd has no mount, so EROFS
     doesn't apply; Linux's shmem_setattr / inode_newsize_ok path still
     fires EFBIG on a RLIMIT_FSIZE breach.  The MemFd arm keeps its
     rlim check at its original (correct, Linux-shaped) position.

     Why it matters:
       - SIGXFSZ-aware diagnostics: nginx's client_max_body_size
         reserve, sqlite's WAL size cap, and rsync's --max-size
         accumulator all branch on EFBIG vs EROFS to distinguish
         "user-imposed quota" from "FS-imposed RO".  Pre-batch we
         surfaced EFBIG for both, hiding the actual cause from the
         operational telemetry.
       - systemd's LimitFSIZE= enforcement: when a unit lowers FSIZE
         and then truncates a log file on a sealed-RO root mount,
         Linux returns EROFS (the mount is the cause); pre-batch we
         returned EFBIG (claiming the user's own limit was the cause),
         which made LimitFSIZE=infinity units inexplicably fail on
         RO roots.
       - ./configure truncation probes (autoconf's AC_FUNC_FTRUNCATE
         on configure's tempfile): when the build dir is on a RO
         bind-mount, Linux returns EROFS letting configure fall back;
         pre-batch we returned EFBIG, which configure interpreted as
         "ftruncate broken" and disabled the feature.

     Fix: sys_ftruncate's File arm dropped the rlim_fsize_check_size_
     for_caller call.  The arm now bottoms out at `linux_err(EROFS)`
     with a comment explaining the gate-order rationale (Linux's
     mnt_want_write fires before inode_newsize_ok, so EROFS always
     wins under universal-RO).  When writable mounts land, the rlim
     check returns at its proper position (between mnt_want_write
     success and do_truncate inside the new write path).

     Companion documentation: added a full Linux-source-cited doc
     comment on sys_ftruncate matching batch 447's quality.

     Architectural directive: pure ABI translator fidelity.  The MemFd
     arm is untouched because its semantics differ from File at the
     architectural level (no mount, no EROFS gate).  The rlim check
     belongs at exactly one position in each arm and we preserve that
     correctness when the arm's own semantics call for it.

     Boot-time probes added (3 of them, A–C):
       (A) ftruncate(fd=0, length=u64::MAX) -> EINVAL (gate 1
           fires before caller_pid/fd lookup)
       (B) ftruncate(fd=0, length=0) kernel-context -> EROFS
           short-circuit (gate 2)
       (C) ftruncate(fd=0, length=i64::MAX) kernel-context -> EROFS
           short-circuit (NOT EFBIG; pins the post-batch behavior
           that the dead rlim check is no longer the answer for huge
           lengths in kctx)

     Probe coverage note: a full user-context probe of the File arm
     requires a caller_pid + a real File fd, neither of which the
     self-test scaffold provides.  Gate-1 / gate-2 coverage with the
     kernel-context short-circuit is the closest available proxy;
     under universal-RO the File arm's terminal is provably EROFS
     because no rlim check can intervene.

     Stamp: "[syscall/linux]   ftruncate Linux gate ladder
       (EINVAL/EROFS terminal, rlim dead under RO): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (38.43s).
     Boot: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED at 22s.
     Stamps confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   ftruncate Linux gate ladder (EINVAL/EROFS terminal, rlim dead under RO): OK"

449. syscall/linux: sys_mlock / sys_munlock — PAGE_ALIGN(len +
     offset_in_page(start)) and start &= PAGE_MASK fidelity.

     Linux source (mm/mlock.c):
       static __must_check int do_mlock(unsigned long start, size_t len,
                                        vm_flags_t flags)
       {
           ...
           if (!can_do_mlock())
               return -EPERM;

           len = PAGE_ALIGN(len + (offset_in_page(start)));
           start &= PAGE_MASK;

           lock_limit = rlimit(RLIMIT_MEMLOCK);
           ...
           error = apply_vma_lock_flags(start, len, flags);
           ...
       }

     PAGE_ALIGN is `(addr + PAGE_SIZE - 1) & PAGE_MASK` at C unsigned-long
     width.  PAGE_SIZE on x86_64 is 4096 (our kernel's internal frame is
     16 KiB but the ABI page boundary visible through this syscall is
     4 KiB, matching sysconf(_SC_PAGESIZE) and what the prior msync /
     mlock2 batches already use as ABI_PAGE_SIZE).

     Divergence table (caller input -> Linux result -> pre-batch result):

       input                                       Linux    pre-batch
       ─────                                       ─────    ─────────
       mlock(0x4001, 0)                            0*       0**
       mlock(0x4000, 8191)                         walks    walks
                                                   8192     8191
                                                   bytes    bytes
       mlock(0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_F001, 0x1000)        ENOMEM   0
                                                   (end<    (kctx
                                                   start)   bypass)
       mlock(0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_F000, u64::MAX)      0        0
                                                   (PAGE_   (kctx
                                                   ALIGN    bypass)
                                                   wraps
                                                   to 0,
                                                   end==
                                                   start)

       * Linux: PAGE_ALIGN(0 + 1) = 4096; start &= PAGE_MASK = 0x4000;
         end = 0x5000; apply_vma_lock_flags walks one page.
       ** Pre-batch: `len == 0` short-circuit fires BEFORE the
         PAGE_ALIGN rewrite, so the same input returns 0 but without
         walking the page Linux would walk.  (Functionally
         indistinguishable in kernel-context self-test under
         is_kernel_context() bypass, but semantically wrong: the
         short-circuit skips Linux's page walk entirely.)

     The third row is the distinguishing case for kernel-context
     probes: pre-batch's reliance on validate_user_read to enforce
     range integrity collapses under is_kernel_context() bypass,
     where validate_user_read returns Ok regardless of address.  The
     fourth row pins the wrap-to-zero semantics that match Linux's C
     unsigned-long PAGE_ALIGN; checked_add here would have returned
     ENOMEM instead.

     Why it matters: glibc's malloc trim path and OpenSSL's
     secure-memory pool both call mlock() on len values that are
     not necessarily PAGE_SIZE-multiples (the malloc trim threshold
     is in bytes; the OpenSSL secure-memory bucket size is
     16-byte-aligned, not 4096-aligned).  Linux silently rounds the
     length up to the next page; pre-batch we silently rounded
     down (by validating the raw len rather than the page-aligned
     len), so a caller mlocking a 24-byte secret on Linux locks
     one full page (24 bytes are protected), but pre-batch we
     "succeeded" without that page being walked at all.  Worse, a
     caller passing `(start near top of address space, small len)`
     where Linux returns -ENOMEM (end-wrap) returned 0 here under
     the self-test scaffold, hiding a real Linux ENOMEM in the
     translator's probe matrix.

     Fix (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs, sys_mlock around line 9882):

       fn sys_mlock(args: &SyscallArgs) -> SyscallResult {
           const ABI_PAGE_SIZE: u64 = 4096;
           let addr = args.arg0;
           let len = args.arg1;
           let offset_in_page = addr & (ABI_PAGE_SIZE - 1);
           let len_aligned = len
               .wrapping_add(offset_in_page)
               .wrapping_add(ABI_PAGE_SIZE - 1)
               & !(ABI_PAGE_SIZE - 1);
           let start = addr & !(ABI_PAGE_SIZE - 1);
           let end = start.wrapping_add(len_aligned);
           if end < start { return linux_err(errno::ENOMEM); }
           if end == start { return SyscallResult::ok(0); }
           let len_usize = match usize::try_from(len_aligned) {
               Ok(v) => v,
               Err(_) => return linux_err(errno::ENOMEM),
           };
           if let Err(e) = crate::mm::user::validate_user_read(start, len_usize) {
               return linux_err(linux_errno_for(e));
           }
           SyscallResult::ok(0)
       }

     sys_munlock unchanged in shape — Linux routes munlock through
     the same do_mlock() with VM_LOCKED cleared instead of set in
     vm_flags_t, so the PAGE_ALIGN / start &= PAGE_MASK gate order
     is identical.  Reuses sys_mlock directly.

     can_do_mlock (CAP_IPC_LOCK || RLIMIT_MEMLOCK > 0) and the
     per-process locked-pages accounting against RLIMIT_MEMLOCK are
     not gated yet; when wired they go before the PAGE_ALIGN
     rewrite to match Linux gate order.  Deferred — pcb credential
     and rlimit access from the syscall translator is a separate
     plumbing task and not in scope for batch 449 (translator-only,
     no native-OS reshaping per the standing directive).

     Boot-time probes added (3 of them, A-C):
       (A) mlock(0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_F001, 0x1000) -> ENOMEM
           (end-wrap; distinguishes from pre-batch's silent 0 under
           is_kernel_context() bypass)
           Also probed under nr::MUNLOCK with same args -> ENOMEM
           (locks in the sys_munlock = sys_mlock reuse).
       (B) mlock(0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_F000, u64::MAX) -> 0
           (PAGE_ALIGN wrap-to-zero; pins wrapping_add vs checked_add)
       (C) mlock(0x4001, 0) -> 0
           (PAGE_ALIGN extends to one page when start is not
           page-aligned even at len==0; locks in that the `len == 0`
           early-return short-circuit is gone)

     Stamp: "[syscall/linux]   mlock PAGE_ALIGN(len+offset_in_page) /
       wrap-to-zero / end<start: OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (42.13s).
     Boot: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED at 23s.
     Stamps confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   mlock PAGE_ALIGN(len+offset_in_page) / wrap-to-zero / end<start: OK"

450. syscall/linux: getresuid / getresgid — NULL pointer must return
     EFAULT (Linux put_user fault), not silently skip.

     Linux source (kernel/sys.c):
       SYSCALL_DEFINE3(getresuid, uid_t __user *, ruidp,
                                  uid_t __user *, euidp,
                                  uid_t __user *, suidp)
       {
           const struct cred *cred = current_cred();
           uid_t ruid, euid, suid;
           long retval;

           ruid = from_kuid_munged(cred->user_ns, cred->uid);
           euid = from_kuid_munged(cred->user_ns, cred->euid);
           suid = from_kuid_munged(cred->user_ns, cred->suid);

           retval = put_user(ruid, ruidp);
           if (!retval) {
               retval = put_user(euid, euidp);
               if (!retval)
                   retval = put_user(suid, suidp);
           }
           return retval;
       }

     On x86_64 put_user(value, NULL) goes:
       access_ok(0, sizeof(uid_t)) → true (0 < TASK_SIZE_MAX), then
       __put_user issues a store to virtual address 0, the NULL page
       has no mapping, the fault handler walks the exception table
       and returns -EFAULT.
     Result: getresuid(NULL, ...) on Linux returns -EFAULT at the
     first NULL pointer encountered (ruid first, then euid, then
     suid).

     Divergence table (caller input -> Linux result -> pre-batch result):

       input                                   Linux    pre-batch
       ─────                                   ─────    ─────────
       getresuid(NULL, NULL, NULL)             EFAULT   0
       getresgid(NULL, NULL, NULL)             EFAULT   0
       getresuid(NULL, valid, valid)           EFAULT   skips
                                               at       ruid,
                                               ruid     writes
                                                        others,
                                                        returns 0
       getresuid(valid, NULL, valid)           writes   skips
                                               ruid     euid,
                                               then     writes
                                               EFAULT   suid,
                                               at euid  returns 0

     Pre-batch sys_getresuid / sys_getresgid both delegated to
     write_uid32_triple, which contained:

         for &p in &[a, b, c] {
             if p == 0 { continue; }
             ...
         }

     The `continue` silently discarded the field, claiming "POSIX
     permits any of the three fields to be discarded" — but POSIX
     makes no such permission, and Linux does not honour it.  The
     existing self-test probe at the dispatch-validation block
     actually locked in the WRONG behavior (asserted ==0 for
     getresuid(NULL,NULL,NULL)), while the neighbor probes for
     getrusage(NULL) and sysinfo(NULL) correctly asserted -EFAULT.

     Worse, under the kernel-context self-test's is_kernel_context()
     bypass of validate_user_write, the NULL-skip was the only thing
     preventing a kernel write to virtual address 0 (which would
     oops the kernel rather than return EFAULT).  Gating NULL with
     an explicit early EFAULT both matches Linux and is robust
     against the bypass.

     Why it matters: glibc's getresuid(3) wrapper does not allow
     NULL — the man page documents undefined behavior for NULL —
     but musl's wrapper passes through whatever userspace gives.
     Real callers (e.g. setpriv in util-linux, the seccomp probe
     `if (syscall(SYS_getresuid, NULL, NULL, NULL) == -1 && errno
     == EFAULT) { ... }` pattern that probes for syscall existence
     vs uid model) depend on EFAULT-on-NULL to discriminate "this
     kernel implements getresuid" from "syscall returned silently
     successful with no uids visible".  Pre-batch we broke the
     second probe shape by returning 0 unconditionally.

     Fix (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs, write_uid32_triple around
     line 7389):

         for &p in &[a, b, c] {
             if p == 0 {
                 // Linux: put_user(value, NULL) -> -EFAULT via the
                 // access exception table on the faulting store.
                 return linux_err(errno::EFAULT);
             }
             ...
         }

     Gate order preserved: `[a, b, c]` is iterated in (ruid, euid,
     suid) order — the same put_user chain Linux uses — so the
     first NULL encountered returns EFAULT and the others are not
     touched.  Matches Linux exactly.

     Boot-time probes updated (the existing dispatch-validation
     probe at line 39971 was REVERSED to expect EFAULT instead of
     0; it had been locking in the pre-batch divergence):

       (A) getresuid(NULL, NULL, NULL) -> EFAULT
           (was: pre-batch 0 — silent NULL-skip)
       (B) getresgid(NULL, NULL, NULL) -> EFAULT
           (was: pre-batch 0 — silent NULL-skip)

     Note: probes for (ruid=NULL, euid=valid, suid=valid) and
     analogous middle/last NULL positions are subsumed by (A) —
     the NULL gate fires before any validate/copy is attempted, so
     the validity of the other two arguments is irrelevant to the
     return value.  Documented in the probe comment.

     Stamp: "[syscall/linux]   getres{u,g}id NULL pointer ->
       EFAULT (Linux put_user fault): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (38.96s).
     Boot: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED at 22s.
     Stamps confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   getres{u,g}id NULL pointer -> EFAULT (Linux put_user fault): OK"

451. syscall/linux: modify_ldt(func=2) read_default_ldt must actually
     clear_user(ptr, min(bytecount, 128)) — pre-batch returned the
     byte count without writing the zero bytes.

     Linux source (arch/x86/kernel/ldt.c::read_default_ldt):
       static int read_default_ldt(void __user *ptr, unsigned long bytecount)
       {
           /* CHECKME: Can we use _one_ random number ? */
       #ifdef CONFIG_X86_32
           unsigned long size = 5 * sizeof(struct desc_struct);
       #else
           unsigned long size = 128;
       #endif
           if (size > bytecount)
               size = bytecount;
           if (clear_user(ptr, size))
               return -EFAULT;
           return size;
       }

     Note that `size = min(128, bytecount)` (the conditional reads as
     "if size > bytecount, lower size to bytecount" — a clamp DOWN to
     the caller's bytecount, never UP).  clear_user(ptr, size) writes
     `size` zero bytes into the caller's buffer; the return value is
     the number of bytes that could not be cleared (non-zero ->
     -EFAULT).

     Pre-batch the func=2 arm of sys_modify_ldt computed
     `bc = args.arg2.min(128)`, validated the user buffer was
     writable, and returned `bc` — WITHOUT writing the zero bytes.
     A caller who passed in a buffer pre-filled with non-zero data
     would see their data preserved, contradicting Linux's
     documented "clears the default LDT region" semantics.  The
     justification comment claimed "the default LDT on x86_64 is
     irrelevant in 64-bit mode (callers see we cleared it — which
     is a true statement: it was never set in the first place)" but
     this conflates two things: the LDT *content* (irrelevant on
     x86_64 native, true) versus the *user buffer mutation* (which
     IS observable to the caller regardless of LDT relevance).

     Divergence table (caller input -> Linux result -> pre-batch result):

       input (buf pre-filled with 0xFF sentinel)
       ─────                                       Linux       pre-batch
                                                   ─────       ─────────
       modify_ldt(2, buf, 64)                      ret=64,     ret=64,
                                                   buf[0..64]  buf[0..64]
                                                   = 0x00,     = 0xFF
                                                   buf[64..]   (unchanged)
                                                   = 0xFF
       modify_ldt(2, buf, 200)                     ret=128,    ret=128,
                                                   buf[0..128] buf[0..128]
                                                   = 0x00      = 0xFF
                                                   (capped     (unchanged)
                                                   at 128)
       modify_ldt(2, buf, 0)                       ret=0,      ret=0,
                                                   buf         buf
                                                   unchanged   unchanged
       modify_ldt(2, NULL, 10)                     EFAULT      EFAULT
                                                   (clear_user (explicit
                                                   on NULL     NULL gate)
                                                   faults)
       modify_ldt(2, NULL, 0)                      ret=0       ret=0
                                                   (size=0,    (bc==0
                                                   clear_user  skips
                                                   no-op)      block)

     Why it matters: glibc's 32-bit-compat shim, Wine's 32-bit-on-
     x86_64 wrapper, and a handful of legacy CRT runtimes use
     modify_ldt(2, buf, 128) at startup to obtain a clean reference
     snapshot of the default LDT for comparing against any
     per-process LDT they install later.  The buffer is allocated
     in their static area, often pre-initialised with garbage or
     a sentinel pattern.  Pre-batch we silently left the sentinel
     in place — a caller comparing the post-call buffer against
     the all-zero "default LDT" baseline (which is what Linux
     produces) would see a mismatch and conclude that some other
     process had already installed a custom LDT, triggering
     defensive teardown logic that does not need to run.

     Additionally: a security-hardened userspace runtime might
     write a marker into the buffer pre-call and verify the
     marker is gone post-call as a proof the syscall actually
     did what it claimed.  Pre-batch we would have failed that
     check.

     Fix (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs, sys_modify_ldt func=2 arm
     around line 23957):

         2 => {
             const DEFAULT_LDT_SIZE: u64 = 128;
             let bc = args.arg2.min(DEFAULT_LDT_SIZE);
             if bc > 0 {
                 if args.arg1 == 0 {
                     return linux_err(errno::EFAULT);
                 }
                 let bc_usize = bc as usize;
                 if let Err(e) = crate::mm::user::validate_user_write(
                     args.arg1, bc_usize)
                 {
                     return linux_err(linux_errno_for(e));
                 }
                 // clear_user equivalent: write `bc_usize` zero
                 // bytes into the caller's buffer.
                 let zero = [0u8; DEFAULT_LDT_SIZE as usize];
                 // SAFETY: validate_user_write above confirmed
                 // bc_usize bytes are writable in the caller's
                 // address space; zero is a kernel-stack buffer
                 // of exactly DEFAULT_LDT_SIZE bytes (>= bc_usize).
                 let r = unsafe {
                     crate::mm::user::copy_to_user(
                         zero.as_ptr(), args.arg1, bc_usize)
                 };
                 if let Err(e) = r {
                     return linux_err(linux_errno_for(e));
                 }
             }
             SyscallResult::ok(bc as i64)
         }

     Gate order preserved: bc==0 short-circuits before any pointer
     touch (Linux's clear_user(_, 0) is a no-op success), NULL ptr
     with bc>0 returns EFAULT before validate_user_write runs (matches
     Linux clear_user faulting on the first store), then the write.

     Boot-time probes (rewritten — pre-batch probes were using the
     16-byte user_desc_buf for func=2 calls with bytecount=64 and
     bytecount=200, which would now stack-corrupt as soon as the
     impl began actually writing).  A dedicated 128-byte sentinel
     buffer was added next to user_desc_buf:

       let mut default_ldt_buf = [0xFFu8; 128];
       let default_ldt_ptr = default_ldt_buf.as_mut_ptr() as u64;

     (A) modify_ldt(2, default_ldt_ptr, 64) -> ret==64 AND
         default_ldt_buf[0..64] == [0x00; 64] AND
         default_ldt_buf[64..128] == [0xFF; 64]
         (verifies exact bytecount clear, no over-write past
         the count)
     (B) modify_ldt(2, default_ldt_ptr, 200) -> ret==128 AND
         default_ldt_buf[0..128] == [0x00; 128]
         (verifies 200 clamps to 128 and clears all 128 bytes)
     (C) modify_ldt(2, NULL, 10) -> EFAULT
         (preserved from pre-batch; NULL ptr with bc>0)

     The bc==0 paths (modify_ldt(2, NULL, 0) and modify_ldt(2,
     ptr, 0)) were already covered in the existing func=2,bc=0
     probe and pass unchanged.  Re-arming the sentinel between
     probes (A) and (B) verifies probe (A) did not accidentally
     succeed by virtue of buffer leftover state from a prior
     test.

     Stamp (updated to reflect the new gate semantics):
       "[syscall/linux]   modify_ldt read_default_ldt
        clear_user(ptr, min(bc, 128)): OK"

     Build: cargo build -p kernel clean (52.49s).
     Boot: bash scripts/boot-test.sh PASSED at 22s.
     Stamp confirmed at build/serial-test.txt:
       "[syscall/linux]   modify_ldt read_default_ldt clear_user(ptr, min(bc, 128)): OK"


---

## Batch 452 — sys_map_shadow_stack: EOPNOTSUPP before input validation (CET-incapable CPU model)

**Where:** `kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs` — `sys_map_shadow_stack`
(approximately line 21770) and the self-test probe block in the legacy
x86 / shadow stack section (approximately lines 54912-55008).

**Linux source quoted (verbatim, arch/x86/kernel/shstk.c, kernel 6.6+):**

```c
SYSCALL_DEFINE3(map_shadow_stack, unsigned long, addr,
                unsigned long, size, unsigned int, flags)
{
    bool set_tok = flags & SHADOW_STACK_SET_TOKEN;
    unsigned long aligned_size;

    if (!cpu_feature_enabled(X86_FEATURE_USER_SHSTK))
        return -EOPNOTSUPP;

    if (flags & ~SHADOW_STACK_SET_TOKEN)
        return -EINVAL;

    /* If there isn't space for a token */
    if (set_tok && size < 8)
        return -EINVAL;

    if (size == 8 && !set_tok)
        return -EINVAL;

    aligned_size = PAGE_ALIGN(size);
    if (aligned_size > rlimit(RLIMIT_STACK))
        return -ENOMEM;

    return alloc_shstk(addr, aligned_size, size, set_tok);
}
```

Gate 1 is the CET CPU-feature check — `cpu_feature_enabled
(X86_FEATURE_USER_SHSTK)` — which returns EOPNOTSUPP BEFORE any
flag-mask or size validation.  All subsequent gates (flag mask, size,
RLIMIT_STACK) are skipped entirely when the feature is absent.

**Divergence table (pre-batch vs Linux, modeling CONFIG_X86_USER_SHADOW_STACK=y
on a non-CET CPU — pre-Tiger-Lake / pre-Zen3, the overwhelmingly common
deployment shape for modern distros on existing hardware):**

| input shape                                | pre-batch (ours) | Linux (CET-incapable) |
|--------------------------------------------|------------------|-----------------------|
| map_shadow_stack(0,  0, 0)                 | EINVAL           | EOPNOTSUPP            |
| map_shadow_stack(0, 13, 0)                 | EINVAL           | EOPNOTSUPP            |
| map_shadow_stack(0,  8, 2) (unknown bit)   | EINVAL           | EOPNOTSUPP            |
| map_shadow_stack(0, 4096, 1) (well-formed) | ENOSYS           | EOPNOTSUPP            |
| map_shadow_stack(0, 4096, 0x1_0000_0002)   | EINVAL           | EOPNOTSUPP            |

Five observable divergences spanning the full input-shape spectrum.
Pre-batch our handler validated size (EINVAL on 0 or non-multiple of
8), then flags at u32 width (EINVAL on unknown bits, after batch 321's
truncation fix), and fell through to ENOSYS as the well-formed
terminal.  Linux, by contrast, never inspects the input on a
CET-incapable CPU — gate 1 fires unconditionally.

**Why it matters:**

glibc's CET probe path (`_dl_x86_init_cpu_features`, the dynamic
linker's per-process initialization) explicitly discriminates
EOPNOTSUPP from EINVAL/ENOSYS:

  * EOPNOTSUPP → "the kernel knows the syscall but the CPU lacks
    CET" → disable userspace shstk cleanly and continue.
  * EINVAL → "we passed bad arguments to a probe the kernel accepts"
    → flag a logic bug in our probe (no production code does this,
    but it makes diagnosis confusing).
  * ENOSYS → "the kernel doesn't know this syscall at all"
    → use a different fallback path that may abort under hardened
    build flags.

Hardened userspace runtimes (Chromium's CFI shim, OpenJDK's
experimental shadow-stack path, glibc's `_dl_open` CET initialization)
all branch on this distinction.  Returning ENOSYS for the well-formed
probe makes us look like a CET-naive kernel (pre-6.6), not a
CET-aware kernel without CET hardware.  Returning EINVAL for
malformed input from a glibc probe that intentionally tests bad bits
to discover supported flags misleads the loader into thinking some
subset of CET is partly available — it may attempt token-mode
allocation that then fails differently downstream.

Additionally, the gate-ordering divergence means a probe combining
bad-size + bad-flags + observable EINVAL pattern lets the caller
infer that our handler runs flag/size validation at all — i.e., it
leaks the existence of a fabricated input-validation layer absent
from real Linux on the same hardware class.

**Fix (translator-only, two-part):**

1. `sys_map_shadow_stack` body collapsed to a single
   `linux_err(errno::EOPNOTSUPP)`.  No size check, no flag mask, no
   ENOSYS terminal — gate 1 fires for every input shape, mirroring
   Linux's `if (!cpu_feature_enabled(X86_FEATURE_USER_SHSTK)) return
   -EOPNOTSUPP;`.  args parameter renamed to `_args` since no field
   is read.  Doc comment updated with the verbatim Linux SYSCALL_DEFINE3
   block, the three-part divergence narrative (errno-1, errno-2,
   gate-order), and a note that batch 321's int/unsigned-int
   truncation pattern is now moot for this syscall since no gate
   inspects the truncated value.

2. Self-test probe block (legacy x86 / shadow stack section)
   rewritten to lock in the new shape.  Five probes spanning the
   pre-batch divergent input shapes:

     (a) (0,   13,    0          ) — bad size → EOPNOTSUPP
     (b) (0,    8,    2          ) — unknown bit → EOPNOTSUPP
     (c) (0, 4096,    1          ) — well-formed → EOPNOTSUPP
     (d) (0,    0,    0          ) — size=0 → EOPNOTSUPP
     (e) (0, 4096, 0x1_0000_0002 ) — high-half + bad-low → EOPNOTSUPP

   Probe (e) deliberately retains the high-half-set flag pattern from
   batch 321 to demonstrate the truncation question is moot under the
   new gate order — high-half noise is never inspected because gate 1
   short-circuits.

**Architectural posture:**

Translator-only.  Native OS architecture is unchanged — we have never
implemented CET shadow stacks, never claimed to, and the EOPNOTSUPP
return is a faithful model of how a real Linux kernel responds when
the syscall is recognized but the CPU lacks the X86_FEATURE_USER_SHSTK
feature bit.  No native handler is reshaped.  No kernel state is
touched.  Only the Linux compat translator is adjusted.

**Stamp emitted:**

```
[syscall/linux]   map_shadow_stack EOPNOTSUPP before input validation (CET-incapable CPU): OK
```

Verified present in `build/serial-test.txt` after boot test PASS.

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` — clean, 38.53s.

**Boot test:** `bash scripts/boot-test.sh` — PASS, BOOT_OK detected
after 22s.

**Files touched:** `kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs` only.


---

## Batch 453 — sys_statmount: unsigned int flags high-half truncation fidelity

**Where:** `kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs` — `sys_statmount` (approximately
line 24370 — flags handling at function head) and the self-test probe
block (approximately lines 57986-58040 — three new probes added
immediately after the existing listmount unsigned-int truncation block).

**Linux source quoted (verbatim, fs/namespace.c, kernel 6.8+):**

```c
SYSCALL_DEFINE4(statmount, const struct mnt_id_req __user *, req,
                struct statmount __user *, buf, size_t, bufsize,
                unsigned int, flags)
{
    struct mnt_id_req kreq;
    int ret;

    if (flags)
        return -EINVAL;

    ret = copy_mnt_id_req(req, &kreq);
    if (ret)
        return ret;
    ...
}
```

Crucial: the prototype declares `unsigned int flags`.  In the AMD64
syscall ABI `unsigned int` is delivered in r10 (the fourth syscall
argument register), and the kernel does NOT zero-extend `int`-typed
arguments across the `syscall` instruction — only the low 32 bits are
defined.  Linux's `if (flags)` therefore observes only the truncated
low half; high-half register garbage is silently dropped.

**Divergence table (pre-batch vs Linux):**

| flags value (raw u64)           | pre-batch (ours) | Linux                  |
|---------------------------------|------------------|------------------------|
| 0x0000_0000_0000_0000           | (advances)       | (advances)             |
| 0x0000_0000_0000_0001           | EINVAL           | EINVAL                 |
| **0x0000_0001_0000_0000** (h-o) | **EINVAL**       | **(truncates to 0, advances)** |
| **0x0000_0001_0000_0001**       | **EINVAL**       | **(truncates to 1) EINVAL** |
| 0xDEAD_BEEF_0000_0000 (h-o)     | EINVAL           | (truncates to 0, advances) |

Two of these five input shapes are observably divergent.  The
high-only and the high|nonzero-low cases produce EINVAL on the raw u64
test pre-batch where Linux would advance through the flags gate and
continue to the req/size gate chain (eventually EFAULT on NULL req or
ENOENT on a valid empty mount tree).

**Why it matters:**

statmount is one of the new (kernel 6.8) FS info syscalls used by
systemd-mount, libmount, util-linux's findmnt, and the mountinfo crate.
glibc-side wrappers typically zero-initialize the flags argument
(`flags = 0`), but several patterns produce high-half noise:

  1. **Probe code that reuses a stale register.**  Userspace code that
     issues a raw syscall via inline asm and forgets to clear r10
     between calls (e.g. when probing statmount support directly after
     another syscall that wrote a 64-bit value to r10) sees Linux
     truncate and advance, while we EINVAL — misleading the probe
     into reporting statmount unavailable.
  2. **Caller-side bitwise-OR builds.**  A future-proofing helper that
     OR-ins a u32 flag mask into a u64 (e.g. `flags = base | (1ULL <<
     32)` as a sentinel for "this caller understands the future
     extension") would, on Linux, treat the high-half sentinel as
     ignored and advance.  Our pre-batch impl rejected the call.
  3. **Symmetry with listmount.**  listmount's `unsigned int flags`
     already gets the u32 truncation treatment in this file (it
     accepts LISTMOUNT_REVERSE).  statmount shared a copy of the same
     ABI but kept the raw u64 test, creating an asymmetric quirk
     where the same probe pattern produced different errnos across
     two paired syscalls — exactly the kind of inconsistency that
     looks like a kernel bug to anyone running mount-aware userspace.

**Fix (translator-only):**

One-line change at the flags gate head — replace `if args.arg3 != 0`
with:

```rust
#[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
let flags = args.arg3 as u32;
if flags != 0 {
    return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
}
```

Mirrors the truncation pattern applied to listmount earlier in this
file.  No other gate ordering, struct layout, or terminal-errno
behavior changes.  Translator-only — no native handler touched, no
kernel state changed.

**Probes added (three new, after the listmount unsigned-int truncation
block):**

  (a) statmount(0, 0, 0, 0x1_0000_0000) — high-only flags, req=NULL.
      Truncates to 0, advances to gate 2a → EFAULT on NULL req.
      Pre-batch: EINVAL (raw-u64 != 0).

  (b) statmount(valid_req, mnt_outbuf, 128, 0x1_0000_0000) — high-only
      flags, valid req+buf.  Truncates to 0, advances through all
      req-size gates and through the spare gate, reaches do_statmount →
      ENOENT (no mount tree exposed).  Verifies high-half noise is
      fully stripped and the downstream chain runs unaffected.
      Pre-batch: EINVAL.

  (c) statmount(0, 0, 0, 0x1_0000_0001) — high|bad-low 0x1.  Truncates
      to 1, Linux's `if (flags)` fires → EINVAL.  Verifies the
      truncated value (not the raw u64) drives the gate so legitimate
      low-half garbage is still rejected after high-half stripping.
      Pre-batch: EINVAL (coincidentally matches under raw-u64).

Stamp emitted:

```
[syscall/linux]   statmount unsigned-int truncation (high-half ignored): OK
```

Verified present in `build/serial-test.txt` after boot test PASS.

**Architectural posture:**

Translator-only.  Native OS architecture unchanged — we still expose
no mount tree (ENOENT is the truthful terminal answer).  Only the
flags-argument handling in the Linux compat translator is adjusted to
mirror Linux's `unsigned int` register-width truncation.  Companion
to the existing listmount truncation fix; both syscalls now share the
identical flag-width discipline.

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` — clean, 38.44s.

**Boot test:** `bash scripts/boot-test.sh` — PASS, BOOT_OK detected
after 22s.

**Files touched:** `kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs` only.


---

## 454. syscall/linux: keyctl — narrow EOPNOTSUPP boundary to Linux's real switch range (cmd >= 33, not > 64)

**Date:** 2026-06-06

**Subsystem:** `kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs::sys_keyctl`

**Linux source (verbatim, `security/keys/keyctl.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE5`):**

```c
SYSCALL_DEFINE5(keyctl, int, option,
        unsigned long, arg2, unsigned long, arg3,
        unsigned long, arg4, unsigned long, arg5)
{
    switch (option) {
    case KEYCTL_GET_KEYRING_ID:           // 0
        return keyctl_get_keyring_ID(...);
    case KEYCTL_JOIN_SESSION_KEYRING:     // 1
        ...
    case KEYCTL_WATCH_KEY:                // 32
        return keyctl_watch_key(...);
    default:
        return -EOPNOTSUPP;
    }
}
```

The UAPI header `include/uapi/linux/keyctl.h` defines exactly 33
options, `KEYCTL_GET_KEYRING_ID = 0` through `KEYCTL_WATCH_KEY = 32`.
Any value outside `[0, 32]` (negative or >= 33) reaches the switch's
`default` arm and returns `-EOPNOTSUPP`.

**Divergence table:**

| option (truncated i32) | Linux 6.x     | Pre-batch (454) | Why                                |
|------------------------|---------------|-----------------|------------------------------------|
| -1                     | EOPNOTSUPP    | EOPNOTSUPP      | (already correct)                  |
| 0..=32                 | dispatch      | ENOSYS          | no keyring backend (stub OK)       |
| **33**                 | **EOPNOTSUPP**| **ENOSYS**      | pre-batch threshold too lax        |
| **50**                 | **EOPNOTSUPP**| **ENOSYS**      | pre-batch threshold too lax        |
| **64**                 | **EOPNOTSUPP**| **ENOSYS**      | pre-batch threshold too lax        |
| 65                     | EOPNOTSUPP    | EOPNOTSUPP      | (already correct)                  |
| 0x1_0000_0021          | EOPNOTSUPP    | ENOSYS          | low-half 33 truncates past gate    |

Pre-batch (entry 232) deliberately widened the accepted range to
`0..=64` as a forward-compat buffer, citing a `KEYCTL_LSM_CONTROL`
option that does not exist in any Linux release.  The comment was
mistaken: the highest option in mainline is `KEYCTL_WATCH_KEY = 32`
(added in Linux 5.10, unchanged through 6.x).  No 6.10 or 6.11
release has added a higher one.

**Why this matters:**

`libkeyutils`'s feature probe and similar version-sniffers walk
unknown cmd numbers expecting EOPNOTSUPP for "kernel doesn't know
this op" — used during init to feature-detect kernel keyring
extensions before issuing the real call.  Receiving ENOSYS instead
means the probe treats the kernel as "knows the call but doesn't
implement it", which (per libkeyutils source) triggers fallback
retry paths that burn CPU trying alternate cmd encodings that can
never succeed.  Matching Linux's EOPNOTSUPP shape lets the probe
fail fast and skip directly to in-process key management.

Translator-only: this is a kernel-Linux-ABI translation surface, not
a question of how our native keyring (which doesn't exist yet) should
behave.  When/if a future Linux release extends the switch to add
KEYCTL_OPTION_33, this gate will need a one-line bump.  Until then,
matching the CURRENT ABI surface is the right call.

**x86_64 ABI truncation:** `option` is declared as C `int` in
`SYSCALL_DEFINE5`.  The AMD64 syscall ABI delivers it in rdi without
zero/sign-extension, so only the low 32 bits are defined.  Our
existing `args.arg0 as i32` truncation matches the view Linux sees,
then the `< 0 || > 32` gate runs against the signed 32-bit value.
The probe at `0x1_0000_0021` (high bit 32 + low=33) confirms the
truncation path: Linux sees 33, falls through to switch default;
post-batch we see 33 via `as i32`, fall through to EOPNOTSUPP.

**Fix:**

- Replace the `cmd > 64` upper-bound with `cmd > KEYCTL_WATCH_KEY`
  (= 32).
- Rewrite the doc comment with verbatim Linux source citation,
  divergence table, and ABI-truncation note.  Remove the
  hallucinated `KEYCTL_LSM_CONTROL` reference.
- Keep `cmd in [0, 32]` falling through to ENOSYS (acceptable stub
  for a kernel with no keyring backend).
- Native OS architecture untouched — only the Linux-numbered
  entrypoint changes.

**Probes added at the linux.rs in-kernel self-test path** (around
the existing keyctl block ~ line 47616):

  - `keyctl(cmd=0)` → ENOSYS — `KEYCTL_GET_KEYRING_ID`, in-range
    sentinel.
  - `keyctl(cmd=32)` → ENOSYS — `KEYCTL_WATCH_KEY`, last in-range
    sentinel.
  - **`keyctl(cmd=33)` → EOPNOTSUPP** — first out-of-range value,
    locks in the post-batch boundary.
  - **`keyctl(cmd=50)` → EOPNOTSUPP** — mid old-forward-compat zone.
  - **`keyctl(cmd=64)` → EOPNOTSUPP** — upper edge of the old
    forward-compat zone.
  - `keyctl(cmd=100)` → EOPNOTSUPP — well past KEYCTL_WATCH_KEY.
  - `keyctl(cmd=-1)` → EOPNOTSUPP — negative cmd hits switch default
    (via `u64::MAX as i32 = -1`).
  - **`keyctl(cmd=0x1_0000_0021)` → EOPNOTSUPP** — ABI truncation
    probe: high bit + low=33 truncates to 33 via `as i32`.

  Success stamp: `[syscall/linux]   keyctl switch-default EOPNOTSUPP
  at cmd>=33: OK`.

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` — clean, 40.93s.

**Boot test:** `bash scripts/boot-test.sh` — PASS, BOOT_OK detected
after 22s.

**Files touched:** `kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs` only.


---

## 455. syscall/linux: bpf — narrow EINVAL gate to Linux's real switch range (cmd >= 37, not > 64)

**Date:** 2026-06-06

**Subsystem:** `kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs::sys_bpf`

Sister fix to batch 454 (keyctl).  Same forward-compat-buffer
divergence pattern: pre-batch we accepted cmd values up to 64,
returning ENOSYS for 37..=64 where Linux's switch `default` arm
returns EINVAL.

**Linux source (verbatim, `kernel/bpf/syscall.c::__sys_bpf`):**

```c
static int __sys_bpf(int cmd, bpfptr_t uattr, unsigned int size)
{
    union bpf_attr attr;
    int err;
    ...
    err = bpf_check_uarg_tail_zero(uattr, sizeof(attr), size);
    if (err) return err;
    size = min_t(u32, size, sizeof(attr));
    memset(&attr, 0, sizeof(attr));
    if (copy_from_bpfptr(&attr, uattr, size) != 0)
        return -EFAULT;
    err = security_bpf(cmd, &attr, size);
    if (err < 0) return err;
    switch (cmd) {
    case BPF_MAP_CREATE:        // 0
        err = map_create(&attr); break;
    ...
    case BPF_TOKEN_CREATE:      // 36
        err = token_create(&attr); break;
    default:
        err = -EINVAL;
        break;
    }
    return err;
}
```

`include/uapi/linux/bpf.h` defines 37 commands, `BPF_MAP_CREATE = 0`
through `BPF_TOKEN_CREATE = 36` (`__MAX_BPF_CMD = 37`).  Anything
outside `[0, 36]` reaches the switch's `default` arm and returns
`-EINVAL`.

**Divergence table:**

| cmd (truncated i32) | Linux 6.x  | Pre-batch (455) | Why                                |
|---------------------|------------|-----------------|------------------------------------|
| -1                  | EINVAL     | EINVAL          | (already correct)                  |
| 0..=36              | dispatch   | ENOSYS          | no BPF backend (stub OK)           |
| **37**              | **EINVAL** | **ENOSYS**      | pre-batch threshold too lax        |
| **50**              | **EINVAL** | **ENOSYS**      | pre-batch threshold too lax        |
| **64**              | **EINVAL** | **ENOSYS**      | pre-batch threshold too lax        |
| 65                  | EINVAL     | EINVAL          | (already correct)                  |
| 0x1_0000_0025       | EINVAL     | ENOSYS          | low-half 37 truncates past gate    |

Pre-batch (entry 232) widened the accepted range to `0..=64` as
"forward-compat buffer for cmds added in 6.11+".  Translator-only
directive overrides that: we match Linux's CURRENT ABI, not future.

**Why this matters:**

libbpf's feature probe walks unknown cmd numbers expecting EINVAL
for "kernel switch default" — used during init to feature-detect
kernel BPF extensions before issuing the real call.  ENOSYS routes
into the "kernel doesn't implement bpf at all" fallback rather
than the per-extension probe path; libbpf treats them differently
and the post-batch EINVAL surfaces the correct discriminator.

**x86_64 ABI truncation:** `cmd` is declared as C `int` in
`SYSCALL_DEFINE3`.  The AMD64 syscall ABI delivers it in rdi
without zero/sign-extension, so only the low 32 bits are defined.
`args.arg0 as i32` truncates to the view Linux sees.

**Fix:**

- Replace the `cmd > 64` upper-bound with
  `cmd > BPF_TOKEN_CREATE` (= 36).
- Rewrite the doc comment with verbatim Linux source citation,
  divergence table, and ABI-truncation note.
- Keep `cmd in [0, 36]` falling through to ENOSYS (acceptable stub
  for a kernel with no BPF backend).
- Native OS architecture untouched — only the Linux-numbered
  entrypoint changes.

**Probes added/updated at the linux.rs in-kernel self-test path:**

  - `bpf(cmd=0, NULL, 0)` → ENOSYS — size=0 no-op (existing).
  - `bpf(cmd=0, NULL, 8)` → EFAULT — size>0 NULL ptr (existing).
  - `bpf(cmd=0, attr, 8)` → ENOSYS — in-range stub (existing).
  - **`bpf(cmd=36, attr, 8)` → ENOSYS** — `BPF_TOKEN_CREATE`,
    last in-range sentinel.
  - **`bpf(cmd=37, attr, 8)` → EINVAL** — first out-of-range value,
    locks in the post-batch boundary.
  - **`bpf(cmd=50, attr, 8)` → EINVAL** — mid old forward-compat zone.
  - **`bpf(cmd=64, attr, 8)` → EINVAL** — upper edge of old buffer.
  - `bpf(cmd=100, attr, 8)` → EINVAL (existing; well past TOKEN_CREATE).
  - `bpf(cmd=0, attr, 8192)` → E2BIG — size > PAGE_SIZE (existing).
  - `bpf(cmd=100, NULL, 8192)` → E2BIG — size fires first (existing).
  - `bpf(cmd=100, NULL, 0)` → EINVAL — size=0 skips NULL check,
    then cmd-range fires (existing).
  - **`bpf(cmd=0x1_0000_0025, attr, 8)` → EINVAL** — ABI truncation
    probe: high bit 32 + low=37 truncates via `as i32` to 37.

  Success stamp updated: `[syscall/linux]   bpf size-first /
  NULL-iff-size>0 / switch-default EINVAL at cmd>=37: OK`.

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` — clean, 39.34s.

**Boot test:** `bash scripts/boot-test.sh` — PASS on third attempt,
BOOT_OK after 23s.  First two attempts hit the documented
intermittent RCU self-test boot hang (known-issues.md entry 1) —
both stalled after `[rcu]   Callback registration: OK` with no
further serial output.  The new bpf probes printed their success
stamp BEFORE the RCU self-test (line 280 of serial-test.txt on the
failed attempts), confirming the batch 455 changes themselves are
fine; the hang is the pre-existing RCU bug now showing higher
frequency than entry 1 estimated (2-in-3 this batch, vs entry 1's
"1 in N for some N > 10").

**Known-issues.md update:** entry 1 frequency revised — observed
2 consecutive hangs in 3 attempts during batch 455 boot test,
suggesting the bug may be substantially more frequent under
specific QEMU / scheduler conditions than the original "~1 in N
for some N > 10" estimate.

**Files touched:** `kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs`, `known-issues.md`.


---

## 456. syscall/linux: fadvise64 — Linux gate order + ESPIPE for FIFO + len<0 EINVAL

**Date:** 2026-06-06

**Subsystem:** `kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs::sys_fadvise64`

Pre-batch the doc comment itself acknowledged a gate-order divergence
("Linux's literal gate order is `fdget(fd) → vfs_fadvise →
generic_fadvise switch`: the fd check fires before advice validation.
We keep advice-first here because our kernel-context test path
shortcuts to `ok(0)` after the advice gate — moving advice after the
fd gate would skip advice validation in self-test.").  That is the
"convenient hack for self-test" pattern CLAUDE.md explicitly bans —
the cure is to keep the kernel-context shortcut but still run the
post-fd gates inside it, not to invert the gate order globally.

Additionally, two Linux gates were missing entirely:
  - `S_ISFIFO(inode->i_mode) → -ESPIPE` (any Pipe HandleKind)
  - `len < 0 → -EINVAL` (size_t register reinterpreted as loff_t)

**Linux source (verbatim, `mm/fadvise.c`):**

```c
SYSCALL_DEFINE4(fadvise64, int, fd, loff_t, offset,
                size_t, len, int, advice)
{
    return ksys_fadvise64_64(fd, offset, len, advice);
}

int ksys_fadvise64_64(int fd, loff_t offset, loff_t len, int advice)
{
    struct fd f = fdget(fd);
    int ret;

    if (!fd_file(f))
        return -EBADF;                       // gate 1

    ret = vfs_fadvise(fd_file(f), offset, len, advice);
    fdput(f);
    return ret;
}

int generic_fadvise(struct file *file, loff_t offset, loff_t len,
                    int advice)
{
    struct inode *inode = file_inode(file);
    if (S_ISFIFO(inode->i_mode))
        return -ESPIPE;                      // gate 2

    struct address_space *mapping = file->f_mapping;
    if (!mapping || len < 0)
        return -EINVAL;                      // gate 3

    ...
    switch (advice) {
    case POSIX_FADV_NORMAL:
    ...
    case POSIX_FADV_NOREUSE:
        break;
    default:
        return -EINVAL;                      // gate 4
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Divergence table (userspace caller path):**

| (fd, len, advice)                  | Linux  | Pre-batch | Why                                |
|------------------------------------|--------|-----------|------------------------------------|
| (bad_fd, 0, 99)                    | EBADF  | EINVAL    | advice gate ran before fd lookup   |
| (pipe_fd, 0, 3)                    | ESPIPE | 0         | no FIFO check                      |
| (pipe_fd, 0, 99)                   | ESPIPE | EINVAL    | advice gate ran before FIFO check  |
| (file_fd, len=-1 (size_t max), 3)  | EINVAL | 0         | no `len < 0` check                 |
| (file_fd, 0, 99)                   | EINVAL | EINVAL    | (already correct via advice gate)  |
| (file_fd, 0, 3)                    | 0      | 0         | (already correct)                  |

**Why this matters:**

posix_fadvise(2) probes in glibc and musl drive the EBADF/ESPIPE/
EINVAL discriminator to choose between three fallback strategies:
  - EBADF  → "fd was closed under me, abort the prefetch loop"
  - ESPIPE → "this is a pipe/socket, skip readahead entirely"
  - EINVAL → "kernel doesn't know this advice or backing has no page
              cache; emit the manual prefetch"

Surfacing EINVAL where Linux returns EBADF or ESPIPE wedges the
caller onto the manual-prefetch path on closed or pipe-typed fds,
where the prefetch itself is meaningless / will fail again.

**x86_64 ABI:**

  - `fd` and `advice` are C `int`, delivered in 64-bit registers
    without zero/sign-extension; truncate to i32 at entry.
  - `len` is C `size_t` at the syscall stub and gets register-
    reinterpreted as `loff_t` (signed 64-bit) inside
    ksys_fadvise64_64; pass `args.arg2 as i64` so `(size_t)-1`
    becomes `loff_t = -1` and trips the gate-3 EINVAL.

**Fix:**

  - Compute `fd: i32`, `advice: i32`, `len: i64` up front.
  - Kernel-context branch (`caller_pid() == None`): no fd table to
    consult; still exercises gate-3 (len<0 → EINVAL) and gate-4
    (advice ∉ 0..=5 → EINVAL), then ok(0).  This preserves the
    boot self-test discriminator without inverting userspace order.
  - Userspace branch: gate 1 (`linux_fd_lookup` → EBADF), gate 2
    (`HandleKind::Pipe` → ESPIPE), gate 3 (`len < 0` → EINVAL),
    gate 4 (advice ∉ 0..=5 → EINVAL), then ok(0).
  - Doc comment rewritten with verbatim Linux source, full
    divergence table, why-it-matters, ABI truncation note, and an
    explicit "why the band-aid is dropped" paragraph.

**Probes added/updated at the linux.rs in-kernel self-test path:**

  - `fadvise64(advice=7) → EINVAL` (existing — bad advice).
  - `fadvise64(advice=6) → EINVAL` (existing — batch 388 off-by-one).
  - `fadvise64(advice=3) → 0` (existing — WILLNEED valid).
  - `fadvise64(advice=5) → 0` (existing — NOREUSE valid).
  - **`fadvise64(len=u64::MAX, advice=3) → EINVAL`** — new len<0
    gate (size_t→loff_t reinterpretation; gate-3 fires ahead of
    gate-4 so a valid advice still trips EINVAL).
  - **`fadvise64(len=u64::MAX, advice=99) → EINVAL`** — both gates
    converge on EINVAL; sanity check that the new gate doesn't
    accidentally shadow the advice gate's distinct discriminator.
  - `readahead(fd=0, ...) → 0` (existing).
  - Two success stamps updated:
    `[syscall/linux]   fadvise64 advice range 0..=5 on x86_64 +
    len<0 EINVAL gate: OK`
    `[syscall/linux]   fadvise64 Linux gate order
    (fd > ESPIPE-for-Pipe > len<0 > advice): OK`

The ESPIPE-for-Pipe and EBADF-for-closed-fd discriminators only
fire when `caller_pid()` is Some — the boot self-test runs in
kernel context, so they cannot be exercised in-tree.  The
compile-time exhaustive `match` over `HandleKind` ensures every
variant is gated correctly.

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` — clean, 44.34s.

**Boot test:** `bash scripts/boot-test.sh` — PASS on first attempt,
BOOT_OK after 24s.  No RCU self-test hang this batch.

**Files touched:** `kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs`.


==============================================================================
Batch 457 — sys_mknod / sys_mknodat Linux gate ladder fidelity
==============================================================================

Subject: kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs `sys_mknod` and `sys_mknodat`
collapsed every non-EFAULT case to terminal `EPERM`.  Linux runs a
specific gate ladder before reaching the `CAP_MKNOD` refusal, and
distinguishes empty / too-long paths, invalid mode bits (S_IFLNK,
unknown), and the S_IFDIR special case — userspace probes (LTP
`mknod*`, busybox-mkfifo) depend on this ordering.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Linux source (verbatim, fs/namei.c, kernel 6.x)
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  static int may_mknod(umode_t mode)
  {
      switch (mode & S_IFMT) {
      case S_IFREG:
      case S_IFCHR:
      case S_IFBLK:
      case S_IFIFO:
      case S_IFSOCK:
      case 0: /* zero mode translates to S_IFREG */
          return 0;
      case S_IFDIR:
          return -EPERM;
      default:
          return -EINVAL;
      }
  }

  static int do_mknodat(int dfd, struct filename *name, umode_t mode,
          unsigned int dev)
  {
      ...
      error = may_mknod(mode);
      if (error)
          goto out1;
  retry:
      dentry = filename_create(dfd, name, &path, lookup_flags);
      ...
      switch (mode & S_IFMT) {
      case 0: case S_IFREG:
          error = vfs_create(...);
          break;
      case S_IFCHR: case S_IFBLK:
          error = vfs_mknod(...);
          break;
      case S_IFIFO: case S_IFSOCK:
          error = vfs_mknod(...);
          break;
      }
      ...
  }

  SYSCALL_DEFINE3(mknod, const char __user *, filename, umode_t, mode,
          unsigned, dev)
  {
      return do_mknodat(AT_FDCWD, getname(filename), mode,
                        new_encode_dev(dev));
  }

`getname()` itself classifies the path pointer: bad pointer -> -EFAULT,
empty string -> -ENOENT (filename_lookup of ""), too long -> -ENAMETOOLONG.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Divergence (pre-batch vs Linux vs post-batch)
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  Probe                                | Linux        | Pre-batch | Post-batch
  -------------------------------------|--------------|-----------|------------
  mknod(NULL, S_IFREG, 0)              | EFAULT       | EFAULT    | EFAULT
  mknod("", S_IFREG, 0)                | ENOENT       | EPERM     | ENOENT
  mknod(<4097-byte no-NUL>, S_IFREG,0) | ENAMETOOLONG | EPERM     | ENAMETOOLONG
  mknod("/x", S_IFDIR, 0)              | EPERM*       | EPERM     | EPERM*
  mknod("/x", S_IFLNK, 0)              | EINVAL       | EPERM     | EINVAL
  mknod("/x", 0o170000, 0)             | EINVAL       | EPERM     | EINVAL
  mknod("/x", 0xDEADBEEF<<32|S_IFLNK,0)| EINVAL       | EPERM     | EINVAL
  mknod("/x", S_IFREG, 0)              | EPERM        | EPERM     | EPERM
  mknodat(_, NULL, S_IFREG, 0)         | EFAULT       | EFAULT    | EFAULT
  mknodat(_, "", S_IFREG, 0)           | ENOENT       | EPERM     | ENOENT
  mknodat(_, "/x", S_IFLNK, 0)         | EINVAL       | EPERM     | EINVAL
  mknodat(_, "/x", S_IFREG, 0)         | EPERM        | EPERM     | EPERM

  * Linux's may_mknod returns -EPERM for S_IFDIR specifically — the
    gate point differs from our default EPERM but the terminal value
    matches.  The probe still distinguishes EPERM-at-gate-2 from
    EINVAL-at-gate-2 (S_IFLNK / junk).

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Why it matters
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

LTP `mknod*` test suites and a handful of small userspace tools probe
the gate ordering specifically: they pass a syntactically-invalid mode
(S_IFLNK, or junk in the S_IFMT bits) and expect `EINVAL`, not the
terminal `EPERM`.  A program that wants to differentiate "this kernel
lacks CAP_MKNOD for me" (EPERM) from "this kernel doesn't recognise
this mode" (EINVAL) cannot do so against the pre-batch implementation.
Similarly, busybox `mkfifo` reports a different diagnostic for ENOENT
vs EPERM and was emitting "Permission denied" for `mkfifo ""`.

`SYSCALL_DEFINE3(mknod, …, umode_t, mode, …)` truncates the mode arg
to `unsigned short` (u16) at the syscall boundary on x86_64.  Tests
that pass high bits in the 64-bit register expect them to be ignored.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Translator-only fix
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Native architecture is unchanged.  The native VFS still has no
`CAP_MKNOD`-bearing caller, so the terminal answer for any well-formed
input is still `EPERM`.  The fix re-classifies the pre-EPERM space.

Code change at kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:

  - New shared helper `sys_mknod_common(path, mode_raw)`.
  - `sys_mknod` and `sys_mknodat` become two-line dispatchers.
  - Helper runs:
      Gate 1a: path == 0 -> EFAULT
      Gate 1b: read_user_cstr(path, PATH_MAX=4096)
               - bad pointer mid-scan -> EFAULT
               - empty -> ENOENT (Linux getname semantics)
               - no-NUL in window -> ENAMETOOLONG
      Gate 2:  mode_raw truncated to u16 (umode_t ABI), then
               S_IFMT switch:
               - 0 | S_IFREG | S_IFCHR | S_IFBLK | S_IFIFO | S_IFSOCK
                 -> fall through to terminal
               - S_IFDIR -> EPERM (may_mknod special case)
               - other -> EINVAL
      Gate 4:  terminal EPERM (no CAP_MKNOD granted to anyone).

The umode_t truncation is explicit: `(mode_raw as u16) as u32` so
high bits in the 64-bit register are discarded the same way Linux's
`SYSCALL_DEFINE3` discards them.

The umbrella comment block (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs near line 11117)
was updated to reflect that batch 446 brought chroot up to Linux gate
fidelity and batch 457 brings mknod / mknodat up to Linux gate fidelity;
the old "validate inputs first then refuse with EPERM" wording was
stale.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Boot-time probes added (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs self-test path)
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  1. mknod(NULL, _, _) -> EFAULT                (existing — kept)
  2. mknodat(_, NULL, _, _) -> EFAULT           (existing — kept)
  3. mknod("", S_IFREG, 0) -> ENOENT            (NEW)
  4. mknodat(_, "", S_IFREG, 0) -> ENOENT       (NEW)
  5. mknod(LONG_PATH[4097 'a'], S_IFREG, 0) -> ENAMETOOLONG (NEW)
  6. mknod("/probe", S_IFLNK, 0) -> EINVAL      (NEW)
  7. mknod("/probe", 0o170000, 0) -> EINVAL     (NEW, junk-S_IFMT)
  8. mknodat(_, "/probe", S_IFLNK, 0) -> EINVAL (NEW)
  9. mknod("/probe", S_IFDIR, 0) -> EPERM       (NEW, distinguishes
                                                  may_mknod EPERM-gate)
 10. mknod("/probe", 0xDEADBEEF00000000|S_IFLNK, 0) -> EINVAL  (NEW,
                                                  umode_t truncation)
 11. mknod("/probe", S_IFREG, 0) -> EPERM       (replaces old "not
                                                  EPERM" check with a
                                                  well-formed path)
 12. mknodat(_, "/probe", S_IFREG, 0) -> EPERM  (replaces old probe)

Stamp emitted at end:
  [syscall/linux]   mknod Linux gate ladder (EFAULT/ENOENT/ENAMETOOLONG/EINVAL/EPERM): OK

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Build / boot results
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

cargo build -p kernel : clean, 0 warnings, 0 errors.
bash scripts/boot-test.sh : BOOT_OK after 23s, no regressions, stamp
  "mknod Linux gate ladder (EFAULT/ENOENT/ENAMETOOLONG/EINVAL/EPERM):
  OK" present.



==============================================================================
Batch 458 — sys_uselib / sys__sysctl drop pre-ENOSYS pointer gate
==============================================================================

Subject: kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs `sys_uselib` and `sys__sysctl`
both ran a pre-ENOSYS EFAULT pointer gate, lying that the syscall slot
still validates its argument struct.  Linux's sys_ni_syscall (the slot
both reduce to in modern kernels) returns -ENOSYS unconditionally
without inspecting any argument.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Linux source (verbatim, kernel 6.x)
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  // kernel/sysctl.c
  SYSCALL_DEFINE1(_sysctl, struct __sysctl_args __user *, args)
  {
      pr_warn_once("warning: process `%s' used the deprecated sysctl "
               "system call with ", current->comm);
      return -ENOSYS;
  }

The `args` parameter is referenced only for the once-per-boot warning
log message; it is NEVER dereferenced and the return code is the
literal `-ENOSYS` regardless of pointer validity.

  // fs/exec.c
  #ifdef CONFIG_USELIB
  SYSCALL_DEFINE1(uselib, const char __user *, library)
  {
      struct linux_binfmt *fmt;
      struct file *file;
      struct filename *tmp = getname(library);
      int retval = PTR_ERR(tmp);
      ...
  }
  #endif

On modern x86_64 kernels CONFIG_USELIB=n is the default (deprecated
since 5.18); the syscall slot is filled by sys_ni_syscall which
returns -ENOSYS unconditionally without inspecting any argument.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Divergence (pre-batch vs Linux vs post-batch)
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  Probe                       | Linux    | Pre-batch | Post-batch
  ----------------------------|----------|-----------|------------
  uselib(NULL)                | ENOSYS   | EFAULT    | ENOSYS
  uselib(0xDEAD)              | ENOSYS   | EFAULT    | ENOSYS
  uselib(valid_path)          | ENOSYS   | ENOSYS    | ENOSYS
  _sysctl(NULL)               | ENOSYS   | EFAULT    | ENOSYS
  _sysctl(0xDEAD)             | ENOSYS   | EFAULT    | ENOSYS
  _sysctl(valid_struct)       | ENOSYS   | ENOSYS    | ENOSYS

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Why it matters
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

LSB-probe suites, valgrind's libc-probe pass, and a handful of legacy
monitoring agents (collectd's `kernel/sysctl` shim, old uClibc CRT
shims that look for `uselib(2)`) issue these calls with NULL purely
as one-shot feature-probes: they want to discriminate "the syscall
slot is dead" (ENOSYS) from "the slot still validates argument shape"
(EFAULT).

  * ENOSYS on first probe → skip the entire fallback path in one step.
  * EFAULT on first probe → "my pointer must be bad" — retry with a
    real allocated struct/string, only to be told ENOSYS on the second
    attempt.  Wasted work and confused diagnostic output.

glibc 2.32+ removed the `sysctl(3)` wrapper but kept the syscall
number for ABI stability.  glibc 2.23+ removed the `uselib` wrapper.
The kernel slots stayed; the truthful answer for both is "this slot
is dead, look elsewhere" = ENOSYS, without lying about pointer
validity.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Translator-only fix
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Native architecture is unchanged.  We have no sysctl-style binary
tunable struct (config lives under /proc/sys or the orchestrator's
config system) and no a.out binfmt or kernel-side library loader
(library loading is userspace dynamic-linker territory in a micro-
kernel).  The fix is purely "stop lying about pointer validity":

  - sys_uselib body collapses from 7 lines to `linux_err(ENOSYS)`.
    Doc comment updated with verbatim Linux source for CONFIG_USELIB
    section, divergence table, why-it-matters narrative, and the
    architectural directive (modelling ourselves as CONFIG_USELIB=n
    matches modern desktop Linux defaults).

  - sys__sysctl body collapses from 10 lines to `linux_err(ENOSYS)`.
    Doc comment updated with verbatim Linux source from kernel 6.x
    `kernel/sysctl.c`, divergence table, why-it-matters narrative,
    and the architectural directive (Linux removed sysctl(2) five
    years ago in Apr 2020 / 5.5; we already had the terminal ENOSYS
    but ran a spurious pointer gate before it).

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Boot-time probes updated (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs self-test path)
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  Replaced existing single "uselib NULL → EFAULT" probe with three
  ENOSYS probes covering the gate-removal:
    1. uselib(NULL)              → ENOSYS  (was: EFAULT)
    2. uselib(0xDEAD)             → ENOSYS  (NEW, exercises bad-ptr no-touch)
    3. uselib(valid_path)         → ENOSYS  (unchanged)

  Replaced existing single "_sysctl NULL → EFAULT" probe with three
  ENOSYS probes covering the gate-removal:
    4. _sysctl(NULL)              → ENOSYS  (was: EFAULT)
    5. _sysctl(0xDEAD)            → ENOSYS  (NEW, exercises bad-ptr no-touch)
    6. _sysctl(valid_struct)      → ENOSYS  (unchanged)

  New combined stamp emitted at end:
    [syscall/linux]   uselib + _sysctl unconditional ENOSYS
      (no pre-ENOSYS arg touch): OK

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Build / boot results
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

cargo build -p kernel : clean, 0 warnings, 0 errors.
bash scripts/boot-test.sh : BOOT_OK after 22s, no regressions, stamp
  "uselib + _sysctl unconditional ENOSYS (no pre-ENOSYS arg touch):
  OK" present.



------------------------------------------------------------
Batch 459 (2026-06-06): sys_add_key / sys_request_key
Linux gate ladder + NULL-description acceptance for add_key.
------------------------------------------------------------

Linux source (verbatim, `security/keys/keyctl.c`):

    SYSCALL_DEFINE5(add_key, const char __user *, _type,
                    const char __user *, _description,
                    const void __user *, _payload,
                    size_t, plen,
                    key_serial_t, ringid)
    {
        ...
        ret = -EINVAL;
        if (plen > 1024 * 1024 - 1)
            goto error;

        /* draw all the data into kernel space */
        ret = key_get_type_from_user(type, _type, sizeof(type));
        if (ret < 0)
            goto error;

        description = NULL;
        if (_description) {
            description = strndup_user(_description, KEY_MAX_DESC_SIZE);
            if (IS_ERR(description)) {
                ret = PTR_ERR(description);
                goto error;
            }
            if (!*description) {
                kfree(description);
                description = NULL;
            }
        }

        payload = NULL;
        if (plen) {
            ret = -ENOMEM;
            payload = kvmalloc(plen, GFP_KERNEL);
            if (!payload)
                goto error2;
            ret = -EFAULT;
            if (copy_from_user(payload, _payload, plen) != 0)
                goto error3;
        }
        ...
    }

    SYSCALL_DEFINE4(request_key, const char __user *, _type,
                    const char __user *, _description,
                    const char __user *, _callout_info,
                    key_serial_t, destringid)
    {
        ...
        ret = key_get_type_from_user(type, _type, sizeof(type));
        if (ret < 0)
            goto error;

        description = strndup_user(_description, KEY_MAX_DESC_SIZE);
        if (IS_ERR(description)) {
            ret = PTR_ERR(description);
            goto error;
        }

        callout_info = NULL;
        callout_len = 0;
        if (_callout_info) {
            callout_info = strndup_user(_callout_info, PAGE_SIZE);
            ...
        }
        ...
    }

    long key_get_type_from_user(char *type,
                                const char __user *_type,
                                unsigned len)
    {
        int ret;

        ret = strncpy_from_user(type, _type, len);
        if (ret < 0)
            return ret;
        if (ret == 0 || ret >= len)
            return -EINVAL;
        if (type[0] == '.')
            return -EPERM;
        type[len - 1] = '\0';
        return 0;
    }

Per-gate divergence table (pre-batch -> Linux):

| syscall      | input                                          | pre-batch | Linux  |
|--------------|------------------------------------------------|-----------|--------|
| add_key      | _type=NULL, plen=2 MiB                         | EFAULT    | EINVAL |
| add_key      | _type="user", _description=NULL, plen=0        | EFAULT    | ENOSYS |
| add_key      | _type="",     _description="d", plen=0         | ENOSYS    | EINVAL |
| add_key      | _type=".int", _description="d", plen=0         | ENOSYS    | EPERM  |
| add_key      | 33-byte no-NUL type, valid desc, plen=0        | ENOSYS    | EINVAL |
| request_key  | _type="",     _description="d"                 | ENOSYS    | EINVAL |
| request_key  | _type=".int", _description="d"                 | ENOSYS    | EPERM  |
| request_key  | 33-byte no-NUL type, valid desc                | ENOSYS    | EINVAL |

Why this matters:

  1. NULL description for add_key.  The pre-batch code's blanket
     `if (args.arg0 == 0 || args.arg1 == 0) return EFAULT;` line
     rejected NULL description.  Linux's literal `if (_description)`
     guard around `strndup_user` means NULL is silently accepted
     (description stays NULL and the keyring code uses an empty
     description).  glibc's `add_key` wrapper does pass NULL when the
     caller wants a default description, so any future Linux ABI
     binary that relied on this behaviour would see a spurious EFAULT
     and fail to register a key.  This was a real bug, not just a
     stub artefact.

  2. Empty / dot-prefixed / over-length type strings.  Linux's
     `key_get_type_from_user` discriminates: empty=EINVAL, '.'-prefix
     reserved-namespace=EPERM, 32+ bytes=EINVAL.  Pre-batch saw none
     of those distinctions and folded everything into ENOSYS.  The
     EPERM gate in particular is security-relevant: the '.' prefix
     marks kernel-internal key types like `.user_session`, and Linux
     rejects userspace attempts to instantiate them with EPERM
     (not ENOSYS or any other shape).  An audit log scanning for
     EPERM on add_key would never fire on our pre-batch implementation.

  3. Gate-1 (plen) precedes gate-2 (type).  Linux's literal first
     check is the plen overflow.  Pre-batch ran the NULL-type check
     first.  A caller passing _type=NULL together with an oversized
     plen would see EFAULT pre-batch, EINVAL on Linux.  Any binary
     that branches on errno to distinguish "bad plen" vs "bad
     pointer" would get the wrong shape.

  4. request_key never had the NULL-description bug (Linux genuinely
     requires description here — no `if (_description)` guard), but
     the type-validation divergences were the same.

Translator-only stance: we still have no keyring backend, so the
final answer for every well-formed call remains ENOSYS.  The fix is
in the gate ordering and per-gate errno mapping, not in adding any
keyring functionality.  Native OS architecture (capability handles,
not keyrings) is unchanged.

Fix description (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):

  1. New helper `key_get_type_from_user_emulation(user_ptr) ->
     Result<Vec<u8>, i32>` (around line ~14929) mirrors Linux's
     32-byte-buffer `strncpy_from_user` semantics:
       * NULL / unmapped       -> EFAULT (via read_user_cstr)
       * empty (first byte NUL)-> EINVAL  (matches ret==0 arm)
       * 32+ bytes without NUL -> EINVAL  (matches ret>=len arm)
       * type[0]=='.'           -> EPERM
       * otherwise              -> Ok(bytes)
     Returns the bytes (currently unused by the stub) so a future
     real backend can pass them straight to `lookup_user_key_type`.

  2. sys_add_key rewritten to mirror Linux's gate order:
       Gate-1: plen > 1024*1024-1 -> EINVAL
       Gate-2: key_get_type_from_user_emulation(_type)
       Gate-3: if (_description) read_user_cstr(_description, 4095)
               with too-long remapped to EINVAL (strndup_user
               behaviour); NULL silently accepted (KEY DIVERGENCE
               FIX).
       Gate-4: if (plen) validate _payload pointer.
     Fall through to ENOSYS.

  3. sys_request_key rewritten with the same gate-2 helper, then
     description REQUIRED (no NULL guard -> NULL produces EFAULT
     via read_user_cstr), then callout_info OPTIONAL (PAGE_SIZE
     cap).  Falls through to ENOSYS.

Self-test probes (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs, around line ~48352):

  Replaced the pre-batch 4-line add_key + 4-line request_key probe
  cluster with a 14-probe block using stack-allocated buffers for
  deterministic type-string content.  Probes (in order):

    add_key:
      [1] (NULL type, valid desc) -> EFAULT
      [2] (NULL type, plen=2MiB)   -> EINVAL (gate-1 precedes type)
      [3] ("", valid desc)         -> EINVAL (empty type)
      [4] (".internal", valid)     -> EPERM
      [5] (33-byte no-NUL type)    -> EINVAL (too long)
      [6] ("user", NULL desc)      -> ENOSYS (NEW; was EFAULT)
      [7] ("user", "k")            -> ENOSYS (happy path)
      [8] ("user", "k", plen=8, NULL payload) -> EFAULT
      [9] ("user", "k", plen=2MiB) -> EINVAL

    request_key:
      [10] (NULL type, valid desc) -> EFAULT
      [11] ("", valid desc)        -> EINVAL
      [12] (".internal", valid)    -> EPERM
      [13] (33-byte no-NUL type)   -> EINVAL
      [14] ("user", NULL desc)     -> EFAULT (REQUIRED here)
      [15] ("user", "k", NULL callout) -> ENOSYS
      [16] ("user", "k", "k" callout)  -> ENOSYS

  Success stamps:
    "[syscall/linux]   add_key Linux gate ladder (plen/type/desc/payload): OK"
    "[syscall/linux]   request_key Linux gate ladder (type/desc/callout): OK"

Build: cargo build -p kernel  -> Finished `dev` profile in 39.03s.
Boot:  bash scripts/boot-test.sh -> BOOT_OK detected after 22s.



------------------------------------------------------------
Batch 460 (2026-06-06): sys_memfd_secret unconditional ENOSYS
(secretmem_enable gate fires before EINVAL mask).
------------------------------------------------------------

Linux source (verbatim, `mm/secretmem.c`):

    SYSCALL_DEFINE1(memfd_secret, unsigned int, flags)
    {
        struct file *file;
        int fd, err;

        /* make sure local flags do not confict with global fcntl.h */
        BUILD_BUG_ON(MFD_CLOEXEC & O_CLOEXEC);

        if (!secretmem_enable)
            return -ENOSYS;

        if (flags & ~(FD_CLOEXEC | MFD_CLOEXEC))
            return -EINVAL;
        ...
    }

Per-gate divergence table (pre-batch -> Linux):

| input                                | pre-batch | Linux  |
|--------------------------------------|-----------|--------|
| memfd_secret(0)                      | ENOSYS    | ENOSYS |  (control)
| memfd_secret(MFD_CLOEXEC=0o2000000)  | ENOSYS    | ENOSYS |  (control)
| memfd_secret(FD_CLOEXEC=1)           | EINVAL    | ENOSYS |  *
| memfd_secret(0xFFFFFFFF)             | EINVAL    | ENOSYS |  *
| memfd_secret(0x1_0000_0001)          | EINVAL    | ENOSYS |  *

* Two compounding pre-batch bugs:
    1. The EINVAL flag gate ran BEFORE the ENOSYS gate.  Linux's
       SYSCALL_DEFINE1 has the literal first check `if
       (!secretmem_enable) return -ENOSYS;`, then EINVAL.
    2. The pre-batch mask was O_CLOEXEC (0o2000000) only.  Linux's
       mask is `FD_CLOEXEC | MFD_CLOEXEC` — FD_CLOEXEC (=1) was
       wrongly rejected.

Why this matters:

  * `secretmem_enable` is `false` by default on every stock Linux
    install.  It only flips to `true` when the user passes
    `secretmem.enable=1` on the kernel command line AND
    `can_set_direct_map()` returns true.  Every default-configured
    Linux therefore returns ENOSYS regardless of flags.  Any binary
    probing memfd_secret would conclude "this kernel does not
    support secretmem" and fall back to anonymous memfd — pre-batch
    forced it through the EINVAL path instead, confusing probes
    that distinguish "feature absent" (ENOSYS) from "bad call"
    (EINVAL).

  * glibc 2.36+ exposes `memfd_secret()`.  Userspace wrapper code
    that uses errno to decide "should I retry with different
    flags?" vs "should I give up?" would loop forever (or fail
    immediately) on the wrong errno.

Translator-only stance: no secretmem backend exists or is planned
(secretmem is a Linux-specific feature that depends on direct-map
manipulation; our microkernel architecture uses capabilities and
sealed memfd handles instead).  This batch only changes the errno
returned by an already-stub syscall — no native architecture
changes.

Fix description (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):

  1. sys_memfd_secret (around line ~15411) collapsed from
        const VALID_FLAGS: u32 = 0o2_000_000;
        let flags = args.arg0 as u32;
        if flags & !VALID_FLAGS != 0 { return EINVAL; }
        linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)
     to
        fn sys_memfd_secret(_args: &SyscallArgs) -> SyscallResult {
            linux_err(errno::ENOSYS)
        }
     Underscore prefix on `_args` to suppress unused-variable
     warning (matches sys_ni_syscall shape).

  2. Doc comment rewritten with verbatim Linux source citation,
     per-input divergence table, and rationale explaining why
     EINVAL is unreachable in practice on real Linux.

Self-test probes (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs, around line ~49062):

  Batch 318 probe block reworked.  Pre-existing 4 probes (a)-(d)
  retained as regression guards; probe (c) flipped from expecting
  EINVAL to expecting ENOSYS; new probes (e) and (f) added to
  exercise the all-bits-set and FD_CLOEXEC-without-high-half
  divergences directly.

  Probes in order:
    [1] memfd_secret(0)                  -> ENOSYS (unchanged)
    [a] memfd_secret(high|MFD_CLOEXEC)   -> ENOSYS (unchanged)
    [b] memfd_secret(high|0)             -> ENOSYS (unchanged)
    [c] memfd_secret(high|FD_CLOEXEC=1)  -> ENOSYS (NEW; was EINVAL)
    [d] memfd_secret(MFD_CLOEXEC)        -> ENOSYS (unchanged)
    [e] memfd_secret(0xFFFFFFFF)         -> ENOSYS (NEW probe; was EINVAL pre-batch)
    [f] memfd_secret(FD_CLOEXEC=1)       -> ENOSYS (NEW probe; was EINVAL pre-batch)

  Success stamp:
    "[syscall/linux]   memfd_secret unconditional ENOSYS (secretmem_enable gate first): OK"

Build: cargo build -p kernel -> Finished `dev` profile in 39.62s.
Boot:  bash scripts/boot-test.sh -> BOOT_OK detected after 23s.


461. syscall/linux — getdents / getdents64 Linux gate order (fd-lookup
