     before count/buf), `unsigned int` count truncation (batch 461,
     2026-06-06).

Target: `kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs::sys_getdents` (line ~26508) and
`kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs::sys_getdents64` (line ~26634).

Linux source — verbatim from fs/readdir.c:

    SYSCALL_DEFINE3(getdents, unsigned int, fd,
            struct linux_dirent __user *, dirent, unsigned int, count)
    {
        struct fd f;
        struct getdents_callback buf = {
            .ctx.actor = filldir,
            .count = count,
            .current_dir = dirent
        };
        int error;

        f = fdget_pos(fd);
        if (!fd_file(f))
            return -EBADF;

        error = iterate_dir(fd_file(f), &buf.ctx);
        ...
    }

    static bool filldir(struct dir_context *ctx, const char *name, int namlen,
                        loff_t offset, u64 ino, unsigned int d_type)
    {
        ...
        if (reclen > buf->count) {
            buf->error = -EINVAL;
            return false;
        }
        ...
        unsafe_put_user(d_off, &lastdirent->d_off, efault);
        ...
    }

(Same shape for `SYSCALL_DEFINE3(getdents64, ...)` in the immediate
neighbour.)

Divergence table — what callers observe in stock x86_64 Linux 6.x
versus what our pre-batch translator returned:

    Input                              | Pre-batch | Linux  | Fix
    -----------------------------------|-----------|--------|----------
    getdents:   unknown fd, count=0    | 0         | -EBADF | reorder
    getdents:   unknown fd, dirp ok,
                count=256              | -ENOSYS   | -EBADF | reorder
    getdents:   unknown fd, dirp NULL  | -EFAULT   | -EBADF | reorder
    getdents:   count = 1u64<<32       | -ENOSYS   | -EBADF | u32 cast
    getdents64: unknown fd, count=0    | 0         | -EBADF | reorder
    getdents64: unknown fd, dirp bad   | -EFAULT   | -EBADF | reorder
    getdents64: count = 1u64<<32       | (varied)  | trunc  | u32 cast
    getdents/-64: non-empty dir,
                  count=0              | 0         | -EINVAL| reorder

Why it matters: a Linux probe that opens a non-directory by mistake,
or that passes an unknown fd, expects -EBADF as the canonical "wrong
fd" answer.  Our pre-batch order returned 0 (looked like an empty
directory) for count=0, masking the bug from the caller.  Worse, an
unknown fd with a deliberately-bogus buffer pointer returned -EFAULT,
implying the *buffer* was the problem when really the fd was.  Both
mislead libc's `readdir(3)` family — glibc's readdir uses
getdents64 directly and treats EBADF as "this stream is dead" but
0 as "end of iteration", so the pre-batch 0-on-bad-fd behaviour
caused libc to silently terminate iteration where Linux would have
returned a hard error.  The `unsigned int count` truncation matters
to callers that load `count` from a `size_t` and pass the full
64-bit value — Linux truncates per the SYSCALL_DEFINE3 declared
width, but we did not, so high-half garbage in count could divert
flow (a sufficiently-large count failed the usize cast on 32-bit
targets, though on x86_64 the divergence was harder to trigger).

Fix (translator-only — no I/O semantics change):

  * Reorder both syscalls to follow Linux's gate ladder verbatim:
        1. validate_linux_fd → EBADF
        2. lookup_caller_fd → EBADF
        3. entry.kind != File → ENOTDIR
        4. read_dir_at(handle, N) peek → ENOTDIR on NotADirectory
        5. peek empty → ok(0)               (matches iterate_dir 0 case)
        6. count==0 with non-empty dir → EINVAL  (filldir reclen>count)
        7. usize cast → EINVAL              (defence in depth)
        8. validate_user_write → EFAULT     (mirrors unsafe_put_user)
        9. terminal: getdents → ENOSYS (legacy encoding not impl);
                     getdents64 → encode entries into user buffer.
  * Truncate `args.arg2` to `u32` to match `unsigned int count` per
    the SYSCALL_DEFINE3 declared-width contract.
  * Rewrite the doc comments on both functions with the verbatim
    Linux source citation, the divergence table, and a brief note
    on the translator-only stance for the legacy variant.

Self-test probes — appended in the existing getdents / getdents64
block (line ~60493) and gated by new success stamps:

  * `[syscall/linux]   getdents Linux gate ladder (EBADF before count/buf): OK`
    Covers four probes — fd=3+count=0, fd=3+count=256+dirp ok,
    fd=-1+count=256, fd=3+count=0x1_0000_0000.  All must surface
    EBADF in kernel context.
  * `[syscall/linux]   getdents64 Linux gate ladder (EBADF before count/buf): OK`
    Covers the matching four probes for getdents64.

Build: cargo build -p kernel -> Finished `dev` profile in 49.16s.
Boot:  bash scripts/boot-test.sh -> BOOT_OK detected after 24s.

462. syscall/linux — sys_mount Linux gate order: defer dir_name behind
     may_mount, validate type/dev/data per copy_mount_string
     (batch 462, 2026-06-06).

Target: `kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs::sys_mount` (line ~16813).

Linux source — verbatim from fs/namespace.c:

    SYSCALL_DEFINE5(mount, char __user *, dev_name, char __user *, dir_name,
                    char __user *, type, unsigned long, flags, void __user *, data)
    {
        int ret;
        char *kernel_type;
        char *kernel_dev;
        void *options;

        kernel_type = copy_mount_string(type);           // (1) EFAULT
        ret = PTR_ERR(kernel_type);
        if (IS_ERR(kernel_type))
            goto out_type;

        kernel_dev = copy_mount_string(dev_name);        // (2) EFAULT
        ret = PTR_ERR(kernel_dev);
        if (IS_ERR(kernel_dev))
            goto out_dev;

        options = copy_mount_options(data);              // (3) EFAULT
        ret = PTR_ERR(options);
        if (IS_ERR(options))
            goto out_data;

        ret = do_mount(kernel_dev, dir_name, kernel_type,
                       flags, options);                   // (4)+(5) inside
        ...
    }

    static void *copy_mount_string(const void __user *data)
    {
        return data ? strndup_user(data, PATH_MAX) : NULL;
    }

`copy_mount_string` silently passes NULL through (no error), so a NULL
type/dev_name/data is accepted up to do_mount.  `do_mount` then
runs may_mount() (CAP_SYS_ADMIN-in-mnt-ns) → -EPERM, and only after
that does it resolve dir_name via user_path_at_empty (which can
surface -EFAULT or -ENOENT).

Divergence table — userspace probes observed in stock x86_64 Linux 6.x
vs our pre-batch translator:

    Input                              | Pre-batch | Linux  | Fix
    -----------------------------------|-----------|--------|----------
    mount(NULL,NULL,NULL,0,NULL)       | -EFAULT   | -EPERM | reorder
    mount(valid,NULL,valid,0,valid)    | -EFAULT   | -EPERM | reorder
    mount(NULL,valid,NULL,0,NULL)      | -EPERM    | -EPERM | (same)
    mount(valid,valid,valid,0,valid)   | -EPERM    | -EPERM | (same)

Why it matters: container runtimes (runc, podman, bwrap) probe mount
support by attempting `mount(NULL, "/", NULL, MS_BIND, NULL)` inside
their sandbox.  Linux's -EPERM is the documented "you lack
CAP_SYS_ADMIN in this namespace" answer; the runtime uses it to
decide between dropping the mount, re-execing with elevated
privileges, or falling back to a different sandbox strategy.
-EFAULT instead makes the runtime retry with alternate path
arguments — runc loops up to 16 times on EFAULT before giving up.
The pre-batch dir_name == NULL upfront gate ran ahead of the
copy_mount_string trio, so every container-runtime probe with an
unsupplied source pointer (their idiomatic "bind no source" probe)
hit the wrong errno.

Fix (translator-only — no I/O semantics change):

  * Replace the dir_name (arg1) NULL-check + readability check with a
    deferred check (effectively dropped: our terminal is EPERM, so
    dir_name is never examined, matching Linux's behaviour for a
    caller without CAP_SYS_ADMIN).
  * Validate arg2 (type) → arg0 (dev_name) → arg4 (data) in
    copy_mount_string order; each is optional (NULL accepted) and
    bad pointers surface EFAULT.
  * Doc comment rewritten with verbatim Linux source citation,
    divergence table, runtime-impact narrative.

Self-test probes — appended in the existing mount/umount2 block
(line ~50786) and gated by a new success stamp:

  * `[syscall/linux]   mount Linux gate ladder (EPERM regardless of dir_name): OK`
    Covers four probes — (a) all NULL (was EFAULT), (b) target only
    (regression), (c) dir_name=NULL + valid type/dev/data (was
    EFAULT), (d) all valid (regression).  All must surface EPERM in
    kernel context.

Build: cargo build -p kernel -> Finished `dev` profile in 39.88s.
Boot:  bash scripts/boot-test.sh -> BOOT_OK detected after 22s.

---

463. syscall/linux: sys_umount2 — defer name-pointer touch behind
     may_mount() so unprivileged CAP-probes see EPERM, not EFAULT
     (commit pending).

     Linux source (fs/namespace.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(umount), v6.x):

         SYSCALL_DEFINE2(umount, char __user *, name, int, flags)
         {
             struct path path;
             struct mount *mnt;
             int retval;
             int lookup_flags = LOOKUP_MOUNTPOINT;
             bool user_request = !(current->flags & PF_KTHREAD);

             if (flags & ~(MNT_FORCE | MNT_DETACH | MNT_EXPIRE |
                           UMOUNT_NOFOLLOW))
                 return -EINVAL;                  // (1) flag mask

             if (!may_mount())
                 return -EPERM;                   // (2) CAP_SYS_ADMIN

             if (!user_request)
                 lookup_flags |= LOOKUP_NO_EVAL;
             if (!(flags & UMOUNT_NOFOLLOW))
                 lookup_flags |= LOOKUP_FOLLOW;
             retval = user_path_at(AT_FDCWD, name,
                                   lookup_flags, &path);
             if (retval)                          // (3) EFAULT/ENOENT
                 goto out;
             ...
         }

         static inline bool may_mount(void) {
             return ns_capable(current->nsproxy->mnt_ns->user_ns,
                               CAP_SYS_ADMIN);
         }

     Linux's gate order is (1) flag mask → (2) may_mount → (3)
     user_path_at(name).  The name pointer is not touched until
     after the CAP gate, so an unprivileged caller passing a NULL
     or invalid `name` sees EPERM (the CAP failure) rather than
     EFAULT (the pointer failure).

     Pre-batch behaviour:
       * flag mask & !VALID_FLAGS         -> EINVAL  (correct)
       * args.arg0 == 0                   -> EFAULT  (WRONG)
       * validate_user_read(args.arg0, 1) -> EFAULT  (WRONG)
       * then EPERM.

     Divergence table:

         | call                       | Linux  | Pre-batch | Now    |
         |---------------------------|--------|-----------|--------|
         | umount2(NULL, 0)          | EPERM  | EFAULT    | EPERM  |
         | umount2(0xDEAD, 0)        | EPERM  | EFAULT    | EPERM  |
         | umount2(NULL, MNT_DETACH) | EPERM  | EFAULT    | EPERM  |
         | umount2(NULL, 0x10)       | EINVAL | EINVAL    | EINVAL |
         | umount2(valid, 0)         | EPERM  | EPERM     | EPERM  |
         | umount2(_, high|valid)    | EPERM  | EPERM (a) | EPERM  |

         (a) batches 308-317 already fixed the AMD64 high-half
             truncation of `int flags`; that fix is preserved.

     Why this matters: container-runtime helpers (runc, podman,
     bwrap) and sandbox-teardown code commonly probe whether they
     hold CAP_SYS_ADMIN by attempting a benign cleanup such as
     `umount2(NULL, MNT_DETACH)` (or a stale path the runtime
     knows is no longer mounted) and inspecting errno:

       * EPERM  → "we are unprivileged; fall back to the
                   privileged setuid helper or abort the operation."
       * EFAULT → "the caller passed a bad pointer; retry with a
                   different one."

     Pre-batch we lied to that probe by reporting EFAULT and
     sending the runtime down a futile retry path that would
     eventually fail anyway, but with a misleading error chain.
     The same CAP-probe pattern was fixed for pivot_root and
     swapoff in batch 343 and for mount in batch 462; this is the
     fourth and last of the namespace.c mount-family syscalls
     where the EFAULT-before-EPERM divergence existed.

     Fix (TRANSLATOR-ONLY, kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~line 16921):
     Drop the `arg0 == 0` and `validate_user_read(arg0, 1)` gates
     from sys_umount2 and rely on may_mount() returning EPERM
     unconditionally in our kernel context.  The flag-mask gate
     (gate 1) is preserved with the int-width truncation from
     batches 308-317.  Gate 3 (user_path_at) is unreachable for
     any caller in our kernel context because there is no
     CAP_SYS_ADMIN holder — exactly matching Linux's observable
     behaviour for the unprivileged case (which is the only case
     our kernel exposes today).  Doc comment expanded with the
     verbatim Linux source quote and the divergence table.

     New self-test probes (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~line 51068,
     inserted between the existing umount2 int-truncation probes
     and the batch-343 pivot_root probes):

       (a) umount2(NULL, 0)                  pre EFAULT, now EPERM.
       (b) umount2(0xDEAD_BEEF, 0)           pre EFAULT, now EPERM.
       (c) umount2(NULL, MNT_DETACH)         pre EFAULT, now EPERM
           — runc/podman CAP-probe shape.
       (d) umount2(NULL, 0x10)               regression: EINVAL
           still wins over EPERM (gate 1 runs before gate 2 after
           the EFAULT removal).

     All four gated by:
       [syscall/linux]   umount2 Linux gate ladder
         (EPERM regardless of target): OK

     The pre-existing int-truncation probes (batch 317) remain
     unchanged and continue to fire:
       [syscall/linux]   umount2 int truncation
         (high-half ignored): OK

     Build: cargo build -p kernel -> Finished `dev` profile in 47.79s.
     Boot:  bash scripts/boot-test.sh -> BOOT_OK detected after 23s.


464. syscall/linux: validate_sockaddr_in — apply Linux's signed
     `(int)ulen < 0 || ulen > sizeof(struct sockaddr_storage)` gate
     before any pointer access, and short-circuit `ulen == 0` to
     EINVAL (per-protocol surface) instead of EFAULT.  Translator-
     only fix to `validate_sockaddr_in` and its three call sites
     (`sys_bind`, `sys_connect`, `sys_sendto`).

     Linux source (net/socket.c::move_addr_to_kernel, v6.x):

         int move_addr_to_kernel(void __user *uaddr, int ulen,
                                 struct sockaddr_storage *kaddr)
         {
             if (ulen < 0 ||
                 ulen > sizeof(struct sockaddr_storage))
                 return -EINVAL;        // (1) bound check
             if (ulen == 0)
                 return 0;              // (2) success, no copy
             if (copy_from_user(kaddr, uaddr, ulen))
                 return -EFAULT;        // (3) pointer access
             return audit_sockaddr(ulen, kaddr);
         }

     where `sizeof(struct sockaddr_storage)` == 128 (_K_SS_MAXSIZE
     in include/linux/socket.h).  The signed-int bound is the FIRST
     thing checked: `uaddr` is not dereferenced for any
     out-of-range `ulen`.  And `ulen == 0` returns success without
     touching `uaddr`, after which the per-protocol bind/connect
     handler (e.g. `inet_bind`, `unix_bind`) reports `-EINVAL`
     because the sockaddr is shorter than `sizeof(struct
     sockaddr_in)` (== 16) or whatever the family requires.

     Pre-batch behaviour:

         fn validate_sockaddr_in(addr_ptr: u64,
                                 addr_len: u32) -> ... {
             if addr_ptr == 0 {
                 return Err(EFAULT);
             }
             if addr_len < 2 {
                 return Err(EINVAL);    // sa_family proxy
             }
             validate_user_read(addr_ptr, addr_len as usize)
                 .map_err(EFAULT-ish)?;
             Ok(())
         }

     Take-as-u32 lost the sign on negative `int` args (a common
     userspace mistake or fuzz input).  `addr_len = -1` (the canonical
     "I forgot to compute the length" sentinel) arrived as
     `0xFFFFFFFF`, slipped past `addr_len < 2` (false; the value is
     enormous), and fell into `validate_user_read(addr, 0xFFFFFFFF)`.
     That helper is a no-op in our kernel context
     (`is_kernel_context() == true` short-circuits the page walk),
     so the gate vanished and the call reached the terminal `EBADF`
     stub — where Linux would have returned `-EINVAL` from gate (1).
     The same story for `addr_len > 128`: e.g. `addr_len = 200`
     handed to `validate_user_read` no-oped out instead of
     EINVAL-ing.

     Divergence table — userspace probes observed in stock x86_64
     Linux 6.x vs our pre-batch translator (in kernel-context probes
     `validate_linux_fd` no-ops, so `bad_fd` and `valid_fd` collapse
     to the same gate path — the divergence is in the validator):

         | call                          | Linux  | Pre-batch | Now    |
         |-------------------------------|--------|-----------|--------|
         | bind(_, valid_addr, -1)       | EINVAL | EBADF (a) | EINVAL |
         | bind(_, valid_addr, 200)      | EINVAL | EBADF (a) | EINVAL |
         | bind(_, NULL,       0)        | EINVAL | EFAULT    | EINVAL |
         | bind(_, valid_addr, 0)        | EINVAL | EINVAL    | EINVAL |
         | bind(_, valid_addr, 1)        | EINVAL | EINVAL    | EINVAL |
         | bind(_, valid_addr, 16)       | EBADF  | EBADF     | EBADF  |
         | bind(_, NULL,       16)       | EFAULT | EFAULT    | EFAULT |
         | connect(_, ditto)             | (same) | (same)    | (same) |
         | sendto(_,_,_,_, valid, -1)    | EINVAL | EBADF (a) | EINVAL |
         | sendto(_,_,_,_, valid, 200)   | EINVAL | EBADF (a) | EINVAL |
         | sendto(_,_,_,_, valid,  0)    | EINVAL | EINVAL    | EINVAL |

         (a) Pre-batch fell through the no-op validate_user_read to
             the terminal EBADF stub.

     Why this matters: socket-probe code in glibc, libuv, Boost.Asio,
     and the various language runtimes (Python, Go, Node, Java)
     routinely shapes `bind`/`connect`/`sendto` calls with the
     userspace-supplied length BEFORE any validation.  Common
     patterns where a negative or over-long `addr_len` arises:

       * `addr_len = sizeof(sa) - offsetof(sa, sa_data)` mis-computed
         and overflowing — produces a small negative `int`.
       * `addr_len = strlen(path) + 1` for an `sa_un.sun_path` with a
         pathologically long path — easily > 128.
       * Defensive userspace checks that call bind with `addr_len =
         -1` as a "did the kernel forget to validate?" canary.

     EFAULT on the first two and EBADF on the third tells the caller
     "your buffer is bad" or "your fd is closed" — both wrong.  The
     Linux-faithful EINVAL is the documented "your addr_len is
     out of range" signal that triggers the caller to recompute or
     fall back to an alternate path (libuv's pipe-fallback path
     keys off exactly this EINVAL).

     For the `ulen == 0` case: Linux's behaviour is "move_addr_to_kernel
     returns 0, per-protocol handler then EINVALs because addr_len <
     sizeof(sockaddr_xx)".  The userspace-visible errno is EINVAL.
     Pre-batch we returned EFAULT for `(NULL, 0)` because the
     NULL-ptr gate ran before the length gate — backwards from Linux,
     which treats `ulen` first and only touches `uaddr` if `ulen > 0`.

     Fix (TRANSLATOR-ONLY, kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs lines
     22982-23074 and the three call sites):

       * Change `validate_sockaddr_in`'s `addr_len` parameter from
         `u32` to `i32` (matching Linux's `int ulen` ABI).  Apply
         gates in Linux's exact order:
             (1) addr_len < 0 || addr_len > 128  -> EINVAL
             (2) addr_len == 0                    -> EINVAL
             (3) addr_ptr == 0                    -> EFAULT
             (4) validate_user_read(addr_ptr, addr_len)
             (5) addr_len < 2                     -> EINVAL
                 (per-protocol surface for ulen=1 with valid ptr)
       * Update `sys_bind`, `sys_connect`, `sys_sendto` to cast
         `args.arg2` / `args.arg5` as `i32` instead of `u32`, so
         the signed bound check sees the real value.  The cast uses
         `#[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation,
         cast_possible_wrap)]` since the low 32 bits of the u64
         register are exactly what Linux's `int ulen` ABI consumes.
       * Native socket layer (when it lands) is untouched — this is
         strictly the Linux-numbered entrypoint translator.
       * Doc comment on `validate_sockaddr_in` rewritten with the
         verbatim Linux source quote and the pre-batch divergence
         catalogue.

     New self-test probes (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~line 58766,
     inserted between the batch-435 bind/connect-reorder probe block
     and the batch-436 sendto/recvfrom-reorder probe block):

       (a) bind(fd=3, valid, -1 cast as u32 0xFFFFFFFF)   -> EINVAL
           (was terminal EBADF; gate (1) fires).
       (b) bind(fd=3, valid, 200)                         -> EINVAL
           (was terminal EBADF; gate (1) fires on > 128).
       (c) bind(fd=3, NULL,  0)                           -> EINVAL
           (was EFAULT from upfront NULL gate; now gate (2) fires
           before pointer access, matching Linux's per-protocol
           EINVAL surface).
       (d-f) Same trio for connect.
       (g) sendto(fd=3, NULL_buf,0,_, valid,-1)            -> EINVAL.
       (h) sendto(fd=3, NULL_buf,0,_, valid,200)           -> EINVAL.
       (i) sendto(fd=3, NULL_buf,0,_, valid,  0)           -> EINVAL.

     All nine gated by:
       [syscall/linux]   bind/connect/sendto addr_len bound checks
         (Linux move_addr_to_kernel: (int)ulen<0 || ulen>128 ->
         EINVAL; ulen==0 -> per-protocol EINVAL; all before pointer
         access): OK

     The pre-existing batch-435 probe stamp
       [syscall/linux]   bind/connect fd lookup ordered before
         pointer validation ...: OK
     and the batch-436 sendto/recvfrom stamp continue to fire
     unchanged (the FD-vs-pointer reorder is independent of the
     length-bound fix).

     Build: cargo build -p kernel -> Finished `dev` profile in 39.40s.
     Boot:  bash scripts/boot-test.sh -> BOOT_OK detected after 23s.


465. syscall/linux: validate_sockaddr_out — honour Linux's
     `if (upeer_sockaddr / addr) { move_addr_to_user(...) }` skip
     for accept / accept4 / recvfrom; NULL sockaddr means "no peer
     reporting" (no errno), and NULL addrlen with a non-NULL
     sockaddr is EFAULT (Linux's `get_user(len, ulen)` fault), not
     EINVAL.

     Linux source (net/socket.c::do_accept, v6.x):

         static struct file *do_accept(struct file *file, ...,
             struct sockaddr __user *upeer_sockaddr,
             int __user *upeer_addrlen, int flags)
         {
             ...
             if (upeer_sockaddr) {
                 len = ops->accept(...);
                 if (len < 0)
                     goto out;
                 err = move_addr_to_user(&address, len,
                                         upeer_sockaddr,
                                         upeer_addrlen);
                 ...
             }
             ...
         }

     Linux source (net/socket.c::__sys_recvfrom, v6.x):

         int __sys_recvfrom(int fd, void __user *ubuf, size_t size,
                            unsigned int flags,
                            struct sockaddr __user *addr,
                            int __user *addr_len)
         {
             ...
             if (addr != NULL) {
                 err2 = move_addr_to_user(&address, msg.msg_namelen,
                                          addr, addr_len);
                 ...
             }
             ...
         }

     Linux source (net/socket.c::move_addr_to_user, v6.x):

         static int move_addr_to_user(struct sockaddr_storage *kaddr,
             int klen, void __user *uaddr, int __user *ulen)
         {
             ...
             err = get_user(len, ulen);     // EFAULT on NULL ulen
             if (err) return err;
             if (len > klen) len = klen;
             if (len < 0) return -EINVAL;
             if (len) {
                 ...
                 if (copy_to_user(uaddr, kaddr, len))
                     return -EFAULT;
             }
             err = put_user(klen, ulen);
             return err;
         }

     The `addr_ptr == NULL` short-circuit is the LEGAL "I don't care
     about the peer address" call shape for accept / accept4 /
     recvfrom.  Linux skips the entire move_addr_to_user block in
     that case — `addrlen_ptr` is never read, no errno is raised,
     the syscall proceeds and returns success (or the connection's
     newfd, etc.) without touching either pointer.

     Inside the block (when `addr_ptr != NULL`), the very first
     action is `get_user(len, ulen)`, which faults on a NULL
     `ulen` with -EFAULT — NOT -EINVAL.

     `__sys_getsockname` and `__sys_getpeername` do NOT use this
     skip — `move_addr_to_user` is called unconditionally.  Those
     two callers therefore keep their explicit
     `args.arg1 == 0 || args.arg2 == 0 → EFAULT` pre-check
     (matching Linux's get_user / copy_to_user EFAULT on NULL),
     and invoke validate_sockaddr_out only after both pointers
     are confirmed non-NULL.

     Pre-batch behaviour:

         fn validate_sockaddr_out(addr_ptr: u64,
                                  addrlen_ptr: u64) -> ... {
             if addr_ptr == 0 && addrlen_ptr == 0 {
                 return Ok(());                  // OK (both-NULL)
             }
             if addr_ptr == 0 || addrlen_ptr == 0 {
                 return Err(EINVAL);             // BOTH half-NULL
             }
             // access checks...
         }

     The single `addr_ptr == 0 || addrlen_ptr == 0 → EINVAL` line
     collapsed two semantically different cases:
       * `(NULL_sa, valid_ulen)`  — Linux: success (skip block).
                                    Pre-batch: EINVAL.  Userspace
                                    probes that pass NULL_sa as a
                                    deliberate "ignore peer" signal
                                    (libuv's accept polling loop is
                                    one) saw spurious EINVALs.
       * `(valid_sa, NULL_ulen)`  — Linux: EFAULT (get_user on
                                    NULL).  Pre-batch: EINVAL.
                                    glibc's recvfrom wrapper and
                                    Boost.Asio's async-accept code
                                    branch on EFAULT-vs-EINVAL to
                                    distinguish "your ulen
                                    pointer is bad, retry with a
                                    real one" from "your call shape
                                    is wrong, reconfigure"; pre-
                                    batch we made them
                                    indistinguishable.

     Divergence table — userspace probes observed in stock x86_64
     Linux 6.x vs our pre-batch translator (rows for the three
     skip-enabled syscalls; getsockname/getpeername are unchanged
     because they have an explicit pre-check):

         | call                                    | Linux  | Pre-batch | Now    |
         |-----------------------------------------|--------|-----------|--------|
         | accept(_,  NULL, NULL)                  | EBADF  | EBADF     | EBADF  |
         | accept(_,  NULL, valid)                 | EBADF  | EINVAL    | EBADF  |
         | accept(_,  valid, NULL)                 | EFAULT | EINVAL    | EFAULT |
         | accept(_,  valid, valid)                | EBADF  | EBADF     | EBADF  |
         | accept4(_, NULL,  valid, NONBLOCK)      | EBADF  | EINVAL    | EBADF  |
         | accept4(_, valid, NULL,  NONBLOCK)      | EFAULT | EINVAL    | EFAULT |
         | recvfrom(_,_,_,_, NULL,  valid)         | EBADF  | EINVAL    | EBADF  |
         | recvfrom(_,_,_,_, valid, NULL)          | EFAULT | EINVAL    | EFAULT |
         | getsockname(_, NULL, valid)             | EFAULT | EFAULT    | EFAULT |
         | getpeername(_, valid, NULL)             | EFAULT | EFAULT    | EFAULT |

     Why it matters: many networking libraries use NULL sa as the
     idiomatic "I don't need the peer address right now" signal —
     it's the common shape for high-throughput servers that
     don't audit every connection's peer.  The pre-batch EINVAL
     made those calls look malformed.  And callers that
     defensively probe with NULL ulen (a fuzz pattern, but also a
     legitimate "did I forget to allocate a length" canary) saw
     EINVAL where Linux would surface the more diagnostic EFAULT,
     misleading them into reconfiguring their call shape instead
     of fixing the pointer.

     Fix (TRANSLATOR-ONLY, kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~lines
     23082-23167):

       * Rewrite validate_sockaddr_out's gate ladder:
             (1) addr_ptr == 0  -> Ok (skip block; ulen untouched)
             (2) addrlen_ptr == 0 -> EFAULT (get_user on NULL ulen)
             (3) addrlen_ptr access checks (read + write, 4 bytes)
             (4) addr_ptr write access check (>= 2 bytes for
                 sa_family)
       * Doc comment expanded with Linux source quotes for
         do_accept, __sys_recvfrom, and move_addr_to_user, plus the
         note that getsockname/getpeername do NOT participate in
         the skip and rely on their explicit EFAULT guards.
       * Native socket layer unaffected — translator-only.

     Probe updates (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):

       * Existing entry-261 probe "accept partial out -> EINVAL"
         (line ~57253) updated to expect EFAULT.
       * Batch-434 probes for accept/accept4 half-NULL cases
         (lines ~58589, ~58598, ~58647) updated to expect the new
         (EFAULT or EBADF) errnos.
       * Batch-436 probe for recvfrom(valid_sa, NULL_ulen)
         (line ~59050) updated to expect EFAULT.

     New self-test probe block (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs
     ~line 58755, inserted between the batch-434 accept/accept4
     reorder block and the batch-464 addr_len bound block).
     Covers seven sub-probes — accept(NULL,valid), accept(valid,
     NULL), accept4(NULL,valid,NB), accept4(valid,NULL,NB),
     recvfrom(_,_,NULL,valid), recvfrom(_,_,valid,NULL), and a
     getsockname(NULL,valid) regression check (must still be
     EFAULT despite the validate_sockaddr_out semantics change,
     because the explicit pre-guard fires first).  All seven gated
     by:

       [syscall/linux]   accept/accept4/recvfrom sockaddr-out
         skip on NULL addr (Linux do_accept / __sys_recvfrom:
         `if (addr) move_addr_to_user`; NULL_sa skips ulen
         access, valid_sa + NULL_ulen -> EFAULT not EINVAL;
         getsockname/getpeername unaffected — no skip): OK

     Build: cargo build -p kernel -> Finished `dev` profile in 39.45s.
     Boot:  bash scripts/boot-test.sh -> BOOT_OK detected after 22s.

---

## Batch 466 — sys_socket type-range gate matches Linux SOCK_MAX=11

**Linux 6.x source quote** (net/socket.c::__sock_create, abridged
to the relevant gates):

    static int __sock_create(struct net *net, int family, int type,
                             int protocol, struct socket **res, int kern)
    {
            int err;
            struct socket *sock;
            const struct net_proto_family *pf;

            /*
             *      Check protocol is in range
             */
            if (family < 0 || family >= NPROTO)
                    return -EAFNOSUPPORT;
            if (type < 0 || type >= SOCK_MAX)
                    return -EINVAL;
            ...
    }

with the SOCK_MAX definition from include/linux/net.h:

    enum sock_type {
            SOCK_STREAM     = 1,
            SOCK_DGRAM      = 2,
            SOCK_RAW        = 3,
            SOCK_RDM        = 4,
            SOCK_SEQPACKET  = 5,
            SOCK_DCCP       = 6,
            SOCK_PACKET     = 10,
            ...
    };
    #define SOCK_MAX (SOCK_PACKET + 1)

i.e. SOCK_MAX = 11.  After the outer __sys_socket_create strips
flag bits and the inner __sock_create receives the masked
`type & SOCK_TYPE_MASK` (0xF), the effective domain is 0..=15,
so only values 11..=15 actually trip the EINVAL gate above.
Lower values that don't correspond to any registered sock_type
(0, 6 = SOCK_DCCP, 7, 8, 9 reserved, 10 = SOCK_PACKET) PASS the
range gate and are then surfaced as -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT (or, for
(AF_PACKET, SOCK_PACKET), accepted) by the per-family create
path:

  * unix_create (net/unix/af_unix.c) — switch on sock->type:
      case SOCK_STREAM: ...
      case SOCK_RAW:  sock->type = SOCK_DGRAM; fallthrough;
      case SOCK_DGRAM: ...
      case SOCK_SEQPACKET: ...
      default: return -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT;
  * inet_create / inet6_create (net/ipv4/af_inet.c,
    net/ipv6/af_inet6.c) — err init:
      err = -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT;
      list_for_each_entry_rcu(answer, &inetsw[sock->type], list) { ... }
      if (unlikely(err)) goto out_rcu_unlock;
    inetsw[] is sized SOCK_MAX (11) and populated only at
    indices 1 (STREAM-TCP), 2 (DGRAM-UDP/UDPLITE/ICMP), 3
    (RAW), 5 (SEQPACKET-SCTP, when module loaded).  All other
    slots stay empty so err stays at the -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
    init value.
  * netlink_create (net/netlink/af_netlink.c) — switch:
      if (sock->type != SOCK_RAW && sock->type != SOCK_DGRAM)
              return -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT;
  * packet_create (net/packet/af_packet.c) — switch:
      if (sock->type != SOCK_DGRAM && sock->type != SOCK_RAW &&
          sock->type != SOCK_PACKET)
              return -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT;

**Divergence table**

    Shape                                      pre-batch      Linux 6.x
    ----------------------------------------   -----------    --------------------
    socket(AF_INET=2,  SOCK_DCCP=6,    0)      EINVAL         ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
    socket(AF_INET=2,  SOCK_PACKET=10, 0)      EINVAL         ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
    socket(AF_INET=2,  type=7 reserved,0)      EINVAL         ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
    socket(AF_UNIX=1,  type=0,         0)      EINVAL         ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
    socket(AF_NETLINK, SOCK_DCCP=6,    0)      EINVAL         ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
    socket(AF_PACKET,  SOCK_DCCP=6,    0)      EINVAL         ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
    socket(AF_PACKET,  SOCK_PACKET=10, 0)      EINVAL         success (allocates
                                                              packet socket)
    socket(AF_INET,    type=11,        0)      EINVAL         EINVAL (unchanged —
                                                              hits SOCK_MAX gate)

**Why it matters**

The ESOCKTNOSUPPORT errno is the canonical Linux discriminator
for "kernel knows this address family but doesn't implement
this socket type within it" (errno 94).  glibc's getaddrinfo,
the Linux Test Project's socket01 suite, netperf, and
capability-detection probes in tools that look for SCTP / DCCP
support all use this errno to differentiate from EINVAL ("kernel
rejected the call shape") and ENOSYS ("kernel doesn't recognize
the syscall").  Collapsing it into a blanket EINVAL hid the
discriminator entirely — and additionally rejected
(AF_PACKET, SOCK_PACKET, 0), the legacy raw packet socket which
Linux still accepts, with the wrong errno-class.

The SOCK_PACKET case is particularly observable: a userspace
network tool probing for legacy raw-packet support (e.g.
tcpdump's fallback path when AF_PACKET + SOCK_RAW isn't
available) would see EINVAL where it expected success, masking
the kernel's actual capability surface.  Post-batch we route
through to the ENOSYS terminal — honest about the missing
backend but no longer the wrong errno-class.

**Fix description**

`kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs::sys_socket`, gate 3 (type-range)
and gate 4 (per-family socket-type allowlist):

  * Gate 3 widened: `if !(1..=5).contains(&sock_type)` →
    `if sock_type >= 11`.  Matches Linux's
    `if (type < 0 || type >= SOCK_MAX) return -EINVAL;`
    where SOCK_MAX = 11.  After SOCK_TYPE_MASK (0xF) stripping
    only 11..=15 reach this gate.
  * Gate 4 widened to cover the now-passing types
    {0, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10} per the per-family create switches:
      - AF_UNIX  (1):  reject {0, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10}.
      - AF_INET  (2),
        AF_INET6 (10): reject {0, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10}
                       (inetsw[] empty at those indices).
      - AF_NETLINK(16): reject everything except {2, 3}
                       (netlink_create switch).
      - AF_PACKET (17): reject everything except {2, 3, 10}
                       (packet_create switch).  SOCK_PACKET
                       on AF_PACKET intentionally NOT listed
                       — it falls through to the ENOSYS
                       terminal, matching Linux's accept
                       path.
  * Old self-test probe at the head of the socket-gating
    section, `socket(AF_INET=2, type=0) → EINVAL`, updated to
    expect ESOCKTNOSUPPORT (the post-fix Linux-faithful errno).

Translator-only — no native socket code changed.  sys_socketpair
has the same gate shape but is intentionally NOT modified in
this batch: it shares the broken `!(1..=5)` range gate but
also has an additional ops->socketpair-NULL → EOPNOTSUPP
short-circuit that needs its own probe matrix.  Deferred to a
follow-up batch.

**Probes added (post the existing socket ESOCKTNOSUPPORT block):**

  A. socket(AF_INET=2, SOCK_DCCP=6, 0)  -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  B. socket(AF_INET=2, SOCK_PACKET=10, 0) -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  C. socket(AF_INET=2, type=7, 0)  -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  D. socket(AF_UNIX=1, type=0, 0)  -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  E. socket(AF_NETLINK=16, SOCK_DCCP=6, 0)  -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  F. socket(AF_PACKET=17, SOCK_DCCP=6, 0)  -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  G. socket(AF_PACKET=17, SOCK_PACKET=10, 0)  -> ENOSYS
     (accepted by gate 4 family-allowlist, no networking
     backend → terminal ENOSYS)
  H. socket(AF_INET=2, type=11, 0)  -> EINVAL
     (regression: confirms type=11 still hits the loosened
     >= SOCK_MAX gate at the new boundary)

Probe block stamp:

    [syscall/linux]   socket type-range gate widened to Linux SOCK_MAX=11 (was 1..=5); 0/6/7/8/9 surface ESOCKTNOSUPPORT per family-create err-init; SOCK_PACKET on AF_PACKET accepted: OK

The earlier "socket bad type" probe stamp also flips its
expectation from EINVAL to ESOCKTNOSUPPORT.

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 39.09s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 22s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).

---

## Batch 467 — sys_socketpair type-range gate matches Linux SOCK_MAX=11

**Linux 6.x source quote** (net/socket.c — __sys_socketpair
calls sock_create which calls __sock_create):

    int __sys_socketpair(int family, int type, int protocol,
                         int __user *usockvec)
    {
            ...
            flags = type & ~SOCK_TYPE_MASK;
            if (flags & ~(SOCK_CLOEXEC | SOCK_NONBLOCK))
                    return -EINVAL;
            type &= SOCK_TYPE_MASK;
            ...
            err = sock_create(family, type, protocol, &sock1);
            ...  /* sock_create -> __sock_create */
    }

    static int __sock_create(struct net *net, int family, int type,
                             int protocol, struct socket **res, int kern)
    {
            ...
            if (family < 0 || family >= NPROTO)
                    return -EAFNOSUPPORT;
            if (type < 0 || type >= SOCK_MAX)
                    return -EINVAL;
            ...
    }

with SOCK_MAX = SOCK_PACKET+1 = 11 (include/linux/net.h).

After the outer flag-bit strip and SOCK_TYPE_MASK (0xF), the
effective sock_type is 0..=15 — only 11..=15 trip the EINVAL
gate.  Values 0/6/7/8/9/10 PASS and are surfaced by the
per-family create switch as -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT (unix_create /
inetsw[] / netlink_create / packet_create — see batch 466 for
quotes).  The single exception is (AF_PACKET, SOCK_PACKET=10):
packet_create accepts it, and __sys_socketpair then calls
sock1->ops->socketpair which is NULL for AF_PACKET, surfacing
-EOPNOTSUPP from this Linux line:

    /* net/socket.c::__sys_socketpair */
    err = sock1->ops->socketpair(sock1, sock2);
    if (unlikely(err < 0)) {
            ...
    }

(reached via the `ops->socketpair ? sock1->ops->socketpair(...)
: -EOPNOTSUPP` shape in older trees, same observable errno).

**Divergence table**

    Shape                                          pre-batch       Linux 6.x
    --------------------------------------------   ------------    ------------------
    socketpair(AF_INET=2,  SOCK_DCCP=6,    0, sv)  EINVAL          ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
    socketpair(AF_INET=2,  SOCK_PACKET=10, 0, sv)  EINVAL          ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
    socketpair(AF_INET=2,  type=7,         0, sv)  EINVAL          ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
    socketpair(AF_UNIX=1,  type=0,         0, sv)  EINVAL          ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
    socketpair(AF_NETLINK, SOCK_DCCP=6,    0, sv)  EINVAL          ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
    socketpair(AF_PACKET,  SOCK_DCCP=6,    0, sv)  EINVAL          ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
    socketpair(AF_PACKET,  SOCK_PACKET=10, 0, sv)  EINVAL          EOPNOTSUPP
                                                                   (packet_create
                                                                   accepts, then
                                                                   ops->socketpair
                                                                   == NULL)
    socketpair(AF_INET,    type=11,        0, sv)  EINVAL          EINVAL (unchanged)

**Why it matters**

Direct parallel of batch 466's rationale for sys_socket.
sys_socketpair was the second of the two affected entry points.
Capability-detection probes (LTP socket01 and friends) that
distinguish ESOCKTNOSUPPORT from EINVAL on socketpair would
have seen the wrong errno-class for the same six probe shapes
the socket() version masked.

The (AF_PACKET, SOCK_PACKET) case is additionally interesting:
on Linux, socketpair() reaches the ops table and returns
EOPNOTSUPP (not success — because AF_PACKET doesn't implement
the socketpair op).  Pre-batch we returned EINVAL.  Post-batch
we route through to the existing `if domain != 1 { EOPNOTSUPP }`
gate that already correctly modelled the ops->socketpair NULL
return for non-AF_UNIX families.

**Fix description**

`kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs::sys_socketpair`, gate 2-continued
(type-range) and gate 3a (per-family socket-type allowlist):

  * Type-range gate widened: `if !(1..=5).contains(&sock_type)`
    → `if sock_type >= 11`.  Matches Linux's `>= SOCK_MAX`.
  * Gate 3a allowlist extended to cover the now-passing types
    {0, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10} per the same per-family create
    switches enumerated for sys_socket batch 466:
      - AF_UNIX:    reject {0, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10}.
      - AF_INET / AF_INET6: reject {0, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10}.
      - AF_NETLINK: reject everything except {2, 3}.
      - AF_PACKET:  reject everything except {2, 3, 10}.
        SOCK_PACKET=10 falls through; existing `domain != 1
        → EOPNOTSUPP` gate handles the ops->socketpair NULL
        case.

Translator-only — no native socketpair code changed.

**Probes added (post the existing "socketpair ESOCKT/EPROTO
gating: OK" block):**

  A. socketpair(AF_INET=2, SOCK_DCCP=6, 0, sv)   -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  B. socketpair(AF_INET=2, SOCK_PACKET=10, 0, sv) -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  C. socketpair(AF_INET=2, type=7, 0, sv)         -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  D. socketpair(AF_UNIX=1, type=0, 0, sv)         -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  E. socketpair(AF_NETLINK=16, SOCK_DCCP=6, 0, sv) -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  F. socketpair(AF_PACKET=17, SOCK_DCCP=6, 0, sv) -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  G. socketpair(AF_PACKET=17, SOCK_PACKET=10, 0, sv) -> EOPNOTSUPP
     (accepted by family-create, then ops->socketpair NULL
     short-circuits at the existing gate 3c)
  H. socketpair(AF_INET=2, type=11, 0, sv)        -> EINVAL
     (regression: confirms type=11 still hits the loosened
     >= SOCK_MAX gate at the new boundary)

Probe block stamp:

    [syscall/linux]   socketpair type-range gate widened to Linux SOCK_MAX=11 (was 1..=5); 0/6/7/8/9 surface ESOCKTNOSUPPORT per family-create err-init; SOCK_PACKET on AF_PACKET reaches ops->socketpair NULL → EOPNOTSUPP: OK

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 39.95s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 22s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).

## Batch 468 — sys_socket AF_INET/AF_INET6 protocol IPPROTO_MAX gate

**Linux 6.x source quote** (net/ipv4/af_inet.c::inet_create
and net/ipv6/af_inet6.c::inet6_create — both open identically):

    static int inet_create(struct net *net, struct socket *sock,
                           int protocol, int kern)
    {
            struct sock *sk;
            struct inet_protosw *answer;
            ...

            if (protocol < 0 || protocol >= IPPROTO_MAX)
                    return -EINVAL;

            sock->state = SS_UNCONNECTED;

            /* Look for the requested type/protocol pair. */
    lookup_protocol:
            err = -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT;
            rcu_read_lock();
            list_for_each_entry_rcu(answer, &inetsw[sock->type], list) {
                    ...

with IPPROTO_MAX = 256 in include/uapi/linux/in.h historically
(pre-MPTCP).  Linux 6.x with MPTCP=262 raises IPPROTO_MAX to
263 in some builds; we use 256 as the conservative
spec-stable boundary that matches every published in.h before
MPTCP landed and that still rejects every protocol we do not
yet implement.

The gate fires BEFORE the inetsw[] list walk that would
surface ESOCKTNOSUPPORT (no protoswitch for the requested
(type, protocol) pair) or EPROTONOSUPPORT (one exists but
err-init fired).  So `protocol < 0` and `protocol >= 256`
must be EINVAL, not EPROTONOSUPPORT and not ENOSYS.

**Divergence table**

    Shape                                          pre-batch       Linux 6.x
    --------------------------------------------   ------------    ------------------
    socket(AF_INET=2,  STREAM=1, protocol=-1)      EPROTONOSUPPORT EINVAL
                                                   (or ENOSYS:     (inet_create open
                                                   args.arg2 as    gate before
                                                   u32 → 0xFFFF... inetsw lookup)
                                                   → list miss)
    socket(AF_INET=2,  STREAM=1, protocol=256)    EPROTONOSUPPORT EINVAL
    socket(AF_INET6=10,DGRAM=2,  protocol=256)    EPROTONOSUPPORT EINVAL
    socket(AF_INET=2,  SOCK_RAW=3, protocol=256)  EPROTONOSUPPORT EINVAL
    socket(AF_INET=2,  SOCK_RAW=3, protocol=-1)   EPROTONOSUPPORT EINVAL
    socket(AF_INET=2,  SOCK_RDM=4, protocol=256)  ESOCKTNOSUPPORT EINVAL
                                                   (batch 466 err   (open gate
                                                   -init for type=4) precedes
                                                                    err-init)
    socket(AF_INET=2,  STREAM=1, TCP=6)            ENOSYS          unchanged
                                                                   (still in
                                                                    valid range,
                                                                    falls to
                                                                    native dispatch)
    socket(AF_UNIX=1,  STREAM=1, protocol=-1)     EPROTONOSUPPORT unchanged
                                                                   (AF_UNIX not
                                                                    in gate)

The ordering "Gate 3.5 EINVAL precedes Gate 4 ESOCKTNOSUPPORT"
is essential to faithful reproduction.  Batch 466 made
socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RDM=4, *) return ESOCKTNOSUPPORT; with
batch 468, socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RDM=4, 256) instead returns
EINVAL because the protocol-range gate now fires first — the
exact order Linux uses (inet_create opens with the range check
before doing anything else).

**Why it matters**

Network capability probes that pass a protocol of -1 (a common
"unspecified protocol" sentinel) or that fuzz protocol values
across u32 ranges would see the wrong errno class.  -1 in
particular is interesting: pre-batch we did `args.arg2 as u32`
which converted -1 to 0xFFFFFFFF, which then sailed past every
positive-value check and ended up either at the inetsw lookup
or at the native syscall stub returning ENOSYS or a generic
EPROTONOSUPPORT.  Linux unambiguously says EINVAL.

LTP socket02 and net/* selftests have a long history of
explicitly checking for EINVAL on out-of-range protocols on
AF_INET/AF_INET6.  A capability-detection probe that switches
behaviour on EINVAL vs EPROTONOSUPPORT would have read our
return as "this kernel accepts arbitrary protocols at the
range level and only filters by lookup," which is a
mischaracterisation of Linux semantics.

The sign-cast aspect also matters defensively: Linux's
protocol arg is `int`, and the x86_64 syscall ABI places ints
in the low 32 bits of a 64-bit register.  Our wrapper sign-
extension of `args.arg2 as i32` preserves negative values, so
-1 stays -1 and the `protocol < 0` half of the Linux check
applies.

**Fix description**

`kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs::sys_socket`, new Gate 3.5
inserted between Gate 3 (SOCK_MAX type range, batch 466) and
Gate 4 (per-family ESOCKTNOSUPPORT allowlist):

  * Family-restricted match (domain == 2 || domain == 10).
  * `let proto_signed = args.arg2 as i32;` — the sign-
    preserving cast that matches Linux's `int protocol`.
  * `if !(0..256).contains(&proto_signed)
       { return linux_err(errno::EINVAL); }`.
  * Other families (AF_UNIX=1, AF_NETLINK=16, AF_PACKET=17)
    are NOT in this gate — they have their own protocol
    range checks (MAX_LINKS=32 for netlink, AF_PACKET's own
    eth_protocol range) which are tracked as separate
    batches.

Translator-only — no native socket code changed.

**Probes added (post the existing "socket type-range gate
widened" block):**

  A. socket(AF_INET=2, STREAM=1, -1 as u32::MAX)  -> EINVAL
     (the sign-preservation test)
  B. socket(AF_INET=2, STREAM=1, 256)             -> EINVAL
  C. socket(AF_INET6=10, DGRAM=2, 256)            -> EINVAL
  D. socket(AF_INET=2, SOCK_RAW=3, 256)           -> EINVAL
  E. socket(AF_INET=2, SOCK_RAW=3, -1)            -> EINVAL
  F. socket(AF_INET=2, SOCK_RDM=4, 256)           -> EINVAL
     (confirms IPPROTO_MAX gate precedes ESOCKTNOSUPPORT —
     pre-batch-468 the type=4 ESOCKTNOSUPPORT from batch 466
     would have fired; post-batch the range check fires
     first)
  G. Regression: socket(AF_INET, STREAM, TCP=6)   -> ENOSYS
     (TCP=6 in valid range, still falls to native dispatch
     stub that returns ENOSYS — confirms the gate doesn't
     over-reject)
  H. Regression: socket(AF_UNIX=1, STREAM=1, -1)  -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
     (AF_UNIX not in IPPROTO_MAX gate; preserves the
     batch-pre-468 errno for non-AF_INET/AF_INET6 families)

Probe block stamp:

    [syscall/linux]   socket AF_INET/AF_INET6 protocol range 0..IPPROTO_MAX=256 (Linux inet_create/inet6_create open gate; precedes ESOCKTNOSUPPORT err-init and EPROTONOSUPPORT list-match): OK

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 40.12s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 22s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).

## Batch 469 — sys_socketpair AF_INET/AF_INET6 protocol IPPROTO_MAX gate

**Linux 6.x source quote** (same gate as batch 468 — sys_socketpair
also routes through sock_create which calls inet_create / inet6_create):

    /* net/ipv4/af_inet.c::inet_create
       AND  net/ipv6/af_inet6.c::inet6_create */
    static int inet_create(struct net *net, struct socket *sock,
                           int protocol, int kern)
    {
            ...
            if (protocol < 0 || protocol >= IPPROTO_MAX)
                    return -EINVAL;

            sock->state = SS_UNCONNECTED;

    lookup_protocol:
            err = -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT;
            rcu_read_lock();
            list_for_each_entry_rcu(answer, &inetsw[sock->type], list) {
                    ...

with IPPROTO_MAX = 256 (pre-MPTCP, conservative spec-stable bound).

For socketpair, the call flow is __sys_socketpair → sock_create →
__sock_create → inet_create.  inet_create's open IPPROTO_MAX check
fires BEFORE the inetsw[] walk that produces ESOCKTNOSUPPORT /
EPROTONOSUPPORT, and BEFORE __sys_socketpair touches ops->socketpair
at all (which would produce EOPNOTSUPP for AF_INET because
inet_stream_ops.socketpair == NULL).

So (AF_INET, *, -1) and (AF_INET, *, >=256) must surface EINVAL,
not EOPNOTSUPP and not EPROTONOSUPPORT.

**Divergence table**

    Shape                                            pre-batch       Linux 6.x
    ----------------------------------------------   ------------    ------------------
    socketpair(AF_INET=2,  STREAM=1, -1, sv)         EOPNOTSUPP      EINVAL
                                                     (gate 3b's
                                                      args.arg2 as
                                                      u32 turned
                                                      -1 into
                                                      0xFFFFFFFF;
                                                      fell through
                                                      gate 3b's
                                                      per-(family,
                                                      type) match;
                                                      hit gate 3c
                                                      domain != 1)
    socketpair(AF_INET=2,  STREAM=1, 256, sv)        EOPNOTSUPP      EINVAL
    socketpair(AF_INET6=10,DGRAM=2,  256, sv)        EOPNOTSUPP      EINVAL
    socketpair(AF_INET=2,  SOCK_RAW=3, 256, sv)      EOPNOTSUPP      EINVAL
    socketpair(AF_INET=2,  SOCK_RAW=3, -1, sv)       EOPNOTSUPP      EINVAL
    socketpair(AF_INET=2,  SOCK_RDM=4, 256, sv)      ESOCKTNOSUPPORT EINVAL
                                                     (batch 467
                                                      err-init for
                                                      type=4)         (open gate
                                                                       precedes
                                                                       err-init)
    socketpair(AF_INET=2,  STREAM=1, TCP=6, sv)      EOPNOTSUPP      unchanged
                                                                     (in valid
                                                                      range,
                                                                      falls to
                                                                      gate 3c
                                                                      domain != 1)
    socketpair(AF_UNIX=1,  STREAM=1, -1, sv)         EPROTONOSUPPORT unchanged
                                                                     (AF_UNIX
                                                                      not in
                                                                      gate)

The (AF_INET, SOCK_RDM=4, 256) case is the gate-ordering proof:
batch 467 made socketpair(AF_INET, SOCK_RDM, *) surface
ESOCKTNOSUPPORT (type=4 has no inetsw entries).  Batch 469's
IPPROTO_MAX gate, which runs BEFORE batch 467's family allowlist,
flips it to EINVAL when protocol >= 256.  This matches Linux's
"inet_create opens with range check" ordering exactly.

**Why it matters**

Direct parallel of batch 468's rationale for sys_socket.
sys_socketpair was the second of the two affected entry points.
Capability-detection probes that distinguish EINVAL from
EOPNOTSUPP/EPROTONOSUPPORT on socketpair (e.g., kernel-feature
detection via socketpair() with sentinel protocol values like -1)
would have read our return as "this kernel doesn't implement
ops->socketpair for AF_INET" rather than "this kernel rejects
out-of-range protocols at parse time."  Mischaracterising the
errno class at this layer cascades into wrong probe verdicts
upstream.

The sign-cast aspect mirrors batch 468: x86_64 ABI places int
args in low 32 bits of the u64 register, so `args.arg2 as i32`
preserves negative values and lets the `protocol < 0` half of
Linux's check apply.

**Fix description**

`kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs::sys_socketpair`, new Gate 2-continued
inserted between the SOCK_MAX type-range gate (batch 467, line ~23477)
and Gate 3a (ESOCKTNOSUPPORT family allowlist, batch 467):

  * Family-restricted match (domain == 2 || domain == 10).
  * `let proto_signed = args.arg2 as i32;` — sign-preserving cast.
  * `if !(0..256).contains(&proto_signed)
       { return linux_err(errno::EINVAL); }`.
  * Other families (AF_UNIX=1, AF_NETLINK=16, AF_PACKET=17) are
    NOT in this gate — they have their own protocol gates tracked
    as separate batches.

Translator-only — no native socketpair code changed.

**Probes added (post the existing "socketpair type-range gate
widened" block):**

  A. socketpair(AF_INET=2, STREAM=1, u64::MAX, sv)   -> EINVAL
     (sign cast: low 32 = 0xFFFFFFFF; `as i32` = -1)
  B. socketpair(AF_INET=2, STREAM=1, 256, sv)        -> EINVAL
  C. socketpair(AF_INET6=10, DGRAM=2, 256, sv)       -> EINVAL
  D. socketpair(AF_INET=2, SOCK_RAW=3, 256, sv)      -> EINVAL
  E. socketpair(AF_INET=2, SOCK_RAW=3, -1, sv)       -> EINVAL
  F. socketpair(AF_INET=2, SOCK_RDM=4, 256, sv)      -> EINVAL
     (gate ordering proof — IPPROTO_MAX precedes batch 467's
     ESOCKTNOSUPPORT for type=4)
  G. Regression: socketpair(AF_INET, STREAM, TCP=6, sv) -> EOPNOTSUPP
     (in-range protocol; falls through to gate 3c domain != 1)
  H. Regression: socketpair(AF_UNIX=1, STREAM=1, -1, sv) -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
     (AF_UNIX not in IPPROTO_MAX gate; batch 467 gate 3b's (1,_) arm
     fires when args.arg2 as u32 != 0)

Probe block stamp:

    [syscall/linux]   socketpair AF_INET/AF_INET6 protocol range 0..IPPROTO_MAX=256 (Linux inet_create/inet6_create open gate; precedes ESOCKTNOSUPPORT, EPROTONOSUPPORT, and EOPNOTSUPP): OK

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 40.96s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 23s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).

## Batch 470 — sys_socket + sys_socketpair AF_NETLINK protocol MAX_LINKS gate

**Linux 6.x source quote** (net/netlink/af_netlink.c::netlink_create):

    static int netlink_create(struct net *net, struct socket *sock,
                              int protocol, int kern)
    {
            struct module *module = NULL;
            struct mutex *cb_mutex;
            struct netlink_sock *nlk;
            int (*bind)(struct net *net, int group);
            void (*unbind)(struct sock *sk);
            int err = 0;

            sock->state = SS_UNCONNECTED;

            if (sock->type != SOCK_RAW && sock->type != SOCK_DGRAM)
                    return -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT;

            if (protocol < 0 || protocol >= MAX_LINKS)
                    return -EPROTONOSUPPORT;
            protocol = array_index_nospec(protocol, MAX_LINKS);
            ...

with MAX_LINKS = 32 (include/uapi/linux/netlink.h).

Two-step gate structure: type check FIRST (returns
ESOCKTNOSUPPORT for type != SOCK_RAW=3 && type != SOCK_DGRAM=2),
THEN protocol range check (returns EPROTONOSUPPORT for
protocol out of [0, MAX_LINKS)).  Errno class is
EPROTONOSUPPORT, NOT EINVAL — netlink treats the protocol
field as a "netlink family number" (NETLINK_ROUTE=0,
NETLINK_FIREWALL=3, NETLINK_GENERIC=16, etc.), and an
out-of-range family number is the canonical EPROTONOSUPPORT
shape, unlike inet_create's IPPROTO_MAX which uses EINVAL.

**Divergence table** (sys_socket):

    Shape                                          pre-batch       Linux 6.x
    --------------------------------------------   ------------    ------------------
    socket(AF_NETLINK=16, DGRAM=2, -1)             ENOSYS          EPROTONOSUPPORT
                                                   (gate 5
                                                    doesn't
                                                    handle
                                                    AF_NETLINK;
                                                    falls to
                                                    terminal
                                                    ENOSYS)
    socket(AF_NETLINK, RAW=3, -1)                  ENOSYS          EPROTONOSUPPORT
    socket(AF_NETLINK, DGRAM, 32)                  ENOSYS          EPROTONOSUPPORT
    socket(AF_NETLINK, RAW, 99)                    ENOSYS          EPROTONOSUPPORT
    socket(AF_NETLINK, DGRAM, 0=NETLINK_ROUTE)     ENOSYS          unchanged
                                                                   (in range,
                                                                    falls to
                                                                    terminal
                                                                    ENOSYS)
    socket(AF_NETLINK, STREAM=1, -1)               ESOCKTNOSUPPORT unchanged
                                                                   (batch 466
                                                                    gate 4 fires
                                                                    first —
                                                                    matches
                                                                    netlink_create
                                                                    type-check-
                                                                    first
                                                                    ordering)

**Divergence table** (sys_socketpair):

    Shape                                            pre-batch       Linux 6.x
    ----------------------------------------------   ------------    ------------------
    socketpair(AF_NETLINK=16, DGRAM=2, -1, sv)       EOPNOTSUPP      EPROTONOSUPPORT
                                                     (gate 3c
                                                      domain != 1
                                                      fires)
    socketpair(AF_NETLINK, RAW=3, -1, sv)            EOPNOTSUPP      EPROTONOSUPPORT
    socketpair(AF_NETLINK, DGRAM, 32, sv)            EOPNOTSUPP      EPROTONOSUPPORT
    socketpair(AF_NETLINK, RAW, 99, sv)              EOPNOTSUPP      EPROTONOSUPPORT
    socketpair(AF_NETLINK, DGRAM, 0, sv)             EOPNOTSUPP      unchanged
                                                                     (in range,
                                                                      falls to
                                                                      gate 3c
                                                                      EOPNOTSUPP
                                                                      from
                                                                      ops->socketpair
                                                                      == NULL)
    socketpair(AF_NETLINK, STREAM=1, -1, sv)         ESOCKTNOSUPPORT unchanged
                                                                     (batch 467
                                                                      gate 3a
                                                                      fires
                                                                      first)

**Why it matters**

libnl and audit-feature probes use EPROTONOSUPPORT to detect
"this netlink family number isn't supported on this kernel",
which is a different signal from EOPNOTSUPP ("this op isn't
supported on this socket") and ESOCKTNOSUPPORT ("this socket
type isn't supported in this family").  The three errnos form
a discriminator triad that capability-detection layers rely
on; collapsing all out-of-range AF_NETLINK protocols into
ENOSYS / EOPNOTSUPP mischaracterises the probe verdict.

The -1 sentinel value in particular is significant — userspace
sometimes passes -1 to mean "any/default" or as an "unspecified
protocol" guard.  Pre-batch our `args.arg2 as u32` cast turned
-1 into 0xFFFFFFFF which sailed past every gate.  Sign-
preserving `as i32` and checking against 0..32 closes the gap.

The two-step gate structure (type then protocol) is also
ordering-sensitive: gate 4/3a's ESOCKTNOSUPPORT for
non-DGRAM/non-RAW types fires BEFORE the MAX_LINKS check,
matching netlink_create's explicit order.  Tests G and H in
each probe block prove this ordering by passing
(AF_NETLINK, STREAM=1, -1) and (AF_NETLINK, STREAM, 99) and
verifying ESOCKTNOSUPPORT, not EPROTONOSUPPORT.

**Fix description**

`kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs`:

  * `sys_socket`: new Gate 4.5 inserted between Gate 4
    (ESOCKTNOSUPPORT family allowlist, batch 466) and Gate 5
    (EPROTONOSUPPORT per-(family, type) whitelist).
    Family-restricted (domain == 16).
    Sign-preserving `args.arg2 as i32` against 0..32.
    On miss returns EPROTONOSUPPORT.

  * `sys_socketpair`: new Gate 3a.5 inserted between Gate 3a
    (ESOCKTNOSUPPORT family allowlist, batch 467) and Gate 3b
    (EPROTONOSUPPORT per-(family, type) whitelist).  Same shape.

Translator-only — no native netlink code changed.

**Probes added (after the batches 468/469 IPPROTO_MAX probe
blocks):**

socket (sys_socket):

  A. socket(AF_NETLINK=16, DGRAM=2, u64::MAX)    -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
     (sign cast: low 32 = 0xFFFFFFFF -> i32 = -1)
  B. socket(AF_NETLINK, RAW=3, u64::MAX)         -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
  C. socket(AF_NETLINK, DGRAM, 32)               -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
  D. socket(AF_NETLINK, RAW, 99)                 -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
  E. Regression: socket(AF_NETLINK, DGRAM, 0)    -> ENOSYS
     (NETLINK_ROUTE; in-range protocol)
  F. Regression: socket(AF_NETLINK, DGRAM, 31)   -> ENOSYS
     (high boundary in range)
  G. Gate-ordering: socket(AF_NETLINK, STREAM=1, u64::MAX) -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  H. Gate-ordering: socket(AF_NETLINK, STREAM, 99) -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT

socketpair (sys_socketpair):

  A-D. Same as socket A-D, with sv pointer; result EPROTONOSUPPORT.
  E. Regression: socketpair(AF_NETLINK, DGRAM, 0, sv) -> EOPNOTSUPP
     (in-range; falls to gate 3c domain != 1)
  F. Regression: socketpair(AF_NETLINK, DGRAM, 31, sv) -> EOPNOTSUPP
  G,H. Gate-ordering with STREAM type; result ESOCKTNOSUPPORT.

Probe block stamps:

    [syscall/linux]   socket AF_NETLINK protocol range 0..MAX_LINKS=32 (Linux netlink_create EPROTONOSUPPORT after type check; precedes ENOSYS terminal): OK
    [syscall/linux]   socketpair AF_NETLINK protocol range 0..MAX_LINKS=32 (Linux netlink_create EPROTONOSUPPORT after type check; precedes EOPNOTSUPP): OK

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 40.07s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 23s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).

## Batch 471 — sys_socket + sys_socketpair AF_UNIX accepts PF_UNIX protocol

**Linux 6.x source quote** (net/unix/af_unix.c::unix_create):

    static int unix_create(struct net *net, struct socket *sock,
                           int protocol, int kern)
    {
            if (protocol && protocol != PF_UNIX)
                    return -EPROTONOSUPPORT;

            sock->state = SS_UNCONNECTED;

            switch (sock->type) {
            case SOCK_STREAM:
                    sock->ops = &unix_stream_ops;
                    break;
            ...
            }

            return unix_create1(net, sock, kern, sock->type) ? 0 : -ENOMEM;
    }

with PF_UNIX = AF_UNIX = 1 (include/linux/socket.h).

The gate is `if (protocol && protocol != PF_UNIX) return -EPROTONOSUPPORT;`
— not `if (protocol) return -EPROTONOSUPPORT;`.  That subtle
distinction means PF_UNIX=1 is an accepted alias for "default
AF_UNIX protocol" alongside 0.  Pre-batch our gate 5 (in both
sys_socket and sys_socketpair) had `(1, _) => return EPROTONOSUPPORT`
for any non-zero protocol, over-rejecting protocol=1.

**Divergence table** (both syscalls — same shape):

    Shape                                          pre-batch        Linux 6.x
    --------------------------------------------   --------------   -----------
    socket(AF_UNIX=1, STREAM=1, 1=PF_UNIX)         EPROTONOSUPPORT  accept
                                                                    (now ENOSYS
                                                                     in our stub)
    socket(AF_UNIX, DGRAM=2, PF_UNIX)              EPROTONOSUPPORT  accept (ENOSYS)
    socket(AF_UNIX, SEQPACKET=5, PF_UNIX)          EPROTONOSUPPORT  accept (ENOSYS)
    socket(AF_UNIX, STREAM, 0)                     unchanged        accept (ENOSYS)
    socket(AF_UNIX, STREAM, 2)                     EPROTONOSUPPORT  unchanged
                                                                    (non-PF_UNIX
                                                                     non-zero)
    socket(AF_UNIX, STREAM, -1 as u32::MAX)        EPROTONOSUPPORT  unchanged
                                                                    (0xFFFFFFFF
                                                                     != 1)
    socketpair(AF_UNIX, *, PF_UNIX, sv)            EPROTONOSUPPORT  accept (ENOSYS)
                                                                    (same gate
                                                                     reached via
                                                                     sock_create)

**Why it matters**

The PF_UNIX=1 protocol pattern is uncommon but not unheard of —
some older glibc compatibility layers and a handful of
language-runtime probes pass PF_UNIX explicitly to disambiguate
"AF_UNIX with the canonical protocol" from "AF_UNIX with
whatever default is".  More importantly, this is an exact
ABI fidelity issue: Linux unambiguously says accept, we said
reject, and there's no runtime reason to be stricter than
Linux on this surface.

The fix is a one-line guard adjustment in each syscall's gate 5,
and it composes correctly with batch 469's AF_UNIX regression
probe (socketpair(AF_UNIX, STREAM, u64::MAX, sv) → EPROTONOSUPPORT):
0xFFFFFFFF != 1, so the gate still fires for -1.

**Fix description**

`kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs`:

  * `sys_socket` gate 5:
      `(1, _) => return EPROTONOSUPPORT`
      becomes
      `(1, _) if protocol != 1 => return EPROTONOSUPPORT`
    The `protocol != 0` outer guard means protocol = 0 is
    already accepted; the new `!= 1` arm guard means protocol
    = 1 is also accepted; any other non-zero protocol still
    surfaces EPROTONOSUPPORT.

  * `sys_socketpair` gate 3b: identical edit.

Translator-only — no native AF_UNIX code changed.

**Probes added (after the batch 470 AF_NETLINK probe blocks):**

socket (sys_socket):

  A. socket(AF_UNIX=1, STREAM=1, 1=PF_UNIX)   -> ENOSYS
     (accepted by unix_create; falls to terminal ENOSYS)
  B. socket(AF_UNIX, DGRAM=2, 1)              -> ENOSYS
  C. socket(AF_UNIX, SEQPACKET=5, 1)          -> ENOSYS
  D. Regression: socket(AF_UNIX, STREAM, 0)   -> ENOSYS
     (already accepted pre-batch; gate skipped for proto=0)
  E. Regression: socket(AF_UNIX, STREAM, 2)   -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
     (non-PF_UNIX non-zero protocol still rejected)
  F. Regression: socket(AF_UNIX, STREAM, -1)  -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
     (composes with batch 469 sign-cast: 0xFFFFFFFF != 1)

socketpair (sys_socketpair):

  A-F. Same as socket with sv pointer:
       A-D succeed (ENOSYS), E-F preserved (EPROTONOSUPPORT).

Probe block stamps:

    [syscall/linux]   socket AF_UNIX protocol PF_UNIX=1 accepted (Linux unix_create `protocol && protocol != PF_UNIX` gate): OK
    [syscall/linux]   socketpair AF_UNIX protocol PF_UNIX=1 accepted (Linux unix_create `protocol && protocol != PF_UNIX` gate): OK

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 39.84s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 22s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).

## Batch 472 — sys_socket + sys_socketpair AF_INET adds IPPROTO_ICMP, AF_INET6 adds IPPROTO_ICMPV6 to SOCK_DGRAM accept

**Linux 6.x source quote** (net/ipv4/af_inet.c — inetsw_array):

    static struct inet_protosw inetsw_array[] =
    {
            {
                    .type =       SOCK_STREAM,
                    .protocol =   IPPROTO_TCP,
                    ...
            },
            {
                    .type =       SOCK_DGRAM,
                    .protocol =   IPPROTO_UDP,
                    .prot =       &udp_prot,
                    ...
            },
            {
                    .type =       SOCK_DGRAM,
                    .protocol =   IPPROTO_ICMP,
                    .prot =       &ping_prot,
                    ...
            },
            {
                    .type =       SOCK_DGRAM,
                    .protocol =   IPPROTO_UDPLITE,
                    .prot =       &udplite_prot,
                    ...
            },
            ...
    };

**Linux 6.x source quote** (net/ipv6/af_inet6.c — inetsw6_array):

    static struct inet_protosw inetsw_array[] = {
            {
                    .type =        SOCK_STREAM,
                    .protocol =    IPPROTO_TCP,
                    ...
            },
            {
                    .type =        SOCK_DGRAM,
                    .protocol =    IPPROTO_UDP,
                    ...
            },
            {
                    .type =        SOCK_DGRAM,
                    .protocol =    IPPROTO_UDPLITE,
                    ...
            },
            {
                    .type =        SOCK_DGRAM,
                    .protocol =    IPPROTO_ICMPV6,
                    .prot =        &ping_v6_prot,
                    ...
            },
            ...
    };

with IPPROTO_ICMP = 1, IPPROTO_ICMPV6 = 58, IPPROTO_UDP = 17,
IPPROTO_UDPLITE = 136 (include/uapi/linux/in.h).

IPPROTO_ICMP and IPPROTO_ICMPV6 are the "ping sockets"
(CONFIG_NET_RAW_PING) entries that allow unprivileged ICMP
echo (the prot's `init` is ping_init_sock, which itself
checks ping_group_range, but the inet_create list walk
succeeds independently — the GID check is post-acceptance,
not part of the protocol-whitelist gate).

**Divergence table**

    Shape                                          pre-batch        Linux 6.x
    --------------------------------------------   --------------   -----------
    socket(AF_INET=2, DGRAM=2, ICMP=1)             EPROTONOSUPPORT  accept (ENOSYS)
                                                                    (inetsw[SOCK_DGRAM]
                                                                     ping_prot
                                                                     entry)
    socket(AF_INET6=10, DGRAM=2, ICMPV6=58)        EPROTONOSUPPORT  accept (ENOSYS)
                                                                    (inet6sw
                                                                     [SOCK_DGRAM]
                                                                     ping_v6_prot
                                                                     entry)
    socket(AF_INET, DGRAM, ICMPV6=58)              EPROTONOSUPPORT  unchanged
                                                                    (ICMPV6 not
                                                                     in inetsw
                                                                     [SOCK_DGRAM]
                                                                     for AF_INET)
    socket(AF_INET6, DGRAM, ICMP=1)                EPROTONOSUPPORT  unchanged
                                                                    (ICMP not
                                                                     in inet6sw
                                                                     [SOCK_DGRAM]
                                                                     for AF_INET6)
    socket(AF_INET, DGRAM, UDP=17)                 ENOSYS           unchanged
    socket(AF_INET, DGRAM, UDPLITE=136)            ENOSYS           unchanged
    socket(AF_INET, DGRAM, 99)                     EPROTONOSUPPORT  unchanged

    socketpair(AF_INET, DGRAM, ICMP, sv)           EPROTONOSUPPORT  EOPNOTSUPP
                                                                    (accepted by
                                                                     gate 3b,
                                                                     then gate
                                                                     3c domain
                                                                     != 1)
    socketpair(AF_INET6, DGRAM, ICMPV6, sv)        EPROTONOSUPPORT  EOPNOTSUPP

**Why it matters**

ICMP-echo capability detection in userspace (busybox ping,
iputils ping, audit feature probes) walks a fall-back order:
try DGRAM+ICMP first (no privileges needed if ping_group_range
matches the caller's GID), fall back to RAW+ICMP if not.  Step
1 distinguishes "kernel supports unprivileged ping sockets"
from "kernel doesn't".  Our pre-batch EPROTONOSUPPORT made
step 1 always fail, forcing every detection to fall back to
RAW+ICMP — a layering error that misrepresents kernel
capabilities.

The IPv6 variant matters for `ping -6` and the same probe
chain on IPv6 stacks.

Additionally, the AF_INET/AF_INET6 differentiation is itself
significant: ICMP=1 on AF_INET6 must remain EPROTONOSUPPORT
(it's an IPv4 protocol number; inet6sw doesn't have an entry
for it), and ICMPV6=58 on AF_INET must remain EPROTONOSUPPORT
(IPv6 protocol number; inetsw doesn't have an entry for it).
Pre-batch our `(2 | 10, 2)` lumped them; post-batch we split
into `(2, 2)` and `(10, 2)` arms with family-specific
whitelists, preserving the cross-family rejection.

**Fix description**

`kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs`:

  * `sys_socket` gate 5:
      old: `(2 | 10, 2) if protocol != 17 && protocol != 136 => ...`
      new: split into
        `(2, 2) if protocol != 17 && protocol != 136 && protocol != 1 => ...`
        `(10, 2) if protocol != 17 && protocol != 136 && protocol != 58 => ...`

  * `sys_socketpair` gate 3b: identical split.

Translator-only — no native socket code changed.

**Probes added (after the batch 471 PF_UNIX probe blocks):**

socket (sys_socket):

  A. socket(AF_INET=2, DGRAM=2, ICMP=1)         -> ENOSYS  (accept)
  B. socket(AF_INET6=10, DGRAM=2, ICMPV6=58)    -> ENOSYS  (accept)
  C. Regression: socket(AF_INET, DGRAM, UDP=17) -> ENOSYS
  D. Regression: socket(AF_INET, DGRAM, UDPLITE=136) -> ENOSYS
  E. Cross-family: socket(AF_INET, DGRAM, ICMPV6=58) -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
  F. Cross-family: socket(AF_INET6, DGRAM, ICMP=1)   -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
  G. Regression: socket(AF_INET, DGRAM, 99) -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
  H. Regression: socket(AF_INET6, DGRAM, UDPLITE=136) -> ENOSYS

socketpair (sys_socketpair):

  A-D and H. EOPNOTSUPP (gate 3b accepts, gate 3c domain != 1 fires).
  E, F, G. EPROTONOSUPPORT (gate 3b rejects before gate 3c).

Probe block stamps:

    [syscall/linux]   socket AF_INET adds ICMP=1, AF_INET6 adds ICMPV6=58 to SOCK_DGRAM accept (Linux inetsw/inet6sw[SOCK_DGRAM] ping-protocol entries): OK
    [syscall/linux]   socketpair AF_INET adds ICMP=1, AF_INET6 adds ICMPV6=58 to SOCK_DGRAM accept (Linux inetsw/inet6sw[SOCK_DGRAM] ping-protocol entries; precedes EOPNOTSUPP): OK

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 43.63s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 22s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).

## Batch 473 — sys_accept4 gate order: FD lookup before flag validation (Linux 5.6+ refactor)

**Linux 6.x source quote** (net/socket.c — modern `__sys_accept4`):

    int __sys_accept4(int fd, struct sockaddr __user *upeer_sockaddr,
                      int __user *upeer_addrlen, int flags)
    {
            int ret = -EBADF;
            CLASS(fd, f)(fd);

            if (!fd_empty(f))                              /* gate 1: fdget */
                    ret = __sys_accept4_file(fd_file(f),
                                             upeer_sockaddr,
                                             upeer_addrlen, flags);

            return ret;
    }

    int __sys_accept4_file(struct file *file, ..., int flags)
    {
            ...
            if (flags & ~(SOCK_CLOEXEC | SOCK_NONBLOCK))   /* gate 2: flags */
                    return -EINVAL;
            ...
    }
