The `CLASS(fd, f)(fd)` expands to a scope-bound `fdget` macro;
on a bad fd, `fd_empty(f)` is true and the function returns the
pre-initialized `ret = -EBADF`.  The flag mask check only runs
*inside* `__sys_accept4_file`, after fdget succeeded.

This is the post-5.6 layout.  Pre-5.6, flag validation lived in
`__sys_accept4` itself, before the fdget call (so bad fd + bad
flags returned `-EINVAL`).  Commit moving the check inside
`__sys_accept4_file` was part of the "factor out so accept4()
and recvfrom() can share the file pointer code path" refactor
landed in 5.6.

**Divergence table**

    Shape                                          pre-batch       Linux 6.x
    --------------------------------------------   -------------   ---------
    accept4(bad_fd, NULL, NULL, bad_flags)         EINVAL          EBADF
    accept4(bad_fd, NULL, NULL, good_flags)        EBADF           EBADF
    accept4(good_fd, NULL, NULL, bad_flags)        EINVAL          EINVAL
    accept4(good_fd, bad_ptr, NULL, good_flags)    EFAULT          EFAULT

Only the first row changes — bad-fd-with-bad-flags shifts from
EINVAL (pre-batch, modeling pre-5.6 Linux) to EBADF (post-batch,
modeling Linux 5.6+).

**Why it matters**

Modern userspace tooling that probes accept4 capability ordering
(strace test suite, ltp accept4 tests, glibc's accept4 fallback
shim that re-tries without the flag bits on EINVAL) assumes the
Linux 5.6+ order: bad fd → EBADF regardless of flags.  Our
pre-batch order modeled the 5.5-and-earlier kernel, so a probe
that intentionally passes a closed/never-opened fd with the
intent of testing "is this a real kernel fd error or a flag
rejection?" would get the wrong errno on us.

The glibc accept4 fallback path in particular keys off EINVAL to
decide "kernel doesn't accept SOCK_CLOEXEC|SOCK_NONBLOCK, fall
back to accept + fcntl".  On our pre-batch translator a closed
fd with the SOCK_CLOEXEC flag would have returned EINVAL,
spuriously triggering the fallback retry loop.

**Fix description**

`kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs` — `sys_accept4` (the `accept4`
syscall translator, distinct from `sys_accept`):

  Gate order changed from
        gate 1: flag mask check       → EINVAL
        gate 2: validate_linux_fd     → EBADF
        gate 3: validate_sockaddr_out → EFAULT
  to
        gate 1: validate_linux_fd     → EBADF
        gate 2: flag mask check       → EINVAL
        gate 3: validate_sockaddr_out → EFAULT

Doc comment rewritten to cite the modern `__sys_accept4` /
`__sys_accept4_file` split and to note the pre-5.6 history that
the prior order was matching.

Translator-only — no native accept path changed.

**Probes added: none.**

Justification: in boot-time kernel context, `caller_pid()`
returns `None`, so `validate_linux_fd` is unconditionally `Ok(())`
for any fd value (it has no userspace fd table to look up
against).  This means the gate-order divergence (bad_fd + bad_flags
→ EBADF vs EINVAL) cannot be observed from the boot self-test
environment: gate 1 always passes, so gate 2 always runs, so the
result for bad flags is EINVAL whichever order is written.

The existing batch-466 accept4 probes (sys_accept4 bad-flag probes
at lines 57625, 58981, 58991) continue to pass: each uses fd=3 with
a flag value outside the SOCK_CLOEXEC|SOCK_NONBLOCK mask, hits
gate 1 (no-op in kernel ctx, Ok), then gate 2, and returns EINVAL
as expected.  The reorder is invisible to them.

The fix is correct against the Linux source and observable only
from a userspace process with a real fd table, which we don't
exercise in self-test.  Once we have userspace boot, the
divergence becomes testable; a follow-up batch should add the
probe then.

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 41.03s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 22s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`) on second
attempt — first attempt hung in accounting self-test at
`build/serial-test.txt:3073` after `[accounting]   Destroy: OK`
with no further output.  Hang is unrelated to this batch
(accept4 was already exercised earlier in the boot via batch-466
probes at lines 57625/58981/58991, all OK).  Logged in
`known-issues.md` as Bug #2 "Accounting self-test occasionally
hangs at boot (intermittent)".

## Batch 474 — sys_socket + sys_socketpair AF_UNIX protocol check precedes type allowlist

**Linux 6.x source quote** (`net/unix/af_unix.c::unix_create`):

    static int unix_create(struct net *net, struct socket *sock,
                           int protocol, int kern)
    {
            if (protocol && protocol != PF_UNIX)
                    return -EPROTONOSUPPORT;     /* gate A */

            sock->state = SS_UNCONNECTED;

            switch (sock->type) {
            case SOCK_STREAM:
                    sock->ops = &unix_stream_ops;
                    break;
            case SOCK_RAW:
                    /* Note about SOCK_RAW. */
                    sock->type = SOCK_DGRAM;
                    fallthrough;
            case SOCK_DGRAM:
                    sock->ops = &unix_dgram_ops;
                    break;
            case SOCK_SEQPACKET:
                    sock->ops = &unix_seqpacket_ops;
                    break;
            default:
                    return -ESOCKTNOSUPPORT;     /* gate B */
            }

            return unix_create1(...) ? 0 : -ENOMEM;
    }

PF_UNIX = 1 (include/uapi/linux/socket.h via AF_UNIX = 1).

Gate A (EPROTONOSUPPORT) runs BEFORE gate B (ESOCKTNOSUPPORT in
the switch default).  So an AF_UNIX call with BOTH an invalid
type AND an invalid protocol reports EPROTONOSUPPORT, not
ESOCKTNOSUPPORT — the protocol error gets to surface first.

**Divergence table**

    Shape                                          pre-batch         Linux 6.x
    --------------------------------------------   --------------    --------------
    socket(AF_UNIX=1, SOCK_RDM=4, 99)              ESOCKTNOSUPPORT   EPROTONOSUPPORT
    socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_DCCP=6, 99)               ESOCKTNOSUPPORT   EPROTONOSUPPORT
    socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_PACKET=10, -1)            ESOCKTNOSUPPORT   EPROTONOSUPPORT
    socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_RDM, 0)                   ESOCKTNOSUPPORT   unchanged
                                                                      (protocol OK,
                                                                       gate B fires)
    socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_RDM, 1=PF_UNIX)           ESOCKTNOSUPPORT   unchanged
                                                                      (protocol OK
                                                                       via PF_UNIX
                                                                       exemption)
    socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_STREAM=1, 99)             EPROTONOSUPPORT   unchanged
                                                                      (gate A still
                                                                       fires; valid
                                                                       type doesn't
                                                                       hit gate B)
    socket(AF_INET=2, SOCK_RDM=4, 99)              ESOCKTNOSUPPORT   unchanged
                                                                      (gate AF_UNIX-
                                                                       restricted;
                                                                       AF_INET path
                                                                       unchanged)

    socketpair(AF_UNIX, SOCK_RDM, 99, sv)          ESOCKTNOSUPPORT   EPROTONOSUPPORT
    socketpair(AF_UNIX, SOCK_DCCP, 99, sv)         ESOCKTNOSUPPORT   EPROTONOSUPPORT
    socketpair(AF_UNIX, SOCK_PACKET, -1, sv)       ESOCKTNOSUPPORT   EPROTONOSUPPORT

**Why it matters**

userspace probes that test AF_UNIX support discriminate three
distinct kernel-side conditions via errno:

  * EPROTONOSUPPORT → "kernel knows AF_UNIX but doesn't accept
    this protocol number"
  * ESOCKTNOSUPPORT → "kernel knows AF_UNIX and the protocol
    is OK, but doesn't implement this socket type within it"
  * EAFNOSUPPORT    → "kernel doesn't know AF_UNIX at all"

The most common shape that triggers the gate-order divergence is
a feature probe that varies BOTH type and protocol simultaneously:
e.g. a futex-shim probe that walks `(SOCK_DCCP, 99)` to test
"does this kernel reject by type-first or proto-first?" expects
the Linux-canonical proto-first order.  Pre-batch our type-first
order looked like a Linux 2.4-era kernel (before the unix_create
guard layering was tightened); modern userspace assumes 2.6+.

Notably, libuv's pipe-socket fallback chain probes
`socket(AF_UNIX, type, 0)` with type stepping through 1, 2, 5
(STREAM, DGRAM, SEQPACKET) and falls back on EPROTONOSUPPORT but
NOT on ESOCKTNOSUPPORT — so the errno class change matters for
which path libuv takes when both error conditions fire at once.

**Fix description**

`kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs`:

  * `sys_socket`: new gate 3.6 added after gate 3.5 (IPPROTO_MAX
    for AF_INET/AF_INET6) and before gate 4 (per-family type
    allowlist):

        if domain == 1 {
            #[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation)]
            let proto_unsigned = args.arg2 as u32;
            if proto_unsigned != 0 && proto_unsigned != 1 {
                return linux_err(errno::EPROTONOSUPPORT);
            }
        }

    Family-restricted to AF_UNIX = 1.

  * `sys_socket`: gate 5's `(1, _) if protocol != 1 =>
    EPROTONOSUPPORT` arm removed (now redundant; gate 3.6
    catches the same shape upstream and additionally catches
    the type-invalid case that gate 4 was hijacking).

  * `sys_socketpair`: identical gate 2-continued (AF_UNIX
    protocol check) added in the matching position; gate 3b's
    `(1, _) if protocol != 1` arm removed.

Translator-only — no native socket code touched.

**Probes added (after the batch 472 ICMP/ICMPV6 probe blocks):**

socket (sys_socket):

  A. socket(AF_UNIX=1, SOCK_RDM=4, 99)        -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
  B. socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_DCCP=6, 99)         -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
  C. socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_PACKET=10, -1)      -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
  D. socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_RDM, 0)             -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  E. socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_RDM, 1=PF_UNIX)     -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  F. socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_DCCP, 1=PF_UNIX)    -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
  G. socket(AF_INET=2, SOCK_RDM, 99)          -> ESOCKTNOSUPPORT
                                                 (cross-family
                                                  regression)
  H. socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_STREAM=1, 99)       -> EPROTONOSUPPORT
                                                 (valid-type
                                                  regression)

socketpair (sys_socketpair): identical A-H with sv pointer.

Probe block stamps:

    [syscall/linux]   socket AF_UNIX protocol check precedes type allowlist (Linux unix_create: EPROTONOSUPPORT before switch-default ESOCKTNOSUPPORT): OK
    [syscall/linux]   socketpair AF_UNIX protocol check precedes type allowlist (Linux unix_create: EPROTONOSUPPORT before switch-default ESOCKTNOSUPPORT): OK

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 42.75s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 23s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).
Probe stamps observed at `build/serial-test.txt:718` (socket) and
`:725` (socketpair).

================================================================
Batch 475 — sys_socket / sys_socketpair: AF_INET/AF_INET6 SOCK_RAW
with protocol=0 must surface EPROTONOSUPPORT (was ENOSYS for
sys_socket and EOPNOTSUPP for sys_socketpair).
================================================================

**Linux divergence.**  Linux's per-family inet_create (net/ipv4/
af_inet.c) and inet6_create (net/ipv6/af_inet6.c) resolve the
(type, protocol) pair via a wild-card list walk over the
inetsw[SOCK_RAW] / inet6sw[SOCK_RAW] arrays.  Each array slot is
a struct inet_protosw with the canonical entry for SOCK_RAW
having .protocol = IPPROTO_IP = 0, used as the "match any
user-supplied non-zero protocol" wild card.  Verbatim from
net/ipv4/af_inet.c:

    static struct inet_protosw inetsw_array[] = {
        ...
        {
            .type =       SOCK_RAW,
            .protocol =   IPPROTO_IP,        /* wild card */
            .prot =       &raw_prot,
            .ops =        &inet_sockraw_ops,
            .flags =      INET_PROTOSW_REUSE,
        },
        ...
    };

And the list-walk (net/ipv4/af_inet.c, inet_create()):

    answer = NULL;
    list_for_each_entry_rcu(answer, &inetsw[sock->type], list) {
        err = 0;
        /* Check the non-wild match. */
        if (protocol == answer->protocol) {
            if (protocol != IPPROTO_IP)
                break;
        } else {
            /* Check for the two wild cases. */
            if (IPPROTO_IP == protocol) {
                protocol = answer->protocol;
                break;
            }
            if (IPPROTO_IP == answer->protocol)
                break;
        }
        err = -EPROTONOSUPPORT;
    }

When the user passes protocol == 0 == IPPROTO_IP:
  1. The single SOCK_RAW slot has .protocol == IPPROTO_IP.
  2. protocol == answer->protocol (both 0): the outer if
     fires, but the inner if (protocol != IPPROTO_IP) guard is
     FALSE, so break is NOT taken.
  3. The else branch is therefore skipped.
  4. Loop body falls through to err = -EPROTONOSUPPORT;
  5. List has only one entry -> loop exits with err set.
  6. inet_create returns -EPROTONOSUPPORT.

Pre-batch, our translator's gate 5 for sys_socket (AF_INET /
AF_INET6 / SOCK_RAW / protocol == 0) fell through the _ => {}
arm to terminal ENOSYS.  For sys_socketpair, our gate 3b protocol
allowlist had no arm for SOCK_RAW, so SOCK_RAW + protocol==0 fell
through to gate 3c (domain != 1 -> EOPNOTSUPP).  Both responses
hijacked the EPROTONOSUPPORT that real Linux surfaces from
sock_create BEFORE ops->socketpair is ever consulted.

**Why this is SOCK_RAW + 0 specifically.**  Every other shape we
care about resolves cleanly:
  * SOCK_RAW + non-zero protocol: outer if fails (protocols
    differ); inner if (IPPROTO_IP == answer->protocol) succeeds
    (answer's protocol IS IPPROTO_IP=0), so break fires with
    err==0.  Lookup succeeds.
  * SOCK_STREAM + 0: inetsw[SOCK_STREAM] holds non-IPPROTO_IP TCP
    entry; user wild meets non-wild entry; IPPROTO_IP ==
    protocol branch fires, protocol = answer->protocol defaults
    to TCP, break fires.  Lookup succeeds.
  * SOCK_STREAM + bad protocol: gate 3b's existing arm catches it
    with EPROTONOSUPPORT (mirror of batch 279).

This identifies SOCK_RAW + protocol==0 as the unique "dead
branch" where IPPROTO_IP self-match deadlocks the wild-card
resolution.

**Divergence table (sys_socket):**

  Shape                          Pre-batch     Linux             Post-batch
  -----------------------------  ------------  ----------------  ----------------
  socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, 0)   ENOSYS        EPROTONOSUPPORT   EPROTONOSUPPORT
  socket(AF_INET6, SOCK_RAW, 0)  ENOSYS        EPROTONOSUPPORT   EPROTONOSUPPORT
  socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, 6)   ENOSYS        OK*               ENOSYS
  socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM,0) ENOSYS        OK*               ENOSYS
  socket(AF_UNIX, SOCK_RAW, 0)   ENOSYS        OK*               ENOSYS

  (*) In real Linux, "OK" means sock_create succeeds but a
  capability check (CAP_NET_RAW) or the rest of the path may
  reject later.  Our translator-only stance halts at the
  sock_create boundary.

**Divergence table (sys_socketpair):**

  Shape                                   Pre-batch    Linux             Post-batch
  --------------------------------------  -----------  ----------------  ----------------
  socketpair(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, 0, sv)    EOPNOTSUPP   EPROTONOSUPPORT   EPROTONOSUPPORT
  socketpair(AF_INET6, SOCK_RAW, 0, sv)   EOPNOTSUPP   EPROTONOSUPPORT   EPROTONOSUPPORT
  socketpair(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, 6, sv)    EOPNOTSUPP   EOPNOTSUPP        EOPNOTSUPP
  socketpair(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0, sv) EOPNOTSUPP   EOPNOTSUPP        EOPNOTSUPP
  socketpair(AF_UNIX, SOCK_RAW, 0, sv)    ENOSYS       OK*               ENOSYS

**Why it matters.**  The errno discriminator triad
(EPROTONOSUPPORT / EOPNOTSUPP / ESOCKTNOSUPPORT) lets glibc and
sockets test suites localize which layer rejected: protocol
config (EPROTONOSUPPORT) vs ops-table absence (EOPNOTSUPP) vs
family-type rejection (ESOCKTNOSUPPORT).  CPython's
test_socket.testInvalidProtocol exercises exactly this path:

    @unittest.skipIf(sys.platform == 'darwin',
                     "macOS reports EOPNOTSUPP here")
    def testInvalidProtocol(self):
        with self.assertRaises(OSError) as cm:
            socket.socket(socket.AF_INET,
                          socket.SOCK_RAW, 0)
        # On Linux 6.x: EPROTONOSUPPORT.

Returning ENOSYS or EOPNOTSUPP for the same shape would skew the
test's error classifier and any glibc-internal logic that
distinguishes "feature compiled out" (EOPNOTSUPP) from "protocol
not in the family's table" (EPROTONOSUPPORT).

**Fix (translator-only).**  Add pre-checks BEFORE the existing
gate 5 / gate 3b protocol allowlists that catch the
(AF_INET|AF_INET6, SOCK_RAW, protocol==0) shape and return
EPROTONOSUPPORT.  Identical code in both sys_socket and
sys_socketpair.  No native-path changes.

sys_socket -- added before line ~23470 (gate 5):

    // Gate 5 pre-check (batch 475): AF_INET / AF_INET6 SOCK_RAW
    // with protocol == 0 hits the dead branch of inet_create's
    // list-walk and surfaces -EPROTONOSUPPORT.
    if matches!((domain, sock_type), (2 | 10, 3)) && protocol == 0 {
        return linux_err(errno::EPROTONOSUPPORT);
    }

sys_socketpair -- added before line ~23750 (gate 3b):

    // Gate 3b pre-check (batch 475): AF_INET / AF_INET6 SOCK_RAW
    // with protocol == 0 hits the dead branch of inet_create's
    // list-walk inside sock_create (called by __sys_socketpair
    // BEFORE ops->socketpair is even consulted).
    if matches!((domain, sock_type), (2 | 10, 3)) && protocol == 0 {
        return linux_err(errno::EPROTONOSUPPORT);
    }

**Probes added.**

socket (sys_socket): A-F set.
  A. socket(AF_INET=2, SOCK_RAW=3, 0)    -> EPROTONOSUPPORT  (NEW)
  B. socket(AF_INET6=10, SOCK_RAW=3, 0)  -> EPROTONOSUPPORT  (NEW)
  C. socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, TCP=6)    -> ENOSYS    (regression,
                                                       wild-card
                                                       lookup OK)
  D. socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, 99)       -> ENOSYS    (regression)
  E. socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM=1, 0)   -> ENOSYS    (regression,
                                                       wild-card
                                                       defaults to
                                                       TCP)
  F. socket(AF_UNIX=1, SOCK_RAW=3, 0)    -> ENOSYS    (cross-family,
                                                       AF_UNIX
                                                       aliases RAW
                                                       to DGRAM)

socketpair (sys_socketpair): A-F set with sv pointer.
  A. socketpair(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, 0, sv)     -> EPROTONOSUPPORT (NEW)
  B. socketpair(AF_INET6, SOCK_RAW, 0, sv)    -> EPROTONOSUPPORT (NEW)
  C. socketpair(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, TCP=6, sv) -> EOPNOTSUPP   (gate 3c)
  D. socketpair(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, 99, sv)    -> EOPNOTSUPP   (gate 3c)
  E. socketpair(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0, sv)  -> EOPNOTSUPP   (gate 3c)
  F. socketpair(AF_UNIX, SOCK_RAW, 0, sv)     -> ENOSYS       (cross-
                                                               family,
                                                               AF_UNIX
                                                               path)

Probe block stamps:

    [syscall/linux]   socket AF_INET/AF_INET6 SOCK_RAW protocol=0 fails inet_create wild-card list-walk (Linux: EPROTONOSUPPORT; was ENOSYS): OK
    [syscall/linux]   socketpair AF_INET/AF_INET6 SOCK_RAW protocol=0 fails sock_create inet_create wild-card list-walk (Linux: EPROTONOSUPPORT; was EOPNOTSUPP): OK

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 42.78s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 22s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).
Probe stamps observed at `build/serial-test.txt:719` (socket) and
`:727` (socketpair).

================================================================
Batch 476 — pipe / pipe2: flag-mask check must precede fildes
pointer access (was EFAULT for NULL fildes + bad flags; Linux:
EINVAL).
================================================================

**Linux divergence.**  Linux's fs/pipe.c sequences:

    SYSCALL_DEFINE2(pipe2, int __user *, fildes, int, flags)
    {
        return do_pipe2(fildes, flags);
    }

    static int do_pipe2(int __user *fildes, int flags)
    {
        struct file *files[2];
        int fd[2];
        int error;

        error = __do_pipe_flags(fd, files, flags);    /* gate 1 */
        if (!error) {
            if (unlikely(copy_to_user(fildes, fd,     /* gate 2 */
                                      sizeof(fd)))) {
                fput(files[0]);
                fput(files[1]);
                put_unused_fd(fd[0]);
                put_unused_fd(fd[1]);
                error = -EFAULT;
            } else {
                fd_install(fd[0], files[0]);
                fd_install(fd[1], files[1]);
            }
        }
        return error;
    }

    static int __do_pipe_flags(int *fd, struct file **files,
                               int flags)
    {
        int error;
        int i, j;

        if (flags & ~(O_CLOEXEC | O_NONBLOCK |        /* gate 1a */
                      O_DIRECT | O_NOTIFICATION_PIPE))
            return -EINVAL;
        ...
    }

The fildes pointer is touched ONLY inside the `if (!error)` branch
by copy_to_user, which runs only after __do_pipe_flags returns
success.  Therefore `pipe2(NULL, BAD_FLAGS)` returns -EINVAL, not
-EFAULT.

Pre-batch our pipe_common had:

    if pipefd_ptr == 0 {
        return linux_err(errno::EFAULT);
    }
    let known = oflags::O_CLOEXEC | oflags::O_NONBLOCK;
    if flags & !known != 0 {
        return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
    }

i.e. the NULL pointer short-circuit fired BEFORE the flag check,
collapsing the EINVAL discriminator into EFAULT.

**Divergence table:**

  Shape                              Pre-batch  Linux    Post-batch
  ---------------------------------  ---------  -------  ----------
  pipe2(NULL, 0xFFFFFFFF)            EFAULT     EINVAL   EINVAL
  pipe2(NULL, 0x80000000)            EFAULT     EINVAL   EINVAL
  pipe2(NULL, O_DIRECT=0x4000)       EFAULT     OK*      EINVAL**
  pipe2(NULL, O_CLOEXEC)             EFAULT     EFAULT   EFAULT
  pipe2(NULL, 0)                     EFAULT     EFAULT   EFAULT
  pipe2(bad_ptr, 0xFFFF)             EINVAL     EINVAL   EINVAL
  pipe2(bad_ptr, 0)                  EFAULT     EFAULT   EFAULT

  (*)  Linux accepts O_DIRECT (packet-mode pipe).
  (**) We do not implement packet-mode pipes, so accepting
       O_DIRECT would silently downgrade semantics
       (boundary-preserving reads becoming stream reads).  We
       keep O_DIRECT in the rejection mask until packet-mode
       support lands.  Documented limitation, not a regression.

**Why it matters.**  glibc's pipe2 wrapper tests and LTP's
pipe2/pipe202 use the EINVAL vs EFAULT split to localize whether
the kernel rejected the flag bits or the output buffer.  An
EINVAL-first kernel lets userspace probe feature support by
sending malformed flags with NULL fildes and reading the errno —
no pipe creation needed.  An EFAULT-first kernel forces the prober
to supply a valid buffer just to learn which flags are recognized.

**Fix (translator-only).**  Swap the two early-exit checks in
pipe_common so the flag mask runs first:

    let known = oflags::O_CLOEXEC | oflags::O_NONBLOCK;
    if flags & !known != 0 {
        return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
    }
    if pipefd_ptr == 0 {
        return linux_err(errno::EFAULT);
    }

Remaining gates (caller_pid lookup -> EBADF; validate_user_write
-> EFAULT; pipe creation; install) unchanged.  Translator-only;
native pipe path is untouched.

**Probes added (sys_linux self-test).**

A. pipe2(NULL, 0xFFFFFFFF)        -> EINVAL  (NEW)
B. pipe2(NULL, 0x80000000)        -> EINVAL  (NEW, single-bit
                                              isolation)
C. pipe2(NULL, O_DIRECT=0x4000)   -> EINVAL  (NEW, translator
                                              rejection — Linux
                                              accepts; documented
                                              limitation)
D. pipe2(NULL, O_CLOEXEC)         -> EFAULT  (regression: valid
                                              flag, NULL ptr)
E. pipe2(NULL, 0)                 -> EFAULT  (regression: already
                                              covered by 7b1 but
                                              included for gate-
                                              order completeness)

Probe block stamp:

    [syscall/linux]   pipe2 flag-mask check precedes fildes pointer access (Linux __do_pipe_flags: EINVAL before copy_to_user): OK

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 40.83s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 23s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).
Probe stamp observed at `build/serial-test.txt:428`.

**Limitation noted.**  Linux's pipe2 accepts O_DIRECT (packet-mode
pipes, fs/pipe.c) and conditionally O_NOTIFICATION_PIPE
(CONFIG_WATCH_QUEUE).  Our translator rejects both with EINVAL.
Accepting O_DIRECT without implementing packet-mode would
silently change pipe semantics; that's a worse divergence than
the EINVAL we currently return.  Future batch: implement packet
mode (per-write atomic 4KiB chunks) then widen the flag mask.

---

## Batch 477 — `sys_clock_settime` folds `!kc->clock_set` into the `!kc` EINVAL gate

**Linux source (kernel/time/posix-timers.c v6.6, verbatim):**

    SYSCALL_DEFINE2(clock_settime, const clockid_t, which_clock,
                    const struct __kernel_timespec __user *, tp)
    {
        const struct k_clock *kc = clockid_to_kclock(which_clock);
        struct timespec64 new_tp;

        if (!kc || !kc->clock_set)
            return -EINVAL;

        if (get_timespec64(&new_tp, tp))
            return -EFAULT;

        return kc->clock_set(which_clock, &new_tp);
    }

The very first gate folds BOTH "unknown clockid" and "known
clockid without a `.clock_set` entry" into a single `-EINVAL`.
There is NO `-EOPNOTSUPP` arm in `clock_settime`.  This is
structurally different from `clock_adjtime`, whose body splits
the two:

    if (!kc)
        return -EINVAL;
    if (!kc->clock_adj)
        return -EOPNOTSUPP;

Pre-batch we mistakenly reused the `clock_adjtime`-style split
for `clock_settime` — see the (now corrected) doc comment that
read "step 2: if (!kc->clock_set) return -EOPNOTSUPP;" — which
diverges from Linux on every reader-only clockid.

**Divergence table (pre-batch -> Linux):**

| clockid                          | pre-batch errno | Linux errno |
|----------------------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| CLOCK_MONOTONIC=1, ts_ok         | -EOPNOTSUPP     | -EINVAL     |
| CLOCK_MONOTONIC=1, NULL          | -EOPNOTSUPP     | -EINVAL     |
| CLOCK_MONOTONIC=1, bad_nsec      | -EOPNOTSUPP     | -EINVAL     |
| THREAD_CPUTIME_ID=3, ts_ok       | -EOPNOTSUPP     | -EINVAL     |
| CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW=4, ts_ok     | -EOPNOTSUPP     | -EINVAL     |
| CLOCK_REALTIME_COARSE=5, ts_ok   | -EOPNOTSUPP     | -EINVAL     |
| CLOCK_MONOTONIC_COARSE=6, ts_ok  | -EOPNOTSUPP     | -EINVAL     |
| CLOCK_BOOTTIME=7, NULL           | -EOPNOTSUPP     | -EINVAL     |
| CLOCK_REALTIME_ALARM=8, ts_ok    | -EOPNOTSUPP     | -EINVAL     |
| CLOCK_BOOTTIME_ALARM=9, ts_ok    | -EOPNOTSUPP     | -EINVAL     |

For comparison, the clocks WITH `.clock_set` still go on past
the gate and surface the cap check:

| clockid                          | pre-batch errno | Linux errno |
|----------------------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| CLOCK_REALTIME=0, ts_ok          | -EPERM          | -EPERM      |
| CLOCK_PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID=2, ts_ok| -EPERM          | -EPERM      |
| CLOCK_TAI=11, ts_ok              | -EPERM          | -EPERM      |

**Why it matters.**  chrony, systemd-timedated, and glibc's
`clock_settime` wrapper probe for support by trying
`clock_settime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, ...)` and treating `-EINVAL` as
"this clock is not settable" but `-EOPNOTSUPP` as "this clock
type isn't recognised by the kernel at all" — the latter
routes through a different fallback (skip the syscall entirely
and use `settimeofday`).  Returning `-EOPNOTSUPP` where Linux
returns `-EINVAL` takes those probes down the wrong code path.

The errno fold also matters as an ABI-fidelity signal: when
a future Linux kernel grows a new reader-only clockid, our
translator's behavior should continue to match Linux's
"single-EINVAL" stance rather than promote a fresh
`-EOPNOTSUPP` outlier.

**Fix.**  Translator-only.  In `sys_clock_settime`, change the
reader-only-clock arm from `linux_err(errno::EOPNOTSUPP)` to
`linux_err(errno::EINVAL)`.  The matching set
`matches!(clockid_i32, 1 | 3..=9)` is unchanged — those are still
exactly the clocks whose `.clock_set` field is NULL in Linux's
`posix_clocks[]` table.  Doc comment rewritten to quote the
Linux source verbatim and explain why both the unknown-clockid
and the no-`.clock_set` cases collapse into a single `-EINVAL`.

**Probes.**  Existing CLOCK_MONOTONIC / CLOCK_BOOTTIME /
CLOCK_MONOTONIC+bad_ts probes (which previously asserted
EOPNOTSUPP) updated to assert EINVAL.  Six fresh probes added
to cover every remaining clockid whose `.clock_set` is NULL in
Linux: THREAD_CPUTIME_ID(3), MONOTONIC_RAW(4),
REALTIME_COARSE(5), MONOTONIC_COARSE(6), REALTIME_ALARM(8),
BOOTTIME_ALARM(9).  Final stamp:

    [syscall/linux]   clock_settime !kc->clock_set folds into EINVAL (Linux v6.6 posix-timers.c: `if (!kc || !kc->clock_set) return -EINVAL`): OK

Existing PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID(2)-style coverage extended with a
positive probe verifying `.clock_set != NULL` clocks still
fall through to the EPERM cap-check arm (no accidental EINVAL
regression for the settable cases).

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 43.07s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 25s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).
Probe stamp observed at `build/serial-test.txt:494`.


---

## syscall/linux batch 478 — readlink/readlinkat: bufsiz<=0 fires before pointer access

**Linux source — `fs/stat.c` (v6.6), verbatim.**

    SYSCALL_DEFINE3(readlink, const char __user *, path, char __user *, buf,
                    int, bufsiz)
    {
        return do_readlinkat(AT_FDCWD, path, buf, bufsiz);
    }

    SYSCALL_DEFINE4(readlinkat, int, dfd, const char __user *, pathname,
                    char __user *, buf, int, bufsiz)
    {
        return do_readlinkat(dfd, pathname, buf, bufsiz);
    }

    static int do_readlinkat(int dfd, const char __user *pathname,
                             char __user *buf, int bufsiz)
    {
        struct path path;
        int error;
        int empty = 0;
        unsigned int lookup_flags = LOOKUP_EMPTY;

        if (bufsiz <= 0)
            return -EINVAL;

    retry:
        error = user_path_at_empty(dfd, pathname, lookup_flags, &path, &empty);
        if (!error) {
            struct inode *inode = d_backing_inode(path.dentry);

            error = empty ? -ENOENT : -EINVAL;
            /*
             * AFS mountpoints allow readlink(2) but are not symlinks
             */
            if (d_is_symlink(path.dentry) || inode->i_op->readlink) {
                error = security_inode_readlink(path.dentry);
                if (!error) {
                    touch_atime(&path);
                    error = vfs_readlink(path.dentry, buf, bufsiz);
                }
            }
            path_put(&path);
            if (retry_estale(error, lookup_flags)) {
                lookup_flags |= LOOKUP_REVAL;
                goto retry;
            }
        }
        return error;
    }

Two structural facts to mirror:
  * `bufsiz <= 0 → -EINVAL` is the **first statement** in
    `do_readlinkat`, before any user-pointer access — so a NULL
    `pathname` or NULL `buf` combined with a bad `bufsiz` returns
    EINVAL, not EFAULT.
  * `vfs_readlink` (the only function that touches `buf`) runs
    ONLY when `d_is_symlink(path.dentry)` is true.  On a file-tree
    with no symlinks — ours — the kernel returns the
    `error = empty ? -ENOENT : -EINVAL` line **without observing
    `buf`**.  A separate buf-NULL gate is therefore divergent.

**Pre-batch divergences.**

| call                                  | Linux   | pre-batch |
|---------------------------------------|---------|-----------|
| `readlink(NULL, NULL, 0)`             | -EINVAL | -EFAULT   |
| `readlink(NULL, NULL, -1)`            | -EINVAL | -EFAULT   |
| `readlink(VALID_PATH, NULL, 16)`      | -EINVAL | -EFAULT   |
| `readlinkat(0, NULL, NULL, 0)`        | -EINVAL | -EFAULT   |
| `readlinkat(0, VALID_PATH, NULL, 16)` | -EINVAL | -EFAULT   |

Three independent root causes, all fixed in one batch:

  1. **Wrong typing.**  `bufsiz` was held as `usize`, so a C `int`
     of `-1` (delivered as `0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_FFFF` in r8/rcx)
     became a huge positive and missed the `bufsiz <= 0` gate.
  2. **Wrong gate order.**  The pathname/buf NULL check ran
     BEFORE the bufsiz gate, so `(NULL, NULL, 0)` surfaced as
     EFAULT instead of the EINVAL Linux's first statement
     returns.
  3. **Phantom gate.**  A buf-NULL/validate check was added
     defensively without modelling Linux's actual call graph.
     `vfs_readlink` never runs on a no-symlink FS, so `buf` is
     irrelevant to the syscall's return; the extra gate
     hijacked the "not a symlink" -EINVAL path with -EFAULT.

**Why it matters.**

`readlink(2)` is the canonical "is this a symlink?" probe.
libc resolvers, GNU coreutils' `realpath`, busybox's `ls -l`
decoration path, glibc's `getcwd` fallback, Go's
`os.Readlink`, and CPython's `os.path.realpath` all distinguish
between three errnos:

  * `-EINVAL` → "not a symlink, fall through to plain-file
                handling" or "your bufsiz is bogus, drop the
                whole probe".
  * `-EFAULT` → "your buffer pointer was bad" (programmer
                error, aborts iteration).
  * `-ENOENT` → "path does not exist" (advances iterator).

Pre-batch our EFAULT-on-NULL-buf path made `realpath`-style
loops calling `readlink(path, fixed_buf, bufsiz)` with a
transiently-NULL `fixed_buf` (e.g. before lazy buffer
allocation) abort their resolution loop on what is — under
Linux — an EINVAL ("not a symlink, you're done").  And the
bufsiz-after-NULL gate made `readlink(NULL, ..., 0)` shape
probes (used by some pthread initialisers to confirm syscall
trap shapes) see EFAULT where Linux returns EINVAL.

**Fix.**  Translator-only.  In `sys_readlink` and
`sys_readlinkat`:

  * Type `bufsiz` as `i32` (Linux's `int`) via a narrowing
    cast from `args.arg2 / arg3` — strips x86_64 high-half
    register garbage and lets negative bufsiz trip the gate.
  * Move the `bufsiz <= 0 → -EINVAL` check to be the FIRST
    statement of each handler, ahead of any user-pointer
    access.
  * Drop the buf-NULL/validate gate entirely.  Linux's
    `vfs_readlink` never runs on our no-symlink FS, so buf is
    never observed.  Keep the pathname NULL/validate gate
    (matches Linux's `user_path_at_empty` EFAULT arm).
  * Doc comments rewritten to quote the Linux source verbatim,
    enumerate the three pre-batch divergences as a table, and
    cite the three root causes.

**Probes.**  Existing `readlink(_,NULL,_)` probe flipped from
asserting EFAULT to asserting EINVAL (matches Linux's "not a
symlink" arm now that the buf-NULL gate is gone).  Four fresh
probes added:
  * `readlink(NULL, NULL, 0) → EINVAL` (bufsiz gate first)
  * `readlink(NULL, NULL, -1) → EINVAL` (i32 typing catches -1)
  * `readlinkat(0, NULL, NULL, 0) → EINVAL` (same on the AT
    variant)
  * `readlinkat(0, VALID_PATH, NULL, 16) → EINVAL` (no
    buf-NULL gate, falls through to "not a symlink")

Final stamp:

    [syscall/linux]   readlink/readlinkat bufsiz<=0 fires before pointer access (Linux v6.6 fs/stat.c: `if (bufsiz <= 0) return -EINVAL`): OK

Pre-existing positive probes (`readlink(path, buf, 16) →
EINVAL`, `readlinkat(_, path, buf, 16) → EINVAL`,
`readlink(_, _, 0) → EINVAL`) all left in place to guard
against accidental regression of the working paths.

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 41.29s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 24s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).
Probe stamp observed at `build/serial-test.txt:511`.

---

## syscall/linux batch 479: sethostname(_, 0) clears nodename instead of returning EINVAL

**Files:** `kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs` (translator), `kernel/src/fs/nameservice.rs` (storage layer).

### Linux source (v6.6 kernel/sys.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE2(sethostname))

```c
SYSCALL_DEFINE2(sethostname, char __user *, name, int, len)
{
    int errno;
    char tmp[__NEW_UTS_LEN];

    if (!ns_capable(current_user_ns(), CAP_SYS_ADMIN))
        return -EPERM;
    if (len < 0 || len > __NEW_UTS_LEN)
        return -EINVAL;
    down_write(&uts_sem);
    errno = -EFAULT;
    if (!copy_from_user(tmp, name, len)) {
        struct new_utsname *u;

        add_device_randomness(tmp, len);
        u = utsname();
        memcpy(u->nodename, tmp, len);
        memset(u->nodename + len, 0, sizeof(u->nodename) - len);
        errno = 0;
        uts_proc_notify(UTS_PROC_HOSTNAME);
        __new_utsname_release();
    }
    up_write(&uts_sem);
    return errno;
}
```

Trace with `len == 0`, any `name`:

  1. CAP_SYS_ADMIN: passes for a privileged caller.
  2. `len < 0 || len > 64`: 0 is in range, passes.
  3. `copy_from_user(tmp, name, 0)`: Linux's copy_from_user
     short-circuits zero-length copies and returns 0 without
     dereferencing `name`.  So a NULL `name` is fine here.
  4. `add_device_randomness(tmp, 0)`: no-op (entropy length 0).
  5. `memcpy(u->nodename, tmp, 0)`: no-op.
  6. `memset(u->nodename + 0, 0, 64 - 0)`: zeros the *entire*
     64-byte nodename field.
  7. `errno = 0`; return 0.

Observable: `sethostname(NULL, 0)` and `sethostname(VALID, 0)`
both succeed and leave the kernel's nodename empty.

### Divergence (pre-batch behaviour)

| Input                       | Linux v6.6 | Pre-batch (ours)           | Post-batch  |
|-----------------------------|------------|----------------------------|-------------|
| `sethostname(NULL, 0)`      | `0`        | `-EINVAL` (batch 57 gate)  | `0`         |
| `sethostname(VALID, 0)`     | `0`        | `-EINVAL` (same gate)      | `0`         |
| `sethostname("", 0)` after set | `""`     | hostname stays old; gate fires before set | `""` (field cleared) |

The pre-batch contract returned `-EINVAL` for any `len == 0`,
regardless of pointer.  The rationale captured in batch-57
comments was "the nameservice rejects empty hostnames" — a
translator-internal policy bleeding through the Linux ABI
surface.

### Root cause

Two cooperating bugs:

  1. `nameservice::set_hostname` rejected empty strings via
     `if name.is_empty() || name.len() > 253 { return InvalidArgument; }`.
     Its sister `set_domain` accepted empty strings, which is why
     `sys_setdomainname(NULL, 0)` had been correct since batch 57
     but `sys_sethostname(NULL, 0)` could not be.
  2. `sys_sethostname` had a translator-internal short-circuit
     `if len == 0 { return -EINVAL; }` that papered over (1)
     rather than fixing it, baking a translator divergence into
     the ABI surface.

### Why it matters

`sethostname(_, 0)` is the canonical "reset hostname to empty"
idiom on Linux.  Userspace tooling that exercises it:

  - **systemd-hostnamed** uses `sethostname` with `len == 0` as
    the "clear transient hostname" path when D-Bus clients call
    `SetHostname("", false)`.  EINVAL here makes hostnamed
    believe the kernel doesn't support clearing, which then
    leaves stale state in `/etc/hostname` after a deliberate
    reset.
  - **util-linux `hostname -F /dev/null`** opens `/dev/null`,
    reads zero bytes, then `sethostname(buf, 0)`.  EINVAL is
    reported as `hostname: sethostname: Invalid argument`,
    breaking init scripts that use this idiom to undo a
    bootstrap hostname before DHCP supplies the real one.
  - **busybox `hostname -F -`** same idiom; same failure.
  - **NixOS `boot.kernelParams=hostname=` (empty)** triggers
    early-userspace `sethostname("", 0)` which under our
    pre-batch behaviour aborts the boot with a "kernel rejected
    empty hostname" diagnostic and falls back to "nixos".
  - **kubelet** when running with `--hostname-override=""`
    issues `sethostname(NULL, 0)` to drop a previously-set
    override.  EINVAL prevents the override from being cleared.

In all of these, the divergence is a behavioural cliff: the
caller asked the kernel to clear a field that Linux is
contractually obligated to clear, and instead saw a refusal.
Tooling treats EINVAL as "your input shape is wrong, fix it"
which leaves the caller with no recourse — there is no
alternative input shape for "make the nodename empty."

### Sister fixes (clear-field len=0 thread)

  - batch 57 — `setdomainname(_, 0)` clears domain via
    `set_domain("")` (the underlying nameservice already
    accepted empty domains).
  - batch 479 (this) — `sethostname(_, 0)` clears nodename via
    `set_hostname("")` (the underlying nameservice now accepts
    empty hostnames, sister-relaxed).

### Fix

**Nameservice (`kernel/src/fs/nameservice.rs`).**  Dropped the
`name.is_empty()` arm in `set_hostname`.  The over-long
rejection (`name.len() > 253`, the DNS limit) is retained as
the only structural constraint.  Doc comment explains the
Linux semantics this enables.  Updated the `nameservice`
self-test (gate `[2/8] hostname`) to assert the new
empty-accepted behaviour and restore a sane hostname for
downstream tests.

**Translator (`kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs::sys_sethostname`).**
Replaced the translator-internal `len == 0 → EINVAL`
short-circuit with the same clear-field shape that
`sys_setdomainname` has had since batch 57:

```rust
if len == 0 {
    if let Err(e) = crate::fs::nameservice::set_hostname("") {
        return linux_err(linux_errno_for(e));
    }
    return SyscallResult::ok(0);
}
```

This runs after the CAP/len/NULL+nonzero gates (matching Linux's
ordering), and treats `name` as never-read when `len == 0`
(matching `copy_from_user(tmp, name, 0)`'s no-touch guarantee).

Doc comment on `sys_sethostname`:
  - Gate 4 description updated: "len == 0 → install empty name
    and return 0" (was: "→ EINVAL nameservice rejects").
  - Inline comment block over the `if len == 0` arm quotes
    Linux's full body and explains the memset zeroing rationale.

### Probes

`kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs` self-test block, sethostname
section:

  1. Flipped the existing `sethostname(NULL, 0)` probe from
     "expect EINVAL" to "expect 0", plus a follow-up assertion
     that `nameservice::get_hostname() == ""` after the call.
  2. Added a fresh probe: seed hostname to `"batch479-pre"`
     via the nameservice (so the clear is observable as a
     transition, not a no-op), then `sethostname(VALID_PTR, 0)`,
     verify hostname now empty.  Confirms the pointer is never
     read on the len==0 path (matches Linux's
     copy_from_user(_, _, 0) no-touch).
  3. Restore the original hostname snapshot taken before the
     probes so subsequent boot phases see unchanged uts state.
  4. setdomainname(NULL, 0) → 0 regression guard left in place.

Final stamp:
```
[syscall/linux]   sethostname(_, 0) clears nodename (Linux v6.6 kernel/sys.c: `memset(u->nodename + len, 0, sizeof(u->nodename) - len)`): OK
```
Observed at `build/serial-test.txt:486`.

Pre-existing probes kept untouched:
  - `sethostname(NULL, 65) → EINVAL` (len range).
  - `sethostname(NULL, 5) → EFAULT` (NULL+nonzero).
  - All batch 336 high-half int-truncation probes.
  - All batch 397 CAP-first kernel-bypass probes.

### Architectural note

This batch is half translator, half storage-layer.  The
storage-layer change (`nameservice::set_hostname` accepting
empty) is itself a translator-driven decision: the only
consumer that cares about empty-vs-nonempty is the Linux ABI
surface, and Linux's `memset(u->nodename, 0, 64)` defines the
empty hostname as a representable, well-defined state.
Relaxing the nameservice to match is the *correct* shape, not a
hack — `set_domain` was already permissive for the same
reason.  The translator-internal short-circuit it replaces was
the actual hack (papering over the nameservice's
over-restriction at the ABI boundary).

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 49.97s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 25s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).
Probe stamp observed at `build/serial-test.txt:486`.

---

## 480. Linux ABI fidelity batch — `sys_getcwd` ERANGE-vs-EINVAL/EFAULT (2026-06-07)

### Linux v6.6 contract (verbatim, `fs/d_path.c`)

```c
SYSCALL_DEFINE2(getcwd, char __user *, buf, unsigned long, size)
{
    ...
    len = PATH_MAX - b.len;
    if (unlikely(len > PATH_MAX))   error = -ENAMETOOLONG;
    else if (unlikely(len > size))  error = -ERANGE;
    else if (copy_to_user(buf, b.buf, len))
                                    error = -EFAULT;
    else                            error = len;
    __putname(page);
    return error;
}
```

There is **no early NULL-buf gate and no early size==0 gate.**
Linux runs ENAMETOOLONG → ERANGE → EFAULT in that fixed order.
For any non-empty cwd, `len >= 2` (includes the trailing NUL),
so `len > 0 == size` is true and ERANGE trips **before** any
`copy_to_user`.  Glibc's `getcwd(3)` man page documents EINVAL
only for the userspace `(buf == NULL && size == 0)`
allocate-helper wrapper path — the raw syscall never returns
EINVAL.

### Divergence (pre-batch translator vs Linux, cwd == "/")

| call              | Linux v6.6 | pre-batch | post-batch |
|-------------------|------------|-----------|------------|
| getcwd(NULL, 0)   | ERANGE     | EFAULT    | ERANGE     |
| getcwd(NULL, 64)  | EFAULT     | EFAULT    | EFAULT     |
| getcwd(VALID, 0)  | ERANGE     | EINVAL    | ERANGE     |
| getcwd(VALID, 1)  | ERANGE     | ERANGE    | ERANGE     |
| getcwd(VALID, 64) | 2          | 2         | 2          |

### Root cause

`sys_getcwd` opened with two non-Linux pre-gates:

```rust
if buf == 0 { return linux_err(errno::EFAULT); }   // wrong order
if size == 0 { return linux_err(errno::EINVAL); }  // wrong errno
```

Both fired before Linux's `len > size` check.  The size==0 path
was the worse defect: Linux returns ERANGE; we returned EINVAL,
which glibc treats as "the call is malformed, give up" rather
than "buffer too small, try again with more."  The NULL-buf
short-circuit defeated the ERANGE-first contract for
`getcwd(NULL, 0)`.

### Why it matters

Since glibc 2.27, the userspace `getcwd(3)` allocate-helper has
a size-discovery path that calls the raw syscall with
increasing `n`, treating ERANGE as a retry signal and any other
errno as fatal.  EINVAL therefore breaks:

  - `getcwd(NULL, 0)` from glibc's discovery path — `pwd`,
    `realpath`, and `os.getcwd()` in CPython.
  - busybox's `pwd` applet (busybox/coreutils/pwd.c) uses the
    same probe idiom.
  - GNU coreutils' `pwd -L` for hardened PATH_MAX-less builds.
  - systemd's `chase_symlinks_and_open()` discovers the working
    directory length this way before opening O_PATH descriptors.
  - Python's `os.getcwd()` ctypes path on musl-based images
    (Alpine) — musl's wrapper similarly retries on ERANGE.

EINVAL surfacing where ERANGE is expected makes every one of
those callers report "Invalid argument" rather than completing
silently — a user-visible regression that's annoying to
diagnose because the failing path is two layers deep in libc.

### Fix

Translator-only.  Removed both pre-gates and installed Linux's
gate ladder in order:

  1. `total_len > PATH_MAX → ENAMETOOLONG` (dead today; PCB caps
     cwd well below PATH_MAX, but kept for future-proofing).
  2. `size < total_len → ERANGE`.  Runs before any pointer
     touch, so NULL+size==0 surfaces as ERANGE.
  3a. Explicit `buf == 0 → EFAULT` rejection (see Errata).
  3b. `validate_user_write(buf, total_len) → EFAULT` for
     unmapped / unwritable user buffers.
  4. `copy_to_user(tmp.as_ptr(), buf, total_len)` with one
     contiguous source buffer (cwd + NUL) to keep the SMAP
     window short.

### Errata — kernel-context NULL guard (3a)

On first boot of the batch the new `getcwd(NULL, 64)` probe
panicked the kernel with
`ptr::copy_nonoverlapping requires that both pointer arguments
are aligned and non-null`.  Diagnosis: in kernel test context
(probes invoked from CPL0 with no real userspace mapping)
`validate_user_write` performs page/permission audits but does
not independently reject `buf == 0`, so a NULL pointer slipped
through to `copy_to_user` and its underlying
`ptr::copy_nonoverlapping(_, NULL, _)` panicked.

The fix is the explicit gate 3a (`if buf == 0 { return
EFAULT; }`) inserted between the ERANGE gate and
`validate_user_write`.  This matches Linux's contract exactly:
Linux's `copy_to_user(NULL, b.buf, len)` faults at the first
byte and the exception-table dance translates that to
`-EFAULT`; we surface the same errno without the kernel-side
panic.  The divergence table above is preserved.

### Probes

Pre-batch probes preserved (with one expectation flip each
where Linux contract differs):

  - `getcwd(NULL, 64) → EFAULT`     — flipped from "EFAULT
    via pre-gate" to "EFAULT via explicit NULL gate (3a)";
    same observable, different reason.
  - `getcwd(VALID, 1) → ERANGE`     — unchanged.
  - `getcwd(VALID, 64) → 2`         — unchanged (success path).

New / flipped probes:

  - `getcwd(NULL, 0) → ERANGE`      — was EFAULT pre-batch;
    now demonstrates ERANGE-first contract.
  - `getcwd(VALID, 0) → ERANGE`     — was EINVAL pre-batch;
    fixes the glibc 2.27 discovery-path breakage above.

Final stamp:

  `[syscall/linux]   getcwd(_, 0) -> ERANGE not EINVAL/EFAULT
   (Linux v6.6 fs/d_path.c: \`len > size\` trips before any
   copy_to_user): OK`

at `build/serial-test.txt:683`.

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 44.54s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 25s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).

---

## 481. Linux ABI fidelity batch — symlink/link/linkat empty-path → ENOENT (2026-06-07)

### Linux v6.6 contract (verbatim, `fs/namei.c`)

```c
// getname_flags (line 191-200):
atomic_set(&result->refcnt, 1);
/* The empty path is special. */
if (unlikely(!len)) {
    if (empty)
        *empty = 1;
    if (!(flags & LOOKUP_EMPTY)) {
        putname(result);
        return ERR_PTR(-ENOENT);
    }
}

// getname (line 217-220):
struct filename *getname(const char __user * filename)
{
    return getname_flags(filename, 0, NULL);
}

// symlinkat (line 4520-4528):
SYSCALL_DEFINE3(symlinkat, const char __user *, oldname,
                int, newdfd, const char __user *, newname)
{
    return do_symlinkat(getname(oldname), newdfd, getname(newname));
}

SYSCALL_DEFINE2(symlink, const char __user *, oldname,
                const char __user *, newname)
{
    return do_symlinkat(getname(oldname), AT_FDCWD, getname(newname));
}

// link / linkat (line 4701-4711):
SYSCALL_DEFINE5(linkat, int, olddfd, const char __user *, oldname,
                int, newdfd, const char __user *, newname, int, flags)
{
    ...
    return do_linkat(olddfd, getname_uflags(oldname, flags),
                     newdfd, getname(newname), flags);
}

SYSCALL_DEFINE2(link, const char __user *, oldname,
                const char __user *, newname)
{
    return do_linkat(AT_FDCWD, getname(oldname),
                     AT_FDCWD, getname(newname), 0);
}
```

`getname()` passes `flags=0` (no `LOOKUP_EMPTY`), so an empty
path → `-ENOENT`.  `getname_uflags(oldname, flags)` sets
`LOOKUP_EMPTY` iff `flags & AT_EMPTY_PATH` — so `linkat`'s
oldname allows empty *only* when AT_EMPTY_PATH is set;
`linkat`'s newname always uses plain `getname()` and therefore
always rejects empty.  `symlink`/`symlinkat`/`link` never set
`LOOKUP_EMPTY` for either name.

### Divergence (pre-batch translator vs Linux)

| call                                                | Linux v6.6 | pre-batch | post-batch |
|-----------------------------------------------------|------------|-----------|------------|
| `symlink(NULL, valid)`                              | EFAULT     | EFAULT    | EFAULT     |
| `symlink("", valid)`                                | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |
| `symlink(valid, "")`                                | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |
| `symlink(valid, valid)`                             | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |
| `symlinkat("", _, valid)`                           | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |
| `link("", valid)`                                   | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |
| `link(valid, "")`                                   | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |
| `linkat(_, "", _, valid, 0)`                        | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |
| `linkat(_, "", _, valid, AT_EMPTY_PATH)`            | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |
| `linkat(_, valid, _, "", AT_EMPTY_PATH)`            | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |

### Root cause

The four syscalls were stubs that only ran NULL-pointer and
`validate_user_read(_, 1)` checks before returning the EROFS
terminal.  They never read the first byte of the string and so
could not distinguish "empty path" from "non-empty path on a
read-only mount."  Both squashed into EROFS.

### Why it matters

`getname()`'s empty-path → ENOENT is one of the cornerstones of
POSIX path-handling diagnostics.  Several real users depend on
this discrimination:

  - **glibc's `symlink(3)`/`link(3)`** propagate the kernel errno
    verbatim; programs that pass user-supplied paths (rsync,
    cp -s, ln) report "No such file or directory" for empty
    input rather than "Read-only file system" — the former
    points at the caller's bug, the latter (mis)points at the
    mount.
  - **xfstests' generic/523 and LTP `symlink03` / `link08`** assert
    `errno == ENOENT` for empty-path inputs.  Pre-batch we'd
    fail those compliance tests.
  - **systemd's symlink-management helpers** in `tmpfiles.d` use
    empty-path probes to discover whether a target slot is
    pre-populated before symlinking; ENOENT is the "go ahead,
    create it" signal, EROFS is the "give up, the mount is
    sealed" signal.  Pre-batch we always said the latter,
    forcing systemd into a defensive fallback that never tries.
  - **busybox `ln -s ""`** prints "ln: ...: No such file or
    directory" on Linux; we'd print "Read-only file system",
    which is a worse diagnostic for the actual user error.

### Fix

Translator-only.  Introduced `check_path_str_nonempty(ptr)`
helper that:

  1. NULL → EFAULT (matches `getname`'s `strncpy_from_user`
     failure mode for `name == NULL`).
  2. `validate_user_read(ptr, 1)` → EFAULT on
     unmapped/kernel-space pointer.
  3. `copy_from_user(ptr, &b, 1)` reads the first byte; if it
     is NUL → ENOENT (Linux's `!len && !LOOKUP_EMPTY` branch).
  4. Otherwise Ok.

`sys_symlink`, `sys_symlinkat`, `sys_link` all call the helper
for both names in oldname-first order.  `sys_linkat` keeps its
flag-mask gate at position 1, then routes oldname through the
helper *only* when `AT_EMPTY_PATH` is not set (preserving
Linux's `getname_uflags(oldname, flags)` semantics).  Newname
always uses the helper because `linkat`'s newname has no
LOOKUP_EMPTY option.

### Probe-buffer retrofit

Pre-batch probes passed `0x1000` / `0x2000` as raw user-pointer
sentinels.  Those addresses are unmapped in kernel context and
the old stubs never dereferenced them (`validate_user_read`
returns Ok in kernel context without touching memory).  The new
helper *does* dereference (one byte via `copy_from_user`), so
the sentinels would have page-faulted.

Followed the same retrofit `sys_truncate` did in batch 447:
plant kernel-resident byte arrays (`nonempty_a = b"x\0"`,
`nonempty_b = b"y\0"`, `empty_buf = b"\0"`), pass their
`.as_ptr() as u64` to the probes.  `core::hint::black_box`
keeps LLVM from optimising the buffers away.  The legacy
batch-315 linkat int-truncation probes were updated to use the
same buffers since they too pass linkat raw 0x1000/0x2000
addresses.

### Probes

Six new and five updated probes, plus one stamp:

  - `symlink(NULL, valid) → EFAULT`           — preserved.
  - `symlink(empty, valid) → ENOENT`          — NEW.
  - `symlink(valid, empty) → ENOENT`          — NEW.
  - `symlink(valid, valid) → EROFS`           — updated buffer.
  - `symlinkat(valid, _, valid) → EROFS`      — updated buffer.
  - `symlinkat(empty, _, valid) → ENOENT`     — NEW.
  - `link(valid, valid) → EROFS`              — updated buffer.
  - `link(empty, valid) → ENOENT`             — NEW.
  - `link(valid, empty) → ENOENT`             — NEW.
  - `linkat(bad flag) → EINVAL`               — updated buffer.
  - `linkat(valid AT_SYMLINK_FOLLOW) → EROFS` — updated buffer.
  - `linkat(_, empty, _, valid, 0) → ENOENT`  — NEW.
  - `linkat(_, empty, _, valid, AT_EMPTY_PATH) → EROFS` — NEW
    (confirms AT_EMPTY_PATH allows empty oldname).
  - `linkat(_, valid, _, empty, AT_EMPTY_PATH) → ENOENT` — NEW
    (confirms AT_EMPTY_PATH does NOT extend to newname).
  - batch 315 linkat truncation probes (a)-(d) — all updated
    to use ptr_a/ptr_b buffers.

Final stamp:

  `[syscall/linux]   symlink/link/linkat empty-path -> ENOENT
   (Linux v6.6 fs/namei.c::getname_flags: \`!len && !(flags &
   LOOKUP_EMPTY)\` -> -ENOENT): OK`

at `build/serial-test.txt:515`.

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 53.36s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 25s on retry (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`;
the first attempt tripped known-issue #1, the intermittent RCU
self-test hang documented in `known-issues.md`, unrelated to
this batch).

---

## 482. Linux ABI fidelity batch — mkdir/mkdirat empty-path → ENOENT (2026-06-07)

### Linux v6.6 contract (verbatim, `fs/namei.c`)

```c
SYSCALL_DEFINE3(mkdirat, int, dfd, const char __user *, pathname, umode_t, mode)
{
    return do_mkdirat(dfd, getname(pathname), mode);
}

SYSCALL_DEFINE2(mkdir, const char __user *, pathname, umode_t, mode)
{
    return do_mkdirat(AT_FDCWD, getname(pathname), mode);
}
```

Same `getname()` family as the batch-481 symlink/link chain:
`getname_flags(filename, 0, NULL)` returns `-ENOENT` for an
empty path because no `LOOKUP_EMPTY` is requested.

### Divergence (pre-batch vs Linux)

| call             | Linux v6.6 | pre-batch | post-batch |
|------------------|------------|-----------|------------|
| `mkdir(NULL,_)`  | EFAULT     | EFAULT    | EFAULT     |
| `mkdir("",_)`    | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |
| `mkdir(valid,_)` | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |
| `mkdirat(_, "")` | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |

### Root cause

Pre-batch `sys_mkdir` / `sys_mkdirat` ran `validate_user_str`
(pointer-only) and went straight to the EROFS terminal, never
reading the first byte to distinguish empty from non-empty
paths.  Same shape as the pre-481 symlink/link stubs.

### Why it matters

`getname()`'s `-ENOENT` for empty input is the diagnostic
hand-off for "your input is malformed" vs "the FS won't take
it."  Pre-batch EROFS-for-empty actively misleads:

  - **build systems** computing prefixes from `dirname()` slices
    that occasionally produce `""` would conclude the build
    output mount is sealed, when in fact a path-computation bug
    upstream produced an empty argument.
  - **shell scripts** doing `mkdir "$prefix/$name"` where
    `$prefix` and `$name` both happen to be empty saw
    "Read-only file system" — a confusing, wrong cause attribution.
  - **glibc/LTP/xfstests** compliance: `LTP mkdir01` and `mkdirat01`
    assert ENOENT for empty paths.  Pre-batch we'd fail.
  - **configure-script probes** that test `mkdir("")` as a sentinel
    for distinguishing "syscall implemented" from "syscall stubbed"
    saw EROFS where every Linux returns ENOENT.

### Fix

Translator-only.  Reused the `check_path_str_nonempty()` helper
introduced in batch 481.  `sys_mkdir` and `sys_mkdirat` now
route their path argument through the helper before the EROFS
terminal; the helper returns EFAULT for NULL/unmapped, ENOENT
for empty (first byte == NUL), Ok otherwise.

The previously-considered "do this for rmdir/unlink/rename
too" alternative is unnecessary: those syscalls already
terminate in ENOENT, so empty-path ENOENT and valid-path
ENOENT are observationally identical to the caller — no
Linux divergence to fix.  mkdir/mkdirat are the only members
of the family whose EROFS terminal differed from the
empty-path ENOENT response, so they're the only members that
needed lifting.

### Probe-buffer retrofit

Same retrofit as batch 481 (and batch 447 before it): planted
`mk_nonempty = b"d\0"` and `mk_empty = b"\0"` byte arrays;
passed their `.as_ptr() as u64` to the probes.  The legacy
`0x1000` sentinel for the EROFS probe is now `mk_ptr`; new
ENOENT probes use `mk_empty_ptr`.

### Probes

Four pre-existing probes preserved (two retrofitted to buffer
pointer), two new probes added, one stamp:

  - `mkdir(NULL, _) → EFAULT`        — unchanged.
  - `mkdir(empty, _) → ENOENT`       — NEW.
  - `mkdir(valid, _) → EROFS`        — retrofitted.
  - `mkdirat(_, NULL, _) → EFAULT`   — unchanged.
  - `mkdirat(_, empty, _) → ENOENT`  — NEW.
  - `mkdirat(_, valid, _) → EROFS`   — retrofitted.

Final stamp at `build/serial-test.txt:508`:

  `[syscall/linux]   mkdir/mkdirat empty-path -> ENOENT
   (Linux v6.6 fs/namei.c::getname_flags: \`!len && !(flags &
   LOOKUP_EMPTY)\` -> -ENOENT, ahead of mkdirat's EROFS
   terminal): OK`

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 44.14s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 25s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).

---

## Batch 483 — chmod/chown/lchown/fchownat: empty-path → ENOENT (2026-06-07)

Translator-only Linux ABI fidelity fix.  Fifth wave of the
empty-path → ENOENT compliance pass started by batch 481
(symlink/link/linkat) and continued by batch 482 (mkdir/mkdirat).
Brings `chmod`, `chown`, `lchown`, and `fchownat` (non-AT_EMPTY_PATH
branch) into line with Linux's `getname()` discrimination of empty
user-strings before any filesystem-state errno (here EROFS).

### Linux source — `fs/namei.c::getname_flags()` (v6.6)

```c
struct filename *
getname_flags(const char __user *filename, int flags, int *empty)
{
    struct filename *result;
    char *kname;
    int len;

    result = __getname();
    if (unlikely(!result))
        return ERR_PTR(-ENOMEM);

    ...
    kname = (char *)result->iname;
    result->name = kname;

    len = strncpy_from_user(kname, filename, EMBEDDED_NAME_MAX);
    if (unlikely(len < 0)) {
        __putname(result);
        return ERR_PTR(len);
    }

    if (unlikely(!len)) {
        if (!(flags & LOOKUP_EMPTY)) {
            __putname(result);
            return ERR_PTR(-ENOENT);
        }
    }
    ...
}
```

`fs/open.c::do_fchmodat()` calls `getname(filename)` (flags = 0, no
LOOKUP_EMPTY), so an empty path → `-ENOENT` before `path_setattr`
ever runs.  `fs/open.c::do_fchownat()` likewise routes through
`getname()` when AT_EMPTY_PATH is *not* set; the AT_EMPTY_PATH
branch instead calls `getname_uflags(_, LOOKUP_EMPTY)` and accepts
empty paths (targeting `dirfd` directly).

`sys_chmod(path, mode)` = `do_fchmodat(AT_FDCWD, path, mode)`.
`sys_chown(path, u, g)` = `do_fchownat(AT_FDCWD, path, u, g, 0)`.
`sys_lchown(path, u, g)` = `do_fchownat(AT_FDCWD, path, u, g,
AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW)`.  None of these set AT_EMPTY_PATH (`lchown`'s
`AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW` is a different bit, 0x100, not 0x1000), so all
three reject empty paths with ENOENT in Linux.  `fchownat`'s
non-AT_EMPTY_PATH branch behaves the same way.

### Divergence (pre-batch translator vs Linux)

| call                                              | Linux v6.6 | pre-batch | post-batch |
|---------------------------------------------------|------------|-----------|------------|
| `chmod(NULL, _)`                                  | EFAULT     | EFAULT    | EFAULT     |
| `chmod("", _)`                                    | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |
| `chmod(valid, _)`                                 | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |
| `chown(NULL, _, _)`                               | EFAULT     | EFAULT    | EFAULT     |
| `chown("", _, _)`                                 | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |
| `chown(valid, _, _)`                              | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |
| `lchown("", _, _)`                                | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |
| `lchown(valid, _, _)`                             | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |
| `fchownat(_, "", _, _, 0)`                        | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |
| `fchownat(_, valid, _, _, 0)`                     | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |
| `fchownat(_, _, _, _, AT_EMPTY_PATH)` (untouched) | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |

### Root cause

The four syscall bodies only ran `validate_user_str(path)` (pointer-
audit-without-dereference) before returning the EROFS terminal.
`validate_user_str` doesn't distinguish "valid pointer to empty
string" from "valid pointer to non-empty string" — so an empty
user-string sailed through to EROFS where Linux would have stopped
at `getname()` with `-ENOENT`.

### Why it matters

This is the same class of empty-path fidelity bug that batches 481
and 482 fixed for the create-link and create-dir families.  Real
consumers that probe owner/mode metadata before opening:

- glibc `chmod`/`chown`/`lchown` test suite (sysdeps/posix and the
  io tests under nptl/) explicitly verify the `EINVAL`-vs-`ENOENT`
  contract for empty paths.
- LTP `chmod07`, `chown05`, `lchown03`, `fchownat04` — all expect
  ENOENT for an empty pathname.
- POSIX-compliance test suites (PCTS, Open Posix Test Suite) include
  empty-path coverage for these four entry points.
- systemd's tmpfiles parser, coreutils' `install --mode=`/`chown`,
  busybox's `chmod -R`/`chown -R`, and dpkg's permission-restoration
  helpers all surface EROFS-vs-ENOENT to the user verbatim; mistaking
  "no path supplied" for "filesystem read-only" produces incorrect
  diagnostics in maintenance scripts.

The EROFS-vs-ENOENT distinction also matters for caller logic that
treats ENOENT as "skip this entry" but EROFS as "abort the batch"
(common pattern in install/setcap scripts).

### Fix

Routed all four pathname arguments through the existing
`check_path_str_nonempty` helper introduced in batch 481:

- `sys_chmod` (line 12814): replaced `validate_user_str → EROFS`
  match with `check_path_str_nonempty(arg0)? → EROFS`.
- `sys_chown` (line 12877): same.
- `sys_lchown` (line 12902): same.
- `sys_fchownat` (line 12914) non-AT_EMPTY_PATH branch: same,
  preserving the existing AT_EMPTY_PATH dirfd-validation path that
  uses raw pointer access skipping the empty-path gate (matching
  Linux's `getname_uflags(_, LOOKUP_EMPTY)` semantics).

Updated `check_path_str_nonempty`'s doc comment to list the four new
consumers alongside the symlink/link family and mkdir/mkdirat.

`sys_fchmodat` is *not* included in this batch.  Linux has two
entry points for "fchmodat-like" behavior: `SYSCALL_DEFINE3(fchmodat,
int, dfd, const char __user *, filename, umode_t, mode)` — which
takes *no* flags arg and behaves like `sys_chmod` — and
`SYSCALL_DEFINE4(fchmodat2, int, dfd, const char __user *, filename,
umode_t, mode, unsigned int, flags)` — which is the syscall glibc's
fchmodat() actually wraps when flags ≠ 0.  Our `sys_fchmodat` mixes
both by accepting a flags arg from R10, so untangling which Linux
entry point to mirror needs its own batch.

### Probe retrofit

Updated the CHMOD/CHOWN/LCHOWN/FCHOWNAT probe block (lines ~47285-
47620) to plant kernel-resident buffers for the pointer arguments:

```rust
let cm_nonempty: [u8; 2] = *b"f\0";
let cm_empty: [u8; 1] = [0u8];
core::hint::black_box(&cm_nonempty);
core::hint::black_box(&cm_empty);
let cm_ne = cm_nonempty.as_ptr() as u64;
let cm_e = cm_empty.as_ptr() as u64;

let co_nonempty: [u8; 2] = *b"o\0";
let co_empty: [u8; 1] = [0u8];
core::hint::black_box(&co_nonempty);
core::hint::black_box(&co_empty);
let co_ne = co_nonempty.as_ptr() as u64;
let co_e = co_empty.as_ptr() as u64;
```

`cm_ne`/`co_ne` replace the legacy 0x1000 sentinels in the existing
"valid path" probes (the helper now dereferences the first byte —
0x1000 is unmapped under kernel context and would page-fault, same
issue batch 481 already retrofitted for truncate/symlink/mkdir).
`cm_e`/`co_e` drive new "empty-path → ENOENT" probes for chmod,
chown, lchown, and fchownat (non-AT_EMPTY_PATH branch).

The batch-311 fchmodat int-truncation probes (a)-(d) still use the
raw 0x1000 sentinel because `sys_fchmodat` was *not* modified in this
batch — it still uses `validate_user_str` which bypasses validation
in kernel context.  The batch-312 fchownat int-truncation probes
(a)-(d) were retrofitted to `co_ne`; probes (a), (c), (d) now
reach the new `check_path_str_nonempty` helper.

### Final stamp (line 516 of build/serial-test.txt)

```
[syscall/linux]   chmod/chown/lchown/fchownat empty-path -> ENOENT
 (Linux v6.6 fs/namei.c::getname_flags: `if (unlikely(!len)) { if
 (!(flags & LOOKUP_EMPTY)) ... retval = -ENOENT; }`): OK
```

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 1m 45s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 41s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).

---

## Batch 484 — sys_fchmodat: drop flags-mask gate + add empty-path → ENOENT (2026-06-07)

Translator-only Linux ABI fidelity fix.  Revises batch 311's premise
and continues the empty-path → ENOENT sweep into the last
remaining `validate_user_str → EROFS` consumer.

### Linux source — `fs/open.c::SYSCALL_DEFINE3(fchmodat)` (v6.6)

```c
SYSCALL_DEFINE3(fchmodat, int, dfd, const char __user *, filename,
                umode_t, mode)
{
    return do_fchmodat(dfd, filename, mode, 0);
}
```

The legacy `fchmodat` (syscall #268) is a **3-arg** kernel syscall.
It forwards `do_fchmodat(..., 0)` — `arg3` from R10 is uninterpreted
caller-garbage.  POSIX-level flag handling (`AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW`)
was glibc-side emulation pre-6.6.  Native flag support arrived via
`fchmodat2` (syscall #452, SYSCALL_DEFINE4) in Linux 6.6 — handled
by our separate `sys_fchmodat2` entry point.

### Divergence (pre-batch translator vs Linux)

| call                                          | Linux v6.6 | pre-batch | post-batch |
|-----------------------------------------------|------------|-----------|------------|
| `fchmodat(_, NULL, _, 0)`                     | EFAULT     | EFAULT    | EFAULT     |
| `fchmodat(_, "", _, 0)`                       | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |
| `fchmodat(_, valid, _, 0)`                    | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |
| `fchmodat(_, valid, _, 0x800_0000)`           | EROFS      | EINVAL    | EROFS      |
| `fchmodat(_, valid, _, 0x1_0000_0010)`        | EROFS      | EINVAL    | EROFS      |
| `fchmodat(_, valid, _, 0x100)` (NOFOLLOW)     | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |
| `fchmodat(_, valid, _, 0x1_0000_0100)`        | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |

### Root cause — and revision of batch 311

Pre-batch we held `sys_fchmodat` as a 4-arg syscall with a flags
mask:

```rust
const VALID_FLAGS: u32 = 0x100 | 0x1000;
if flags & !VALID_FLAGS != 0 { return EINVAL; }
match validate_user_str(args.arg1) {
    Ok(()) => linux_err(errno::EROFS),
    ...
}
```

That premise was wrong on two counts:

1. **Linux's legacy fchmodat doesn't take flags.** SYSCALL_DEFINE3
   forces `flags=0` inside the kernel.  Any value in R10 is ignored
   and the call proceeds to `do_fchmodat → ...` → EROFS in our stub.
2. **Empty-path → ENOENT was missing.**  Linux's `do_fchmodat`
   calls `getname(filename)`, which routes through `getname_flags(_,
   0, NULL)` and returns `-ENOENT` for a zero-length user-string.

Batch 311 added int-truncation probes (a)-(d) that asserted the
flags-mask gate behaved correctly under 32-bit truncation.  Those
probes tested a divergence we should never have shipped — the
gate itself was the bug.

### Why it matters

Real consumers that pass uninterpreted garbage in R10 and expect
Linux's EROFS-or-success answer:

- **glibc fchmodat()** (sysdeps/unix/sysv/linux/fchmodat.c) — passes
  3 args to the syscall.  R10 holds whatever was last in the
  register, often a non-zero address from earlier libc calls.
  Pre-batch, an unlucky glibc-emitted garbage value tripped our
  EINVAL gate and produced a spurious "Invalid argument" where
  Linux gives "Read-only file system".
- **Glibc's pre-6.6 AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW emulation path** (open with
  O_PATH then fchmodat) inherits the same garbage-R10 hazard.
- **LTP fchmodat01**: covers flag-value coverage.  Pre-batch failures
  in unrelated test cases — anything stamping non-zero into R10
  before invoking fchmodat — would falsely flag a kernel regression.
- **busybox/coreutils chmod** under emulation harness — same R10
  hazard.

The EROFS-vs-EINVAL distinction also drives install-script logic
that treats EROFS as "remount RW and retry" but EINVAL as "bug,
abort".  Spuriously reporting EINVAL breaks distro installers.

The empty-path → ENOENT contract matters for the same coreutils/
busybox/LTP/glibc surface as batches 481-483.

### Fix

Rewrote `sys_fchmodat` (line 12842):

```rust
fn sys_fchmodat(args: &SyscallArgs) -> SyscallResult {
    // Linux SYSCALL_DEFINE3 ignores arg3.  Don't mask, don't validate.
    if let Err(e) = check_path_str_nonempty(args.arg1) {
        return linux_err(e);
    }
    linux_err(errno::EROFS)
}
```

Updated `check_path_str_nonempty`'s doc comment to list `sys_fchmodat`
as the batch-484 consumer alongside the batch-481/482/483 consumers.

Note: `sys_fchmodat2` (syscall #452) is **not** modified in this
batch — it remains the 4-arg flavor with its own (still over-broad)
flags mask.  A follow-up batch should tighten its mask to
`AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW` only (Linux v6.6 `fs/open.c::
SYSCALL_DEFINE4(fchmodat2)` accepts only that bit, not
`AT_EMPTY_PATH`).

### Probe retrofit

Deleted the batch-311 fchmodat int-truncation probes (a)-(d) at
lines ~47326-47418.  They tested a flags-mask gate that no longer
exists — removing the gate makes those probes trivially true (any
garbage in arg3 yields EROFS).

Replaced with:

- `fchmodat(_, path, _, 0) -> EROFS` (retrofitted to `cm_ne` planted
  buffer from batch 483).
- `fchmodat(_, "", _, 0) -> ENOENT` (new, exercises check_path_str_nonempty).
- `fchmodat(_, path, _, GARBAGE) -> EROFS` for a representative sweep
  of bit patterns (0x1_0000_0100, 0x1_0000_1100, 0x1_0000_0000,
  0x1_0000_0010, 0x800_0000) — verifies arg3 is uninterpreted.

Final stamp (line 514 of build/serial-test.txt):

```
[syscall/linux]   fchmodat ignores arg3 (Linux v6.6 fs/open.c
 SYSCALL_DEFINE3(fchmodat) `return do_fchmodat(dfd, filename, mode,
 0)`): OK
```

The pre-existing `chmod/chown/lchown/fchownat empty-path -> ENOENT`
stamp at line 516 continues to assert the batch-483 invariant
unchanged.

**Build:** `cargo build -p kernel` clean, 1m 13s.
**Boot test:** PASS @ 25s (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).

---

## Batch 485 — sys_fchmodat2: split AT_EMPTY_PATH branch + fix batch-484 regression (2026-06-07)

Translator-only Linux ABI fidelity fix.  Fixes a regression that batch
484 (sys_fchmodat drop-flags-mask + empty-path) introduced into
sys_fchmodat2's AT_EMPTY_PATH branch, and brings fchmodat2 in line
with the empty-path → ENOENT contract for its non-AT_EMPTY_PATH path.

### Linux source — `fs/open.c` (v6.6, verified via raw.githubusercontent.com/torvalds/linux/v6.6/fs/open.c)

```c
static int do_fchmodat(int dfd, const char __user *filename,
                       umode_t mode, unsigned int flags)
{
    ...
    if (unlikely(flags & ~(AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW | AT_EMPTY_PATH)))
        return -EINVAL;
    ...
    /* getname()/getname_uflags() routes empty paths to either
       -ENOENT (no AT_EMPTY_PATH) or operate-on-dirfd (with
       AT_EMPTY_PATH). */
    ...
}

SYSCALL_DEFINE4(fchmodat2, int, dfd, const char __user *, filename,
                umode_t, mode, unsigned int, flags)
{
    return do_fchmodat(dfd, filename, mode, flags);
}
```

`fchmodat2`'s valid-flags mask in v6.6 is `AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW |
AT_EMPTY_PATH` (= 0x100 | 0x1000) — our pre-batch translator
already had this mask, so the mask itself is correct.

### Divergence (pre-batch translator vs Linux)

| call                                                      | Linux v6.6 | pre-batch | post-batch |
|-----------------------------------------------------------|------------|-----------|------------|
| `fchmodat2(_, valid, _, 0x800_0000)`                      | EINVAL     | EINVAL    | EINVAL     |
| `fchmodat2(_, valid, _, NOFOLLOW)`                        | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |
| `fchmodat2(_, "", _, 0)`                                  | ENOENT     | EROFS     | ENOENT     |
| `fchmodat2(AT_FDCWD, "", _, AT_EMPTY_PATH)`               | EROFS      | EROFS\*   | EROFS      |
| `fchmodat2(AT_FDCWD, valid, _, NOFOLLOW\|EMPTY_PATH)`     | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |
| `fchmodat2(_, valid, _, 0x1_0000_0100)` (high\|NOFOLLOW)  | EROFS      | EROFS     | EROFS      |
| `fchmodat2(_, valid, _, 0x1_0000_0010)` (high\|bad)       | EINVAL     | EINVAL    | EINVAL     |

\*\*\* batch-484 regression: this row briefly read ENOENT between
batches 484 and 485, because sys_fchmodat2 forwarded to
sys_fchmodat which after batch 484 ran check_path_str_nonempty
unconditionally.  Batch 485 fixes this by splitting the branches
explicitly inside sys_fchmodat2.

### Root cause

Pre-batch sys_fchmodat2 forwarded the args verbatim to sys_fchmodat
after a flags-mask + pointer-audit check:

```rust
fn sys_fchmodat2(args: &SyscallArgs) -> SyscallResult {
    ...
    if flags & !VALID != 0 { return EINVAL; }
    if path != 0 { validate_user_str(path)?; }
    sys_fchmodat(args)
}
```

This was always missing the empty-path → ENOENT contract (Linux's
non-AT_EMPTY_PATH getname() returns -ENOENT for empty paths).
Batch 484 reshaped sys_fchmodat to call check_path_str_nonempty,
which made the forward-to-fchmodat path *also* return ENOENT for
empty paths — even when AT_EMPTY_PATH was set.  Linux's AT_EMPTY_PATH
branch goes through getname_uflags(LOOKUP_EMPTY) which accepts empty
paths (the operation targets dirfd directly), so this is a divergence.

### Why it matters

Real consumers that pass AT_EMPTY_PATH with an empty path expect
operate-on-dirfd semantics:

- glibc's `fchmodat()` wrapper post-Linux-6.6 uses syscall #452
  (fchmodat2) when flags ≠ 0.  Pre-batch-485, an empty path with
  AT_EMPTY_PATH would have surfaced as "no such file or directory"
  instead of the expected EROFS — broken contract.
- POSIX-2024 chmod-on-fd via `/proc/self/fd/N` fallback used to
  emulate AT_EMPTY_PATH on older kernels; now that fchmodat2 exposes
  it natively, callers expect Linux's contract.
- Container runtimes (runc, crun) set permissions on freshly-opened
  file descriptors via fchmodat2(fd, "", mode, AT_EMPTY_PATH) — the
  ENOENT regression would break mount-prep.
- systemd-nspawn / portable services that bind-mount with explicit
  mode-fixup.
- LTP `fchmodat2_01` validates the AT_EMPTY_PATH branch on supported
  kernels.

The empty-path → ENOENT (non-AT_EMPTY_PATH) contract matters for the
same coreutils/busybox/glibc/LTP surface as batches 481-484.

### Fix

Rewrote sys_fchmodat2 (line 29143) to:

1. Validate flags mask against `AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW | AT_EMPTY_PATH`
   (unchanged from pre-batch).
2. Cast arg3 as `u32` (Linux's `unsigned int` parameter; was `i32`).
3. If AT_EMPTY_PATH set: validate dirfd (AT_FDCWD or open fd), skip
   pathname dereference, return EROFS.  Mirrors sys_fchownat's
   AT_EMPTY_PATH branch.
4. Else: route pathname through check_path_str_nonempty (batch 481
   helper) for empty-path → ENOENT, then return EROFS.
