    if (!task)                        // 3. ESRCH (process existence)
        return -ESRCH;
    file = __pidfd_fget(task, fd);    // 4. EBADF (target fd validity)
    if (IS_ERR(file)) return PTR_ERR(file);

So the exact order is: EINVAL (flags) -> EBADF (pidfd) -> ESRCH
(target process) -> EBADF (target fd).  A live caller holding a
VALID pidfd to a now-dead target, passing a negative target fd,
must observe ESRCH (the get_pid_task gate fires first), NOT EBADF.
The pre-batch code returned EBADF in that case.

Fix: move the `if target_fd < 0 { return EBADF }` check from before
the pidfd lookup to after the `name(target_pid).is_none() -> ESRCH`
check.  No behavioural change for any other input shape (a bad pidfd
still EBADF, a missing process still ESRCH, a valid process with a
bad fd still EBADF — just now in the correct relative order vs ESRCH).
Kept the explicit target_fd < 0 guard (defensive: linux_fd_lookup
must not be handed a negative-cast-to-usize index).

Self-test: the existing "negative target_fd -> EBADF" probe runs in
kernel context (no caller PCB), so lookup_caller_fd now surfaces
EBADF before the relocated target_fd check — same observable EBADF,
probe still passes.  Updated its comment to reflect the new order.

Build: clean (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).
Boot test: PASS @ 25s.

================================================================
Batch 521 — pidfd_send_signal takes NO flags in v6.6: any non-zero
flags value -> EINVAL (revert the 6.7 scope-flag handling)
================================================================

sys_pidfd_send_signal (~line 16892) accepted three signal-scope
flags — PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD (1), PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD_GROUP (2),
PIDFD_SIGNAL_PROCESS_GROUP (4) — with a `flags & ~MASK` + single-bit
`hweight32(flags) > 1` gate (added in batch 391, citing "Linux 6.7").

That is correct for 6.7+ but WRONG for our v6.6 fidelity target.
Verified against the authoritative v6.6 source:

  * include/uapi/linux/pidfd.h (v6.6) defines ONLY:
        #define PIDFD_NONBLOCK O_NONBLOCK
    — no PIDFD_SIGNAL_THREAD / _THREAD_GROUP / _PROCESS_GROUP and no
    PIDFD_SEND_SIGNAL_FLAGS.  (WebFetch of the raw v6.6 header.)

  * kernel/signal.c (v6.6) SYSCALL_DEFINE4(pidfd_send_signal),
    lines 3890-3892 (gh api repos/torvalds/linux/contents/
    kernel/signal.c?ref=v6.6):
        /* Enforce flags be set to 0 until we add an extension. */
        if (flags)
            return -EINVAL;

So in v6.6 ANY non-zero flags value — including the would-be scope
bits 1/2/4 — returns EINVAL at the top gate, BEFORE fdget.  This is
the same version-attribution error class that batch 519 fixed for
pidfd_open's PIDFD_THREAD (a Linux 6.9 flag).

Fix:
  * Replace the two-stage flag gate with `if flags != 0 -> EINVAL`,
    keeping the u32 truncation so flags=0x1_0000_0000 truncates to 0
    and falls through to fdget (EBADF in kernel ctx), matching the
    ABI behaviour already documented.
  * Remove the now-dead PIDFD_SIGNAL_PROCESS_GROUP post-fdget EINVAL
    check (flags is guaranteed 0 by that point) and the three scope
    consts.
  * Self-test: the three scope-flag probes (flags=1/2/4 + bad fd)
    previously asserted EBADF (fall-through to fdget); flip them to
    EINVAL (top gate fires first).  unknown-flag (0x100) and multi-bit
    (3) probes already expected EINVAL — comments updated to cite the
    v6.6 single gate rather than the 6.7 mask/hweight gates.  flags=0
    probes (bad sig, bad sig+info, valid-args kernel-ctx) unchanged.

Build: clean (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).
Boot test: PASS @ 30s.

Batch 522 — landlock_add_rule: drop the Linux 6.7 NET_PORT rule
type and the Linux 6.10 IOCTL_DEV access bit (v6.6 fidelity)
================================================================

sys_landlock_add_rule (~line 22858) accepted two things that do not
exist in our v6.6 fidelity target, each with a comment that itself
attributed them to a post-6.6 kernel (same bug shape as batches
519/521 in the pidfd family):

  1. rule_type == 2 (LANDLOCK_RULE_NET_PORT) — the `matches!(1 | 2)`
     gate let it through to the NET access-mask / port checks and the
     EOPNOTSUPP terminal.  NET_PORT landed in Linux 6.7.
  2. LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_MASK = 0xffff — the 0x8000 bit is
     LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_IOCTL_DEV, added in Linux 6.10.

Verified against the authoritative v6.6 source
(WebFetch of raw v6.6 include/uapi/linux/landlock.h):
  * `enum landlock_rule_type` defines ONLY
    `LANDLOCK_RULE_PATH_BENEATH = 1`.  No NET_PORT.
  * FS access flags run only EXECUTE(1<<0)..TRUNCATE(1<<14) = 0x7fff.
    No LANDLOCK_ACCESS_NET_* defines, no IOCTL_DEV.

So in v6.6: rule_type=2 hits the switch `default: return -EINVAL`,
and an allowed_access with bit 0x8000 set is outside the FS mask
(EINVAL).

Fix:
  * rule_type gate: `matches!(1 | 2)` -> `rule_type != 1` (NET_PORT
    now EINVAL at the gate, before the attr/access/port checks).
  * LANDLOCK_ACCESS_FS_MASK: 0xffff -> 0x7fff; removed the now-unused
    LANDLOCK_ACCESS_NET_MASK const.
  * Collapsed the per-rule-type `match { 1 => .., 2 => .., _ }` to the
    single PATH_BENEATH FS-mask subset check (rule_type guaranteed 1).
  * Doc comment rewritten to state v6.6 semantics and cite the source.

Self-test:
  * PATH_BENEATH bad-access probe: changed the test bit from 0x10000
    to 0x8000 (IOCTL_DEV) so it specifically pins the v6.6 mask
    boundary (a 6.10 bit must be rejected).
  * Replaced the three NET_PORT probes (valid->EOPNOTSUPP, bad-access,
    port>65535) with one probe: rule_type=2 + valid-looking attr ->
    EINVAL (NET_PORT is 6.7).
  * rule_type high-half-truncation probe (b): rule_type=0x1_0000_0002
    truncates to 2 -> now EINVAL (was EFAULT via the NET_PORT attr=NULL
    path); still exercises the truncation (EINVAL comes from low=2).

Build: clean (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).
Boot test: PASS @ 28s.

Follow-up queued for next batch: landlock_create_ruleset still has a
LANDLOCK_CREATE_RULESET_ERRATA (0x2) special-case; ERRATA is absent
from v6.6 (header defines only _VERSION = 1<<0), so flags=0x2 should
fall through to the generic EINVAL rather than the ERRATA EOPNOTSUPP
query path.   [DONE — Batch 523 below.]

================================================================
Batch 523 — landlock_create_ruleset: drop the Linux 6.10 ERRATA
(0x2) flag special-case (v6.6 fidelity)
================================================================

sys_landlock_create_ruleset (~line 22744) had a dedicated branch for
`flags == LANDLOCK_CREATE_RULESET_ERRATA (0x2)` (with attr=NULL,
size=0) that returned EOPNOTSUPP, mirroring the VERSION-query branch.
This is the same version-attribution bug shape as batches 519/521/522:
ERRATA is a Linux 6.10 addition and does NOT exist in v6.6.

Verified against the authoritative v6.6 source
(gh api repos/torvalds/linux/contents/security/landlock/syscalls.c
?ref=v6.6).  The literal v6.6 gate is:

    if (!landlock_initialized)
        return -EOPNOTSUPP;
    if (flags) {
        if ((flags == LANDLOCK_CREATE_RULESET_VERSION) && !attr && !size)
            return LANDLOCK_ABI_VERSION;   /* = 3 in v6.6 */
        return -EINVAL;
    }

The ONLY recognised flag is VERSION (0x1).  ERRATA (0x2) is therefore
an unknown flag and must hit the `return -EINVAL` arm — there is no
errata-query path in v6.6.  (LANDLOCK_ABI_VERSION is 3 in v6.6, not the
"6 in 6.10+" the old doc comment claimed; we model
!landlock_initialized so we still answer EOPNOTSUPP on the VERSION
query rather than 3, per the batch-384 hybrid model.)

Fix:
  * Removed the `LANDLOCK_CREATE_RULESET_ERRATA` const and the entire
    `if flags == ERRATA && attr==0 && size==0 { return EOPNOTSUPP }`
    branch, so flags=0x2 falls through to the generic
    `return linux_err(errno::EINVAL)`.
  * VERSION-query branch (flags=0x1, attr=0, size=0 -> EOPNOTSUPP)
    kept unchanged — that is the one real v6.6 flag, and the
    !landlock_initialized hybrid model keeps it at EOPNOTSUPP.
  * Doc comment: corrected the ABI-version note (= 3 in v6.6) and
    rewrote the flag list to state VERSION is the only v6.6 flag, with
    ERRATA explicitly called out as a 6.10 addition handled by the
    generic EINVAL arm.

Self-test:
  * flags=0x2 probe (was "errata-query -> EOPNOTSUPP") flipped to
    expect EINVAL; success message reworded to
    "VERSION query -> EOPNOTSUPP, ERRATA(0x2, 6.10) -> EINVAL: OK".
  * flags=0x3 and flags=0x2+size probes already expected EINVAL
    (unchanged behaviour); their comments were corrected to stop
    describing ERRATA as a real flag.

Build: clean (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).
Boot test: PASS @ 28s.

Batch 524 — landlock_restrict_self: reject all flags (v6.6 has
no flags; LOG_* bits are 6.10/6.12 additions)
================================================================

sys_landlock_restrict_self (~line 22964) accepted three flag bits as
valid: LANDLOCK_RESTRICT_SELF_FLAGS = (1<<0)|(1<<1)|(1<<2).  Its own
doc comment attributed them to post-6.6 kernels — the same version-
attribution bug shape as batches 519/521/522/523:

  * LOG_SAME_EXEC_OFF  (1<<0) — Linux 6.10
  * LOG_NEW_EXEC_ON    (1<<1) — Linux 6.10
  * LOG_SUBDOMAINS_OFF (1<<2) — Linux 6.12

Verified against the authoritative v6.6 source
(gh api repos/torvalds/linux/contents/security/landlock/syscalls.c
?ref=v6.6).  SYSCALL_DEFINE2(landlock_restrict_self) gates:

    if (!landlock_initialized)            return -EOPNOTSUPP;
    if (!task_no_new_privs(...) && !CAP)  return -EPERM;
    /* No flag for now. */
    if (flags)                            return -EINVAL;

v6.6's only valid flags value is 0.  The prior "accept the LOG bits"
change was itself the bug — the behaviour it called "pre-batch"
(reject any non-zero flags) was the v6.6-correct behaviour.

Fix:
  * Replaced `const LANDLOCK_RESTRICT_SELF_FLAGS` + the
    `flags & ~FLAGS != 0` mask check with the v6.6 `if flags != 0 {
    return EINVAL }`.  Kept the `args.arg1 as u32` truncation (AMD64
    int args are not zero-extended) so high-half garbage is stripped
    before the != 0 test.
  * Doc comment rewritten to state v6.6 has no flags and to call out
    the LOG_* bits as 6.10/6.12 additions that must be rejected.
  * Terminal unchanged: EOPNOTSUPP (batch-384/396 !landlock_initialized
    hybrid model — flags=0 reaches the modelled terminal).

Self-test:
  * flags 0x1/0x2/0x4/0x7 probes (were EOPNOTSUPP "valid LOG flags")
    flipped to EINVAL with comments naming the introducing kernel.
  * Truncation probes: (a) 0x1_0000_0001 and (b) 0x1_0000_0007 flipped
    to EINVAL (low half non-zero).  (c) 0x1_0000_0000 unchanged
    (EOPNOTSUPP) — this is now the sole probe proving the high half is
    stripped (low=0 reaches the terminal; an un-truncated value would
    be non-zero -> EINVAL).  (d) 0x1_0000_0008 unchanged (EINVAL).
  * Reworded the section success line.

Build: clean (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).
Boot test: PASS @ 27s.
