Batch 525 — preadv2/pwritev2: restrict RWF flag mask to v6.6
RWF_SUPPORTED (0x1F); reject the 6.9/6.11/6.14 RWF bits
================================================================

sys_preadv2 (~line 29875) and sys_pwritev2 (~line 29917) both used
`const RWF_VALID: u32 = 1|2|4|8|16|32|64|128` (= 0xFF).  The preadv2
comment even read "RWF_* mask, kernel >= 6.11 maximum bits" — an
explicit admission it was sourced from a post-6.6 kernel (same
version-attribution bug class as batches 519/521/522/523/524).

Verified against the authoritative v6.6 source
(gh api repos/torvalds/linux/contents/include/uapi/linux/fs.h?ref=v6.6):

    #define RWF_HIPRI   0x1
    #define RWF_DSYNC   0x2
    #define RWF_SYNC    0x4
    #define RWF_NOWAIT  0x8
    #define RWF_APPEND  0x10
    #define RWF_SUPPORTED (RWF_HIPRI | RWF_DSYNC | RWF_SYNC | RWF_NOWAIT |
                           RWF_APPEND)        /* = 0x1F */

The bits above APPEND are later additions and do NOT exist in v6.6:
  * RWF_NOAPPEND  (0x20) — Linux 6.9
  * RWF_ATOMIC    (0x40) — Linux 6.11
  * RWF_DONTCACHE (0x80) — Linux 6.14

v6.6's kiocb_set_rw_flags() returns -EOPNOTSUPP for any bit outside
RWF_SUPPORTED, so flags=0x20/0x40/0x80 must be EOPNOTSUPP (our error
code was already EOPNOTSUPP — only the mask was too permissive).

Fix:
  * RWF_VALID: 0xFF -> 0x1F at both call sites.
  * preadv2 doc comment rewritten to list the v6.6 RWF_SUPPORTED set
    and annotate each excluded bit with its introducing kernel; added
    a cross-reference note on pwritev2.

Self-test:
  * Kept the existing high-bit (0x10_0000) EOPNOTSUPP probe.
  * Added boundary probes: preadv2 RWF_APPEND(0x10) must pass the mask
    and reach EBADF (kernel ctx, no fd table); preadv2 and pwritev2
    RWF_NOAPPEND(0x20, 6.9) must be rejected with EOPNOTSUPP.  Pins the
    v6.6 mask boundary against the 6.9/6.11/6.14 RWF additions.

Build: clean (`bash scripts/boot-test.sh`).
Boot test: PASS @ 26s.

================================================================
Batch 526 — v6.6 attribution-comment cleanup (swapon / seccomp /
fanotify) + forward-compat-feature policy question (F_DUPFD_QUERY)
================================================================

Comment-only fixes (values were already v6.6-correct; only the doc
labels said "Linux 6.10", which risked misleading a future session
into thinking the masks include post-6.6 bits):

  * sys_swapon: SWAP_FLAGS_VALID = 0x7_FFFF.  Verified against
    include/linux/swap.h@v6.6 — the five PRIO/PREFER/DISCARD* bits are
    unchanged since Linux 3.x; no post-6.6 swap flag exists to exclude.
    Relabelled "Linux 6.10" -> "v6.6" with source citation.
  * sys_seccomp: SECCOMP_FILTER_FLAG_MASK = 0x3f.  Verified against
    include/uapi/linux/seccomp.h@v6.6 — exactly six filter flags
    (TSYNC..WAIT_KILLABLE_RECV, bit 5 highest, last added in 5.19); no
    7th bit in v6.6.  Relabelled.
  * sys_fanotify_init / sys_fanotify_mark: FANOTIFY_INIT_FLAGS tops out
    at FAN_REPORT_TARGET_FID (0x1000) — FAN_REPORT_FD_ERROR (0x2000) is
    a 6.11 addition, correctly absent; FANOTIFY_MARK_FLAGS = 0x7ff (all
    11 v6.6 mark bits).  Verified against include/uapi/linux/fanotify.h
    @v6.6.  Relabelled four "Linux 6.10" comments to "v6.6".

Commits: 8b148396 (swapon/seccomp), 5ef57a57 (fanotify).
Both boot-tested PASS (26s / 27s).

------------------------------------------------------------------
## Judgment Call / POLICY QUESTION (2026-06-10, needs human):
   forward-compat WHOLE FEATURES vs strict v6.6 fidelity
------------------------------------------------------------------

The recent ABI-fidelity sweep removed post-6.6 *flag bits* from masks
inside syscalls that DO exist in v6.6 (landlock ERRATA 6.10 → batch
523; RWF 6.9/6.11/6.14 → batch 525; etc.).  That is unambiguous: a
v6.6 syscall must not silently accept a newer flag.

A DIFFERENT category exists that the sweep has NOT touched: whole
commands/syscalls that don't exist in v6.6 at all but which we
deliberately *implemented* as forward-compat.  Two sub-cases:

  (a) Returns ENOSYS/EINVAL = same as real v6.6.  NO conflict.
      Example: futex2 (futex_wake/wait/requeue, 6.7+) returns ENOSYS,
      which is exactly what a v6.6 kernel returns for an absent
      syscall.  Keep as-is — it's both forward-safe AND v6.6-faithful.

  (b) Actually ANSWERS where v6.6 would reject.  REAL divergence.
      Example: fcntl(F_DUPFD_QUERY = 1027), a Linux 6.10 addition
      (batch 94, linux.rs ~line 43029 + handler).  We compute the
      real same-open-file-description answer; real v6.6 fcntl returns
      EINVAL for unknown cmd 1027.  So a program probing "does this
      kernel support F_DUPFD_QUERY?" concludes YES on our OS but NO on
      v6.6 — a forward-compat divergence from the stated fidelity
      target.

The question for the human: for category (b), do we want
  (i) STRICT v6.6 fidelity — make F_DUPFD_QUERY (and any future
      category-(b) feature) return the v6.6 errno (EINVAL), matching
      what a real 6.6 kernel does; or
 (ii) PRAGMATIC forward-compat — keep useful, correctly-implemented
      newer features even though they diverge from bare v6.6?

I did NOT change F_DUPFD_QUERY autonomously: it is working, tested,
and useful (io_uring fd-table validation, profiler introspection,
runtimes sharing fds), and removing a feature is a behavioral-policy
decision, not a mechanical mask fix.  Flagging for an explicit call.
If (i) is chosen, the fix is a one-line route of cmd 1027 to the
fcntl catch-all (EINVAL) plus flipping the batch-94 self-test probe.
If (ii), add a short "forward-compat features intentionally retained"
note to the Linux Compatibility Boundary section so future sweeps
don't re-flag it.

RESOLVED 2026-06-10 — user chose option (ii) "baseline + honored
extras."  F_DUPFD_QUERY is RETAINED (correctly implemented, honors
full semantics).  Policy now documented in roadmap-detailed.md
"Version-surface policy" under the Linux Compatibility Boundary
section.  Key consequences for future sweeps:
  * Do NOT strip a post-6.6 feature merely for postdating the 6.6
    baseline — only strip/EINVAL features whose semantics we do NOT
    actually implement (the "never accept-without-honoring" rule).
  * NEW obligation: keep SIBLING features consistent.  When adding a
    post-baseline feature, also implement (or deliberately document
    the absence of) the closely-related siblings a caller is likely
    to assume ship alongside it (paired *_QUERY/*_SET, same-family
    ops, same-release companion flags), so cross-feature inference
    does not hit a surprise ENOSYS.
  * The within-syscall flag-attribution fixes already done (landlock
    ERRATA, RWF mask, etc.) remain correct: those were flags we do
    NOT implement, so rejecting them is the honest answer, fully
    consistent with option (ii).

================================================================
SIBLING-CONSISTENCY GAP (identified 2026-06-10): CLONE_PIDFD
================================================================

Status: DEFERRED with plan (NOT yet implemented).  Found while
auditing the pidfd syscall family for sibling consistency after the
futex/robust-list batch.

The gap:
  * pidfd_open(2) (434), pidfd_send_signal(2) (424), pidfd_getfd(2)
    (438) all WORK — they install/consume HandleKind::PidFd entries
    (FdEntry::pidfd in kernel/src/proc/linux_fd.rs:225).
  * But clone3(CLONE_PIDFD) returns EINVAL (validate_clone3_args,
    kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs ~line 1406) and legacy clone(CLONE_PIDFD)
    SILENTLY IGNORES the flag (the bit is in neither THREAD_BITS nor
    UNSUPPORTED_BITS in linux_clone_inner, so it falls through to a
    plain fork and never writes a pidfd).
  * CLONE_PIDFD is the canonical race-free way to obtain a pidfd for a
    child you just created; a program that uses pidfd_open would
    reasonably assume clone3(CLONE_PIDFD) also yields one.  This is
    exactly the cross-feature inference surprise the option-(ii)
    sibling-consistency obligation is meant to prevent.

A stale/false comment was the tell: linux.rs:1238 used to say
"CLONE_PIDFD — we don't have a pidfd HandleKind yet."  That is wrong
(pidfd_open proves the HandleKind exists).  Fixed the comment to state
the real blocker (below) in this batch.

v6.6 semantics to implement (verified against kernel/fork.c@v6.6
copy_process + copy_clone_args_from_user):
  * Gates: CLONE_PIDFD combined with CLONE_THREAD or CLONE_DETACHED
    -> EINVAL (v6.6: "CLONE_THREAD is blocked until someone needs it";
    CLONE_DETACHED reserved).  PIDFD_THREAD (the 6.9 flag that relaxes
    the CLONE_THREAD restriction) does NOT exist in v6.6.
  * clone3: the pidfd is written to the int at *clone_args.pidfd
    (offset 8, currently parsed into the discarded `_pidfd_out`,
    linux.rs:1380).  legacy clone(2): the pidfd overloads the
    parent_tid slot (frame.arg2).
  * The pidfd file is O_RDWR | O_CLOEXEC (set entry.fd_flags =
    crate::proc::linux_fd::FD_CLOEXEC).  It refers to the CHILD pid and
    is installed in the PARENT (caller) fd table.

Implementation plan:
  1. validate_clone3_args: stop early-rejecting CLONE_PIDFD; add the
     CLONE_PIDFD & (CLONE_THREAD|CLONE_DETACHED) -> EINVAL gate; carry
     pidfd_out (buf[1]) in ClonedArgs.  For legacy clone(), add the
     same gate in linux_clone_inner and treat arg2 as the pidfd ptr.
  2. All-or-nothing ordering (the real engineering content): Linux
     reserves the pidfd BEFORE the point of no return.  Our fork path
     creates the child first, so we must either (a) reserve a parent
     fd slot + validate_user_write(pidfd_ptr, 4) BEFORE forking and
     fill in the child pid after, or (b) at minimum pre-validate
     writability + fd-table capacity before fork and install after,
     documenting the tiny TOCTOU.  Prefer (a) if linux_fd exposes a
     reserve-then-fill primitive; otherwise add one.
  3. On fork success: install FdEntry::pidfd(child_pid, O_RDWR) with
     FD_CLOEXEC into the caller table, copy_to_user the fd to the
     pidfd ptr, return child pid.  On any pre-fork failure: EFAULT /
     EMFILE with NO child created.

TESTING BLOCKER (why this isn't a quick win): the happy path forks a
real userspace child and installs a pidfd in a process fd table.
sys_pidfd_open already returns EBADF in kernel context ("no caller
PCB"), and the futex-style boot self-test harness runs in kernel
context — so it CANNOT exercise CLONE_PIDFD's success path the way the
futex PI tests (which touch only raw words + the PI table) could.
Only the negative gates (CLONE_PIDFD|CLONE_THREAD -> EINVAL, bad
pidfd ptr -> EFAULT) are kernel-context-testable.  Properly satisfying
"ship nothing untested" for the install/write-back path needs a
userspace integration-test harness for fork/exec/fd-table syscalls,
which does not exist yet.  That harness is itself a prerequisite for
honestly testing this whole class of category-(b) process/fd features
(CLONE_PIDFD, CLONE_INTO_CGROUP, pidfd_getfd round-trips, etc.) and is
the reason CLONE_PIDFD is deferred rather than implemented blind.
TRIGGER to implement: once a userspace syscall integration-test
harness exists (or alongside building one).

================================================================
Batch 527 — sched: SCHED_RESET_ON_FORK strip / store / report
================================================================

The divergence (confirmed against the documented limitation that was
in pcb.rs ~1469): SCHED_RESET_ON_FORK was neither stripped from the
legacy sched_setscheduler policy arg, nor stored, nor reported back —
so the whole flag was silently unsupported.

v6.6 behavior (kernel/sched/core.c):
  * sched_setscheduler legacy ABI ORs the flag 0x4000_0000 into the
    `policy` int.  `_sched_setscheduler` fixes up the hack:
        if (policy & SCHED_RESET_ON_FORK) {
            attr.sched_flags |= SCHED_FLAG_RESET_ON_FORK;  // 0x01
            policy &= ~SCHED_RESET_ON_FORK;
            attr.sched_policy = policy;
        }
    then __sched_setscheduler runs valid_policy() on the STRIPPED
    policy.  Pre-batch we matched on the raw policy incl. the flag bit
    so e.g. sched_setscheduler(pid, SCHED_NORMAL|RESET_ON_FORK) =
    0x4000_0000 missed every valid_policy arm and returned EINVAL.
  * sched_getscheduler returns `policy | (reset_on_fork ?
    SCHED_RESET_ON_FORK : 0)`.
  * sched_setattr/getattr use SCHED_FLAG_RESET_ON_FORK (0x01) in the
    sched_flags field (offset 8..16 of the 48-byte sched_attr v0).
    The bit was already accepted by the SCHED_FLAG_ALL mask gate
    (batch 507); now it's actually recorded and reported.
  * __sched_fork reset semantics: the child NEVER inherits the flag.
    If the parent had it set: RT(FIFO=1,RR=2)/DEADLINE(6) collapse to
    SCHED_NORMAL(0)/prio0/nice0; NORMAL(0)/BATCH(3)/IDLE(5) keep
    policy+prio but clamp negative nice to 0 (non-negative preserved).

The fix (translator + PCB bookkeeping only — our scheduler is
priority-round-robin and does not honor RT/DEADLINE; these fields are
pure ABI round-trip state):
  * kernel/src/proc/pcb.rs:
      - new field `linux_sched_reset_on_fork: bool` (default false,
        both the default-init and forked Process literals).
      - pure helper `sched_fork_child_params(policy, prio, nice,
        reset_on_fork) -> (policy, prio, nice)` encoding the
        __sched_fork reset table; called on the fork path so the
        child's recorded sched params follow v6.6 and the flag is
        always cleared in the child.
      - `get_sched_reset_on_fork` / `set_sched_reset_on_fork` helpers.
  * kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs:
      - sys_sched_setscheduler: strip 0x4000_0000 before valid_policy,
        store the flag (gates 6/7 already use the stripped value).
      - sys_sched_getscheduler: OR the flag back into the return.
      - sys_sched_setattr: persist SCHED_FLAG_RESET_ON_FORK from
        sched_flags.
      - sys_sched_getattr: report it back in sched_flags @ 8..16.

Deliberate omission (documented in the setscheduler comment): v6.6
also returns -EPERM when an *unprivileged* task CLEARS a
previously-set reset_on_fork.  We don't model the unprivileged/
privileged split with a clean capability at this gate, so we skip it
the same way other rlimit/cap gates exempt kernel-context callers.

Boot self-tests (linux.rs self_test, kernel context — pid=0 doesn't
persist, so a synthetic PCB carries the round-trip):
  * setscheduler(NORMAL|RESET_ON_FORK) -> 0; getscheduler ->
    0x4000_0000; get_sched_reset_on_fork == Some(true).
  * setscheduler(FIFO|RESET_ON_FORK, prio1) -> 0; getscheduler ->
    0x4000_0001.  Clear via plain NORMAL -> getscheduler 0,
    get_sched_reset_on_fork == Some(false).
  * setattr(sched_flags=0x01) persists + getattr reports buf[8..16]=
    0x01; clear -> getattr reports 0.
  * sched_fork_child_params direct table checks:
    (2,50,-5,t)->(0,0,0); (1,99,3,t)->(0,0,0); (6,0,7,t)->(0,0,0);
    (0,0,-5,t)->(0,0,0); (0,0,7,t)->(0,0,7); (3,0,2,t)->(3,0,2);
    (5,0,-1,t)->(5,0,0); (2,50,7,f)->(2,50,7).
  Serial stamps:
    "SCHED_RESET_ON_FORK set/get/fork round-trip (batch 527): OK"
    "sched_{set,get}attr SCHED_FLAG_RESET_ON_FORK round-trip (batch 527): OK"

Build clean, clippy clean (no new warnings), boot-test PASS (26s).
NOTE: numbered 527 (not 511) — 511 was already taken by the uname
batch; 526 was the previous highest.

----------------------------------------------------------------
SIBLING-CONSISTENCY GAP (identified 2026-06-12, during batch 527):
   sched_setattr SCHED_FLAG_KEEP_POLICY / KEEP_PARAMS semantics
----------------------------------------------------------------

Status: RESOLVED 2026-06-12 by Batch 528 (see the Batch 528 entry at
the end of this file).  The deferral plan below was implemented with
the verbatim v6.6 source in hand.  Left in place for the historical
record of the gap and the analysis.

Status: DEFERRED with plan (NOT yet implemented).  Found while doing
batch 527 (RESET_ON_FORK) — the sibling-consistency obligation in the
resolved "version-surface policy" (option ii) says when we honor one
sched_flags bit we should also honor (or deliberately document the
absence of) the same-family companion bits a caller assumes ship
together.  batch 527 made RESET_ON_FORK (0x01) round-trip; its
same-syscall siblings KEEP_POLICY (0x08) and KEEP_PARAMS (0x10) are
currently accepted-by-mask (batch 507's SCHED_FLAG_ALL=0x7F gate) but
their SEMANTICS are ignored.

The gap: sys_sched_setattr unconditionally reads attr.sched_policy /
attr.sched_priority and stores them into the PCB.  With KEEP_POLICY a
caller means "keep my current policy, ignore attr.sched_policy"; with
KEEP_PARAMS "keep my current priority/nice".  We overwrite anyway, so
e.g. a FIFO task doing setattr(KEEP_POLICY, sched_policy=0) to tweak
only nice gets its policy clobbered to SCHED_NORMAL — observable via
sched_getattr / sched_getscheduler.

v6.6 mechanism (as understood — MUST be verified verbatim against
kernel/sched/syscalls.c before implementing; this is the reason it's
deferred rather than done now):
  * SYSCALL_DEFINE3(sched_setattr): after sched_copy_attr,
      if ((int)attr.sched_policy < 0) return -EINVAL;        // pre-gate
      if (attr.sched_flags & SCHED_FLAG_KEEP_POLICY)
          attr.sched_policy = SETPARAM_POLICY;  // SETPARAM_POLICY = -1
    So even with KEEP_POLICY the *input* sched_policy must be >= 0, but
    its value is then discarded.
  * __sched_setscheduler: `if (policy < 0) { reset_on_fork =
    p->sched_reset_on_fork; policy = oldpolicy = p->policy; }` — note
    under KEEP_POLICY the effective policy AND reset_on_fork come from
    the TASK, not from attr.sched_flags (so RESET_ON_FORK in the same
    call is ignored when KEEP_POLICY is set — a subtle interaction with
    batch 527 that must be preserved).
  * KEEP_PARAMS: skips the priority/nice change (keep current).  The
    exact code path (the "nothing changed" fast-path vs. an explicit
    keep) is the part I'm least sure of and is precisely why this needs
    source verification.

Implementation plan (once verified):
  1. After reading attr, if sched_flags & KEEP_POLICY: substitute the
     effective policy with the target PCB's current
     linux_sched_policy, and take reset_on_fork from the PCB's current
     linux_sched_reset_on_fork (NOT from attr) — guard the pre-gate
     that attr.sched_policy still had to be >= 0.
  2. Run sched_priority_check_for_policy against the EFFECTIVE policy.
  3. If sched_flags & KEEP_PARAMS: do not call set_sched_priority
     (and skip nice once nice is wired through setattr).
  4. Store only the fields not "kept".
  5. Boot self-tests on a synthetic PCB: set FIFO/prio via
     setscheduler, then setattr(KEEP_POLICY, sched_policy=0) and
     verify getscheduler still reports FIFO; setattr(KEEP_PARAMS) and
     verify priority unchanged; verify KEEP_POLICY makes a same-call
     RESET_ON_FORK bit be ignored (taken from task instead).

TRIGGER to implement: next time the v6.6 sched-syscall source is in
hand to quote verbatim (the campaign's citation bar), fold this into a
batch alongside re-reading __sched_setscheduler's KEEP_PARAMS path.
Low user-visible urgency (no known program depends on KEEP_* against
our OS yet); tracked here so it isn't re-discovered from scratch.

SOURCE-ACCESS NOTE (2026-06-12): attempted to pull the verbatim v6.6
source via WebFetch and hit dead-ends — recording them so a future
session skips the fumbling:
  * In v6.6 these functions live in kernel/sched/core.c (NOT
    syscalls.c — the kernel/sched/syscalls.c split is a LATER release;
    the existing batch-507 note's "syscalls.c" path label is the
    modern name, not the v6.6 location).
  * raw.githubusercontent.com/torvalds/linux/v6.6/kernel/sched/core.c
    is ~11k+ lines; WebFetch only delivers the file HEAD and truncates
    before reaching __sched_setscheduler / sched_setattr (deep in the
    file), so it can't quote them.
  * elixir.bootlin.com source pages are JS-rendered — WebFetch gets
    only the navigation chrome, not the code.
  * v6.7 and v6.10 .../kernel/sched/syscalls.c both 404 at those tags
    (split not present yet at those versions).
  Better approach to try next: fetch the specific function from a
  line-anchored cgit "plain" view of a SMALL range, or fetch the
  version where syscalls.c first exists (verify the exact tag), read
  the logic there, then confirm the KEEP_* lines are byte-identical to
  v6.6 core.c (the split was a mechanical move) before citing v6.6.

================================================================
Batch 528 — Linux ABI fidelity: sched_setattr SCHED_FLAG_KEEP_POLICY
   (0x08) / SCHED_FLAG_KEEP_PARAMS (0x10) semantics
================================================================
Done 2026-06-12.  Resolves the "SIBLING-CONSISTENCY GAP" deferral
above (companion bits to the RESET_ON_FORK bit batch 527 wired up).

Source obtained verbatim this time: the source-access note above is
now stale for the fetch method — `gh api -H "Accept:
application/vnd.github.raw" repos/torvalds/linux/contents/kernel/sched/
core.c?ref=v6.6` returns the FULL 12103-line core.c (WebFetch
truncates; bootlin is JS-rendered).  In v6.6 the sched syscalls live
in kernel/sched/core.c (the kernel/sched/syscalls.c split first
appears in v6.11).  Grep can't read /tmp, so it was copied to
build/v66_core.c (gitignored) to grep, then removed.

What v6.6 does (kernel/sched/core.c, verbatim-confirmed):
  SYSCALL_DEFINE3(sched_setattr):
    if ((int)attr.sched_policy < 0) return -EINVAL;       // pre-gate
    if (attr.sched_flags & SCHED_FLAG_KEEP_POLICY)
        attr.sched_policy = SETPARAM_POLICY;   // = -1
    ... find_process_by_pid (ESRCH) ...
    if (likely(p)) {
        if (attr.sched_flags & SCHED_FLAG_KEEP_PARAMS)
            get_params(p, &attr);              // pre-loads current params
        retval = sched_setattr(p, &attr);
    }
  get_params: RT task -> attr->sched_priority = p->rt_priority;
              fair task -> attr->sched_nice = task_nice(p).
  __sched_setscheduler recheck: policy<0 -> reset_on_fork =
    p->sched_reset_on_fork; policy = p->policy (valid_policy skipped).
    Else reset_on_fork = !!(attr RESET_ON_FORK bit); valid_policy req.
    Store: p->sched_reset_on_fork = reset_on_fork (UNCONDITIONAL);
    if (!KEEP_PARAMS) __setscheduler_params (writes policy + priority).

Consequences reproduced in sys_sched_setattr (PCB bookkeeping; the
kernel scheduler is priority-round-robin, these fields are ABI
round-trip state read back by sched_get{scheduler,attr}):
  * KEEP_POLICY: effective policy AND reset_on_fork come from the
    TASK — the attr sched_policy value is discarded (only its >=0-ness
    is checked by the pre-gate) and a same-call RESET_ON_FORK bit is
    IGNORED.  valid_policy() is NOT re-checked.  The attr PRIORITY is
    still used (KEEP_POLICY != KEEP_PARAMS), so a kept RT policy still
    requires a valid RT priority in the attr.
  * KEEP_PARAMS: get_params pre-loads the task's current rt_priority
    (RT) before validity checks, and __setscheduler_params is skipped
    so neither policy nor priority is stored (only reset_on_fork).
  * Pre-batch (507) both flags were accepted by the sched_flags mask
    but ignored: we always stored the input policy/priority and took
    reset_on_fork from the attr bit — so e.g. a FIFO task doing
    setattr(KEEP_POLICY, sched_policy=0, prio=5) was clobbered to
    SCHED_NORMAL instead of staying FIFO.

Implementation (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs sys_sched_setattr):
  restructured to v6.6 order — pre-gate ((int)policy<0 -> EINVAL) ->
  resolve target pid + ESRCH up front -> fetch current
  policy/priority/reset_on_fork -> KEEP_POLICY effective-policy /
  reset_on_fork resolution -> DEADLINE EOPNOTSUPP concession (now keyed
  on effective_policy) -> sched_flags mask gate (batch 507) ->
  KEEP_PARAMS rt_priority pre-load -> priority validity (batch's shared
  helper) against effective_policy -> RLIMIT_RTPRIO -> store
  (reset_on_fork unconditional; policy+priority only when !KEEP_PARAMS).

Tests: boot self-test on a synthetic PCB (kpkp-b528): seed FIFO
prio=10 via setscheduler; (a) KEEP_PARAMS+FIFO+prio99 -> stays
FIFO/prio10; (b) KEEP_POLICY+BATCH(ignored)+prio5 -> stays FIFO,
prio updates to 5; (c) KEEP_POLICY|RESET_ON_FORK+prio5 -> reset_on_fork
stays false (bit ignored); (d) plain setattr RR/7 -> stores RR/7
(regression guard the keep flags don't over-suppress).  Serial stamp:
"sched_setattr SCHED_FLAG_KEEP_POLICY/KEEP_PARAMS semantics
(batch 528): OK".  cargo build + clippy + boot-test all pass.

================================================================
Batch 529 — Linux ABI fidelity: nice round-trips through the
   sched_attr ABI (sched_setattr / sched_getattr sched_nice field)
================================================================
Done 2026-06-12.  Source verbatim from v6.6 kernel/sched/core.c
(fetched via gh api raw, as in batch 528).

The gap: setpriority/getpriority already round-trip nice through the
PCB (pcb::set_nice / get_nice), but the sched_attr ABI did not —
sched_getattr hardcoded the sched_nice field (offset 16..20) to 0, and
sched_setattr never read the nice field at all.  So a process that set
its nice via setpriority saw 0 from sched_getattr (inconsistent with
getpriority reading the same field), and sched_setattr(SCHED_NORMAL,
nice=N) silently dropped N.  This was the loose end explicitly flagged
in the batch 528 entry ("skip nice once nice is wired through setattr").

What v6.6 does (verbatim-confirmed, kernel/sched/core.c):
  * sched_copy_attr: attr->sched_nice = clamp(attr->sched_nice,
    MIN_NICE, MAX_NICE)  -> [-20, 19], unconditional, lenient (saturate
    not reject).
  * get_params(p, attr) (used for KEEP_PARAMS pre-load AND by
    sched_getattr): dispatches on the TASK's CURRENT class —
      dl  -> __getparam_dl
      rt  -> attr->sched_priority = p->rt_priority
      else (fair: NORMAL/BATCH/IDLE) -> attr->sched_nice = task_nice(p)
  * __setscheduler_params (the !KEEP_PARAMS store path):
      fair_policy(policy) -> p->static_prio = NICE_TO_PRIO(nice)
    where fair_policy() == (policy==SCHED_NORMAL || policy==SCHED_BATCH)
    — NOTE it EXCLUDES SCHED_IDLE(5).  So switching to IDLE does NOT
    overwrite the stored nice (and getattr's non-RT branch still
    reports the prior nice).
  * user_check_sched_setscheduler: fair task lowering nice without
    privilege -> EPERM (RLIMIT_NICE / is_nice_reduction).

Implementation (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs):
  sched_getattr: report nice = get_nice for non-RT policies {0,3,5};
    0 for RT {1,2} (DL=6 never stored).  Write to buf[16..20] as i32.
  sched_setattr: read+clamp nice@16 to [-20,19]; compute effective_nice
    (KEEP_PARAMS pre-loads cur_nice for a currently-fair task);
    RLIMIT_NICE gate when storing a fair nice that drops below 0 (same
    gate setpriority applies — they write the same field); store via
    set_nice only when !KEEP_PARAMS && effective_policy ∈ {0,3}.
  ALSO corrected a batch-528 subtlety: the KEEP_PARAMS rt_priority
    substitution now keys on the TASK's current class (cur_is_rt) rather
    than the requested effective_policy, matching get_params' dispatch
    on the task — so KEEP_PARAMS while switching a fair task to RT keeps
    the user-supplied priority instead of a phantom zero.  (No behavior
    change for the common KEEP_PARAMS-without-policy-change / KEEP_ALL
    case where effective_policy == cur_policy; batch 528's self-tests
    still pass.)

Tests: boot self-test on synthetic PCB (nice-b529) — (a) setpriority
nice=10 -> getattr reports 10 (setpriority/getattr consistency); (b)
setattr(NORMAL,nice=5) stores 5; (c) setattr(KEEP_PARAMS,nice=99)
preserves 5; (d) setattr(BATCH,nice=-3) stores negative; (e)
setattr(IDLE) keeps nice=-3 (fair_policy excludes IDLE) yet getattr
reports it; (f) setattr(FIFO,prio=10) -> getattr nice=0/prio=10 (RT
branch).  Serial stamp "sched_setattr/getattr nice round-trip
(batch 529): OK".  cargo build + clippy (baseline, no new warnings) +
boot-test all pass.

================================================================
ABI fidelity batch 530 — clock_getres per-clock resolution +
coarse-clock coarsening (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
================================================================
Divergence: sys_clock_getres returned tv_nsec=1 (1ns) for EVERY valid
clockid.  v6.6's k_clock.clock_getres handlers report per-clock-class
resolution:
  * High-res clocks {REALTIME(0), MONOTONIC(1), MONOTONIC_RAW(4),
    BOOTTIME(7), REALTIME_ALARM(8), BOOTTIME_ALARM(9), TAI(11)} ->
    posix_get_hrtimer_res -> hrtimer_resolution (1ns when high-res
    timers active; our hrtimer is ns-granular).  ALREADY FAITHFUL.
  * Well-known CPU-time clocks {PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID(2),
    THREAD_CPUTIME_ID(3)} -> posix_cpu_clock_getres.  These are built
    as PROCESS_CLOCK/THREAD_CLOCK = make_*_cpuclock(0, CPUCLOCK_SCHED),
    so CPUCLOCK_WHICH(...) == CPUCLOCK_SCHED and the handler reports
    tv_nsec = 1 (1ns), NOT (NSEC_PER_SEC+HZ-1)/HZ.  ALREADY FAITHFUL.
    (The 10ms ceil branch only applies to PROF/VIRT CPU clocks, which
    are reachable only via negative fd-encoded dynamic clockids that we
    don't implement.)
  * Coarse clocks {REALTIME_COARSE(5), MONOTONIC_COARSE(6)} ->
    posix_get_coarse_res -> KTIME_LOW_RES = TICK_NSEC = 10ms at our
    HZ=100.  THIS was the real divergence (we said 1ns).

Verbatim v6.6 citations (kernel/time/posix-timers.c):
  static int posix_get_coarse_res(...) { *tp =
      ktime_to_timespec64(KTIME_LOW_RES); return 0; }
  static int posix_get_hrtimer_res(...) { tp->tv_sec=0;
      tp->tv_nsec=hrtimer_resolution; return 0; }
  clock_realtime/monotonic/monotonic_raw/tai/boottime/...:
      .clock_getres = posix_get_hrtimer_res;
  clock_realtime_coarse/clock_monotonic_coarse:
      .clock_getres = posix_get_coarse_res;
(kernel/time/posix-cpu-timers.c):
  posix_cpu_clock_getres: tp->tv_nsec = ((NSEC_PER_SEC+HZ-1)/HZ);
      if (CPUCLOCK_WHICH(which_clock)==CPUCLOCK_SCHED) tp->tv_nsec=1;
  #define PROCESS_CLOCK make_process_cpuclock(0, CPUCLOCK_SCHED)
  #define THREAD_CLOCK  make_thread_cpuclock(0, CPUCLOCK_SCHED)

Fix — the PROPER fix, not just patching getres:
  Reporting 10ms in getres alone would make getres lie relative to
  clock_gettime, which returned FULL-resolution values for coarse
  clockids (REALTIME_COARSE routed to clock_realtime(), MONOTONIC_COARSE
  to hrtimer::now_ns()).  On Linux a coarse clock genuinely advances
  only at the periodic tick (ktime_get_coarse_*), so its values change
  only every TICK_NSEC and getres/gettime agree.  To stay self-
  consistent AND match Linux semantics we made the coarse clocks
  actually coarse:
    1. sys_clock_gettime: for clockid 5/6, floor the high-res reading to
       the TICK_NSEC (10ms) grid: ns.checked_div(10_000_000).map_or(ns,
       |q| q.saturating_mul(10_000_000)).  Distinct values now appear
       only every 10ms and are always <= true time (a past-tick
       snapshot), exactly like Linux's cached coarse time.
    2. sys_clock_getres: report tv_nsec=10_000_000 for clockid 5/6,
       tv_nsec=1 for every other valid clock.
  HZ=100 is our established model-wide jiffies rate (sys_times uses
  clock_monotonic()/10_000_000); TICK_NSEC=1e9/100=10ms is consistent
  with it.

Decision (Claude autonomous): chose the consistent fix (coarsen gettime
+ 10ms getres) over either (a) reporting fake 10ms getres while gettime
stays high-res — internally inconsistent — or (b) leaving both at 1ns —
diverges from Linux's coarse-clock semantics.  Coarse clocks explicitly
mean "approximate/low-res", so flooring opted-in callers to the 10ms
grid is faithful and cannot break a correct program.  Reversible (pure
translator change; no PCB/state).

Tests: extended the existing clock_getres boot self-test —
  * REALTIME(0) getres -> tv_nsec=1;
  * clocks {1,2,3,4,7,8,9,11} getres -> 1ns each (loop);
  * coarse {5,6} getres -> 10_000_000ns each (loop);
  * clock_gettime(MONOTONIC_COARSE) value lands on the 10ms grid
    ((sec*1e9+nsec) % 10ms == 0).
Serial stamp "clock_getres clockid gating + per-class resolution
(batch 530): OK".  cargo build clean; clippy no new warnings
(production coarse-floor uses checked_div/saturating_mul — no bare
arithmetic); boot-test PASSED (BOOT_OK 25s, stamp present, no clock
FAILs).

================================================================
ABI batch 531 — mmap MAP_FIXED alignment / advisory-hint fidelity
================================================================
Date: 2026-06-12   Decided by: Claude (autonomous)
File: kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs (translator only)

Divergence: the Linux mmap translator returned EINVAL for ANY non-zero
unaligned address hint, regardless of MAP_FIXED.  Linux only rejects an
unaligned address when MAP_FIXED is set; an unaligned address WITHOUT
MAP_FIXED is a mere advisory hint that the kernel rounds/relocates, so
the call succeeds.

Verbatim v6.6 citation (mm/mmap.c, get_unmapped_area):
  - get_unmapped_area runs `if (offset_in_page(addr)) return -EINVAL;`
    on the address it resolves.
  - For MAP_FIXED, the addr is returned unchanged (no relocation), so an
    unaligned MAP_FIXED addr trips offset_in_page -> EINVAL.
  - For non-FIXED, arch_get_unmapped_area aligns/relocates the hint, so
    offset_in_page is 0 -> no EINVAL; an unaligned hint succeeds.
  - do_mmap order: !len -> EINVAL; non-FIXED round_hint_to_min(addr);
    len align/overflow; get_unmapped_area (the FIXED-unaligned EINVAL);
    then RLIMIT_AS accounting inside mmap_region.  So the alignment
    EINVAL precedes VM accounting.

Root cause: native sys_mmap (handlers.rs:717) rejects any non-zero
unaligned vaddr_hint with BadAlignment->EINVAL, with no notion of
MAP_FIXED.  Native mmap is the wrong layer to encode Linux's
FIXED-vs-hint policy (it serves the SlateOS-native ABI too).

Fix (translator-only): added pure helper resolve_mmap_addr_hint(addr,
fixed, frame_size) -> Option<u64>:
  - FIXED + aligned   -> Some(addr)   (pass through)
  - FIXED + unaligned -> None         (caller maps to EINVAL)
  - non-FIXED aligned -> Some(addr)   (pass through)
  - non-FIXED unaligned -> Some(0)    (drop hint; let kernel pick,
                                       mirroring Linux relocation)
Wired into sys_mmap AFTER the len==0 EINVAL and BEFORE the RLIMIT_AS
charge, matching the do_mmap ordering above.  native_flags now derives
MAP_FIXED (0x01) from the same `fixed` bool.

Tests: boot self-test verifies the helper directly (side-effect-free,
unlike exercising the native mapping path which needs a real user
address space the boot task lacks):
  * FIXED+unaligned(0x1234)   -> None
  * FIXED+aligned(0x8000)     -> Some(0x8000)
  * non-FIXED+unaligned(0x1234) -> Some(0)
  * non-FIXED+aligned(0x8000) -> Some(0x8000)
  * addr==0 both modes        -> Some(0)
  * end-to-end dispatch_linux(MMAP, FIXED|unaligned) -> EINVAL (the
    alignment gate fires before any native mapping; no side effects).
Note: the native SUCCESS path is not exercisable from the boot task
(pid 0 lacks a user PML4; a real mapping could leak) — only the EINVAL
gate is dispatch-tested.

Serial stamp "mmap MAP_FIXED alignment / advisory-hint fidelity
(batch 531): OK".  cargo build clean; clippy back at 17477 baseline
(self-test EINVAL compare uses wrapping_neg to avoid arithmetic
side-effects); boot-test PASSED (BOOT_OK 26s, stamp present, no mmap
FAILs).  Reversible (pure translator change; no PCB/state).

================================================================
ABI batch 532 — mmap byte-offset page-alignment gate
================================================================
Date: 2026-06-12   Decided by: Claude (autonomous)
File: kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs (translator only)

Divergence: the mmap translator never validated the 6th argument (byte
offset).  Linux rejects a non-page-aligned offset with EINVAL as the
very first action of the syscall, before any other validation.

Verbatim v6.6 citation (arch/x86/kernel/sys_x86_64.c):
  SYSCALL_DEFINE6(mmap, unsigned long, addr, unsigned long, len,
          unsigned long, prot, unsigned long, flags,
          unsigned long, fd, unsigned long, off)
  {
      if (off & ~PAGE_MASK)
          return -EINVAL;
      return ksys_mmap_pgoff(addr, len, prot, flags, fd, off >> PAGE_SHIFT);
  }
The `off & ~PAGE_MASK` gate precedes ksys_mmap_pgoff -> vm_mmap_pgoff ->
do_mmap, hence ahead of the anon/file dispatch, the !len EINVAL, and the
MAP_FIXED alignment check.  ~PAGE_MASK == PAGE_SIZE-1; our PAGE_SIZE is
the 16 KiB frame.  The check applies even to anonymous maps (which
ignore the offset functionally) — Linux still EINVALs an unaligned one.

Fix (translator-only): added, as the first statement in sys_mmap (after
reading args, before the anon/file ENOSYS checks), 
  if (offset & (FRAME_SIZE as u64).wrapping_sub(1)) != 0 {
      return linux_err(errno::EINVAL);
  }
Placed ahead of our ENOSYS-for-unsupported-kind returns so that, as on
Linux, the offset EINVAL wins when both would apply.

Tests: boot self-test dispatches an otherwise-valid anonymous PRIVATE
mmap (len=16384, fd=-1) with off=0x1234 and asserts EINVAL; plus a
direct page-mask predicate sanity on an aligned (0x4000) and unaligned
(0x1234) offset.  The aligned-offset passthrough is NOT dispatched (the
boot task lacks a user PML4; a real mapping could leak into pid 0).
Serial stamp "mmap byte-offset page-alignment gate (batch 532): OK".
cargo build clean; clippy at 17477 baseline (EINVAL compare uses
wrapping_neg); boot-test PASSED (BOOT_OK 25s, stamp present, no mmap
FAILs).  Reversible (pure translator change; no PCB/state).

================================================================
ABI batch 533 — munmap zero-length EINVAL
================================================================
Date: 2026-06-12   Decided by: Claude (autonomous)
File: kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs (translator only)

Divergence: munmap(addr, 0) returned 0 (success) instead of EINVAL.
The native munmap (handlers.rs:892) treats size==0 as an idempotent
no-op — correct for the SlateOS-native ABI — but Linux rejects a
zero-length unmap.

Verbatim v6.6 citation (mm/mmap.c, do_vmi_munmap):
  if ((offset_in_page(start)) || start > TASK_SIZE || len > TASK_SIZE-start)
      return -EINVAL;
  end = start + PAGE_ALIGN(len);
  if (end == start)
      return -EINVAL;
For len==0, PAGE_ALIGN(0)==0 so end==start -> EINVAL.  (An unaligned
start also EINVALs via offset_in_page; the native handler already maps
that BadAlignment -> EINVAL, and since both produce the same errno,
gating len==0 first in the translator is faithful in every combination.)

Fix (translator-only): in sys_munmap, return EINVAL when len==0 before
dispatching to the native handler.  Simplified the downstream AS-refund
guard (`result.value == 0 && len != 0` -> `result.value == 0`) since
len==0 now returns early.

Tests: boot self-test dispatches munmap(addr=0x4000, len=0) and asserts
EINVAL (the gate returns before the native path, so no address space is
touched — side-effect-free).  Serial stamp "munmap zero-length EINVAL
(batch 533): OK".  cargo build clean; clippy at 17477 baseline; boot-test
PASSED (BOOT_OK 25s, stamp present, no munmap FAILs).  Reversible (pure
translator change; no PCB/state).

================================================================
ABI batch 534 — readahead FMODE_READ gate (EBADF on write-only fd)
================================================================
Date: 2026-06-12   Decided by: Claude (autonomous)
File: kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs (translator only)

Divergence: sys_readahead accepted a write-only (O_WRONLY) descriptor
and returned 0, where Linux returns EBADF.  We honoured the missing-fd
EBADF and the file-type EINVAL gate, but skipped the "open for reading"
check that sits between them.

Verbatim v6.6 citation (mm/readahead.c, ksys_readahead):
  ssize_t ksys_readahead(int fd, loff_t offset, size_t count)
  {
      ssize_t ret;
      struct fd f;
      ret = -EBADF;
      f = fdget(fd);
      if (!f.file || !(f.file->f_mode & FMODE_READ))
          goto out;
      ret = -EINVAL;
      if (!f.file->f_mapping || !f.file->f_mapping->a_ops ||
          !S_ISREG(file_inode(f.file)->i_mode))
          goto out;
      ret = vfs_fadvise(f.file, offset, count, POSIX_FADV_WILLNEED);
  out:
      fdput(f);
      return ret;
  }
The FMODE_READ check (gate 1) is EBADF and runs ahead of the file-type
EINVAL (gate 2).  FMODE_READ derives from OPEN_FMODE = (flags+1) &
O_ACCMODE, so only the pure O_WRONLY access mode clears it.

Fix (translator-only): added pure helper fd_access_is_readable(
status_flags) -> bool (readable iff (status_flags & O_ACCMODE) !=
O_WRONLY) and gated sys_readahead on it (EBADF) immediately after the
fd lookup, before the HandleKind EINVAL check — matching Linux's gate
order.  FdEntry.status_flags already records the open access mode.

Tests: the full dispatch path is not reachable from the boot task —
sys_readahead short-circuits to 0 when caller_pid() is None (kernel
context) — so the boot self-test verifies the pure predicate
fd_access_is_readable (the exact decision the EBADF gate makes):
O_RDONLY, O_RDWR, and O_RDWR|O_APPEND are readable; pure O_WRONLY and
O_WRONLY|O_APPEND are not.  Serial stamp "readahead FMODE_READ gate
(batch 534): OK".  cargo build clean; clippy at 17477 baseline (a doc
list-continuation lint required relocating the helper above the
sys_readahead doc block so the two doc comments stay separate);
boot-test PASSED (BOOT_OK 25s, stamp present, no readahead FAILs).
Reversible (pure translator change; no PCB/state).

================================================================
ABI batch 535 — ftruncate FMODE_WRITE gate (EINVAL on read-only fd)
================================================================
Date: 2026-06-12   Decided by: Claude (autonomous)
File: kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs (translator only)

Divergence: sys_ftruncate did not check the fd's access mode.  A
read-only (O_RDONLY) File fd returned EROFS and a read-only MemFd fd
proceeded to a (possibly successful) resize, where Linux returns EINVAL
for both because the fd lacks FMODE_WRITE.

Verbatim v6.6 citation (fs/open.c, do_sys_ftruncate):
  error = -EINVAL;
  if (length < 0)
      goto out;
  error = -EBADF;
  f = fdget(fd);
  if (!f.file)
      goto out;
  ...
  error = -EINVAL;
  if (!S_ISREG(inode->i_mode) || !(f.file->f_mode & FMODE_WRITE))
      goto out_putf;
  ...
  error = do_truncate(...);          // mnt_want_write -> EROFS lives here
Gate order: length<0 EINVAL, then EBADF, then the S_ISREG/FMODE_WRITE
EINVAL, and only afterwards do_truncate (where a RO mount yields EROFS).
So a read-only regular-file fd is EINVAL, not EROFS, and never reaches
the resize.

Fix (translator-only): added pure helper fd_access_is_writable(
status_flags) -> bool (writable iff (status_flags & O_ACCMODE) !=
O_RDONLY) and, in sys_ftruncate after the fd lookup, return EINVAL for a
File|MemFd fd that is not writable — before the File EROFS terminal and
the MemFd truncate.  The non-regular kinds keep their existing EINVAL
(their inode mode is not S_ISREG, the other half of the same gate), so
the scoped File|MemFd check is the missing FMODE_WRITE half.

Tests: the full dispatch is not reachable from the boot task —
sys_ftruncate short-circuits to EROFS when caller_pid() is None (no fd
table) — so the boot self-test verifies the pure predicate
fd_access_is_writable: O_WRONLY, O_RDWR, O_WRONLY|O_APPEND writable;
pure O_RDONLY and O_RDONLY|O_APPEND not.  Serial stamp "ftruncate
FMODE_WRITE gate (batch 535): OK".  cargo build clean; clippy at 17477
baseline; boot-test PASSED (BOOT_OK 25s, stamp present, no ftruncate
FAILs).  Reversible (pure translator change; no PCB/state).

## DRM Linux-ABI shim — commit 7 design (MAP_DUMB + mmap-on-/dev/dri-fd)
##
## Status: commits 1-6 done (uAPI ABI, KMS structs, /dev/dri nodes +
## HandleKind::DrmCard fd family, core ioctl dispatch, KMS resource
## enumeration, dumb-buffer+framebuffer ALLOCATION lifecycle).  Commit 7
## makes dumb buffers CPU-mappable and flips DRM_CAP_DUMB_BUFFER 0->1.
##
## The hard part is mapping the GEM object's *allocator-owned* physical
## frames into a userspace process WITHOUT the address-space teardown
## double-freeing them.  Verified mechanism:
##   - kernel/src/mm/page_table.rs `clear_user_address_space` walks every
##     present user PTE on exit and calls frame::free_frame on each frame.
##     free_frame is REFCOUNTED (mm/frame.rs ~2124): rc>1 just decrements
##     (and skips zero-on-free), only rc==1->0 truly frees.
##   - frame::ref_inc (mm/frame.rs:2486) / ref_dec (:2519) exist.
##   => Proper design: when mapping GEM frames into a process for MAP_DUMB,
##      call ref_inc on each frame first.  Teardown's free_frame then just
##      decrements the extra ref; the GEM object keeps its own ref until
##      gem_destroy (DrmDevice::gem_destroy -> GemObject::free_backing ->
##      free_frame).  Whichever drops last truly frees.  This mirrors how
##      CoW shared frames are handled.  (Note: ipc/shm.rs has NOT built a
##      shared-frame user-mapping path yet — shm.rs:241 "future mmap"; so
##      this is the first such path and should become the template.)
##
## Pieces:
## 1. Fake-offset allocator (new small module, e.g. kernel/src/drm/dumb_mmap.rs
##    or a table in card_fd.rs): bidirectional map offset <-> (device idx,
##    gem_handle).  MAP_DUMB allocates a 16 KiB-aligned fake offset for a
##    (device, gem_handle) pair and returns it; idempotent per buffer
##    (same buffer -> same offset, like Linux drm_vma_offset_manager).
##    Offsets come from a monotonic counter in frame units, in a private
##    "fake mmap offset" space (only meaningful to the drm fd's mmap).
##    DESTROY_DUMB should also drop the offset mapping (extend
##    drm_card_ioctl_mode_destroy_dumb).
## 2. drm accessor: DrmDevice::gem_phys_frames(handle) -> Vec<PhysFrame> (or
##    Vec<u64> phys addrs) + a size/frame-count getter.  gem_frame_addrs
##    already returns HHDM *virtual* addrs; add a phys variant for mapping.
## 3. MAP_DUMB handler (drm_card_ioctl_mode_map_dumb): render-node gated
##    (EACCES), look up the gem handle on the device, allocate/return the
##    fake offset in DrmModeMapDumb.offset.
## 4. mmap interception: in syscall/linux.rs sys_mmap, BEFORE the
##    `(flags & MAP_ANONYMOUS)==0 || fd>=0 -> ENOSYS` rejection, resolve fd
##    in the Linux fd table; if it is HandleKind::DrmCard, route to a drm
##    mmap path that: validates offset->(_,gem_handle); fetches phys frames;
##    checks length <= buffer size; picks a user vaddr (mmap_alloc_vaddr or
##    honor MAP_FIXED); for each frame: ref_inc then page_table::map_frame
##    with USER|WRITABLE|PRESENT (cacheable is fine for system-RAM dumb
##    buffers); full rollback (unmap + ref_dec) on partial failure; charge
##    RLIMIT_AS.  No anonymous VMA (these are device-shared frames, not
##    demand-paged) — but consider a VmaKind so /proc/maps + munmap behave;
##    teardown frees by walking PTEs regardless.
## 5. Flip uapi::cap_value(DRM_CAP_DUMB_BUFFER) 0 -> 1.
##
## Fork caveat to document: a process that forks AFTER mmap'ing a dumb
## buffer will hit CoW dup (refcount bump + CoW mark) -> first write yields
## a private copy, breaking MAP_SHARED semantics for the buffer.  Graphics
## clients almost never fork post-map, so acceptable for now; the correct
## fix is a shared/foreign VmaKind that fork copies the mapping (not the
## frames) for.  Log under Judgment Calls if shipped this way.
##
## Commit 8 (after 7): PAGE_FLIP + vblank-event delivery — queue a
## drm_event (struct drm_event_vblank) readable/pollable on the card fd so
## DRM_MODE_PAGE_FLIP_EVENT clients don't hang; PAGE_FLIP itself maps to
## DrmDevice::page_flip(crtc_id, fb_id).

## ----------------------------------------------------------------------
## membarrier(2) membarrier_state NOT reset on execve (TD8 residual)
## ---- RESOLVED 2026-06-14 ----
## ----------------------------------------------------------------------
## Added 2026-06-14.  TD8 (per-mm membarrier registration gating) is
## resolved: Process now carries a `membarrier_state: u32` READY bitmask
## (pcb.rs) that REGISTER_* sets and the PRIVATE_EXPEDITED* issues are
## gated on (EPERM if unset).  It is inherited verbatim across fork (Linux
## dup_mm memcpy).  Linux ALSO resets it to 0 on execve
## (membarrier_exec_mmap), but we had no exec-time PCB-reset hook yet, so
## a registration survived exec.
##
## RESOLUTION (2026-06-14): added the shared exec-time PCB-reset hook —
## pcb::reset_linux_state_for_exec(pid), called from spawn::exec_process
## right after reset_vmas_for_exec.  It clears, under one PROCESS_TABLE
## lock, exactly the fields Linux unconditionally resets on every exec:
##   * membarrier_state -> 0   (exec_mmap -> membarrier_exec_mmap zeroes
##                              the new mm's membarrier_state)
##   * linux_dumpable -> 1     (begin_new_exec calls set_dumpable explicitly
##                              on every exec; no cred change here so always
##                              SUID_DUMP_USER — this explicit reset wins
##                              over the MMF_DUMPABLE MMF_INIT_MASK carry-in)
##   * linux_securebits SECBIT_KEEP_CAPS bit (bit 4) -> 0
##                             (cap_bprm_creds_from_file clears only this
##                              bit on every exec; lock bit + other bits
##                              preserved).  Bit 4 is now the single source
##                              of truth for PR_SET_KEEPCAPS (see refactor
##                              note below), so this resets keepcaps too.
## This also closes the deferred exec-reset notes on entries 94 (dumpable),
## 95 (keepcaps), and the securebits field doc's KEEP_CAPS exec caveat.
##
## CORRECTION (also 2026-06-14, same session): entry 98's claim that
## linux_thp_disable is "cleared on execve (new mm gets default flags)" is
## WRONG, and an initial version of this hook wrongly reset it.  MMF_DISABLE_THP
## lives in mm->flags and is in MMF_INIT_MASK; mm_init does
## `mm->flags = current->mm->flags & MMF_INIT_MASK`, and that path runs for
## exec too (bprm_mm_init -> mm_alloc -> mm_init, while current->mm is still
## the old image's mm).  begin_new_exec has NO explicit THP override, so the
## flag SURVIVES exec — identical mechanism to linux_memory_merge
## (MMF_VM_MERGE_ANY), whose pcb doc already correctly says "preserved across
## exec".  So thp_disable is now in the PRESERVED set, not the cleared set.
##
## Also deliberately NOT reset (correcting over-broad earlier notes):
##   * pdeathsig — prctl(2): cleared only when exec'ing a set-uid/set-gid
##     binary or one with file capabilities; otherwise PRESERVED across
##     execve.  We never change credentials at exec, so preserve.
##   * personality — on x86_64 set_personality_64bit() inherits the
##     personality and only clears READ_IMPLIES_EXEC (unmodelled);
##     ADDR_NO_RANDOMIZE survives exec (that's how `setarch -R` works).
## linux_no_new_privs (sticky), linux_child_subreaper (Linux preserves),
## linux_memory_merge / linux_thp_disable (MMF_INIT_MASK mm-flags), and the
## timer-slack pair are likewise preserved.
## Self-test: pcb::test_reset_linux_state_for_exec dirties all eight fields,
## calls the hook, and asserts the three cleared + five preserved; boot test
## PASSED ("[proc]   exec Linux-state reset: OK").
##
## FOLLOW-UP (2026-06-14, same session): keepcaps/securebits single source
## of truth.  The exec-reset audit exposed a real ABI bug from split storage:
## entry 95 added a standalone `linux_keepcaps` field, but entry 112 stored
## SECBIT_KEEP_CAPS in `linux_securebits` — the SAME logical flag, which Linux
## keeps in ONE place (cred->securebits bit 4).  PR_SET_KEEPCAPS wrote only
## linux_keepcaps; PR_SET_SECUREBITS wrote only linux_securebits; so
## PR_GET_KEEPCAPS and PR_GET_SECUREBITS could disagree where Linux keeps them
## identical.  FIX (the proper one, not a sync-hack): removed the linux_keepcaps
## field entirely; pcb::get_keepcaps/set_keepcaps are now thin views over
## linux_securebits bit 4 (set/clear only bit 4).  Also added the missing Linux
## gate to PR_SET_KEEPCAPS: SECBIT_KEEP_CAPS_LOCKED (bit 5) set -> EPERM
## (cap_task_prctl, verified against torvalds/linux v6.6 commoncap.c — the
## check is `if (issecure(SECURE_KEEP_CAPS_LOCKED)) return -EPERM;` after the
## arg2>1 EINVAL).  Gate extracted as pure `keepcaps_change_allowed(securebits)`
## so it is unit-testable without a caller PCB.  Tests: self_test_prctl_dispatch
## keepcaps block asserts get/set coherence both directions + the lock truth
## table; test_reset_linux_state_for_exec proves set_keepcaps drives bit 4.
## Build + clippy clean; boot test PASSED.

## [BLOCKED prerequisite 2026-06-19] Path Z: compiling C that #includes glibc headers
## The hosted-tcc toolchain line (Path Z Parts 35-39) now covers freestanding
## compile, hosted glibc-linked compile (puts; printf/malloc), separate
## compilation (tcc -c objects + multi-object link), and GNU make driving tcc to
## build a multi-file project — all using `extern` prototypes so NO system
## headers are needed.
##   The natural next rung — compiling a source that does `#include <stdio.h>`
## (and friends), the form real-world C / configure scripts / CMake projects
## use — is BLOCKED: the glibc/system header tree (/usr/include/*) is staged in
## NEITHER the rootfs.ext4 image (verified: /usr/include/stdio.h absent) NOR the
## current build host (this WSL has no gcc and no /usr/include). The rootfs is
## produced elsewhere by scripts/create-ext4-rootfs.sh on a machine with a full
## glibc-dev toolchain.
##   PROPER FIX (when a header-carrying build environment is available): extend
## scripts/create-ext4-rootfs.sh to copy the glibc + linux + gcc/tcc header trees
## (/usr/include, /usr/include/x86_64-linux-gnu, and tcc's own include dir) into
## the image, mind the 48MB image / ~free-space budget (trim to the headers a
## hosted compile actually needs if it doesn't fit), and ensure tcc's default
## include search path (or a `-I`/`-isystem` / CPATH env in the self-test) finds
## them. Then add a Path Z rung that compiles `#include <stdio.h>` + printf and
## runs it, asserting the include-path resolution + preprocessor + glibc link all
## work end to end. Until the header tree is stageable from this environment, this
## rung cannot be implemented or validated here.

## [RESOLVED 2026-07-15] OCI build-time RUN now starts at WORKDIR (was: rootfs root)
## exec_build_run (kernel/src/oci.rs) launched a Dockerfile `RUN` command via
## container::exec_path_env, which had no working-directory argument — so the
## process started at "/" inside the merged rootfs rather than the image's
## `WORKDIR`. A RUN relying on a relative cwd (e.g. `RUN ./configure`) resolved
## against "/" instead of WORKDIR.
##   FIX (2026-07-15): container::exec_path_env gained a `cwd: Option<&[u8]>`
## param (sets SpawnOptions.cwd, which already existed + was unit-tested via
## test_spawn_with_cwd); exec_build_run / exec_build_run_inner thread
## spec.working_dir down and pass it (empty → None → default "/"). WORKDIR
## already materialises the dir as a layer, so it exists in the merged rootfs
## when the RUN executes. Built + clippy clean + boot-test green.
##   REMAINING follow-up — RESOLVED 2026-07-15: `docker exec` / `container
## run-in` now DO default to the container's configured WorkingDir (Docker
## parity). Implementation:
##   - `struct Container` (kernel/src/container.rs) gained a `working_dir:
##     String` field (init empty in new_empty()).
##   - `run_with_abi` persists the init process's spawn `options.cwd` into that
##     field at the Created→Running flip, so whatever WorkingDir the runner
##     chose (CLI `-w` > image WorkingDir > none) is stored for later exec.
##   - `exec_path_env` defaults its cwd to the stored WorkingDir when the caller
##     passes `None`; an explicit `Some(dir)` still wins. Logic extracted to
##     `resolve_exec_cwd()` and unit-tested in the container self-test (now 61
##     tests: explicit-wins / fallback-to-stored / empty-stored-is-default /
##     explicit-with-no-stored).
##   - `container_exec_rootfs` (kshell.rs) — the handler behind `container
##     run-in`, `container exec --rootfs`, and the `docker exec` delegate — now
##     parses `-w`/`--workdir DIR` (absolute-path required) before the id, and
##     routes through exec_path_env so both the explicit flag and the stored
##     WorkingDir default take effect. Built + boot-test green (61 tests pass).
##   (The userspace `podman exec` remains a print-only stub — not wired to the
##   kernel container API — so it needs no change here.)

## Services embedded via include_bytes! must be pre-built before the kernel
## (build-workflow gap; pre-existing, now covers 3 services)
##   The kernel embeds several bare-metal `services/*` ELFs at compile time via
##   include_bytes!:
##     - services/hello   -> kernel/src/container.rs (container exec tests)
##     - services/netstack -> kernel/src/proc/spawn.rs (net→userspace Phase 2
##       self_test_userspace_netstack)
##   Each service's `target/` is gitignored (**/target/), so these ELFs are NOT
##   committed. `scripts/boot-test.sh` only runs `cargo build` for the default
##   workspace members (kernel/posix/stubs) — it does NOT build the services.
##   Consequence: on a fresh checkout the kernel FAILS to build until each
##   embedded service is built manually, e.g.:
##       (cd services/netstack && cargo build --release)
##       (cd services/hello    && cargo build --release)
##   PROPER FIX: add a build step (kernel/build.rs or a top-level script invoked
##   by boot-test.sh) that builds every service whose ELF the kernel embeds,
##   before the kernel compiles. Keep artifacts out of git; make the build
##   reproducible instead. Deferred (matches the existing hello/ticker pattern),
##   but the gap is real and grows with each embedded service.

## [2026-07-14] Net→userspace endgame sequencing (post-5.6) — strategy note
##   5.6 is DONE and the net.userspace cutover switch now genuinely works at
##   runtime (limine kernel-file request-ID fix + init_defaults-at-boot; proven
##   via the real Limine cmdline). Remaining Phase-5 endgame:
##     5.7 flip the default → 5.8 delete the kernel L2–L4 core.
##   DO NOT flip the default (5.7) yet: the daemon-backed socket path is strictly
##   less capable than the mature in-kernel stack (known-issues D-NETSOCK-SYNC:
##   IPv4-only connect, no true O_NONBLOCK / poll-epoll readiness, no
##   listen/accept). Flipping now would regress DEFAULT networking. Correct
##   order: close D-NETSOCK-SYNC parity gaps behind the (default-off) switch,
##   THEN flip (5.7), THEN delete (5.8). Operator already approved the overall
##   staged cutover (§66 Q22b: prove parity, flip, delete) — this is just being
##   careful not to regress on the flip.
##   KEY BLOCKER for that parity work (RESOLVED 2026-07-15): reliable switch-ON
##   boot validation was undermined by the moving-location container-exec /
##   ring-3 spawn-dispatch race (known-issues B-PTHREAD-YIELDBUDGET family). That
##   race is now root-caused and FIXED — it was two holder-preemption spinlock
##   deadlocks (a task holding a raw `spin::Mutex` involuntarily preempted mid-
##   critical-section on the UP boot, the spinner then never yielding to the
##   Ready holder): (1) the global kernel heap lock (fixed 83307bdfc, preempt-
##   disable via ManuallyDrop) and (2) the container TABLE/EVENT_LOG locks (fixed
##   fa87bbb5e, migrated to the preempt-aware crate::sync::Mutex). NOT the futex
##   (confirmed sound) and NOT an AB-BA inversion (the guest is UP, so lockdep_mm
##   would not have caught it — that earlier recommendation is moot). Validation:
##   a 40/40-clean default-path soak, a 15/15-clean switch-ON soak, and the live
##   ring-3 HTTP capstone (services/httpget) all green. So the endgame is no
##   longer blocked on the race. Remaining endgame gates are operator decisions:
##   Q23 (server-socket session model, blocks sys_bind/listen/accept4) and the
##   5.7 default-flip itself (user-visible policy). See open-questions.md.

## Known limitation (osh, 2026-07-19): pipeline `set -x` xtrace ordering is racy
##   `set -x; echo a | cat` traces `+ cat` then `+ echo a` (or any order),
##   whereas bash deterministically traces left-to-right (`+ echo a` then
##   `+ cat`). Cause: osh runs the last pipeline stage on the main thread and
##   the earlier stages on scoped threads (exec_threaded_pipeline, interp.rs
##   ~1316); each stage emits its own xtrace inside exec_command, so the output
##   order follows thread scheduling, not pipeline order. Confirmed racy (varies
##   run to run for a 3-stage pipeline). Not fixed because the clean fix — emit
##   each stage's xtrace in the parent, in order, before spawning — would have to
##   expand the stage's words in the parent, double-evaluating any command
##   substitution in the traced command (a correctness bug worse than the
##   cosmetic ordering issue). Pipeline-stage trace order is POSIX-unspecified,
##   so this is cosmetic. Proper fix if ever needed: compute a side-effect-free
##   trace string per stage in the parent (only when every stage's command word
##   is expansion-free), else accept the racy order.

## Known limitation (osh, 2026-07-19): several `set -o` options are listed but inert
##   `set -o` now lists the full standard bash option set (27 names, matching a
##   Linux bash) so listings and validation match bash, and the following are
##   fully functional: allexport, braceexpand (set -B/+B), errexit, errtrace,
##   functrace, noclobber, noexec, noglob, nounset, pipefail, xtrace (plus the
##   always-on defaults hashall and interactive-comments). The remaining standard
##   options are accepted by `set -o NAME`/`set +o NAME` and reported truthfully
##   in `set -o`/`$SHELLOPTS`, but do NOT yet change behavior: emacs, vi, history,
##   histexpand (line-editing — osh has no interactive line editor), monitor,
##   notify (job-control notification), ignoreeof, keyword, onecmd, physical,
##   posix, nolog, privileged, verbose. They are modeled as off (their bash
##   default in non-interactive `-c`/script mode). Proper fix if/when needed:
##   give each real behavior — most are interactive-only (emacs/vi/history/
##   ignoreeof) and thus low priority for a non-interactive-first shell; physical
##   (cd -P symlink handling), posix mode, and verbose (echo input as read) are
##   the most script-relevant to implement first. State lives in the individual
##   bool fields plus shell_option_enabled() (interp.rs); STANDARD_SET_O_OPTIONS
##   is the canonical name list.

## Known limitation (osh, 2026-07-19): residual syntax-error-message format gaps
##   Parser/lexer diagnostics now match bash's wording for the common cases:
##   unclosed quotes/substitutions report `unexpected EOF while looking for
##   matching `C'` (C = single closing delimiter), stray tokens report
##   `syntax error near unexpected token `T'`, unclosed compounds/EOF report
##   `syntax error: unexpected end of file`, and bad identifiers report
##   ``NAME': not a valid identifier` (no `syntax error:` tag). Two residual
##   format-level gaps remain, deemed not worth the machinery for now:
##   (1) A bare `for`/`select`/`case` keyword with nothing after it at EOF:
##       bash reports `syntax error near unexpected token `newline'` (it treats
##       the implicit trailing newline as a token), while osh reports
##       `syntax error: unexpected end of file`. Matching bash would require the
##       lexer to emit a synthetic trailing-newline token so the parser can name
##       it; not justified for the marginal wording difference.
##   (2) In `-c` command mode bash inserts an extra `-c: ` segment
##       (`bash: -c: line N: ...`) and, for some errors, echoes the offending
##       source as a second line. osh emits `osh: line N: ...` without the
##       `-c:` segment or the source-echo line. err_prefix()/format_parse_error
##       in interp.rs are where this would be extended if we choose to match it.

## Known limitation (osh, 2026-07-19): per-COMMAND line granularity for $LINENO/errors
##   The lexer stamps every token with its true source line, and the parser
##   stamps each top-level Item (and-or list) with the line of its first token,
##   so line numbers stay correct across newlines swallowed inside here-doc
##   bodies, quoted strings, and command substitutions (previously every
##   diagnostic after such a construct reported too small a line). Additionally
##   each SimpleCommand now carries its own source line (SimpleCommand.line,
##   populated from Parser::cur_line() in parse_simple), and exec_simple_inner
##   sets self.current_line = sc.line before executing the command. This makes
##   $LINENO and error diagnostics per-SIMPLE-COMMAND accurate, matching bash:
##   (1) FIXED: A multi-line pipeline blames the failing stage's own line
##       (`echo a |`⏎`grep a |`⏎`no_such` -> line 3), and and-or lists likewise.
##       Verified against bash for first/middle/last pipeline stages and and-or
##       lists; regression test interp::tests::line_number_is_tracked_per_simple_command.
##   One case still differs from bash:
##   (2) A single simple command with an argument that itself contains an
##       embedded newline (e.g. a double-quoted arg spanning two lines): bash
##       reports the line the command finishes on, osh the line it starts on.
##       Niche; would need the command node to record its END line as well as
##       its start line. Deferred as very low value.

## DONE (initiative-F / native mprotect) — 2026-07-22
##   Implemented the native SYS_MPROTECT handler (native syscall #22). Took the
##   preferred DRY route: factored an ABI-neutral core into
##   kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs — mprotect_validate_core (KernelResult gate) +
##   pub(crate) mprotect_core (KernelResult<()> holding VMA-coverage check,
##   protect_vma_range, per-4KiB change_flags_4k, batched mprotect_flush_range).
##   sys_mprotect (Linux #10) is now a thin wrapper mapping KernelError→linux
##   errno; syscall::handlers::sys_mprotect calls the same core and returns raw
##   KernelError codes, registered at #22 in syscall::dispatch. posix
##   mman::mprotect's errno::translate now maps 0/EINVAL/ENOMEM/ESRCH — no more
##   ENOTSUP. New native dispatch self-test test_dispatch_mprotect_native proves
##   the handler is wired (not NotSupported) + the gate order. mprotect_validate_args
##   kept as a thin wrapper so sys_pkey_mprotect is unchanged. See known-issues.md
##   TD-NATIVE-MPROTECT (RESOLVED).

## DONE (O_EXCL exclusive-create) — 2026-07-22
##   Implemented POSIX O_EXCL end-to-end (was the BUG-OPENFLAGS residual
##   limitation in known-issues.md). Added OpenFlags::EXCL (bit 6, 0x40) to
##   kernel/src/fs/handle.rs; open() now returns AlreadyExists (→ EEXIST) when
##   both CREATE and EXCL are set and the path already exists — checked before
##   the dir/regular split so exclusive create over an existing directory also
##   fails with EEXIST. EXCL without CREATE is ignored (Linux behaviour). posix
##   translate_open_flags (posix/src/file.rs) maps O_EXCL → N_EXCL (0x40).
##   Added false-pass-proof handle self-test (section 16): (a) CREATE|EXCL over
##   an existing file → AlreadyExists, (b) fresh path → succeeds+creates, (c)
##   second exclusive create → AlreadyExists, (d) EXCL w/o CREATE opens the file.
##   Residual (tracked in known-issues.md): stat-based check has a narrow TOCTOU
##   window because the FileSystem trait lacks an atomic create-exclusive
##   primitive; exists-at-open-time guarantee (the primary use) holds regardless.

## DONE (O_NOFOLLOW + errno ELOOP fix) — 2026-07-22
##   Implemented POSIX O_NOFOLLOW enforcement (was accepted+stored but ignored:
##   open() silently followed a final symlink). Added OpenFlags::NOFOLLOW (bit 7,
##   0x80) to kernel/src/fs/handle.rs; open() runs a NOFOLLOW guard before
##   resolve_path — Vfs::lstat (does not follow the final component); if the last
##   component is a Symlink → returns TooManyLinks (→ ELOOP); parent symlinks
##   still followed (POSIX); non-symlink/missing final components fall through.
##   posix translate_open_flags maps O_NOFOLLOW → N_NOFOLLOW (0x80). Handle
##   self-test section 17: (a) no-NOFOLLOW follows to target, (b) NOFOLLOW on a
##   symlink → TooManyLinks, (c) NOFOLLOW on a non-symlink opens normally.
##   Also fixed a latent bug (BUG-ERRNO-TOOMANYLINKS-EMLINK): native
##   errno::translate mapped TooManyLinks (-506) → EMLINK, but that code is
##   symlink-loop (ELOOP) semantics everywhere in the kernel (no hard-link-count
##   producer). Now maps → ELOOP, matching the Linux ABI; test + comments updated.

## DONE (O_EXCL + O_NOFOLLOW Linux-ABI parity) — 2026-07-22
##   Closed the parallel Linux-ABI gap: the kernel-side Linux translator
##   (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs::translate_open_flags, ~5352) mapped only
##   READ/WRITE/CREATE/TRUNCATE/APPEND/DIRECTORY — it dropped both O_EXCL and
##   O_NOFOLLOW, so glibc/Linux-ABI programs got neither guarantee even though
##   sys_openat/open_kernel_path_install funnel through the same fs::handle::open.
##   Added oflags::O_NOFOLLOW (0o400_000) constant and now map O_EXCL →
##   OpenFlags::EXCL and O_NOFOLLOW → OpenFlags::NOFOLLOW. The shared handle::open
##   guards (added earlier this day) now fire identically for the native posix
##   and Linux ABIs. Kernel builds clean; boot test green.

## DONE (openat2 RESOLVE_NO_SYMLINKS enforcement) — 2026-07-22
##   Fixed a real security bug: sys_openat2 (kernel/src/syscall/linux.rs)
##   accepted RESOLVE_NO_SYMLINKS but never enforced it — the comment falsely
##   claimed "no symlinks at all", but memfs+ext4 both support symlinks, so
##   openat2 with that resolve bit silently followed symlinks (defeating a
##   symlink-swap-attack defense). Added Vfs::resolve_no_symlinks (vfs.rs) — a
##   resolver mode returning ELOOP on ANY symlink component (parent or final),
##   stronger than O_NOFOLLOW; dcache bypassed. Added OpenFlags::NO_SYMLINKS
##   (bit 8, handle.rs) — open() uses the strict resolver when set. Threaded a
##   non-forgeable no_symlinks bool sys_openat2 -> sys_openat_ex ->
##   open_common/open_kernel_path_install, ORing the bit into native OpenFlags
##   (a program can only opt INTO stricter resolution). Handle self-test §18:
##   (a) parent symlink followed w/o flag, (b) parent symlink -> ELOOP w/ flag
##   (the case NOFOLLOW misses), (c) final symlink -> ELOOP, (d) clean path OK.

## TODO (initiative-F / fastpy pure-mode file I/O) — 2026-07-21
##   Enable real file I/O for fastpy *pure-mode* (SlateOS) programs — the main
##   prerequisite for a shipping fastpy component that touches the filesystem
##   (package manager, cat, a config reader, etc.).
##   Current gap: fastpy's file/Path machinery (open(), Path.read_text/
##   write_text/is_file/iterdir, etc.) lives in runtime/cpython_bridge.c, which
##   is REPLACED by bridge_stub.c in pure mode (-DFPY_PURE_MODE). Worse, those
##   functions take a CPython Path *object* (PyObject*) and use _path_as_cstr()/
##   Py* calls, so it is not a simple "move the fopen() calls" refactor — the
##   pure-mode representation of a Path/file object must be designed.
##   Good news: the SlateOS posix libc already implements the full C file
##   surface backed by SYS_FS_* VFS syscalls — fopen/fread/fwrite/fclose/fseek/
##   ftell in posix/src/stdio.rs and open/read/write/close in posix/src/file.rs.
##   So once fastpy has a pure-mode file/Path object model, backing it with
##   plain C stdio "just works" on-target (and on host).
##   Proper fix (in the fastpy repo): design a bridge-neutral file/Path object
##   for pure mode (a small C struct + a FILE*), emit open()/read()/write()/
##   close() and Path.read_text/write_text against it via standard stdio, and
##   compile that TU in BOTH host and pure builds. Then validate on-target with
##   a kernel self-test that writes a file to the VFS and reads it back.
##   Note: sys.argv and sys.exit(code) already work in pure mode (runtime.c,
##   fpy_argv / fastpy_sys_exit) — validated on-target by
##   self_test_fastpy_slateos_tls (exits with argc). print() also works.
